> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lean Manufacturing, Mass Customization, Green Manufacturing, Total Quality Management, Agile Manufacturing, Just in Time, Original Equipment Manufacturer, Reconfigurable Manufacturing, Assembly Lines, Concept of Inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Alignment:** This module establishes the vocabulary of modern manufacturing. The board examiner expects precise definitions of paradigms, plus a working understanding of how they interconnect. Memorize the keywords in **bold**.

---

## 1.1 Lean Manufacturing (LM)

**Formal Definition:** Lean Manufacturing is a systematic, socio-technical methodology focused on the **systematic elimination of waste (Muda)** across the value stream, while continuously improving flow and delivering exactly what the customer needs, when they need it.

The waste eliminated is classified into **seven deadly wastes (TIMWOOD)**:
- **T**ransportation (unnecessary movement of materials)
- **I**nventory (excess raw material, WIP, finished goods)
- **M**otion (unnecessary movement of people/equipment)
- **W**aiting (idle time of people, machines, or materials)
- **O**ver-production (making more than demanded)
- **O**ver-processing (doing more work than necessary)
- **D**efects (rework, scrap, warranty)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Origin:** Derived from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** and **Eiji Toyoda** post-WWII. The term "Lean" was coined by **James P. Womack and Daniel T. Jones** in their 1990 book *The Machine That Changed the World*.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine making a cup of chai (tea). A *lean* approach means having the sugar, milk, water, and tea leaves perfectly pre-measured in a tray beside the stove — no walking to the shop, no waiting for water to boil twice, no sugar wasted. Every second is *value-adding* for the person who will drink it.

**Core Pillars of Lean:**
1. **Value** — defined strictly by the customer
2. **Value Stream** — mapping every step from raw material to customer
3. **Flow** — ensuring smooth, uninterrupted movement
4. **Pull System** — produce only when the next step "pulls"
5. **Perfection (Kaizen)** — continuous incremental improvement

---

## 1.2 Mass Customization (MC)

**Formal Definition:** Mass Customization is a production strategy that combines the **low unit cost of mass production** with the **flexibility and personalization of custom manufacturing**, enabling customers to specify unique product features from a modular platform at near-mass-production prices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Archetypes of Mass Customization (Pine, 1993):**
> 1. **Collaborative Customization** — firm interacts with each customer
> 2. **Adaptive Customization** — standard product, customer modifies it post-sale
> 3. **Cosmetic Customization** — same product, different packaging/labels
> 4. **Transparent Customization** — different products, customer unaware of variation

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of **Bata Shoes** or **Nike By You** — you choose the colour, sole type, and lace design from a website, but the factory uses the same standardized rubber, leather cutting machines, and stitching patterns. The "custom" part is just the *configuration* at the end.

---

## 1.3 Green Manufacturing (GM)

**Formal Definition:** Green Manufacturing (also called **Sustainable Manufacturing**) is the design and operation of production processes that **conserve energy, minimize waste, reduce pollution, and recycle/reuse materials** across the entire product life cycle, ensuring minimal negative environmental impact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4R Principle:** **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover** — the operational backbone of Green Manufacturing.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A paper factory that uses recycled pulp, runs on solar power, and purifies its own wastewater before releasing it. The *output* is paper, but the *process* leaves the river cleaner than it found it.

**Regulatory Anchors:** ISO 14001 (Environmental Management System), Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement, and India's CPCB norms.

---

## 1.4 Total Quality Management (TQM)

**Formal Definition:** TQM is a **comprehensive, customer-focused, continuous-improvement management philosophy** that involves every employee — from the shop floor to top management — in improving products, services, and processes using data-driven quality tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **TQM Guru Founders (MUST remember for KTU):**
> - **W. Edwards Deming** — 14 Points for Management, PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle
> - **Joseph Juran** — Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement)
> - **Philip B. Crosby** — "Quality is Free", Zero Defects concept
> - **Kaoru Ishikawa** — Quality Circles, Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram
> - **Genichi Taguchi** — Taguchi Loss Function, Robust Design
> - **Armand Feigenbaum** — Total Quality Control, coined "Total Quality Control"

**Conceptual Analogy:** Running a hospital. TQM means the *receptionist*, *nurse*, *surgeon*, and *janitor* all share a single mission: zero infections, zero readmissions, zero wait-time complaints. Quality is not just the doctor's job.

---

## 1.5 Agile Manufacturing (AM)

**Formal Definition:** Agile Manufacturing is a **system-wide capability to respond quickly to unpredictable changes in customer demand and market conditions**, by leveraging flexible organizational structures, skilled workforces, advanced information technology, and rapid reconfigurable production systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Agility Capabilities (Youssef, 1992):**
> 1. **Mastery of Change** — handling volume, mix, and design changes
> 2. **Robustness** — producing low-cost, defect-free products
> 3. **Customization** — variety without sacrificing speed
> 4. **Time-to-Market** — fast product launch

**Conceptual Analogy:** A pizza shop that, within 10 minutes, switches from making Margherita to a gluten-free vegan specialty pizza because the order app just said so. The *recipe book* is digital, the *oven* is programmable, the *delivery boy* is already en route.

**Contrast with Lean:** Lean eliminates *variability*; Agile *embraces* it. Lean optimizes a *known* value stream; Agile prepares for an *unknown* future.

---

## 1.6 Just-in-Time (JIT)

**Formal Definition:** Just-in-Time is a **pull-based production philosophy** that aims to deliver the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time — drastically reducing inventory levels and associated costs by synchronizing supply with actual demand.

**Core Characteristics:**
- **Zero Inventory ideal** (practically, minimal buffer)
- **Small lot sizes** (often "lot size = 1")
- **Short setup times** (Single Minute Exchange of Die — **SMED**)
- **Pull signals via Kanban cards**
- **Supplier proximity** and long-term partnerships
- **Takt Time** — pace of production matching customer demand

**Conceptual Analogy:** A restaurant kitchen. The chef doesn't pre-cook 100 rotis at 10 AM hoping customers come. He makes one roti *exactly* when the order slips through the pass window. No wastage, no stale food, no large fridge.

**Takt Time Formula:**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}
$$

---

## 1.7 Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM)

**Formal Definition:** An OEM is a **company that manufactures products or components that are purchased by another company and retailed under that purchasing company's brand name**. OEMs are the *actual fabricators* in a supply chain.

**Related Terminology (frequently asked in KTU):**
- **OBM** — Original Brand Manufacturer (designs, makes, and brands its own product)
- **ODM** — Original Design Manufacturer (designs and makes; the buyer brands it)
- **Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 Suppliers** — hierarchical layers in the supply chain
- **VAR** — Value Added Reseller (adds features to OEM product)

**Conceptual Analogy:** **Foxconn** manufactures iPhones — but the iPhone is sold by **Apple** (the OBM). Foxconn is the OEM. Apple designs it, but Foxconn *makes* it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** In KTU, students often confuse *OEM* (the *maker*) with *OBM* (the *brand*). Memorize: **O**riginal **E**quipment → the **equipment itself is produced** by them.

---

## 1.8 Reconfigurable Manufacturing System (RMS)

**Formal Definition:** An RMS is a manufacturing system designed for **rapid, scalable, modular change in structure (capacity and functionality)** to respond to changing market conditions and product requirements, by adding, removing, or rearranging modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Core Characteristics of RMS (Mehrabi, Ulsoy, Koren, 2000):**
> 1. **Modularity** — system built from standardized modules
> 2. **Integrability** — modules integrate via open interfaces
> 3. **Customized Flexibility** — flexibility for *specific* product families
> 4. **Scalability** — capacity expanded by adding identical modules
> 5. **Convertibility** — functionality changed for new products
> 6. **Diagnosability** — easy to identify and correct faults

**Conceptual Analogy:** A modular kitchen. Want a new oven? Slide one out, slide another in — same electrical and gas fittings. RMS applies the same philosophy to factory machines.

**RMS vs. FMS vs. DML:**

| System | Change Strategy | Primary Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **DML (Dedicated)** | None — fixed | High volume, low variety |
| **FMS (Flexible)** | Software/NC changes | Medium variety |
| **RMS (Reconfigurable)** | Hardware + software | Future, uncertain variety |

---

## 1.9 Assembly Lines

**Formal Definition:** An Assembly Line is a **flow-oriented production arrangement** in which workstations are sequentially arranged along a material handling path (conveyor, trolley, or AGV), and each station performs a *specific, limited set of tasks* on a moving product.

**Key Design Metrics:**
- **Cycle Time (CT)** — time between successive units coming off the line
- **Workstation Time (T_i)** — sum of task times at station *i*
- **Number of Workstations (N)** — minimum stations needed
- **Balance Delay (BD)** — idle time percentage
- **Line Efficiency (η)** — productive time / total available time
- **Balance Efficiency** — measure of task distribution

**Conceptual Analogy:** The **Maruti Suzuki assembly line in Manesar**. The car body enters as a bare shell. Station 1 mounts the engine, Station 2 attaches doors, Station 3 fits seats, Station 4 connects the dashboard, Station 5 adds wheels — and a finished car rolls out every 60 seconds.

**Types of Assembly Lines:**
1. **Single Model** — one product (e.g., old Ford Model T)
2. **Multi-Model** — several similar products on one line
3. **Mixed-Model** — variants produced in any order
4. **U-Shaped** — operator in middle, reduces walking
5. **Two-Sided** — long products (truck chassis) on a branch layout

---

## 1.10 Concept of Inventory

**Formal Definition:** Inventory is the **stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, and other supplies** held by an organization to meet production and customer demand, decoupling successive operations and buffering against uncertainty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Inventory (KTU-Favourite):**
> 1. **Raw Materials (RM)** — unprocessed inputs
> 2. **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** — partially completed units
> 3. **Finished Goods (FG)** — completed, awaiting sale
> 4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating)** — supplies for plant upkeep
> 5. **Safety Stock (SS)** — buffer against demand/supply variability
> 6. **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** — in-transit between stages
> 7. **Anticipation Stock** — built up for seasonal demand

**Why Hold Inventory? (5 Reasons):**
- **Decouple operations** — smooth flow when stages run at different rates
- **Buffer against demand variability** — prevent stockouts
- **Economies of scale** — bulk purchasing discounts
- **Hedge against price changes / inflation**
- **Smooth production** — avoid frequent setups

**Conceptual Analogy:** A water tank on a building's roof. The municipal supply is irregular (variable demand), but you always need water in the taps (constant demand). The tank *decouples* the two, *buffers* against shortfalls, and *stores* enough to survive a 2-day supply cut.

**Cost Components of Inventory:**

| Cost Type | Description | Behaviour vs. Q |
|---|---|---|
| **Holding Cost (H)** | Storage, insurance, obsolescence | Increases with Q |
| **Ordering Cost (S)** | Setup, transport, admin per order | Decreases with Q |
| **Purchase Cost (P·D)** | Variable cost of units bought | Independent of Q |
| **Shortage / Stockout Cost** | Lost sales, backorders | Decreases with Q |

> [!WARNING]
> **Trade-off:** Larger order quantity → less frequent orders (low S) BUT more inventory to carry (high H). The **EOQ formula** finds the optimal balance. This derivation is **guaranteed to appear** in KTU 2024 ESE — see Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Of the 10 topics in this module, *Assembly Lines* and *Concept of Inventory* are the only **calculation-intensive** ones. All others are **theory/definition** questions. KTU almost always pairs one theory question with one calculation question per module.

---

## 2.1 The 7 Wastes (DOWNTIME) — Modern Lean Extension

Modern lean thinkers (often called "Lean Six Sigma") add an **8th waste**:
- **N** — Non-utilized talent / skills of employees

| # | Original 7 (TIMWOOD) | 8th Waste | 9th & 10th (Digital Age) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transportation | Non-utilized Talent | Data Inaccuracy |
| 2 | Inventory | — | Energy waste |
| 3 | Motion | — | — |
| 4 | Waiting | — | — |
| 5 | Over-production | — | — |
| 6 | Over-processing | — | — |
| 7 | Defects | — | — |

---

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix: Modern Manufacturing Paradigms

| Paradigm | Primary Driver | Volume | Variety | Variability | Lead Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lean** | Waste elimination | High | Low | Low | Short |
| **Agile** | Change responsiveness | Med-High | High | High | Very Short |
| **Mass Customization** | Customer choice | High | High | Low-Med | Medium |
| **Green** | Sustainability | Any | Any | Any | Variable |
| **JIT (subset of Lean)** | Pull-based flow | High | Low | Low | Very Short |
| **Reconfigurable (RMS)** | Future-proofing | Medium | Med-High | High | Short (changeover) |
| **TQM (overarching)** | Quality excellence | Universal concept — applies to all the above |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TQM is NOT a competing system to Lean/Agile — it is the QUALITY FOUNDATION on which all of them are built.** TQM provides the *quality culture*; Lean provides the *flow*; Agile provides the *responsiveness*; RMS provides the *hardware flexibility*; OEM/MC define the *business model*; JIT is the *execution method*; Green is the *environmental overlay*.

---

## 2.3 Assembly Line Balancing — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1: Determine the Cycle Time (CT).**
Given customer demand and available production time:

$$
CT = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Demand (units per period)}}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the Theoretical Minimum Number of Workstations (N_min).**

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{CT} \right\rceil
$$

where $t_i$ is the time for task $i$, and $n$ is total tasks. The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures a whole number of stations.

**Step 3: Assign tasks to stations using the Ranked Positional Weight (RPW) heuristic.**

For each task, calculate:
$$
RPW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in \text{successors of } i} t_j
$$

Sort tasks in **descending** order of $RPW$. Assign the highest $RPW$ task to the first available station that can still fit it within $CT$. Continue until all tasks are placed.

**Step 4: Compute Line Efficiency (η).**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{N_{actual} \times CT} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute Balance Delay (BD).**

$$
BD = 1 - \eta = \left(1 - \frac{\sum t_i}{N \times CT}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 6: Smoothness Index (SI) — KTU expects this occasionally.**

$$
SI = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(CT - T_i\right)^2}
$$

Lower $SI$ = better balance.

---

## 2.4 Inventory Models — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. It is the single highest-yield reference for Module 1.**

| # | Model / Parameter | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost/order, $H$ = holding cost/unit/year |
| 2 | **EOQ — Total Cost (TC)** | $TC(Q^*) = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Minimum total variable cost |
| 3 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | $d$ = avg daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days) |
| 4 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $z$ = service-level Z-score, $\sigma_d$ = std dev of daily demand |
| 5 | **Average Inventory** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Without safety stock |
| 6 | **Holding Cost (H)** | $H = h \cdot C$ | $C$ = unit cost, $h$ = holding rate (fraction/yr) |
| 7 | **EOQ with quantity discount** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{iC}}$ | $i$ = inventory carrying rate |
| 8 | **Production Order Quantity (EPQ)** | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $p$ = production rate (units/day) |
| 9 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | Pacing of lean production |
| 10 | **Cycle Time (line)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{available time}}{\text{output required}}$ | Sets the pace of stations |
| 11 | **Line Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \cdot CT}$ | Ideal: $\eta \to 100\%$ |
| 12 | **Balance Delay (BD)** | $BD = 1 - \eta$ | Inefficiency measure |

**Standard Z-values (Safety Stock — memorize):**

| Service Level | 90% | 95% | 97.5% | 99% | 99.9% |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **z** | 1.28 | 1.65 | 1.96 | 2.33 | 3.00 |

---

## 2.5 Why These Models Matter in Engineering

- **Assembly Line Balancing** determines the **capital investment** in factory layout — too few stations and the line is a bottleneck; too many and you waste labour.
- **EOQ** determines **working capital locked in inventory** — critical in capital-intensive industries (steel, automotive, FMCG).
- **Takt Time** aligns **machine speed with market demand** — a miscalculation creates either overstock (waste) or stockouts (lost sales).
- **Reconfigurable Design** future-proofs capital — a ₹50 Cr machine must not be obsolete in 3 years.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The two derivations below are the most frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).** Practice writing them out neatly — partial credit depends on each intermediate step.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula

**Assumptions:**
1. Demand rate $D$ is known and **constant**.
2. Ordering cost $S$ is **fixed per order**, independent of quantity.
3. Holding cost $H$ is **linear** in average inventory.
4. Lead time is **known and constant**.
5. No stockouts are permitted.

**Step 1: Express Average Inventory.**

Since inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to 0 between orders, the average inventory level is:

$$
\bar{I} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Express Annual Holding Cost ($TC_h$).**

$$
TC_h = H \cdot \bar{I} = H \cdot \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{HQ}{2}
$$

*Logic:* Larger $Q$ → larger average stock → more warehouse rent, insurance, spoilage, and capital tied up. Hence $TC_h$ is a **linearly increasing function** of $Q$.

**Step 3: Express Annual Ordering Cost ($TC_s$).**

Number of orders per year = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.

$$
TC_s = S \cdot \frac{D}{Q} = \frac{DS}{Q}
$$

*Logic:* Larger $Q$ → fewer orders per year → lower ordering cost. Hence $TC_s$ is a **hyperbolically decreasing function** of $Q$.

**Step 4: Write Total Variable Cost Function.**

$$
TC(Q) = TC_h + TC_s = \frac{HQ}{2} + \frac{DS}{Q}
$$

The **purchase cost** $P \cdot D$ is constant and is omitted from the optimization because it does not depend on $Q$.

**Step 5: Minimize TC(Q) by Differentiating with Respect to Q and Setting to Zero.**

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = \frac{H}{2} - \frac{DS}{Q^2} = 0
$$

**Step 6: Solve for Q.**

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

**Step 7: Verify the Second Derivative is Positive (Confirms Minimum).**

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = 0 + \frac{2DS}{Q^3} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(since all variables are positive)}
$$

A positive second derivative confirms that $TC(Q)$ is convex and the critical point is indeed a **minimum**.

**Step 8: Substitute $Q^*$ Back into TC to Get Minimum Total Cost.**

$$
TC(Q^*) = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} + \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}}
$$

$$
TC(Q^*) = \sqrt{\frac{2DSH}{4}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH \cdot 2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{DSH}
$$

Wait — let us re-simplify carefully:

$$
TC(Q^*) = \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} + \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}}
$$

First term:

$$
\frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{H^2 \cdot \frac{2DS}{H}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2DSH}
$$

Second term:

$$
\frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = DS \cdot \sqrt{\frac{H}{2DS}} = \sqrt{\frac{D^2 S^2 H}{2DS}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}
$$

Adding both:

$$
TC(Q^*) = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{2DSH} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}
$$

$$
TC(Q^*) = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

**Final Result:** The minimum total variable cost at the EOQ is $TC(Q^*) = \sqrt{2DSH}$.

---

## 3.2 NUMERICAL: Assembly Line Balancing by RPW Heuristic

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**

A product requires 10 tasks with precedence relationships and times (in seconds) as given:

| Task | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | J |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time (s) | 8 | 6 | 4 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 10 |
| Immediate Predecessors | — | A | A | B | C | C | D,E | F | F,G | H,I |

**Daily demand** = 480 units, **Available time** = 8 hours = 28,800 seconds.

**Step 1: Compute Cycle Time.**

$$
CT = \frac{28{,}800 \text{ s}}{480 \text{ units}} = 60 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Theoretical Minimum Workstations.**

$$
\sum t_i = 8+6+4+5+3+7+6+5+4+10 = 58 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{58}{60} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.967 \rceil = 1 \text{ station}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A single workstation is **theoretically** enough (sum of task times < CT), but **precedence constraints will force more stations in practice**. Real-world $N_{actual} \ge N_{min}$ due to logical ordering.

**Step 3: Compute Ranked Positional Weights (RPW).**

Each $RPW_i$ = task time + sum of times of all its successors (transitive closure).

- **A**: 8 + (6+4+5+3+7+6+5+4+10) = 8 + 50 = **58**
- **B**: 6 + 5 + 6 = **17** (B→D→G, B→D has no more; successors of D are G; sum = 5+6=11; plus B=6 → 17)
  Actually recompute: B → D → G; times = 6 + 5 + 6 = **17**
- **C**: 4 + 3 + 7 + 5 + 10 = **29** (C → E → G; C → F → H → J; C → F → I → J; so C + E + G + F + H + I + J = 4+3+6+7+5+4+10 = 39? Let me carefully redo.)

Let us carefully re-derive with the **exact successor sets**:

Successors (including transitive):
- A → {B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J}
- B → {D, G}
- C → {E, F, G, H, I, J}
- D → {G}
- E → {G}
- F → {H, I, J}
- G → {I, J}
- H → {J}
- I → {J}
- J → {}

| Task | Time | All Successors | RPW |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J (6+4+5+3+7+6+5+4+10) | 8 + 50 = **58** |
| B | 6 | D,G (5+6) | 6 + 11 = **17** |
| C | 4 | E,F,G,H,I,J (3+7+6+5+4+10) | 4 + 35 = **39** |
| D | 5 | G (6) | 5 + 6 = **11** |
| E | 3 | G (6) | 3 + 6 = **9** |
| F | 7 | H,I,J (5+4+10) | 7 + 19 = **26** |
| G | 6 | I,J (4+10) | 6 + 14 = **20** |
| H | 5 | J (10) | 5 + 10 = **15** |
| I | 4 | J (10) | 4 + 10 = **14** |
| J | 10 | — | 10 + 0 = **10** |

**Step 4: Sort Tasks in Descending Order of RPW.**

| Rank | Task | RPW | Preds |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 58 | — |
| 2 | C | 39 | A |
| 3 | F | 26 | C |
| 4 | G | 20 | D,E |
| 5 | B | 17 | A |
| 6 | H | 15 | F |
| 7 | I | 14 | F,G |
| 8 | D | 11 | B |
| 9 | E | 9 | C |
| 10 | J | 10 | H,I |

**Step 5: Assign Tasks to Stations (CT = 60 s).**

| Station | Tasks Assigned | Cumulative Time | Idle Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A (8) → C (4) → F (7) → G (6) → B (6) → H (5) | 8+4+7+6+6+5 = 36 | 60-36 = 24 |
| 2 | I (4) → D (5) → E (3) → J (10) | 4+5+3+10 = 22 | 60-22 = 38 |

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — at Station 1, can we fit tasks respecting precedence? A first (no preds). After A, both B and C become eligible. We pick highest RPW: C (39) > B (17). After C, F becomes eligible. F has RPW 26. Add F → cumulative = 8+4+7 = 19. After F, H becomes eligible (RPW 15). After C, also E (RPW 9). Highest among eligible: G (20) is not yet eligible — needs D and E. So among B, H, E, I, D, J — we pick B (17). Cumulative = 26. Then eligible: D, H, E, I. Pick H (15) → cumulative = 31. Then eligible: I (14), D (11), E (9). Pick I (14) → cumulative = 45. Then eligible: D, E. Pick D (11) → cumulative = 56. Then E (9) → would exceed 60 (56+9=65). So D cannot fit with E in this order. Let me redo properly.

Let us redo Station 1 cleanly:

- Place A (8s). Cumulative = 8. Free time = 52.
- Eligible (predecessors satisfied): B, C. Higher RPW: **C (39)**. Place C. Cumulative = 12. Free = 48.
- Eligible: B, E, F. RPW: B(17), E(9), F(26). Pick **F (26)**. Cumulative = 19. Free = 41.
- Eligible: B, E, H, I. RPW: B(17), E(9), H(15), I(14). Pick **B (17)**. Cumulative = 25. Free = 35.
- Eligible: D, E, H, I. RPW: D(11), E(9), H(15), I(14). Pick **H (15)**. Cumulative = 40. Free = 20.
- Eligible: D, E, I. RPW: D(11), E(9), I(14). Pick **I (14)**. Cumulative = 54. Free = 6.
- Eligible: D, E. Neither (11 or 9) fits in 6 s. **Close Station 1.** Station 1 holds: A, C, F, B, H, I. Time = 54 s. Idle = 6 s.

| Station | Tasks | Total Time (s) | Idle Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, C, F, B, H, I | 8+4+7+6+5+4 = 34 | Wait — 8+4=12, +7=19, +6=25, +5=30, +4=34. Idle = 26. |

Correcting arithmetic:

**Station 1:** A(8) + C(4) + F(7) + B(6) + H(5) + I(4) = 34 s. Idle = 60-34 = **26 s**.

**Station 2:** Remaining tasks: D, E, G, J.
- Place D (5). Cumul = 5. Eligible after D: G.
- Place G (6). Cumul = 11. Eligible after G: J.
- Place J (10). Cumul = 21. Eligible after J: (none). E (3) — predecessor of E is C, which is in Station 1, so E is still eligible. Place E (3). Cumul = 24. Idle = 60-24 = **36 s**.

> [!WARNING]
> Hmm — we need to verify G's eligibility: G needs D AND E. D is done (cumul 5), but E not yet done (cumul 5). So G is NOT eligible after D. Let me redo Station 2:

**Station 2 (Redo):**
- Remaining tasks: D(5), E(3), G(6), J(10).
- Place D (5). Cumul = 5. Eligible: E (C done). G needs D and E — not yet.
- Place E (3). Cumul = 8. Now G is eligible.
- Place G (6). Cumul = 14. Eligible: J (H and I done).
- Place J (10). Cumul = 24. Idle = 60-24 = **36 s**.

Final Line:

| Station | Tasks | Time (s) | Idle (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, C, F, B, H, I | 34 | 26 |
| 2 | D, E, G, J | 24 | 36 |

**Step 6: Compute Line Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N_{actual} \cdot CT} = \frac{58}{2 \times 60} \times 100\% = 48.33\%
$$

**Step 7: Compute Balance Delay.**

$$
BD = 1 - 0.4833 = 0.5167 = 51.67\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A line efficiency of **48.33%** is poor, but mathematically valid. In KTU, do **not** be afraid to report low efficiency values — the question tests your **methodology**, not whether you get a "good" line. The board examiner allocates marks for **correct application** of RPW and **correct formula usage**.

**Step 8: Compute Number of Orders per Year & Cycle Time of Orders (Inventory Tie-in).**

Suppose the product's annual demand $D = 120{,}000$ units, ordering cost $S = ₹250$/order, holding cost $H = ₹20$/unit/year:

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 120{,}000 \times 250}{20}} = \sqrt{3{,}000{,}000} = 1732.05 \text{ units}
$$

Number of orders per year:

$$
N_{orders} = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{120{,}000}{1732.05} \approx 69.3 \text{ orders/year}
$$

Minimum total variable cost:

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 120{,}000 \times 250 \times 20} = \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^9} = ₹34{,}641.02
$$

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** No physical drawings are required in the answer sheet, but examiners **appreciate** well-drawn flowcharts and block diagrams for full marks in 14-mark theory questions. The four Mermaid diagrams below cover the four most-asked paradigms.

---

## 4.1 Lean Manufacturing — The 5-Principle House

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCust["CUSTOMER VALUE"]
    nodeStream["VALUE STREAM MAPPING"]
    nodeFlow["CONTINUOUS FLOW"]
    nodePull["PULL SYSTEM"]
    nodePerf["PERFECTION KAIZEN"]
    nodeFdn["FOUNDATION: TPM 5S STANDARD WORK JIT"]
    nodeGoal["ELIMINATE 7 WASTES TIMWOOD"]

    nodeCust --> nodeStream
    nodeStream --> nodeFlow
    nodeFlow --> nodePull
    nodePull --> nodePerf
    nodePerf --> nodeCust
    nodeFdn --> nodeCust
    nodeFdn --> nodeStream
    nodeFdn --> nodeFlow
    nodeFdn --> nodePull
    nodeFdn --> nodePerf
    nodeGoal --> nodeStream
    nodeGoal --> nodeFlow
    nodeGoal --> nodePull
```

**How to draw this in an exam (no software):** Draw a triangular arrow chain: **Value → Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection**, looping back to Value. Underneath, draw a wide base labelled *"Foundation: 5S, TPM, Standard Work"*. On the right, list the **7 wastes (TIMWOOD)** with a cross (✗) symbol indicating they are to be eliminated.

---

## 4.2 Toyota Production System (TPS) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeJust["JUST IN TIME"]
    nodeAuto["JIDOKA AUTONOMATION"]
    nodeKai["KAIZEN CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT"]
    nodeHei["HEIJUNKA LEVEL PRODUCTION"]
    nodeKan["KANBAN PULL SIGNAL"]
    nodeSMED["SMED QUICK SETUP"]
    nodePoka["POKA YOKE ERROR PROOFING"]
    nodeAndon["ANDON VISUAL ALERT"]
    nodeStd["STANDARDIZED WORK"]
    nodeCust["CUSTOMER SATISFACTION"]

    nodeJust --> nodeCust
    nodeAuto --> nodeCust
    nodeKai --> nodeCust
    nodeHei --> nodeJust
    nodeKan --> nodeJust
    nodeSMED --> nodeHei
    nodePoka --> nodeAuto
    nodeAndon --> nodeAuto
    nodeStd --> nodeHei
    nodeStd --> nodeJust
    nodeKai --> nodeStd
```

**Exam Tip:** The "House of TPS" has **two pillars** — *Just-in-Time* and *Jidoka (Autonomation)* — and a *roof* called *Customer Satisfaction*. The *foundation* is *Heijunka* (level scheduling), and *Standardized Work* + *Kaizen* run through the middle.

---

## 4.3 Reconfigurable Manufacturing System (RMS) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput["MARKET DEMAND FLUCTUATION"]
    nodeRqmt["NEW PRODUCT REQUIREMENT"]
    nodeCtrl["RMS CONTROLLER SOFTWARE"]
    nodeMod1["MODULE A PROCESSING"]
    nodeMod2["MODULE B ASSEMBLY"]
    nodeMod3["MODULE C INSPECTION"]
    nodeMod4["MODULE D PACKAGING"]
    nodeOut["RECONFIGURED OUTPUT"]
    nodeDiag["DIAGNOSTICS OPEN ARCHITECTURE"]

    nodeInput --> nodeCtrl
    nodeRqmt --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl --> nodeMod1
    nodeCtrl --> nodeMod2
    nodeCtrl --> nodeMod3
    nodeCtrl --> nodeMod4
    nodeMod1 --> nodeOut
    nodeMod2 --> nodeOut
    nodeMod3 --> nodeOut
    nodeMod4 --> nodeOut
    nodeDiag -.monitors.-> nodeMod1
    nodeDiag -.monitors.-> nodeMod2
    nodeDiag -.monitors.-> nodeMod3
    nodeDiag -.monitors.-> nodeMod4
```

**Six RMS Characteristics (Koren et al.):** **M**odularity, **I**ntegrability, **C**ustomized Flexibility, **S**calability, **C**onvertibility, **D**iagnosability → remember the mnemonic **MICSD** (or "**MICS D**" — pronounced "mixed").

---

## 4.4 Inventory Flow in EOQ Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSup["SUPPLIER"]
    nodeInv["INVENTORY WAREHOUSE Q UNITS"]
    nodeProd["PRODUCTION CONSUMPTION"]
    nodeCust["CUSTOMER DEMAND D PER YEAR"]
    nodeROP["REORDER POINT ROP d L plus SS"]
    nodeOrder["REORDER Q STAR UNITS"]
    nodeCycle["CYCLE TIME T D DIVIDED BY Q STAR"]

    nodeSup --->|"arrival Q"| nodeInv
    nodeInv --->|"usage"| nodeProd
    nodeProd --->|"ship"| nodeCust
    nodeCust -.->|"triggers"| nodeROP
    nodeROP --->|"signal"| nodeOrder
    nodeOrder --->|"places"| nodeSup
    nodeInv -.->|"cycle repeats"| nodeCycle
    nodeCycle -.-> nodeOrder
```

**Key observation:** Inventory rises to $Q$ on replenishment, then falls linearly to 0 over the cycle. The **average inventory is $Q/2$**, which is why holding cost is $H \cdot Q / 2$.

---

## 4.5 Mass Customization Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeMod1["MODULE PLATFORM COMMON BASE"]
    nodeMod2["MODULE OPTION A"]
    nodeMod3["MODULE OPTION B"]
    nodeMod4["MODULE OPTION C"]
    nodeVar1["PRODUCT VARIANT 1"]
    nodeVar2["PRODUCT VARIANT 2"]
    nodeVar3["PRODUCT VARIANT 3"]
    nodeVarN["PRODUCT VARIANT N"]
    nodeIT["IT CONFIGURATOR WEB PORTAL"]
    nodeCust["CUSTOMER ORDER"]

    nodeCust --> nodeIT
    nodeIT --> nodeMod1
    nodeIT --> nodeMod2
    nodeIT --> nodeMod3
    nodeIT --> nodeMod4
    nodeMod1 --> nodeVar1
    nodeMod2 --> nodeVar2
    nodeMod3 --> nodeVar3
    nodeMod4 --> nodeVarN
```

**Principle:** A *common platform* + *customizable modules* + *IT configurator* = Mass Customization. The platform absorbs the *common cost*; the modules carry the *customer-specific* feature.
