# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A = 3 marks × 4 to 5 questions (short). Part B = 14 marks × 2 to 3 questions (with internal choice, 7+7 sub-parts). Module 1 typically contributes **1 Part A + 1 Part B** in the full paper.

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer, ~60 words)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Lean Manufacturing. List the seven wastes (TIMWOOD) it seeks to eliminate.**

**Model Answer:**

> Lean Manufacturing is a systematic methodology aimed at the **elimination of waste (Muda)** and the creation of value for the customer, derived from the Toyota Production System.
>
> The **seven wastes (TIMWOOD)** are:
> 1. **T**ransportation
> 2. **I**nventory
> 3. **M**otion
> 4. **W**aiting
> 5. **O**ver-production
> 6. **O**ver-processing
> 7. **D**efects
>
> *(Modern extensions add an 8th: Non-utilized Talent.)*
>
> **Marks allocation:** Definition 1 mark + listing wastes 1.5 marks + accuracy 0.5 mark.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is Just-in-Time (JIT)? State two advantages and one limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

> JIT is a **pull-based production philosophy** that produces only what is needed, when it is needed, in the quantity needed.
>
> **Advantages:** (i) Drastically reduces inventory carrying cost; (ii) Improves quality by exposing defects quickly (small lots surface problems faster).
>
> **Limitation:** Highly vulnerable to **supply chain disruptions** — a single delayed shipment halts the entire production line (as seen during COVID-19 in 2020–21).
>
> **Marks allocation:** Definition 1.5 + advantages 1 + limitation 0.5.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Long Answer with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. Discuss the role of **Jidoka** and **Just-in-Time** as its two pillars. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A product requires **8 tasks** with the following precedence and times:

| Task | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time (s) | 10 | 8 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 5 | 4 | 11 |
| Preds | — | A | A | B | B | C | D, E | F, G |

If the **cycle time is 25 seconds**, find:
- (i) The **number of workstations** using the **RPW heuristic**.
- (ii) The **line efficiency** and **balance delay**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

The Toyota Production System (TPS) rests on **two pillars** and a **foundation**, with a *roof* of customer satisfaction.

**Pillar 1: Just-in-Time (JIT).** Produces only what is pulled by the next operation. Enabled by:
- **Heijunka** (level production scheduling)
- **Kanban** (visual pull signals — cards or electronic)
- **SMED** (Single Minute Exchange of Die) for quick changeovers
- **Takt Time** matching production rate to demand rate

**Pillar 2: Jidoka (Autonomation).** "Automation with a human touch." The machine **automatically stops** when it detects an abnormality, preventing defective production. Tools:
- **Andon** — visual signal board (lights/alarms) at every station
- **Poka-Yoke** — error-proofing devices (mistake-proofing jigs)
- **Quality at the source** — every worker is empowered to stop the line

**Foundation:** Heijunka (level production), Standardized Work, Kaizen (continuous improvement), 5S workplace organization.

**Roof:** Customer Satisfaction — the ultimate goal.

**Marks allocation:** TPS overview 2 + JIT pillar 1.5 + Jidoka pillar 1.5 + Foundation/Roof 1 + Neat block diagram 1.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Cycle Time.**

Given: $CT = 25$ s (no need to compute from demand).

**Step 2: Compute Ranked Positional Weights (RPW).**

Successor sets (transitive closure):
- A → {B, C, D, E, F, G, H}
- B → {D, E, G, H}
- C → {F, H}
- D → {G, H}
- E → {G, H}
- F → {H}
- G → {H}
- H → {}

| Task | Time | All Successors' Times | RPW |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 10 | 8+6+9+7+5+4+11 = 50 | 10+50 = **60** |
| B | 8 | 9+7+4+11 = 31 | 8+31 = **39** |
| C | 6 | 5+11 = 16 | 6+16 = **22** |
| D | 9 | 4+11 = 15 | 9+15 = **24** |
| E | 7 | 4+11 = 15 | 7+15 = **22** |
| F | 5 | 11 | 5+11 = **16** |
| G | 4 | 11 | 4+11 = **15** |
| H | 11 | 0 | 11+0 = **11** |

**Step 3: Sort in Descending Order of RPW.**

| Rank | Task | RPW | Preds |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 60 | — |
| 2 | B | 39 | A |
| 3 | D | 24 | B |
| 4 | C | 22 | A |
| 5 | E | 22 | B |
| 6 | F | 16 | C |
| 7 | G | 15 | D, E |
| 8 | H | 11 | F, G |

**Step 4: Assign to Stations (CT = 25 s).**

| Station | Tasks (in order) | Cumulative Time (s) | Idle (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A (10) → B (8) → C (6) | 10+8+6 = 24 | 25-24 = 1 |
| 2 | D (9) → E (7) | 9+7 = 16 | 25-16 = 9 |
| 3 | F (5) → G (4) → H (11) | 5+4+11 = 20 | 25-20 = 5 |

Verification of eligibility:
- **Station 1:** A (no pred) ✓ → B (A done) ✓ → C (A done) ✓. Total = 24 ✓.
- **Station 2:** D (B done) ✓ → E (B done) ✓. F not eligible (C done — yes) but F has RPW 16 < E 22, so we pick E. Total = 16 ✓.
- **Station 3:** F (C done) ✓ → G (D, E done) ✓ → H (F, G done) ✓. Total = 20 ✓.

**Step 5: Compute Line Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{\sum t_i}{N \times CT} \times 100\% = \frac{60}{3 \times 25} \times 100\% = \frac{60}{75} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 6: Compute Balance Delay.**

$$
BD = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(i)** Number of workstations = **3**
- **(ii)** Line Efficiency = **80%**, Balance Delay = **20%**

**Marks allocation:** RPW table 2 marks, station assignment 2 marks, efficiency 1.5 marks, balance delay 1 mark, units and labelling 0.5 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Mass Customization**. Differentiate between **Mass Production, Mass Customization, and Custom Production** using a suitable comparison table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory has an **annual demand of 50,000 units** for a component. The **ordering cost is ₹400 per order** and the **holding cost is ₹25 per unit per year**. Compute:
- (i) The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**
- (ii) The **number of orders per year**
- (iii) The **total variable cost** at EOQ
- (iv) If the supplier offers a **5% quantity discount** for orders ≥ 1,500 units, should the factory accept it? Assume unit cost = ₹100. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Mass Customization (MC):** A production strategy that delivers **customized products** at **near-mass-production costs** by combining flexible manufacturing processes, modular product design, and customer-driven configuration.

**The Three Production Strategies:**

| Feature | Mass Production | Mass Customization | Custom Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Customer Touch-point** | At the end (buy what's made) | Throughout (configure & produce) | At the start (specify from scratch) |
| **Volume** | Very High | High | Low |
| **Variety** | Very Low | High | Very High |
| **Cost per unit** | Low | Medium-Low | Very High |
| **Lead time** | Short | Medium | Long |
| **Customer involvement** | None (post-sale) | High (pre-sale) | Maximum |
| **Process type** | Dedicated line | Modular, flexible line | Job shop / project |
| **Example** | Coca-Cola bottling | Nike By You | Custom yacht building |
| **Key enabler** | Standardization | Modular design + IT | Skilled craftsmen |

**Key Trade-off:** Mass Production sacrifices **variety** for cost; Custom Production sacrifices **cost** for variety; Mass Customization achieves both through **modularity + IT + flexible processes**.

**Marks allocation:** Definition 2 + comparison table 3 + trade-off explanation 2.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $D = 50{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹400$/order, $H = ₹25$/unit/year, $C = ₹100$/unit.

**(i) Economic Order Quantity:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 400}{25}} = \sqrt{\frac{40{,}000{,}000}{25}} = \sqrt{1{,}600{,}000} = 1264.91 \text{ units}
$$

**$Q^* \approx 1265$ units.**

**(ii) Number of Orders per Year:**

$$
N_{orders} = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{50{,}000}{1264.91} = 39.53 \approx 40 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**(iii) Total Variable Cost at EOQ:**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 400 \times 25} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000{,}000} = ₹31{,}622.78
$$

Plus purchase cost: $C \cdot D = 100 \times 50{,}000 = ₹50{,}00{,}000$.
**Total cost = ₹50,31,622.78 per year** (variable + purchase).

**(iv) Should the factory accept the 5% quantity discount for orders of ≥ 1,500 units?**

At $Q = 1500$, unit cost = $100 \times 0.95 = ₹95$. Holding cost recalculated: $H = 0.25 \times 95 = ₹23.75$/unit/year (assuming 25% holding rate, though sometimes H is given directly; we will use direct value $H = ₹25$ as in problem).

**Total cost with discount at $Q = 1500$:**

Holding cost:

$$
TC_h = \frac{H \cdot Q}{2} = \frac{25 \times 1500}{2} = ₹18{,}750
$$

Ordering cost:

$$
TC_s = \frac{DS}{Q} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 400}{1500} = ₹13{,}333.33
$$

Purchase cost with discount:

$$
TC_p = D \times C_{new} = 50{,}000 \times 95 = ₹47{,}50{,}000
$$

**Total cost (discounted):**

$$
TC_{discount} = 18{,}750 + 13{,}333.33 + 47{,}50{,}000 = ₹47{,}82{,}083.33
$$

**Total cost at EOQ (from part iii):**

$$
TC_{EOQ} = 31{,}622.78 + 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹50{,}31{,}622.78
$$

**Savings with discount:**

$$
\Delta TC = 50{,}31{,}622.78 - 47{,}82{,}083.33 = ₹2{,}49{,}539.45 \text{ saved per year}
$$

**Decision:** **Yes**, the factory should accept the quantity discount. The savings of **₹2,49,539.45 per year** are significant (≈ 5% of total purchase cost). Even though $Q = 1500$ is not the EOQ, the **discount on unit cost** more than offsets the **slight increase in holding cost**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students compute EOQ correctly but forget the **purchase cost** when comparing total costs in part (iv). Always include $D \times C$ in the comparison.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Students use the *old* $H$ when the unit cost changes. Strictly, $H = i \times C$, so $H$ should be recalculated as $i \times C_{new}$. If the problem gives $H$ directly, use it as-is. **Read the question carefully!**
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Students write the EOQ formula without the **square root** or omit the **2 in the numerator**. Marks are deducted for formula errors.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In assembly line balancing, students forget to **verify precedence** when assigning tasks. Marks are lost if a task appears in a station before its predecessor.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Reporting line efficiency in **decimal (0.80)** instead of **percentage (80%)**. The question always asks for percentage — read the units!

**Marks allocation:** EOQ formula 1, calculation 1, number of orders 1, total cost 1, discount analysis 2, decision 0.5, units/labelling 0.5.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your study wall one day before the exam.**

- [x] **Lean Manufacturing** = Waste elimination; 7 wastes = **TIMWOOD**; origin = **Toyota Production System (TPS)**; key thinkers = **Ohno, Womack, Jones**.
- [x] **Mass Customization** = Low cost + high variety; enablers = **modular design + IT configurator + flexible processes**; 4 archetypes = **Collaborative, Adaptive, Cosmetic, Transparent**.
- [x] **Green Manufacturing** = Sustainable production; 4R = **Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover**; standards = **ISO 14001**.
- [x] **TQM** = Customer-focused continuous improvement involving *all* employees; gurus = **Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Taguchi, Feigenbaum**; tools = **PDCA, 7 QC tools, QCC, Fishbone**.
- [x] **Agile Manufacturing** = Quick response to *unpredictable* changes; 4 capabilities = **Mastery, Robustness, Customization, Time-to-Market**; **embraces** variability (Lean eliminates it).
- [x] **JIT** = Pull-based, small lots, Kanban signals, SMED setups, **zero inventory ideal**; vulnerable to supply disruptions.
- [x] **OEM** = Original Equipment Manufacturer — the *fabricator* (e.g., Foxconn makes iPhones for Apple, who is the OBM).
- [x] **RMS** = Reconfigurable Manufacturing System — six characteristics = **Modularity, Integrability, Customized Flexibility, Scalability, Convertibility, Diagnosability** (mnemonic: **MICSD**).
- [x] **Assembly Lines** = Sequential workstations on a flow path; key formulas: **CT, N_min, η, BD, RPW, SI**; balancing by **RPW heuristic**.
- [x] **Inventory** = 7 types (RM, WIP, FG, MRO, SS, Pipeline, Anticipation); 4 cost components (**H, S, P·D, Stockout**); EOQ = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$; ROP = $dL + SS$.
- [x] **Takt Time** = Available time / Demand — the heartbeat of Lean/JIT.
- [x] **EOQ total cost** at optimal quantity = $\sqrt{2DSH}$ — *always* report this in exams.
- [x] **Line efficiency** = (Sum of task times) / (N × CT); perfect line has η = 100%; balance delay = 1 - η.
- [x] **Service-level Z-values** (for safety stock): 90% → 1.28; 95% → 1.65; 99% → 2.33.
- [x] **For 14-mark questions:** always show the **assumption, the formula, the substitution, the calculation, the answer with units, and a final decision statement**.
- [x] **Last-day Mantra:** *Definitions carry marks; formulas carry more marks; neat diagrams carry full marks; neatness is not graded, but examiners are humans.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Glass and Ceramic Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Glass and Ceramic Manufacturing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Glass — Formal Definition **Glass** is an amorphous, non-crystalline solid material — typically derived from silica (SiO₂) — that exhibits a random, disordered 3-D network structure (the **continuous random network**, CRN model by Zachariasen) and undergoes a glass transition rather than a discrete melting point. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Glass is a **supercooled liquid** in solid form. The structure has short-range order (Si–O tetrahedra) but **no long-range periodic order** (unlike crystalline ceramics). This is why it has **no sharp melting point** — it softens gradually over a range (the *transformation range*, typically 500 °C – 700 °C for soda-lime glass). **Principal constituents of commercial glass:** | Constituent | Function | Typical wt% (Soda-lime) | |---|---|---| | **Silica (SiO₂)** — network former | Forms the backbone | **69 – 74 %** | | **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃)** — flux | Lowers melting point | **12 – 16 %** | | **Limestone (CaCO₃)** — stabilizer | Prevents water attack | **5 – 12 %** | | **Cullet** (recycled glass) | Saves energy | **15 – 30 %** | | Minor additives | Colouring / decolourising / refining | < 5 % | > [!NOTE] > **Why cullet?** Adding pre-melted glass scraps reduces the energy required to melt the batch by roughly **1.2 % for every 10 % cullet** added (industry benchmark). It is both economical and environmentally necessary. ## 1.2 Ceramics — Formal Definition **Ceramics** are inorganic, non-metallic solids composed of metallic and non-metallic elements held together by **ionic and/or covalent bonds**. They are generally classified into: - **Traditional (clay-based) ceramics** — bricks, tiles, pottery, porcelain. - **Advanced / Engineering ceramics** — Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂, Si₃N₄ used for wear, heat, and electronics applications. **KTU-relevant definition:** A ceramic part is produced by **shaping a powder compact** and then **densifying it through high-temperature firing (sintering)**, where atomic diffusion bonds the particles into a polycrystalline mass. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ### 🍯 Glass — "Frozen Honey" Imagine **honey** slowly cooling. Instead of crystallising into a rigid, ordered lattice (like sugar candy), the molecules just slow down and *jam* into a disordered, frozen state. Glass is essentially **sand (silica) that has been melted, then cooled too fast for its atoms to organise into crystals** — producing transparency because light is not scattered by grain boundaries. ### 🧱 Ceramic — "Sintered Snowball" Picture making a **snowball by pressing loose snowflakes** together. Individually they are weak, but apply gentle pressure and time, and surface melting (or in ceramics, atomic diffusion) welds them into a solid block. Sintering in ceramics works exactly the same way: **heat + time + contact = bonded, dense ceramic body**. ## 1.4 Common Important Properties to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Property snapshot (must-memorise for KTU):** > - **Glass:** transparent, brittle, isotropic, no cleavage, softens gradually, poor conductor (band gap ≈ 9 eV). > - **Ceramics:** high hardness (Mohs 7 – 9), high melting point (> 1500 °C), low thermal expansion (Al₂O₃ ≈ 8 × 10⁻⁶ /K), **brittle** (low fracture toughness, K_IC ≈ 3 – 5 MPa·m^(1/2) for Al₂O₃). > - **Thermal shock resistance parameter:** R = σ_f·k / (α·E) — a high R is desirable; **glass has low R**, **SiC and Si₃N₄ have high R**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Glass — The Manufacturing Pipeline The glass manufacturing chain is a classic **continuous flow process** and KTU loves the four-stage flow: ### Stage 1: **Batching & Weighing** - Raw materials (silica sand, soda ash, limestone, cullet) are precisely weighed. - Mixed in a **dry or wet mixer** to ensure homogeneity. - Moisture content: typically **3 – 5 %** for wet batch. ### Stage 2: **Melting (Furnace)** - Carried out in a **regenerative / recuperative furnace** at **1400 – 1600 °C**. - The silica network breaks down (depolymerisation); CO₂ evolves from carbonates. - Refining stage removes bubbles; homogenisation evens out composition. - Heat source: natural gas or electric boosting (oxy-fuel gives lower NOₓ). ### Stage 3: **Forming** The molten glass (working range ~ 10⁴ poise) is shaped. Main processes: | Process | Principle | Product | |---|---|---| | **Float Process** | Glass floats on molten tin | Flat glass (sheets) | | **Blowing** | Air pressure into gob | Bottles, jars | | **Drawing** | Pulled upward / downward | Sheet glass, fibres | | **Pressing** | Plunger into mould | Tableware, lenses | | **Rolling** | Passed through rolls | Patterned / wired glass | | **Centrifugal** | Rotating mould | TV screens, insulators | ### Stage 4: **Annealing (Ladle to Lehr)** - Glass is cooled slowly through the **annealing point** (~ 10¹³ poise) to relieve residual stresses. - **Annealing temperature** of soda-lime glass ≈ **550 °C**; strain point ≈ **510 °C**. > [!NOTE] > **Lehr** is a long, temperature-controlled oven (typically 30 – 60 m) that provides controlled cooling. Skipping the lehr creates **"chill"** — internal stress that may cause spontaneous fracture. ## 2.2 Ceramics — The Manufacturing Pipeline ### Stage 1: **Powder Preparation** - Raw materials (clay, Al₂O₃, SiC, etc.) are ground in **ball mills, attrition mills, or jet mills**. - Particle size: typically **0.5 – 50 µm** for traditional ceramics; **sub-micron** for advanced ceramics. - A **binder** (PVA, PEG) is added to give green strength. ### Stage 2: **Shaping / Forming** | Forming Method | Principle | Typical Product | |---|---|---| | **Dry Pressing** | Powder compacted in rigid die under 10 – 100 MPa | Tiles, refractories | | **Isostatic Pressing** | Pressure from fluid (cold / hot) — uniform density | Spark plugs, insulators | | **Extrusion** | Plastic mass forced through die | Bricks, pipes, rods | | **Slip Casting** | Suspension poured in porous mould drains water | Sanitaryware, figurines | | **Injection Moulding** | Heated feedstock injected | Turbine blades, medical parts | | **Jiggering** | Profile of rotating mould by heated tool | Tableware | ### Stage 3: **Drying** Critical for clay-based parts to avoid **drying cracks** (because of differential shrinkage). - Air drying → humidity drying → final drying. - **Linear drying shrinkage:** S = (L_wet − L_dry) / L_wet — typically **5 – 8 %** for clay bodies. ### Stage 4: **Firing / Sintering** - Green compact is heated to **0.5 – 0.85 of the melting point (T_m in K)**. - Atomic diffusion mechanisms eliminate pores and bond particles. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Note | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Glass viscosity vs temperature (Fulcher equation)** | $\log_{10}\eta = A + \dfrac{B}{T - T_0}$ | η in poise, T in K | | 2 | **Working point** | η ≈ 10⁴ poise | Glass formable | | 3 | **Softening point (Littleton)** | η ≈ 10⁷·⁶ poise | Glass deforms under own weight | | 4 | **Annealing point** | η ≈ 10¹³ poise | Stress relief possible in minutes | | 5 | **Strain point** | η ≈ 10¹⁴·⁵ poise | Permanent stress-free | | 6 | **Float bath temperature** | T ≈ 600 – 650 °C | Molten tin (T_m(Sn) = 232 °C) | | 7 | **Cullet energy saving** | ΔE ≈ 1.2 % per 10 % cullet | Empirical | | 8 | **Sintering — Herring scaling** | $\Delta L / L_0 \propto t^{n}$ | n = 0.31 (evaporation), 0.40 (grain boundary), 0.46 (volume) | | 9 | **Firing shrinkage** | $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | **Bulk density from porosity** | $\rho_b = \rho_{th}(1 - P)$ | P = fractional porosity | | 11 | **Thermal shock parameter** | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot k}{\alpha \cdot E}$ | Higher = better | | 12 | **Glass transition condition** | $\eta = 10^{12}$ Pa·s | Definition of T_g | | 13 | **Coble creep (crystalline ceramics)** | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{A \cdot \sigma \cdot \Omega \cdot \delta \cdot D_{gb}}{k_B \cdot T \cdot d^3}$ | Grain-boundary diffusion | | 14 | **Sintering activation energy (Arrhenius)** | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K | | 15 | **Modulus of rupture (MOR) for ceramics** | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3F L}{2 b d^2}$ | 3-point bend test | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU trap:** Do not confuse **annealing point (10¹³ poise)** with **softening point (10⁷·⁶ poise)**. The annealing point is the temperature at which internal stress is relieved in *minutes*; softening is when the glass visibly sags. ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Flat glass (float):** The most widely produced glass (≈ 70 % of all flat glass) — used for automotive and architectural glazing. - **Gorilla Glass / aluminosilicate:** Chemically strengthened glass for smartphone covers. - **Alumina (Al₂O₃) ceramics:** Cutting tool inserts, hip implants, spark plug insulators. - **SiC / Si₃N₄:** High-temperature gas-turbine blades, bearings, heat exchangers. - **Piezoelectric ceramics (PZT):** Sensors, actuators, ultrasound transducers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Herring's Sintering Scaling Law (Volume Diffusion) > **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** It links **time, temperature, and particle size** to the linear shrinkage during sintering — a frequent 14-mark question. Consider two adjacent spherical particles of radius $a$, in contact at a "neck" of radius $x$. The chemical-potential gradient between the neck (high curvature, tensile stress) and the particle surface drives vacancy flux **into** the neck. **Step 1 — Gibbs–Thomson (Laplace) stress at the neck** The hydrostatic tensile stress that develops at the neck surface of curvature $\rho$ is: $$ \sigma_{neck} = \gamma_s \left(\dfrac{1}{x} - \dfrac{1}{\rho}\right) $$ For an early-stage sintering geometry, $x \gg \rho$, so the term $1/x$ is dropped: $$ \sigma_{neck} \approx -\dfrac{\gamma_s}{\rho} $$ The negative sign indicates **tensile** (pulling) stress on the neck surface. **Step 2 — Vacancy concentration at the neck** Vacancies are in equilibrium at concentration $C_0$ (under zero stress). Under the tensile stress $\sigma_{neck}$, the equilibrium concentration becomes (Boltzmann): $$ C_{neck} = C_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{neck} \Omega}{k_B T}\right) $$ Because $\sigma_{neck} \Omega \ll k_B T$ in sintering, linearise the exponential: $$ C_{neck} \approx C_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{\sigma_{neck} \Omega}{k_B T}\right) $$ The concentration **gradient** between neck and flat surface (no stress) is therefore: $$ \Delta C \approx \dfrac{C_0 \sigma_{neck} \Omega}{k_B T} = -\dfrac{C_0 \gamma_s \Omega}{k_B T \rho} $$ **Step 3 — Vacancy flux (Fick's first law)** $$ J_v = -D_v \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} \approx \dfrac{D_v C_0 \gamma_s \Omega}{k_B T \rho \, \Delta x} $$ Taking the diffusion distance $\Delta x \approx \rho$ (order of magnitude): $$ J_v \approx \dfrac{D_v C_0 \gamma_s \Omega}{k_B T \rho^2} $$ **Step 4 — Volume of material transported to the neck** The number of vacancies arriving per unit time is $J_v A$, and the volume removed at the particle surface is filled by atomic flux from the grain boundary to the neck. Equating volumes leads (after geometric substitution of neck radius in terms of $\rho$) to the **Herring scaling law**: $$ \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \left(\dfrac{K \, t}{a^m \, T}\right)^{n} $$ where $K$ depends on the dominant diffusion mechanism: | Mechanism | m | n | |---|---|---| | Volume diffusion (Nabarro–Herring) | 3 | 0.466 | | Grain-boundary diffusion (Coble) | 4 | 0.40 | | Surface diffusion (no densification) | 4 | 0.31 | | Viscous flow (amorphous) | 1 | 1.0 | **Key engineering conclusion (what KTU wants):** - Doubling particle size **quadruples** the time required to reach the same shrinkage (since $t \propto a^m$). - Halving particle size from 10 µm → 5 µm reduces sintering time by a factor of $2^3 = 8$ (volume diffusion). > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to use this on a KTU exam:** If a problem states, *"Sintering takes 4 h with 10 µm powder; how long with 20 µm powder assuming volume diffusion?"*, apply $t_2 / t_1 = (a_2 / a_1)^3 = 2^3 = 8$ → answer = 32 h. Always show the table above in your answer. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Float Glass Sheet Production Rate A float-glass line produces flat glass of width $w = 3.0$ m and thickness $h = 6$ mm, moving off the tin bath at velocity $V = 0.012$ m/s. Find the **mass flow rate of glass** (in kg/s) and the **annual production** (tonnes/year, 90 % uptime). Take glass density $\rho_g = 2500$ kg/m³. **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the ribbon** $$ A = w \times h = 3.0 \times 0.006 = 0.018 \ \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate** $$ \dot{V} = A \times V = 0.018 \times 0.012 = 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Mass flow rate** $$ \dot{m} = \rho_g \dot{V} = 2500 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} = 0.54 \ \text{kg/s} $$ **Step 4 — Annual production (with 90 % uptime)** Operating hours per year = $24 \times 365 \times 0.90 = 7884$ h $$ M_{annual} = 0.54 \times 3600 \times 7884 = 1.5326 \times 10^7 \ \text{kg} \approx 15{,}326 \ \text{tonnes} $$ > [!NOTE] > A **single modern float line** typically produces **150 – 800 tonnes/day** depending on ribbon width. Our value of ~ 46.6 t/day (assuming 24 h continuous, no uptime loss) is on the low side — increase $w$ to 4 m to reach industrial norms. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Glass vs. Ceramics | Parameter | Glass (Soda-lime) | Ceramic (Al₂O₃) | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | Atomic order | Amorphous | Polycrystalline | Glass transparent; ceramic opaque (grain-boundary scattering) | | Bond type | Covalent + ionic | Ionic / covalent | Both brittle; high stiffness | | Density (kg/m³) | 2500 | 3970 | Alumina is heavier — important in aerospace | | Young's modulus (GPa) | 70 | 380 | Ceramic is ~ 5× stiffer | | Fracture toughness K_IC (MPa·m^(1/2)) | 0.7 – 0.8 | 3 – 5 | Glass much more fragile | | Hardness (Mohs) | 5.5 | 9 | Alumina scratch-resistant | | Thermal expansion α (10⁻⁶/K) | 9 | 8 | Similar; SiC only 4 | | Max service T (°C) | ~ 500 | ~ 1750 | Ceramic for high-T | | Manufacturing route | Melting + forming | Powder + sintering | Glass easier, ceramic cheaper raw mat. | | Forming method | Float, blow, draw | Press, extrude, slip-cast | Different machinery | | Typical defect | Bubbles, stones | Porosity, cracks | Different QA methods | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Slip-Casting Time Calculation A plaster mould of wall thickness $t_m = 25$ mm is used to cast a sanitaryware piece. The slip water-content is $W_0 = 35$ %. Empirical constant $K_{cast} = 1.6$ mm/s^(1/2). Find the casting time for a 5 mm wall thickness. **Step 1 — Use the slip-casting law (square-root of time kinetics):** $$ L = K_{cast} \sqrt{t_c} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for casting time:** $$ t_c = \left(\dfrac{L}{K_{cast}}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{5}{1.6}\right)^2 = 9.766 \ \text{s} $$ **Step 3 — In practice, the demoulding time is much longer (~ 30 – 60 min)** because the cast wall continues to thicken by capillary suction until equilibrium is reached. The above is the **time to reach the desired wall thickness** before demoulding. > [!NOTE] > Industrial slip casting of a wash basin typically uses $t_m = 40$–$60$ mm mould walls and a casting time of **45 – 90 min** for an 8 – 10 mm wall. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Glass Manufacturing Flow (Float Process) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material
Storage] --> B[Batch Weighing
and Mixing] B --> C[Batch Silo] C --> D[Furnace
Melting 1450 C] D --> E[Refining and
Homogenisation] E --> F[Floating on
Molten Tin Bath] F --> G[Annealing Lehr
Controlled Cooling] G --> H[Cutting and
Inspection] H --> I[Warehouse
Dispatch] subgraph SideLoop S1[Glass Cullet
Recycling] -.-> D end subgraph Energy E1[Regenerator
Waste Heat] -.-> D end ``` **How to read this for KTU:** - Main path = **batching → melting → forming → annealing → cutting**. - Side loop = cullet re-injection (energy saving). - Energy recovery = **regenerative chamber** captures flue-gas heat to preheat combustion air (efficiency ≈ 60 – 70 %). ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ceramic Manufacturing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Raw Material
Crushing] --> P2[Ball Milling
and Sizing] P2 --> P3[Slip or Powder
Preparation with Binder] P3 --> P4A[Shaping Method A
Dry Pressing] P3 --> P4B[Shaping Method B
Slip Casting] P3 --> P4C[Shaping Method C
Extrusion] P4A --> P5[Drying] P4B --> P5 P4C --> P5 P5 --> P6[Glazing
Optional] P6 --> P7[Sintering / Firing
1100 to 1700 C] P7 --> P8[Cooling] P8 --> P9[Inspection
and Finishing] P9 --> P10[Packaging] ``` **Reading tip:** Note the **branching forming stage** — students often miss that *shaping* has multiple alternatives, not a single process. ## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Architecture — Sintering Mechanisms Map ```mermaid flowchart TB start[Sintering Heat Applied] --> p1[Particle Contact
Neck Formation] p1 --> p2{Dominant
Mechanism} p2 --> m1[Volume Diffusion
Nabarro Herring] p2 --> m2[Grain Boundary
Diffusion Coble] p2 --> m3[Surface Diffusion
No Densification] p2 --> m4[Viscous Flow
Amorphous] m1 --> out1[Densification
Pore Elimination] m2 --> out1 m3 --> out2[Grain Growth
No Shrinkage] m4 --> out1 out1 --> fin[Dense Sintered Body] out2 --> fin ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanism selection logic (KTU favourite):** > - **Volume diffusion** dominates for *large grains, high T, long time*. > - **Grain-boundary diffusion** dominates for *fine grains, lower T*. > - **Surface diffusion** causes *neck growth WITHOUT shrinkage* → undesirable if you need densification. > - **Viscous flow** is the mechanism for *amorphous ceramics and glass-ceramics*. ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Float Glass Cooling Through Transformation Range ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Molten Molten --> Working: eta 10 4 poise Working --> Softening: eta 10 7.6 poise Softening --> Annealing: eta 10 13 poise Annealing --> Strain: eta 10 14.5 poise Strain --> Rigid: T below Tg Rigid --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **transformation range** is between *softening* and *strain* points. It is the critical region in which the lehr must closely control cooling rate (typically 1 – 5 °C/min for soda-lime). Too fast = residual stress; too slow = production loss. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **Differentiate between the "annealing point" and "softening point" of glass. Why is the lehr temperature profile critical?** **Model Answer:** | Property | Annealing Point | Softening Point | |---|---|---| | Viscosity | 10¹³ poise | 10⁷·⁶ poise | | Temperature (soda-lime) | ~ 550 °C | ~ 720 °C | | Behaviour | Internal stress relieved in minutes | Glass flows under its own weight | | Significance | Used to set the upper lehr temperature | Glass is formable at this viscosity | The lehr must keep the glass within the transformation range (between strain and softening) at a controlled cooling rate of **1 – 5 °C/min** so that thermal-gradient-induced residual stresses are minimised. If cooled too fast, the surface freezes first, contracts, and puts the core in tension — leading to **spontaneous fracture** ("chill cracks"). *(3 marks)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` **List any three shaping methods used in traditional ceramic manufacturing and state the product form best suited to each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Dry pressing** — best for simple, axisymmetric parts such as *tiles, electrical insulators*. 2. **Slip casting** — best for *hollow, complex shapes* like sanitaryware and figurines. 3. **Extrusion** — best for *long, uniform cross-section* products such as bricks, pipes, and rods. *(Alternative: jiggering for tableware, injection moulding for engineering ceramics.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the float-glass process. List its advantages over the older sheet-drawing process. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principle:** The float process (Pilkington, 1959) produces flat glass by floating a continuous ribbon of molten glass on a bath of **molten tin** under a controlled atmosphere (N₂ + H₂ to prevent Sn oxidation). Surface tension and gravity naturally give a flat, fire-polished surface — no grinding or polishing is required. **Stages (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):** 1. **Batching and weighing** of silica, soda ash, limestone, cullet. 2. **Melting** in a cross-fired regenerative furnace at ~ **1450 – 1550 °C**. 3. **Delivery canal** carries molten glass (η ≈ 10⁴ poise) onto the tin bath. 4. **Float bath** length ≈ 50 – 60 m, temperature ≈ **600 – 650 °C**, glass thickness controlled by **top rollers / tweel** (default 6 mm; thinner by stretching). 5. **Transition rollers** lift the ribbon at ~ 600 °C (viscosity 10⁹ poise) onto rollers. 6. **Annealing lehr** cools glass from 600 °C → 200 °C at a controlled rate. 7. **Cutting and inspection** at the cold end. **Advantages over sheet-drawing (Fourcault, Pittsburgh):** - **Flatter surface** (no draw marks, fire-polished both sides). - **Higher production rate** (one float line ≈ 800 t/day vs 100 – 200 t/day for drawing). - **Lower thickness tolerance** (± 0.2 mm achievable). - **Lower cost per m²** (no grinding or polishing). - **No mark/ripple defects**. **Disadvantages:** high capital cost (~ ₹ 1500 crore per line), sensitivity to tin-bath atmosphere. *(7 marks — 1 mark for principle, 3 for stages, 2 for advantages, 1 for neat diagram.)* **(b) Explain the role of each of the following constituents in glass batches: (i) silica, (ii) soda ash, (iii) limestone, (iv) cullet. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Constituent | Function | Detail | |---|---|---| | (i) **Silica (SiO₂)** | Network former | Forms the 3-D random network of corner-sharing SiO₄ tetrahedra. Provides strength, hardness, chemical durability, and high softening point. **70 – 74 %** of soda-lime glass. | | (ii) **Soda ash (Na₂CO₃)** | Flux | Reduces melting point of pure silica from ~ 1700 °C to ~ 1450 °C by breaking Si–O–Si bridges. Provides Na⁺ ions for easier processing. **12 – 16 %**. | | (iii) **Limestone (CaCO₃)** | Stabiliser | Na⁺ ions alone make glass water-soluble ("water glass"); Ca²⁺ restores durability and chemical resistance. Also reduces devitrification tendency. **5 – 12 %**. | | (iv) **Cullet** | Energy saver & flux | Recycled broken glass added to the batch. Saves energy (**~ 1.2 % per 10 % cullet**), accelerates melting, reduces raw-material cost. **15 – 30 %** typical. | *(7 marks — 1 per constituent, 1 for summary interaction; use a small table like above to get full marks.)* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` *(Internal Choice)* **(a) Describe with a flow diagram the manufacturing process of traditional (clay-based) ceramic products. Mention the role of each stage. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Stages (refer to the flow diagram in Section 4.2):** 1. **Raw-material preparation** — crushing and grinding of clay, silica, feldspar in ball mills to a controlled particle size (typically 50 – 100 µm for traditional ceramics). 2. **Batch mixing & slip preparation** — measured quantities of clay (plastic binder), silica (filler), and feldspar (flux) are mixed. For slip casting, the powder is suspended in water (**35 % water**) with a **deflocculant** (e.g., sodium silicate) to make a pourable slip. 3. **Shaping** — selected method (dry pressing, extrusion, slip casting, jiggering). The choice depends on part geometry and production volume. 4. **Drying** — moisture removed gradually to avoid shrinkage cracks. Drying shrinkage = **5 – 8 %** for clay bodies. 5. **Glazing** (optional) — a thin vitreous coating is applied to the dried body to provide a smooth, decorative, impermeable surface. 6. **Firing / Sintering** — the body is heated to **1000 – 1300 °C** for traditional ceramics (porcelain ~ 1400 °C). Vitrification (formation of glassy phase from feldspar) bonds the particles. **Firing shrinkage = 8 – 12 %** is typical. 7. **Cooling** — slow, controlled cooling to avoid thermal-shock cracking. 8. **Inspection and finishing** — quality check, sorting, packaging. *(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for stage description, 1 for role, 1 for typical temperatures.)* **(b) Explain the various mechanisms of sintering with suitable sketches. Why is surface diffusion considered undesirable for densification? (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** Sintering is the process of bonding adjacent particles in a powder compact by atomic diffusion at elevated temperature (typically 0.5 – 0.85 T_m in K). **Six mechanisms** are generally identified, classified by the path the atoms take: | # | Mechanism | Atomic Path | Effect on Densification | Activation Energy | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Surface diffusion** | Surface → neck | Grows neck, **no shrinkage** | Low | | 2 | **Volume / lattice diffusion** (Nabarro–Herring) | Grain interior → neck | Densifies | High | | 3 | **Grain-boundary diffusion** (Coble) | GB → neck | Densifies | Medium | | 4 | **Evaporation–condensation** | Surface → neck | Grows neck, **no shrinkage** | High | | 5 | **Viscous flow** | Bulk flow (amorphous) | Densifies | — | | 6 | **Solution–reprecipitation** (liquid phase) | Through liquid film | Densifies (fast) | Low | **Why surface diffusion is undesirable for densification:** - Atoms migrate from the particle **surface** to the **neck** (high chemical-potential gradient at the curved neck surface). - This causes **neck growth** and an *apparent* strengthening of the body, but the **centre-to-centre distance between particles does not change** — i.e., there is **no macroscopic shrinkage**. - Pores remain; the body becomes **strong but porous**, which is bad for ceramics needing impermeability (e.g., alumina substrates, porcelain insulators). - Surface diffusion dominates at **low temperatures** and **small particle sizes**; to suppress it, one must sinter at higher T, or with a longer time so that volume/grain-boundary diffusion takes over (Herring's scaling: $n = 0.31$ for surface, $0.40$ for GB, $0.46$ for volume). *(7 marks — 3 for mechanisms table, 2 for "why surface is bad", 2 for sketches/descriptions.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing T_m with T_g** — the *melting point* T_m applies to crystalline solids; the *glass-transition* T_g applies to amorphous ones. Soda-lime glass has NO T_m. > 2. **Sintering shrinkage ≠ firing shrinkage** — green shrinkage (drying) is *separate* from firing shrinkage. Many students combine them incorrectly in calculations. > 3. **Float bath temperature ≈ 600 °C, NOT 1450 °C** — that is the *furnace* temperature. The bath itself is held at 600 – 650 °C. Mixing these up is an instant 1-mark cut. > 4. **Herring's law exponent** — examiners expect the *exact* values (0.31, 0.40, 0.46). Vague answers like "n is small" get zero. > 5. **Forgetting to mention cullet in glass raw materials** — it is the largest single constituent by mass in many modern batches and is a guaranteed sub-question. > 6. **Drawing a melting curve for glass** — glass has no melting curve, only a smooth viscosity–temperature curve. Drawing a sharp peak is a factual error. > 7. **Units in the thermal-shock parameter R** — students often omit the units and lose a half mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### 🪟 Glass - ✅ Glass = **amorphous** SiO₂-based solid; **no long-range order**; **no sharp melting point**. - ✅ Three principal raw materials: **silica, soda ash, limestone** + **cullet**. - ✅ Viscosity landmarks: working 10⁴, softening 10⁷·⁶, annealing 10¹³, strain 10¹⁴·⁵ poise. - ✅ **Float process** = molten glass on **molten tin bath** at **600 – 650 °C**; tin bath is inert (N₂/H₂ atmosphere). - ✅ **Annealing lehr** = controlled cool through transformation range to relieve stress. - ✅ **Fulcher equation:** $\log \eta = A + B / (T - T_0)$ — viscosity drops rapidly with T. - ✅ Cullet saves ~ 1.2 % energy per 10 % added. - ✅ Float glass is **fire-polished** — no secondary grinding. ### 🧱 Ceramics - ✅ Ceramics = **inorganic non-metallic**, ionic/covalent bonding, polycrystalline. - ✅ Two classes: **traditional (clay-based)** and **advanced (engineering) ceramics**. - ✅ Four main stages: **powder prep → shaping → drying → firing (sintering)**. - ✅ Shaping methods: dry pressing, isostatic pressing, extrusion, slip casting, injection moulding, jiggering. - ✅ **Sintering** = atomic diffusion bonds particles; typically at **0.5 – 0.85 T_m**. - ✅ Herring scaling: $t \propto a^m$, with $m = 3$ (volume), $m = 4$ (GB / surface). - ✅ **Surface diffusion** causes neck growth **without densification** → undesirable. - ✅ **Grain-boundary diffusion (Coble)** and **Volume diffusion (Nabarro–Herring)** are the *densifying* mechanisms. - ✅ Slip casting: wall thickness $L = K \sqrt{t}$ (square-root of time law). - ✅ Firing shrinkage: **8 – 12 %** for clay bodies. - ✅ Thermal shock resistance: $R = \sigma_f k / (\alpha E)$ — high $R$ = good (SiC, Si₃N₄ are excellent; glass is poor). - ✅ **Deflocculants** (sodium silicate) keep slip fluid at low water content — important in slip casting. ### 🎯 Cross-Cutting Concepts - ✅ Both glass and ceramics are **brittle** → low $K_{IC}$ → sensitive to flaws. - ✅ Both are **chemically inert** and **high-temperature capable**. - ✅ Glass is processed by **melting + forming**; ceramics by **powder + sintering** — fundamental processing distinction. - ✅ Adding **recycled material** (cullet in glass, broken bisque in ceramics) is essential for energy/material economy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU tip:** When asked to "compare and contrast", always structure your answer in a **table** with at least **5 rows** (e.g., atomic order, bonding, manufacturing route, typical products, key defects). Examiners reward visual clarity.

topics.title: Relevant Properties of Glass and Ceramics, Processing Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant Properties of Glass and Ceramics, Processing Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Glass — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Glass** is an **amorphous, non-crystalline solid** typically produced by the rapid cooling (quenching) of a molten silica-based melt, such that the atomic arrangement lacks the long-range periodic order of a crystalline lattice. It is most commonly composed of **silica (SiO₂)** along with modifiers like **soda (Na₂O)** and **lime (CaO)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, glass belongs to the family of **inorganic non-metallic materials** that exhibit a **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** rather than a sharp melting point. The absence of grain boundaries makes glass **isotropic** (properties identical in all directions) — a hallmark feature examined in Module 2.

## 1.2 Ceramics — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Ceramics** are **inorganic, non-metallic, often crystalline solids** composed of metallic and non-metallic elements bonded primarily by **ionic and/or covalent bonds**. They are typically formed by the action of heat (firing/sintering) on raw earthy materials such as **clay, silica, alumina, and feldspar**.

Traditional ceramics (pottery, bricks) and advanced/technical ceramics (alumina $Al_2O_3$, zirconia $ZrO_2$, silicon carbide $SiC$, silicon nitride $Si_3N_4$) both fall under this definition in the KTU syllabus.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Glass like "frozen supercooled liquid syrup":** Imagine heating sugar syrup and then pouring it onto a cold metal plate. It hardens but never has time to form crystals — the molecules are "frozen in disorder." That is exactly how glass behaves at the molecular level. It is rigid like a solid but structurally disordered like a liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Ceramics like "baked clay bricks in a kiln":** Picture a potter taking wet, soft clay, shaping it into a vase, drying it, and then baking it in a furnace at 1000 °C. The atoms re-arrange and bond strongly, producing a hard, brittle, heat-resistant object. This **"shape-then-fire"** philosophy is the heart of ceramic processing.

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot

| Material Class | Atomic Structure | Typical Bonding | Representative Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass | Amorphous (no long-range order) | Covalent + Ionic | Soda-lime glass, Borosilicate |
| Traditional Ceramics | Polycrystalline | Ionic + Covalent | Porcelain, Bricks, Tiles |
| Advanced Ceramics | Polycrystalline / Single crystal | Predominantly Covalent | $Al_2O_3$, $SiC$, $ZrO_2$ |
| Glass-Ceramics | Partially crystalline | Mixed | Zerodur, Pyroceram |

> **Standard Reference Metrics (to memorize for KTU):**
> - Density of soda-lime glass $\approx$ **2500 kg/m³**
> - Softening point of soda-lime glass $\approx$ **700 °C**
> - Working point of glass $\approx$ **1000 °C**
> - Firing temperature of porcelain $\approx$ **1200–1400 °C**
> - Melting point of alumina $Al_2O_3 \approx$ **2072 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Glass

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Properties
1. **High Hardness** — typically **5–7 Mohs scale** for soda-lime glass. Resistant to scratching.
2. **High Compressive Strength** ($\sigma_c \approx 700\text{–}1000$ MPa) but **Very Low Tensile Strength** ($\sigma_t \approx 30\text{–}70$ MPa).
3. **Extreme Brittleness** — virtually zero plastic deformation; failure occurs by sudden crack propagation.
4. **Low Toughness / Fracture Toughness** — typically $K_{IC} \approx 0.7\text{–}0.8$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why glass shatters so easily:** Theoretically, glass should be 10× stronger than measured strength. The discrepancy is explained by **Griffith's micro-crack theory** — sub-microscopic surface flaws act as stress concentrators, dramatically reducing actual strength.

### 2.1.2 Thermal Properties
1. **Low Thermal Expansion Coefficient** ($\alpha \approx 9 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for soda-lime, $3 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for fused silica).
2. **Poor Thermal Conductivity** ($k \approx 1$ W/m·K) — makes glass a good insulator.
3. **Glass Transition Temperature $T_g$** — the temperature at which glass transitions from rigid to rubbery state.
4. **Viscosity-Temperature Behavior** — viscosity drops several orders of magnitude as temperature rises (see formula sheet).

### 2.1.3 Optical Properties
1. **Transparency** in the visible spectrum (300–700 nm) for pure silica-based glass.
2. **Refractive Index** $n \approx 1.5$ for soda-lime glass, $n \approx 1.46$ for fused silica.
3. **Colorability** by addition of transition metal oxides (Fe, Co, Ni, Cr).

### 2.1.4 Electrical & Chemical Properties
1. **Excellent Electrical Insulator** — volume resistivity $\rho \approx 10^{12}\text{–}10^{14}$ Ω·m.
2. **High Chemical Durability** — resistant to most acids (except HF).
3. **Isotropic** — uniform properties in all directions due to amorphous structure.

## 2.2 Properties of Ceramics

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Properties
1. **Very High Hardness** — $Al_2O_3 \approx 9$ Mohs, $SiC \approx 9.5$ Mohs.
2. **High Compressive Strength** ($\sigma_c$ up to **5000 MPa** for advanced ceramics).
3. **Low Tensile Strength** ($\sigma_t$ typically **200–400 MPa**) and **low ductility**.
4. **Brittleness** — strain to failure often $< 0.1\%$.
5. **High Stiffness / Modulus** — Young's modulus $E$ of $Al_2O_3 \approx 380$ GPa.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Properties
1. **Very High Melting Points** — $Al_2O_3$: 2072 °C, $SiC$: 2700 °C, $Si_3N_4$: 1900 °C.
2. **Low Thermal Expansion** for some ceramics (e.g., zirconia, cordierite).
3. **Excellent Thermal Shock Resistance** for some engineered ceramics.
4. **High Refractoriness** — ability to withstand high temperatures without deformation.

### 2.2.3 Electrical & Chemical Properties
1. **Electrical Insulation** (most ceramics) — except special conductive ceramics (SiC, $LaCrO_3$).
2. **Chemical Inertness** — resistant to acids, bases, and oxidizing environments.
3. **High Dielectric Strength** — used in capacitors and insulators.
4. **Low Density** compared to metals — typically **2000–6000 kg/m³**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Equation | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ |
| 2 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | GPa |
| 3 | Stress Intensity Factor | $K_{IC} = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | MPa·m$^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | m |
| 5 | Heat Capacity | $Q = m c \Delta T$ | J |
| 6 | Refractive Index (Snell's) | $n = \dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Weibull Modulus relation | $P_f = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}\right)^{m}\right]$ | failure probability |
| 8 | Viscosity (Arrhenius form) | $\eta = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Pa·s |
| 9 | Thermal Shock Resistance | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Volume Resistivity | $\rho = R \dfrac{A}{L}$ | Ω·m |

> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):** The thermal shock parameter $R$ is critical in selecting refractories for furnace linings, rocket nozzles (using $SiC$, $Si_3N_4$), and brake discs. The Weibull modulus $m$ governs the reliability statistics of ceramic components in aerospace and biomedical implants.

## 2.4 Theoretical 'Why' Behind the Properties

- **Brittleness arises from ionic/covalent bonds** that disallow dislocation movement → no plastic flow.
- **High stiffness comes from strong directional bonds** between atoms (Si–O, Al–O).
- **Low thermal expansion** in silica-based glasses is due to the open, flexible Si–O tetrahedral network that contracts very little on cooling.
- **Transparency in glass** is because amorphous SiO₂ has no grain boundaries or defined band gaps to scatter visible light photons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Viscosity–Temperature Relationship for Glass Forming

The forming of glass (blowing, drawing, pressing) requires precise viscosity control. The **Fulcher equation** is the industry-standard empirical model:

$$\eta(T) = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{T - T_0}\right)$$

Where:
- $\eta(T)$ = viscosity at temperature $T$ (Pa·s)
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (Pa·s)
- $B$ = activation energy constant (K)
- $T_0$ = ideal glass transition temperature (K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step-by-step derivation (from Arrhenius base):**

> **Step 1 — Begin with the Arrhenius form** for thermally activated viscous flow:

$$\eta = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

> **Step 2 — Recognize the inadequacy.** Pure Arrhenius fails for glass near $T_g$ because the activation energy $E_a$ is not constant; it rises sharply as temperature approaches $T_g$ from above.

> **Step 3 — Introduce the configurational entropy model.** Adam and Gibbs (1965) proposed that the configurational entropy $S_c$ of a supercooled liquid controls relaxation:

$$\eta = \eta_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{C}{T \cdot S_c(T)}\right)$$

> **Step 4 — Approximate $S_c(T)$ as linearly vanishing near $T_0$:**

$$S_c(T) \approx S_c'(T - T_0)$$

where $T_0$ is the **Kauzmann temperature** (where configurational entropy would extrapolate to zero).

> **Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.** Let $B = C / S_c'$, yielding the **Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann (VFT) equation**:

$$\eta = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{T - T_0}\right)$$

> **Step 6 — Typical numerical example for soda-lime glass:**

$$A \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s},\quad B \approx 4500\ \text{K},\quad T_0 \approx 555\ \text{K}$$

At the **working point** ($T \approx 1273$ K):

$$\eta = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp\!\left(\dfrac{4500}{1273 - 555}\right) = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp(6.26) \approx 10^{2.6}\ \text{Pa·s}$$

This is in the order of $10^2\text{–}10^3$ Pa·s — the viscosity at which glass can be **worked/shaped** by hand or machine.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Processing of Glass vs. Ceramics

| Stage | Glass Processing | Ceramic Processing |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Raw Material** | Silica sand ($SiO_2$), soda ash ($Na_2CO_3$), limestone ($CaCO_3$), cullet | Clay ($Al_2O_3 \cdot 2SiO_2 \cdot 2H_2O$), feldspar, silica, talc, alumina |
| **2. Preparation** | Batch weighing & mixing; cullet addition | Powder crushing, grinding, classification, slip preparation |
| **3. Forming Method** | Blowing, pressing, drawing, rolling, floating | Pressing, extrusion, slip casting, injection molding, jiggering |
| **4. Thermal Treatment** | Melting at **~1500 °C** in furnace | Firing/Sintering at **~1200–2000 °C** in kiln |
| **5. Post-Treatment** | Annealing (stress relief), tempering, laminating | Glazing, machining, polishing, surface coating |
| **6. Key Process Parameter** | Viscosity control | Sintering kinetics & shrinkage control |
| **7. End Product Examples** | Bottles, windows, optical lenses | Tiles, sanitary ware, insulators, cutting tools |

## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step: Glass Forming Process

> **Step 1 — Batch Preparation:** Raw materials (silica sand, soda ash, limestone, feldspar, cullet) are weighed precisely. A typical soda-lime glass batch composition:

$$72\%\ SiO_2 + 14\%\ Na_2O + 9\%\ CaO + 4\%\ MgO + 1\%\ Al_2O_3$$

> **Step 2 — Melting:** Charged into a **regenerative furnace** (Siemens-type) at **~1500 °C**. The following simplified reaction occurs:

$$SiO_2 + Na_2CO_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} Na_2O \cdot SiO_2 + CO_2\uparrow$$

> **Step 3 — Refining & Homogenization:** Bubbling, stirring to remove gas bubbles and ensure chemical uniformity.

> **Step 4 — Forming (shaping)** at the **working point** where $\eta \approx 10^4$ Pa·s:
- **Float Process** (flat glass): glass floats on molten tin.
- **Blowing** (bottles, jars).
- **Pressing** (lenses, dishes).
- **Drawing** (fibers, tubes).

> **Step 5 — Annealing:** Glass is slowly cooled through the **annealing range** ($T_g$ to strain point) to relieve residual stresses and prevent spontaneous fracture.

> **Step 6 — Finishing:** Cutting, grinding, polishing, coating (e.g., silvering of mirrors, anti-reflective coatings).

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step: Ceramic Forming Process

> **Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation:** Crushing, grinding, sieving, magnetic separation, and blending of clays and non-plastic materials.

> **Step 2 — Powder Conditioning:** Spray drying to obtain free-flowing granules with controlled particle size distribution and moisture content.

> **Step 3 — Shaping** — Four main methods:
1. **Dry Pressing:** Powder compressed in rigid die (uniaxial pressing) at **20–200 MPa**. Used for tiles, refractories.
2. **Isostatic Pressing:** Powder in flexible mold subjected to equal pressure from all sides (cold isostatic pressing — CIP at 100–400 MPa).
3. **Slip Casting:** Aqueous suspension (slip) poured into a porous plaster mold; water is absorbed leaving a solid layer.
4. **Extrusion:** Plastic mix (clay + water + binder) forced through a die to produce pipes, bricks.

> **Step 4 — Drying:** Controlled removal of moisture to prevent cracking and shrinkage defects. Critical for clay-based ceramics due to high shrinkage.

> **Step 5 — Sintering / Firing:** Powder compact heated below its melting point, causing **diffusion bonding** between particles. The driving force is reduction of surface energy. Densification typically achieves **>95% of theoretical density**.

> **Step 6 — Glazing & Finishing:** For traditional ceramics, a vitreous coating (glaze) is applied before a second firing, producing a smooth, non-porous, decorative surface.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Thermal Shock Calculation

A ceramic tile has the following properties:
- Tensile strength $\sigma_f = 250$ MPa
- Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.22$
- Young's modulus $E = 320$ GPa
- Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C

Find the maximum allowable temperature difference $\Delta T_{max}$ for thermal shock survival.

> **Step 1 — Apply the thermal shock resistance formula:**

$$R = \dfrac{\sigma_f (1-\nu)}{E \alpha}$$

> **Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$R = \dfrac{(250 \times 10^6)(1 - 0.22)}{(320 \times 10^9)(6 \times 10^{-6})}$$

> **Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator:**

$$R = \dfrac{250 \times 0.78}{320 \times 6} \times \dfrac{10^6}{10^3} = \dfrac{195}{1920} \times 10^3 \approx 101.6\ \text{°C}$$

> **Step 4 — Interpret:** The tile can survive a sudden $\Delta T \approx 100$ °C without cracking. This metric is used in designing **cookware, brake discs, and rocket nozzles**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Atomic Structure Comparison — Glass vs. Ceramic

This Mermaid diagram shows the block-level functional comparison between amorphous (glass) and crystalline (ceramic) atomic architectures.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["GLASS: AMORPHOUS STRUCTURE"] --> B1["Short-range order SiO4 tetrahedra"]
    A1 --> C1["No long-range periodicity"]
    A1 --> D1["Random network of Si-O-Si bonds"]
    A1 --> E1["Modifier ions Na, Ca randomly placed in voids"]
    A1 --> F1["Property: ISOTROPIC"]

    A2["CERAMIC: CRYSTALLINE STRUCTURE"] --> B2["Long-range periodic lattice"]
    A2 --> C2["Grain boundaries present"]
    A2 --> D2["Polycrystalline or single crystal"]
    A2 --> E2["Strong ionic or covalent bonds"]
    A2 --> F2["Property: ANISOTROPIC in single crystal"]
```

## 4.2 Glass Manufacturing Process Flow

A schematic block-level flow of the industrial glass manufacturing process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Raw Material Storage: Silica Sand, Soda Ash, Limestone, Cullet"] --> P2["Weighing and Batch Mixing"]
    P2 --> P3["Charging into Regenerative Furnace"]
    P3 --> P4["Melting at approx 1500 degree Celsius"]
    P4 --> P5["Refining: Bubble Removal and Homogenization"]
    P5 --> P6["Conditioning: Cooling to Working Point"]
    P6 --> P7["Forming: Float OR Blowing OR Pressing OR Drawing"]
    P7 --> P8["Annealing Lehr: Stress Relief"]
    P8 --> P9["Inspection and Quality Control"]
    P9 --> P10["Cutting, Grinding, Coating"]
    P10 --> P11["Finished Glass Product"]
```

## 4.3 Ceramic Manufacturing Process Flow

A schematic block-level flow of the industrial ceramic manufacturing process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Raw Material: Clay, Feldspar, Silica, Alumina"] --> Q2["Crushing, Grinding, Sieving"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Slip Preparation OR Powder Conditioning"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Forming: Pressing OR Extrusion OR Slip Casting OR Injection Molding"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Drying: Moisture Removal and Shrinkage Control"]
    Q5 --> Q6["Bisque Firing OR Presintering"]
    Q6 --> Q7["Glaze Application for traditional ceramics"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Final Firing or Sintering at 1200 to 2000 degree Celsius"]
    Q8 --> Q9["Cooling and Annealing"]
    Q9 --> Q10["Machining, Polishing, Surface Treatment"]
    Q10 --> Q11["Finished Ceramic Product"]
```

## 4.4 Viscosity–Temperature Zones of Glass (Block Architecture)

Different shaping operations are performed in different viscosity ranges of glass.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1["Melting Zone: eta equals 1 to 10 Pa.s, T equals 1500 degree Celsius"] --> V2["Working Zone: eta equals 10 to 10 power 4 Pa.s, T equals 1000 degree Celsius"]
    V2 --> V3["Forming Zone: eta equals 10 to 10 power 4 to 10 to 10 power 7 Pa.s, T equals 800 degree Celsius"]
    V3 --> V4["Softening Zone: eta equals 10 to 10 power 7 Pa.s, Littleton Softening Point"]
    V4 --> V5["Annealing Zone: eta equals 10 to 10 power 12 to 10 to 10 power 13 Pa.s, near Tg"]
    V5 --> V6["Strain Point: eta equals 10 to 10 power 13.5 Pa.s, glass becomes rigid"]
    V6 --> V7["Room Temperature: Solid Glass, eta greater than 10 to 10 power 18 Pa.s"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the atomic structures of glass and ceramic materials.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Glass (Amorphous):** The atoms (mainly Si and O) are arranged in a **disordered, random network** with only short-range order (SiO₄ tetrahedra) but **no long-range periodicity**. There are no grains, no grain boundaries, and no crystallographic planes.

> **Ceramic (Crystalline):** The atoms are arranged in a **regular, repeating, three-dimensional periodic lattice** with long-range order. The microstructure consists of **grains separated by grain boundaries**, which can make the material anisotropic in single-crystal form and give rise to properties dependent on grain size, orientation, and porosity.

> **Key distinguishing property:** Glass is **isotropic** (same properties in all directions), while a single-crystal ceramic is **anisotropic** (properties differ with direction). Polycrystalline ceramics are statistically isotropic only if grains are randomly oriented.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is glass brittle even though it has high theoretical strength?**

**Model Answer:**

> Although the **theoretical strength of glass** (estimated from Si–O bond strength) could be on the order of **10 GPa or more**, the **actual measured tensile strength** of ordinary soda-lime glass is only about **30–70 MPa**.

> This enormous discrepancy is explained by **Griffith's theory of brittle fracture (1920)**: glass always contains **sub-microscopic surface flaws, micro-cracks, and scratches** introduced during manufacture, handling, and service. Under tensile loading, these flaws act as **stress concentrators**, where the local stress at the crack tip is amplified by a factor proportional to $\sqrt{a}$ (crack length).

> Because glass has **no ductile deformation mechanism** (no dislocation motion, no plastic flow), it cannot relieve this localized stress. The crack propagates catastrophically at the **critical stress intensity factor $K_{IC}$**, leading to sudden brittle fracture. Strengthening methods like **tempering, chemical strengthening (ion exchange), and lamination** are used to introduce compressive surface stresses that resist crack initiation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanical, thermal, and optical properties of glass**. Why is float glass preferred for window applications? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Mechanical Properties of Glass:**
> 1. **Hardness:** 5–7 Mohs; resistant to scratching but can be scratched by harder materials like quartz.
> 2. **Brittleness:** Essentially zero plastic deformation; fails by sudden fracture.
> 3. **High compressive strength** (700–1000 MPa) but **low tensile strength** (30–70 MPa) due to Griffith flaws.
> 4. **Low fracture toughness** ($K_{IC} \approx 0.7$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$).
> 5. **Isotropic** mechanical behavior due to amorphous structure.

> **Thermal Properties of Glass:**
> 1. **Low thermal expansion** ($\alpha \approx 9 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for soda-lime) — especially low for fused silica ($0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C), making it resistant to thermal shock.
> 2. **Low thermal conductivity** ($k \approx 1$ W/m·K) — good insulator.
> 3. **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** — typically **550–600 °C** for soda-lime glass.
> 4. **No sharp melting point** — viscosity changes continuously with temperature (VFT behavior).

> **Optical Properties of Glass:**
> 1. **Transparency** to visible light (300–700 nm wavelength) for high-purity silica glass.
> 2. **Refractive index $n \approx 1.5$** — used in lenses and prisms.
> 3. **Dispersion** — different wavelengths bend by different amounts (basis of spectrum formation).
> 4. **Colorability** by adding metallic oxides (Co for blue, Cr for green, Fe for amber, etc.).

> **Why Float Glass is Preferred for Windows:**
> The **float process** (developed by Sir Alastair Pilkington in 1959) produces glass by floating molten glass on a bath of **molten tin** under a controlled atmosphere. This yields:
> - **Perfectly flat, parallel surfaces** without requiring grinding or polishing.
> - **Uniform thickness** (2–25 mm) with no distortion.
> - **High optical clarity** with no surface waves.
> - **Economical, continuous mass production** for architectural and automotive applications.

---

**(b)** Describe the **complete processing steps for manufacturing flat glass by the float process**, including the role of the tin bath, annealing lehr, and atmosphere control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Raw Material Preparation:** Silica sand (72%), soda ash (14%), limestone (9%), dolomite (4%), and cullet (recycled glass, ~20–30% of batch) are weighed and mixed. Cullet reduces energy consumption.

> **Step 2 — Melting in Furnace:** The batch is charged into a **regenerative cross-fired furnace** (Siemens-type) and melted at **~1500 °C**. The chemical reaction:

$$SiO_2 + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow Na_2O \cdot SiO_2 + CO_2\uparrow$$

> **Step 3 — Refining:** Bubbles are removed by passing the melt through a refining zone. Sulfates may be added as fining agents.

> **Step 4 — Float Bath (Tin Bath):** The molten glass is poured continuously onto a bath of **molten tin** at **~1050 °C**. Because **glass is less dense than tin** and they do not mix, the glass floats and spreads out under **surface tension** to form a flat, uniform ribbon of glass. The bath is enclosed and filled with a **reducing atmosphere of nitrogen and hydrogen** to prevent oxidation of the tin.

> **Step 5 — Cooling and Thickness Control:** Glass thickness is controlled by **top rollers (top rolls)** stretching the ribbon and by the **flow rate of glass from the spout lip**. The glass is gradually cooled from 1050 °C to ~600 °C as it moves along the ~50-meter-long bath.

> **Step 6 — Annealing Lehr:** The glass ribbon passes into a **long annealing lehr** (oven) where it is reheated to a temperature just above $T_g$ and then **slowly cooled** at a controlled rate (typically 1–5 °C/min) to relieve residual stresses.

> **Step 7 — Cutting and Inspection:** The continuous ribbon is cut into standard sheets, inspected for defects, and stored for shipment.

> **Atmosphere Control Importance:** The H₂–N₂ mixture prevents:
> - **Tin oxidation** to SnO₂ (which would create surface defects).
> - **Oxygen contamination** that would tarnish the glass surface.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanical and thermal properties of ceramics** that make them suitable for **high-temperature applications** like furnace linings, cutting tools, and aerospace components. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Mechanical Properties of Ceramics:**
> 1. **High Hardness** (e.g., $Al_2O_3$: 9 Mohs; $SiC$: 9.5 Mohs) → wear-resistant cutting tools and abrasives.
> 2. **High Compressive Strength** (up to **5000 MPa** for advanced ceramics) → structural load-bearing capacity.
> 3. **High Stiffness** (Young's modulus $E$ of $Al_2O_3$ is **380 GPa**, of $SiC$ is **410 GPa**) → dimensional stability under load.
> 4. **Low Density** (2000–6000 kg/m³) → lightweight structural components.
> 5. **Brittleness and low fracture toughness** are disadvantages; mitigated by **transformation toughening** in zirconia ($ZrO_2$) and by **fiber reinforcement** in composites (CMCs).

> **Thermal Properties of Ceramics:**
> 1. **Very High Melting Points:** $Al_2O_3$ (2072 °C), $SiC$ (2700 °C), $Si_3N_4$ (1900 °C) — usable far above the limits of metals.
> 2. **Refractoriness** — ability to withstand prolonged high-temperature exposure without deformation or chemical degradation.
> 3. **Low Thermal Expansion** (e.g., cordierite: $1 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C; fused silica: $0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C) — excellent thermal shock resistance.
> 4. **Low Thermal Conductivity** for insulating refractories, **High Thermal Conductivity** for $SiC$ and $BeO$ used as heat sinks.
> 5. **Thermal Shock Resistance Parameter** $R = \sigma_f (1-\nu) / (E \alpha)$ is high for low-expansion, high-strength ceramics like $Si_3N_4$ and $SiC$.

> **Application Examples:**
> - **Furnace linings:** Use **refractory bricks** (magnesia, chromia, alumina) due to high melting point and low thermal conductivity.
> - **Cutting tools:** Use **$Al_2O_3$ and $SiC$** for their hardness and wear resistance at high cutting speeds.
> - **Aerospace components (rocket nozzles, re-entry tiles):** Use **$SiC$ and $Si_3N_4$** for thermal shock resistance and retention of strength at elevated temperatures.

---

**(b)** Explain the **various shaping (forming) methods used for ceramic products**, with neat sketches of the process flow for **slip casting** and **dry pressing**. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **A. Slip Casting:**
> 1. A **slip** is prepared by mixing ceramic powder (e.g., porcelain, alumina) with **water, deflocculant (e.g., sodium silicate), and binder** in a ball mill to form a stable colloidal suspension with **30–50% solids content** and viscosity ~0.5–1 Pa·s.
> 2. The slip is poured into a **porous plaster of Paris mold**.
> 3. Water from the slip is absorbed by the mold capillary action, forming a **solid layer of ceramic** on the mold wall.
> 4. After the desired wall thickness is built up (5–60 minutes), the **excess slip is drained** (drain casting) — for solid castings, all slip is allowed to solidify.
> 5. The **green body** is removed from the mold after partial drying, then dried completely and fired.

> **Advantages:** Can produce **complex shapes** (sanitary ware, sculptures); low equipment cost; suitable for traditional clay-based ceramics.
> **Limitations:** Slow process; mold wears out; dimensional control is limited; not suitable for non-clay advanced ceramics.

> **B. Dry Pressing (Uniaxial Pressing):**
> 1. Ceramic powder is **spray-dried** with binder (PVA) to form **free-flowing granules** of controlled size (50–500 µm).
> 2. A measured quantity of granules is fed into a **rigid steel die**.
> 3. A **punch** applies **uniaxial pressure (20–200 MPa)** from one or both directions.
> 4. The compacted "green body" is ejected, then **sintered** at high temperature.

> **Advantages:** Fast cycle time; precise dimensional control; high production rate; suitable for **tiles, electronic substrates, and refractories**.
> **Limitations:** Limited to **simple prismatic shapes**; density gradients in tall parts; high tooling cost for small batches.

> **Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Slip Casting | Dry Pressing |
|---|---|---|
| Shape complexity | High | Low (simple shapes) |
| Production rate | Low | High |
| Equipment cost | Low | High |
| Dimensional accuracy | Moderate | High |
| Suitable material | Clay-based | Most ceramics |
| Typical products | Sanitary ware, art ware | Tiles, insulators, refractories |

> **Other forming methods** (briefly): **Extrusion** (clay columns, pipes), **Injection Molding** (complex small parts), **Isostatic Pressing** (high-uniformity parts), **Jiggering/Jollying** (tableware).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $T_g$ (glass transition) with $T_m$ (melting).** Glass does not have a sharp melting point — it has a glass transition range. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 2. **Glass is amorphous ≠ glass has no structure.** It has **short-range order** (SiO₄ tetrahedra). Saying "no structure" is incorrect.
> 3. **Ceramics are not always crystalline.** Glass is also classified as a ceramic in some taxonomies; the KTU module treats them as distinct non-metallic inorganic families.
> 4. **For float glass:** Many students forget to mention the **reducing atmosphere (H₂ + N₂)** that prevents tin oxidation. This is a frequent 2-mark loss point.
> 5. **For ceramics:** Never write "ceramics are weak" — they are weak in **tension** but very strong in **compression**. Use the correct qualification.
> 6. **Units:** Always mention units in numerical answers. KTU board examiners deduct marks for missing SI units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU PCPET205 Module 2:**

- [ ] **Glass = amorphous + isotropic + no sharp melting point + has $T_g$**.
- [ ] **Ceramic = crystalline + ionic/covalent bonds + high $T_m$ + brittle**.
- [ ] Glass composition **72% SiO₂ + 14% Na₂O + 9% CaO** is **soda-lime glass**.
- [ ] **Theoretical strength $\gg$ Actual strength** of glass (Griffith flaw theory).
- [ ] **Float process** = molten glass on **molten tin** under **N₂/H₂ atmosphere** — produces flat glass.
- [ ] **Annealing lehr** relieves residual stress by slow cooling near $T_g$.
- [ ] Glass working point: $\eta \approx 10^4$ Pa·s; softening point: $\eta \approx 10^{6.6}$ Pa·s.
- [ ] Ceramics: **press → dry → fire** (or slip cast / extrude).
- [ ] **Sintering** = diffusion bonding below $T_m$, achieving >95% theoretical density.
- [ ] **Firing temperature:** Porcelain 1200–1400 °C; $Al_2O_3$ sintering 1500–1700 °C; $SiC$ 2000–2200 °C.
- [ ] Ceramics strong in **compression**, weak in **tension** and **shear**.
- [ ] **Thermal shock resistance** $R = \sigma_f (1-\nu) / (E \alpha)$ — high for low $\alpha$ materials like fused silica and cordierite.
- [ ] **Refractoriness** = ability to withstand high temperatures without melting or deforming.
- [ ] **VFT equation** for glass viscosity: $\eta = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{B}{T - T_0}\right)$.
- [ ] **Major industrial ceramics:** $Al_2O_3$, $ZrO_2$, $SiC$, $Si_3N_4$, $TiB_2$, $TiC$.
- [ ] **Major industrial glasses:** Soda-lime, borosilicate (Pyrex), fused silica, lead glass, aluminosilicate.
- [ ] KTU board favourite questions: **Compare glass vs. ceramic structure**, **Explain float process**, **List ceramic forming methods**, **State 5 properties of glass**.
