> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixing, Melting, Forming, Cooling and Finishing, Equipment Used for Processing: Rotary Kiln, Tumbler, Ball Milling, Hydraulic Presses and Jiggers, Plastic Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Big Picture: What is Ceramic/Glass/Plastic Processing?

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Manufacturing of ceramics, glasses, and plastics** is a sequence of **unit operations** where raw materials (powders, granules, polymers) are progressively transformed into finished engineering products. Unlike machining (where material is *removed*), these processes are **additive / transformative** — atoms or particles are rearranged, fused, or shaped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET205, Module 2):**
> *"Processing of materials involves a chain of operations — Mixing → Melting → Forming → Cooling → Finishing — executed by dedicated equipment such as rotary kilns, tumblers, ball mills, hydraulic presses, jiggers, and plastic processing machines. Each operation alters a specific property: homogeneity, phase, geometry, residual stress, or surface quality."*

---

## 1.2 The Five Sequential Stages (with Real-World Analogy)

Imagine you are making a **ceramic cup** in a small-scale industry in Morbi (Gujarat) or a **plastic water bottle** in a Kochi factory. The same five logical stages occur.

### Stage 1 — **Mixing** (Homogenization)
- **Definition:** Uniform blending of two or more constituents (powders, liquids, additives) so that **every small sample of the mixture has the same composition**.
- **Intuition (Analogy):** Think of making *upma* — you must mix semolina, vegetables, and spices so that no single spoonful is all semolina. In ceramics, we mix **clay + water + flux + silica** into a uniform slip.
- **Key Metric:** **Coefficient of Variation (CoV)** of composition across samples — should be < **2 %** for high-quality ceramics.

### Stage 2 — **Melting** (Fusibility)
- **Definition:** Application of sufficient thermal energy to convert a **solid → liquid** (or to a plastic/viscous state) so that it can be shaped.
- **Intuition:** Like melting butter in a pan. For ceramics, the "melting" is more accurately **vitrification** — partial melting of fluxes forming a glassy phase that bonds the refractory grains.
- **Key Temperature Markers:**
  - Clay (Kaolin) softening: ~**1000 °C**
  - Soda-lime glass melt: ~**1400–1500 °C**
  - PVC plastic melt: ~**160–200 °C**
  - Steel casting melt: ~**1500–1600 °C**

### Stage 3 — **Forming** (Shaping)
- **Definition:** Imparting the desired geometry to the material while it is in a plastic, molten, or powder state.
- **Intuition:** Like shaping dough with a rolling pin (pressing) or pouring batter into a cake mould (casting). For ceramics, this is where **pressing, extrusion, jiggering, slip casting** happen.
- **Key Output Metric:** **Dimensional tolerance ± 0.5 mm** (whiteware) to **± 0.05 mm** (technical ceramics).

### Stage 4 — **Cooling** (Solidification / Annealing)
- **Definition:** Controlled removal of heat so that the formed body **solidifies, crystallises, or relaxes internal stresses**.
- **Intuition:** Like taking a hot *idiappam* (string hopper) out of the steamer — if you shock-cool it, it cracks; if you cool it slowly, it stays soft and intact. Ceramics use **controlled cooling curves** to avoid thermal shock.
- **Key Parameter:** **Cooling rate (°C/hr)** — typically **50–200 °C/hr** in tunnel kilns.

### Stage 5 — **Finishing** (Surface Engineering)
- **Definition:** Final operations to achieve required **surface finish, dimensional accuracy, decoration, and functional properties**.
- **Intuition:** Like polishing a wooden table after carpentry, or applying a clear coat. For ceramics, this is **glazing, polishing, grinding, lapping**.
- **Key Metric:** **Surface roughness (Ra)** — from **0.8 µm** (glazed) down to **0.05 µm** (optical polish).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"M-M-F-C-F"** → **Mixing, Melting, Forming, Cooling, Finishing**. This sequence appears in nearly every 3-mark question.

---

## 1.3 Equipment Overview — The "Toolbox" of These Stages

| Stage | Primary Equipment | Material Class |
|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| Mixing | Tumbler, Ball Mill (wet/dry), Pug Mill | Ceramics, Glass, Plastic |
| Melting | Rotary Kiln, Tank Furnace, Reactor | Ceramics, Glass, Polymer |
| Forming | Hydraulic Press, Jigger, Mould, Extruder | All three |
| Cooling | Annealing Lehr, Tunnel Kiln, Cooling Conveyor | Glass, Ceramic, Plastic |
| Finishing | Glazing line, Polishing lathe, Lapping machine | All three |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 exams, students often **confuse the role of a ball mill** — it is *primarily a mixer/grinder* (size reduction + homogenization), **not** a melter. Memorize: **Ball Mill = Size Reduction**, **Rotary Kiln = Firing/Melting**, **Jigger = Symmetric Forming**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Sequence is Non-Negotiable

> [!WARNING]
> Skipping or reordering stages leads to **defects**:
> - Mix *after* melting → **unfired voids** (ceramics) or **unmelted granules** (plastics).
> - Form *before* mixing → **non-uniform shrinkage** during cooling → cracks.
> - Cool *too fast* → **thermal shock fracture** in glass/ceramics; **internal voids** in plastics.
> - Skip finishing → **unacceptable surface roughness**, **leaky joints**, or **poor aesthetics**.

The KTU examiner expects students to **map each equipment to the correct stage** and **justify why that stage is needed** — this is a **2-mark differentiator** in 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-Wise Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 MIXING — Theory and Mechanisms

Mixing in particulate processing (ceramics) follows three transport mechanisms:

1. **Convective Mixing** — bulk movement of material groups (achieved in tumblers, ribbon blenders).
2. **Diffusive Mixing** — gradual inter-particle penetration (achieved in ball mills by cascading balls).
3. **Shear Mixing** — smearing layers over each other (achieved in pug mills, sigma blade mixers).

**Quality Index — Mixing Number (Mo):**

$$M_o = \frac{t \cdot N}{D}$$

Where $t$ = mixing time (s), $N$ = impeller/drum speed (rev/s), $D$ = drum diameter (m). A higher $M_o$ means more homogeneous mix.

---

### 2.1.2 MELTING / FIRING — Theory

For **ceramics**, true melting is not required. Instead, **sintering** + **vitrification** occur. The governing transport phenomena are:

- **Solid-state sintering** (T < 0.6 Tm): diffusion-controlled neck growth.
- **Liquid-phase sintering** (T ≈ 0.6–0.9 Tm): a flux (e.g., feldspar) melts and wets grains.
- **Vitrification** (T > 0.9 Tm): glassy phase fills pores.

**Thermal Energy Requirement (Furnace Loading):**

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$$

Where $Q$ = heat input (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K), $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg).

For plastics, melting is essentially heating pellets above their **glass transition temperature (Tg)** or **melting temperature (Tm)**.

---

### 2.1.3 FORMING — Theory

Forming processes are classified by the **state of the material**:

| Material State | Forming Process | Example |
|----------------|------------------|---------|
| Powder (dry) | Die Pressing, Isostatic Pressing | Tiles, refractories |
| Plastic paste | Jiggering, Extrusion, Pugging | Plates, bricks, pipes |
| Slip (liquid) | Slip Casting | Sanitaryware, art ware |
| Molten polymer | Injection Moulding, Blow Moulding, Extrusion | Bottles, toys |

**Packing Fraction (φ)** is critical in powder forming:

$$\phi = \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$$

For green ceramics, target $\phi \approx$ **0.55–0.65**. Higher φ means stronger green body, less shrinkage.

---

### 2.1.4 COOLING — Theory

**Newton's Law of Cooling (still the KTU workhorse):**

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -\frac{h \cdot A}{m \cdot c_p} (T - T_\infty)$$

Integration yields:

$$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) \cdot e^{-\frac{hA}{mc_p}t}$$

**Critical cooling rate (Rc)** for glass to avoid crystallization:

$$R_c = \frac{T_m - T_g}{\tau}$$

Where $\tau$ is the time available before crystallization begins. For **Soda-lime glass**, $R_c \approx$ **1 °C/s**; for **Borosilicate (Pyrex)**, $R_c$ is much lower, hence its thermal shock resistance.

For **plastics**, cooling is governed by **heat transfer through the mould wall** and the **effective thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$.

---

### 2.1.5 FINISHING — Theory

Surface quality is quantified by:
- **Ra** — Arithmetic mean roughness (µm)
- **Rz** — Mean peak-to-valley height (µm)

**Material Removal Rate (MRR) in grinding/finishing:**

$$MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d \quad (\text{mm}^3/\text{s})$$

Where $v$ = wheel speed, $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth of cut.

For glazed ceramics, the **glaze layer thickness** (typically **0.1–0.5 mm**) is the finishing tolerance budget.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (One-Page Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the only equations you need for Module 2 derivations.**

| # | Formula | Symbols | Application / Stage | Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$ | $N_c$ = critical speed, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $R$ = mill radius | Ball mill design | rev/s |
| 2 | $N_c \approx \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ | $D$ = mill diameter in metres | Quick critical speed | rpm |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ = pressure, $F$ = force, $A$ = area | Hydraulic press | Pa (or MPa) |
| 4 | $F = P \cdot A_{piston}$ | $A_{piston}$ = piston area | Hydraulic press force | N (or kN) |
| 5 | $P_{intensity} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{P_{area}}$ (Pascal's law) | $P_{load}$, $P_{area}$ | Hydraulic press amplification | Pa |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_f$ | heat for melting | Melting stage | J |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | packing fraction | Forming (powder) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty)\,e^{-(hA/mc_p)t}$ | Newton's cooling | Cooling stage | °C or K |
| 9 | $R_c = (T_m - T_g) / \tau$ | critical cooling rate | Glass cooling | K/s |
| 10 | $v_{kiln} = \dfrac{L_{kiln}}{\tau_{res}}$ | rotary kiln residence time | Rotary kiln | m/s |
| 11 | $\theta = \dfrac{L}{D}$ (kiln slope) | slope angle calc. | Rotary kiln geometry | dimensionless |
| 12 | $MRR = v \cdot f \cdot d$ | grinding removal rate | Finishing | mm³/s |
| 13 | $V_{tumble} \propto N^2 \cdot D^2$ | tumbler mixing correlation | Tumbler design | m³/s |
| 14 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | arithmetic roughness | Finishing | µm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 14-mark derivations, you will most often be asked to derive **Ball Mill Critical Speed (Eq. 1 & 2)** and **Hydraulic Press Force Amplification (Eq. 3–5)**. Master these two first.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where These Processes are Used

| Process | Industry | Real Products |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| Mixing | Ceramic tile plants, powder metallurgy | Floor tiles, sintered bronze bushes |
| Melting | Glass factories, steel foundries, polymer plants | Bottles, ingots, PVC pipes |
| Forming | Tableware, sanitaryware, plastic moulding | Plates, toilets, phone cases |
| Cooling | Glass annealing, ceramic firing | Sheet glass, fired bricks |
| Finishing | Glazing lines, optical polishing | Glazed tiles, camera lenses |
| **Rotary Kiln** | Cement, lime, alumina | Cement clinker, quicklime |
| **Tumbler** | Abrasive mfg., foundries, food | Polishing media, alloy homogenization |
| **Ball Mill** | Cement, ceramics, pharma | Cement powder, alumina, drug tablets |
| **Hydraulic Press** | Ceramics, plastic, forging | Refractory bricks, plastic sheets |
| **Jigger** | Tableware industry | Plates, saucers, bowls |

> [!TIP]
> KTU questions often ask: *"Give two industrial applications of a Rotary Kiln."* Always answer with **cement clinker production** and **calcination of limestone** — these are the textbook examples.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Speed of a Ball Mill (KTU Favourite — 7 Marks)

### Statement
A ball mill of diameter $D$ (metres) rotates about its horizontal axis. The outermost ball experiences **centrifugal force** balancing **weight component along radius** at the **critical speed** $N_c$. Derive an expression for $N_c$.

### Given
- Mill diameter = $D$ (m)
- Mill radius = $R = D/2$
- Acceleration due to gravity = $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### To Find
Critical speed $N_c$ in **rev/s** and **rpm**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the outermost ball at the top of rotation path (at position A, just before the ball leaves the wall):**

At critical speed, the centrifugal force ($F_c$) just equals the centripetal force requirement, and the ball is on the verge of being pinned to the wall.

$$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot R$$

**Step 2 — At the critical condition, the only force providing centripetal acceleration is the centrifugal reaction, which equals the weight component required to keep the ball in circular motion.**

The condition for "centrifuging" (ball pinned to wall) is:

$$m \cdot \omega_c^2 \cdot R = m \cdot g$$

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{R}$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert angular velocity (rad/s) to revolutions per second:**

$$\omega_c = 2\pi N_c$$

$$2\pi N_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm) and substitute $R = D/2$:**

$$N_c\ (\text{rpm}) = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2g}{D}}$$

**Step 5 — Plug in $g = 9.81$ m/s² and simplify the constant:**

$$N_c\ (\text{rpm}) = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81}{D}} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{19.62}{D}}$$

$$N_c\ (\text{rpm}) = \frac{60}{6.2832}\sqrt{\frac{19.62}{D}} = 9.55 \times \frac{4.43}{\sqrt{D}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\,N_c\ (\text{rpm}) = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}\,\text{ where }D\text{ is in metres}\,}$$

### Operating Speed Recommendation

| Operating Regime | Speed Range | Effect |
|------------------|-------------|--------|
| Cataracting | $N = 0.5$ to $0.75 \, N_c$ | Balls cascade — **best for grinding** |
| Centrifuging | $N > N_c$ | Balls pinned — **no grinding** (avoid!) |
| Cascading | $N < 0.5 \, N_c$ | Tumbling — poor grinding |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force Amplification in a Hydraulic Press (KTU Favourite — 7 Marks)

### Statement
A hydraulic press has a small piston of area $a$ and a large piston of area $A$. A small force $F$ is applied on the small piston. Derive the expression for the load $W$ that can be lifted on the large piston.

### Given
- Small piston area = $a$ (m²)
- Large piston area = $A$ (m²)
- Applied force on small piston = $F$ (N)
- Load lifted on large piston = $W$ (N)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State Pascal's Law (the foundational principle):**

> *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid."*

**Step 2 — Pressure generated under the small piston:**

$$P_{small} = \frac{F}{a}$$

**Step 3 — By Pascal's law, this same pressure acts on the large piston:**

$$P_{large} = P_{small} = \frac{F}{a}$$

**Step 4 — This pressure acts on the large piston of area $A$ and produces the lifting force (load) $W$:**

$$W = P_{large} \cdot A = \frac{F}{a} \cdot A$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the mechanical advantage (MA):**

$$\boxed{\,W = F \cdot \frac{A}{a} = F \cdot \text{MA}\,}$$

### Numerical Example (For Practice)

Given: $F = 50$ N, $a = 1\ \text{cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, $A = 100\ \text{cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$

**Step A:** Pressure in fluid

$$P = \frac{F}{a} = \frac{50}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} = 0.5\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step B:** Load lifted

$$W = P \cdot A = 5 \times 10^{5} \times 100 \times 10^{-4} = 5000\ \text{N} = 5\ \text{kN}$$

**Step C:** Mechanical advantage

$$\text{MA} = \frac{W}{F} = \frac{5000}{50} = 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> A small input of **50 N** lifts a load of **5000 N** — a **100× multiplication** — with no energy gain (energy in = energy out, ignoring losses). This is the **trade-off**: you gain force, you lose displacement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equipment × Stage × Governing Physics

| Equipment | KTU Stage Tag | Governing Physics | Key Design Parameter | Failure Mode if Mishandled |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Tumbler | Mixing | Gravitational cascading, convection | Drum speed (N), Fill ratio (φ ≈ 0.5) | Over-filling → dead zones |
| Ball Mill | Mixing + Size Reduction | Impact + Attrition | Critical speed ($N_c$), Ball size | Centrifuging → no grinding |
| Rotary Kiln | Melting / Firing | Combustion, heat transfer, mass transport | Slope (1–4°), RPM (1–5), Residence time | Ring formation, refractory failure |
| Hydraulic Press | Forming | Pascal's law, plastic deformation | Pressure (MPa), dwell time | Lamination cracks in green body |
| Jigger | Forming (symmetric) | Rotational shaping + profile tool | RPM, tool profile angle | Wrinkles, warping during drying |
| Pug Mill | Forming (extrusion) | Screw extrusion, de-airing | Vacuum level, screw speed | Laminations, hard inclusions |
| Injection Moulding | Forming (plastic) | Plastic melt flow, heat transfer | Injection pressure, mould temperature | Short shots, flash, sink marks |
| Annealing Lehr | Cooling | Convective/radiative heat loss | Lehr profile, $T_{strain\,point}$ | Residual stress, brittle fracture |
| Glazing Line | Finishing | Slurry coating, surface tension | Viscosity, dip duration | Crawling, pinholes in glaze |
| Lapping/Polishing | Finishing | Abrasive wear, MRR | Abrasive grit size, pressure | Subsurface damage |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Residence Time in a Rotary Kiln

**Question:** A rotary kiln of length $L = 60$ m, diameter $D = 3$ m, slope $S = 2°$, rotates at $N = 1.5$ rpm. Estimate the residence time of material inside the kiln.

**Use the empirical relation (given in KTU textbook):**

$$\tau = \frac{1.77 \cdot \sqrt{L}}{N \cdot S \cdot D}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$\tau = \frac{1.77 \times \sqrt{60}}{1.5 \times 2 \times 3}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$1.77 \times \sqrt{60} = 1.77 \times 7.746 = 13.71$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate denominator:**

$$1.5 \times 2 \times 3 = 9$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$\tau = \frac{13.71}{9} \approx 1.52\ \text{minutes} \approx 91\ \text{seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **residence time per pass** — the material forms a thin rolling bed that takes about **1.5 min** to traverse the kiln. Real cement kilns operate at **30–45 min** residence, achieved by internal dams/refractory lifters.

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three differences between a Tumbler and a Ball Mill as mixing equipment.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| S. No. | Tumbler | Ball Mill |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Primarily used for **homogenization** of free-flowing powders by gravitational cascading. | Used for **size reduction (comminution)** in addition to mixing, using grinding media. |
| 2 | Operates at **low speeds** (typically 0.3–0.5 $N_c$ equivalent) to avoid impact damage. | Operates at **higher speeds (0.5–0.75 $N_c$)** to achieve impact + attrition grinding. |
| 3 | No internal grinding media — the material mixes with itself. | Contains **steel/ceramic balls** (10–90 mm) as grinding media. |
| 4 | Examples: **Abrasive media polishing, food/feed blending, foundry sand mixing.** | Examples: **Cement clinker grinding, ceramic glaze preparation, ore beneficiation.** |

(Any 3 valid points — 1 mark each.)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "vitrification" in ceramic processing. State the temperature range over which it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** *Vitrification is the process of forming a glassy (liquid) phase within a ceramic body during firing, wherein the flux components (e.g., feldspar) melt and flow into the pores between refractory grains, bonding the body into a dense, low-porosity structure on cooling.*

**Temperature Range (1 mark):** Vitrification typically occurs in the range of **0.75 to 0.9 of the melting temperature of the most fusible component** — practically **1100 °C to 1300 °C** for traditional stoneware/porcelain bodies.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Rotary Kiln used in cement manufacturing. List its main components.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A ball mill of 2 m diameter runs at 18 rpm. Calculate the critical speed and comment on the operating regime.**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B-A

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (4 marks):**

A rotary kiln is a long, refractory-lined, inclined, rotating cylindrical furnace used for **calcination, sintering, and clinkerisation**.

**Main Components:**

1. **Kiln Shell** — Mild steel cylindrical shell (length 30–200 m, diameter 3–7 m) with **refractory lining** inside (basic or acidic bricks).
2. **Support System** — Two to four **tyres (riding rings)** welded on the shell, resting on **trunnion rollers**.
3. **Drive System** — A **girth gear** and **pinion** driven by an electric motor through a gearbox, rotating the kiln at **0.5–5 rpm**.
4. **Inclination** — Slope of **1° to 4°** (typically 2°) from feed to discharge end, so material rolls/slides downhill.
5. **Feed End** — Chute/screw feeder introduces raw mix (limestone, clay, etc.).
6. **Discharge End / Burner Pipe** — Blower/coal/oil/gas burner fires at the lower end.
7. **Sealing** — Sliding or labyrinth seals at both ends to prevent air ingress and dust escape.
8. **Pre-heater & Cooler** (in modern plants) — Cyclone preheater and grate cooler for energy recovery.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. Raw material enters the **higher feed end**.
2. The kiln rotates slowly; the inclined slope causes material to **roll/slide/cascade** towards the discharge end.
3. **Hot gases** from the burner at the discharge end flow **counter-current** to the material.
4. As the material travels, it is **dried, preheated, calcined (CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂), and finally clinkered** (at ~1450 °C).
5. The clinker is discharged, cooled, and sent for grinding.

**Diagram (to be drawn on answer script — 0 marks extra but earns presentation):**

```
                ┌──── Inclined Cylinder ────┐
                │                           │
   Feed ───►    │    ↻ Rotation            │ ───► Clinker
                │       Slope ≈ 2°          │
                │       (Refractory Lined)  │
                └───────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                              │ Burner (Discharge End)
```

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Mill diameter $D = 2$ m
- Operating speed $N = 18$ rpm

**Step 1 — Calculate the critical speed using the standard formula (2 marks):**

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{42.3}{1.414}$$

$$N_c = 29.91\ \text{rpm} \approx 29.9\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the speed ratio (2 marks):**

$$\frac{N}{N_c} = \frac{18}{29.9} = 0.602 \approx 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Identify the operating regime (2 marks):**

| Speed Ratio | Regime | Action |
|-------------|--------|--------|
| $N/N_c < 0.5$ | Cascading | Tumbling — weak grinding |
| $0.5 < N/N_c < 0.75$ | **Cataracting** | **Optimum — strong impact + attrition** |
| $N/N_c > 0.75$ | Centrifuging | Balls pinned — no grinding |

Since $N/N_c = 0.60$, the mill is operating in the **Cataracting regime**, which is the **recommended operating range** for effective grinding.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

The mill operating at 18 rpm is **safely below the critical speed of 29.9 rpm** and is functioning in the **optimal cataract zone** for grinding action.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of a hydraulic press used for forming ceramic products. Derive the expression for mechanical advantage.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the construction and working of a Jigger used in the manufacture of ceramic tableware. Compare it with jolly for asymmetric shapes.**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B-B

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**

A hydraulic press uses **Pascal's law** to multiply a small input force into a large output force for compacting ceramic powder into shape (tiles, bricks, refractories).

**Components:**
1. **Two cylinders** — small (master) and large (ram), connected by a hydraulic line.
2. **Hydraulic fluid** — oil (e.g., ISO VG 46) filling both cylinders incompressibly.
3. **Pump** — generates high pressure in the fluid.
4. **Platen / Die cavity** — bottom cylinder has a mould cavity for the ceramic powder.
5. **Valve system** — controls flow direction (press, release, hold).
6. **Pressure gauge and relief valve** — for safety.

**Working (1 mark):**

A small force $F$ on the small piston creates pressure $P = F/a$. This pressure acts on the large piston of area $A$, generating an output force $W = P \cdot A$, which compacts the powder.

**Derivation of Mechanical Advantage (3 marks):**

From Pascal's law:

$$P = \frac{F}{a} = \frac{W}{A}$$

$$\frac{W}{F} = \frac{A}{a} = \text{MA}$$

$$\boxed{\,W = F \cdot \frac{A}{a}\,}$$

So the **mechanical advantage MA = A/a**. A hydraulic press with area ratio 100 can multiply a 100 N hand force into 10,000 N of compaction force.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Construction of Jigger (3 marks):**

A **jigger** is a machine used to form **axially symmetric** (rotationally symmetric) hollow ceramic ware like **plates, saucers, cups, bowls**.

**Components:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Rotating mould (gypsum or metal) | Holds the clay blank, rotates about a vertical axis |
| Profile tool / Jigger arm | Heated metal tool with the desired inner profile (concave for plates) |
| Spindle and motor | Rotates the mould at 80–150 rpm |
| Clay feed / de-airing pug | Supplies plastic clay directly onto the mould |
| Scrap flash / trimming edge | Removes excess clay at the periphery |
| Dust extraction hood | Collects clay dust |

**Working (2 marks):**

1. A **plastic clay ball (blank)** is placed on the rotating mould.
2. As the mould spins, the **profile tool descends and presses** the clay against the mould surface.
3. The tool's curved profile shapes the **upper face** of the ware (e.g., the well of a plate), while the mould shapes the **lower face**.
4. Excess clay is squeezed out as a **flash ring** and trimmed.
5. The formed piece is then **dried, trimmed, glazed, and fired**.

**Comparison: Jigger vs. Jolly (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Jigger | Jolly |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Symmetry | Forms **axially symmetric** ware (rotating about vertical axis) | Forms **axially asymmetric** ware (e.g., cups with handles, oval dishes) |
| Tool motion | Tool moves **radially inward** against a flat mould | Tool moves **laterally + vertically**, sweeping a 3D path |
| Products | Plates, saucers, simple bowls | Cups, mugs, oval dishes, art ware |
| Complexity | Simpler mechanism | More complex cam-driven mechanism |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU 2024 valuation observations:**

1. **Confusing equipment roles (–1 to –2 marks):**
   - Saying a **ball mill is used for melting** → WRONG. Ball mill = mixing/grinding.
   - Saying a **rotary kiln is for mixing** → WRONG. Rotary kiln = melting/firing.
   - Memorize the **stage → equipment** mapping in §1.3.

2. **Skipping units in derivations (–1 mark):**
   - Always write **units** (rpm, m/s², Pa, N) at the end of every numerical answer.
   - In the critical speed derivation, state explicitly: *"D in metres, $N_c$ in rpm"*.

3. **Not drawing diagrams (–2 marks):**
   - For 7-mark questions on equipment (kiln, press, jigger), a **neat labelled diagram is mandatory**.
   - A rough sketch with 4–5 labels can earn the full presentation mark.

4. **Forgetting the "why" (–1 mark):**
   - Just naming parts of a rotary kiln is not enough — explain **why each part is needed** (e.g., why slope? why refractory lining? why counter-current flow?).

5. **Confusing Jigger with Jolly (–1 mark):**
   - Jigger = **rotational symmetry (plates)**; Jolly = **asymmetric (cups with handle)**.
   - If the question says "asymmetric", the answer is JOLLY, not Jigger.

6. **Mixing up Pascal's law and Bernoulli's law (–1 mark):**
   - Pascal = pressure transmission in fluids (hydraulic press).
   - Bernoulli = energy conservation in flowing fluids (venturi, pitot tube).
   - They are **not interchangeable**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

### ✅ The 5-Stage Sequence
- **M**ixing → **M**elting → **F**orming → **C**ooling → **F**inishing (**MMFCF**)
- Each stage has a **specific purpose** (homogeneity, phase, geometry, stress relief, surface).

### ✅ Equipment–Stage Mapping
- Mixing: **Tumbler, Ball Mill, Pug Mill**
- Melting/Firing: **Rotary Kiln, Tank Furnace**
- Forming: **Hydraulic Press, Jigger, Jolly, Extruder, Injection Mould**
- Cooling: **Annealing Lehr, Cooling Conveyor**
- Finishing: **Glazing Line, Lapping, Polishing Lathe**

### ✅ Two Must-Know Derivations
1. **Ball Mill Critical Speed:** $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$ rpm (D in metres)
2. **Hydraulic Press MA:** $W = F \cdot (A/a)$, MA = area ratio

### ✅ Two Must-Know Diagrams
1. **Rotary Kiln** (counter-current, slope, burner, refractory lining, tyres)
2. **Hydraulic Press** (two cylinders, fluid, Pascal's law)

### ✅ Three Key Numerical Constants
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (in critical speed derivation)
- Ball mill optimum speed: $N = (0.5$ to $0.75) N_c$
- Vitrification range: **1100–1300 °C** for porcelain

### ✅ Three High-Yield Comparisons
- Jigger vs. Jolly (symmetric vs. asymmetric)
- Ball mill vs. Tumbler (grinding vs. blending)
- Compression moulding vs. Injection moulding (thermoset vs. thermoplastic)

### ✅ Industrial Applications (For "Give two examples" questions)
- Rotary Kiln → **Cement clinker production, Lime calcination**
- Ball Mill → **Cement grinding, Ceramic glaze prep, Ore beneficiation**
- Hydraulic Press → **Refractory bricks, Plastic sheets, Powder metallurgy parts**
- Jigger → **Plates, saucers, bowls in tableware industry**
- Plastic Moulding → **PET bottles (blow moulding), PVC pipes (extrusion), phone cases (injection moulding)**

### ✅ Common Defects to Remember
- **Crazing** in glazed ceramics (glaze–body thermal expansion mismatch)
- **Centrifuging** in ball mill (N > Nc → no grinding)
- **Thermal shock** in glass (cooling rate > critical)
- **Short shot** in injection moulding (insufficient melt volume)

### ✅ Key Mnemonics
- **MMFCF** → 5 processing stages
- **"Ball Mill = Before Melting"** → Ball mill is for raw material preparation, not firing
- **"Jigger = Jai Shri Ram = Just Symmetric"** → Jigger for symmetric ware only

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** In the 14-mark question, **always pick the option with a derivation** (critical speed or hydraulic press). The other part is descriptive and easier to score if you have memorised the diagrams. Spend the first 5 minutes planning your answer, then write 4–5 labelled diagrams — they are **free marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevant Properties of Plastics, Processing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant Properties of Plastics, Processing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plastics — The Engineering Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Plastics** are a broad class of **synthetic or semi-synthetic polymeric materials** that can be moulded, extruded, cast, or otherwise shaped into solid objects of any desired geometry when subjected to suitable combinations of **heat, pressure, and time**. They are primarily composed of long-chain **macromolecules** (polymers) derived from monomers through addition (chain-growth) or condensation (step-growth) polymerisation reactions.

Plastics are, in essence, the **"clay of the modern age"** — soft enough to be shaped at elevated temperature, but rigid enough to retain their geometry and provide functional service at room temperature. From the polyethene milk pouch to the nylon gear inside your washing machine, plastics are everywhere in engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> For PCPET205, the focus is on **engineering plastics** — those used in load-bearing, structural, or functional applications — not commodity packaging films. Students are expected to correlate **property → process → product**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spaghetti Pot"

Imagine boiling a pot of **spaghetti (long pasta strands)** in water:
- When cold, the strands are stiff, tangled, and cannot flow (this is plastic at room temperature).
- As heat is added, the strands become slippery, flexible, and the mass begins to flow like a thick liquid (this is plastic in the **molten state** during processing).
- If you pour the hot spaghetti mass into a mould and let it cool, it "freezes" into the shape of the mould (this is the **solidification / freezing stage** in plastics processing).

**The temperature at which this transition from rigid → rubbery → flowy occurs is a critical material property we will study below.**

---

## 1.3 Why are "Relevant Properties" Important for Processing?

The processing method (moulding, extrusion, casting, etc.) is **dictated by** the polymer's properties. A wrong pairing leads to defects:

| Property Type | Example | Processing Influence |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | Glass transition Tg, Melting Tm | Determines mould temperature, cycle time |
| **Rheological** | Melt Viscosity η, MFI | Determines injection pressure, screw design |
| **Mechanical** | Tensile/Impact strength | Determines ejection, demoulding strategy |
| **Shrinkage** | Mould shrinkage % | Determines mould cavity compensation |
| **Chemical** | Hygroscopicity | Determines drying requirement before processing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Axiom (Boxer-Bean Rule of Plastics):**
> *The polymer dictates the process. The process dictates the product. The product dictates the economics.*

---

## 1.4 Classification of Plastics (Quick Refresher)

| Family | Behaviour on Heating | Examples | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastics** | Soften on heating, harden on cooling — **reversible** | PE, PP, PVC, PS, ABS, PA (Nylon), POM, PC | Re-processable, recyclable |
| **Thermosetting Plastics** | Undergo irreversible cross-linking; **cannot be remelted** | Phenolics, Epoxies, Urea-Formaldehyde, Melamine, Polyester | High heat resistance, structural |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:**
> *Thermoplastics* are processed predominantly by **injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding** (because they melt and re-solidify). *Thermosets* are processed by **compression, transfer moulding, RIM** (because they chemically cure).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Relevant Properties of Plastics (Engineering-Relevant)

### 2.1.1 Thermal Properties

**(a) Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)**
- The temperature below which the polymer is in a **rigid, glassy state** and above which it becomes **rubbery/leathery**.
- Below Tg: molecular motion is restricted to vibrations only.
- Above Tg: long-chain segments gain mobility; the polymer softens.

**(b) Melting Temperature (Tm)**
- For **semi-crystalline thermoplastics** (e.g., PE, PP, PA, POM), Tm is a sharp transition where crystalline regions melt.
- Amorphous polymers (e.g., PS, PC, PMMA) **do not have a true Tm** — they gradually soften above Tg.

**(c) Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) / Deflection Temperature Under Load (DTUL)**
- The temperature at which a standard test bar deflects by **0.25 mm** under a specified load (usually **0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa**).
- Indicates **short-term heat resistance** for design purposes.

**(d) Vicat Softening Point (VSP)**
- The temperature at which a standardised needle penetrates **1 mm** into the polymer under a defined load.
- Used for quality control of thermoplastics.

**(e) Continuous Use Temperature (CUT)**
- Maximum temperature at which the polymer can be used for **long periods** without significant property loss.

---

### 2.1.2 Rheological (Flow) Properties

**(a) Melt Viscosity (η)**
- Resistance of the molten polymer to flow.
- Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**: viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate.
- This is critical for **injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding**.

**(b) Melt Flow Index (MFI) / Melt Flow Rate (MFR)**
- The mass of polymer (in grams) that flows through a standard capillary of **2.095 mm diameter** in **10 minutes** under a specified load (typically **2.16 kg at 190 °C for PE/PP, or 5 kg at 230 °C for PS/ABS**) — per **ASTM D1238 / ISO 1133**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> **High MFI → Low viscosity → Easy flow → Suitable for thin-walled injection moulding, complex geometry.**
> **Low MFI → High viscosity → Difficult flow → Suitable for extrusion, blow moulding, structural parts.**

**(c) Spiral Flow Length**
- The length of material that flows into a spiral mould cavity under standard conditions.
- Used to compare **processability** of different grades.

---

### 2.1.3 Mechanical Properties

| Property | Definition | Typical Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Tensile Strength** | Maximum tensile stress before break (MPa) | Design of load-bearing parts |
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | Stiffness in elastic region (MPa/GPa) | Deflection calculations |
| **Elongation at Break** | % strain before break | Ductility indicator |
| **Impact Strength (Izod/Charpy)** | Energy absorbed per unit notch (J/m) | Toughness, fracture resistance |
| **Hardness (Shore D / Rockwell R)** | Resistance to indentation | Wear resistance, surface durability |
| **Flexural Strength / Modulus** | Bending behaviour (MPa) | Beam, structural elements |

---

### 2.1.4 Physical & Other Properties

| Property | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Density (ρ)** | Mass per unit volume (g/cm³). Plastics are **1/4 to 1/8 the density of steel** — key for lightweighting. |
| **Mould Shrinkage** | Volumetric contraction from molten → solid. Typical **0.2 – 3.5%**. Must be compensated in mould cavity. |
| **Water Absorption (%)** | Mass gain after 24 hr immersion. Critical for **dimensional stability** of hygroscopic polymers (e.g., Nylon, ABS). |
| **Hygroscopicity** | Tendency to absorb atmospheric moisture. **Must be dried before processing** (e.g., Nylon dried at 80°C / 4 hrs; PET at 120°C / 4–6 hrs). |
| **Chemical Resistance** | Resistance to acids, alkalis, solvents. |
| **Weathering / UV Resistance** | Property retention under sunlight exposure. |
| **Flammability** | LOI (Limiting Oxygen Index) value. Self-extinguishing if LOI > 27. |
| **Dielectric Strength** | Voltage per unit thickness the insulation can withstand (kV/mm). |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) MFI} \quad & \text{MFR} = \frac{m \, (\text{g})}{t \, (\text{min})} \quad \left[ \text{g} / 10 \, \text{min} \right] \\[4pt]
\textbf{(2) Volumetric Shrinkage} \quad & S_v = \frac{V_{\text{mould}} - V_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{mould}}} \times 100 \% \\[4pt]
\textbf{(3) Linear Shrinkage} \quad & S_L \approx \frac{S_v}{3} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(4) Cavity Compensation} \quad & L_{\text{cavity}} = L_{\text{part}} \times (1 + S_L) \\[4pt]
\textbf{(5) Shot Weight (Injection)} \quad & W_{\text{shot}} = V_{\text{shot}} \times \rho_{\text{melt}} \times n \\[4pt]
\textbf{(6) Cooling Time (Ejection)} \quad & t_{\text{cool}} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{\text{wall}}}{T_e - T_{\text{wall}}}\right) \\[4pt]
\textbf{(7) Clamping Force} \quad & F_{\text{clamp}} = P_{\text{cavity}} \times A_{\text{projected}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(8) Extruder Output} \quad & Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(9) Density of Polymer Melt} \quad & \rho_{\text{melt}} \approx \rho_{\text{solid}} \times (1 - S_v) \\[4pt]
\textbf{(10) Power (Plasticising)} \quad & P = Q \times \Delta p \quad \text{or via viscous dissipation}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Where**:
> - $m$ = mass extruded, $t$ = time
> - $S_v, S_L$ = volumetric & linear shrinkage
> - $V_{\text{shot}}$ = shot volume, $n$ = number of cavities
> - $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T_m$ = melt temp, $T_e$ = ejection temp, $T_{\text{wall}}$ = mould wall temp
> - $D$ = screw dia, $N$ = rpm, $d$ = channel depth, $\phi$ = helix angle

---

## 2.3 KTU Process-Property Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-favourite** for "Compare the suitability of X for Y process" type questions.

| Polymer | Tg (°C) | Tm (°C) | MFI (g/10 min) | Best Suited Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LDPE** | −110 | 110 | 0.2 – 25 | Film blowing, blow moulding |
| **HDPE** | −120 | 130 | 0.1 – 15 | Blow moulding, injection, pipe extrusion |
| **PP** | −20 | 165 | 0.5 – 30 | Injection, fibre spinning, sheet |
| **PS** | 100 | — (amorphous) | 1 – 25 | Injection (cups, cases) |
| **ABS** | 105 | — (amorphous) | 1 – 30 | Injection (housings, automotive) |
| **PA 6,6 (Nylon)** | 50 | 265 | 5 – 50 (dry) | Injection (gears, bearings) |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | 145 | — (amorphous) | 3 – 25 | Injection (CDs, safety helmets) |
| **POM (Acetal)** | −50 | 175 | 1 – 30 | Precision injection (gears, fasteners) |
| **PMMA (Acrylic)** | 105 | — (amorphous) | 1 – 15 | Casting, injection (lenses) |
| **PVC (rigid)** | 80 | — (amorphous) | 0.4 – 2 | Pipe extrusion, profile extrusion |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** PP dashboards (low density, good impact), Nylon under-the-bonnet (high HDT).
- **Packaging:** LDPE film (high MFI, easy film blowing), PET bottles (extrusion-blow moulding).
- **Electronics:** ABS housings (good impact + aesthetic), PC for connectors (high Tg, dielectric).
- **Medical:** PMMA for intraocular lenses (castable, optically clear), PE for syringes.
- **Construction:** PVC pipes (extruded, chemically resistant, low cost).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Plastic Processing Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 scheme emphasis is on **Injection Moulding, Extrusion, Blow Moulding, Compression Moulding, Transfer Moulding, and Thermoforming**. Each is described below with mechanism, machine, products, advantages, limitations, and defects.

---

### 3.1.1 Injection Moulding (IM)

**Definition:** A process in which **molten polymer is forced under high pressure into a closed, cooled mould cavity**, where it solidifies into the desired shape. The most widely used plastics process for **high-volume, high-precision parts**.

**Working Cycle (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Mould Clamping** — Two halves of mould (cavity + core) are clamped by the toggle/ hydraulic clamping unit.
2. **Plasticising / Injection** — Rotating screw inside heated barrel melts, mixes, and conveys polymer forward. The screw acts as a plunger when the shot accumulates.
3. **Filling** — Molten polymer is injected at **high pressure (50 – 150 MPa)** through nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate into the mould cavity.
4. **Packing / Holding** — Additional material is packed in to compensate for volumetric shrinkage during cooling.
5. **Cooling** — Polymer solidifies in cavity; cooling time depends on wall thickness (typically **1–2 sec per mm wall**).
6. **Mould Opening & Ejection** — Mould opens; ejector pins push the part out.

---

**Key Derivation — Shot Weight & Number of Cavities**

A part has finished mass $W_p = 250 \, \text{g}$ and projected area $A_p = 200 \, \text{cm}^2$. The machine is to produce **4 cavities** with a runner system of $W_r = 80 \, \text{g}$. Melt density of PP is $\rho_m = 0.74 \, \text{g/cm}^3$. Calculate the **total shot weight**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Given:} \quad & W_p = 250 \, \text{g}, \quad W_r = 80 \, \text{g}, \quad n = 4 \text{ cavities} \\
\textbf{Step 1: Total part weight} \quad & W_{\text{parts}} = n \times W_p = 4 \times 250 = 1000 \, \text{g} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Add runner weight} \quad & W_{\text{shot}} = W_{\text{parts}} + W_r = 1000 + 80 = 1080 \, \text{g} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Verify machine capacity} \quad & V_{\text{shot}} = \frac{W_{\text{shot}}}{\rho_m} = \frac{1080}{0.74} = 1459.46 \, \text{cm}^3 \\
& V_{\text{shot}} \approx 1.46 \, \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The required shot volume of $\approx 1.46 \, \text{L}$ suggests a machine in the **200 – 250 tonne** clamping force range (rule of thumb: **1 tonne clamping per 1 – 2 cm² of projected area**).

---

**Key Derivation — Cooling Time (Simplified for KTU)**

For a part of wall thickness $s = 4 \, \text{mm}$, $T_m = 240 \, °C$, ejection temp $T_e = 90 \, °C$, mould wall temp $T_{\text{wall}} = 40 \, °C$, and $\alpha = 0.001 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Given:} \quad & s = 0.4 \, \text{cm}, \quad T_m = 240, \quad T_e = 90, \quad T_{\text{wall}} = 40, \quad \alpha = 0.001 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{s} \\
\textbf{Step 1: Compute the bracket} \quad & \frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{\text{wall}}}{T_e - T_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{8}{9.87} \cdot \frac{240 - 40}{90 - 40} = 0.8105 \cdot 4 = 3.242 \\
\textbf{Step 2: ln term} \quad & \ln(3.242) = 1.176 \\
\textbf{Step 3: Denominator} \quad & \pi^2 \alpha = 9.87 \times 0.001 = 0.00987 \\
\textbf{Step 4: Solve} \quad & t_{\text{cool}} = \frac{(0.4)^2 \times 1.176}{0.00987} = \frac{0.16 \times 1.176}{0.00987} = \frac{0.18816}{0.00987} \approx 19.06 \, \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Cooling time for a 4 mm PP wall is **≈ 19 s**, i.e., roughly **4–5 s/mm** — useful for KTU numericals.

---

### 3.1.2 Extrusion

**Definition:** A continuous process in which a **polymer melt is forced through a shaped die** to produce a continuous profile (pipe, sheet, film, rod, wire coating, etc.).

**Key Components:** Hopper → Heated Barrel (3–5 zones) → **Single-Screw Extruder** (compression ratio 2:1 to 4:1) → Die → Cooling → Puller → Cutter/Winder.

**Three Functional Zones of Screw:**
- **Feed Zone (≈ 50% length):** Conveying solid pellets (deep channel, ~0.15D).
- **Compression/Melting Zone (≈ 25–30%):** Convective + shear melting (channel depth decreases).
- **Metering Zone (≈ 20–25%):** Homogenised melt pumping (shallow channel, ~0.05D).

> [!NOTE]
> **Extruder Output Derivation (Newtonian Approximation, for KTU):**
> For a single-screw extruder of diameter $D$, screw speed $N$, channel depth $H$, and helix angle $\phi$:
> $$Q_{\text{drag}} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \quad \text{(drag flow, forward)}$$
> $$Q_{\text{pressure}} = -\frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\phi \, \Delta P}{12 \eta L} \quad \text{(pressure flow, backward)}$$
> $$\boxed{Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{drag}} - Q_{\text{pressure}}}$$

> For **open discharge** ($\Delta P = 0$), $Q = Q_{\text{drag}}$.

**Worked Example — Extruder Output (KTU Standard):**
A single-screw extruder has $D = 60 \, \text{mm}$, $N = 60 \, \text{rpm}$, $H = 3 \, \text{mm}$, $\phi = 17.65°$ (commonly used), open discharge. Compute the output.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Convert units to SI} \quad & D = 0.06 \, \text{m}, \quad H = 0.003 \, \text{m}, \quad N = 1 \, \text{rps} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Trig values} \quad & \sin 17.65° = 0.303, \quad \cos 17.65° = 0.953 \\
\textbf{Step 3: Substitute} \quad & Q = \frac{\pi^2 (0.06)^2 (1) (0.003) (0.303)(0.953)}{2} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Compute numerator} \quad & \pi^2 \times 0.0036 \times 0.003 \times 0.2888 = 0.03294 \\
\textbf{Step 5: Divide} \quad & Q = \frac{0.03294}{2} = 0.01647 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 16.47 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Convert to throughput (LDPE, $\rho \approx 0.92$):** $\dot{m} = 16.47 \times 0.92 = 15.15 \, \text{g/s} \approx 54.5 \, \text{kg/hr}$. A typical 60 mm extruder is rated 50–80 kg/hr — matches.

---

### 3.1.3 Blow Moulding

**Definition:** A process for producing **hollow plastic parts** (bottles, containers, tanks) by inflating a molten polymer **parison (tube)** inside a mould using compressed air.

**Two Main Variants:**

| Variant | Process | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** | Continuous extruder → parison → mould closes → air blown → cool → eject | HDPE bottles, jerry cans |
| **Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)** | Injection moulded preform → reheated → blown in second mould | PET bottles (water, soft drink) |
| **Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** | Preform is axially stretched + blown for biaxial orientation | PET carbonated drink bottles |

---

### 3.1.4 Compression Moulding

**Definition:** A process in which a **pre-weighed charge of thermosetting resin (or BMC/SMC)** is placed directly in an **open heated mould cavity**. The mould is then closed, applying **heat and pressure** to cure the material.

- **Suitable for:** Thermosets (Phenolic, Melamine, Epoxy, Urea-formaldehyde), **SMC** (Sheet Moulding Compound), **BMC** (Bulk Moulding Compound), and rubber products.
- **Cycle time:** 1–10 min (due to chemical curing).
- **Pressure:** 10–50 MPa.

---

### 3.1.5 Transfer Moulding

**Definition:** A hybrid of injection and compression moulding. The thermoset charge is **preheated in a transfer pot (above the mould)** and then forced through a **sprue and runner system** into the closed heated mould cavity.

- **Advantages over Compression:** Less flash, better dimensional accuracy, suitable for **inserts** (metal inserts in mouldings).
- **Disadvantage:** Wastage of material in sprue/runner.

---

### 3.1.6 Thermoforming

**Definition:** A process in which a **plastic sheet** is heated to its **softening temperature**, then **draped or forced against a mould** by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means.

- **Sub-types:** Vacuum forming, Pressure forming, Mechanical (matched-die) forming.
- **Materials:** HIPS, PVC, PP, PET, ABS sheets.
- **Products:** Disposable cups, blister packs, refrigerator liners, shower cubicles, dashboard skins.

---

### 3.1.7 Other Important Processes (Brief, for KTU Awareness)

| Process | One-line Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotational Moulding** | Powder placed in closed mould → heated & rotated biaxially → fuses to inner wall | Large hollow tanks, kayaks |
| **Casting** | Liquid resin (e.g., PMMA monomer, PU, epoxy) poured into mould, cured without pressure | Acrylic sheets, PU encapsulations, prototype parts |
| **Calendering** | Molten polymer passed through a series of heated rollers to form a thin continuous film/sheet | PVC films, flooring sheets |
| **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** | Two reactive liquid components (polyol + isocyanate) mixed at high pressure, injected into mould, cures rapidly | Automotive bumpers, PU foams |
| **Film Blowing** | Extrusion of a thin tube + air inflation to form a thin film | LDPE/HDPE packaging film |
| **Fibre Spinning** | Melt or solution extruded through spinnerets to form continuous filaments | Polyester, Nylon fibres |
| **3D Printing / Additive Mfg.** | Layer-by-layer deposition of material (FDM, SLA, SLS) | Prototypes, low-volume custom parts |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Guide (For 14-Mark Answers)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as a **comparison-table backbone** in KTU 14-mark "Compare/Discuss" questions.

| Criterion | Injection | Extrusion | Blow | Compression | Transfer | Thermoforming |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Type** | Cyclic | Continuous | Cyclic | Cyclic | Cyclic | Cyclic |
| **Polymer State** | Thermoplastic | Thermoplastic | Thermoplastic | Thermoset | Thermoset | Thermoplastic (sheet) |
| **Melt Pressure** | High (50–150 MPa) | Medium (5–30 MPa) | Low–Med (0.3–1 MPa) | Med (10–50 MPa) | Med–High | Vacuum/Low |
| **Mould Cost** | Very High | Low (die) | Med | Med–High | High | Low (often wood/cast Al) |
| **Product Type** | 3D solid parts | Continuous profile | Hollow | Solid (often large) | Solid w/ inserts | Shallow shells |
| **Surface Finish** | Excellent | N/A (continuous) | Good | Fair–Good | Good | Med–Good |
| **Cycle Time** | 10–120 s | Continuous | 5–30 s | 60–600 s | 60–300 s | 5–60 s |
| **Typical Materials** | PP, ABS, PA, PC | PE, PP, PVC | HDPE, PET, PP | Phenolic, SMC, BMC | Epoxy, Phenolic | HIPS, PVC, PET |

---

## 3.3 Common Defects in Plastics Processing (KTU Favourite — 7 mark sub-part)

| Defect | Process | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sink marks** | Injection | Insufficient packing; thick section | Increase pack/hold pressure; redesign wall |
| **Weld lines** | Injection/Blow | Two melt fronts meet | Increase melt temp; better venting |
| **Flash** | Compression/Injection | Excess pressure; worn mould | Lower pressure; mould maintenance |
| **Bubbles** | Extrusion/IM | Moisture in resin | Pre-dry the resin |
| **Shark skin** | Extrusion | Surface instability at high shear | Reduce output; use processing aid |
| **Short shot** | Injection | Insufficient material/pressure | Increase shot size; reduce flow length |
| **Warping** | Injection/Thermoforming | Uneven cooling/shrinkage | Uniform cooling; reduce mould temp gradient |
| **Blow pin mark** | Blow | Pin punch too cold | Heat blow pin |
| **Burn marks** | Injection | Air entrapment | Improve venting; lower injection speed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Plastics (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[PLASTICS]
    A1[THERMOPLASTICS]
    A2[THERMOSETTING PLASTICS]

    B1[Amorphous]
    B2[Semi Crystalline]

    C1[PS ABS PMMA PC]
    C2[PE PP PA POM PET]

    D1[Addition Polymerised]
    D2[Condensation Polymerised]

    E1[Phenolic Melamine Urea Epoxy Unsaturated Polyester]
    E2[PU Silicones Alkyd]

    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    A2 --> D1
    A2 --> D2
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
```

---

## 4.2 Injection Moulding Machine — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hopper and Feed Throat]
    A2[Heated Barrel Zone 1 to 3]
    A3[Reciprocating Screw]
    A4[Nozzle]
    A5[Sprue and Runner System]
    A6[Closed Mould Cavity]
    A7[Cooling Channels in Mould]
    A8[Clamping Unit Toggle or Hydraulic]
    A9[Ejector Pins]
    A10[Finished Plastic Part]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A8 --- A6
    A6 --> A9 --> A10
```

> **Reading Guide:** Polymer pellets fall from the hopper, get melted in the heated barrel by the rotating screw, are injected through the nozzle/sprue/runner into the closed mould, cooled via internal water channels, and finally ejected by pins after mould opening.

---

## 4.3 Injection Moulding Cycle — Process State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StepMouldClose
    StepMouldClose: M1 Mould Closing
    StepInject: M2 Injection and Filling
    StepPack: M3 Packing and Holding
    StepCool: M4 Cooling
    StepOpen: M5 Mould Opening
    StepEject: M6 Ejection

    StepMouldClose --> StepInject
    StepInject --> StepPack
    StepPack --> StepCool
    StepCool --> StepOpen
    StepOpen --> StepEject
    StepEject --> StepMouldClose
    StepEject --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Extrusion Line — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Resin Hopper]
    P2[Heated Barrel with Screw]
    P3[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
    P4[Shaped Die]
    P5[Air Ring or Cooling Bath]
    P6[Puller or Haul Off Unit]
    P7[Cutter or Winder]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7
```

> **Die Examples:** Pipe Die, Sheet Die, Film Blowing Die, Profile Die, Wire Coating Die.

---

## 4.5 Compression vs Transfer Moulding — Comparative Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Thermoset Processing]
    N1[Compression Moulding]
    N2[Transfer Moulding]

    C1[Charge placed in open mould]
    C2[Plunger forces melt from transfer pot]
    C3[Mould closes and cures]
    C4[Mould closed already plunger injects through sprue]
    C5[Eject cured part]
    C6[Eject cured part with sprue wastage]

    N0 --> N1
    N0 --> N2
    N1 --> C1 --> C3 --> C5
    N2 --> C2 --> C4 --> C6
```

---

## 4.6 Process Property Relationship — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Polymer type}
    Q1 --> Q2[Thermoplastic]
    Q1 --> Q3[Thermosetting]

    Q2 --> Q4{Geometry}
    Q4 --> Q5[3D Solid]
    Q4 --> Q6[Continuous Profile]
    Q4 --> Q7[Hollow]

    Q5 --> R1[Injection Moulding]
    Q6 --> R2[Extrusion]
    Q7 --> R3[Blow Moulding]

    Q3 --> Q8{Charge method}
    Q8 --> Q9[Direct in cavity]
    Q8 --> Q10[Transfer from pot]

    Q9 --> R4[Compression Moulding]
    Q10 --> R5[Transfer Moulding]
```

> **Engineering Takeaway:** This tree is a **complete visual answer** to any KTU question of the form *"Select a suitable plastics processing method for a given component."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define Melt Flow Index (MFI) of a polymer. Mention its significance in injection moulding. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Melt Flow Index (MFI), also called Melt Flow Rate (MFR), is defined as **the mass of polymer in grams that flows through a standard capillary of 2.095 mm diameter in 10 minutes under a specified load and temperature**, as per **ASTM D1238 / ISO 1133**.

**Mathematically:**
$$\text{MFI} = \frac{m}{t} \quad \text{[g/10 min]}$$

**Significance in Injection Moulding:**
- A **high MFI** indicates low melt viscosity → easy filling of thin-walled, complex geometry parts at lower pressures.
- A **low MFI** indicates high viscosity → suitable for thick sections, structural parts, extrusion.
- Helps in **grade selection, mould design (gate size, runner), and processing parameter setting**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting plastics. Give two examples of each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting Plastic |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular structure** | Linear or branched chains | 3D cross-linked network |
| **Effect of heat** | Softens on heating, hardens on cooling (reversible) | Undergoes irreversible chemical curing |
| **Recyclability** | Can be remelted and reprocessed | Cannot be remelted (degrades) |
| **Processing** | Injection, extrusion, blow moulding, thermoforming | Compression, transfer moulding, RIM |
| **Mechanical strength at high T** | Reduces (above Tg) | Retained up to degradation T |
| **Examples** | PE, PP, PVC, PS, ABS, Nylon, PC | Phenolic, Melamine, Epoxy, Urea-Formaldehyde, Unsaturated Polyester |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **internal choice** (OR option). Both options are presented below with full model answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Internal Choice Option 1)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
> **Q. A(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **injection moulding process** for plastics. Discuss the function of the **screw and barrel assembly**, and the role of **clamping force**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. A(b)** A polypropylene part has a finished mass of **120 g**, projected area of **150 cm²**, mould cavity shrinkage of **1.5%**, and runner/sprue mass of **60 g**. Calculate **(i) the required shot volume**, **(ii) the cavity dimension if a 100 mm linear dimension is to be achieved**, and **(iii) the indicative clamping force**. Take density of PP melt = 0.74 g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Part A(a)**

**1. Process Description (Sketch in words):**
Injection moulding is a cyclic process in which a **measured quantity of molten thermoplastic is injected under high pressure into a closed, water-cooled mould**, where it solidifies into the desired shape.

**2. Screw and Barrel Assembly (Key function):**
- The **barrel** is externally heated (typically 3–4 zones, 180–280 °C) and internally conveys, melts, and pressurises the polymer.
- The **screw** performs three functions sequentially:
  - **Feed zone** — Conveys solid pellets forward by friction.
  - **Compression (melting) zone** — Shear + external heat melts pellets.
  - **Metering zone** — Homogenises melt and generates pumping pressure.
- During injection, the screw acts as a **plunger** (axial translation) without rotation, forcing melt through nozzle into mould.

**3. Clamping Force (Role):**
- Keeps the mould halves closed against the high cavity pressure (50–150 MPa).
- Must exceed the **cavity pressure × projected area** of the part.
- Insufficient clamping → **flash**, mould opening, or mould damage.

**Sketch Reference:** See SECTION 4.2 (block diagram) and SECTION 4.3 (state machine).

---

#### **Model Answer — Part A(b)**

**Given:** $W_p = 120 \, \text{g}$, $W_r = 60 \, \text{g}$, $S_v = 1.5\%$, $L_{\text{part}} = 100 \, \text{mm}$, $\rho_m = 0.74 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, $A_p = 150 \, \text{cm}^2$.

**Sub-part (i) — Shot Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Total mass} \quad & W_{\text{shot}} = W_p + W_r = 120 + 60 = 180 \, \text{g} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Volume} \quad & V_{\text{shot}} = \frac{W_{\text{shot}}}{\rho_m} = \frac{180}{0.74} = 243.24 \, \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $V_{\text{shot}} \approx 243.2 \, \text{cm}^3$ (or **0.243 L**).

**Sub-part (ii) — Cavity Dimension:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Linear shrinkage} \quad & S_L \approx \frac{S_v}{3} = \frac{1.5}{3} = 0.5\% \\
\textbf{Step 2: Cavity size} \quad & L_{\text{cavity}} = L_{\text{part}} \times (1 + S_L) = 100 \times (1 + 0.005) = 100.5 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** $L_{\text{cavity}} = 100.5 \, \text{mm}$.

**Sub-part (iii) — Clamping Force (indicative):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Using rule of thumb} \quad & F_{\text{clamp}} \approx 5 \, \text{to} \, 7 \, \text{tonne per} \, 100 \, \text{cm}^2 \\
& A_p = 150 \, \text{cm}^2 \Rightarrow F_{\text{clamp}} \approx 7.5 \, \text{to} \, 10.5 \, \text{tonne} \\
\textbf{Or via cavity pressure} \quad & P_{\text{cav}} = 30 \, \text{MPa} \text{ (typical for PP)} \\
& F_{\text{clamp}} = P_{\text{cav}} \times A_p = 30 \times 150 = 4500 \, \text{kN} \\
& 4500 \, \text{kN} \approx 459 \, \text{tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (typical exam expectation):** Use the simpler **tonne/area rule** → **≈ 8–10 tonne clamping force** is reasonable for KTU answers. Always state the assumption explicitly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Internal Choice Option 2)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **Q. B(a)** List and briefly explain **any four important properties of plastics** that influence their processing behaviour. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. B(b)** With the help of a **labelled schematic**, explain the **extrusion process** for plastics. A single-screw extruder has **screw diameter 50 mm, screw speed 80 rpm, channel depth 2.5 mm, and helix angle 17.65°**. For open discharge conditions, calculate the **volumetric output in cm³/s and throughput in kg/hr** (take polymer density = 0.92 g/cm³). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Part B(a)**

**Four Important Properties Influencing Processing:**

**(1) Melt Viscosity (η):**
- Resistance of molten polymer to flow; decreases with shear (pseudoplastic).
- High viscosity → high injection pressure required, risk of shear heating.
- Low viscosity → easy filling but risk of flash and drooling.

**(2) Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) and Melting Point (Tm):**
- Determines **mould temperature, melt temperature, and demoulding temperature**.
- Amorphous polymers (PS, PC) process above Tg; semi-crystalline (PE, PP, PA) process above Tm.
- Defines upper service temperature.

**(3) Mould Shrinkage:**
- Volumetric contraction of polymer on cooling (typical 0.2 – 3.5%).
- Must be compensated in mould cavity design.
- Influences **dimensional accuracy** and post-mould warping.

**(4) Hygroscopicity / Water Absorption:**
- Nylon, ABS, PC, PMMA absorb moisture → cause **hydrolysis, bubbles, voids, surface streaks**.
- Must be **pre-dried** (e.g., Nylon at 80 °C for 4 hr; PC at 120 °C for 4–6 hr) before processing.

> *(Optional 5th & 6th for higher marks: Heat Deflection Temperature, Melt Flow Index.)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Part B(b)**

**1. Extrusion Process — Schematic Description:**

The plastic extrusion process consists of:
- **Hopper** — feeds polymer pellets.
- **Heated Barrel with Screw** — melts and pumps the polymer.
- **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack** — filters contaminants and converts rotary to linear flow.
- **Die** — shapes the melt into the desired profile (pipe, sheet, film, etc.).
- **Cooling System** — water bath or air ring to solidify the extrudate.
- **Puller / Haul-off** — pulls the extrudate at constant velocity.
- **Cutter or Winder** — cuts to length or winds as continuous product.

**Screw Zones:** Feed (≈ 50%) → Compression/Melting (≈ 25–30%) → Metering (≈ 20–25%).

(See SECTION 4.4 for block diagram.)

**2. Numerical:**

**Given:** $D = 50 \, \text{mm} = 5 \, \text{cm}$, $N = 80 \, \text{rpm}$, $H = 2.5 \, \text{mm} = 0.25 \, \text{cm}$, $\phi = 17.65°$, $\rho = 0.92 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, open discharge.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Convert N to rps} \quad & N = \frac{80}{60} = 1.333 \, \text{rps} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Trig values} \quad & \sin 17.65° = 0.303, \quad \cos 17.65° = 0.953 \\
\textbf{Step 3: Drag flow formula} \quad & Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Substitute} \quad & Q = \frac{(9.87)(5^2)(1.333)(0.25)(0.303)(0.953)}{2} \\
\textbf{Step 5: Numerator} \quad & 9.87 \times 25 \times 1.333 \times 0.25 \times 0.2888 = 23.76 \\
\textbf{Step 6: Divide} \quad & Q = \frac{23.76}{2} = 11.88 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Volumetric output:** $Q \approx 11.88 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$.

**Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} & = Q \times \rho = 11.88 \times 0.92 = 10.93 \, \text{g/s} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{hr}} & = 10.93 \times 3600 = 39,348 \, \text{g/hr} = 39.35 \, \text{kg/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Throughput:** $\approx 39.3 \, \text{kg/hr}$ (a reasonable value for a 50 mm lab extruder).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module-2 Plastics Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Tg and Tm:** Tg is *not* a melting point. Amorphous plastics **do not have a Tm** — only Tg. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Units of MFI:** Always state MFI in **g/10 min**, not g/min or g/s. Wrong unit = 0.5 mark lost.
> 3. **Forgetting drying:** If a question mentions **Nylon, PC, ABS, PMMA, PET** and the answer says "feed into hopper directly" → **2 marks lost** (hygroscopicity rule).
> 4. **Confusing injection vs extrusion shot size:** Injection is **discrete (one shot)**, extrusion is **continuous (per second or per hour)**. Mixing units = full-step loss.
> 5. **Clamping force calculation:** Most students forget to **multiply by projected area**. Simply stating "50 tonne" without calculation = 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Screw zones:** Writing only "melting" without naming **feed-compression-metering** = 1.5 marks lost in 7-mark questions.
> 7. **Cooling time formula:** Using $t = s^2 / \alpha$ alone (only valid for very thick sections) — examiners expect the **Tmm–Te–Twall form** for mould design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Plastics (Properties + Processing):**

- [x] **Plastics = polymers + additives (plasticisers, stabilisers, fillers, colourants, flame retardants).**
- [x] **Two families: Thermoplastics (reversible) vs Thermosets (irreversible cross-link).**
- [x] **Key thermal properties: Tg, Tm, HDT, Vicat, CUT.**
- [x] **Key rheological property: Melt Viscosity η and MFI (g/10 min).**
- [x] **MFI inversely proportional to viscosity; high MFI → thin-wall injection; low MFI → extrusion/blow.**
- [x] **Mould shrinkage: $S_L \approx S_v / 3$; cavity oversize by factor $(1 + S_L)$.**
- [x] **Hygroscopic polymers (Nylon, PC, ABS, PET, PMMA) MUST be pre-dried.**
- [x] **Six major processes:** Injection (cyclic, 3D solid), Extrusion (continuous, profile), Blow (hollow), Compression (thermoset direct), Transfer (thermoset via pot), Thermoforming (sheet → shell).
- [x] **Injection cycle:** Clamp → Plasticise → Inject → Pack → Cool → Open → Eject.
- [x] **Extruder screw zones:** Feed (50%) → Compression (25–30%) → Metering (20–25%); compression ratio 2:1–4:1.
- [x] **Extruder output (open discharge): $Q = (\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi)/2$.**
- [x] **Clamping force rule:** $F_{\text{clamp}} = P_{\text{cav}} \times A_{\text{projected}}$; or 5–7 tonne per 100 cm².
- [x] **Cooling time:** proportional to (wall thickness)² — **doubling wall thickness quadruples cooling time**.
- [x] **Common defects:** Sink marks (insufficient pack), weld lines (front meeting), flash (excess pressure), short shot (insufficient material), warping (uneven cooling), bubbles (moisture).
- [x] **Process selection mantra:** *Polymer → Process → Product → Economics.*
- [x] **Always pair:** Process with the polymer's *Tg/Tm range*, *viscosity range*, and *shrinkage behaviour* when answering "selection" questions.

---

> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
