<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plastic Extrusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Plastic Extrusion is a high-volume continuous manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic polymer compound (in pellet, granule, or powder form) is melted, homogenized, and forced under high pressure through a shaped die to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste horizontally through a shaped nozzle opening. The toothpaste (molten plastic) takes the exact cross-sectional shape of the nozzle opening (die) and emerges as a continuous, uniform strand. A real-world example is a pasta-making machine — the dough is pushed through a perforated plate and emerges as spaghetti (constant cross-section) regardless of how long you keep pushing.

**Key Process Variables:**
- **Barrel Temperature Profile** (typically 150 °C – 280 °C depending on polymer)
- **Screw Rotation Speed (N)** in **rpm**
- **Die Pressure Drop** (ΔP) in **MPa**
- **Throughput (Q)** in **kg/hr** or **cm³/s**

---

## 1.2 Injection Moulding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Injection moulding is a cyclic, high-pressure manufacturing process in which a measured quantity (the "shot") of molten thermoplastic or thermosetting polymer is injected under high pressure into a closed, temperature-controlled mould cavity, where it solidifies by cooling (or curing) to form a finished part, after which the mould opens and the part is ejected.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of injecting molten chocolate into a fancy silicone ice-tray mould using a syringe. You push (inject) the molten material into the cavity, wait for it to harden, then open the mould and pop out the finished chocolate shape. Industrial injection moulding does this thousands of times per day at pressures of 50–150 MPa.

**Core Elements:**
- **Shot Volume (Vₛ)** — volume of polymer injected per cycle
- **Injection Pressure (Pᵢ)** — typically **50 MPa to 150 MPa**
- **Clamping Force (F_c)** — keeps mould closed against injection pressure
- **Cycle Time (t_c)** — total time for one complete cycle (typically 10 s – 120 s)

---

## 1.3 Rotational Moulding (Roto-Moulding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Rotational moulding is a low-pressure, high-temperature, slow-cycle manufacturing process in which a measured quantity of polymer powder is placed inside a closed hollow mould, the mould is rotated biaxially (around two perpendicular axes) in an oven, the polymer melts and coats the inside surface of the mould by gravity and centrifugal action, then the mould is cooled and the hollow part is removed.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine putting a small amount of butter inside a metal ball, lighting a fire under the ball, and slowly rotating it like a chicken on a rotisserie spit (along both X and Y axes simultaneously). The melted butter coats the entire inner wall uniformly. Cool it down, cut it open, and you have a hollow plastic shell — like a water tank or a kayak.

**Key Feature:** Unlike injection moulding, there is **NO PRESSURE** involved. The process relies on **gravity** and mild centrifugal action.

---

## 1.4 Plastic Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Extrusion Blow Moulding is a continuous process for manufacturing hollow thermoplastic parts (bottles, containers, tanks) in which a hollow polymer tube called a "parison" is extruded vertically downward, captured between two halves of a cooled mould, and inflated by air pressure against the mould walls to take the mould shape.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Blow a soap bubble using a bubble wand dipped in soap solution. The soap film (parison) is "extruded" by dipping, then air is blown in to expand it into a spherical shape. Industrial extrusion blow moulding is essentially this — but the "soap film" is molten plastic and the "wand" is a precision extrusion die.

---

## 1.5 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Injection Blow Moulding is a two-stage process combining injection moulding of a preform (a test-tube-shaped piece) followed by blow moulding of that preform inside a bottle-shaped mould using compressed air. It produces hollow parts with very accurate neck finish and excellent dimensional consistency.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** First, you make a small thick-walled test tube (the preform) using injection moulding. Then you place this preform inside a bottle-shaped mould and blow air into it (like inflating a balloon inside a bottle-shaped box). The preform stretches and conforms to the bottle shape.

---

## 1.6 Vacuum Casting (Silicone Mould Vacuum Casting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Vacuum Casting (also called Polyurethane Vacuum Casting) is a prototyping process in which liquid polyurethane resin is poured into a soft silicone rubber mould placed inside a vacuum chamber. The vacuum removes entrapped air bubbles, allowing the resin to faithfully replicate fine surface detail from a master pattern.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of making Jello in a fancy silicone ice-cube tray — but instead of just pouring and letting it set in air, you do it inside a vacuum cleaner bag so that no air bubbles get trapped inside the Jello. The result is a crystal-clear, defect-free replica.

---

## 1.7 Thermoforming and Vacuum Forming

> [![!IMPORTANT]**
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Thermoforming is a manufacturing process in which a flat thermoplastic sheet is heated to its softening temperature, then forced against a mould surface by mechanical pressure, vacuum, or compressed air, where it cools and retains the mould shape. Vacuum Forming is the specific subtype in which the differential pressure is created by evacuating the air between the sheet and the mould.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Place a piece of cling-wrap over a bowl, then use a vacuum cleaner hose to suck the air out from underneath. Atmospheric pressure on top pushes the cling-wrap down, making it conform perfectly to the bowl's shape. Industrial vacuum forming does exactly this with thick plastic sheets and aluminium moulds.

**Two Main Subtypes:**
- **Vacuum Forming** — atmospheric pressure pushes sheet onto mould (pressure differential ≈ 0.1 MPa)
- **Pressure Forming** — compressed air (≈ 0.3–0.7 MPa) pushes the sheet

---

## 1.8 Compression Moulding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Compression Moulding is a process in which a pre-measured charge of thermosetting plastic (or rubber) compound is placed directly into an open heated mould cavity, the mould is closed under high pressure (typically 10–50 MPa), heat and pressure cure/harden the material, and the finished part is ejected.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Making a sandwich in a panini press! You put the dough (compound) between two heated plates (mould halves), press down hard (moulding pressure), wait for the heat to cook/cure it, and then open the press to take out the finished sandwich (moulded part).

**Used for:** Phenolic resins, melamine, urea-formaldehyde, rubber products (tyres, gaskets), sheet moulding compounds (SMC), bulk moulding compounds (BMC).

---

## 1.9 Rubber Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Rubber manufacturing encompasses the processes by which raw natural rubber (latex) or synthetic rubber is compounded with additives, shaped, and vulcanized (cross-linked with sulphur at elevated temperature) to produce elastic, durable elastomeric products.*

**Conceptual Analogy:** Raw natural rubber is sticky and useless — like a piece of chewing gum that melts in summer. By mixing it with sulphur powder and baking it (vulcanization), the rubber molecules get "tied together" with sulphur bridges, becoming the elastic, bouncy, heat-resistant rubber we use in tyres, gaskets, and shoe soles.

**Major Process Routes:**
- **Compression Moulding of Rubber** — most common for thick rubber parts (tyres, large gaskets)
- **Injection Moulding of Rubber** — for small precision rubber parts
- **Extrusion of Rubber** — for hoses, weather-stripping, tyre treads
- **Calendaring** — for rubber sheets
- **Tyre Building** — automated assembly of rubber-coated layers on a rotating drum

---

## 1.10 Process Selection Matrix — Quick Comparison

| Process | Pressure | Cycle/Continuous | Polymer Type | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Extrusion | High | Continuous | Thermoplastics | Pipes, sheets, profiles, films |
| Injection Moulding | Very High | Cyclic | Thermoplastics | Toys, caps, housings |
| Rotational Moulding | None (gravity) | Cyclic | Thermoplastics (PE) | Tanks, kayaks, balls |
| Extrusion Blow Moulding | Medium | Cyclic | Thermoplastics | Bottles, jerry cans |
| Injection Blow Moulding | Medium | Cyclic | Thermoplastics (PET) | PET bottles |
| Vacuum Casting | Vacuum | Cyclic | PU resins | Prototypes |
| Thermoforming | Low | Cyclic | Thermoplastic sheets | Trays, cups, blisters |
| Compression Moulding | High | Cyclic | Thermosets/Rubber | Tyres, switches, plates |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Plastic Extrusion — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Single-Screw Extruder — Three Functional Zones

**Why three zones?** Because the polymer must undergo three distinct physical changes in sequence:

1. **Solid Conveying Zone (Feed Zone)** — *Why?* Pellets are solid; they must be transported forward without melting. The deep screw flights (≈ 0.1–0.15 D, where D is screw diameter) and the barrel friction propel them.
2. **Compression/Melting Zone (Transition Zone)** — *Why?* Heat from the barrel (resistive heaters on outside) and frictional shear between screw and barrel melt the pellets. Screw depth gradually decreases (compression ratio typically 2:1 to 4:1).
3. **Metering Zone (Pumping Zone)** — *Why?* Molten polymer must be pressurized and delivered at a steady, uniform rate. Shallow, constant-depth flights act as a positive displacement pump.

### 2.1.2 Throughput Equation (Newtonian Approximation)

For a Newtonian polymer melt in the metering zone of an extruder:

$$Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric throughput (m³/s)
- $D$ = screw diameter (m)
- $N$ = screw rotational speed (rev/s)
- $d$ = channel depth in metering zone (m)
- $\theta$ = helix angle of screw (typically 17.65°)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along extruder (Pa)
- $\eta$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = axial length of metering zone (m)

**Why two terms?** 
- **First term (drag flow)** — represents forward pumping due to screw rotation against stationary barrel.
- **Second term (pressure flow)** — represents backward leakage due to pressure build-up.

**Net Throughput:** $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$

### 2.1.3 Die Pressure Drop for a Capillary (Circular) Die

$$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

This is the classic **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**. Re-arranged for pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Pressure drop in the die is **inversely proportional to the fourth power of radius**. Halving the radius increases pressure drop by **16×**. This is why very thin extrudates are difficult to produce.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
- Pipe manufacturing (PE/PVC water and gas pipes)
- Blown film (PE packaging films)
- Sheet extrusion for thermoforming feedstock
- Wire and cable insulation
- 3D printer filament (PLA, ABS)

---

## 2.2 Injection Moulding — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 The Injection Moulding Cycle (Step-by-Step)

**Why each step matters:**

1. **Mould Closing** — Two mould halves clamp together. Aligning surfaces must mate precisely.
2. **Injection (Filling)** — Screw acts as a plunger; pushes melt into cavity at high speed.
3. **Packing/Holding** — Pressure is maintained to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as material solidifies. *Why?* Without packing, sink marks and voids appear.
4. **Cooling** — Water/oil circulates through mould channels. Polymer solidifies. Most cycle time is here.
5. **Plastication** — Screw rotates, melts next shot, retracts to metering position.
6. **Mould Opening & Ejection** — Mould halves separate; ejector pins push part out.

### 2.2.2 Clamping Force — The Most Critical Machine Specification

The mould must be held closed against the hydraulic pressure trying to push it open.

$$F_c = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$$

where:
- $F_c$ = required clamping force (N or kN or tonnes-force)
- $P_{cavity}$ = pressure inside cavity (typically 30–80 MPa; for engineering plastics up to 120 MPa)
- $A_{projected}$ = projected area of part + runner system on mould parting plane (mm²)

**In tonnes-force** (commonly used in industry):
$$F_c \text{ [tonnes]} = \frac{P \text{ [MPa]} \times A \text{ [cm}^2\text{]}}{98.1}$$

### 2.2.3 Shot Weight and Shot Volume

$$V_s = n \cdot V_{cavity}$$

where $n$ is the number of cavities. The corresponding weight is:

$$W_s = V_s \cdot \rho_{melt}$$

where $\rho_{melt}$ is the melt density (typically 90–95% of solid density).

### 2.2.4 Cooling Time Estimate (Simplified)

$$t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{eject} - T_{mould}}\right)$$

where:
- $s$ = maximum wall thickness (m)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of polymer (≈ 1 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s for most plastics)
- $T_{melt}$ = injection melt temperature
- $T_{mould}$ = mould wall temperature
- $T_{eject}$ = safe ejection temperature (typically above glass transition $T_g$ for amorphous, or below crystallisation temperature for semi-crystalline)

### 2.2.5 Real-World Utility
- Consumer electronics housings (mobile phone cases, laptops)
- Automotive parts (dashboards, bumpers, clips)
- Medical disposables (syringes, IV components)
- Bottle caps and closures

---

## 2.3 Rotational Moulding — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 The Three Critical Stages

1. **Charging** — Polymer powder (or liquid plastisol) is placed inside the closed hollow mould.
2. **Heating & Rotation** — Mould is rotated biaxially in an oven. Centrifugal force:
$$F_c = m \omega^2 r$$
This acts radially outward, but because the mould is constantly rotating, the powder "tumbles" and gradually adheres to the inner surface as it melts.
3. **Cooling** — Mould is moved to a cooling station (forced air, water spray). Part crystallises/solidifies and is removed.

### 2.3.2 Critical Thickness Ratio (Trapped Air Theory)

The wall thickness of a roto-moulded part is governed by **the amount of powder charged** and **mould volume**:

$$t_{part} = \frac{m_{powder}}{\rho_{melt} \times A_{mould}}$$

where $A_{mould}$ is the total inner surface area of the mould.

### 2.3.3 Real-World Utility
- Large water storage tanks (up to 50,000 litres)
- Kayaks and small boats
- Playground equipment
- Traffic cones, bollards
- Insulated containers (ice boxes)

---

## 2.4 Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM) — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.4.1 Parison Formation
A **parison** is an extruded hollow tube of molten polymer. Its dimensions are critical — it must contain enough material to form the final part without thinning or bursting.

### 2.4.2 Parison Swell (Die Swell)
When molten polymer exits the die, it expands radially and shortens axially. The **die swell ratio** is:

$$\text{Swell Ratio} = \frac{D_{parison}}{D_{die}} > 1$$

Typically 1.2 to 1.6 depending on polymer, shear rate, and die length.

### 2.4.3 Inflation Pressure
The parison is inflated inside the mould by air pressure:

$$P_{blow} = \frac{2 t_{parison} \cdot \sigma_{hoop}}{D_{bottle}}$$

Typical blow pressures: **0.4 MPa to 1.0 MPa**.

### 2.4.4 Real-World Utility
- HDPE milk bottles, shampoo bottles
- Jerry cans, fuel tanks
- Industrial drums

---

## 2.5 Injection Blow Moulding (IBM) — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.5.1 Three-Station Rotary Machine

1. **Station 1 — Injection:** Preform is injection moulded around a core rod.
2. **Station 2 — Blowing:** Preform is transferred to blow mould, reheated, and inflated.
3. **Station 3 — Ejection:** Finished bottle is stripped off the core rod.

### 2.5.2 Preform Design Equation (for PET Bottles)
The preform's outer diameter and wall thickness are chosen to give the correct **blow-up ratio (BUR)**:

$$\text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bottle,max}}{D_{preform}}$$

Typical BUR values: **2.0 to 3.5**. Excessive BUR (>4) leads to uneven stretching and haze.

### 2.5.3 Real-World Utility
- PET water and soft-drink bottles (the most common application globally)
- Pharmaceutical bottles requiring precise neck threads

---

## 2.6 Vacuum Casting — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.6.1 Master Pattern Preparation
A master (usually CNC-machined or SLA 3D-printed) is used to create the silicone mould. The mould is made by pouring liquid silicone around the master and curing it.

### 2.6.2 Casting Process
1. Mix two-part PU resin (A + B) under vacuum to remove air.
2. Pour into pre-heated silicone mould.
3. Place mould in vacuum chamber again to evacuate any trapped air.
4. Cure for 30–90 minutes at 60–80 °C.
5. Demould and (optionally) post-cure for 24 h.

### 2.6.3 Real-World Utility
- **Rapid prototyping** (15–50 parts per master)
- Functional testing prototypes
- Low-volume production of cosmetic casings

---

## 2.7 Thermoforming & Vacuum Forming — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.7.1 Forming Ratio
Defines how deep you can draw a sheet without excessive thinning:

$$\text{Forming Ratio} = \frac{\text{Depth of Draw}}{\text{Lateral Dimension}}$$

Typical limits: **0.5:1 (shallow) to 1.5:1 (deep draw)**. Beyond this, plug-assisted forming is needed.

### 2.7.2 Sheet Thinning in Vacuum Forming
For a simple hemisphere with starting thickness $t_0$:

$$t_{final} = t_0 \times \frac{A_0}{A_{final}}$$

where $A_0$ = original sheet area and $A_{final}$ = final mould surface area.

For a hemispherical dome from a circular blank:
$$A_0 = \pi R^2, \quad A_{final} = 2\pi R^2$$
$$\therefore t_{final} = \frac{t_0}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY INSIGHT:** The final wall thickness is **exactly half** the original sheet thickness for a perfect hemisphere. This is because the hemisphere has twice the surface area of its circular blank.

### 2.7.3 Heating Time Estimate
$$t_{heat} = \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot s \cdot (T_{form} - T_{initial})}{q}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = sheet density
- $c_p$ = specific heat
- $s$ = sheet thickness
- $q$ = heat flux from heater

### 2.7.4 Real-World Utility
- Disposable cups, lids, food trays
- Blister packaging for pharmaceuticals
- Aircraft interior panels
- Bathtubs, shower trays (using thick acrylic sheets)

---

## 2.8 Compression Moulding — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.8.1 Charge Preparation
For thermosets, the charge is usually:
- **Powder/Granule** — for small parts
- **Preform** — pre-shaped by tableting press
- **SMC/BMC** — Sheet Moulding Compound or Bulk Moulding Compound (fibreglass-reinforced)

For rubber, the charge is a measured blank of uncured rubber compound.

### 2.8.2 Moulding Pressure
$$P = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_{cavity}}$$

Typical pressures:
- Phenolic/Bakelite: 15–25 MPa
- Melamine: 20–35 MPa
- Rubber: 5–15 MPa
- SMC: 5–10 MPa

### 2.8.3 Cure Time
$$t_{cure} = \frac{\pi \rho c_p s^2}{4 k} \ln\left(\frac{T_{mould} - T_{initial}}{T_{mould} - T_{cure}}\right)$$

where $T_{cure}$ is the temperature at which cross-linking is complete.

### 2.8.4 Real-World Utility
- Electrical switchgear, fuse boxes (phenolic)
- Dinnerware (melamine)
- Tyre curing (rubber)
- Fibreglass panels (SMC)

---

## 2.9 Rubber Manufacturing — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.9.1 Compounding Ingredients
- **Elastomer base** — Natural Rubber (NR), SBR, NBR, EPDM, silicone
- **Vulcanising agent** — Sulphur (1–3 phr for soft rubber; up to 30 phr for hard rubber/Ebonite)
- **Accelerators** — MBT, CBS, TMTD (speed up vulcanisation)
- **Activators** — Zinc oxide + stearic acid
- **Fillers** — Carbon black (reinforcing), silica, clay
- **Plasticisers** — Oils for processability
- **Antioxidants/Antiozonants** — prevent ageing
- **Peptisers** — break down long chains for easier mastication

### 2.9.2 Vulcanisation (Charles Goodyear, 1839)
Cross-linking of rubber chains by sulphur bridges. Number of sulphur atoms per cross-link determines rigidity:
- **Mono-sulphidic** — 1 S atom, flexible, heat-resistant
- **Di-sulphidic** — 2 S atoms, balanced
- **Poly-sulphidic** — many S atoms, tough but less heat-resistant

**Vulcanisation Parameters:**
- **Time (t₉₀)** — Time to reach 90% of maximum torque (≈ optimum cure)
- **Time (t_s2)** — Scorch time (premature curing must be avoided)
- **MDR/MDR Rheometer Curves** — Plot torque vs. time; the slope gives cure rate.

### 2.9.3 Rubber Processing Routes

| Process | Charge Form | Pressure | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Moulding | Blank/Pellet | High | Tyres, gaskets, O-rings |
| Injection Moulding | Strip from extruder | Very High | Small seals, grommets |
| Extrusion | Strips fed to screw | High | Hoses, weatherstrip |
| Calendaring | Compound strip | Roller pressure | Tyre treads, sheets |

### 2.9.4 Real-World Utility
- Tyres (largest rubber industry segment)
- Conveyor belts
- Hoses (radiator, fuel, hydraulic)
- Seals and O-rings
- Shoe soles
- Vibration dampers

---

## 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ | Single-screw extruder throughput |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{die} = \frac{8\eta L Q}{\pi R^4}$ | Capillary die pressure drop (Hagen-Poiseuille) |
| 3 | $F_c = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$ | Injection mould clamping force |
| 4 | $V_s = n \cdot V_{cavity}$ | Shot volume calculation |
| 5 | $W_s = V_s \cdot \rho_{melt}$ | Shot weight |
| 6 | $F_{centrifugal} = m \omega^2 r$ | Rotational moulding particle adhesion |
| 7 | $t_{part} = \frac{m_{powder}}{\rho_{melt} \cdot A_{mould}}$ | Roto-mould wall thickness |
| 8 | $\text{Swell Ratio} = \frac{D_{parison}}{D_{die}}$ | Extrusion die swell |
| 9 | $P_{blow} = \frac{2 t \sigma_{hoop}}{D}$ | Blow moulding inflation pressure |
| 10 | $\text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}}$ | Injection blow moulding stretch ratio |
| 11 | $\text{Forming Ratio} = \frac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Lateral}}$ | Thermoforming draw limit |
| 12 | $t_{final} = \frac{t_0 \cdot A_0}{A_{final}}$ | Sheet thickness after forming |
| 13 | $P_{mould} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_{cavity}}$ | Compression moulding pressure |
| 14 | $\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Newtonian viscosity (apparent) |
| 15 | $\Delta H_{melt} = m \cdot L_f$ | Energy to melt polymer |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Single-Screw Extruder Throughput (Drag Flow Term)

**Start with:** The polymer in the metering channel can be modelled as a fluid between two parallel plates, where the bottom plate (screw root) is stationary and the top plate (barrel) moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$.

**Step 1:** Unwrap the helical channel and treat it as a flat channel of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (where $e$ is flight width) and depth $d$, inclined at angle $\theta$. The barrel velocity component along the channel direction is:

$$V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** For a Newtonian fluid in Couette flow between parallel plates, the velocity profile is linear:

$$v_z(z) = V_{bx} \cdot \frac{z}{d}$$

where $z$ is the depth from the barrel.

**Step 3:** Volumetric flow rate per unit width = integral of velocity over depth:

$$q = \int_0^d v_z(z) \, dz = V_{bx} \cdot \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by channel width $W$ and account for the fact that the actual down-channel velocity is lower due to the angle (we are projecting along the channel):

$$Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

This is the famous **drag-flow equation** for a Newtonian fluid in an extruder screw channel.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Pressure Flow Term (Back-Flow)

**Step 1:** For a Newtonian fluid in a pressure-driven (Poiseuille) flow between parallel plates with separation $d$, the velocity profile is parabolic:

$$v_z^{pressure}(z) = -\frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dp}{dx}(d \cdot z - z^2)$$

**Step 2:** Integrate over channel depth to get pressure-driven flow per unit width:

$$q_{pressure} = -\frac{d^3}{12 \eta} \frac{dp}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** The negative sign indicates that flow is opposite to the down-channel direction (this is the **leakage flow** that opposes drag flow). Multiply by width $W$ and project to down-channel direction:

$$Q_{pressure} = \frac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 4:** The total net throughput is therefore:

$$Q_{net} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

which matches the KTU formula sheet entry #1.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Extruder Throughput

**Given:**
- Screw diameter $D = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- Screw speed $N = 60$ rpm $= 1$ rev/s
- Metering zone depth $d = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m
- Helix angle $\theta = 17.65°$ (so $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$)
- Pressure rise $\Delta P = 10$ MPa $= 10 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Metering zone length $L = 1.2$ m
- Polymer melt viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s

**Step 1:** Compute drag flow:

$$Q_{drag} = \frac{\pi^2 \times (0.06)^2 \times 1 \times 0.003 \times 0.303 \times 0.953}{2}$$

$$Q_{drag} = \frac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.003 \times 0.2888}{2}$$

$$Q_{drag} = \frac{9.8696 \times 3.12 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{3.08 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 1.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{drag} = 15.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute pressure flow:

$$Q_{pressure} = \frac{\pi \times 0.06 \times (0.003)^3 \times (0.303)^2 \times 10 \times 10^6}{12 \times 500 \times 1.2}$$

$$Q_{pressure} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.06 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 10^7}{7200}$$

$$Q_{pressure} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.06 \times 2.478 \times 10^{-1}}{7200}$$

$$Q_{pressure} = \frac{0.0467}{7200} = 6.49 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{pressure} = 6.49 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Net throughput:

$$Q_{net} = 15.4 - 6.49 = 8.91 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kg/hr (assuming polymer density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³):

$$\dot{m} = Q_{net} \times \rho = 8.91 \times 10^{-6} \times 950 = 8.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m} = 8.46 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 30.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Injection Moulding Clamping Force

**Step 1:** Consider a single-cavity mould for a flat rectangular part of dimensions $L \times W$ and thickness $t$.

**Step 2:** When the polymer melt at pressure $P_{cavity}$ acts on the part surface, the force pushing the mould halves apart is:

$$F_{opening} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$$

where $A_{projected} = L \times W$ for a rectangular part (projected onto the parting plane).

**Step 3:** For a multi-cavity mould (n cavities), if all cavities project the same way:

$$F_{opening} = P_{cavity} \times n \times A_{projected}$$

**Step 4:** The clamping system must apply at least this force to keep the mould closed:

$$F_c \geq P_{cavity} \times A_{projected,total}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Clamping Force

**Given:**
- Part: Rectangular cover, 200 mm × 150 mm
- Cavity pressure: 50 MPa
- Number of cavities: 4
- Runner projected area: 30 cm²

**Step 1:** Compute projected area per cavity:

$$A_{cavity} = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Total projected area:

$$A_{total} = 4 \times 300 + 30 = 1230 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Clamping force:

$$F_c = 50 \text{ MPa} \times 1230 \text{ cm}^2 = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \times 1230 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_c = 50 \times 1230 \times 10^2 = 6.15 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 6150 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4:** In tonnes-force:

$$F_c = \frac{6150 \text{ kN}}{9.81 \text{ kN/tonne}} \approx 627 \text{ tonnes}$$

---

## 3.6 Detailed Derivation: Thermoforming Wall Thickness for Hemisphere

**Step 1:** Initial sheet is a flat circular disc of radius $R$ and uniform thickness $t_0$.

**Step 2:** Volume of polymer is conserved (incompressible material):

$$V_{initial} = V_{final}$$

$$\pi R^2 \cdot t_0 = A_{hemisphere} \cdot t_{final}$$

**Step 3:** Surface area of a hemisphere:

$$A_{hemisphere} = 2 \pi R^2$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\pi R^2 \cdot t_0 = 2 \pi R^2 \cdot t_{final}$$

$$t_{final} = \frac{t_0}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** The wall of the vacuum-formed hemisphere is **exactly half** the original sheet thickness, distributed uniformly. (In practice, it is slightly non-uniform — thinner at the top and thicker at the corners due to material flow dynamics.)

---

## 3.7 Detailed Derivation: Rotational Moulding Wall Thickness

**Step 1:** Mass of powder charged = $m_{powder}$ (kg).
**Step 2:** After melting, this mass fills the inside of the mould uniformly to a wall thickness $t_{part}$.
**Step 3:** Volume of polymer in the wall = surface area of mould × wall thickness:

$$V_{polymer} = A_{mould} \cdot t_{part}$$

**Step 4:** Equate this to the powder volume (mass divided by melt density):

$$\frac{m_{powder}}{\rho_{melt}} = A_{mould} \cdot t_{part}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t_{part}$:

$$t_{part} = \frac{m_{powder}}{\rho_{melt} \cdot A_{mould}}$$

This is the exact expression in KTU Formula Sheet entry #7.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Hollow cube mould, side = 1 m
- Powder charge: 25 kg of polyethylene powder
- Melt density: 940 kg/m³

**Step 1:** Inner surface area of mould:

$$A_{mould} = 6 \times (1)^2 = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Wall thickness:

$$t_{part} = \frac{25}{940 \times 6} = \frac{25}{5640} = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4.43 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Extrusion | Injection Moulding | Blow Moulding | Rotational | Thermoforming |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure (MPa)** | 5–30 | 50–150 | 0.4–1.0 | 0 (gravity) | 0.1 (vacuum) |
| **Cycle time** | Continuous | 10–120 s | 5–30 s | 20–60 min | 30–120 s |
| **Wall thickness control** | Good | Excellent | Variable | Poor (depends on charge) | Variable |
| **Tooling cost** | Moderate | Very High | High | Low | Low |
| **Part complexity** | Constant profile only | Very complex | Hollow only | Hollow only | Shallow to medium |
| **Material waste** | Low (in-line scrap) | Sprue/runner waste | Flash & trim | Negligible | Trim scrap |
| **Suitable polymers** | TP only | TP & TS | TP only | PE mainly | TP sheets |

## 4.1 Single-Screw Extruder — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B1 --> C1[Solid Conveying Zone]
    C1 --> D1[Compression or Melting Zone]
    D1 --> E1[Metering Zone with Shallow Flight]
    E1 --> F1[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
    F1 --> G1[Die Adapter]
    G1 --> H1[Shaped Die]
    H1 --> I1[Cooling and Sizing Tank]
    I1 --> J1[Haul Off and Cutting Station]
    J1 --> K1[Finished Profile]

    Z1[Barrel Heaters around barrel] -.-> C1
    Z2[Barrel Heaters around barrel] -.-> D1
    Z3[Barrel Heaters around barrel] -.-> E1

    M1[Motor and Gearbox] --> N1[Thrust Bearing]
    N1 --> O1[Screw Shaft]
    O1 --> C1
```


## 4.3 Rotational Moulding — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Polymer Powder Charge] --> B2[Open Mould Half A]
    B2 --> C2[Close Mould with Half B]
    C2 --> D2[Mount on Biaxial Rotating Arm]
    D2 --> E2[Enter Oven 250 to 350 C]
    E2 --> F2[Rotation during heating - powder melts and coats inner surface]

```

## 4.4 Extrusion Blow Moulding — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B3 --> C3[Mould closes and pinches off parison at bottom]

    D3 --> E3[Parison inflates against cooled mould walls]

    F3 --> G3[Mould opens and finished hollow part is ejected]
    G3 --> H3[Deflash and trim excess material]
```

## 4.6 Thermoforming and Vacuum Forming — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A4[Thermoplastic sheet roll] --> B4[Clamp frame]
    B4 --> C4[Heater panel radiates to soften sheet]
    C4 --> D4[Sheet reaches forming temperature and sags]
```

## 4.7 Compression Moulding — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A5[Thermoset charge or rubber blank] --> B5[Place in lower heated mould half]
    B5 --> C5[Upper mould half descends under pressure]
    C5 --> D5[Material flows and fills cavity]
    D5 --> E5[Heat cures material]
    E5 --> F5[Cure complete - mould opens]
    F5 --> G5[Ejector pins push part out]
    G5 --> H5[Deflash and trim]
```

---

## 4.8 Rubber Manufacturing Process Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A6[Natural Rubber Latex or Synthetic Rubber Bale] --> B6[Mastication - mechanical breakdown of long chains]
    B6 --> C6[Compounding - add sulphur, accelerators, fillers, oils]
    C6 --> D6{Choose Process}
    D6 -->|Compression| E6A[Compression Moulding of Rubber]
    D6 -->|Injection| E6B[Injection Moulding of Rubber]
    D6 -->|Extrusion| E6C[Extrusion of Rubber Profiles]
    D6 -->|Calendaring| E6D[Calendaring into Sheets]
    E6A --> F6[Vulcanisation - heat and pressure]
    E6B --> F6
    E6C --> F6
    E6D --> F6
    F6 --> G6[Cross linking by sulphur bridges]
    G6 --> H6[Deflashing and Inspection]
    H6 --> I6[Finished Elastomeric Product]
```

---

## 4.9 Comparative Process-Selection Flowchart (Engineering Decision Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Start: Choose Polymer Process]) --> Q1{Is it hollow and large?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, tank or kayak| P1[Rotational Moulding]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need constant cross section profile?}
    Q2 -->|Yes pipes and sheets| P2[Extrusion]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Hollow with bottle neck?}
    Q3 -->|Yes PET bottles| P3[Injection Blow Moulding]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Hollow flexible container?}
    Q4 -->|Yes HDPE bottles| P4[Extrusion Blow Moulding]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Thermoset or rubber part?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| P5[Compression Moulding]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Flat sheet to moulded shape?}
    Q6 -->|Yes shallow trays| P6[Thermoforming]
    Q6 -->|No complex 3D| P7[Injection Moulding]
    Q7{Prototyping under 50 units?} -->|Yes| P8[Vacuum Casting]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **extrusion** and **injection moulding** based on (i) process continuity, (ii) typical pressure, and (iii) product geometry.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Extrusion | Injection Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| Process continuity | **Continuous** — material flows out of die uninterrupted | **Cyclic (batch)** — discrete moulding cycles |
| Typical pressure | 5–30 MPa (moderate) | 50–150 MPa (very high) |
| Product geometry | **Constant cross-section** profiles only (pipes, sheets, films) | **Complex 3D parts** with varying cross-section (housings, gears) |

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q:** Define **rotational moulding**. Why is it called a "zero-pressure" process and list any two products made by it.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Rotational moulding is a process in which a measured quantity of polymer powder is placed inside a hollow mould, the mould is rotated biaxially in an oven until the polymer melts and coats the inside surface, and then the mould is cooled to give a hollow seamless part.

**Why zero-pressure:** The process uses **no external pressure** to force the polymer against the mould wall. Coating occurs purely by **gravity** (during initial tumbling) and mild **centrifugal action** from rotation.

**Two Products:** (any two) Water storage tanks, kayaks, traffic cones, insulated ice boxes, playground slides.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> - Skipping the **derivation steps** of any formula (e.g., extruder throughput) will cost you 3–4 marks. Always state assumptions (Newtonian fluid, parallel-plate model).
> - Forgetting to **convert units consistently** (Pa vs MPa, mm vs m) is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in numericals.
> - In comparison questions, a **table format** scores higher than a paragraph — examiners tick off rows.
> - Always **draw a labelled diagram** for process questions. A question without a diagram typically gets 1–2 marks less than one with.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **single-screw extrusion process** for plastics. Describe the functions of the three zones of the screw. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the volumetric throughput** of a Newtonian polymer melt in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A single-screw extruder consists of a heated barrel containing a rotating Archimedean screw, a hopper at one end for feeding polymer pellets, and a shaping die at the other end. (See Section 4.1 diagram.)

**Three Zones and Their Functions:**

**1. Solid Conveying (Feed) Zone** — *Function:* Conveying solid pellets from the hopper throat forward into the heated barrel without melting. The flights are deep here (≈ 0.1–0.15 D) to maximise conveying volume. The barrel wall is intentionally cooled near the hopper (water jacket) to prevent premature melting that would cause "bridging".

**2. Compression (Melting/Transition) Zone** — *Function:* Compressing and melting the pellets. Screw channel depth decreases progressively (compression ratio 2:1 to 4:1) which compresses the solid bed. Heat from external barrel heaters and frictional shear melt the polymer. A thin melt film forms on the barrel wall and the solid bed gradually disappears.

**3. Metering Zone** — *Function:* Acting as a positive-displacement pump to deliver a uniform, pressurised melt stream to the die. Screw flights are of constant, shallow depth. The melt is homogenised (mixed) here by the high shear. Pressure builds up here and forces the melt through the breaker plate (a perforated disc with a screen pack of wire meshes that filters contaminants and creates back-pressure).

**Auxiliary Components:**
- **Breaker plate & screen pack** — filter contaminants, build pressure, prevent screw surging.
- **Die** — shapes the extrudate (pipe die, sheet die, film die, profile die).
- **Cooling/sizing tank** — for pipes/profiles, calibrates dimensions using vacuum sizing or bushings.
- **Haul-off & cutter** — pulls the product at controlled speed and cuts to length.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation — see Section 3.1 and 3.2 above for full derivation.**

Assumptions:
1. Polymer melt is **Newtonian** ($\tau = \eta \dot\gamma$).
2. Screw channel is unwrapped into a **flat rectangular channel** of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta$ and depth $d$.
3. Steady, laminar, isothermal flow.
4. No leakage over flight tips.
5. Inertia and gravity negligible compared to viscous forces.

**Result:**

$$Q_{net} = \underbrace{\frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}}_{\text{Drag flow } Q_d} - \underbrace{\frac{\pi D d^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}}_{\text{Pressure flow } Q_p}$$

The drag flow is the forward pumping due to relative motion of the barrel over the screw. The pressure flow is the backward leakage due to pressure build-up.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **injection moulding process** with a labelled diagram. List the major steps of the moulding cycle and state the function of the **clamping unit**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A mould has **4 identical cavities**, each producing a rectangular part of dimensions **120 mm × 80 mm × 3 mm** with a runner projected area of **25 cm²**. The required cavity pressure during injection is **45 MPa**. Calculate the **minimum clamping force** required in (i) Newtons and (ii) tonnes-force. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Injection moulding is a cyclic process for producing complex plastic parts in high volume. The machine has two main units: the **injection unit** (heats, melts, and injects the polymer) and the **clamping unit** (holds the mould closed).

**Moulding Cycle Steps:**

1. **Mould closing** — Moving platen moves to clamp the two mould halves together.
2. **Injection (Filling)** — Screw moves forward like a plunger, injecting melt through the nozzle and sprue into the cavity at high pressure and speed.
3. **Packing / Holding** — Pressure is maintained (lower than injection pressure) to compensate for shrinkage during solidification.
4. **Cooling** — Water/oil circulates in mould cooling channels; polymer solidifies into final shape.
5. **Plastication (Metering)** — Screw rotates, drawing in fresh pellets and melting them for the next shot.
6. **Mould opening** — Moving platen retracts.
7. **Ejection** — Ejector plate pushes the part out using ejector pins.
8. **Part removal** — Part is taken off, often by a robot or drop chute.

**Function of Clamping Unit:**
- Holds the two mould halves tightly together against the **injection pressure** trying to push them apart.
- Provides **opening and closing** motion of the mould.
- Provides **ejector stroke** to remove the moulded part.
- Three types: **toggle (mechanical)**, **hydraulic**, and **hydromechanical (toggle + hydraulic booster)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute projected area per cavity (rectangular part):
$$A_{cavity} = 12 \text{ cm} \times 8 \text{ cm} = 96 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Total projected area of cavities:
$$A_{cavities} = 4 \times 96 = 384 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Add runner projected area:
$$A_{total} = 384 + 25 = 409 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 4:** Convert to SI units:
$$A_{total} = 409 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.0409 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Convert cavity pressure to Pa:
$$P = 45 \text{ MPa} = 45 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6:** Compute clamping force in Newtons:
$$F_c = P \times A_{total} = 45 \times 10^6 \times 0.0409 = 1.841 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-force = 9.81 kN = 9810 N):
$$F_c = \frac{1.841 \times 10^6}{9810} = 187.6 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) $F_c = 1.841 \times 10^6$ N (or 1841 kN)
- (ii) $F_c ≈ 187.6$ tonnes-force

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Process Identification (must-know)

- **Extrusion** → continuous, constant cross-section → pipes, sheets, profiles
- **Injection Moulding** → cyclic, complex 3D, highest pressure → consumer goods
- **Rotational Moulding** → hollow, no pressure, slow cycle → tanks, kayaks
- **EBM** → continuous parison then blow → HDPE bottles
- **IBM** → preform + blow → PET bottles
- **Vacuum Casting** → PU resin in silicone mould → prototypes
- **Thermoforming** → heated sheet over mould → trays, blisters
- **Compression Moulding** → thermoset or rubber, press cure → switches, tyres
- **Rubber Manufacturing** → mastication, compounding, vulcanisation → all rubber goods

### Key Formulas (must memorise)

- $F_c = P \times A_{projected}$
- $Q_{drag} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$
- $\Delta P_{die} = \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^4}$ (Poiseuille)
- $t_{final} = \frac{t_0 \cdot A_0}{A_{final}}$ (volume conservation in thermoforming)
- $t_{part} = \frac{m_{powder}}{\rho \cdot A_{mould}}$ (roto-mould)
- $\text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}}$ (IBM)

### Numbers You Should Know by Heart

- Typical **injection pressure**: 50–150 MPa
- Typical **clamping pressure**: 30–80 MPa at cavity
- **Compression moulding** pressure: 10–50 MPa
- **Blow moulding** pressure: 0.4–1.0 MPa
- **Vacuum forming** pressure differential: 0.08–0.10 MPa (≈ atmospheric)
- **Screw L/D ratio** in extruder: 20:1 to 30:1 (modern up to 40:1)
- **Compression ratio**: 2:1 to 4:1
- **Helix angle**: 17.65° (gives $\sin 2\theta = 0.6$, simplifies formulas)
- **Blow-up ratio (BUR)**: 2.0–3.5 for PET bottles
- **Sulphur content** in rubber: 1–3 phr (soft) to 30 phr (ebonite)
- **Vulcanisation temperature**: 140–180 °C

### Common Mistakes Students Make

- ❌ Confusing **injection moulding pressure** (in the cylinder/nozzle) with **cavity pressure** (in the mould).
- ❌ Using **solid density** instead of **melt density** for shot weight calculations.
- ❌ Forgetting to add **runner area** to cavity area in clamping force.
- ❌ Treating polymer as Newtonian when it's actually shear-thinning (pseudoplastic).
- ❌ Confusing **thermoforming** (uses both vacuum and pressure variants) with **vacuum forming** (only vacuum variant).
- ❌ Forgetting that **rotational moulding has no pressure** at all — students often cite centrifugal force values that are negligibly small.

### Process Selection Rule of Thumb

> **If you need a constant cross-section → extrude. If you need a complex 3D shape → inject. If you need a large hollow object with no internal stress → rotationally mould. If you need a bottle → blow mould. If you need a thermoset or rubber part → compression mould. If you need a prototype in small quantity → vacuum cast. If you need a thin-walled package cheaply → thermoform.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relevant Properties of Rubber, Processing Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relevant Properties of Rubber, Processing Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rubber? — KTU Formal Definition

**Rubber** is a polymeric elastomer — a high-molecular-weight, long-chain, naturally occurring or synthetically manufactured amorphous polymer that exhibits **hyperelastic behaviour**, meaning it can undergo very large elastic deformations (often 200%–1000% strain) and recover its original shape almost immediately upon removal of the applied stress, provided the deformation is carried out within the elastic limit and below the glass transition temperature ($T_g$).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET205)** context, rubber is treated as one of the major non-metallic engineering materials along with ceramics and polymers, with specific emphasis on how its *unique combination of properties* dictates its **manufacturing (processing) sequence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, students must learn *both* the **relevant engineering properties** of rubber (which decide the application) and the **processing steps** (which transform raw rubber into a finished, useful product). These two halves are directly correlated — every property is created or modified by a specific processing step.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Rubber (Background Context)

| Family | Examples | Source / Synthesis |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber (NR)** | Polyisoprene (cis-1,4) | Latex from *Hevea brasiliensis* tree |
| **Synthetic Rubber (SR)** | SBR, BR, NBR, EPDM, CR, IIR, Silicone | Polymerization of petrochemical monomers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Nomenclature Tip for KTU:** The abbreviations follow IUPAC-recommended names — *SBR* (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber), *NBR* (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber), *EPDM* (Ethylene Propylene Diene Monomer), *CR* (Chloroprene Rubber / Neoprene), *IIR* (Isobutylene Isoprene Rubber / Butyl).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — "The Rubber Analogy"

Imagine a **balloon**: it stretches enormously when you blow air into it, but as soon as the air escapes, it snaps back to its original shape. Now imagine that this balloon could:
- Be stretched to **8–10 times** its length without tearing,
- Return to its exact original shape millions of times without wearing out,
- Be made as soft as jelly or as hard as a car tyre,
- Resist water, oil, heat, or cold, depending on the recipe used to make it.

**This is the engineering miracle of rubber** — and *the properties are the "personality" of the rubber, while the processing steps are the "manufacturing recipe" that gives it that personality.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of rubber as **dough** and vulcanization as **baking the bread** — the raw dough (raw rubber + additives) is soft, sticky and useless, but after "baking" (vulcanization with sulphur at controlled temperature), it becomes the firm, elastic, durable material we know.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Manufacturing

Rubber products are **omnipresent in mechanical assemblies** — tyres, seals, O-rings, gaskets, hoses, vibration dampers, conveyor belts, and shoe soles. The KTU examiner tests whether the student can:
1. **Identify** a property and link it to a real industrial application.
2. **Justify** why a specific processing step is needed.
3. **Trace** the flow of raw rubber from bale to finished part.

---

## 1.5 Core Property Set to be Studied

The **relevant properties** of rubber, as defined by the KTU 2024 Production Engineering syllabus, are grouped into **four families**:

1. **Mechanical Properties** — Hardness, Tensile Strength, Elongation, Modulus
2. **Elastic / Viscoelastic Properties** — Resilience, Rebound Resilience, Compression Set
3. **Toughness Properties** — Abrasion Resistance, Tear Strength, Flex Resistance
4. **Processing / Rheological Properties** — Mooney Viscosity, Scorch Time, Cure Time

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "ME-TR"**:
> **M**echanical | **E**lastic | **T**oughness | **R**heological — the four pillars of rubber properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Engineering Properties of Rubber

### 2.1.1 Hardness (Shore A Durometer Scale)

Hardness is the **resistance of rubber to local indentation** by a standardized indenter under a defined load. The most common scale is the **Shore A** (softer rubbers) and **Shore D** (harder rubbers).

- **Unit:** dimensionless Shore A (0–100 scale)
- **Typical Range:** Tyre tread ~ 60–70 Shore A; Soft gel ~ 20 Shore A; Ebonite ~ 100 Shore D
- **Test Method:** ASTM D2240 / ISO 868

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Hardness tells you how *firm* the rubber feels. A pencil eraser is soft (~ 30 Shore A); a hockey puck is hard (~ 90 Shore A).

**Why it matters in processing:** Hardness is *controlled during compounding* by the type and amount of filler (carbon black, silica) and plasticizer — it is **not changed after vulcanization** except by re-formulating.

---

### 2.1.2 Tensile Strength ($TS$)

The maximum **tensile stress** that a standard rubber dumbbell specimen can withstand before rupture.

$$TS = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$$

where:
- $F_{max}$ = maximum tensile force (N)
- $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area (m²)
- **Unit:** MPa (typical range for NR: 17–25 MPa; SBR: 14–21 MPa; silicone: 5–10 MPa)

---

### 2.1.3 Elongation at Break ($E_b$)

The percentage increase in the **original gauge length** of a specimen at the moment of rupture.

$$E_b = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $L_0$ = original gauge length
- $L_b$ = gauge length at break
- **Typical Range:** Natural Rubber: 600%–1000%; Silicone: 100%–400%

---

### 2.1.4 Modulus of Elasticity (Stress at a Given Elongation)

For rubber, the **M100, M200, M300** values are quoted — the **stress required to elongate the specimen to 100%, 200%, 300%** of its original length, respectively.

$$\text{M100} = \frac{F \text{ at } 100\% \text{ elongation}}{A_0}$$

- **Unit:** MPa
- **Used to:** indicate *stiffness in the working elastic range*

---

### 2.1.5 Resilience & Rebound Resilience

**Resilience** is the ratio of **energy returned** (recovered) to the **energy supplied** (input) during a rapid deformation cycle.

$$R = \frac{W_{\text{recovered}}}{W_{\text{supplied}}} \times 100\%$$

- **Test Method:** Rebound pendulum test (Schob) — drop a pendulum from a fixed height onto the rubber sample and measure the rebound height.
- **Typical Values:** Natural Rubber: 75%–90% (highly resilient — used in springs/anti-vibration mounts); Butyl Rubber: 10%–20% (low resilience — used in damping applications).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** *High resilience* → rubber acts like a **spring** (energy stored and returned). *Low resilience* → rubber acts like a **shock absorber** (energy dissipated as heat). The KTU exam often tests this application-property linkage.

---

### 2.1.6 Compression Set (Permanent Set)

The **permanent deformation** remaining in a rubber specimen after it has been held at a defined strain (usually 25%) for a defined time and temperature, then released and allowed to recover.

$$CS = \frac{t_0 - t_2}{t_0 - t_1} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $t_0$ = original thickness
- $t_1$ = thickness under compression
- $t_2$ = thickness after recovery
- **Unit:** % — *Lower is better*
- **Test:** ASTM D395, ISO 815

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is critical:** Compression set determines whether a **gasket or seal** will continue to seal after months/years of compression. High CS = leaky seal.

---

### 2.1.7 Abrasion Resistance

The **volume of material lost** when a rubber specimen is rubbed against an abrasive surface under controlled conditions (e.g., DIN Abrader, Akron Abrader, Taber Abrader).

$$\text{Abrasion Index} = \frac{V_{\text{reference}}}{V_{\text{sample}}} \times 100$$

- **Application:** Tyre treads, conveyor belts, shoe soles.
- **Typical Ranking:** NR ≈ SBR ≈ BR > NBR > IIR > Silicone

---

### 2.1.8 Tear Strength

The **resistance to the propagation of a cut or nick** under tension. Measured as force per unit thickness required to tear a specimen.

$$T_{\text{tear}} = \frac{F}{d}$$

where $d$ = specimen thickness. **Unit:** kN/m.

---

### 2.1.9 Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 at 100°C)

The **viscosity of unvulcanized rubber** measured by a Mooney viscometer — a disc rotates inside a heated rubber chamber, and the torque required is recorded in **Mooney Units (MU)**.

- **Indication:** Processability, molecular weight, mastication effectiveness.
- **Typical:** NR: 60–90 MU; SBR: 40–70 MU.

---

### 2.1.10 Scorch Time ($t_{s2}$) and Cure Time ($t_{90}$)

- **Scorch Time ($t_{s2}$):** Time for Mooney viscosity to rise by 2 units above minimum — represents the *onset of vulcanization*. Defines **processing safety window**.
- **Optimum Cure Time ($t_{90}$):** Time to reach 90% of maximum torque in an oscillating disc rheometer (ODR) — defines the **vulcanization time at a given temperature**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Scorch and Cure are *opposite ends* of the same process. Scorch = "start" (must be long enough for safe handling). Cure = "complete" (must be short enough for high production rates).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property | Formula | Typical Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Strength | $TS = F_{\max} / A_0$ | MPa | Load-bearing capacity |
| 2 | Elongation at Break | $E_b = (L_b - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | % | Ductility / stretchability |
| 3 | Modulus (M100, M200) | $M = F/A_0$ at given % strain | MPa | Stiffness in elastic range |
| 4 | Resilience | $R = W_{\text{rec}} / W_{\text{sup}} \times 100$ | % | Energy return (spring vs damper) |
| 5 | Compression Set | $CS = (t_0 - t_2)/(t_0 - t_1) \times 100$ | % | Permanent deformation (lower = better) |
| 6 | Tear Strength | $T_{\text{tear}} = F/d$ | kN/m | Crack propagation resistance |
| 7 | Abrasion Index | $I = V_{\text{ref}} / V_{\text{sam}} \times 100$ | – | Wear life |
| 8 | Mooney Viscosity | Read from disc torque | MU | Processability |
| 9 | Hardness (Shore A) | Dial reading | Shore A | Surface firmness |
| 10 | Specific Gravity | $\rho_{\text{rubber}}/\rho_{\text{water}}$ | – | Mass per unit volume |
| 11 | Optimum Cure | From ODR $t_{90}$ | seconds/min | Production cycle time |
| 12 | Scorch Time | $t_{s2}$ from Mooney curve | seconds/min | Processing safety window |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, examiners often give a sample diameter/thickness and a force, and ask the student to compute **TS, E_b, M100, or Tear Strength**. Memorize the formula table above — it covers ~ 80% of calculation-type questions.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Property → Best Application | Industry Use |
|---|---|
| **High Resilience** (NR, BR) | Engine mounts, anti-vibration pads, springs |
| **Low Resilience** (Butyl, EPDM) | Acoustic dampers, shock absorbers |
| **High Oil Resistance** (NBR) | Fuel hoses, O-rings, gaskets |
| **High Weather/Ozone Resistance** (EPDM) | Window seals, roofing membranes |
| **High Heat Resistance** (Silicone, FKM) | Gaskets in ovens, aerospace seals |
| **High Abrasion Resistance** (SBR, NR + carbon black) | Tyres, conveyor belts |
| **High Tensile + Elongation** (NR) | Rubber springs, structural elastic elements |
| **Low Compression Set** (FKM, EPDM) | Long-life seals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Linkage:** The 2024 scheme explicitly tests *mapping a property to its application*. Example: "A manufacturer needs a sealing material for hydraulic oil at 120°C. Justify the choice of NBR or FKM." The model answer must cite **oil resistance + low compression set + temperature resistance** as the three justifying properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rubber Processing Steps — The Complete Manufacturing Flow

The transformation of raw rubber into a finished engineering product involves the following **six sequential stages**. Each stage alters or refines one of the engineering properties discussed in Section 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Map:** *Property* ↔ *Processing Step* — every property is **created, modified, or destroyed** by one of the following steps. Memorize this mapping for 14-mark questions.

| Stage # | Process Step | Input → Output | Property Created / Modified |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mastication** | Raw bale → Softened, plasticized mass | Reduces Mooney viscosity (improves processability) |
| 2 | **Compounding / Mixing** | NR/SR + additives → Green compound | Establishes hardness, tensile, abrasion, ageing |
| 3 | **Shaping / Forming** | Compound → "Green" preform | Establishes final geometry |
| 4 | **Vulcanization (Curing)** | Green rubber → Cross-linked rubber | Establishes elasticity, resilience, tensile, compression set |
| 5 | **Finishing / Deflashing** | Cured part → Trimmed part | Cosmetic and dimensional accuracy |
| 6 | **Quality Control & Inspection** | Trimmed part → Accepted / Rejected | Verifies all properties meet spec |

---

### 3.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Processing Step

#### **STEP 1: Mastication**

**Definition:** Mastication is the *mechanical breakdown of long polymer chains* in raw rubber under heat and shear, using a two-roll mill or an internal mixer (Banbury).

**Objective:** Reduce **Mooney viscosity** from the as-received value (e.g., 80 MU) to a processable value (e.g., 50–60 MU) by **chain scission**.

**Mechanism:** The high shear between the rolls (or rotors) breaks the C–C bonds in the longest polymer chains, lowering the average molecular weight. In NR, mastication is assisted by adding **peptizers** (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol) that catalyse chain scission.

**Mathematical Relationship:** Mooney Viscosity after mastication is empirically related to mastication energy input:

$$ML_{\text{final}} = ML_{\text{initial}} \cdot e^{-k \cdot E}$$

where:
- $E$ = specific energy input (kWh/kg)
- $k$ = mastication efficiency constant
- $ML$ = Mooney Viscosity (MU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why":** A very high Mooney viscosity makes the rubber *too stiff* to mix, extrude, or mold. Mastication ensures the rubber can flow into every cavity of the mold — directly impacting **surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and the absence of voids**.

---

#### **STEP 2: Compounding / Mixing**

**Definition:** Compounding is the *precise addition and homogeneous dispersion of additives* into the masticated rubber to engineer specific properties.

**Additives added (with their function):**

| Additive Type | Example | Effect on Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Vulcanizing agent** | Sulphur (1–3 phr) | Cross-links → elasticity, tensile |
| **Accelerators** | MBT, CBS, TMTD | Speed up vulcanization → reduces cure time |
| **Activators** | ZnO + Stearic acid | Activate accelerators |
| **Fillers (Reinforcing)** | Carbon black (HAF, ISAF), Silica | Increase tensile, hardness, abrasion |
| **Fillers (Non-reinforcing)** | Calcium carbonate, China clay | Reduce cost, modify hardness |
| **Plasticizers / Softeners** | Mineral oil, DOP | Lower Mooney viscosity, improve flexibility |
| **Antioxidants / Antiozonants** | TMQ, 6PPD | Improve ageing resistance |
| **Pigments** | Titanium dioxide, carbon black | Colour |
| **Blowing agents** | Azodicarbonamide | Produce cellular / sponge rubber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on "phr":** Parts per **Hundred Rubber** — the standard dosage unit in rubber compounding. **2 phr sulphur** means 2 g of sulphur per 100 g of rubber. This is **not** the same as "percentage by weight" of the total compound.

---

#### **STEP 3: Shaping / Forming**

The green compound is shaped by one of three primary methods:

**3.1 Compression Molding**
- The compound is placed directly in an open mold cavity; the mold is closed in a hydraulic press; heat and pressure cause the rubber to flow, fill the cavity, and cure.
- **Used for:** Large, low-volume items — gaskets, O-rings, seals.
- **Property controlled:** Geometry and surface finish.

**3.2 Transfer Molding**
- The compound is pre-heated in a transfer pot above the mold cavity, then forced through a runner into the closed cavity.
- **Used for:** Complex inserts (e.g., metal-bonded rubber parts).
- **Property controlled:** Insert positioning, low flash.

**3.3 Injection Molding**
- The compound is plasticized in a screw, injected under high pressure into a closed, heated mold.
- **Used for:** High-volume precision parts — automotive parts, medical components.
- **Property controlled:** Tight tolerances, repeatability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Forming Methods (for KTU awareness):**
> - **Extrusion** — continuous profiles (hoses, tubes, weather strips).
> - **Calendaring** — thin sheets (conveyor belts, rubberized fabrics).
> - **Dip coating** — gloves, balloons.

---

#### **STEP 4: Vulcanization (Curing)**

**Definition:** Vulcanization is the *irreversible chemical process* in which long polymer chains are joined by short **sulphur cross-links** (–C–S$_x$–C–) under heat and pressure, transforming soft, plastic, sticky raw rubber into a strong, elastic, durable engineering material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Invented by Charles Goodyear in 1839.** Vulcanization is the *single most important processing step* — without it, rubber is unusable. The 1839 discovery is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

**Mechanism (Simplified):**
1. Activators (ZnO + stearic acid) react with accelerators to form active sulphurating species.
2. The active species react with the $\alpha$-methylene group (–CH$_2$–) on the polymer backbone, opening the S–S bond of the accelerator.
3. Sulphur bridges (–S$_x$–) are formed between adjacent chains. Typical $x$ = 1–4 (mono-, di-, poly-sulphidic cross-links).
4. The 3-D network of cross-links gives rubber its characteristic **hyperelastic behaviour**.

**Governing Equation — Flory-Rehner Theory:**

The **degree of cross-linking** is expressed in terms of the *molecular weight between cross-links* ($M_c$):

$$M_c = \frac{-\rho_r \cdot V_s \cdot \phi^{1/3}}{(\ln(1 - \phi) + \phi + \chi \phi^2) \cdot 2}$$

where:
- $\rho_r$ = density of rubber (g/cm³)
- $V_s$ = molar volume of solvent
- $\phi$ = volume fraction of rubber in swollen state
- $\chi$ = Flory-Huggins interaction parameter

**Engineering interpretation:**
- **Lower $M_c$** (more cross-links) → harder, less extensible, more resilient, higher modulus.
- **Higher $M_c$** (fewer cross-links) → softer, more extensible, less resilient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of vulcanization as **"weaving"** the polymer chains into a 3-D net. With *few* cross-links (loose net), the rubber is soft and stretchy (like a fishing net). With *many* cross-links (tight net), the rubber is hard and inextensible (like a stiff canvas). The **vulcanization level** is the "tightness" of the net.

---

#### **STEP 5: Finishing / Deflashing**

After vulcanization, the part is removed from the mold and has **flash** — a thin excess rubber film at the mold parting line. Flash is removed by:
- **Manual trimming** (scissors, knives) — low volume.
- **Cryogenic / Tumbling deflashing** — flash frozen with liquid nitrogen and tumbled; brittle flash breaks off.
- **Cryogenic barrel deflashing** — automated version of the above.

Other finishing steps: **post-cure** (silicones, fluoroelastomers require oven post-cure to drive off by-products), **surface treatment** (chlorination for bonding), **lubrication** (O-rings).

---

#### **STEP 6: Quality Control & Inspection**

Each property is verified against the specification:
- **Hardness** — Shore A durometer.
- **Tensile / Elongation** — universal testing machine.
- **Compression Set** — ASTM D395 jig.
- **Specific Gravity** — Archimedes balance.
- **Visual / Dimensional** — gauges, CMM, profile projector.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "vulcanization improves *all* properties". This is **incorrect** — vulcanization **reduces** plasticity, tack, and solubility. It does not improve hardness by itself (fillers do). Be precise.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Model 14-Mark Q):**
> A rubber dumbbell specimen has an original gauge length $L_0 = 25$ mm and an original cross-section of 6 mm × 2 mm. At break, the gauge length was 175 mm and the maximum force recorded was 240 N. Calculate the **(a)** Tensile Strength, **(b)** Elongation at Break, **(c)** M100 (stress at 100% elongation) if the force at 100% elongation was 96 N.

---

### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area:**
$$A_0 = 6 \, \text{mm} \times 2 \, \text{mm} = 12 \, \text{mm}^2 = 12 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Tensile Strength:**
$$TS = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_0} = \frac{240 \, \text{N}}{12 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2} = 20 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = \boxed{20 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Elongation at Break:**
$$E_b = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\% = \frac{175 - 25}{25} \times 100\% = \frac{150}{25} \times 100\% = \boxed{600\%}$$

**Step 4 — M100 (Stress at 100% elongation):**
At 100% elongation, the strain = 100% = 1.0, so $L = L_0 (1 + 1.0) = 50$ mm.
$$M100 = \frac{F_{100\%}}{A_0} = \frac{96 \, \text{N}}{12 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2} = 8 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = \boxed{8 \, \text{MPa}}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Process Step Cross-Reference

| Property | Mastication | Compounding | Shaping | Vulcanization | Finishing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | ✗ | ✓✓ (fillers) | ✗ | ✓ (cross-link density) | ✗ |
| **Tensile Strength** | ✗ | ✓✓ (carbon black) | ✗ | ✓✓✓ (cross-links) | ✗ |
| **Elongation** | ✓ (slight ↑) | ✓ (softeners ↑) | ✗ | ✓ (high cross-link ↓) | ✗ |
| **Resilience** | ✗ | ✓ (filler type) | ✗ | ✓✓ (cross-link type) | ✗ |
| **Compression Set** | ✗ | ✓ (curative system) | ✗ | ✓✓✓ (dominant) | ✗ |
| **Mooney Viscosity** | ✓✓✓ (dominant) | ✓ (plasticizers) | ✗ | N/A | ✗ |
| **Abrasion Resistance** | ✗ | ✓✓ (carbon black) | ✗ | ✓ (cross-link density) | ✗ |
| **Tear Strength** | ✗ | ✓✓ (filler, antiozonant) | ✗ | ✓✓ (cross-link type) | ✗ |
| **Geometry** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ (dominant) | ✓ (mold) | ✓✓ (deflash) |
| **Surface Finish** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **Legend:** ✓ = affects, ✓✓ = strongly affects, ✓✓✓ = dominant/primary control, ✗ = no significant effect. Use this matrix in KTU answers to demonstrate **cross-linkage of theory** — examiners reward answers that connect properties to processes.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rubber Manufacturing Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Rubber Bale<br>NRSBRBRCR] --> nodeB[Step 1 Mastication<br>Two Roll Mill or Banbury Mixer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2 Compounding<br>Addition of Additives]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3 Shaping<br>CompressionTransferInjection Molding]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4 Vulcanization<br>Heat and Pressure with Sulphur]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5 Finishing<br>Deflashing and Trimming]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6 Quality Control<br>HardnessTensileCompression Set Tests]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Final Product Pass QC}
    nodeH --> nodeI[Accepted Part TyreSealGasketHose]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Rejected Part Recycling]
    nodeC -.->|Property Creation| nodeK[Hardness Tensile Abrasion]
    nodeE -.->|Property Creation| nodeL[Elasticity Resilience Compression Set]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation:** The **solid arrows** show the *process flow*, while the **dotted arrows** show the *property-creation linkage* — examiners reward diagrams that distinguish *physical flow* from *property transformation*.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Property → Process → Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRaw[Raw Rubber Polymer]
    nodeProc1[Mastication]
    nodeProc2[Compounding]
    nodeProc3[Shaping]
    nodeProc4[Vulcanization]
    nodePropM[Mooney Viscosity Reduced]
    nodePropH[Hardness Adjusted]
    nodePropT[Tensile Boosted]
    nodePropE[Elasticity Established]
    nodeApp1[Engine Mounts High Resilience]
    nodeApp2[Seals and Gaskets Low Compression Set]
    nodeApp3[Conveyor Belts High Abrasion]
    nodeApp4[Oil Hoses Oil Resistance]
    nodeRaw --> nodeProc1
    nodeProc1 -->|Reduces molecular weight| nodePropM
    nodeProc2 -->|Filler loading| nodePropH
    nodeProc2 -->|Carbon black addition| nodePropT
    nodeProc4 -->|Cross linking| nodePropE
    nodePropM --> nodeProc2
    nodePropE --> nodeApp1
    nodePropH --> nodeApp2
    nodePropT --> nodeApp3
    nodePropE --> nodeApp2
```

---

## 4.3 Compression Molding Press — Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTop[Top Mold Half Fixed]
    nodeBot[Bottom Mold Half Movable]
    nodeHyd[Hydraulic Press Platen]
    nodeHeat[Heating Platens 150C to 180C]
    nodeRaw[Green Compound Preform]
    nodeCav[Mold Cavity Shape]
    nodeFlash[Flash at Parting Line]
    nodePart[Finished Cured Part]
    nodeHyd --> nodeTop
    nodeHeat --> nodeTop
    nodeHeat --> nodeBot
    nodeRaw --> nodeCav
    nodeTop --> nodeCav
    nodeBot --> nodeCav
    nodeCav -->|Heat and pressure| nodePart
    nodeCav -.->|Excess| nodeFlash
    nodeHyd -->|Apply pressure 10 to 20 MPa| nodeTop
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Cross-Reference:** This block diagram represents a **compression molding press**. The hydraulic press applies ~ 10–20 MPa pressure; the heated platens (typically 150°C–180°C) provide the temperature for vulcanization. The mold cavity defines the part shape; the flash at the parting line is later trimmed in Step 5.

---

## 4.4 Sulphur Cross-Link Formation — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Polymer Chain A CH2 groups]
    nodeP2[Polymer Chain B CH2 groups]
    nodeAcc[Accelerator Active Species]
    nodeS[Sulphur S8 Ring]
    nodeX[Cross Link C Sx C Bridge]
    nodeP1 -->|Activation| nodeAcc
    nodeS -->|Ring opening| nodeAcc
    nodeAcc --> nodeX
    nodeP2 --> nodeX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Sulphur ($\text{S}_8$) rings open at vulcanization temperature, react with the accelerator, and bridge the two adjacent polymer chains. The variable $x$ in $\text{C-S}_x\text{-C}$ represents the number of sulphur atoms per bridge (1, 2, or more). **Mono-sulphidic bridges** ($x=1$) are thermally stable (good for high-temperature seals); **Poly-sulphidic bridges** ($x=3$–$4$) give high resilience (good for springs).

---
