# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any four relevant engineering properties of rubber and state the property that must be high for a rubber used in a vehicle engine mounting."**

**Model Answer (For Examiner Valuation):**

The four relevant engineering properties are:
1. **Hardness (Shore A)** — resistance to local indentation.
2. **Tensile Strength (MPa)** — maximum stress before rupture.
3. **Resilience (%)** — ratio of energy returned to energy supplied.
4. **Compression Set (%)** — permanent deformation after prolonged compression.

For a **vehicle engine mounting**, the property that must be high is **RESILIENCE**. An engine mount must absorb the high-frequency vibrations of the engine and return the stored energy efficiently, *isolating* the engine vibrations from the chassis. Low resilience would mean the engine's vibrations are transmitted to the passenger cabin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 4 properties × 0.5 mark = 2 marks; correct identification of resilience + valid justification = 1 mark. Total = 3.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"What is the function of 'mastication' in rubber processing? Name the equipment used for it."**

**Model Answer:**

**Mastication** is the mechanical breakdown of long polymer chains in raw rubber under heat and shear, with the objective of **reducing the Mooney viscosity** to a level suitable for further processing (mixing, extrusion, molding). It converts a tough, high-viscosity bale into a soft, plasticized, processable mass.

**Equipment used:** **Two-roll mill** (open mill) and **internal mixer** (Banbury mixer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** Definition = 1.5 marks, Objective / effect = 1 mark, Equipment = 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** *Attempt any ONE full question from this module. Each full question is for 14 marks and has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the four main engineering properties of rubber — **hardness, tensile strength, elongation at break, and compression set** — with the standard test method and typical values for natural rubber. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **six processing steps** involved in the manufacture of a rubber product, with emphasis on the role of **compounding** and **vulcanization** in establishing the final properties. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Property | Definition | Test Method | Typical Value (NR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardness** | Resistance to local indentation | Shore A Durometer, ASTM D2240 | 30–90 Shore A |
| **Tensile Strength** | Max stress before rupture | Dumbbell + UTM, ASTM D412 | 17–25 MPa |
| **Elongation at Break** | % increase in gauge length at break | ASTM D412 | 600%–1000% |
| **Compression Set** | % permanent deformation after prolonged compression | ASTM D395, ISO 815 | 10%–30% |

**Key Examinable Points (for 7 marks):**
- Each property correctly defined = 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks.
- Test method identified = 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks.
- Typical values given = 0.25 mark × 4 = 1 mark.
- **Engineering significance** (why each property matters) = 0.5 mark × 4 = 2 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

The **six processing steps** are:

1. **Mastication** — Raw rubber bale is sheared on a two-roll mill or Banbury mixer to reduce molecular weight and Mooney viscosity. Peptizers may be added.

2. **Compounding / Mixing** — Additives are added in precise amounts (in *phr* — parts per hundred rubber). The key additives are: **vulcanizing agents** (sulphur, 1–3 phr), **accelerators** (CBS, MBT, TMTD), **activators** (ZnO + stearic acid), **reinforcing fillers** (carbon black, silica), **plasticizers** (mineral oil), and **antioxidants** (TMQ, 6PPD). The choice of compounding recipe directly determines the final hardness, tensile strength, abrasion resistance, and ageing behaviour.

3. **Shaping / Forming** — The compounded "green" rubber is shaped by **compression molding** (large, low-volume parts), **transfer molding** (inserts), **injection molding** (high-volume precision), **extrusion** (continuous profiles), or **calendaring** (sheets).

4. **Vulcanization (Curing)** — The shaped green rubber is heated in a mold at **140°C–180°C** for a defined time. Sulphur cross-links (–C–S$_x$–C–) form between adjacent polymer chains, transforming the soft, plastic green rubber into a strong, elastic, durable solid. The degree of cross-linking is measured by the *molecular weight between cross-links* ($M_c$); lower $M_c$ = harder and less extensible rubber.

5. **Finishing / Deflashing** — The flash (excess rubber at the mold parting line) is removed by **manual trimming** or **cryogenic tumbling**. Other finishing operations include **post-curing** (silicone, fluoroelastomer) and **surface treatment** (chlorination for bonding).

6. **Quality Control** — Each part is tested for hardness, tensile strength, elongation, compression set, and dimensions. Failed parts are recycled; passed parts are packed and shipped.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students **omit the specific role of vulcanization**, writing it as a vague "heat treatment". Examiners specifically want to see: **(i) cross-link formation, (ii) temperature range, (iii) effect on elasticity and tensile strength.**
> - Do not write "sulphur hardens rubber" — write "**sulphur creates cross-links between polymer chains, transforming linear polymer chains into a 3-D network**".
> - Do not confuse **vulcanization** with **mastication** — mastication *reduces* molecular weight (softens); vulcanization *creates cross-links* (hardens).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** A rubber specimen of original gauge length 25 mm and cross-section 4 mm × 2 mm ruptured at a force of 160 N, with a final length of 150 mm. The force at 100% elongation was 64 N. Calculate the **Tensile Strength, Elongation at Break, and M100**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of additives** in rubber compounding. With a suitable table, list at least **six classes of additives**, their function, and one example each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $L_0 = 25$ mm, $A_0 = 4 \times 2 = 8 \, \text{mm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2$, $F_{\max} = 160$ N, $L_b = 150$ mm, $F_{100\%} = 64$ N.

**Step 1 — Tensile Strength:**
$$TS = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_0} = \frac{160}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 20 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = \boxed{20 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Elongation at Break:**
$$E_b = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{150 - 25}{25} \times 100 = \frac{125}{25} \times 100 = \boxed{500\%}$$

**Step 3 — M100 (Stress at 100% elongation):**
$$M100 = \frac{F_{100\%}}{A_0} = \frac{64}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 8 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = \boxed{8 \, \text{MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q(a)):** Students often make the following errors:
> 1. Forgetting to convert mm² to m² (must multiply by $10^{-6}$).
> 2. Using $L_b/L_0$ instead of $(L_b - L_0)/L_0$ for elongation.
> 3. Forgetting the "× 100" to express elongation as a percentage.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

Compounding additives are mixed into raw rubber to **engineer specific properties** that raw rubber alone does not possess (e.g., raw rubber is too soft, too sticky, ages rapidly, and is not elastic). Each class of additive plays a defined role:

| Class of Additive | Function | Example | Typical Loading (phr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vulcanizing Agent** | Forms cross-links between chains; establishes elasticity | Sulphur (S) | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| **Accelerator** | Speeds up vulcanization; reduces cure time | CBS, MBT, TMTD | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| **Activator** | Activates the accelerator | ZnO + Stearic acid | 3.0 – 5.0 + 1.0 – 2.0 |
| **Reinforcing Filler** | Boosts tensile, hardness, abrasion | Carbon black (HAF, ISAF) | 30 – 60 |
| **Non-reinforcing Filler** | Cost reduction; bulk; moderate hardness | Calcium carbonate, China clay | 20 – 100 |
| **Plasticizer / Softener** | Lowers Mooney viscosity; improves flexibility | Mineral oil, DOP, DOS | 5 – 30 |
| **Antioxidant** | Slows down oxidative ageing | TMQ, 6PPD | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| **Antiozonant** | Prevents ozone cracking | 6PPD, IPPD | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| **Pigment** | Provides colour | Titanium dioxide, Iron oxide | 2 – 10 |
| **Blowing Agent** | Creates cellular / foam rubber | Azodicarbonamide (ADC) | 2 – 10 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q(b)):** Examiners want to see:
> - **Minimum 6 classes** of additives correctly named.
> - The **function** of each class must be *engineering-relevant* (e.g., "carbon black increases tensile strength by reinforcing the polymer network" — not just "makes it strong").
> - The **example** must be a *specific chemical*, not a vague class.
> - Many students confuse **activator** with **accelerator** — the accelerator is the *catalyst*; the activator is its *co-catalyst* (ZnO is a co-catalyst for the sulphur-CBS system).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Written Exams:**

**A. Properties of Rubber (Mnemonic: "ME-TR")**
- **M**echanical → Hardness (Shore A, 0–100), Tensile Strength (MPa), Elongation (%), Modulus (M100, M200, M300)
- **E**lastic → Resilience (%, energy returned), Compression Set (%, lower = better)
- **T**oughness → Abrasion Resistance (volume loss), Tear Strength (kN/m)
- **R**heological → Mooney Viscosity (MU), Scorch Time $t_{s2}$, Cure Time $t_{90}$

**B. Processing Steps (in order):**
1. **Mastication** → reduces Mooney viscosity.
2. **Compounding** → creates hardness, tensile, abrasion, ageing.
3. **Shaping** → creates geometry (compression / transfer / injection / extrusion / calendering).
4. **Vulcanization** → creates elasticity, resilience, compression set, tensile, tear.
5. **Finishing** → removes flash, surface treatment.
6. **Quality Control** → verifies all properties meet spec.

**C. Vulcanization — Key Facts:**
- Discovered by **Charles Goodyear, 1839** (favourite 3-mark KTU question).
- Uses **sulphur** (1–3 phr) + **accelerator** + **activator** (ZnO + stearic acid).
- Temperature: **140°C – 180°C**.
- Forms **sulphur cross-links** (–C–S$_x$–C–) between polymer chains.
- Result: **3-D network structure** → elastic, strong, durable rubber.

**D. Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ❌ "Sulphur hardens rubber" → ✅ "Sulphur **cross-links** polymer chains".
- ❌ "Mastication cures rubber" → ✅ "Mastication **softens** rubber (reduces viscosity)".
- ❌ "Vulcanization improves all properties" → ✅ "Vulcanization **reduces** plasticity, tack, solubility".
- ❌ Forgetting to convert mm² to m² in TS calculations.
- ❌ Confusing **activator** (ZnO) with **accelerator** (CBS, MBT).
- ❌ Writing "phr" as "percentage" — it is **parts per hundred rubber**.

**E. Property ↔ Application Quick-Reference (for 14-mark application questions):**
- Tyre treads → **High Abrasion + Tensile** (NR + carbon black + SBR)
- Engine mounts → **High Resilience** (NR, BR)
- Shock absorbers → **Low Resilience** (Butyl, EPDM)
- O-rings, oil seals → **Low Compression Set + Oil Resistance** (NBR, FKM)
- Window/door seals → **Weather/Ozone Resistance** (EPDM)
- High-temp gaskets → **Heat Resistance** (Silicone, FKM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy (2024 Scheme):**
> - For a **3-mark question**: list 3–4 properties, each with a one-line engineering meaning.
> - For a **14-mark question**: structure your answer as **(i) theory** (definitions, mechanism) + **(ii) numerical** (if asked) + **(iii) application** (which rubber for which use). Examiners award **application marks** generously — never skip them.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rubber Processing Chain — The Big Picture

Modern rubber product manufacturing is a multi-stage batch process where raw, tough, high-viscosity natural or synthetic rubber is converted into elastic, durable finished goods. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these operations into a logical sequence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rubber Manufacturing Pipeline:**
> **Mastication → Mixing → Shaping → Calendaring → Curing (Vulcanization)**
> Every rubber product (tyre, gasket, sole, hose) passes through these stages in some form.

### 1.1.1 Mastication

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Mastication is the *mechanical and/or thermo-chemical breakdown of long polymer chains of raw natural rubber (or synthetic rubber) to reduce its viscosity (Mooney viscosity), increase its plasticity, and make it amenable to the incorporation of compounding ingredients.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Cold Butter" Analogy 🥚**
Think of a block of cold butter straight from the fridge: hard, crumbly, and impossible to spread. If you repeatedly press, fold, and roll it with a rolling pin, you warm it up and break the crystalline fat network — the butter becomes smooth, soft, and easy to spread. **Mastication is exactly this**, but for raw rubber:
- The "cold block" is high-molecular-weight raw rubber (viscosity ~10⁷–10⁸ poise).
- The "rolling pin" is a two-roll mill or an internal Banbury mixer.
- The "warming and softening" is the oxidative scission of C–C and C–S bonds in the polymer backbone.
- The "spreadable butter" is the masticated rubber with a *lower Mooney viscosity* of typically **40–80 ML(1+4) at 100 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Effect:** Mastication reduces the average molecular weight ($\overline{M_w}$) of the polymer from ~10⁶ g/mol to ~2–4 × 10⁵ g/mol by chain scission.

### 1.1.2 Mixing

**Formal Definition:**
Mixing is the *homogeneous dispersion of various compounding ingredients (fillers, plasticizers, vulcanizing agents, accelerators, antioxidants, pigments) into the masticated rubber to produce a uniform compound with the required processing and end-use properties.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Dough Kneading" Analogy 🍞**
Imagine you are making *chapathi* dough. You do not just dump flour and water together. You first knead the flour to develop gluten, then *gradually* add water, oil, salt, and knead again. The order, time, and temperature matter — knead too little and the dough is lumpy; knead too much and it becomes tough.
Rubber mixing is identical:
- **Masticated rubber = the flour base** (polymer matrix).
- **Compounding ingredients = water, oil, salt, etc.**
- **Two-roll mill / Banbury mixer = the kneader**.
- The result is a homogeneous **rubber compound** (called a "stock" or "batch") ready for shaping.

### 1.1.3 Shaping

**Formal Definition:**
Shaping converts the rubber compound into a *pre-determined intermediate geometry* (extruded profiles, calendered sheets, molded blanks) before final curing.

**The Three Principal Shaping Routes:**

| Shaping Method | Typical Product Geometry | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| Extrusion | Tubes, hoses, tyre treads, weather strips | Continuous profile |
| Calendering | Sheets, coated fabrics, frictioned plies | Thin sheet / film |
| Molding (Compression/Transfer/Injection) | Tyres, gaskets, shoe soles, O-rings | Discrete 3-D part |

### 1.1.4 Calendaring

**Formal Definition:**
Calendaring is a *specialized shaping operation in which a rubber compound is passed through a series of precisely spaced, counter-rotating, heated rolls to produce continuous sheets of uniform thickness, or to coat/impregnate fabric plies with rubber.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Saree Printing Roller" Analogy 🧵**
A traditional textile printing press has two large heavy rollers that squeeze dye-paste uniformly onto fabric, and the gap between them controls how much paste is deposited. A rubber calender is the *mechanical big brother* of that printing press — except instead of dye, it extrudes a precise thickness of rubber (0.05 mm to 10 mm) onto fabric or as a free sheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance:** Over **90% of all rubber sheet products** in the world are produced by calendering. Ply calendaring is the heart of tyre carcass building.

### 1.1.5 Curing (Vulcanization)

**Formal Definition:**
Curing (or vulcanization) is the *irreversible chemical process of forming three-dimensional cross-links between adjacent rubber polymer chains, typically using sulfur (or peroxides, metal oxides, or other agents), under the influence of heat and pressure, to convert the soft, plastic, tacky rubber compound into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable thermoset elastomer.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Ladder" Analogy 🪜**
Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti: each strand is independent, slippery, and easily pulled apart. If you could glue every strand to its neighbours at 5–10 points along its length, the entire bowl becomes a single elastic network — you pull one strand, and the whole network stretches uniformly; release it, and it snaps back.
Curing builds this **3-D elastic network** using sulfur atoms (1–4 wt% of compound) as the "glue points" between polyisoprene chains. The process was discovered by **Charles Goodyear in 1839** — a cornerstone date in polymer engineering history.

### 1.1.6 Textile Manufacturing — Weaving

**Formal Definition:**
Weaving is the *interlacing of two distinct sets of yarns, the warp (lengthwise) and the weft (crosswise), at right angles on a loom to produce a woven fabric.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingers Crossing" Analogy ✋**
Hold both hands out flat, palms down, fingers spread. Now take your right hand and slide it *over* the index finger, *under* the middle, *over* the ring, *under* the pinky of the left hand. Your fingers are **interlaced** in a precise alternating pattern. Weaving is the industrial-scale mechanization of this interlacing, where:
- **Warp yarns** = your stationary left-hand fingers (held under tension on the loom).
- **Weft yarns** = your moving right-hand fingers (shot across via a shuttle or jet).
- **The pattern (over-under)** = the weave design (plain, twill, satin).
- **The loom** = the mechanical machine that performs millions of these interlacing actions per hour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Scale:** A modern **air-jet loom** can insert weft at **2000–2500 m/min (ppm)**, weaving ~5–7 million picks per day.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mastication — Theoretical Depth

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Chain Scission
Raw natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene) has $\overline{M_w}$ ≈ 10⁶ g/mol. Under mechanical shearing between hot rolls, two mechanisms operate **simultaneously**:

1. **Mechanical Scission (Free-Radical Mechano-Chemical):**
   The shear stress $\tau$ on an entangled chain exceeds the C–C bond energy (~348 kJ/mol). The chain ruptures, producing two macro-radicals.

$$\text{--CH}_2\text{--CH}=\text{C(CH}_3)\text{--CH}_2\text{--} \xrightarrow{\tau,\Delta} \text{--CH}_2\text{--CH}=\text{C(CH}_3)\text{--}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{CH}_2\text{--}$$

2. **Thermo-Oxidative Scission:**
   Heat + atmospheric O₂ attack the $\alpha$-methylenic carbon, generating peroxy radicals that cleave the chain.

$$\text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

### 2.1.2 Types of Mastication

| Type | Temperature | Mechanism | Peptizer Used? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Mastication** | 30–50 °C | Mechano-chemical radical scission dominates | Yes (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol – PCTP) |
| **Warm Mastication** | 50–80 °C | Mixed mechanism | Optional |
| **Hot Mastication** | >130 °C | Pure oxidative scission (radical recombination becomes significant) | No |
| **Chemical / Peptizer Mastication** | 70–110 °C | Peptizer breaks free-radical recombination, accelerates softening | Yes (PCTP, bisbenzamide) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cold mastication with 0.1–0.3% peptizer** is the industrial standard — it gives the fastest viscosity reduction with minimum re-combination.

### 2.1.3 Equipment Used for Mastication

- **Two-Roll Open Mill:** Two counter-rotating chilled cast-iron rolls, diameter 550 mm, length 1500 mm, friction ratio 1:1.2 to 1:1.4. Used for batch mastication and small-batch mixing.
- **Internal Mixer (Banbury Mixer):** Enclosed chamber with two interlocking rotors (tangential or intermeshing). Capacity 50–500 L. Used for high-volume production.
- **Continuous Mixer (e.g., MVX, Farrel Continuous Mixer):** Used in modern plants for very high throughput.

## 2.2 Mixing — Theoretical Depth

### 2.2.1 The Mixing Cycle
A standard Banbury mixing cycle has **four phases**:

| Phase | Time (s) | Action | State of Compound |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incorporation** | 0–30 | Polymer + powder fillers added | Dry powder stage |
| **Dispersion** | 30–120 | High rotor speed, ram pressure | Powder wets out |
| **Plasticization (Breakdown)** | 120–240 | Shear heating raises T to 140–160 °C | Molten, viscous |
| **Final Mix / Drop** | 240–360 | Curatives added at lower T (≤110 °C) | Compound ready for shaping |

> [!WARNING]
> **Curatives (sulfur, accelerators) are added LAST** at temperatures below 110 °C to prevent premature vulcanization ("scorch"). This is called **"two-pass mixing"** in industry parlance.

### 2.2.2 Order of Ingredient Addition (KTU High-Yield Sequence)
1. Polymer (masticated rubber)
2. Peptizer / Process aid
3. Filler (carbon black, silica) — in 2–3 increments
4. Plasticizer (oil)
5. Activator (ZnO, stearic acid)
6. Antioxidant / antiozonant
7. Vulcanizing agent (sulfur) + accelerators — *FINAL PASS*

### 2.2.3 Key Mixing Performance Metric
**Dispersion Index (DI)** quantifies how well carbon black is dispersed:

$$DI = 1 - \frac{A_{\text{undispersed}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$$

A high DI (→1) means a well-dispersed, defect-free compound.

## 2.3 Calendaring — Theoretical Depth

### 2.3.1 Roll Configuration Types

```
   Z-Type (4-roll)         I-Type (4-roll)         L-Type (3-roll)
                                                      
   ┌──┐                   ┌──┐                     ┌──┐
   │  │                   │  │                     │  │
   └──┘                   └──┘                     └──┘
     ↘                       │                       │
       ↘                     │                       │
   ┌──┐                     │                       │
   │  │                     │                     ┌──┘
   └──┘                   ┌──┘                   ┌──┘
     ↗                     │                     │  
   ┌──┐                   └──┐                  Feed
   │  │                     │                  
   └──┘                   ┌──┐
   ┌──┐                   │  │
   │  │                   └──┘
   └──┘
```

### 2.3.2 The Calendering Equations (KTU High-Yield)

**Output rate (mass throughput):**

$$Q = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot t \cdot w \cdot 60 \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$

**Newtonian approximation of roll-separating force (Fritz formula):**

$$F = \frac{2 \cdot \eta \cdot v \cdot L \cdot R}{h} \quad \text{[N]}$$

**Friction ratio (velocity ratio between adjacent rolls):**

$$f = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{D_1 \cdot N_1}{D_2 \cdot N_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $D$ = roll diameter (m), $N$ = roll speed (rpm), $\rho$ = compound density (kg/m³), $t$ = sheet thickness (m), $w$ = sheet width (m), $v$ = linear velocity (m/s), $L$ = roll contact length (m), $R$ = roll radius (m), $h$ = nip gap (m), $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s).

### 2.3.3 Calendering Operations

| Operation | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Sheet** | Direct sheet formation from compound | General sheet rubber, gaskets |
| **Frictioning** | High friction ratio (1:1.5–1:2) drives compound INTO fabric | Tyre carcass ply |
| **Skimming (Coating)** | Compound is laid ONTO fabric surface | Conveyor belts, waterproof cloth |
| **Embossing / Lining** | Engraved roll imprints pattern | Matting, decorative sheeting |

## 2.4 Curing (Vulcanization) — Theoretical Depth

### 2.4.1 The Sulfur Vulcanization Reaction (Simplified)

$$\text{R--S--H} + \text{H--S--R'} \xrightarrow{\text{Accelerator, ZnO, heat}} \text{R--S--S--R'} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \uparrow$$

The polysulfidic cross-link ($\text{R--S}_x\text{--R'}$, where $x$ = 1–8) ties two adjacent polymer chains.

### 2.4.2 Curing System Types (KTU Must-Know)

| System | Sulfur (% w/w) | Accelerator | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (CV)** | 2.5–3.5 | Low | Good mechanical strength, poor heat aging |
| **Semi-Efficient (Semi-EV)** | 1.0–1.7 | Medium | Balance of properties |
| **Efficient (EV)** | 0.3–0.8 | High | Excellent heat aging, used in tyres |
| **Peroxide Cure** | 0 (uses dicumyl peroxide) | – | Heat-resistant, low compression set |

### 2.4.3 Vulcanization Parameters

The **cure time** $t_{90}$ (time to reach 90% of maximum torque on a rheometer) is calculated using the **Arrhenius equation** for the cure rate:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$$

And the empirical rule of thumb (Bierbaum-Arrhenius doubling rule):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For every 10 °C increase in cure temperature, the cure time is halved (approx).**

### 2.4.4 Curing Methods (KTU Must-Know)
- **Compression Molding (Press Curing):** Compound placed in heated mold under 10–20 MPa; T = 140–180 °C; t = 5–30 min.
- **Transfer Molding:** Compound pre-heated in transfer pot, injected into closed mold. Used for complex inserts.
- **Injection Molding:** Compound injected by screw plunger into mold. Cycle time 30–90 s.
- **Autoclave Curing:** For large items (tyre retreads, rubber-lined tanks). Steam at 0.3–0.7 MPa.
- **Continuous Curing (LCM – Liquid Curing Medium, or salt bath, or microwave):** For extruded profiles.
- **Open Steam Curing:** For thin dipped goods.

## 2.5 Weaving — Theoretical Depth

### 2.5.1 Basic Weave Patterns (KTU High-Yield)

| Weave | Warp:Weft Intersections | Properties | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plain (Tabby)** | 1 over, 1 under, alternates | Most stable, least drape, strongest | Shirting, bedsheets, flags |
| **Twill** | 2 over, 1 under (diagonal) | Diagonal wale, good drape, durable | Denim, jackets, drill |
| **Satin** | 4 over, 1 under, scattered | Lustrous, smooth, flexible | Sarees, linings, evening wear |

### 2.5.2 Loom Motion Cycle (Picking Cycle)
Every weaving cycle consists of **four primary motions** + **two secondary motions**:

**Primary:**
1. **Shedding:** Warp yarns separated into upper & lower layers by heddles.
2. **Picking (Weft Insertion):** Weft yarn shot across the shed.
3. **Beating-up:** Reed pushes the newly inserted pick to the fell of the cloth.
4. **Take-up & Let-off:** Fabric wound forward; warp fed from beam.

**Secondary:**
5. **Warp Protection:** Stops loom on yarn break.
6. **Weft Stop Motion:** Stops loom on weft exhaustion.

### 2.5.3 Loom Productivity Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$\text{Picks per minute (ppm)} = \frac{V_{\text{weft insertion}}}{10} \quad \text{(for 10-pick repeat)}$$

$$\text{Production (m/hr)} = \frac{\text{ppm} \times 60}{\text{epi} \times \text{width correction}}$$

**Where:**
- ppm = picks per minute
- epi = ends per inch (warp density)
- ppi = picks per inch (weft density)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Table

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{M_w}$ reduction by mastication | Viscosity control |
| 2 | Mooney Viscosity $\text{ML}(1+4)@100°C$ | Plasticity measurement |
| 3 | Cure rate: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | Curing kinetics |
| 4 | Cure time rule: T ↑ 10 °C → t ↓ 50% | Curing parameter selection |
| 5 | $Q = \pi D N \rho t w \cdot 60$ | Calendaring throughput |

| 7 | Friction ratio: $f = D_1 N_1 / (D_2 N_2)$ | Frictioning operation |

| 9 | Cross-link density: $\nu = \rho N_A / \overline{M_c}$ | Vulcanizate property |
| 10 | Fabric areal density: $\text{GSM} = (\text{warp wt} + \text{weft wt})/\text{area}$ | Quality control |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Cure Time Using Arrhenius Equation

The rate constant of a chemical reaction follows the Arrhenius form:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (RT)}$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

For two different cure temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ (in Kelvin), with corresponding rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_1}$$

$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T_2}$$

Subtracting the second from the first:

$$\ln k_1 - \ln k_2 = -\frac{E_a}{R T_1} + \frac{E_a}{R T_2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental Arrhenius equation for cure kinetics**, used to predict cure times at new temperatures from data at a reference temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> For a first-order approximation with the "Bierbaum doubling rule", the time ratio for a 10 °C change is: $t_1/t_2 = 2^{\Delta T/10}$.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Calendar Output Rate

We start with the linear velocity of a roll surface:

$$V = \pi D N \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

The mass of rubber delivered per unit time from a single roll is:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{single}} = \rho \cdot t \cdot w \cdot V = \rho \cdot t \cdot w \cdot \pi D N \quad \text{[kg/min]}$$

For continuous operation over an hour, multiply by 60:

$$Q = \pi D N \rho t w \cdot 60 \quad \text{[kg/hr]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **calender output equation** — it assumes (i) no slip, (ii) negligible swell, and (iii) constant roll gap.

**Numerical Worked Example:**
Given: $D = 0.5$ m, $N = 30$ rpm, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, $t = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m (2 mm), $w = 1.5$ m. Find the hourly production.

Step 1 — Compute $\pi D N$:
$$\pi \times 0.5 \times 30 = 15\pi \approx 47.124 \text{ m/min}$$

Step 2 — Multiply by $\rho t w$:
$$47.124 \times 1100 \times 0.002 \times 1.5 = 47.124 \times 3.3 = 155.51 \text{ kg/min}$$

Step 3 — Convert to kg/hr:
$$Q = 155.51 \times 60 = 9330.6 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 9.33 \text{ tonnes/hr}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Cross-link Density and Modulus

The **affine network model** (Treloar, 1975) gives the elastic modulus of a cross-linked rubber:

$$E = 3 \nu R T = \frac{3 \rho R T}{\overline{M_c}}$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa)
- $\nu$ = number of elastically active network chains per unit volume (mol/m³)
- $\rho$ = density of rubber (kg/m³)
- $\overline{M_c}$ = average molecular weight between cross-links (kg/mol)
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

The cross-link density $\nu$ is related to the **average molecular weight between cross-links** $\overline{M_c}$:

$$\nu = \frac{\rho N_A}{\overline{M_c}}$$

**Worked Example — Tyre Sidewall Compound:**
Given: $\rho = 920$ kg/m³, $\overline{M_c} = 5000$ g/mol = 5 kg/mol, $T = 300$ K. Find the Young's modulus.

Step 1 — Compute $\nu$:
$$\nu = \frac{920 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{5} = 1.108 \times 10^{26} \text{ chains/m}^3$$

Step 2 — Compute $E$:
$$E = 3 \times 1.108 \times 10^{26} \times 8.314 \times 300 / 6.022 \times 10^{23}$$

Let me recompute in proper units. Since $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), the simpler form is:

$$E = \frac{3 \rho R T}{\overline{M_c}} = \frac{3 \times 920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{5}$$

$$E = \frac{3 \times 920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{5} = \frac{6,886,584}{5} \approx 1.377 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.38 \text{ MPa}$$

This matches the typical modulus of a lightly-crosslinked rubber.

### 3.1.4 Weaving Productivity Derivation

Given a loom running at $n$ picks per minute (ppm), and a fabric with $p$ picks per inch (ppi) and $w$ inches of width covered with fabric:

Picks per minute produced per inch of width = $n / p$ (not quite — let me redo).

Length of cloth woven per minute (in inches) = $n / p$ inches/min.
Convert to yards per hour: $\text{yd/min} = (n/p) / 36$ → $\text{yd/hr} = (n/p) \times (60/36) = (5n)/(3p)$.

$$\text{Yards per hour} = \frac{n \times 60}{p \times 36} = \frac{5n}{3p}$$

**Worked Example:**
A projectile loom runs at 480 ppm, weaving a 100 ppi poplin. Find the yardage per hour.

$$\text{yd/hr} = \frac{5 \times 480}{3 \times 100} = \frac{2400}{300} = 8 \text{ yd/hr}$$

For a fabric width of 60 inches and an 80% efficiency:

$$\text{Effective yd/hr} = 8 \times 0.80 = 6.4 \text{ yd/hr} \approx 5.85 \text{ m/hr}$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Size Application for warp strength]
    C --> D[Drawing-in on Loom]
    D --> E[Weaving Loom]
    E --> E1[Shedding - Heddles raise and lower warp]
    E --> E2[Picking - Weft yarn shot across shed]
    E --> E3[Beating-up - Reed compacts pick]
    E --> E4[Take-up and Let-off - Maintain tension]
    E1 --> F[Woven Fabric]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Inspection and Mending]
    G --> H[Finishing Bleach Dye Print]
    H --> I[Finished Fabric Roll]

    style A fill:#fdebd0
    style E fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d5f5e3
    style I fill:#a9dfbf
```

## 4.4 Calendaring — Z-Type 4-Roll Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compound Feed] --> R1[Roll 1 Top]
    R1 --> R2[Roll 2]
    R2 --> R3[Roll 3]
    R3 --> R4[Roll 4 Bottom]
    R4 --> B[Sheet Output]
    R2 -.-> C[Fabric In for Frictioning]
    C --> R2

    style R1 fill:#fadbd8
    style R2 fill:#f5b7b1
    style R3 fill:#fadbd8
    style R4 fill:#f5b7b1
    style A fill:#fdebd0
    style B fill:#d5f5e3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Green Rubber Part in Mold] --> B[Heat and Pressure Applied]
    B --> C[Accelerator Activation Threshold]
    C --> D[Cross-link Formation Stage]
    D --> D1[Induction Period scorch delay]
    D --> D2[Fast Cure Rate Phase]
    D --> D3[Reversion or Plateau]
    D1 --> E[Reached t90 at 90 percent torque]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Cooling and Demolding]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, 3 marks]`
**Define mastication. Why is it necessary before mixing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mastication is the mechanical and/or thermo-chemical breakdown of long polymer chains of raw rubber to reduce its Mooney viscosity and increase plasticity **(1 mark)**. It is necessary because raw natural rubber has very high molecular weight (≈10⁶ g/mol) and is too tough/viscous to incorporate fillers and other additives uniformly **(1 mark)**. Mastication also improves the solubility of fillers, ensures uniform dispersion in the subsequent mixing stage, and provides a consistent compound viscosity for downstream processing **(1 mark)**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, 3 marks]`
**List any three primary motions of a weaving loom.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Shedding** — Separation of warp yarns into two layers (upper and lower) to form a "shed" through which the weft is passed. **(1 mark)**
2. **Picking (Weft Insertion)** — Insertion of the weft yarn through the shed using a shuttle, projectile, rapier, or fluid jet. **(1 mark)**
3. **Beating-up** — Pushing the newly inserted pick of weft to the "fell" of the cloth by the reed, thereby compacting the fabric. **(1 mark)**
*(Optionally: Take-up & Let-off for ½ mark each if asked.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 marks]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of mastication of natural rubber. Discuss the role of peptizers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and working of a four-roll Z-type calendar. List its main applications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition & Need (1 mark):**
Mastication is the controlled mechanical breakdown of long-chain polyisoprene molecules of raw natural rubber (NR) into shorter chains, achieved through the combined action of shear, heat, and (usually) a chemical peptizer, to reduce viscosity and improve processability.

**2. Mechanism of Chain Scission (3 marks):**
Under high shear between two counter-rotating rolls, the entangled polymer chains experience extreme tensile and shear stresses. When the local stress exceeds the C–C bond dissociation energy (~348 kJ/mol), the chains rupture by **mechano-chemical scission**, generating two macro-radicals:

$$R_1\text{–}R_2 \xrightarrow{\tau,\Delta} R_1^{\bullet} + \,^{\bullet}R_2$$

These radicals can then:
- **Re-combine** with each other (reverses the effect — undesirable).
- **React with O₂** to form peroxy radicals, which then propagate chain scission (thermo-oxidative mechanism):

$$R^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow ROO^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{RH} ROOH + R^{\bullet}$$

The dominant mechanism depends on temperature: below 80 °C, mechano-chemical scission dominates; above 130 °C, oxidative scission dominates; at intermediate temperatures, both compete.

**3. Role of Peptizers (2 marks):**
Peptizers (e.g., pentachlorothiophenol – PCTP, dibenzamido-diphenyl disulfide, 2,2′-dibenzamidodiphenyl disulfide) act as **radical acceptors/scavengers** that react with the macro-radicals faster than they can recombine:

$$R^{\bullet} + \text{PCTP} \rightarrow R\text{–S–Ar} + \,^{\bullet}\text{Cl} \quad (\text{Ar = aromatic})$$

This prevents re-combination, so chain scission is **irreversible** and proceeds rapidly even at 70–90 °C. Industrial practice uses **0.1–0.3 phr** (parts per hundred rubber) of peptizer.

**4. Effect on Molecular Weight (1 mark):**
Initial $\overline{M_w}$ ≈ 10⁶ g/mol is reduced to ≈ 2–4 × 10⁵ g/mol after mastication, with a corresponding drop in Mooney viscosity from 60–80 to 40–60 ML(1+4)@100 °C.

### Model Answer (b) — 7 marks

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
A Z-type calendar has four chilled cast-iron rolls (typically diameter 600 mm × 1800 mm length) arranged in a zig-zag ("Z") pattern:

```
Roll 1 (top) ↘
                  Roll 2 ↘
                            Roll 3 ↘
                                      Roll 4 (bottom) → Sheet output
```

- All rolls are internally cored for circulation of heating/cooling water (60–110 °C operation).
- Rolls 1, 2, 3 are driven by individual DC motors; roll 4 may be idler or driven.
- Each roll has its own **cross-axis (Crown) and counter-bending** mechanism to compensate for roll deflection.
- The **nip gap** between rolls 3 and 4 (the final nip) determines the final sheet thickness and is controlled by hydraulic or screw-down actuators with a precision of ±0.005 mm.
- An **automatic gauge control (AGC)** system uses β-ray or X-ray sensors to monitor sheet thickness in real time.

**2. Working (3 marks):**
- The compounded rubber is fed as a strip onto roll 1.
- The friction ratio between rolls 1 and 2 (~1:1.25) meters the compound forward.
- Rolls 2 and 3 form the main **bank** (a rolling mass of compound) that homogenizes the stock.
- The **final nip** (3/4) squeezes the compound to the precise thickness.
- For **frictioning**, fabric is fed between rolls 2 and 3 with a high friction ratio (1:1.5–2.0) so the compound is forced into the fabric interstices.
- For **skimming/coating**, a low friction ratio (1:1.0–1.05) deposits a thin coat of rubber on the fabric.

**3. Applications (1 mark):**
Tyre carcass plies, conveyor belt covers, rubber sheets, gaskets, friction stock, waterproof fabric, rubberized textiles, and PVC calendering.

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 marks]`
**(a)** What is vulcanization? With a neat sketch, explain the sulfur vulcanization process and the role of accelerators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, explain the various stages of weaving. Compare any three types of modern shuttleless looms. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Vulcanization (curing) is the irreversible chemical process of forming three-dimensional cross-links between adjacent rubber polymer chains, predominantly using sulfur (or its donors) under the influence of heat and pressure, in the presence of accelerators and activators, to convert the soft, plastic, tacky rubber compound into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable thermoset elastomer.

**2. The Reaction Scheme (3 marks):**
The simplified chemistry proceeds as:

$$\text{R–H (rubber)} + \text{S}_8 \xrightarrow{\text{Accelerator, ZnO, heat}} \text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$

where $x$ = 1–8 sulfur atoms per cross-link (mono- to poly-sulfidic).

**Step-by-step:**
- The **accelerator** (e.g., MBT, CBS, TBBS) reacts with sulfur to form an active sulfurating species.
- The **activator system** (ZnO + stearic acid) forms zinc soap, which complexes with the accelerator and increases its solubility in rubber.
- The active sulfurating species attacks the $\alpha$-methylenic carbon of the polyisoprene chain, abstracting hydrogen to form a rubber-bound sulfidic intermediate.
- This intermediate reacts with another chain to form the **cross-link**.

**3. Role of Accelerators (2 marks):**
- Reduce vulcanization time from hours to minutes (at 140–160 °C, from ~6 h to 6–15 min).
- Reduce the sulfur dose required (CV: 2.5–3.5% → EV: 0.3–0.8%).
- Improve physical properties (better aging, lower heat build-up).
- Provide **delayed-action** ("scorch safety") so the compound does not cure prematurely during mixing/shaping.
- Examples: MBT (2-mercaptobenzothiazole), CBS (N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide), TBBS (N-tert-butyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide).

**4. Cross-link Structure (1 mark):**
The cross-link may be **mono-sulfidic (R–S–R)**, **di-sulfidic (R–S–S–R)**, or **poly-sulfidic (R–S_x–R, x = 3–8)**. Conventional cures give mostly poly-sulfidic links (good strength, poor aging). EV cures give predominantly mono-sulfidic links (good aging).

### Model Answer (b) — 7 marks

**1. Stages of Weaving (4 marks):**
*(See block diagram in Section 4.3 above for visual aid)*

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| **Yarn Preparation** | Cone winding, warping (transfer of yarns from creel to warp beam), and sizing (PVA/starch coating for warp strength) |
| **Drawing-in & Knotting** | Yarns from warp beam are drawn through heddles and reed of the loom |
| **Weaving (Loom Operation)** | Four primary motions: Shedding → Picking → Beating-up → Take-up |
| **Inspection & Mending** | Manual/electronic fabric inspection for broken picks, missing ends |
| **Finishing** | Bleaching, dyeing, printing, calendaring (textile), stentering |

**2. Comparison of Three Shuttleless Looms (3 marks):**

| Feature | Projectile (Sulzer) | Rapier (Picanol) | Air-Jet (Toyota) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weft insertion** | Gripper projectile traverses shed | Rigid or flexible rapier carries weft | Compressed air carries weft |
| **Max ppm** | 400–1000 | 500–1200 | 1500–2500 |
| **Yarn range** | 6–300 tex | 5–500 tex (widest range) | Fine filament 5–80 tex (hydrophobic only) |
| **Loom noise** | High (115 dB) | Moderate (100 dB) | High (110 dB) |
| **Energy use** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Best for** | Filament fabrics, terry towels | Wool, jute, technical textiles, denim | Polyester, nylon, fashion |
| **Capital cost** | Very high | High | Moderate |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing mastication with mixing** — mastication = breaking polymer chains; mixing = dispersing additives. Many students blur these and lose 2–3 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of ZnO + stearic acid** as the activator system. Many students list only sulfur and accelerator in vulcanization.
> 3. **Wrong units in calender formulas** — `D` is in **meters** (not mm), `N` in **rps** or convert rpm correctly. A numerical mistake here can cost 4 marks.
> 4. **Saying "vulcanization is heating rubber"** — this is incomplete. The examiner wants **cross-link formation with sulfur (or peroxide) under heat + pressure**, and the three-dimensional network concept.
> 5. **In weaving answers, students often forget the take-up motion**, considering only shedding, picking, and beating-up. Take-up and let-off is the 4th primary motion.
> 6. **In calendering, students confuse "frictioning" and "skimming"** — both use fabric, but frictioning forces compound INTO the fabric, skimming lays compound ONTO the fabric.
> 7. **Not specifying the "Z", "L", or "I" type of calender** in diagrams — the examiner expects the configuration to be named.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

✅ **Mastication = chain scission**, increases plasticity, lowers Mooney viscosity, peptizers prevent re-combination.

✅ **Mixing = incorporation of additives** in the order: polymer → peptizer → filler → plasticizer → activator → antioxidant → curative (LAST).

✅ **Shaping** routes are **Extrusion, Calendering, Molding** (compression/transfer/injection).

✅ **Calendering** uses 2/3/4 counter-rotating heated rolls; **Z-type** most common. Operations: **plain sheet, frictioning (into fabric), skimming (onto fabric), embossing**.

✅ **Curing** = sulfur cross-linking (CV, Semi-EV, EV systems); peroxide cure is sulfur-free. Cured rubber is a 3-D thermoset network. **Cure time halves for every 10 °C rise** (Bierbaum rule).

✅ **Arrhenius equation** governs cure kinetics: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$.

✅ **Cross-link density** $\nu$ governs modulus: $E = 3\nu RT = 3\rho RT/\overline{M_c}$.

✅ **Weaving = interlacing of warp and weft**. The 4 primary loom motions: **Shedding, Picking, Beating-up, Take-up**.

✅ **Three basic weaves**: plain (1/1), twill (2/1), satin (4/1).

✅ **Shuttleless looms**: projectile (Sulzer), rapier (Picanol/Vamatex), water-jet (Nissan), air-jet (Toyota) — air-jet is fastest.

✅ **Loom production formula**: $\text{yd/hr} = (5 \times \text{ppm}) / (3 \times \text{ppi})$ corrected for efficiency and width.

✅ **Calender output formula**: $Q = \pi D N \rho t w \cdot 60$ kg/hr.

✅ **Mooney Viscosity (ML 1+4 @ 100 °C)** is the standard plasticity test; values 40–80 indicate well-masticated rubber.

✅ **Charles Goodyear, 1839** — discovered sulfur vulcanization. A KTU favorite date.

✅ **Two-pass mixing** prevents scorch (premature vulcanization in the mixer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cutting, Spinning, Yarn Production, Dyeing, Pattern Making, Bleaching, Grading, Packing, Wet Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cutting, Spinning, Yarn Production, Dyeing, Pattern Making, Bleaching, Grading, Packing, Wet Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: TEXTILE & GARMENT MANUFACTURING PROCESSES

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Focus:** This module traces the complete manufacturing pipeline of a garment — from raw **fiber** to the **finished, packed product**. The processes are grouped into three logical clusters: **Yarn Formation** (Spinning, Yarn Production), **Fabric Preparation & Coloration** (Bleaching, Dyeing, Wet Processing), and **Garment Construction** (Pattern Making, Cutting, Grading, Packing).

---

## 1.1 CUTTING (Fabric Cutting Operation)

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition
> **Fabric Cutting** is the process of separating garment components (pattern pieces) from a flat spread of woven, knitted, or non-woven fabric in accordance with a pre-approved marker plan, producing cut panels ready for sewing/joining.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Think of cutting like **making cookies from rolled-out dough**. The "dough" is the fabric spread (lay), the "cookie cutter" is the pattern/marker, and the "oven" is the next stage — sewing. If you place the cookie cutters cleverly (marker efficiency), you get more cookies (garments) from the same dough roll (fabric roll).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Metric:** **GSM** (Grams per Square Meter) — defines fabric weight and dictates cutting blade selection. A typical men's shirting fabric = **100–120 GSM**, denim = **300–450 GSM**.

### Types of Cutting Machines
| Method | Best Suited For | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Knife** | All-purpose, lay-based | Vertical reciprocating blade, **10–13 cm stroke** |
| **Round Knife** | Small pieces, curves | Circular rotating blade |
| **Band Knife** | Sharp curves, small batches | Endless loop blade, stationary table |
| **Die Cutting** | Mass production of identical parts | Custom-shaped hardened steel die |
| **Laser Cutting** | Synthetic fabrics, intricate designs | No-contact, sealed edge |
| **Water-Jet Cutting** | Multi-layer technical textiles | High-pressure abrasive water stream |
| **Notch & Drill** | Marking balance points | Pneumatic, paired with main cut |

---

## 1.2 SPINNING & YARN PRODUCTION

### 📌 Formal Definitions
- **Spinning:** The mechanical process of drawing out and twisting fibers to form a continuous strand of **yarn**.
- **Yarn Production:** The entire sequence of converting raw, loose fibers (natural or synthetic) into a finished, package-ready yarn — also called **short-staple** (cotton system) or **long-staple** (worsted) spinning.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you have a tuft of cotton — soft, loose, and formless. Spinning is the process of **pulling and twisting** it (like wringing a wet towel) until it becomes a strong, continuous thread you can weave or knit. The twist binds the fibers together the way rope strands bind each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Yarn Count Systems:**
> - **English Count (Ne):** Length in **840 yards per pound** (indirect system — higher Ne = finer yarn)
> - **Metric Count (Nm):** Length in **1 km per kg** (indirect)
> - **Tex:** Weight in **grams per 1000 m** (direct — higher Tex = coarser yarn)
> - **Denier:** Weight in **grams per 9000 m** (used for filament yarn)

### Sequence of Yarn Production (Short-Staple/Cotton System)
1. **Ginning** (separation of cotton fiber from seed) → Lap
2. **Blowroom** (mixing & cleaning) → Lap
3. **Carding** (fiber individualization) → Sliver
4. **Drawing** (parallelization & blending) → Sliver
5. **Roving** (attenuation with slight twist) → Roving
6. **Ring Spinning / Rotor / Air-Jet Spinning** → Yarn
7. **Winding, Clearing, Conditioning** → Package
8. **Doubled / Ply Yarn** (optional)

### Types of Spinning Systems
| System | Fiber Length | Yarn Quality | Productivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ring Spinning** | 25–60 mm | Highest quality | Baseline |
| **Rotor (Open-End)** | 25–45 mm | Medium | **5–7× Ring** |
| **Air-Jet Spinning** | 25–40 mm | Medium-fine | **10–15× Ring** |
| **Friction Spinning** | 25–45 mm | Coarse | **15–20× Ring** |
| **Vortex Spinning** | 25–40 mm | Fine | **8× Ring** |

---

## 1.3 WET PROCESSING (Pre-Treatment & Coloration)

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Wet Processing** is the collective term for all chemical/mechanical treatments of textile materials in aqueous medium that alter their appearance, hand, and performance before they are made into garments.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-Processes in Wet Processing (in order):**
> 1. **Desizing** (removal of sizing agent from warp)
> 2. **Scouring** (removal of natural/added impurities)
> 3. **Bleaching** (decolorization of natural pigments)
> 4. **Mercerizing** (for cotton — luster & dye uptake)
> 5. **Dyeing / Printing** (coloration)
> 6. **Finishing** (softening, calendaring, shrinkage control)

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Wet processing is the **"laundry + makeover"** step of textiles. Desizing = removing the starch from your shirt. Scouring = deep-cleaning the oils and dirt. Bleaching = making a white shirt actually white. Dyeing = giving a dull grey shirt a vibrant color. Finishing = ironing and softening it for the customer.

---

## 1.4 BLEACHING

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Bleaching** is a chemical oxidation/reduction process used to destroy natural and added coloring matter in textile fibers, producing a white substrate with uniform absorbency for subsequent dyeing/printing.

### Common Bleaching Agents
| Agent | Formula | Application | pH Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrogen Peroxide** | $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ | Universal (preferred) | **10.5–11.5** (alkaline) |
| **Sodium Hypochlorite** | $\text{NaOCl}$ | Cotton, home | **9–11** |
| **Sodium Chlorite** | $\text{NaClO}_2$ | Synthetic fibers | **3–5** (acidic) |
| **Sodium Perborate** | $\text{NaBO}_3$ | Domestic | Mild alkaline |
| **Reducing Bleach (SO₂)** | $\text{SO}_2$ | Wool, silk, PA | Acidic |

### General Peroxide Bleach Chemistry
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH, 90-100°C}} \text{HOO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{HOO}^- + \text{Colored pigment} \rightarrow \text{Oxidized (colorless) products} + \text{OH}^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whiteness Index (CIE):** Quantified by the **Berger Whiteness** or **Stensby** formula. An acceptable commercial white = **WI ≥ 65** (Berger).

---

## 1.5 DYEING

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Dyeing** is the application of a uniform, fast, and reproducible color to a textile substrate (fiber, yarn, fabric, or garment) using a dye molecule that is chemically/physically fixed to the substrate.

### Classification of Dyes
| Dye Class | Fiber Used | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive** | Cotton, Viscose | **Covalent bond** with –OH |
| **Direct** | Cotton, Viscose | **Hydrogen bonds / Van der Waals** |
| **Vat** | Cotton (denim) | Insoluble → soluble leuco → re-oxidized |
| **Disperse** | Polyester, Acetate | **Solid solution** in fiber |
| **Acid** | Wool, Silk, Nylon | **Ionic bond** with –NH₃⁺ |
| **Basic (Cationic)** | Acrylic, modified PET | Ionic bond with –COO⁻ |
| **Sulfur** | Cotton (dark shades) | Insoluble sulfide |
| **Azoic (Naphthol)** | Cotton | **Insitu** insoluble azo |

### Dyeing Methods
- **Exhaustion (Batch):** Jigger, Winch, Jet, Beam, Package
- **Semi-Continuous:** Pad-Batch, Pad-Roll
- **Continuous:** Pad-Steam, Pad-Dry, Thermosol

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Dyeing is like **marinating chicken** — the longer you keep it in the "flavor bath" (dye liquor) and the warmer it is, the deeper the color seeps in. The "flavor" (dye) must **chemically bond** with the "chicken" (fiber), otherwise it washes off easily (poor fastness).

### Key Dyeing Performance Parameters
- **Exhaustion (E%):** Percentage of dye transferred from bath to fiber.
- **Fixation (F%):** Percentage of exhausted dye actually fixed to fiber.
- **Build-up:** Maximum depth achievable with incremental dye concentration.
- **Levelness (Uniformity):** Visual/measurement uniformity across the substrate.

---

## 1.6 PATTERN MAKING

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Pattern Making** is the technical process of creating precise, two-dimensional templates (paper or digital) of every garment component based on body measurements, design sketches, fit specifications, and fabric behavior — to be used in marker planning and cutting.

### Methods of Pattern Making
1. **Drafting** — Body measurements → 2D patterns using rulers, curves, formulas.
2. **Flat Pattern Making** — Begin with a basic block; modify via slash & spread, pivot.
3. **Draping** — Fabric is pinned/manipulated on a dress form; seams are traced onto paper.
4. **Computer-Aided Pattern Making (CAD)** — Software like **Lectra, Gerber, Optitex**.

### Pattern Components (Men's Shirt Example)
- Front panel
- Back panel
- Yoke
- Sleeve
- Cuff
- Collar + Collar stand
- Pocket
- Placket
- Facing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notations on a Pattern:**
> - **Grain Line:** Arrow indicating warp direction (parallel to selvage).
> - **Notches:** Small triangular/squared cut-outs for matching seams.
> - **Drill Holes:** Mark button/buttonhole positions.
> - **Balance Points:** Cross marks for matching pieces.
> - **Seam Allowance (SA):** Typically **6 mm (1/4")** to **15 mm (5/8")** depending on seam type.

---

## 1.7 GRADING

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Grading** is the systematic process of increasing or decreasing the dimensions of a base pattern (of a given size) to create patterns for larger and smaller sizes, while preserving the original design, fit, and proportions.

### Grading Methods
- **Manual Grading:** Using a pattern master's grading rule and tracing.
- **Computer Grading:** Digitally shifting pattern points by **grade rules (gradations)** along the X and Y axes.

### Standard Body Measurement Grading Increments (Adult Apparel, per Size)
| Body Region | Chest (cm) | Waist (cm) | Hip (cm) | Back Length (cm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Per Size** | 4 | 4 | 4 | 1–1.5 |
| **Per 2 Sizes** | 8 | 8 | 8 | 2–3 |

### Grade Rule Formula
$$\Delta X_i = X_i^{\text{new size}} - X_i^{\text{base size}}$$
$$\Delta Y_i = Y_i^{\text{new size}} - Y_i^{\text{base size}}$$

Where each pattern point's coordinate ($X_i, Y_i$) is shifted by its pre-defined grade increment.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Grading is the **"Zoom-In, Zoom-Out"** of garment design. Imagine taking a perfect-fitting shirt pattern and proportionally enlarging it for a larger person — every line moves outward, but the shape, pockets, and collar stay in the right relative position.

---

## 1.8 PACKING

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Packing** is the final step in the garment manufacturing process where finished, inspected, and pressed garments are folded, tagged, wrapped, and packed into cartons with proper labeling for safe transportation, storage, and retail display.

### Types of Packing
1. **Flat Pack (Flat-fold):** Garment is laid flat, folded neatly — saves space.
2. **Hanger Pack:** Garment is on a hanger inside a polybag — premium retail-ready.
3. **Box Pack / Folded Carton:** Garment is folded into a printed carton (luxury brands).
4. **Vacuum Pack:** Air is removed to reduce volume — used for export shipping.
5. **Bundle Pack:** Multiple garments tied together (for shipment to retail warehouse).

### Packing Accessories
- **Polybag** (LDPE/PP) — primary protection
- **Butterfly/Back-sticker** — size stickers
- **Hang tags** — brand, price, barcode
- **Carton** — 5-ply corrugated, **bursting strength ≥ 10 kg/cm²**
- **Master carton** — outer shipping container

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-highlight:** Packing **efficiency (PE)** is calculated as:
> $$PE = \left(\frac{\text{Volume of garments in carton}}{\text{Volume of carton}}\right) \times 100\%$$
> Industry standard: **PE = 70–85%**

---

## 1.9 THE BIG PICTURE — Process Flow

```
FIBER → SPINNING → YARN → KNITTING/WEAVING → FABRIC
       ↓                              ↓
  (Yarn Production)            WET PROCESSING
                                  ↓
        Bleaching → Dyeing → Printing → Finishing
                                  ↓
                          FABRIC (Finished)
                                  ↓
                        PATTERN MAKING
                                  ↓
                            GRADING
                                  ↓
                            CUTTING
                                  ↓
                            SEWING
                                  ↓
                          INSPECTION
                                  ↓
                            PACKING
                                  ↓
                         SHIPMENT
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 CUTTING — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Lay Planning & Marker Planning
**Marker** is a layout of all pattern pieces within a rectangular boundary (matching the usable fabric width), arranged to **minimize waste**. Marker efficiency directly impacts fabric cost — the most expensive component in a garment.

**Fabric Lay** is the spreading of multiple plies (layers) of fabric on the cutting table, with all plies aligned identically.

**Spreading Methods:**
| Method | Direction | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat (Face-up)** | Single direction | Solid, non-directional prints |
| **Face-to-Face** | Alternating | Nap fabrics, asymmetric prints |
| **Cut & Fold** | Plies cut progressively | Large pattern pieces |
| **Stepped** | Staggered for pattern matching | Checks, stripes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cutting Room Efficiency (CRE)** is the ratio of usable cut parts to total fabric consumed.
> $$CRE = \left(\frac{\text{Area of usable parts}}{\text{Total area of fabric consumed}}\right) \times 100\%$$
> Industry benchmark: **85–95%** for woven, **88–92%** for knit.

### 2.1.2 Spread & Cut Math
Let:
- $L$ = marker length (cm)
- $W$ = fabric width (cm)
- $N$ = number of plies
- $A_p$ = total area of pattern pieces for one garment
- $E_m$ = marker efficiency (%)

**Total fabric length consumed (m):**
$$F_L = \frac{L}{100 \times E_m} \text{ (per ply)}$$

**Total fabric consumption per garment (m²):**
$$F_C = \frac{L \times W}{10000 \times E_m \times N} \text{ m}^2/\text{garment}$$

**Fabric weight consumed (kg):**
$$F_W = F_C \times GSM / 1000$$

### 2.1.3 Cutting Time Estimation
Cutting time depends on **linear knife travel** and **perimeter-based formulas**.

**Perimeter of all pattern pieces (cm):**
$$P_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \text{ (per garment)}$$

**Knife travel per ply:**
$$T_p = \frac{P_{tot}}{v_c \times 60} \text{ (minutes)}$$

Where $v_c$ = cutting speed (cm/min) — straight knife typically **1000–2000 cm/min**.

**Total cutting time (minutes):**
$$T_{cut} = \frac{T_p \times N + T_{setup}}{N \times G_{per\ ply}}$$

Where $G$ = garments per ply, $T_{setup}$ = spreading + handling time.

---

## 2.2 YARN COUNT CONVERSION — KTU Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Count Systems Recap
| System | Symbol | Definition | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| English Cotton Count | $\text{Ne}$ | 840 yds / lb | Indirect (length-based) |
| Metric Count | $\text{Nm}$ | 1 km / kg | Indirect |
| Tex | $\text{Tex}$ | g / 1000 m | Direct |
| Denier | $\text{D}$ | g / 9000 m | Direct |
| Worsted Count | $\text{Ne}_W$ | 560 yds / lb | Indirect |
| Linen Count | $\text{Ne}_L$ | 300 yds / lb | Indirect |

### 2.2.2 Conversion Formulas
**English ↔ Tex:**
$$\text{Tex} = \frac{590.5}{\text{Ne}}$$

**English ↔ Metric:**
$$\text{Nm} = \text{Ne} \times 1.693$$
$$\text{Ne} = \frac{\text{Nm}}{1.693}$$

**English ↔ Denier:**
$$\text{D} = \frac{5315}{\text{Ne}}$$

**Tex ↔ Denier:**
$$\text{D} = 9 \times \text{Tex}$$

**Two-fold (plied) yarn count:**
$$N_{ply} = \frac{N_{single}}{\sqrt{n}}$$
Where $n$ = number of plies (for balanced ply yarns, twist multiplier considered).

### 2.2.3 Twist Calculations
**Twist Multiplier (TM or α):**
$$TM = \frac{TPC \times \sqrt{\text{Ne}}}{10}$$
or
$$TM = \frac{TPM}{\sqrt{Nm}}$$

Where TPC = turns per cm, TPM = turns per meter.

**Direction of Twist:**
- **S-twist:** Fibers move from upper-left to lower-right (≈ "S" shape).
- **Z-twist:** Fibers move from upper-right to lower-left (≈ "Z" shape).
- Standard sewing thread: **Z-S** (plies Z, final twist S).

---

## 2.3 BLEACHING — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Whiteness Problem
Raw cotton contains natural pigments (mostly **flavonoids**), seed fragments, motes, and residual wax. Bleaching must destroy chromophoric groups without damaging the cellulose polymer.

**Whiteness assessment** uses:
- **CIE Whiteness Index (WI):** $WI = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$
- **Yellowness Index (YI):** $YI = \frac{100(1.28X - 1.06Z)}{Y}$

### 2.3.2 Process Variables
| Variable | H₂O₂ Bleach Range |
|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 85–100 °C |
| **pH** | 10.5–11.5 |
| **Time** | 45–90 min |
| **H₂O₂ Concentration** | 2–5 g/L |
| **Stabilizer** | Sodium silicate (8 g/L) or organic stabilizers |
| **Liquor Ratio (M:L)** | 1:8 to 1:15 |

### 2.3.3 Bleach Bath Recipe (Example for 100 kg cotton fabric)
| Component | Quantity |
|---|---|
| **H₂O₂ (50%)** | 4 L |
| **NaOH (flake)** | 2 kg |
| **Sodium silicate** | 0.8 kg |
| **Wetting agent** | 0.2 kg |
| **Stabilizer (organic)** | 0.3 kg |
| **Water** | to 1:10 M:L |

### 2.3.4 Damage Control — KTU Critical Concept
Excessive bleaching degrades cellulose:
$$\text{Cellulose} \xrightarrow{\text{Excess H}_2\text{O}_2} \text{Oxycellulose} \rightarrow \text{DP} \downarrow$$
DP = Degree of Polymerization — drops from **~2000 (raw)** to below **1500 = over-bleached, weak fabric**.

**Copper number** and **Cuprammonium fluidity** are the test methods for damage.

---

## 2.4 DYEING — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 Exhaustion Dyeing Theory
**Dyeing isotherm (Freundlich / Langmuir type for cellulosic dyes):**
$$\frac{x}{m} = K \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

Where:
- $x$ = mass of dye on fiber (mg/g)
- $m$ = mass of fiber (g)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium dye concentration in bath (mg/L)
- $K, n$ = constants

**Exhaustion percentage:**
$$E\% = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$$

Where $C_0$ = initial dye concentration in bath.

### 2.4.2 Fixation Percentage
$$F\% = \frac{D_{fixed}}{D_{exhausted}} \times 100$$

**Overall Dye Yield:**
$$\eta_{dye} = \frac{D_{fixed}}{D_{applied}} \times 100 = \frac{E\% \times F\%}{100}$$

### 2.4.3 Reactive Dye-Cellulose Reaction
Reactive dyes form a **covalent bond** with the –OH group of cellulose via a nucleophilic substitution:
$$\text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OSO}_3\text{Na} + \text{Cell-OH} \xrightarrow{\text{alkali}} \text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-O-Cell} + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$
The alkali (NaOH, Na₂CO₃) activates cellulose to form **Cell-O⁻**, a strong nucleophile.

### 2.4.4 Liquor Ratio (LR) in Dyeing
$$LR = \frac{\text{Mass of liquor (kg)}}{\text{Mass of fabric (kg)}}$$

**Reactive dye dyeing:** LR = 1:6 to 1:10
**Jet dyeing (polyester):** LR = 1:8 to 1:12
**Padding (continuous):** LR controlled by **pick-up %**
$$\text{Pick-up \%} = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$$

---

## 2.5 PATTERN MAKING — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 Block/Block Pattern (Sloper)
A **basic block (sloper)** is a master pattern with standard seam allowances (typically 0 or minimal), drafted from standard body measurements. All style patterns are derived from a block.

### 2.5.2 Key Drafting Measurements (Women's Bodice Block, cm)
| Measurement | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Bust** | B | 90 |
| **Waist** | W | 72 |
| **Back length** | BL | 42 |
| **Across back** | AB | 35 |
| **Shoulder** | SH | 12 |
| **Armhole** | AH | 22 |
| **Neck** | N | 36 |

### 2.5.3 Drafting Principles
- **Bust line (BL):** = BL from nape
- **Waist line (WL):** = BL + 18–20 cm (waist drop)
- **High Point Shoulder (HPS):** Reference for armhole curve
- **Princess line:** Vertical line from HPS to dart apex to bust point
- **Bust dart:** Angle = 2–5° (depending on design)

---

## 2.6 GRADING — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.6.1 Grade Rule Direction Convention
Grade rules are **vector-based displacements** applied to each pattern point. Convention (ASTM):

| Direction | Movement | Sign Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **Lengthwise (vertical)** | Up = smaller, Down = larger | +ve = larger size |
| **Widthwise (horizontal)** | Outward = larger | +ve = larger size |
| **Diagonal** | For armhole, neck | Vector addition |

### 2.6.2 Nesting Grade Rule Table (Men's Shirt, per size)
| Point | X (cm) | Y (cm) |
|---|---|---|
| **Center front neck** | 0 | +0.5 |
| **Across shoulder** | +1.0 | +0.3 |
| **Chest line edge** | +1.0 | 0 |
| **Waist line edge** | +1.0 | +0.3 |
| **Hem corner** | +1.0 | +0.3 |
| **Sleeve width** | +0.5 | 0 |
| **Sleeve length** | 0 | +0.7 |
| **Cuff width** | +0.5 | 0 |

### 2.6.3 Grade Rule for Body Width (Standard ASTM)
$$\Delta W_{size} = \frac{W_{max} - W_{min}}{N_{sizes} - 1}$$

For ASTM sizes S-M-L-XL-XXL (chest 90-100-110-120-130):
$$\Delta W = \frac{130 - 90}{5 - 1} = 10 \text{ cm (per size)}$$

So each size increases chest by **5 cm** (half of total, since two sides).

---

## 2.7 PACKING — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.7.1 Carton Dimensions
$$V_{carton} = L_c \times W_c \times H_c \text{ (cm}^3\text{)}$$

**Garment volume (flat-folded):**
$$V_{garment} = t \times W_f \times L_f$$
Where $t$ = average fold thickness, $W_f$ = fold width, $L_f$ = fold length.

**Number of garments per carton:**
$$n_{carton} = \frac{V_{carton} \times PE/100}{V_{garment}}$$

### 2.7.2 Compression Packaging (Export)
**Compression ratio:**
$$CR = \frac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$$
$$CR_{typical} = 1.5 - 3.0$$

Vacuum packing achieves $CR = 4$ to $6$, but risks **permanent creasing**.

---

## 2.8 📋 KTU FORMULA SHEET — QUICK REVISION TABLE

| # | Process | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Marker Efficiency** | $E_m = \frac{A_{pieces}}{A_{marker}} \times 100$ | Fabric cost |
| 2 | **Fabric Consumption** | $F_C = \frac{L \times W}{E_m \times N}$ | Garment costing |
| 3 | **Fabric Weight** | $F_W = F_C \times GSM / 1000$ | Material cost |
| 4 | **English ↔ Tex** | $Tex = 590.5/Ne$ | Yarn conversion |
| 5 | **English ↔ Denier** | $D = 5315/Ne$ | Filament conversion |
| 6 | **Twist Multiplier** | $TM = TPC \times \sqrt{Ne}/10$ | Yarn twist |
| 7 | **Exhaustion %** | $E\% = (C_0 - C_e)/C_0 \times 100$ | Dyeing |
| 8 | **Fixation %** | $F\% = D_{fixed}/D_{exh} \times 100$ | Dyeing |
| 9 | **Dye Yield** | $\eta = E\% \times F\% / 100$ | Process efficiency |
| 10 | **Liquor Ratio** | $LR = M_{liquor}/M_{fabric}$ | Wet processing |
| 11 | **Pick-up %** | $PU = (W_{wet}-W_{dry})/W_{dry} \times 100$ | Padding |
| 12 | **Grade Rule** | $\Delta P_i = P_i^{new} - P_i^{base}$ | Grading |
| 13 | **Width Grade** | $\Delta W = (W_{max}-W_{min})/(n-1)$ | Size grading |
| 14 | **Cutting Time** | $T_{cut} = T_p \times N + T_{setup}$ | Production planning |
| 15 | **Packing Efficiency** | $PE = V_{garments}/V_{carton} \times 100$ | Logistics |
| 16 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = V_{orig}/V_{comp}$ | Export packing |
| 17 | **CIE Whiteness** | $WI = Y + 800(x_n-x) + 1700(y_n-y)$ | Bleaching QC |
| 18 | **Yellowness Index** | $YI = 100(1.28X-1.06Z)/Y$ | Bleaching QC |
| 19 | **Production Rate** | $R = \frac{60 \times S}{SMV}$ | Garment/operator |
| 20 | **Standard Minute Value** | $SMV = \text{BSM} + \text{ALW}$ | Time study |

> [!NOTE]
> **BSM** = Basic Stitch Minute (machine time); **ALW** = Allowed Lost Work (15% of BSM typical).

# 📘 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

---

## 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE 1: Fabric Consumption Calculation

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A men's shirt marker has the following data:
> - Marker length $L = 4.5$ m
> - Fabric width $W = 1.5$ m
> - Number of plies in the lay = 60
> - Marker efficiency = 88%
> - Number of garments per marker = 18
> - GSM of fabric = 110 g/m²
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Fabric consumed per garment (m²)
> (b) Total fabric weight for the entire lay (kg)
> (c) Cost of fabric per shirt if fabric price = ₹250/kg.

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Given:**
- $L = 4.5$ m, $W = 1.5$ m, $N = 60$, $E_m = 0.88$, $G = 18$ shirts/marker, $GSM = 110$, $P = ₹250/kg$

**Step 1:** Total fabric area in one marker (per ply):
$$A_{marker} = L \times W = 4.5 \times 1.5 = 6.75 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Fabric area consumed per ply (corrected for marker efficiency):
$$A_{consumed/ply} = \frac{A_{marker}}{E_m} = \frac{6.75}{0.88}$$
$$A_{consumed/ply} = 7.6705 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (per ply)}$$

**Step 3:** Total fabric area for full lay (60 plies):
$$A_{total} = A_{consumed/ply} \times N = 7.6705 \times 60$$
$$A_{total} = 460.227 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 (a):** Fabric area per garment:
$$A_{garment} = \frac{A_{total}}{G \times N} = \frac{460.227}{18 \times 60}$$
$$A_{garment} = \frac{460.227}{1080} = 0.4261 \text{ m}^2/\text{garment}$$

**Step 5 (b):** Total fabric weight:
$$W_{fabric} = A_{total} \times \frac{GSM}{1000} = 460.227 \times \frac{110}{1000}$$
$$W_{fabric} = 50.625 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 (c):** Cost per shirt:
$$C_{shirt} = \frac{W_{fabric} \times P}{G \times N} = \frac{50.625 \times 250}{1080}$$
$$C_{shirt} = \frac{12656.25}{1080} = ₹11.72/\text{shirt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always carry **4 decimal places** for intermediate steps in marker efficiency problems — examiners often give partial credit for correct methodology even with arithmetic errors.

---

## 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 2: Yarn Count Conversion + Twist

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A yarn has the following properties:
> - Linear density = 30 Tex
> - Twist = 800 TPM
> - Twist direction = Z
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Equivalent English Cotton Count (Ne)
> (b) Equivalent Metric Count (Nm)
> (c) Equivalent Denier (D)
> (d) Twist Multiplier (TM) using the Ne system

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 (a):** Ne from Tex:
$$Ne = \frac{590.5}{Tex} = \frac{590.5}{30}$$
$$Ne = 19.683$$

**Step 2 (b):** Nm from Ne:
$$Nm = Ne \times 1.693 = 19.683 \times 1.693$$
$$Nm = 33.32 \text{ (unitless)}$$

**Step 3 (c):** Denier from Tex:
$$D = 9 \times Tex = 9 \times 30 = 270 \text{ D}$$

**Step 4 (d):** TPC from TPM:
$$TPC = \frac{TPM}{100} = \frac{800}{100} = 8 \text{ turns/cm}$$

**Step 5:** Twist Multiplier (using Ne system):
$$TM = \frac{TPC \times \sqrt{Ne}}{10} = \frac{8 \times \sqrt{19.683}}{10}$$
$$\sqrt{19.683} = 4.436$$
$$TM = \frac{8 \times 4.436}{10} = \frac{35.49}{10}$$
$$TM = 3.55 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry benchmarks for TM:**
> - Cotton warp yarn: TM = 3.5–4.5
> - Cotton weft yarn: TM = 3.0–3.5
> - Hosiery yarn: TM = 2.5–3.0
> - Crepe yarn: TM = 5.0–6.0
>
> Our TM = 3.55 lies in the **cotton warp range** — yarn is suitable as warp.

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 3: Dyeing Exhaustion & Fixation

### 📋 Problem Statement
> In a reactive dyeing bath for 50 kg cotton fabric:
> - Dye applied = 2% on weight of fabric (owf) = 1.0 kg
> - After dyeing, dye in residual bath = 0.15 kg
> - After soaping wash, unfixed dye removed = 0.42 kg
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Exhaustion percentage (E%)
> (b) Fixation percentage (F%)
> (c) Total dye yield (η)

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Given:** $D_{applied} = 1.0$ kg, $D_{exhausted} = 1.0 - 0.15 = 0.85$ kg, $D_{fixed} = 0.85 - 0.42 = 0.43$ kg

**Step 1 (a):** Exhaustion:
$$E\% = \frac{D_{exhausted}}{D_{applied}} \times 100 = \frac{0.85}{1.0} \times 100$$
$$E\% = 85\%$$

**Step 2 (b):** Fixation:
$$F\% = \frac{D_{fixed}}{D_{exhausted}} \times 100 = \frac{0.43}{0.85} \times 100$$
$$F\% = 50.59\%$$

**Step 3 (c):** Total dye yield:
$$\eta = \frac{E\% \times F\%}{100} = \frac{85 \times 50.59}{100}$$
$$\eta = 43.0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student error is to compute $F\%$ using $D_{applied}$ instead of $D_{exhausted}$. Always remember: **Fixation is a fraction of what is exhausted**, not of what is applied. The dye in the residual bath has never had a chance to fix.

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 4: Pattern Grading — Men's Shirt

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A base pattern (Size 40, chest 100 cm) for a men's shirt has the following key points (X, Y) in cm:
> - **A** (center front neck): (0, 0)
> - **B** (shoulder tip): (12, 3)
> - **C** (armhole at chest line): (25, 18)
> - **D** (side seam at waist): (25, 42)
>
> Grade rules per size (per ASTM):
> - Width grade: +2.5 cm at chest/waist, +0.5 cm at neck
> - Length grade: +1.5 cm overall
>
> **Calculate the coordinates for Size 42 (next size up).**

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Width grade per size = **+2.5 cm**, Length grade = **+1.5 cm** (for half-chest increment of +2.5 means +1.25 per size... let's use the given +2.5/size per problem).

**Step 2:** Point A' (Size 42):
$$X_{A'} = 0 + 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{A'} = 0 + 1.5 = 1.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$A' = (0.5, 1.5)$$

**Step 3:** Point B' (Size 42):
$$X_{B'} = 12 + 0.5 = 12.5 \text{ cm (neck rule) + 2.5 (chest rule)}$$
Wait — use proper rule: shoulder tip follows combined rule.

Let me use a **linear interpolation rule** for clarity:
- Shoulder tip X = neck rule + half-shoulder grade
- $X_{B'} = 12 + (0.5 + 2.0) = 14.5$ cm
- $Y_{B'} = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5$ cm
$$B' = (14.5, 4.5)$$

**Step 4:** Point C' (Size 42, armhole at chest line):
$$X_{C'} = 25 + 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{C'} = 18 + 1.5 = 19.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$C' = (27.5, 19.5)$$

**Step 5:** Point D' (Size 42, side seam at waist):
$$X_{D'} = 25 + 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{D'} = 42 + 1.5 = 43.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$D' = (27.5, 43.5)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Grading Rule Check:** The shape must remain proportional. If the shoulder slope (B-A) changes angle between sizes, the design is **distorted** — this is a common grading error that examiners deduct marks for.

---

## 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 5: Packing Carton Optimization

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A folded shirt has dimensions: $L_f = 30$ cm, $W_f = 25$ cm, fold thickness $t = 2$ cm.
> Carton internal dimensions: $50 \times 40 \times 30$ cm.
> Packing efficiency target = 80%.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) Maximum shirts that can be packed (theoretical, full volume).
> (b) Actual shirts per carton at PE = 80%.

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Garment volume:
$$V_{g} = 30 \times 25 \times 2 = 1500 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2:** Carton volume:
$$V_c = 50 \times 40 \times 30 = 60000 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 (a):** Theoretical maximum shirts:
$$n_{max} = \frac{V_c}{V_g} = \frac{60000}{1500} = 40 \text{ shirts}$$

**Step 4 (b):** Actual at PE = 80%:
$$n_{actual} = n_{max} \times PE = 40 \times 0.80 = 32 \text{ shirts}$$

**Verification:** Effective volume used = $32 \times 1500 = 48000$ cm³
$$PE = \frac{48000}{60000} \times 100 = 80\% \checkmark$$

---

## 3.6 WORKED EXAMPLE 6: Whiteness Index Computation

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A bleached cotton fabric has the following CIE tristimulus values:
> - $X = 82.4$, $Y = 85.6$, $Z = 95.1$
> - Standard illuminant CIE-D65 chromaticity: $x_n = 0.3127$, $y_n = 0.3290$
>
> **Compute the CIE Whiteness Index.**

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Convert tristimulus to chromaticity:
$$x = \frac{X}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{82.4}{82.4+85.6+95.1} = \frac{82.4}{263.1} = 0.3132$$
$$y = \frac{Y}{X+Y+Z} = \frac{85.6}{263.1} = 0.3254$$

**Step 2:** Apply CIE Whiteness formula:
$$WI = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$$
$$WI = 85.6 + 800(0.3127 - 0.3132) + 1700(0.3290 - 0.3254)$$
$$WI = 85.6 + 800(-0.0005) + 1700(0.0036)$$
$$WI = 85.6 - 0.40 + 6.12$$
$$WI = 91.32$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A WI of **91.32** is **excellent** (commercial standard ≥ 65). The fabric would be classified as a **premium white**.

---

## 3.7 WORKED EXAMPLE 7: Production Rate from SMV

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A sewing line has:
> - Standard Minute Value (SMV) = 18 minutes
> - Working hours per day = 8
> - Line efficiency = 75%
> - Number of operators = 30
>
> **Find:** Daily production in pieces.

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Effective minutes per operator per day:
$$M_{eff} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2:** Production per operator per day:
$$R_{operator} = \frac{M_{eff} \times \eta}{SMV} = \frac{480 \times 0.75}{18} = \frac{360}{18} = 20 \text{ pcs}$$

**Step 3:** Total line production:
$$P_{line} = R_{operator} \times n_{op} = 20 \times 30 = 600 \text{ pcs/day}$$

---

## 3.8 WORKED EXAMPLE 8: Compression Packaging Analysis

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A shipment of 1000 garments has original carton volume $V_o = 60$ m³. After compression packing, the volume is $V_c = 20$ m³.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) Compression Ratio (CR)
> (b) Saving in shipping cost if shipping is ₹500/m³.

### 🔢 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 (a):** Compression Ratio:
$$CR = \frac{V_o}{V_c} = \frac{60}{20} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 (b):** Volume saved:
$$\Delta V = V_o - V_c = 60 - 20 = 40 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Cost saved:
$$S = 40 \times 500 = ₹20,000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off note:** Higher CR saves shipping cost but increases risk of **permanent creases**, especially in **wool** and **rayon** garments. Maximum safe CR for these fibers = 2.0–2.5.

# 📘 SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Complete Manufacturing Process Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

