# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between S-twist and Z-twist in yarn. Which twist direction is preferred for the final yarn in a 2-ply sewing thread, and why?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):**
- **S-twist:** The fibers/held yarn strands are twisted in a direction resembling the central part of the letter **"S"** — i.e., the spirals go from upper-left to lower-right.
- **Z-twist:** The fibers/yarn strands are twisted in a direction resembling the central part of the letter **"Z"** — i.e., the spirals go from upper-right to lower-left.

**Detection (1 mark):** Hold the yarn vertically. If the diagonal fibers slope to the right (\\), it is Z-twist; if they slope to the left (//), it is S-twist.

**Application (1 mark):** For 2-ply sewing thread, the **standard convention is Z-S twist** (plies twisted in Z-direction, then final assembly twist in S-direction). This balances the residual torque, producing a **balanced, kink-resistant** thread that lies flat during sewing.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three bleaching agents used in textile wet processing. State the chemical formula and suitable pH range for one of them.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 marks):

**Three Bleaching Agents (2 marks):**
1. **Hydrogen Peroxide** ($\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$) — universal, preferred
2. **Sodium Hypochlorite** ($\text{NaOCl}$) — cotton, domestic
3. **Sodium Chlorite** ($\text{NaClO}_2$) — synthetic fibers

**Detailed for Hydrogen Peroxide (1 mark):**
- **Formula:** $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$
- **Suitable pH:** **10.5 – 11.5 (alkaline)**
- **Temperature:** 85 – 100 °C
- **Stabilizer:** Sodium silicate (8 g/L) or organic stabilizer

---

## 📝 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Two alternative question choices as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Answer ANY ONE complete set.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Cutting, Pattern Making, Grading, Packing Focus

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified Pattern]**

#### **(a) Explain the various types of fabric cutting methods used in the garment industry. Compare straight knife cutting and laser cutting on the basis of speed, fabric suitability, edge quality, and cost.** **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer (7 marks):

**1. Types of Fabric Cutting Methods (4 marks):**

(i) **Straight Knife Cutting:**
- Most common, vertical reciprocating blade (stroke 10–13 cm)
- Operated manually on multi-ply lay (up to 150 mm height)
- Blade moves up-down 1500–3000 cycles/min

(ii) **Round Knife Cutting:**
- Circular rotating blade, table-fed
- Best for small parts, sharp curves, notches

(iii) **Band Knife Cutting:**
- Continuous loop blade, stationary table
- Excellent for intricate shapes, sample making

(iv) **Die Cutting:**
- Hardened steel rule die struck with hammer press
- High volume, identical pieces, single-ply mostly

(v) **Notch and Drill Machine:**
- Pneumatic, marks balance points and drill holes
- Used alongside main cutter

(vi) **Laser Cutting:**
- Computer-controlled CO₂ or fiber laser
- No contact, sealed synthetic edges, no fraying

(vii) **Water-Jet Cutting:**
- High-pressure water (4000 bar) + abrasive (garnet)
- Multi-layer, no heat, no chemical change

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Straight Knife | Laser Cutting |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | 1000–2000 cm/min blade travel | 500–5000 cm/min (depends on thickness) |
| **Fabric Suitability** | All fabrics (woven, knit, non-woven) | Synthetics, leather, technical textiles; **not PVC** (chlorine release) |
| **Edge Quality** | Open edge (may fray); needs overlock | **Sealed edge**, no fraying |
| **Setup Cost** | ₹ 30,000 – 1,00,000 | ₹ 20,00,000 – 1,00,00,000 |
| **Operating Cost** | Low (electricity + blades) | Moderate (laser gas, power) |
| **Operator Skill** | High (manual) | Low (CNC, automated) |
| **Accuracy** | ±2 mm | ±0.1 mm |
| **Best For** | Bulk production, multi-ply | Samples, intricate designs, synthetics |

---

#### **(b) With a neat sketch, describe the process of pattern grading. A men's shirt base pattern (Size 40, chest 100 cm) is to be graded up to Size 44 using the following grade rules: width +2.5 cm/size, length +1.5 cm/size. Compute the new coordinates for the following points of the base pattern: shoulder tip (12, 3), armhole at chest line (25, 18), and side seam at waist (25, 42). Take a factor of half for shoulder tip width movement.** **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer (7 marks):

**1. Pattern Grading Process (3 marks):**

Pattern grading is the systematic enlargement or reduction of a base pattern to produce patterns of other sizes while preserving original proportions, design lines, and fit.

**Steps:**
1. Start with a **base pattern** (e.g., Size 40 / chest 100 cm).
2. Identify **all key pattern points** with (X, Y) coordinates.
3. Establish a **grade rule table** — X and Y increment for each size.
4. **Apply the grade rule** to each point:
   - $X_{new} = X_{base} + \Delta X$
   - $Y_{new} = Y_{base} + \Delta Y$
5. **Re-plot the new pattern** by connecting shifted points.
6. **Verify** proportions, dart placement, design lines.
7. **Add seam allowance** and grain line, then cut pattern.

**2. Computation (4 marks):**

Given grade rules (per size):
- **Width grade** = +2.5 cm
- **Length grade** = +1.5 cm
- **Shoulder tip width movement** = half of width grade = 2.5/2 = +1.25 cm

**Step 1:** Shoulder tip (12, 3) → Size 44 (+1 size)
$$X_{new} = 12 + 1.25 = 13.25 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{new} = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$B' = (13.25, 4.5)$$

**Step 2:** Armhole at chest line (25, 18) → Size 44
$$X_{new} = 25 + 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{new} = 18 + 1.5 = 19.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$C' = (27.5, 19.5)$$

**Step 3:** Side seam at waist (25, 42) → Size 44
$$X_{new} = 25 + 2.5 = 27.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$Y_{new} = 42 + 1.5 = 43.5 \text{ cm}$$
$$D' = (27.5, 43.5)$$

**Verification of shape consistency:**
$$\text{Shoulder slope (Size 40)} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25$$
$$\text{Shoulder slope (Size 44)} = \frac{1.5}{13.25} = 0.113$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Shape Distortion:** Notice the shoulder slope changes from 0.25 to 0.113 — this means the **shoulder line has flattened**, which is a **grading distortion**! The examiner will deduct 1 mark if the student doesn't validate the design proportions.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Yarn, Bleaching, Dyeing, Wet Processing Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

#### **(a) With the help of a flow diagram, explain the short-staple (cotton) yarn production process in detail. Also, write the conversion formula between English count (Ne), Tex, and Denier.** **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer (7 marks):

**1. Short-Staple Yarn Production Flow (5 marks):**

| Stage | Machine | Output | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ginning** | Seed-free cotton lint | Separates fiber from seed |
| 2 | **Blowroom** | Lap | Mixing, opening, cleaning (heavy + light impurities) |
| 3 | **Carding** | Sliver (~5 ktex) | Fiber individualization, removing neps |
| 4 | **Draw Frame** (2 passes) | Sliver (~3–4 ktex) | Parallelization, blending, evenness |
| 5 | **Roving Frame** | Roving (~0.5–1.5 ktex) | Attenuation + slight protective twist |
| 6 | **Ring Spinning** | Yarn | Final draft + real twist, winding on bobbin |
| 7 | **Auto-cone Winding** | Cone/Cheese package | Clearing, knotting, large package |
| 8 | **(Optional) Doubling** | Plied yarn | Multi-ply for strength/evenness |

**Detailed Process:**

**Blowroom:** Bale plucker → mixing machine → coarse cleaner → fine cleaner → lap former. Removes **60–70% of trash**.

**Carding:** Licker-in → cylinder (wire-covered) → flat strip → doffer. Removes **neps, short fibers, microdust**. Sliver linear density **~4.0–5.0 ktex**.

**Draw Frame:** 4–8 rollers with apron drafting. **2 passages** ensure evenness and blend homogeneity. Sliver becomes parallel.

**Roving Frame:** Drafts the sliver to **roving** (0.4–1.2 ktex) and inserts a **protective false twist** for handling strength.

**Ring Spinning:** Drafts roving to final yarn, inserts real twist, winds on **bobbin** via traveler-and-ring system. Output: **15–30 m/min**.

**Winding:** Knots, clears faults, builds large transport-friendly cones.

**2. Count Conversion Formulas (2 marks):**

$$Tex = \frac{590.5}{Ne}$$
$$Denier = \frac{5315}{Ne}$$
$$Denier = 9 \times Tex$$

**Example:** If a yarn is **30 Tex**, then:
- $Ne = 590.5/30 = 19.68$
- $Denier = 9 \times 30 = 270$

---

#### **(b) Describe the mechanism of reactive dyeing of cotton. Explain exhaustion, fixation, and total dye yield with formulas. A dye bath contains 2% owf reactive dye on 50 kg cotton. After dyeing, the residual bath contains 0.15 kg dye, and after soaping wash, 0.42 kg unfixed dye is removed. Compute E%, F%, and total dye yield.** **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer (7 marks):

**1. Reactive Dyeing Mechanism on Cotton (3 marks):**

Reactive dyes contain a **reactive group** (e.g., vinyl sulphone, chlorotriazine, dichloroquinoxaline) that forms a **covalent bond** with the hydroxyl (–OH) group of cellulose under alkaline conditions.

**Step-by-step chemistry:**

(i) **Dye Dissolution (in water):**
$$\text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OSO}_3\text{Na} \rightarrow \text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-OH} + \text{NaHSO}_4$$

(ii) **Cellulose activation by alkali:**
$$\text{Cell-OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-O}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Na}^+$$

(iii) **Covalent bond formation (nucleophilic substitution):**
$$\text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH=CH}_2 + \text{Cell-O}^- \rightarrow \text{Dye-SO}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-O-Cell}$$

This bond is **permanent** — washing cannot break it, giving reactive dyes **excellent wash fastness**.

**2. Definitions and Formulas (2 marks):**

**Exhaustion (E%):** Percentage of applied dye that transfers from bath to fiber.
$$E\% = \frac{D_{exhausted}}{D_{applied}} \times 100 = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$$

**Fixation (F%):** Percentage of exhausted dye that is actually chemically fixed.
$$F\% = \frac{D_{fixed}}{D_{exhausted}} \times 100$$

**Total Dye Yield (η):**
$$\eta = \frac{E\% \times F\%}{100}$$

**3. Numerical Solution (2 marks):**

Given: $D_{applied} = 1.0$ kg (2% owf × 50 kg), $D_{residual} = 0.15$ kg, $D_{unfixed} = 0.42$ kg

**Step 1:** Exhausted dye:
$$D_{exhausted} = 1.0 - 0.15 = 0.85 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2:** Fixed dye:
$$D_{fixed} = 0.85 - 0.42 = 0.43 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 (a):** E%:
$$E\% = \frac{0.85}{1.0} \times 100 = 85\%$$

**Step 4 (b):** F%:
$$F\% = \frac{0.43}{0.85} \times 100 = 50.59\%$$

**Step 5 (c):** Total dye yield:
$$\eta = \frac{85 \times 50.59}{100} = 43.0\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract residual AND unfixed** — students often write $F\% = 0.43/1.0 = 43\%$ (wrong — should be 50.59%).
> 2. **Mixing up the denominator** — $F\%$ denominator is **exhausted** dye, not applied dye.
> 3. **Unit confusion** — kg must be consistent. Mixing mg/g with g/kg loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** in the final answer — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Confusing the role of salt** — NaCl/Na₂SO₄ promotes **exhaustion** (neutralizes –COO⁻ on cellulose), while alkali promotes **fixation**. Mixing these roles costs 1 mark.

---

## 🔥 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🧵 Yarn Production
- ✅ Short-staple (cotton) system: Bale → Blowroom → Card → Draw × 2 → Roving → Ring Spin → Winding
- ✅ Ring spinning: highest quality, baseline productivity
- ✅ Rotor/Open-end: 5–7× faster, lower quality
- ✅ Air-jet: 10–15× faster, used for fine synthetic blends

### 📏 Yarn Count Systems
- ✅ Ne = 840 yd/lb (indirect, cotton)
- ✅ Nm = km/kg (indirect, metric)
- ✅ Tex = g/1000 m (direct, universal)
- ✅ Denier = g/9000 m (filament)
- ✅ **Tex = 590.5/Ne**, **Denier = 9 × Tex**

### 🌀 Twist
- ✅ S-twist: fibers go \\  (right slope)
- ✅ Z-twist: fibers go // (left slope)
- ✅ Twist Multiplier: $TM = TPC \times \sqrt{Ne}/10$
- ✅ Standard sewing thread: Z-S construction

### 🧪 Bleaching
- ✅ H₂O₂ is preferred (universal, eco-friendly)
- ✅ pH 10.5–11.5, 85–100 °C
- ✅ Over-bleaching → oxycellulose → DP drops → weak fabric
- ✅ CIE Whiteness ≥ 65 = commercial white

### 🎨 Dyeing
- ✅ Reactive dyes = **covalent bond** with cellulose (best wash fastness)
- ✅ E% measures transfer; F% measures fixation
- ✅ Total dye yield η = E% × F% / 100
- ✅ Salt promotes exhaustion; alkali promotes fixation
- ✅ Disperse dyes for polyester; Acid dyes for wool/nylon

### 📐 Pattern Making
- ✅ Block (sloper) is the master pattern
- ✅ Flat pattern = modify block via slash & spread
- ✅ Grain line parallel to selvage
- ✅ Standard SA = 6–15 mm

### 📊 Grading
- ✅ Grade rule = (X, Y) increment per size
- ✅ Width grade follows body proportion; length grade is constant
- ✅ Shape must remain proportional (slope check)

### ✂️ Cutting
- ✅ Marker efficiency target: 85–95% woven, 88–92% knit
- ✅ Straight knife: most common, manual
- ✅ Laser: synthetic, sealed edge, no fraying
- ✅ Water-jet: multi-layer, no heat

### 📦 Packing
- ✅ Packing efficiency = V_garments / V_carton × 100
- ✅ Industry standard PE = 70–85%
- ✅ Compression saves shipping but risks creasing
- ✅ CR > 2.5 unsafe for wool/rayon

### 🔗 Process Linkage
- ✅ **Fiber → Yarn → Fabric → Bleach → Dye → Finish → Cut → Sew → Pack → Ship**
- ✅ Each step's output is the next step's input
- ✅ Quality at each stage compounds — a fault in bleaching is amplified after dyeing

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 EXAM STRATEGY (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Always draw a labeled diagram** for cutting, pattern making, and wet processing — earns 1–2 extra marks.
> 2. **Show all intermediate calculations** in numerical problems — partial marking demands visible work.
> 3. **Memorize the conversion formulas** (Tex, Ne, Denier) — they appear in 70% of yarn-related questions.
> 4. **Always state units** in the final answer of any numerical.
> 5. **Use industry terminology** (e.g., "owf" = on weight of fabric; "LR" = liquor ratio; "SMV" = standard minute value) — examiners reward domain language.

> [!NOTE]
> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — CUTTING, SPINNING, YARN PRODUCTION, DYEING, PATTERN MAKING, BLEACHING, GRADING, PACKING, WET PROCESSING**
> *Total marks in KTU ESE for this module: Typically 28 marks (Q5 or Q6 of Part B, two sub-parts of 7+7).*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal Industries - Ferrous Metals and Alloys - Raw Materials for Iron Making

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Ferrous Metals and Alloys

**Ferrous metals** are metals and alloys that contain **iron (Fe)** as their principal constituent. The term *ferrous* is derived from the Latin word *ferrum*, meaning iron. These materials form the backbone of modern industrial civilization, accounting for over **90% of global metal production by tonnage**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Ferrous metals are metallic materials in which iron is the principal element, typically combined with carbon and other alloying elements to produce a wide spectrum of engineering materials ranging from wrought iron (≈0.02% C) to high-alloy tool steels (≈2% C + alloying additions).

### 1.1.1 Classification of Ferrous Metals

The ferrous family is broadly classified into:

| Category | Carbon Content (wt%) | Key Properties | Typical Applications |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Pure Iron** | < 0.008% | Soft, ductile, magnetic | Electrical laminations, magnetic cores |
| **Wrought Iron** | 0.02 – 0.05% | Tough, malleable, fibrous | Chains, ornamental work, hooks |
| **Low Carbon Steel** | 0.05 – 0.25% | Ductile, weldable | Sheets, wires, structural sections |
| **Medium Carbon Steel** | 0.25 – 0.60% | Balanced strength & ductility | Rails, gears, crankshafts |
| **High Carbon Steel** | 0.60 – 1.0% | Hard, wear-resistant | Springs, cutting tools, chisels |
| **Cast Iron** | 2.0 – 4.5% | Hard, brittle, excellent castability | Pipes, machine beds, engine blocks |
| **Stainless Steel** | < 0.1% + Cr ≥ 10.5% | Corrosion-resistant, hygienic | Cutlery, chemical plants, medical |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Iron Family Tree"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of iron-making raw materials as the **ingredients of a baker's recipe for a massive industrial cake**. The *iron ore* is the flour (the main bulk), *coke* is the oven-fuel that bakes the cake by providing heat and chemical reduction, *limestone* is the baking soda that cleans impurities by forming a removable scum (slag), and *air* (oxygen enrichment) is the matchstick that ignites the whole process. Without any one of these in the right proportion, the "cake" (molten iron) will not be properly "baked" (reduced and refined).

## 1.3 Raw Materials for Iron Making — The Burden Concept

In iron-making metallurgy, the term **"burden"** refers to the total solid charge fed into the blast furnace from the top (via the skip car or conveyor). The burden consists of:

1. **Ferrous Burden** — Iron-bearing materials (iron ore, sinter, pellets)
2. **Flux Burden** — Limestone, dolomite (for slag formation)
3. **Fuel Burden** — Metallurgical coke (provides heat + reducing gas + structural support)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly differentiate between *iron ore* (naturally occurring mineral), *sinter* (agglomerated fine ore), and *pellets* (balled fine ore) — these are the three principal forms of the **ferrous burden** charged to the modern blast furnace.

## 1.4 Why These Specific Raw Materials?

The choice of raw materials for iron making is governed by three governing principles:
- **Economical extraction of iron** from its oxide form (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄).
- **Generation of high temperatures** (> 1500 °C) needed to melt the reduced iron and slag.
- **Removal of gangue (impurities)** like silica (SiO₂), alumina (Al₂O₃), and phosphorus (P₂O₅) via slag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** A modern integrated steel plant producing **1 tonne of hot metal** (pig iron) consumes approximately:
> - **1.6 – 1.8 tonnes** of iron ore (sinter + pellets + lump ore)
> - **0.45 – 0.55 tonnes** of metallurgical coke
> - **0.20 – 0.30 tonnes** of limestone/dolomite flux
> - **2.0 – 2.5 tonnes** of process air (≈ 4.5 tonnes with oxygen enrichment)
> - **15 – 25 m³** of water for cooling and gas cleaning

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 IRON ORE — The Primary Ferrous Burden

Iron ore is a naturally occurring rock or mineral from which metallic iron can be economically extracted. The iron in these ores exists predominantly as **oxides**, **carbonates**, **silicates**, or **sulphides**.

### 2.1.1 Mineralogical Classification of Iron Ores

| Ore Type | Chemical Formula | Theoretical Fe Content | Color | Industrial Significance |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|-------|------------------------|
| **Hematite** | Fe₂O₃ | 70.0% | Red / Reddish-brown | Most important; > 80% of global production |
| **Magnetite** | Fe₃O₄ | 72.4% | Black | High grade; magnetic separation possible |
| **Limonite** | 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O | 59.8% | Yellowish-brown | Hydrated; lower grade |
| **Siderite** | FeCO₃ | 48.3% | Grey / Brown | Carbonate ore; calcined before use |
| **Pyrite** | FeS₂ | 46.5% | Yellow metallic | Sulphide; NOT used directly (sulphur problem) |
| **Taconite** | Fe₃O₄ in cherty matrix | 25 – 35% | Dark | Low-grade; requires beneficiation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Reality Check:** Although magnetite has the *highest theoretical* Fe content (72.4%), **hematite** is the most widely used ore because of its easier reducibility (Fe₂O₃ reduces more readily than Fe₃O₄) and lower processing cost.

### 2.1.2 Key Quality Parameters of Iron Ore

- **Fe Content**: Acceptable commercial grade ≥ **60% Fe**.
- **SiO₂ + Al₂O₃** (acidic gangue): should be < **5%** combined.
- **Phosphorus (P)**: should be < **0.05%** for basic steelmaking.
- **Sulphur (S)**: should be < **0.05%** to avoid hot shortness.
- **Moisture**: < **2%** for efficient sintering and charging.
- **Porosity**: High porosity improves reducibility (gas permeability).
- **Size**: Lump ore: **10 – 30 mm**; Sinter: **5 – 50 mm**; Pellets: **9 – 16 mm**.

## 2.2 METALLURGICAL COKE — The Fuel and Reducing Agent

### 2.2.1 Definition and Production

**Coke** is a hard, porous, black carbonaceous residue obtained by the destructive distillation (pyrolysis) of selected **coking coals** in the absence of air at temperatures of **1000 – 1100 °C** in **coke ovens**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Metallurgical coke is a macro-porous, high-carbon (≈ 90% fixed carbon) solid residue produced by the carbonization of coking coal, specially designed to withstand the load of the blast furnace burden while providing heat, reducing gases, and permeability to the ascending gas stream.

### 2.2.2 Essential Properties of Metallurgical Coke

| Property | Required Specification | Engineering Significance |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Fixed Carbon** | > 85% (preferably 88–92%) | Higher C = more heat & reducing gas |
| **Ash Content** | < 12% (preferably < 10%) | Low ash = less slag, more Fe yield |
| **Sulphur (S)** | < 0.6% | Excess S causes hot shortness in steel |
| **Moisture** | < 3% | Affects charge calculations & heating |
| **Porosity** | 45 – 55% | Allows gas permeability |
| **Cold Strength (Micum Index M40)** | > 80% | Resists breakage during charging |
| **Hot Strength (CSR)** | > 60% | Resists degradation in furnace |
| **Abrasion Index (M10)** | < 7% | Minimizes fines generation |
| **Size** | 25 – 70 mm (lump) | Permeability & combustion efficiency |

### 2.2.3 The Threefold Role of Coke in the Blast Furnace

1. **As a Fuel** — Combustion at the tuyeres generates temperatures up to **2000 °C**:
   $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

2. **As a Reducing Agent** — The CO gas produced reduces iron oxides:
   $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$$

3. **As a Structural Support** — The coke column remains solid in the cohesive zone (where ore melts), providing a permeable skeleton for the ascending gas.

## 2.3 FLUX (LIMESTONE & DOLOMITE) — Slag Former

### 2.3.1 Limestone (CaCO₃)

**Limestone** is a sedimentary rock composed primarily of **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)**. It serves as a **flux** in iron making, combining with acidic gangue (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) to form a fusible **slag** that floats on top of the molten iron.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Functions of Limestone in the Blast Furnace:**
> 1. Removes **silica (SiO₂)** and **alumina (Al₂O₃)** from the charge.
> 2. Removes **sulphur** from the metal (desulphurization).
> 3. Forms a fluid slag that is easily tapped from the furnace.

### 2.3.2 Dolomite (CaMg(CO₃)₂)

Dolomite is a double carbonate of calcium and magnesium. When calcined, it yields **CaO + MgO**. **MgO** improves the **fluidity and stability** of the slag, especially in low-silica slags. MgO also raises the slag melting point, which is beneficial in the lower part of the furnace.

### 2.3.3 Calcination Reaction (Pre-calculated for clarity)

$$\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow \quad (900-1000 \text{ °C})$$

$$\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{MgO} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

## 2.4 ANCILLARY RAW MATERIALS

### 2.4.1 Manganese Ore
- **Source**: Pyrolusite (MnO₂), Rhodochrosite (MnCO₃), Braunite (Mn₂O₃).
- **Function**: Improves steel strength, deoxidizes steel, neutralizes harmful FeS by forming MnS.
- **Typical addition**: 5 – 25 kg per tonne of hot metal.

### 2.4.2 Silica (SiO₂) and Alumina (Al₂O₃)
- Often present as gangue in iron ore.
- Must be fluxed with CaO to form slag.
- The ratio **(CaO + MgO) / (SiO₂ + Al₂O₃)** defines the **Basicity Index (BI)**.

### 2.4.3 Sinter and Pellets
Because lump ore of desired size is becoming scarce, fine ores (< 6 mm) are agglomerated:
- **Sintering**: Fine ore + coke breeze + limestone is heated to 1300 °C on a sinter strand to form **sinter** (porous, lumpy mass).
- **Pelletizing**: Fine ore + bentonite (binder) is rolled into 9 – 16 mm balls, then indurated at 1200 °C in a shaft or grate-kiln.

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Equation | Significance / Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$ | Primary reduction reaction (indirect reduction) |
| 2 | $\text{FeO} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$ | Direct reduction (in lower stack) |
| 3 | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Tuyere combustion of coke |
| 4 | $\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | Boudouard reaction (endothermic) |
| 5 | $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$ | Calcination of limestone |
| 6 | $\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3$ (slag) | Acidic fluxing |
| 7 | $\text{CaO} + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO}\cdot\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | Alumina neutralization |
| 8 | $\text{2CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{2CaO}\cdot\text{SiO}_2\cdot\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | Complex slag (C₂S·Alite) |
| 9 | $\text{FeO} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$ | Direct reduction in tuyere zone |
| 10 | $\text{CaO} + \text{FeS} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaS} + \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$ | Desulphurization in hearth |
| 11 | $\text{Micum Index} = \frac{\text{Weight of coke retained on 40 mm screen}}{\text{Total weight}} \times 100$ | Coke strength test |
| 12 | $\text{CSR} = \frac{\text{Weight of coke remaining after 600 rev in I-tumbler}}{\text{Original weight}} \times 100$ | Coke hot strength |
| 13 | $\text{Basicity Index (BI)} = \frac{\% \text{CaO} + \% \text{MgO}}{\% \text{SiO}_2 + \% \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}$ | Slag basicity |
| 14 | $\text{Coke Rate (kg/t HM)} = \frac{\text{Mass of dry coke charged}}{\text{Mass of hot metal produced}}$ | Furnace efficiency metric |
| 15 | $\text{Slag Volume (kg/t HM)} = \frac{\text{Mass of flux + gangue + ash}}{\text{Steel produced}}$ | Process output metric |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Analysis of Iron Ore Reduction

The reduction of iron oxides in the blast furnace is a **stepwise process**, moving from higher oxides to metallic iron. This is critical to KTU questions on reaction mechanisms.

### 3.1.1 Stage 1: Hematite (Fe₂O₃) → Magnetite (Fe₃O₄)

**Operating Temperature:** < 570 °C (top of the stack)
**Reducing Agent:** CO (indirect reduction, exothermic)

$$\text{3 Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{2 Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

**Logical Steps in the Mechanism:**
1. CO gas diffuses from the gas phase toward the ore particle surface.
2. CO adsorbs on the oxide surface.
3. Surface oxygen ion (O²⁻) reacts with adsorbed CO, forming CO₂.
4. CO₂ desorbs and diffuses back to the gas stream.
5. Electrons released by the oxidation of CO reduce Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺, converting Fe₂O₃ to Fe₃O₄.

### 3.1.2 Stage 2: Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) → Wüstite (FeO)

**Operating Temperature:** < 570 °C (top of the stack)
**Reducing Agent:** CO (indirect reduction, exothermic)

$$\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{3 FeO} + \text{CO}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept:** Below **570 °C**, the stable form is Fe (not FeO). However, in the practical blast furnace, FeO always appears as a transient phase because temperatures above 570 °C are encountered very early in the descending burden. The three-stage reduction is a textbook idealization; the *two-stage* reduction (Fe₂O₃ → Fe₃O₄ → Fe) is closer to reality above 570 °C.

### 3.1.3 Stage 3: Wüstite (FeO) → Iron (Fe)

**Operating Temperature:** 570 – 1000 °C (middle and lower stack)
**Reducing Agent:** CO (above 800 °C) and C (below 800 °C, direct reduction)

**Path A — Indirect reduction (CO):**
$$\text{FeO} + \text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}_2$$

**Path B — Direct reduction (C):**
$$\text{FeO} + \text{C} \longrightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from indirect to direct reduction is a key KTU concept. Direct reduction is **endothermic** (consumes heat), while indirect reduction is **exothermic** (releases heat). The balance of these two governs the thermal efficiency of the blast furnace.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Slag Formation Analysis

### 3.2.1 The Three Slag Formation Steps

**Step 1 — Calcination of limestone in the stack:**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$
The CaO generated descends with the burden to hotter zones.

**Step 2 — Initial fluxing of acidic gangue at 1000 – 1200 °C:**
$$\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3 \quad (\text{wollastonite, T}_m = 1540 \text{ °C})$$

**Step 3 — Complex slag formation (final composition):**
$$\text{2CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 + \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{2CaO}\cdot\text{SiO}_2\cdot\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$$
This complex silicate-aluminate has a lower melting point (≈ 1400 °C) than pure CaSiO₃, allowing the slag to flow freely.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Coke Rate Calculation (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A blast furnace produces **1500 tonnes of hot metal per day**. The coke charged is **750 tonnes per day** (dry basis). The moisture in the coke is **3%**. Calculate the **coke rate** in kg per tonne of hot metal.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**
$$\text{Coke Rate} = \frac{\text{Dry Coke Charged (kg)}}{\text{Hot Metal Produced (tonnes)}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert moisture to dry mass:**

Given: Total charged coke = 750 t/day at 3% moisture.
$$\text{Mass of water} = 750 \times 0.03 = 22.5 \text{ t/day}$$
$$\text{Dry coke} = 750 - 22.5 = 727.5 \text{ t/day}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula (unit conversion: 1 t = 1000 kg):**
$$\text{Dry coke in kg} = 727.5 \times 1000 = 727500 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**
$$\text{Coke Rate} = \frac{727500 \text{ kg/day}}{1500 \text{ t/day}} = 485 \text{ kg/t HM}$$

> **Result:** Coke rate = **485 kg per tonne of hot metal**.
> **Acceptable Range for KTU:** Modern BF operates at 450 – 550 kg/t HM. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Basicity Index Calculation

> **Problem:** A blast furnace slag has the following composition: **CaO = 42%**, **MgO = 8%**, **SiO₂ = 34%**, **Al₂O₃ = 12%**. Calculate the **Basicity Index (BI)** and classify the slag.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**
$$\text{BI} = \frac{\% \text{CaO} + \% \text{MgO}}{\% \text{SiO}_2 + \% \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$\text{BI} = \frac{42 + 8}{34 + 12} = \frac{50}{46}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the result:**
$$\text{BI} = 1.087$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**
- BI < 1.0 → **Acidic slag** (insufficient flux, more SiO₂ than CaO)
- BI = 1.0 → **Neutral slag**
- BI > 1.0 → **Basic slag** (excess CaO, capable of desulphurization)
- 1.0 < BI < 1.5 → **Slightly basic** (typical BF range)
- BI > 1.5 → **Highly basic** (LD/BOF steelmaking slags)

> **Conclusion:** The slag is **slightly basic** (BI = 1.09), which is **ideal for blast furnace iron making**.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Theoretical Iron Yield from Hematite

> **Problem:** Calculate the **percentage yield of metallic iron** from pure **hematite (Fe₂O₃)** if reduction is **95% complete**.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Molar masses:**
- Fe₂O₃ = 2(55.85) + 3(16) = **159.7 g/mol**
- Fe (atomic mass) = **55.85 g/mol**

**Step 2 — Theoretical yield from 1 mole Fe₂O₃:**
$$1 \text{ mole Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2 \text{ moles Fe}$$
$$159.7 \text{ g Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2 \times 55.85 = 111.7 \text{ g Fe}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage theoretical Fe content:**
$$\% \text{Fe} = \frac{111.7}{159.7} \times 100 = 69.94\%$$

**Step 4 — Apply the 95% reduction efficiency:**
$$\text{Actual Fe yield} = 0.95 \times 69.94\% = 66.45\%$$

> **Result:** From 1 tonne of pure hematite, we obtain **664.5 kg of metallic iron** at 95% reduction efficiency.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Raw Material Properties

| Property | Hematite | Magnetite | Siderite | Limonite | Coke | Limestone | Dolomite |
|----------|----------|-----------|----------|----------|------|-----------|----------|
| **Formula** | Fe₂O₃ | Fe₃O₄ | FeCO₃ | 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O | C (90%) | CaCO₃ | CaMg(CO₃)₂ |
| **% Fe (theory)** | 70.0 | 72.4 | 48.3 | 59.8 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **% Fe (practical)** | 60 – 68 | 65 – 70 | 35 – 45 | 45 – 55 | — | — | — |
| **Reducibility** | Excellent | Good | Fair | Fair | — | — | — |
| **Magnetic** | No | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 5.0 – 5.3 | 5.0 – 5.2 | 3.7 – 3.9 | 3.5 – 4.0 | 0.8 – 1.1 | 2.6 – 2.8 | 2.8 – 3.0 |
| **Hardness (Mohs)** | 5.5 – 6.5 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 3.5 – 4.5 | 4.0 – 5.5 | 2.0 – 2.5 | 3.0 | 3.5 – 4.0 |
| **Porosity** | Low | Very Low | Medium | High | Very High | Low | Low |
| **Role in BF** | Burden | Burden | Burden | Burden | Fuel + Reductant | Flux | Flux |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **hematite** and **magnetite** iron ore with respect to their chemical formula, theoretical iron content, and industrial use.

### **Model Answer:**

| Property | Hematite | Magnetite |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Chemical Formula** | Fe₂O₃ | Fe₃O₄ |
| **Theoretical Fe Content** | 70.0% | 72.4% |
| **Color** | Red / Reddish-brown | Black |
| **Magnetic Property** | Non-magnetic | Strongly magnetic |
| **Industrial Use** | Most widely used (>80% global production); easier to reduce | Beneficiated via magnetic separation; used after pelletization |
| **Reducibility** | Excellent (high porosity in natural form) | Lower (dense structure, often pelletized) |

> *(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark for formula, 1 for Fe content, 1 for use)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **Q2.** State any **three essential functions** of metallurgical coke in the blast furnace.

### **Model Answer:**

1. **Fuel** — Combustion at the tuyeres generates the **high temperatures (1800 – 2000 °C)** required to melt the iron and slag.

2. **Reducing Agent** — The CO gas produced from coke combustion reduces iron oxides to metallic iron via the reactions Fe₂O₃ + CO → Fe + CO₂.

3. **Structural Support** — Coke remains solid even in the highest-temperature zones of the furnace, providing a **permeable skeleton** for gas flow and supporting the overlying burden of ore and flux.

> *(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark per function)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students commonly lose marks by: (1) writing only the chemical equation without the **temperature zone** and the **reducing agent**, (2) failing to state whether the reaction is **endothermic/exothermic**, and (3) not drawing a labeled **flow diagram** (mandatory for full marks in process questions).

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q.** (a) List the **principal raw materials** required for iron making in a blast furnace. Explain the role of each raw material with suitable chemical reactions. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Describe the **stepwise reduction of iron oxides** in the blast furnace with the relevant chemical equations, indicating the temperature zones. Explain **direct and indirect reduction** mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The principal raw materials required for iron making in a blast furnace are:

**1. Iron Ore (Ferrous Burden):**
Hematite (Fe₂O₃) is the most commonly used ore. The key reduction reaction is:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$$
The ore provides the iron atoms that are extracted as metallic iron.

**2. Metallurgical Coke (Fuel + Reducing Agent + Structural Support):**
Coke serves three roles:
- Combustion: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$ (ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol)
- Gasification: $\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ (ΔH = +172.5 kJ/mol)
- The CO produced reduces the ore, and the coke column supports the burden.

**3. Limestone (Flux):**
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$
The CaO formed combines with SiO₂ and Al₂O₃ gangue to form slag:
$$\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3$$

**4. Dolomite (Secondary Flux):**
$$\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{MgO} + 2\text{CO}_2$$
MgO improves slag fluidity and stability.

**5. Hot Blast (Air + Oxygen):**
Supplies the O₂ for coke combustion and is preheated to 1000 – 1200 °C in Cowper stoves to reduce coke consumption.

**6. Ancillary: Manganese Ore, Silica, etc.**
Used in small quantities for specific metallurgical purposes.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

The reduction of iron oxides occurs in **three discrete stages** as the ore descends from the top of the stack to the hearth:

| Stage | Reaction | Temperature Zone | Type | Enthalpy |
|-------|----------|------------------|------|----------|
| 1 | $\text{3 Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{2 Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{CO}_2$ | Top of stack (< 400 °C) | Indirect | Exothermic |
| 2 | $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{3 FeO} + \text{CO}_2$ | Upper stack (400 – 900 °C) | Indirect | Exothermic |
| 3 | $\text{FeO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}_2$ | Lower stack (900 – 1100 °C) | Indirect | Exothermic |
| 3' | $\text{FeO} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$ | Lower stack (1100 – 1300 °C) | Direct | Endothermic |

**Direct vs. Indirect Reduction:**

- **Indirect Reduction:** Reduction by **CO gas** in the upper and middle stack (900 – 1000 °C). It is **exothermic**, supplies heat, and produces CO₂.
- **Direct Reduction:** Reduction by **solid carbon** in the lower stack and tuyere zone (> 1000 °C). It is **endothermic**, consumes heat, and produces CO. Direct reduction is unavoidable at high temperatures because the Boudouard reaction equilibrium shifts toward CO at high T.

> The **thermal reserve zone** (1100 – 1200 °C) is where the transition from indirect to direct reduction occurs and is a critical engineering design parameter.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

> **Q.** (a) With the help of a neat flowchart, describe the **manufacture of metallurgical coke** from coking coal. List the **essential quality parameters** that a metallurgical coke must satisfy for blast furnace use. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Define **slag** and **basicity index**. A blast furnace slag has the composition: **CaO = 40%**, **MgO = 10%**, **SiO₂ = 35%**, **Al₂O₃ = 15%**. Calculate the basicity index and classify the slag. Describe the **functions of slag** in iron making. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Manufacture of Metallurgical Coke (Flowchart):**

```
Coking Coal Selection
   ↓
Crushing and Sizing (< 3 mm fine coal)
   ↓
Blending (10–15% of coking coal with non-coking coal)
   ↓
Charging into Coke Ovens (slot-type, 18–22 hours)
   ↓
Carbonization at 1000–1100 °C (anaerobic conditions)
   ↓
Quenching (Wet or Dry Cooling)
   ↓
Screening and Sizing
   ↓
Metallurgical Coke (25–70 mm) → Blast Furnace
Coke Breeze (< 10 mm) → Sinter Plant
```

**By-products:** Coke oven gas (COG), coal tar, ammonium sulphate, light oil.

**Quality Parameters of Metallurgical Coke:**

| Parameter | Required Value | Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| Fixed Carbon | > 88% | Provides heat and reducing gas |
| Ash | < 10% | Reduces slag volume |
| Sulphur | < 0.6% | Prevents hot shortness in steel |
| Moisture | < 3% | Charge accuracy |
| Micum Index (M40) | > 80% | Cold strength |
| Micum Index (M10) | < 7% | Abrasion resistance |
| CSR | > 60% | Hot strength in cohesive zone |
| Size | 25 – 70 mm | Permeability and combustion |

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Definition of Slag:**
Slag is a **molten oxide mixture** (predominantly CaO, SiO₂, Al₂O₃, MgO) that floats on top of the hot metal in the blast furnace hearth. It is formed by the chemical combination of flux (CaO, MgO) with gangue (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) and coke ash.

**Definition of Basicity Index (BI):**
$$\text{BI} = \frac{\% \text{CaO} + \% \text{MgO}}{\% \text{SiO}_2 + \% \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}$$

**Calculation:**

Given: CaO = 40%, MgO = 10%, SiO₂ = 35%, Al₂O₃ = 15%.

**Step 1:** Numerator:
$$\%\text{CaO} + \%\text{MgO} = 40 + 10 = 50\%$$

**Step 2:** Denominator:
$$\%\text{SiO}_2 + \%\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 = 35 + 15 = 50\%$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:
$$\text{BI} = \frac{50}{50} = 1.00$$

**Step 4:** Classification:
- BI = 1.0 indicates a **neutral slag** (equally acidic and basic oxides).
- For optimal blast furnace operation, BI should be **slightly basic (1.05 – 1.25)**.
- This slag is **just below the optimal range** and may require minor flux adjustment.

**Functions of Slag in Iron Making:**

1. **Removes gangue** (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) from the metal.
2. **Removes sulphur** via desulphurization: $\text{CaO} + \text{FeS} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{CaS} + \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$.
3. **Protects hot metal** from reoxidation by forming a physical barrier against the hot blast.
4. **Carries heat** to the tuyeres (slag acts as a thermal buffer).
5. **Acts as a sink** for impurities like P₂O₅ (in basic slags).
6. **Saves refractory life** in the hearth by controlling chemistry.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Yield KTU Points):**

- ☐ **Ferrous metals** are those with iron as the principal element; classification is based primarily on **carbon content** (0.02% wrought iron → 4.5% cast iron).

- ☐ **Hematite (Fe₂O₃)** is the most important iron ore in industry; magnetite (Fe₃O₄) has the highest theoretical Fe content (72.4%) but lower reducibility.

- ☐ **Limestone (CaCO₃)** is calcined to **CaO** in the stack; CaO is the actual flux that combines with **SiO₂** to form **CaSiO₃ slag**.

- ☐ **Coke** serves **three roles**: fuel, reducing agent, and structural support — its high CSR (>60%) and low ash (<10%) are critical.

- ☐ **Reduction occurs in three stages**: Fe₂O₃ → Fe₃O₄ → FeO → Fe, governed by the **Boudouard reaction** in the lower stack.

- ☐ **Direct reduction** (FeO + C) is endothermic; **indirect reduction** (FeO + CO) is exothermic; balance is a thermal efficiency marker.

- ☐ **Basicity Index (BI)** = (CaO + MgO) / (SiO₂ + Al₂O₃); BI = 1.0 is neutral; BF slag should be **1.05 – 1.25** (slightly basic).

- ☐ **Coke rate** = mass of dry coke per tonne of hot metal; modern BF target is **450 – 500 kg/t HM**.

- ☐ **Sinter** and **pellets** are agglomerated forms of fine ore; they improve permeability and reduce flue dust generation.

- ☐ **Dolomite** supplies **MgO** which improves slag fluidity and stabilizes the slag composition in low-silica slags.

- ☐ **Hot blast** (preheated air at 1000 – 1200 °C) reduces coke consumption and is generated by recovering waste heat from top gas in **Cowper stoves**.

- ☐ **Standard memory values**: Coke combustion ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol; Boudouard ΔH = +172.5 kJ/mol; Hematite → Fe theoretical Fe = 70.0%.

- ☐ **For 14-mark answers**, always include: (i) chemical equation, (ii) temperature zone, (iii) endo/exothermic nature, (iv) labeled block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blast Furnace and Electric Furnace Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blast Furnace and Electric Furnace Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Blast Furnace — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Blast Furnace (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Blast Furnace** is a tall, shaft-type, counter-current, continuous metallurgical reactor used for the **smelting of iron ore (Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄)** into **pig iron / hot metal**. It operates by blowing a high-velocity, pre-heated stream of air (**hot blast**, **1100 °C – 1350 °C**) through tuyeres into a descending column of alternating layers of iron ore, coke, and limestone (the **burden**). The coke acts simultaneously as a **fuel**, a **reducing agent**, and a **permeable structural support**, while the limestone acts as a **flux** to form **slag** with gangue impurities.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Filter in Reverse"

Imagine a giant vertical tube where **solid balls (ore + coke + limestone)** are dropped in from the top, and **hot air** is blasted in from the bottom. As the solids descend slowly (taking 6–8 hours), they meet hotter and hotter rising gases. Think of it like a multi-storey building in which:

* Each floor represents a **temperature zone** (cooler at top, ~200 °C → hotter at bottom, ~2000 °C).
* Tenants (ore) lose their **oxygen** (get reduced) floor by floor.
* The **cleaner (slag)** carries unwanted dirt (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) down and out.
* Pure **iron (hot metal)** is tapped at the bottom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Blast Furnace still dominant?**
> It is the **most thermodynamically and economically efficient** bulk route to produce primary iron at scales of **10,000 – 13,000 tonnes/day** from a single unit. Global crude steel production (~1.9 billion tonnes/year) is still **~70 % blast-furnace–basic-oxygen-furnace (BF–BOF)** route dependent.

### 1.2 Electric Furnace — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electric Furnace (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Electric Furnace** is a metallurgical vessel in which the **primary source of heat** for melting, refining, or heat-treatment of metals is **electrical energy** rather than combustion of fossil fuels. The three principal classes are:
>
> 1. **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** — heat generated by striking an arc between graphite electrodes and the metallic charge.
> 2. **Induction Furnace** — heat generated by induced eddy currents in the charge (coreless or channel type).
> 3. **Resistance Furnace** — heat generated by I²R dissipation in resistance elements (used mainly for heat treatment, not bulk steelmaking).

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Induction Cooktop on Steroids"

Picture your **domestic induction stove** at home — it uses a high-frequency magnetic field to make a metal pan hot without any flame. An industrial **coreless induction furnace** is the same idea, scaled up enormously for melting a few tonnes of steel or copper. An **Electric Arc Furnace**, on the other hand, behaves more like a controlled **lightning bolt** — a controlled electric discharge between carbon rods and the metal scrap creates temperatures exceeding **3000 °C**, hotter than the surface of the sun.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Electric Furnaces?**
> * Allow **steelmaking from 100 % scrap** (recycling route → circular economy).
> * No coke oven or sintering plant needed → **lower capital cost** (~⅓ of a BF–BOF plant).
> * **Lower direct CO₂ emissions per tonne** when powered by renewable electricity.
> * Share of EAF in global steelmaking is growing rapidly (~30 %, rising each year).

---

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot

| Furnace Family | Heat Source | Typical Charge | Product | Operating T (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blast Furnace (BF)** | Combustion of coke + hot blast air | Iron ore + coke + limestone | Hot metal / Pig iron | 1500 – 2000 |
| **Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF)** | Pure O₂ blowing | Hot metal + scrap | Liquid steel | 1600 – 1700 |
| **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** | Electric arc (graphite electrodes) | DRI / scrap | Liquid steel | 1600 – 1700 |
| **Induction Furnace** | Eddy currents | Scrap / returns | Cast iron, steel, Cu, Al | 1200 – 1600 |
| **Cupola Furnace** | Coke combustion | Pig iron + coke + limestone | Cast iron | 1400 – 1500 |

---

### 1.4 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> * **Hot blast temperature** at tuyere: **1000 – 1350 °C** (modern stoves)
> * **Top gas temperature** (exit): **150 – 250 °C**
> * **Blast volume**: ~**1000 – 1500 Nm³ per tonne of hot metal**
> * **Coke rate**: ~**300 – 500 kg per tonne of hot metal** (modern best practice)
> * **EAF power rating**: ~**60 – 100 MVA** (ultra-high-power, UHP)
> * **EAF electrode diameter**: ~**600 – 750 mm** for 100 t furnaces
> * **EAF tap-to-tap time**: ~**35 – 45 minutes**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Blast Furnace — Constructional Anatomy

The blast furnace is a **vertical, refractory-lined steel shell** with a circular cross-section. Modern units are **25 – 35 m tall**, **10 – 15 m diameter at the bosh**, and produce up to **13,000 t/day**.

**Key Zones (top → bottom):**

1. **Stock Line / Throat** — top charging level (double bell or bell-less top).
2. **Stack** — upper converging zone where **indirect reduction** by CO dominates (Fe₂O₃ → FeO).
3. **Bosh** — lower diverging zone, hottest region (2000 °C). **Direct reduction** by C: FeO + C → Fe + CO.
4. **Hearth (Well)** — collects molten **hot metal (density ~7.0 t/m³)** below and **slag (density ~2.5 t/m³)** above (slag floats).
5. **Tuyere Zone** — 16–36 tuyeres admit hot blast through copper coolers.
6. **Tap Hole & Slag Hole** — separate notches for iron and slag tapping.

### 2.2 Burden Materials and their Roles

| Material | Chemical Role | Typical Grade |
|---|---|---|
| **Iron Ore** (Hematite Fe₂O₃, Magnetite Fe₃O₄) | Source of iron | Fe ≥ 60 % |
| **Coke** (C ≈ 88 – 92 %) | Fuel + Reductant + Permeable bed | CSR ≥ 60 |
| **Limestone** (CaCO₃) / **Dolomite** (CaMg(CO₃)₂) | Flux to form slag | CaO ≥ 50 % |
| **Sinter / Pellets** | Agglomerated ore (improves permeability) | Fe ≈ 62 – 67 % |

### 2.3 The Three Master Chemical Reaction Sequences

#### Sequence A — Combustion Zone (at tuyeres)

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{(exothermic, releases heat)}$$

#### Sequence B — Solution-Loss / Boudouard Reaction

$$CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> This is **endothermic** and absorbs heat — it is the **rate-limiting step** for blast furnace productivity.

#### Sequence C — Stepwise Reduction of Iron Oxides (Indirect Reduction)

$$3\,Fe_2O_3 + CO \rightarrow 2\,Fe_3O_4 + CO_2 \quad (T < 570°C)$$

$$Fe_3O_4 + CO \rightarrow 3\,FeO + CO_2 \quad (570 < T < 1000°C)$$

$$FeO + CO \rightarrow Fe + CO_2 \quad (T > 1000°C)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Above 1000 °C**, the **direct reduction** dominates:
> $$FeO + C \rightarrow Fe + CO \quad \Delta H = +151.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
> Direct reduction consumes expensive coke. Good blast furnace practice minimises it.

#### Sequence D — Slag Formation

$$CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2 \quad (calcination, \sim 900°C)$$

$$CaO + SiO_2 \rightarrow CaSiO_3 \quad (\text{slag})$$

$$CaO + Al_2O_3 + 2\,SiO_2 \rightarrow CaAl_2Si_2O_8$$

### 2.4 Ellingham Diagram — The Theoretical Backbone

> [!NOTE]
> The **Ellingham diagram** plots **ΔG°** (Gibbs free energy of formation of oxides) versus **temperature**. Lines that lie **lower** on the chart indicate oxides that are **more stable** (harder to reduce). The **position of the C–CO and C–CO₂ lines** below most metal oxide lines explains why carbon is a universal reducing agent above **~700 °C**.

* At the **intersection** of the **C–CO line** with the metal oxide line lies the **reduction temperature**.
* For FeO, this intersection is around **700 – 750 °C**, confirming coke's ability to reduce iron oxide at normal BF temperatures.
* For Al₂O₃, the intersection is far higher (>2000 °C) — explaining why **aluminium** is made by **electrolysis**, not carbothermic reduction.

### 2.5 Blast Furnace Process Control Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot Blast Temperature** | 1100 – 1350 °C | Higher T → lower coke rate |
| **Blast Pressure** | 2 – 4 bar | Drives gas through burden |
| **O₂ Enrichment** | 21 – 30 % O₂ | Increases flame temperature |
| **Top Pressure** | 1.5 – 2.5 bar | Improves gas–solid contact |
| **Burden Distribution** | Centre vs Wall | Controls gas flow pattern |
| **Productivity** | 2 – 3 t/m³/day | Specific productivity index |

### 2.6 Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) — Constructional Details

* **Shell**: Cylindrical, water-cooled panels, lined with **MgO–C bricks** (basic lining to resist basic slag).
* **Roof**: Delta-shaped or eccentric, swings aside for charging.
* **Electrodes**: 3 (3-phase AC) or 1 (DC), **graphite or graphite-with-phenolic-impregnated tips**, diameters up to **750 mm**.
* **Bottom**: Porous plugs for **argon stirring**, **EBT (Eccentric Bottom Tapping)**.
* **Oxy-fuel burners + lances**: For scrap preheating and post-combustion of CO.

### 2.7 EAF Operating Phases (One Heat Cycle)

1. **Charging** — scrap bucket dropped from roof (~3 buckets per heat).
2. **Melting** — electrodes bore down; arc melts charge (long arc mode to protect roof).
3. **Refining** — flat bath; oxygen and carbon injected to decarburise.
4. **Slag-off** — foamy slag practice stabilises arc, improves heat transfer.
5. **Tapping** — through EBT into ladle; alloy additions + deoxidation.

### 2.8 EAF Electrical & Energy Balance

| Energy Input | Share (%) |
|---|---|
| Electrical (transformer → arc) | **55 – 65** |
| Chemical (oxidation of C, Si, Fe) | **20 – 25** |
| Oxy-fuel burners | **5 – 10** |
| Hot heel (carryover from previous heat) | **5 – 10** |

**Typical specific energy consumption:** ~**350 – 450 kWh/tonne** of liquid steel.

### 2.9 Induction Furnace — Theory

An alternating current in a **water-cooled copper coil** (solenoid) generates a **time-varying magnetic flux**, which induces **eddy currents** in the metal charge. These eddy currents dissipate energy as heat by Joule effect (I²R).

**Skin depth (δ)** quantifies penetration of electromagnetic field into charge:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\,\rho}{\mu\,\omega}} = 503\,\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r\,f}}$$

where **ρ** is resistivity (Ω·m), **μ** is permeability (H/m), **ω = 2πf** is angular frequency, **f** is supply frequency (Hz).

* **Coreless type** — main foundry furnace; frequencies 150 Hz – 10 kHz; capacities up to 100 t.
* **Channel type** — lower frequency (50 Hz); higher efficiency (~95 %); used for holding/duplicating melts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of induction furnace:** It **cannot refine** — it only melts. Slag-metal reactions are limited because no arc means no superheat zone.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Application / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Fe_2O_3 + 3\,CO \rightarrow 2\,Fe + 3\,CO_2$ | Overall reduction stoichiometry | — |
| 2 | $CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2\,CO$ | Boudouard / Solution-loss reaction | — |
| 3 | $\text{Coke Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of coke per heat}}{\text{Mass of hot metal produced}}$ | Blast furnace productivity metric | kg/t |
| 4 | $\text{Specific Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Daily hot metal output}}{\text{Hearth volume}}$ | BF productivity index | t/m³/day |
| 5 | $\delta = 503 \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$ | Induction furnace skin depth | m |
| 6 | $P_{\text{arc}} = V_{\text{arc}} \cdot I_{\text{arc}}$ | Power dissipated in EAF arc | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{Q_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | Furnace thermal efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{Balance Index} = \dfrac{\text{O}_{2, \text{in}}}{\text{O}_{2, \text{out}}}$ | Mass balance check | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Slag Basicity} = \dfrac{\text{CaO}\%}{\text{SiO}_2\%}$ | Slag chemistry control | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat to raise charge temperature | kJ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse **direct reduction ratio** $\left(\dfrac{Fe_{\text{from C}}}{Fe_{\text{total}}}\right)$ with **indirect reduction ratio**. Examiners commonly ask: *"Why is indirect reduction preferred?"* — Answer: Indirect reduction by CO is **exothermic**, so it **saves coke** and **increases productivity**.

---

### 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Steel Industry**: BF + BOF still produces ~70 % of global crude steel; EAF route rising fast.
* **Foundry Practice**: Induction furnaces dominate iron and aluminium foundries for clean, controllable melting.
* **Ferro-alloy Production**: Submerged-arc electric furnaces (SAF) produce FeCr, FeMn, FeSi, SiMn.
* **Speciality Metals**: Vacuum arc remelting (VAR) and electroslag remelting (ESR) used for aerospace-grade Ti, Ni super-alloys.
* **Recycling Economy**: EAF + induction furnaces are the cornerstone of **urban mining** of steel, copper, aluminium scrap.
* **Sustainability Metrics**: Scope-1 CO₂ for BF–BOF ≈ **2.1 t CO₂/t steel** vs. EAF (with renewable power) ≈ **0.4 – 0.7 t CO₂/t steel**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Coke Requirement for Iron Reduction

**Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):**
A blast furnace is charged with **1000 kg of Fe₂O₃ (hematite)** per batch. Determine the:
(a) Theoretical minimum carbon (coke) required for complete reduction to Fe.
(Assume coke is **90 % C** by mass; only **direct reduction** is considered for simplicity.)

---

#### Step 1 — Write the Overall Reduction Reaction

The complete reduction of hematite by carbon proceeds in two conceptual steps:

**Step (i) — Carbothermic reduction of Fe₂O₃:**

$$Fe_2O_3 + 3\,C \rightarrow 2\,Fe + 3\,CO \quad \text{(Direct, endothermic)}$$

**Step (ii) — CO formed then acts as secondary reductant:**

$$Fe_2O_3 + 3\,CO \rightarrow 2\,Fe + 3\,CO_2$$

> Note that **3 mol of C** generate **3 mol of CO**, which can then reduce another **1 mol of Fe₂O₃**. So overall **6 mol of C** reduce **2 mol of Fe₂O₃**.

#### Step 2 — Molar Masses (Recall)

| Species | Molar Mass (kg/kmol) |
|---|---|
| Fe₂O₃ | 2(55.85) + 3(16.00) = **159.70** |
| Fe | **55.85** |
| C | **12.01** |
| CO | 28.01 |
| CO₂ | 44.01 |

#### Step 3 — Compute Moles of Fe₂O₃ in the Charge

$$n_{Fe_2O_3} = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{159.70 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 6.262 \text{ kmol}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Stoichiometry of Reaction (i)

From $Fe_2O_3 + 3C \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO$, the mole ratio is:

$$\frac{n_C}{n_{Fe_2O_3}} = 3$$

Therefore:

$$n_C = 3 \times 6.262 = 18.785 \text{ kmol}$$

#### Step 5 — Convert Moles of C to Mass of C

$$m_C = n_C \times M_C = 18.785 \times 12.01 = 225.61 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 6 — Convert Mass of C to Mass of Coke (90 % C)

$$m_{\text{coke}} = \frac{m_C}{0.90} = \frac{225.61}{0.90} = 250.68 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 7 — Final Answer

> **Theoretical minimum coke required ≈ 251 kg per 1000 kg of Fe₂O₃.**

Equivalent to:

$$\text{Coke rate} = \frac{251}{0.6994 \times 1000} = \frac{251}{699.4} = 0.359 \text{ kg coke / kg Fe}$$

Here, **699.4 kg Fe** is the iron content of 1000 kg Fe₂O₃ (= $6.262 \times 2 \times 55.85$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Modern BF practice consumes ~**300 – 500 kg coke per tonne hot metal**. Our theoretical value (359 kg) ignores heat losses, slag formation heat, tuyere combustion — so a small margin above the theoretical minimum is excellent practice.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: EAF Specific Energy Consumption

**Problem:** An EAF produces **80 tonnes** of liquid steel in a heat that consumes:
* **28,000 kWh** electrical energy
* **1200 Nm³** natural gas (HHV = 35.5 MJ/Nm³)
* **600 kg** of injected carbon (oxidation releases 11,000 kJ/kg C)

Determine the **specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne)**.

#### Step 1 — Total Electrical Energy (per tonne)

$$E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{28{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{80 \text{ t}} = 350 \text{ kWh/t}$$

#### Step 2 — Chemical Energy from C Oxidation (per tonne)

$$Q_C = 600 \times 11{,}000 = 6.6 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^6}{3600} = 1833.3 \text{ kWh}$$

$$Q_{C,\text{per t}} = \frac{1833.3}{80} = 22.92 \text{ kWh/t}$$

#### Step 3 — Chemical Energy from Natural Gas (per tonne)

$$Q_{\text{gas}} = 1200 \times 35.5 = 42{,}600 \text{ MJ} = 42{,}600 \times \frac{1}{3.6} = 11{,}833 \text{ kWh}$$

$$Q_{\text{gas, per t}} = \frac{11{,}833}{80} = 147.9 \text{ kWh/t}$$

#### Step 4 — Total Specific Energy (electrical equivalent)

$$E_{\text{specific}} = 350 + 22.92 + 147.9 = 520.8 \text{ kWh/t}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Answer

> **Specific energy consumption ≈ 521 kWh/t of liquid steel.**

This is realistic for a **conventional EAF**. Modern best-in-class EAFs reach **~350 – 380 kWh/t** with hot heel, oxygen injection, and foamy slag.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Induction Furnace Skin Depth

**Problem:** A 1-tonne **medium-frequency coreless induction furnace** operates at **1000 Hz** to melt cast iron. Resistivity of cast iron ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ Ω·m**, relative permeability ≈ **200**. Find the **skin depth (δ)**.

#### Step 1 — Recall Skin Depth Formula

$$\delta = 503 \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r f}}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$\delta = 503 \sqrt{\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 1000}} = 503 \sqrt{\frac{10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{5}}}$$

$$\delta = 503 \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-12}} = 503 \times 2.236 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 1.13 \text{ mm}}$$

#### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the charge is much thicker than **δ**, the **eddy currents concentrate near the surface**, generating heat that is conducted inward. This is the **"skin effect"** — the basis of induction heating.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Decision Table

| Criterion | Blast Furnace | EAF | Induction Furnace |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy form** | Chemical (coke) | Electrical (arc) | Electrical (induction) |
| **Charge form** | Ore + coke + flux | Scrap / DRI | Clean scrap only |
| **Refining capability** | None (produces hot metal) | Decarburise, desulphurise | **None** — melt only |
| **Operating temp (°C)** | 1500 – 2000 | 1600 – 1700 | 1200 – 1600 |
| **Capital cost (relative)** | Very High | Medium | Low |
| **Throughput** | Very High (continuous) | Medium (batch ~40 min) | Low – Medium |
| **CO₂ (t/t steel)** | 2.0 – 2.5 | 0.4 – 0.7 (renewable) | ~0.4 – 0.7 |
| **Best application** | Bulk iron from ore | Steel from scrap | Foundry melting |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — Burden Calculation

**Problem:** A blast furnace produces **5000 tonnes/day** of hot metal. Iron content in hot metal = **94 %**. The burden contains **62 % Fe** (ore), **88 % C** (coke). Coke rate is **450 kg/t HM**. Calculate:
(a) Daily ore requirement.
(b) Daily coke requirement.
(c) Daily limestone if flux requirement is **0.45 t CaO per t HM**.

---

#### (a) Daily Iron Output

$$m_{Fe} = 5000 \times 0.94 = 4700 \text{ t/day}$$

#### (b) Ore Required (assuming 100 % Fe recovery)

$$m_{\text{ore}} = \frac{4700}{0.62} = 7580.6 \text{ t/day}$$

#### (c) Coke Required

$$m_{\text{coke}} = 5000 \times 0.450 = 2250 \text{ t/day}$$

#### (d) Pure Carbon in Coke

$$m_C = 2250 \times 0.88 = 1980 \text{ t/day}$$

#### (e) Limestone Required

$$m_{\text{limestone}} = 5000 \times 0.45 \times \frac{M_{CaCO_3}}{M_{CaO}} = 5000 \times 0.45 \times \frac{100.09}{56.08}$$

$$m_{\text{limestone}} = 5000 \times 0.45 \times 1.7848 = 4015.8 \text{ t/day}$$

---

### 3.6 Critical Reaction Energy Snapshot

| Reaction | ΔH (kJ/mol) | Type | Effect on Coke Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Fe_2O_3 + 3CO \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO_2$ | −24.8 | Exothermic | Saves coke (Indirect reduction) |
| $FeO + C \rightarrow Fe + CO$ | +151.5 | Endothermic | **Consumes extra coke** |
| $CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO$ (Boudouard) | +172.4 | Endothermic | Slowest, consumes coke |
| $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | −393.5 | Exothermic | Provides heat |
| $CaCO_3 \rightarrow CaO + CO_2$ | +178.0 | Endothermic | Consumes heat in stack |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Blast Furnace — Vertical Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Top Charging System<br>Bell Less Top or Double Bell] --> A2[Stock Line Throat<br>Solid Burden Charged Here]
    A2 --> A3[Stack Zone<br>Indirect Reduction by CO<br>T 200 to 1000 C]
    A3 --> A4[Bosh Zone<br>Direct Reduction by Carbon<br>T 1000 to 2000 C]
    A4 --> A5[Tuyere Zone<br>Hot Blast Injection<br>Combustion of Coke]
    A5 --> A6[Hearth Well<br>Molten Iron Collection]
    A6 --> A7[Tap Hole for Hot Metal Discharge]
    A6 --> A8[Slag Hole for Slag Tapping]
    A5 --> A9[Hot Blast Main<br>Preheated Air 1100 to 1350 C]
    A3 --> A10[Top Gas Downcomer<br>Recovered BF Gas]
    A10 --> A11[Gas Cleaning and Dust Collection]
    A11 --> A12[Stove or Power Plant Reuse]
```

**Description of Diagram Logic:**
* Vertical flow represents the **downward journey of solids** (ore, coke, limestone).
* Tuyere zone receives the **hot blast** laterally.
* Top gas is recovered for **heat regeneration in Cowper stoves** and for **combustion in steel plant**.

---

### 4.2 Blast Furnace Burden Flow and Gas Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        IO[Iron Ore Sinter Pellets]
        CK[Coke]
        LS[ Limestone Flux ]
        HB[Preheated Hot Blast]
    end
    subgraph FURNACE
        F1[Top of Furnace Charging]
        F2[Reduction and Smelting Zone]
        F3[Combustion at Tuyere]
        F4[Hearth Collection]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        OM[Molten Hot Metal to BOF]
        OS[Slag to Cement or Landfill]
        OG[Top Gas BF Gas]
    end
    IO --> F1
    CK --> F1
    LS --> F1
    HB --> F3
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> OM
    F4 --> OS
    F1 --> OG
    F2 --> OG
```

---

### 4.3 Electric Arc Furnace Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Scrap Bucket Charge 1] --> B2[Roof Lifted and Scrap Dropped]
    B2 --> B3[Electrode Bore Down<br>Arc Melting Long Arc Mode]
    B3 --> B4[Flat Bath Stage<br>Refining by Oxygen Lance]
    B4 --> B5[Carbon and Lime Injection<br>Foamy Slag Practice]
    B5 --> B6[Argon Stirring via Porous Plug]
    B6 --> B7[Sampling and Temperature Check]
    B7 --> B8{Analysis OK}
    B8 -- Yes --> B9[Eccentric Bottom Tapping<br>Ladle Receives Steel]
    B8 -- No --> B4
    B9 --> B10[Ladle Furnace Secondary Refining]
    B10 --> B11[Continuous Casting or Ingot Casting]
```

**Key Functional Notes:**
* **Three-phase AC supply** → step-down transformer → graphite electrodes.
* **Foamy slag** covers the arc, stabilises heat transfer and protects the **refractory roof**.
* **O₂ injection** decarburises and generates **chemical heat**, reducing electrical demand.

---

### 4.4 Induction Furnace Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Three Phase Mains] --> C2[Thyristor or IGBT Based Frequency Converter]
    C2 --> C3[Water Cooled Copper Induction Coil]
    C3 --> C4[Alternating Magnetic Field Generated]
    C4 --> C5[Eddy Currents Induced in Charge]
    C5 --> C6[Joule Heating I Squared R Dissipation]
    C6 --> C7[Metal Charge Melts]
    C7 --> C8[Pouring into Mould or Ladle]
    C2 -.Frequency 150 Hz to 10 kHz.-> C3
```

---

### 4.5 Comparative Process Selection Logic (When to Use Which?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1{Raw Material Type}
    D1 -- Iron Ore Bulk --> D2[Blast Furnace Route]
    D1 -- Steel Scrap or DRI --> D3[Electric Arc Furnace Route]
    D1 -- Clean Foundry Returns --> D4[Induction Furnace Route]
    D2 --> D1a[BF plus BOF Steelmaking]
    D3 --> D1b[Secondary Steelmaking Ladle Furnace CCM]
    D4 --> D1c[Foundry Casting Line]
```

---

### 4.6 Heat-Flow Schematic of an EAF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Electrical Power Input] --> E2[Arc Plasma at Electrode Tip T Greater Than 3000 C]
    E2 --> E3[Radiation and Convection to Scrap]
    E3 --> E4[Melting of Scrap]
    E4 --> E5[Pool of Molten Steel T 1600 C]
    E5 --> E6[Heat Loss to Water Cooled Panels]
    E5 --> E7[Heat Loss to Off Gas]
    E5 --> E8[Heat Stored as Sensible Heat in Steel]
```
