## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Production Engineering, Module 3]**

**Differentiate between the blast furnace and the electric arc furnace with respect to (a) source of heat, (b) form of charge, and (c) refining capability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Blast Furnace | Electric Arc Furnace |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Source of heat | Combustion of metallurgical coke with preheated air blast at tuyeres | Electric arc struck between graphite electrodes and the metallic charge |
| (b) Form of charge | Iron ore (sinter/pellets), coke, limestone (solid burden) | Steel scrap, DRI, hot metal (recycled ferrous feed) |
| (c) Refining capability | Cannot refine — only reduces iron ore to hot metal | Can refine — decarburisation, desulphurisation, alloy additions, foamy slag control |

> Each correct row of the table carries **1 mark** (3 × 1 = 3 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]**

**Explain the role of coke in a blast furnace. Why is coke preferred over coal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Coke performs **three simultaneous roles** in the blast furnace:

1. **Fuel** — combustion at tuyeres provides heat for endothermic reduction and slag formation.
2. **Reducing agent** — generates CO (via Boudouard reaction $CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO$) which reduces Fe₂O₃ stepwise.
3. **Permeable structural support** — coke remains solid throughout the descent, maintaining gas permeability in the softening-melting zone.

Coke is preferred over coal because:
* Coal is soft, contains volatile matter (tars, gases), and would **crush under load**, choking gas flow.
* Coke is **porous but mechanically strong**, with low volatile content (<2 %), high CSR (Coke Strength after Reaction ≥ 60 %), ensuring it survives the burden descent.

> Role 1 — **1 mark**, Role 2 — **1 mark**, Why coke over coal — **1 mark**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> * Do **not** confuse **direct** and **indirect reduction** — most students mix them up and lose 4–5 marks immediately.
> * When asked to *"explain with a neat sketch"*, **always draw a labelled diagram** even if it is rough — examiners give 2 marks for the sketch alone.
> * For numerical problems, **always state assumptions** (e.g., 100 % Fe recovery, 90 % C in coke).
> * In EAF questions, mention **foamy slag** — it is the single most important modern practice.
> * Spell **Ellingham** correctly and explain what **ΔG° < 0** means in plain English.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [Choose A or B]**

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** Describe the construction and working of a modern blast furnace with a neat sketch. Explain the different zones and the major chemical reactions occurring in each zone.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** Calculate the specific energy consumption of an EAF that produces **100 tonnes** of liquid steel per heat using:
* Electrical energy = **36,000 kWh**
* Natural gas = **1500 Nm³** (HHV = 35.5 MJ/Nm³)
* Injected carbon = **800 kg** (oxidation releases 11,000 kJ/kg)
Comment on whether the practice is energy efficient.

---

##### Model Solution for Q. A(a) — Blast Furnace Construction and Working

**Construction (4 marks):**

The modern blast furnace is a **vertical, refractory-lined, steel-shell shaft furnace**, 25 – 35 m tall, 10 – 15 m diameter at the bosh. Major parts:

1. **Throat / Stock Line** — top, fitted with bell-less top or double bell charging system.
2. **Stack** — upper tapering section, lined with high-alumina or carbon refractories; indirect reduction dominates.
3. **Bosh** — lower diverging section, hottest zone (2000 °C); lined with carbon blocks and water-cooled copper cooling plates.
4. **Hearth** — bottom well where molten iron and slag collect; lined with carbon and dolomite refractories; tap hole and slag hole.
5. **Tuyere Zone** — 16 – 36 water-cooled copper tuyeres admit hot blast (1100 – 1350 °C).
6. **Stoves (Cowper)** — external regenerators that preheat blast using cleaned top gas.

**Working (3 marks):**

* **Charging** — alternate layers of iron ore (sinter/pellets), coke, and limestone charged from the top.
* **Hot blast** — air preheated in stoves, optionally enriched with O₂, blown through tuyeres.
* **Combustion at tuyeres** — $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$; this is followed by the endothermic Boudouard reaction generating CO.
* **Indirect reduction in stack** — $Fe_2O_3 \rightarrow Fe_3O_4 \rightarrow FeO$ via CO.
* **Direct reduction in bosh** — $FeO + C \rightarrow Fe + CO$.
* **Slag formation** — CaO + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃.
* **Tapping** — molten iron (~1450 °C) tapped every 1 – 2 hours; slag tapped separately.

---

##### Model Solution for Q. A(b) — EAF Specific Energy Calculation

**Given:**
* Mass of liquid steel, $m_{\text{steel}} = 100$ t
* Electrical energy, $E_{\text{elec}} = 36{,}000$ kWh
* Natural gas, $V_{\text{gas}} = 1500$ Nm³, HHV = 35.5 MJ/Nm³
* Injected carbon, $m_C = 800$ kg, oxidation energy = 11,000 kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Electrical specific energy (2 marks):**

$$E_{\text{elec,sp}} = \frac{36{,}000}{100} = 360 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Step 2 — Chemical energy from natural gas (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{gas}} = 1500 \times 35.5 = 53{,}250 \text{ MJ} = \frac{53{,}250}{3.6} = 14{,}791.7 \text{ kWh}$$

$$Q_{\text{gas,sp}} = \frac{14{,}791.7}{100} = 147.9 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Step 3 — Chemical energy from carbon oxidation (2 marks):**

$$Q_C = 800 \times 11{,}000 = 8.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ} = \frac{8.8 \times 10^6}{3600} = 2444.4 \text{ kWh}$$

$$Q_{C,\text{sp}} = \frac{2444.4}{100} = 24.4 \text{ kWh/t}$$

**Step 4 — Total specific energy (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{total,sp}} = 360 + 147.9 + 24.4 = 532.3 \text{ kWh/t}}$$

**Comment:** Modern best-in-class EAFs achieve **350 – 400 kWh/t**. This value (532 kWh/t) is **higher than optimum**, indicating the heat is not well optimised. Possible improvements: better foamy slag practice, more oxygen injection, oxy-fuel burners, increased hot heel, shaft furnace preheating of scrap.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]**

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **coreless induction furnace**. Derive an expression for skin depth and explain its significance.

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Ellingham diagram** and explain how it determines the choice of reducing agent for metal oxide reduction in extractive metallurgy. Use the example of iron oxide reduction by carbon.

---

##### Model Solution for Q. B(a) — Coreless Induction Furnace

**Principle (2 marks):**
A high-frequency alternating current flowing through a water-cooled copper coil generates a **time-varying magnetic field**. By **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, eddy currents are induced in the metallic charge placed inside the coil. These eddy currents dissipate energy as **Joule heat (I²R)**, melting the metal without any physical contact.

**Construction (2 marks):**
* Crucible (silica, magnesia, or alumina based on metal).
* Water-cooled copper induction coil surrounding the crucible.
* Laminated steel yoke outside the coil to confine the magnetic flux.
* Medium-frequency power supply (150 Hz – 10 kHz) using **thyristor or IGBT inverter**.
* Tilting mechanism for pouring.

**Working (1 mark):**
Charge is added gradually; eddy currents are strongest near the outer surface (skin effect). Stirring is inherent due to **electromagnetic pinch forces** ensuring homogeneous melt.

**Derivation of Skin Depth (2 marks):**

The skin depth is the distance at which the eddy current density falls to $1/e$ of its surface value:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\,\rho}{\mu\,\omega}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi\,\mu\,f}}$$

Substituting $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m:

$$\boxed{\delta = 503\,\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu_r\,f}} \quad \text{(metres)}}$$

**Significance:** When charge thickness $\gg \delta$, heating concentrates at the surface — heat then conducts inward. For higher frequencies, δ is smaller, giving more surface heating. For melting large masses, lower frequencies are used so that δ is large enough to penetrate the entire charge.

---

##### Model Solution for Q. B(b) — Ellingham Diagram

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Ellingham diagram plots **standard Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔG°)** of metal oxides against **absolute temperature**. A line that lies lower on the diagram represents an oxide that is **more stable** (harder to reduce).

**Key features (2 marks):**
* Most metal-oxide lines have **positive slope** (entropy-driven; ΔS° becomes less negative at higher T).
* **C–CO line** has a positive slope and lies below many metal oxides above ~700 °C — confirming C is a powerful reductant at high T.
* **C–CO₂ line** is nearly horizontal (slope ~0).
* **H₂–H₂O line** lies above C–CO → hydrogen is less effective than carbon at high T.

**Application to Iron Oxide Reduction (2 marks):**

For the reaction:

$$Fe_2O_3 + 3C \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO$$

At temperatures above the **intersection point (~700 – 750 °C)** of the Fe–FeO line and the C–CO line, **ΔG° < 0**, so the reaction proceeds spontaneously. This is why **coke is the universal reducing agent** in iron and steelmaking above 700 °C.

For aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃ line), the intersection with C–CO occurs at **>2000 °C**, which is impractical. Hence **aluminium** is extracted by **electrolysis of Al₂O₃ in cryolite melt** (Hall–Héroult process), not by carbothermic reduction.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Ellingham diagram is a powerful tool to **predict feasibility** of reduction reactions and to **choose between carbon, hydrogen, or electrolysis** as the reduction route.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* **Blast Furnace** = tall vertical shaft, continuous operation, reduces iron ore to **hot metal (pig iron)** using **coke** as fuel + reductant.
* **Coke has 3 roles**: fuel, reducing agent, permeable support.
* **Limestone flux** → CaO → combines with SiO₂ to form **slag (CaSiO₃)**.
* **Reduction occurs in two modes**:
  * **Indirect** (by CO) → exothermic → saves coke.
  * **Direct** (by C) → endothermic → consumes coke.
* **Modern hot blast temperature** ≈ 1100 – 1350 °C.
* **Specific productivity index** ≈ 2 – 3 t/m³/day of hearth volume.
* **Coke rate** is the **single most important economic metric** of BF operation.
* **Top gas** is cleaned and reused in **Cowper stoves** and for power generation.
* **EAF** = batch furnace, scrap + arc, secondary refining capability.
* **EAF phases**: Charging → Melting (long arc) → Refining (flat bath + O₂ lance) → Foamy slag → EBT tap.
* **Foamy slag** = slag + CO bubbles; shields the arc from the refractory, improves heat transfer.
* **Specific energy** of modern EAF ≈ **350 – 450 kWh/t**.
* **Induction furnace** uses **electromagnetic induction (Faraday's law)** for melting.
* **Skin depth** $\delta = 503\,\sqrt{\rho / (\mu_r f)}$ — higher f → smaller δ.
* **Induction furnaces cannot refine** — only melt; useful for foundry + clean returns.
* **Ellingham diagram** = ΔG° vs T for oxide formation; lower line = more stable oxide.
* **Carbon (coke) reduces iron oxide above ~700 °C** because C–CO line lies below Fe–FeO line on Ellingham diagram.
* **Slag basicity** = CaO % / SiO₂ %; basic slags are used in steelmaking, acidic slags in copper smelting.
* **BOF uses pure oxygen**, **EAF uses electric arc** — both produce liquid steel, both followed by ladle metallurgy and continuous casting.
* **BF–BOF route** = ~70 % of global steel; **EAF route** = ~30 % and rising due to scrap availability & sustainability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Topper Tip:** Always link **thermodynamics (Ellingham)** to **process choice (BF vs EAF vs electrolytic)**. Examiners love this linkage. A single sentence like *"Since the Fe–FeO line intersects the C–CO line at ~700 °C, carbothermic reduction in the blast furnace is thermodynamically spontaneous"* can fetch you 2–3 extra marks in any furnace-related question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaping and Finishing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shaping and Finishing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Shaping and Finishing Processes

In the context of **PCPET205 - Fundamentals of Manufacturing Systems and Processes** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)**, the topic *Shaping and Finishing* represents the second major stage of the manufacturing value chain — converting a rough, near-net-shape workpiece into a dimensionally accurate and surface-finished final component.

### 1.1.1 What is Shaping?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Shaping is a material removal process (MRP) in which a single-point cutting tool removes material from a stationary workpiece through a linear, reciprocating cutting motion. The process is performed on a dedicated machine tool called a **Shaper** (or **Planer** for large workpieces), classified under *reciprocating-type machine tools* in the family of *conventional machining processes*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:** Shaping is NOT a continuous cutting process like turning or milling. The cutting action is **intermittent** — material is removed only during the forward (cutting) stroke, while the return (idle) stroke is used purely for tool retraction and repositioning.

**Three Simultaneous Motions in Shaping:**
1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Motion):** Linear reciprocation of the tool or workpiece at constant cutting speed ($V_c$).
2. **Secondary Motion (Feed Motion):** Incremental cross-feed of the tool after each cutting stroke (measured in mm/stroke).
3. **Tertiary Motion (Depth of Cut):** Vertical adjustment of the tool position relative to the workpiece (measured in mm).

### 1.1.2 What is Finishing?

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Finishing processes are secondary manufacturing operations performed after rough shaping/machining to achieve high **dimensional accuracy** (tolerances as tight as **±0.001 mm**), superior **surface finish** (surface roughness $R_a$ values as low as **0.05 μm**), and improved functional properties (fatigue life, wear resistance, fatigue strength, corrosion resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** The fundamental difference between *shaping* and *finishing* lies in the **stock removal rate** and the **surface integrity goal**. Shaping prioritizes rapid bulk material removal; finishing prioritizes precision, surface quality, and functional performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Wood-Plane" Analogy)

Imagine a carpenter using a **hand plane** to smooth a rough wooden plank. The carpenter pushes the plane forward (cutting stroke) — wood shavings peel off. Then he lifts and pulls the plane back (idle stroke) — no cutting happens. After several passes, the wood becomes smoother. This is **exactly how a mechanical shaper works**, except the "plane" is a rigid single-point tool and the "wood" is metal.

Now, after the carpenter's planing, the surface is still slightly rough with visible tool marks. To make it mirror-smooth and silky to the touch, he rubs it with **fine sandpaper** (analogy: *grinding/lapping*) and then polishes it with a soft cloth (analogy: *buffing/polishing*). This final stage is **finishing** — small material removal, but huge improvement in surface quality.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Trick for KTU Exams:** *Shaping = Carving the sculpture; Finishing = Polishing the marble.* Both are essential, but they serve different purposes.

### 1.3 Classification Overview (Map for Module 3)

The shaping and finishing family is classified as follows:

| Category | Sub-Processes | Typical $R_a$ Achieved |
|---|---|---|
| **Shaping (Reciprocating)** | Shaper, Planer, Slotter | 1.6 – 6.3 μm |
| **Abrasive Finishing** | Grinding, Honing, Lapping | 0.1 – 1.6 μm |
| **Superfinishing** | Superfinishing, Buffing | 0.05 – 0.4 μm |
| **Polishing** | Polishing, Electropolishing | 0.0125 – 0.2 μm |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlighting)

The following key metrics are **bolded** for KTU examiner emphasis:

- **Cutting Speed ($V_c$):** Typically **15–30 m/min** in shaping.
- **Feed Rate ($f$):** Measured in **mm/stroke**, typically **0.1–1.5 mm/stroke**.
- **Depth of Cut ($d$):** Measured in **mm**, typically **0.5–5 mm**.
- **Surface Roughness ($R_a$):** Measured in **μm or μin** (microinches).
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR):** Measured in **mm³/min** or **cm³/min**.
- **Specific Energy (Energy per unit volume):** Measured in **J/mm³** or **W·s/mm³**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** Always quote units in SI form. Never write "$in$" for inches unless the question specifically requests imperial units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shaper Machine — Principle, Construction, and Working

### 2.1.1 Principle of Operation

The shaper operates on the **Quick-Return Mechanism** principle. Because the cutting stroke requires positive power and the return stroke is idle, the return stroke is designed to be faster than the cutting stroke. This is achieved mechanically using either a:

- **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism** (older design)
- **Crank and Slotted Lever Mechanism** (most common, KTU high-yield)
- **Hydraulic Quick-Return Mechanism** (modern design)

**Ratio Definition (KTU Board Favorite):**

$$\text{Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)} = \frac{\text{Time of cutting stroke}}{\text{Time of return stroke}} = \frac{T_c}{T_r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical QRR for industrial shapers lies in the range of **1.5 to 2.0**. A higher QRR implies a longer cutting stroke relative to the return stroke.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operation Logic of a Shaper

1. **Workpiece Mounting:** The workpiece is clamped on the **machine table** using a **vice** or **T-bolts and clamps**.
2. **Tool Mounting:** The single-point cutting tool is rigidly held in the **tool post** mounted on the **ram**.
3. **Ram Stroke Initiation:** The **ram** reciprocates linearly along horizontal guideways, driven by the **bull gear** through the crank-and-slotted-lever mechanism.
4. **Cutting Stroke:** During forward motion, the tool is rigid (no deflection), engages the workpiece, and removes a chip of thickness equal to the feed.
5. **Return Stroke:** The tool is automatically lifted (via a **clapper box** or **tool head swivel mechanism**) to clear the workpiece, preventing rubbing/drag.
6. **Cross-Feed:** After the return stroke, the **table** (holding the workpiece) advances perpendicular to the tool motion by a small increment (the feed).
7. **Cycle Repetition:** The cycle repeats, and successive cuts gradually reduce the workpiece thickness until the final dimension is achieved.

### 2.1.3 The Clapper Box — A Critical Design Element

The clapper box is a hinged tool holder that allows the tool to lift automatically during the return stroke. **Why is it essential?** If the tool remained rigid, it would rub against the freshly cut surface during return, causing:
- Tool wear
- Surface scratching
- Heat generation
- Power loss

## 2.2 The Planer Machine — Scaling Up the Shaper Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction Question (3-Mark Favorite):** *What is the difference between a Shaper and a Planer?*

| Parameter | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving Element** | Tool (ram) reciprocates | Workpiece (table) reciprocates |
| **Tool Holding** | Single tool, single-point | Multiple tools, simultaneous cutting |
| **Workpiece Size** | Small to medium (up to ~1 m) | Large (up to ~10 m or more) |
| **Cutting Force** | Limited (tool-side) | Higher (workpiece-side stability) |
| **Production Rate** | Lower | Higher (multi-tool simultaneous cutting) |
| **Typical Application** | Small parts, slots, keyways | Large machine beds, columns, ways |

## 2.3 The Slotter Machine — Vertical Shaping

A **slotter** is essentially a shaper with a **vertical ram**. It is used for:
- Internal keyways
- Internal slots
- Internal contours
- Die and punch machining
- Cylindrical surfaces (using a special circular attachment)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** These formulas are tested every year. Memorize the units carefully.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed (Shaping)** | $V_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000}$ m/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $N$ = strokes/min | Time calculation |
| **Stroke Length** | $L = (1 + \alpha) \cdot W + 10$ mm | $\alpha$ = clearance factor (0.3–0.5), $W$ = workpiece width (mm) | Tool travel setup |
| **Quick-Return Ratio** | $QRR = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r} = \dfrac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}$ | $\beta$ = crank angle for return stroke (rad) | Mechanism design |
| **Mean Cutting Speed** | $V_m = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (1 + QRR)}{2000 \cdot QRR}$ m/min | — | Hydraulic shaper analysis |
| **MRR (Shaping)** | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ mm³/min | $f$ = feed (mm/stroke), $d$ = depth (mm) | Productivity |
| **Time for Shaping** | $T = \dfrac{L \cdot n}{N \cdot 1000}$ min | $n$ = number of passes | Production planning |
| **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000}$ kW | $F_c$ = cutting force (N) | Machine selection |
| **Specific Cutting Energy** | $U = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ J/mm³ | — | Machinability index |
| **Surface Roughness (Theoretical)** | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ μm | $f$ = feed (mm/rev), $R$ = tool nose radius (mm) | Theoretical $R_a$ |
| **Grinding Ratio (G)** | $G = \dfrac{V_w}{V_s}$ | $V_w$ = workpiece volume removed (mm³), $V_s$ = wheel volume lost (mm³) | Wheel life |
| **MRR (Grinding)** | $MRR = v_w \cdot a \cdot b$ mm³/s | $v_w$ = work feed (mm/s), $a$ = depth (mm), $b$ = width (mm) | Grinding productivity |
| **Equivalent Chip Thickness (Honing/Lapping)** | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{v_w \cdot f_p}{v_s}$ | $v_w$ = work speed, $f_p$ = feed per pass, $v_s$ = stone speed | Surface generation |
| **Stock Removal (Lapping)** | $\Delta h = K \cdot p \cdot t$ | $K$ = coefficient, $p$ = pressure, $t$ = time | Lapping depth |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often confuse $V_c$ (cutting speed) with $V_w$ (workpiece feed velocity) in grinding problems. They are fundamentally different physical quantities.

## 2.5 Theoretical Background of Finishing Processes

### 2.5.1 Why Are Finishing Processes Necessary?

Even after precision shaping, surfaces exhibit:
- **Geometric errors** (form, waviness)
- **Surface roughness** (micro-irregularities)
- **Subsurface damage** (residual stresses, micro-cracks, work-hardening)
- **Surface contamination** (oxides, coolant residues)

Finishing processes address these issues through:
- **Micro-scale material removal** using abrasives (grinding, honing, lapping)
- **Plastic deformation smoothing** (burnishing, roller finishing)
- **Chemical/electrochemical dissolution** (electropolishing, chemical polishing)
- **Coating without removal** (electroplating, PVD, CVD)

### 2.5.2 The Four Pillars of Abrasive Finishing

1. **Grinding:** Uses a bonded abrasive wheel; material removal by multi-point cutting.
2. **Honing:** Uses bonded abrasive stones with controlled low pressure; primarily for cylindrical bores.
3. **Lapping:** Uses loose abrasive particles in a carrier fluid between two surfaces.
4. **Superfinishing:** Uses fine bonded stones with oscillatory motion; final surface refinement.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *G-H-L-S* = "**G**ood **H**ands **L**ight **S**urfaces" — the order of progressively finer finishing.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Critical Finishing Process | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (Engine Blocks)** | Honing of cylinder bores | Oil retention, piston ring sealing |
| **Aerospace (Turbine Shafts)** | Superfinishing + Lapping | Fatigue life at high RPM |
| **Hydraulics (Pumps, Valves)** | Lapping of mating surfaces | Leak-tight sealing |
| **Tooling (Dies, Punches)** | EDM + Polishing | Surface release, wear resistance |
| **Bearings (Raceways)** | Superfinishing | Rolling contact fatigue life |
| **Optical (Lenses, Mirrors)** | Polishing + Pitch Lapping | Sub-nanometer flatness |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quick-Return Ratio for a Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Mechanism

This is a classic KTU 14-mark question and the most frequently asked derivation in Module 3.

### 3.1.1 Mechanism Description

The crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism consists of:
- A **crank** $OC$ of length $r$, rotating uniformly at angular velocity $\omega$.
- A **slotted lever** $OP$ pivoted at $O$, oscillating between two extreme positions.
- A **slider (pin)** $Q$ that slides within the slot of the lever.
- The slider is connected to the **ram**, which carries the cutting tool.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup

Let us define the angular positions of the crank:
- During the **cutting stroke**: the crank rotates through an angle of $(\pi + 2\beta)$ radians.
- During the **return stroke**: the crank rotates through an angle of $(\pi - 2\beta)$ radians.

Where $\beta$ is the angle of crank rotation beyond the dead-center position (90° to the line of stroke).

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the cutting stroke time.**

If the crank rotates at uniform angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), the time taken for the cutting stroke is:

$$T_c = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\omega}$$

**Step 2: Define the return stroke time.**

The return stroke corresponds to the crank rotating through $(\pi - 2\beta)$ radians:

$$T_r = \frac{\pi - 2\beta}{\omega}$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio.**

The Quick-Return Ratio is defined as:

$$QRR = \frac{T_c}{T_r}$$

Substituting:

$$QRR = \frac{\dfrac{\pi + 2\beta}{\omega}}{\dfrac{\pi - 2\beta}{\omega}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

The $\omega$ terms cancel out:

$$QRR = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}$$

**Final Derived Result:**

$$\boxed{QRR = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}}$$

This is the **standard KTU 2024 board answer**.

### 3.1.4 Numerical Verification Example

**Given:** $\beta = 30° = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ rad.

**Step 1:** Convert angle to radians.

$$\beta = 30° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the formula.

$$QRR = \frac{\pi + 2 \cdot (\pi/6)}{\pi - 2 \cdot (\pi/6)}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify numerator.

$$\pi + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{3\pi + \pi}{3} = \frac{4\pi}{3}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify denominator.

$$\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{3\pi - \pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio.

$$QRR = \frac{4\pi/3}{2\pi/3} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \times \frac{3}{2\pi} = 2$$

**Interpretation:** The cutting stroke takes **twice as long** as the return stroke. This is a typical design value for industrial shapers.

## 3.2 Derivation of Cutting Speed in Shaping

### 3.2.1 Setup

In shaping, the cutting speed is **not constant** during a stroke (unlike turning). It is **variable** because the ram follows a quasi-simple-harmonic motion. However, an **average cutting speed** is defined for practical use.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the linear distance per stroke.**

The ram travels a distance of $2L$ per complete cycle (forward + return), where $L$ is the stroke length in mm.

**Step 2: Define the number of strokes per minute.**

Let $N$ = number of complete cycles (double strokes) per minute.

**Step 3: Compute the total linear distance per minute.**

$$\text{Distance per minute} = 2L \cdot N \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 4: Convert to meters per minute.**

$$V_c = \frac{2L \cdot N}{1000} \text{ m/min}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_c = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot N}{1000} \text{ m/min}}$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example

**Given:** Stroke length $L = 200$ mm, Number of strokes $N = 30$ strokes/min.

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$V_c = \frac{2 \times 200 \times 30}{1000}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate.

$$V_c = \frac{12000}{1000} = 12 \text{ m/min}$$

**Verification:** This value lies in the typical range of **8–30 m/min** for mild steel shaping — physically reasonable.

## 3.3 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Shaping

### 3.3.1 Setup

In shaping, the chip is generated only during the cutting stroke. Per stroke, the volume of material removed is:

$$V_{\text{per stroke}} = L \cdot f \cdot d \text{ mm}^3$$

where:
- $L$ = stroke length (mm)
- $f$ = feed per stroke (mm/stroke)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Volume per stroke.

$$V_{\text{per stroke}} = L \cdot f \cdot d$$

**Step 2:** Number of cutting strokes per minute.

If $N$ is the number of double strokes per minute, then the cutting strokes per minute equal $N$ (since each double stroke = 1 cutting + 1 return, but return is idle).

Wait — correction: $N$ double strokes = $N$ cutting strokes per minute.

**Step 3:** Volume per minute (MRR).

$$MRR = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $V_c = 2LN/1000$, so $L \cdot N = V_c \cdot 1000 / 2 = 500 V_c$.

$$MRR = 500 \cdot V_c \cdot f \cdot d \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{MRR = 500 \cdot V_c \cdot f \cdot d \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

### 3.3.4 Worked Example

**Given:** $V_c = 12$ m/min, $f = 0.5$ mm/stroke, $d = 2$ mm.

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$MRR = 500 \times 12 \times 0.5 \times 2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate.

$$MRR = 500 \times 12 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Grinding Ratio and MRR in Surface Grinding

### 3.4.1 Grinding Ratio (G)

The **Grinding Ratio** is a measure of wheel life. It is the ratio of workpiece volume removed to wheel volume worn away.

$$G = \frac{V_w}{V_s}$$

where:
- $V_w$ = volume of workpiece material removed (mm³)
- $V_s$ = volume of grinding wheel consumed (mm³)

A high $G$ means the wheel is efficient (long life). Typical values: **$G$ = 10 to 100** for soft wheels on hard materials; can exceed **1000** for CBN wheels.

### 3.4.2 MRR in Surface Grinding

For surface grinding with a reciprocating table:

$$MRR = v_w \cdot a \cdot b$$

where:
- $v_w$ = table feed speed (mm/s)
- $a$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $b$ = width of grinding (mm, equal to wheel width)

### 3.4.3 Worked Example

**Given:** Table feed $v_w = 5$ mm/s, depth of cut $a = 0.02$ mm, width $b = 25$ mm.

**Step 1:** Substitute.

$$MRR = 5 \times 0.02 \times 25$$

**Step 2:** Calculate.

$$MRR = 2.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mm³/min.

$$MRR = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a very low removal rate — characteristic of grinding, where the goal is precision, not bulk removal.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Shaping vs. Planing vs. Slotting vs. Milling

| Parameter | Shaping | Planing | Slotting | Milling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating Element** | Tool (ram) | Workpiece (table) | Tool (vertical ram) | — (rotary) |
| **Number of Tools** | Single | Multiple | Single | Multiple |
| **Stroke Length Range** | 100–1000 mm | 500–10000 mm | 50–300 mm | Continuous |
| **Feed Direction** | Cross (table) | Vertical (tool) | Cross (table) | Perpendicular to cutter axis |
| **MRR (Relative)** | 1 (baseline) | 2–3 (multi-tool) | 0.5–0.8 | 5–10 |
| **Surface Finish $R_a$** | 1.6–6.3 μm | 1.6–3.2 μm | 1.6–3.2 μm | 0.8–3.2 μm |
| **Accuracy Grade (IT)** | IT10–IT12 | IT9–IT11 | IT10–IT12 | IT7–IT10 |
| **Best Suited For** | Slots, keyways, small flat surfaces | Large flat surfaces, machine beds | Internal keyways, slots | Complex 3D contours |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between a Shaper and a Planer with respect to the moving element and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving Element** | The **ram (tool)** reciprocates linearly; the workpiece remains stationary on the table. | The **workpiece (table)** reciprocates linearly; the tool remains stationary on the tool head. |
| **Typical Application** | Used for machining small to medium workpieces such as slots, keyways, and small flat surfaces. | Used for machining large workpieces such as machine tool beds, columns, and large flat plates. |
| **Multi-tool Capability** | Single-tool operation. | Multi-tool simultaneous cutting (higher productivity). |

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for each correct contrasting point, 1 mark for application. Total 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the function of a clapper box in a shaper? Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **clapper box** is a hinged tool holder mounted on the ram of the shaper. Its functions are:

1. **Tool Lifting on Return Stroke:** During the return (idle) stroke, the clapper box pivots upward, lifting the cutting tool clear of the freshly machined surface. **(1 mark)**

2. **Prevention of Rubbing and Tool Wear:** Without the clapper box, the tool would rub against the workpiece during return, causing rapid tool wear, surface scratching, and heat generation. **(1 mark)**

3. **Improved Surface Finish:** By keeping the tool engaged only during the cutting stroke, the clapper box ensures clean chip removal and superior surface integrity. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the Quick-Return Ratio (QRR) of a crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism used in a shaper. (7 marks)**

**(b) A shaper is used to machine a flat surface on a mild steel workpiece of width 150 mm. The stroke length is set at 220 mm, the quick-return ratio is 1.75, and the cutting speed is 18 m/min. If the feed is 0.8 mm/stroke and depth of cut is 2.5 mm, calculate: (i) the number of strokes per minute, (ii) the material removal rate in cm³/min, and (iii) the time required to complete the machining. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the mechanism.
The crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism converts uniform rotary motion of the crank into variable reciprocating motion of the ram (tool). The cutting stroke is slower (more time), and the return stroke is faster (less time).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the crank angles.
- Cutting stroke corresponds to crank rotation of $(\pi + 2\beta)$ radians.
- Return stroke corresponds to crank rotation of $(\pi - 2\beta)$ radians.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Express the stroke times.
With uniform angular velocity $\omega$:

$$T_c = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\omega} \quad \text{and} \quad T_r = \frac{\pi - 2\beta}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Form the QRR definition.
$$QRR = \frac{T_c}{T_r}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Substitute and simplify.
$$QRR = \frac{(\pi + 2\beta)/\omega}{(\pi - 2\beta)/\omega} = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}$$

**Final Answer:** 
$$\boxed{QRR = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Workpiece width $W = 150$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 220$ mm
- $QRR = 1.75$
- $V_c = 18$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke
- Depth of cut $d = 2.5$ mm

**(i) Number of strokes per minute (2 marks):**

$$V_c = \frac{2 L N}{1000} \implies N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{2L}$$

$$N = \frac{18 \times 1000}{2 \times 220} = \frac{18000}{440} = 40.91 \approx 41 \text{ strokes/min}$$

**(ii) MRR in cm³/min (3 marks):**

$$MRR = 500 \times V_c \times f \times d \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$MRR = 500 \times 18 \times 0.8 \times 2.5 = 18000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 18 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iii) Time required to complete machining (2 marks):**

The volume to be removed per unit pass:
$$V_{\text{per pass}} = W \times f \times d = 150 \times 0.8 \times 2.5 = 300 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ per stroke (per unit width stripped)}$$

Wait — correction: the total volume to be removed from the full workpiece (assuming we are reducing the entire width to depth $d$):

$$V_{\text{total}} = W \times L_{\text{work}} \times d$$

But in this question, the workpiece width is the width being machined, and the stroke length is 220 mm, so the area to be machined per pass is $W \times L = 150 \times 220 = 33000$ mm², and we are removing a depth of $d = 2.5$ mm.

Total volume to remove:
$$V_{\text{total}} = 150 \times 220 \times 2.5 = 82500 \text{ mm}^3$$

Time:
$$T = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{MRR} = \frac{82500}{18000} = 4.58 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 4.58 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Explain the principle of surface grinding with a neat sketch. List the various types of surface grinding operations. (7 marks)**

**(b) During a surface grinding operation, the grinding wheel has a diameter of 250 mm and rotates at 1500 rpm. The workpiece is fed longitudinally at 5 m/min, the depth of cut is 0.02 mm, and the width of grinding is 30 mm. Calculate the material removal rate (MRR) in mm³/min and the specific energy consumption if the tangential cutting force is 25 N. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle of Surface Grinding (4 marks):**

Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process in which a **rotating grinding wheel** (bonded abrasive) removes material from a flat workpiece surface. The workpiece is mounted on a **reciprocating table** (or rotary table for creep-feed grinding) and passes beneath the wheel. A **coolant** is flooded over the grinding zone to:
- Reduce heat generation
- Flush away chips
- Improve surface finish
- Extend wheel life

The grinding wheel rotates at high peripheral speed (typically **20–35 m/s**), and the material is removed by the **micro-cutting action** of thousands of abrasive grits on the wheel periphery. Each grit acts as a miniature single-point cutting tool.

**Types of Surface Grinding (3 marks):**
1. **Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table Grinder** — wheel axis horizontal, table reciprocates.
2. **Vertical Spindle Reciprocating Table Grinder** — wheel axis vertical, table reciprocates.
3. **Horizontal Spindle Rotary Table Grinder** — wheel axis horizontal, table rotates.
4. **Vertical Spindle Rotary Table Grinder** — wheel axis vertical, table rotates (used for creep-feed).
5. **Creep-Feed Grinding** — very low feed, very high depth of cut (single pass).

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Wheel diameter $D = 250$ mm
- Wheel speed $N_w = 1500$ rpm
- Workpiece feed $v_w = 5$ m/min $= 5000$ mm/min
- Depth of cut $a = 0.02$ mm
- Width of grinding $b = 30$ mm
- Tangential cutting force $F_t = 25$ N

**(i) Material Removal Rate (4 marks):**

$$MRR = v_w \times a \times b$$

$$MRR = 5000 \times 0.02 \times 30$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 3000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(ii) Specific Energy (3 marks):**

First, compute the wheel peripheral speed:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi \times D \times N_w}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1500}{1000} = 1178.1 \text{ m/min}$$

Power consumed:
$$P = \frac{F_t \times V_s}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{25 \times 1178.1}{60000} = 0.491 \text{ kW}$$

Specific energy:
$$U = \frac{P \times 60}{MRR} = \frac{0.491 \times 60}{3000} \times 1000 = 9.82 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

(Or directly: $U = F_t \times v_w / MRR$ if we consider the cutting force component...)

$$\boxed{U \approx 9.82 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a typical specific energy value for grinding mild steel (typical range: **10–50 J/mm³**), confirming the calculation is physically reasonable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The single most common mistake in shaping/grinding numericals is forgetting to convert **mm³/min to cm³/min** (divide by 1000) or **m/min to mm/s** (multiply by 1000/60). Always write down the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Quick-Return Ratio Direction:** Some students compute $T_r / T_c$ instead of $T_c / T_r$. By **KTU convention**, QRR = Cutting Stroke Time / Return Stroke Time, which is **always greater than 1**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $V_c$ with $v_w$:** In grinding, $V_c$ (or $V_s$) refers to wheel peripheral speed, while $v_w$ refers to workpiece feed velocity. Mixing these up leads to dimensionally wrong answers.
> 
> 4. **Stroke Length vs. Workpiece Width:** The stroke length $L$ is set longer than the workpiece width $W$ to allow tool entry and exit. A common formula is $L = (1.3 \text{ to } 1.5) \times W$. Forgetting this clearance causes incomplete machining.
> 
> 5. **Missing Clapper Box Explanation:** In "Explain the working of a shaper" questions, examiners often allocate **1–2 marks specifically for the clapper box**. Skipping it costs easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Shaping and Finishing (Module 3):**

### A. Shaping Machine Fundamentals
- [ ] Shaper is a **reciprocating-type machine tool** using **single-point cutting tool**.
- [ ] Three motions: **Primary (cutting stroke), Secondary (cross feed), Tertiary (depth of cut)**.
- [ ] **Crank-and-slotted-lever** is the most common quick-return mechanism.
- [ ] **Quick-Return Ratio** $QRR = (\pi + 2\beta) / (\pi - 2\beta)$ — always greater than 1.
- [ ] **Clapper box** lifts the tool on return stroke — prevents rubbing and tool wear.
- [ ] **Cutting speed** $V_c = 2LN/1000$ m/min (where $L$ is stroke length in mm, $N$ is strokes/min).
- [ ] **MRR** $= 500 \times V_c \times f \times d$ mm³/min.

### B. Planer vs. Shaper vs. Slotter
- [ ] **Planer**: Workpiece reciprocates; multi-tool; for large workpieces.
- [ ] **Shaper**: Tool reciprocates; single-tool; for small to medium workpieces.
- [ ] **Slotter**: Vertical ram; for internal keyways, slots, and contours.

### C. Finishing Processes
- [ ] **Grinding**: Bonded abrasive wheel; multi-point cutting; high precision; uses coolant.
- [ ] **Honing**: Bonded stones with oscillating motion; for cylindrical bores; improves roundness.
- [ ] **Lapping**: Loose abrasive in carrier fluid; ultra-high precision; for sealing surfaces.
- [ ] **Superfinishing**: Fine stones with low pressure oscillation; lowest $R_a$ achievable mechanically.
- [ ] **Polishing**: Uses soft wheels and fine abrasives; mirror finish; no dimensional change.
- [ ] **Buffing**: Similar to polishing; uses softer wheels; for decorative finish.

### D. Key Numerical Formulas
- [ ] $QRR = (\pi + 2\beta)/(\pi - 2\beta)$ (derivable).
- [ ] $V_c = 2LN/1000$ m/min.
- [ ] $MRR = 500 V_c f d$ mm³/min (shaping).
- [ ] $MRR = v_w \times a \times b$ mm³/min (grinding).
- [ ] $G = V_w / V_s$ (grinding ratio).
- [ ] $R_a = f^2 / 8R$ (theoretical surface roughness).
- [ ] $U = P / MRR$ (specific energy).

### E. Standard Surface Roughness Values
- [ ] Shaping: $R_a$ = **1.6 to 6.3 μm**.
- [ ] Grinding: $R_a$ = **0.1 to 1.6 μm**.
- [ ] Honing: $R_a$ = **0.1 to 0.8 μm**.
- [ ] Lapping: $R_a$ = **0.05 to 0.4 μm**.
- [ ] Superfinishing: $R_a$ = **0.025 to 0.2 μm**.
- [ ] Polishing: $R_a$ = **0.0125 to 0.1 μm**.

### F. Examiner's Pet Topics (Year-on-Year High-Weightage)
- [ ] QRR derivation (asked almost every semester).
- [ ] Shaper vs. Planer vs. Slotter comparison.
- [ ] Clapper box function.
- [ ] MRR and cutting speed numericals.
- [ ] Surface grinding sketch and MRR calculation.
- [ ] Honing vs. Lapping distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Iron and Steel, Non-Ferrous Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of Iron and Steel, Non-Ferrous Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Engineering Materials Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPET205 — Module 3):**
> *Engineering materials are broadly classified into **Ferrous Metals** (those whose principal constituent is iron, Fe) and **Non-Ferrous Metals** (all other metallic elements and their alloys). The selection of a material for a specific engineering application is governed by its **mechanical properties, physical properties, chemical properties, machinability, availability, and cost**.*

For the purpose of KTU 2024 examinations, the focus of this module rests squarely on **iron and steel** (which constitute roughly **90 %** of all metallic materials used worldwide by tonnage) and the most industrially relevant **non-ferrous metals**, namely **Copper (Cu), Aluminium (Al), Lead (Pb), Tin (Sn), Zinc (Zn), Magnesium (Mg), Titanium (Ti), and Nickel (Ni)** along with their alloys.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of materials as the "ingredients" of the engineering world.**
> Just as a chef selects *wheat* (a base structural ingredient) for bread and uses *butter, sugar, and spices* (specialty ingredients) to give bread character, a mechanical engineer selects **steel** as the structural backbone of machines and uses **non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu, Mg, Ti) when a specific trait is required — lightness, corrosion resistance, electrical conductivity, or non-magnetic behaviour.

A bicycle is a perfect analogy:
- The **frame and gears** are made of **steel** (strong, cheap, stiff).
- The **rims and handlebars** are made of **aluminium alloy** (lightweight).
- The **cables and electrical wiring** use **copper** (high conductivity).
- The **bearings** use **bronze (Cu-Sn alloy)** (low friction).
- The **screws and nuts** may be **brass (Cu-Zn alloy)** (corrosion resistant, decorative).

---

## 1.2 Iron (Fe) — The Backbone Metal

> [!NOTE]
> **Iron** is a chemical element (atomic number **26**, density **7874 kg/m³**, melting point **1538 °C**). Pure iron is comparatively soft and weak; it is **rarely used in its commercially pure form** for structural engineering. Its industrial utility arises almost entirely from the vast family of **iron–carbon alloys** (steels and cast irons) and from **alloy steels** (Fe + Cr, Ni, Mo, V, W, Mn, Si, etc.).

### Forms of Iron (As Produced)

| Form | Carbon Content (% by mass) | Manufacturing Process | Typical Use |
|------|---------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Pig Iron** | **3.5 – 4.5 % C** | Smelting of iron ore in a blast furnace | Raw material for steel-making |
| **Wrought Iron** | **< 0.1 % C** (with slag fibres) | Refining pig iron in a puddling furnace | Ornamental gates, chains, hooks |
| **Cast Iron** | **2.0 – 4.0 % C** | Re-melting pig iron with coke & limestone | Machine beds, pipes, engine blocks |
| **Steel** | **0.02 – 2.0 % C** | Removing excess carbon from pig iron | Universal structural/engineering metal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Why carbon content matters:**
> The carbon atoms sit in the interstitial spaces of the iron's Body-Centred Cubic (BCC) lattice (α-iron) and Face-Centred Cubic (FCC) lattice (γ-iron). This **interstitial solid-solution strengthening** is the *fundamental mechanism* that makes steel stronger than pure iron. As carbon content rises, hardness and tensile strength increase, but **ductility and weldability decrease**. This single trade-off defines nearly every property discussion in Module 3.

---

## 1.3 Steel — The Most Versatile Engineering Material

> [!NOTE]
> **Steel** is an alloy of **iron and carbon** (typically **0.02 – 2.0 % C**), often containing small amounts of **Manganese (Mn ≤ 1.65 %), Silicon (Si), Sulphur (S), and Phosphorus (P)**. When deliberate additions of alloying elements such as **Cr, Ni, Mo, V, W, Co, Ti** exceed **5 % by mass**, the steel is termed an **alloy steel**.

### Broad Classification of Steel (as per KTU / IS Standards)

```
STEEL
├── Carbon Steel (Plain Carbon Steel)
│   ├── Low Carbon Steel (Mild Steel)  — 0.05 – 0.25 % C
│   ├── Medium Carbon Steel            — 0.25 – 0.60 % C
│   ├── High Carbon Steel              — 0.60 – 1.0 % C
│   └── Ultra-High Carbon Steel        — 1.0 – 2.0 % C
├── Alloy Steel
│   ├── Low-Alloy Steel   (alloying elements < 5 %)
│   └── High-Alloy Steel  (alloying elements > 5 %)
├── Stainless Steel (Fe-Cr-Ni system, ≥ 10.5 % Cr)
│   ├── Austenitic (300 series)
│   ├── Ferritic (400 series)
│   ├── Martensitic (400 series)
│   └── Duplex
└── Tool Steel (HSS, die steel, etc.)
```

---

## 1.4 Non-Ferrous Metals — Definition & Importance

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-ferrous metals** are metals and alloys **not based on iron**. They are more expensive than ferrous metals but provide indispensable properties: **light weight (Al, Mg, Ti), corrosion resistance (Cu, Pb, Zn), high electrical & thermal conductivity (Cu, Al), non-magnetic behaviour (Al, Cu), low friction (Sn, Pb)**, and **high strength-to-weight ratio (Ti, Al)**.

### Common Non-Ferrous Metals & Their Signature Property

| Metal | Symbol | Density (kg/m³) | Melting Point (°C) | Signature Property |
|-------|--------|----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Aluminium | Al | 2700 | 660 | Light weight, corrosion resistance |
| Copper | Cu | 8960 | 1085 | Highest electrical conductivity among cheap metals |
| Zinc | Zn | 7140 | 419 | Sacrificial galvanic protection |
| Lead | Pb | 11340 | 327 | High density, radiation shielding |
| Tin | Sn | 7280 | 232 | Non-toxic, low-melt, food-safe coating |
| Magnesium | Mg | 1740 | 650 | Lightest structural metal |
| Titanium | Ti | 4500 | 1668 | Highest strength-to-weight ratio among metals |
| Nickel | Ni | 8908 | 1455 | Corrosion resistance, magnetic alloys |

---

## 1.5 Why Engineers Must Master This Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based framework, this topic directly maps to **CO3**: *"Identify suitable manufacturing materials and processes for engineering applications."* You will be expected to **justify material selection** based on service conditions — strength, temperature, corrosion, wear, conductivity, weight, and cost — which is precisely what this module trains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cast Iron — The Cheap, Castable, High-Damping Material

Cast iron contains **2 – 4 % carbon** and **1 – 3 % silicon**, along with traces of Mn, S, P. The high carbon content means much of it exists as **graphite flakes or nodules** embedded in a metallic matrix. The form of the carbon dictates the type of cast iron.

### 2.1.1 Types of Cast Iron & Their Properties

| Type | Carbon Form | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Ductility | Key Application |
|------|------------|------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Grey Cast Iron (GCI)** | Flake graphite | **150 – 400** | Very low (< 1 %) | Machine tool beds, engine blocks, pump housings |
| **White Cast Iron (WCI)** | Cementite (Fe₃C) | **200 – 600** | Negligible (brittle) | Wear surfaces, ball-mill liners, crusher jaws |
| **Malleable Cast Iron (MCI)** | Tempered graphite rosettes | **350 – 700** | 2 – 10 % | Pipe fittings, agricultural implements, automotive parts |
| **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron (DCI / SG Iron)** | Spheroidal graphite | **400 – 900** | 2 – 18 % | Crankshafts, gears, pipes, heavy-duty casings |
| **Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)** | Short, thick graphite worms | **350 – 500** | 1.5 – 4 % | Diesel engine blocks, brake discs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grey cast iron** is named for the **grey fracture surface** caused by graphite flakes. These flakes interrupt the metallic matrix, giving it **excellent machinability, vibration damping, and compressive strength**, but very low tensile strength. **Ductile iron** (developed in 1948 by Keith Millis) achieves **spherical graphite** by adding **magnesium (Mg)** as a nodulizer, dramatically improving ductility.

---

## 2.2 Plain Carbon Steels — The Workhorse

Plain carbon steels contain only iron and carbon (plus residual Mn, Si, S, P). They account for **~90 % of all steel produced globally**. Classification is purely on carbon content.

### 2.2.1 Low Carbon Steel (Mild Steel) — 0.05 – 0.25 % C

- **UTS:** 350 – 500 MPa
- **Elongation:** 25 – 35 %
- **Hardness:** 120 – 160 BHN
- **Weldability:** Excellent
- **Machinability:** Good (especially with added S as AISI 12L14 free-machining steel)
- **Applications:**
  - Structural shapes (I-beams, channels, angles) — the **skeleton of buildings**
  - Automobile body panels (drawing-quality steel, DDS)
  - Boiler plates, ship plates
  - Wire, nails, screws, rivets
  - Railway tracks (with higher Mn)
  - Reinforcement bars (rebars) in concrete construction

### 2.2.2 Medium Carbon Steel — 0.25 – 0.60 % C

- **UTS:** 550 – 700 MPa (after quenching & tempering, up to **1200 MPa**)
- **Elongation:** 15 – 22 %
- **Hardness:** 170 – 250 BHN
- **Heat Treatment:** Responds well to **hardening & tempering**
- **Applications:**
  - Railway tracks (higher Mn variants like AISI 1080)
  - Crankshafts, connecting rods, axles, gears, couplings
  - Crankshafts of IC engines
  - Pressure vessels
  - Forged components (connecting rods, knuckles)

### 2.2.3 High Carbon Steel — 0.60 – 1.0 % C

- **UTS:** 700 – 1200 MPa (after heat treatment)
- **Elongation:** 5 – 12 %
- **Hardness:** 250 – 400 BHN
- **Wear resistance:** Excellent
- **Applications:**
  - Springs (leaf springs, coil springs)
  - Chisels, punches, dies
  - Cutting tools (files, hacksaw blades)
  - Ropes and cables (high-tensile wire)
  - Shear blades, railway springs
  - Mandrels, hammers

### 2.2.4 Ultra-High Carbon Steel — 1.0 – 2.0 % C

- **UTS:** Up to **1400 MPa** (after austempering)
- Very brittle in as-cast condition
- **Applications:**
  - Razor blades, surgical instruments
  - Punches, dies for blanking and piercing
  - Axes, chisels for stone cutting
  - Axles requiring extreme surface hardness

---

## 2.3 Alloy Steels — Tailoring Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **Alloy steels** are iron-carbon alloys with deliberate additions of one or more alloying elements (**> 5 % total** classifies it as a high-alloy steel). Each alloying element contributes a specific, scientifically-understood effect on the iron lattice and the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram.

### 2.3.1 Effect of Common Alloying Elements

| Element | Symbol | Typical % in Steel | Principal Effect |
|---------|--------|--------------------|-------------------|
| Manganese | Mn | 0.5 – 1.5 % | Deoxidizer, increases hardenability, combines with S to form MnS |
| Silicon | Si | 0.2 – 2.5 % | Deoxidizer, increases strength, used in transformer steel (Si-steel) |
| Chromium | Cr | 0.5 – 18 % | Hardness, wear & corrosion resistance, forms Cr-carbides |
| Nickel | Ni | 1 – 30 % | Toughness, corrosion resistance, austenite stabilizer |
| Molybdenum | Mo | 0.2 – 5 % | Creep resistance, reduces temper brittleness |
| Vanadium | V | 0.1 – 0.5 % | Forms V-carbides (VC), grain refiner, increases strength |
| Tungsten | W | 1 – 20 % | Hot hardness (red hardness), HSS cutting tools |
| Cobalt | Co | 2 – 10 % | Retains hardness at elevated temperatures |
| Copper | Cu | 0.2 – 1.5 % | Atmospheric corrosion resistance |

### 2.3.2 Famous Alloy Steels & Applications

- **AISI 4140** (Cr-Mo steel): Gears, shafts, couplings in automotive and aerospace.
- **AISI 4340** (Ni-Cr-Mo steel): Landing gear, crankshafts, high-strength structural parts.
- **AISI 52100** (Cr steel): Ball-bearing and roller-bearing races.
- **AISI 8620** (Ni-Cr-Mo case-hardening steel): Gears requiring carburizing.

---

## 2.4 Stainless Steel — The Corrosion Fighter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Stainless steel must contain a **minimum of 10.5 % chromium** (per ISO 3506) so that a thin, self-healing, transparent **chromium-oxide (Cr₂O₃) passive layer** forms on the surface, blocking further oxidation.

### 2.4.1 The Four Families of Stainless Steel

| Family | Crystal Structure | Typical Grade | Magnetic? | Applications |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| Austenitic | FCC | AISI 304 (18 Cr-8 Ni), 316 (Mo added) | No | Kitchen sinks, surgical implants, chemical tanks, food processing |
| Ferritic | BCC | AISI 430 (16 – 18 % Cr) | Yes | Automotive exhaust, washing machine drums, architectural trim |
| Martensitic | Body-centred tetragonal | AISI 410, 420 | Yes | Cutlery, turbine blades, surgical scalpels |
| Duplex | Mixed FCC + BCC | AISI 2205 | Partially | Marine shafts, chemical plants, oil & gas pipelines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no nickel in some stainless steels?** Nickel is an expensive, strategic metal. Ferritic stainless steels (Cr-only) avoid Ni, making them cheaper but less ductile and weldable. This is why austenitic grades (which contain Ni) dominate the global stainless market (~70 % by tonnage).

---

## 2.5 Tool Steels & High-Speed Steel (HSS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** was the revolutionary invention of **Robert Mushet (1868)** and **Frederick Winslow Taylor (1900)** that allowed cutting tools to machine metals at high speeds **without losing hardness** — the red-hardness property. The classic HSS grade **M2 (AISI)** contains: **0.85 % C, 6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V**.

- **M-series (Mo-based):** M1, M2, M7, M10 — drills, taps, milling cutters.
- **T-series (W-based):** T1, T6, T15 — high-temperature tooling.
- **Co-based HSS** (e.g., M35, M42 with 5 – 8 % Co) for hard, abrasive machining.

---

## 2.6 Non-Ferrous Metals — Detailed Engineering Applications

### 2.6.1 Aluminium and Its Alloys

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Typical Use |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------|
| 1xxx | Pure Al (≥ 99 %) | Foil, electrical conductors, chemical tanks |
| 2xxx | Cu | Aircraft structures (2024), truck frames |
| 3xxx | Mn | Beverage cans (3004), roofing, heat exchangers |
| 4xxx | Si | Welding filler, pistons (4032), brazing sheet |
| 5xxx | Mg | Marine hulls, pressure vessels (5083) |
| 6xxx | Mg + Si | Extrusions, automotive panels (6061, 6063) |
| 7xxx | Zn | Aircraft wings (7075) — among the strongest Al alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Duralumin (Al-4%Cu-0.5%Mg-0.5%Mn)** was the first heat-treatable Al alloy, patented by **Alfred Wilm (1906)** and famously used in the **zeppelin airships and Junkers J 1 aircraft** of World War I.

### 2.6.2 Copper and Its Alloys

| Alloy | Composition | Application |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| Pure Copper (C11000) | 99.9 % Cu | Electrical wiring, busbars, heat exchangers |
| Brass (Cartridge brass) | 70 Cu – 30 Zn | Ammunition casings, plumbing, decorative |
| Brass (Muntz metal) | 60 Cu – 40 Zn | Condenser tubes, marine fittings |
| Bronze (Gun metal) | 88 Cu – 10 Sn – 2 Zn | Bearings, gears, bushes |
| Phosphor Bronze | 95 Cu – 5 Sn + P | Springs, electrical contacts |
| Aluminium Bronze | 90 Cu – 10 Al | Marine propellers, pump impellers |
| Cupronickel | 70 Cu – 30 Ni | Coinage, marine condenser tubes |
| Beryllium Copper | 98 Cu – 2 Be | Non-sparking tools, springs, moulds |

### 2.6.3 Other Important Non-Ferrous Metals

- **Magnesium (Mg):** Lightest structural metal; **AZ91 alloy** (Mg-9Al-1Zn) used in steering wheels, laptop housings, automotive gearbox housings.
- **Titanium (Ti):** Grade 5 (Ti-6Al-4V) — aircraft turbines, medical implants, marine hardware. Aerospace applications consume ~45 % of all Ti produced.
- **Zinc (Zn):** Die-cast components (Zn-4Al), sacrificial galvanic anodes, roofing.
- **Lead (Pb):** Lead-acid battery plates, X-ray shielding, ammunition.
- **Tin (Sn):** Solder (Sn-Pb, Sn-Ag-Cu lead-free), tinplate food cans.
- **Nickel (Ni):** Coinage, Ni-Cd batteries, superalloys (Inconel 718), Ni-Cu (Monel 400) marine.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Quick-Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are routinely used in numerical problems from this module.

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **% C in steel** by mass | $C = \dfrac{m_C}{m_{steel}} \times 100$ | Composition verification |
| 2 | **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** for weldability (IIW formula) | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Predicts cold-cracking susceptibility in weldments |
| 3 | **Strength-to-Weight Ratio (Specific Strength)** | $S_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ (MPa per kg/m³) | Material selection in aerospace |
| 4 | **Stiffness-to-Weight Ratio (Specific Modulus)** | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Resists elastic deflection |
| 5 | **Tin–Lead Eutectic Composition** | **61.9 % Sn, 38.1 % Pb at 183 °C** | Lowest-melting solder |
| 6 | **Lead–Tin Alloy Density (rule of mixtures)** | $\rho_{alloy} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_{Sn}}{\rho_{Sn}} + \dfrac{w_{Pb}}{\rho_{Pb}}}$ | Solder density calculation |
| 7 | **Hardness–Tensile Strength Correlation** | $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN}$ (MPa) for steels | Estimate strength from hardness |
| 8 | **Young's Modulus Approximation for Steel** | $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$ | Deflection in machine design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the vertical bar `\|` appears** in equations above, it has been replaced by `\vert` or by descriptive text to keep the markdown table intact. KTU valuation scripts often auto-format tables — students should hand-write carefully.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Mild steel (body), medium-carbon steel (gears), HSS (cutting tools), Al alloys (engine blocks, wheels), Mg alloys (steering column), Cu (wiring harness).
- **Aerospace:** Ti alloys (turbine blades, fuselage), 7075 Al (wing skins), HSS (machining of Ti).
- **Civil construction:** Reinforced concrete (rebar + mild steel), structural I-sections, weather-resistant steel (e.g., **Cor-Ten** with Cu, Cr, Ni, P).
- **Biomedical:** 316L stainless steel, Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr-Mo — all biocompatible.
- **Marine:** Monel (Cu-Ni), Al-bronze propellers, Cu-Ni condenser tubes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carbon Equivalent (CE) for Weldability

The Carbon Equivalent is an empirical formula that **lumps the hardenability contributions of alloying elements into an equivalent carbon percentage**. A CE greater than **0.45 %** warns of **cold-cracking** risk during welding.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Starting point:** The IIW (International Institute of Welding) definition is empirical — derived from correlating weld-cracking data with composition. We shall *derive* a simplified two-element version and then state the full form.

For a binary **Fe-C-Mn** alloy, the martensite-start (Ms) temperature depends on combined hardenability:

$$\text{Ms} = 539 - 423\,C - 30.4\,\text{Mn} - 17.7\,\text{Ni} - 12.1\,\text{Cr} - 7.5\,\text{Mo} \;(^{\circ}C)$$

The probability of martensite formation (and hence cold-cracking) is governed by the **combined effect** of all these elements. To simplify, we ask:

*"What weight of manganese produces the same hardening as 1 weight of carbon?"*

Experimental data: 6 g of Mn ≈ 1 g of C in hardenability.

$$\therefore \quad CE_{Mn} = \dfrac{Mn}{6}$$

Repeating for each element using empirical equivalences:

$$CE_{Cr+Mo+V} \approx \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5}$$

$$CE_{Ni+Cu} \approx \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

Summing all contributions plus the base carbon:

$$\boxed{\;CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}\;} \quad (\%\, \text{by mass})$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A structural steel has composition: **C = 0.18 %, Mn = 1.4 %, Cr = 0.25 %, Mo = 0.10 %, V = 0.04 %, Ni = 0.10 %, Cu = 0.20 %**.

Substituting each term:

$$CE = 0.18 + \dfrac{1.4}{6} + \dfrac{0.25 + 0.10 + 0.04}{5} + \dfrac{0.10 + 0.20}{15}$$

$$CE = 0.18 + 0.2333 + 0.0780 + 0.0200$$

$$CE = 0.5113 \;\%$$

**Interpretation:**

- CE > 0.45 % → **Preheat required** before welding (typically 100 – 200 °C).
- CE > 0.60 % → **Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** mandatory.
- For our steel, CE = 0.51 % is in the **medium-risk** band — preheat at 100 °C is recommended.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Density of a Binary Alloy by Rule of Mixtures

Suppose we mix two metals **A** (mass fraction $w_A$, density $\rho_A$) and **B** (mass fraction $w_B$, density $\rho_B$).

By conservation of mass:

$$m_{alloy} = m_A + m_B$$

By conservation of volume:

$$V_{alloy} = V_A + V_B = \dfrac{m_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{m_B}{\rho_B}$$

Therefore:

$$\rho_{alloy} = \dfrac{m_{alloy}}{V_{alloy}} = \dfrac{m_A + m_B}{\dfrac{m_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{m_B}{\rho_B}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $m_{alloy}$ and using $w_i = m_i / m_{alloy}$:

$$\boxed{\;\rho_{alloy} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{w_B}{\rho_B}}\;}$$

### Worked Numerical Example — 60/40 Solder (60 % Sn, 40 % Pb)

Given: $\rho_{Sn} = 7280 \; kg/m^3$, $\rho_{Pb} = 11340 \; kg/m^3$.

$$\rho_{solder} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{0.60}{7280} + \dfrac{0.40}{11340}}$$

Compute each term:

$$\dfrac{0.60}{7280} = 8.242 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/kg$$

$$\dfrac{0.40}{11340} = 3.527 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/kg$$

Add them:

$$8.242 \times 10^{-5} + 3.527 \times 10^{-5} = 1.1769 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/kg$$

Invert:

$$\rho_{solder} = \dfrac{1}{1.1769 \times 10^{-4}} = 8497 \; kg/m^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **measured** density of 60/40 Sn-Pb solder is approximately **8500 kg/m³** — the calculation matches experiment within 0.04 %, validating the rule of mixtures for these *immiscible-in-the-melt-but-homogeneous-in-bulk* solders.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Strength Comparison

The **specific strength** is the defining criterion when the design is **mass-critical** (aircraft, rockets, vehicles).

### Derivation

By definition:

$$S_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$$

For **structural steel** ($\sigma_{uts} = 500$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³):

$$S_{sp,steel} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^6}{7850} = 63694 \; N \cdot m / kg$$

For **aluminium alloy 7075-T6** ($\sigma_{uts} = 572$ MPa, $\rho = 2810$ kg/m³):

$$S_{sp,Al7075} = \dfrac{572 \times 10^6}{2810} = 203558 \; N \cdot m / kg$$

For **titanium Ti-6Al-4V** ($\sigma_{uts} = 950$ MPa, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³):

$$S_{sp,Ti} = \dfrac{950 \times 10^6}{4430} = 214447 \; N \cdot m / kg$$

For **magnesium AZ91** ($\sigma_{uts} = 230$ MPa, $\rho = 1800$ kg/m³):

$$S_{sp,Mg} = \dfrac{230 \times 10^6}{1800} = 127778 \; N \cdot m / kg$$

### Result Matrix

| Material | UTS (MPa) | Density (kg/m³) | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) |
|----------|-----------|----------------|------------------------------|
| Structural steel | 500 | 7850 | **63.7** |
| Mg alloy AZ91 | 230 | 1800 | **127.8** |
| Al 7075-T6 | 572 | 2810 | **203.6** |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 950 | 4430 | **214.4** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Titanium delivers **3.4 ×** the specific strength of structural steel, but costs ~30× more. Aluminium 7075 strikes the best cost-to-performance balance, which is why it dominates airframe skins. **Steel is still preferred where cost dominates and mass is secondary** (e.g., bridges, ships).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hardness–Tensile Strength Correlation

The empirical relation between Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) and ultimate tensile strength for steels is:

$$\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times \text{BHN} \; (\text{MPa})$$

### Sample Calculation

A medium-carbon steel sample gives **BHN = 220**.

$$\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times 220 = 759 \; \text{MPa}$$

Compare with the **range for medium-carbon steels (550 – 700 MPa)** — our sample, presumably quenched and tempered, sits at the **upper end** of this range, consistent with the expected strengthening from heat treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This relation is a **rule of thumb**, not a law. It works well for plain-carbon steels but deviates for highly alloyed steels (e.g., HSS), where the factor can be as high as **3.7 – 3.8**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mass of Steel Required for a Component

### Problem Statement

A machine frame is a flat steel plate of dimensions **2 m × 1 m × 25 mm**, made of structural steel ($\rho = 7850 \; kg/m^3$). Find the mass.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute the volume:**

$$V = l \times b \times t = 2 \times 1 \times 0.025 = 0.050 \; m^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the density relation:**

$$m = \rho \times V$$

$$m = 7850 \times 0.050$$

$$\boxed{\;m = 392.5 \; \text{kg}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Cost estimate** (assume steel rate ₹ 65/kg):

$$C = 392.5 \times 65 = \text{₹ } 25{,}513$$

If we redesign the frame in **Al 6061** ($\rho = 2700 \; kg/m^3$):

$$m_{Al} = 2700 \times 0.050 = 135 \; \text{kg}$$

The mass drops by:

$$\Delta m = 392.5 - 135 = 257.5 \; \text{kg} \quad (\text{a } 65.6\% \text{ reduction})$$

But the **cost** rises sharply (Al ≈ ₹ 250/kg):

$$C_{Al} = 135 \times 250 = \text{₹ } 33{,}750$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Aluminium saves 65 % of the mass but costs 32 % more. This is **exactly the kind of multi-objective decision-making** a production engineer faces daily.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Justification Guide

| Service Condition | Recommended Material | Justification |
|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Cryogenic storage (-196 °C) | AISI 304L austenitic SS | Toughness at cryogenic temperatures |
| High-temperature boiler tubes | Low-alloy Cr-Mo steel (T/P22) | Creep resistance |
| Aircraft wing skin | Al 7075-T6 | Specific strength, fatigue resistance |
| Marine propeller | Al-bronze (Cu-10Al-5Ni-5Fe) | Cavitation & corrosion resistance |
| Cutting tool for hardened steel | HSS M2 or cemented carbide | Red hardness, wear resistance |
| Car engine block | Al-Si alloy A356 or CGI | Lightweight, good thermal conductivity |
| Food-grade piping | AISI 316L SS | Corrosion resistance, hygiene |
| Battery plate grids | Pb-Ca-Sn alloy | Corrosion resistance in acid |
| Architectural roofing | Cu sheet or Zn sheet | Atmospheric corrosion, patina |
| Springs (high fatigue) | Oil-tempered Si-Cr spring steel | High elastic limit, fatigue life |
| Coinage | Cu-Ni (75:25) or Ni-coated steel | Wear resistance, appearance |
| Surgical implants | Ti-6Al-4V ELI | Biocompatibility, strength |
| Bicycle frame | 6061-T6 Al or 4130 Cr-Mo steel | Specific strength, cost |
