# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Differentiate between ferrous and non-ferrous metals with at least four points of distinction and one suitable application of each category.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — about 80 – 100 words)

| S.No. | Ferrous Metals | Non-Ferrous Metals |
|-------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Iron (Fe) is the principal base metal.** | Iron is **not** the base metal; based on Cu, Al, Mg, Ti, Zn, Pb, Sn, etc. |
| 2 | Generally **magnetic** (with exceptions like austenitic SS). | Generally **non-magnetic** (with exceptions like Ni, Co). |
| 3 | High **density** — e.g., steel 7850 kg/m³. | Often **low density** — e.g., Al 2700 kg/m³, Mg 1740 kg/m³. |
| 4 | **Lower corrosion resistance** unless alloyed (e.g., stainless). | Many exhibit **excellent corrosion resistance** (Cu, Zn, Al). |
| 5 | **Cheaper** and abundantly available. | Usually **costlier** due to scarcity and processing. |
| **Application** | Structural beams, machine frames, railway tracks | Aircraft skins, electrical wiring, marine propellers |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner expects at least **3 – 4 crisp bullet points** plus one application each. Total ~80 words for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**Explain the term "High-Speed Steel". Why is it preferred for cutting tools used in machining?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — about 80 – 90 words)

**High-Speed Steel (HSS)** is a class of highly alloyed tool steel containing strong carbide-forming elements such as **tungsten (W), molybdenum (Mo), vanadium (V), and chromium (Cr)** along with 0.7 – 1.4 % C.

**Why it is preferred for cutting tools:**

- It retains its **hardness and cutting edge at temperatures up to 600 – 650 °C** (a property called **red hardness**) generated at the tool–chip interface during high-speed machining.
- It possesses excellent **wear resistance** due to hard W-, Mo-, and V-carbides.
- It can be **resharpened and re-used** many times, unlike cemented carbide inserts which require brazing.
- Classic grades are **AISI M2** (6 % W, 5 % Mo, 4 % Cr, 2 % V) and **AISI T1** (18 % W).

**Examples of HSS tools:** drills, taps, milling cutters, broaches, gear hobs, and reamers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Part B questions carry **Module-Based Internal Choice** — the student attempts *either* Choice A *or* Choice B in full.

---

### Question Choice A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Paper]**

**(a) Classify the different types of cast iron based on the form of carbon present in them. Discuss the properties and engineering applications of each. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

Cast iron is an alloy of iron, carbon (2 – 4 %), and silicon (1 – 3 %). The form in which carbon exists in the metallic matrix determines the type of cast iron.

**1. Grey Cast Iron (GCI) — Flake graphite**

- *Microstructure:* Graphite exists as **random, interconnected flakes** in a ferrite/pearlite matrix. Fracture surface appears **grey** because flakes scatter light.
- *Properties:* Excellent **damping capacity** (flakes absorb vibration); high **compressive strength** but low **tensile strength** (150 – 400 MPa); very low ductility (< 1 %); excellent **machinability** (flakes cause chip to break); good wear resistance under sliding.
- *Applications:* **Machine tool beds and columns** (vibration damping), **engine cylinder blocks, gearboxes, pump housings, manhole covers, brake drums, IC engine pistons (older designs), pipes.**

**2. White Cast Iron (WCI) — Cementite (Fe₃C)**

- *Microstructure:* All carbon is present as **iron carbide (cementite, Fe₃C)**. Fracture surface is white because it follows the brittle cementite.
- *Properties:* Extremely **hard** (400 – 600 BHN) and **brittle**; very difficult to machine; excellent **abrasive wear resistance**.
- *Applications:* **Ball-mill liners, crusher jaws, grinding balls, slurry pump impellers, wear plates** in mining and cement industries.

**3. Malleable Cast Iron (MCI) — Temper graphite rosettes**

- *Microstructure:* Produced by **annealing white cast iron** at 800 – 900 °C for long durations. Cementite decomposes into **irregular clusters of tempered graphite** ("popcorn" structure).
- *Properties:* Moderate strength (350 – 700 MPa) and **2 – 10 % elongation**; good impact resistance; reasonably machinable.
- *Applications:* **Pipe fittings, agricultural plough shares, automotive brackets, railway wagon couplings, hinge joints.**

**4. Ductile (Nodular / Spheroidal Graphite) Cast Iron (DCI / SG Iron)**

- *Microstructure:* Graphite in **spherical nodules** produced by adding **magnesium (Mg) or cerium (Ce)** to the melt before pouring (invented by Keith Millis, 1948).
- *Properties:* Highest strength among cast irons (400 – 900 MPa) with significant ductility (2 – 18 %); can be heat-treated (austempered ductile iron, **ADI**, reaches 1000 – 1600 MPa).
- *Applications:* **Crankshafts, camshafts, steering knuckles, heavy-duty gears, water and sewer pipes, wind turbine hubs, automotive differential cases.**

**5. Compacted Graphite Iron (CGI)**

- *Microstructure:* Short, thick, interconnected **vermicular graphite worms** — intermediate between flake and nodular.
- *Properties:* 75 % of ductile iron's strength with 80 % of grey iron's thermal conductivity; higher fatigue strength than grey iron.
- *Applications:* Modern **diesel engine blocks, cylinder heads, brake discs, commercial vehicle exhaust manifolds.**

---

**(b) Explain the different types of plain carbon steels based on carbon content. Give two specific applications for each. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

Plain carbon steels contain only iron, carbon (≤ 2 %), and residual Mn, Si, S, P. Classification is by carbon content:

**1. Low Carbon Steel / Mild Steel (0.05 – 0.25 % C)**

- Soft, ductile, excellent weldability, machinable.
- *Applications:* (i) **Structural I-beams, channels, angles for buildings and bridges**; (ii) **Automobile body panels, sheets, and wire ropes**; (iii) **Boiler and pressure vessel plates**; (iv) **Rivets, nails, screws, bolts**.

**2. Medium Carbon Steel (0.25 – 0.60 % C)**

- Higher strength and hardness; responds well to heat treatment (Q&T).
- *Applications:* (i) **Railway tracks and rail sleepers** (with higher Mn, e.g., AISI 1080); (ii) **Crankshafts, connecting rods, axles, gears, couplings**; (iii) **Hot-forged components** for automobiles; (iv) **Leaf springs (lower range)**.

**3. High Carbon Steel (0.60 – 1.0 % C)**

- Hard and wear-resistant but less ductile; difficult to weld.
- *Applications:* (i) **Springs (leaf and coil)** and **high-tensile wire ropes for bridges, elevators, cranes**; (ii) **Hand tools — chisels, punches, files, hammers, hand-saw blades**; (iii) **Shear blades, dies for blanking operations.**

**4. Ultra-High Carbon Steel (1.0 – 2.0 % C)**

- Very hard, very brittle in as-cast; must be austempered.
- *Applications:* (i) **Razor blades, surgical scalpels, knife edges, ice-skate blades**; (ii) **Punches and dies for blanking, piercing, and embossing**; (iii) **Axes and stone-cutting tools**.

---

### Question Choice B — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Paper]**

**(a) Describe the major families of stainless steel. Explain the role of chromium and nickel in conferring corrosion resistance. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

Stainless steel contains a **minimum of 10.5 % chromium** (ISO 3506), which forms a **thin, self-healing, transparent passive film of chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)** on the surface. This film is only ~2 – 3 nm thick but is impervious to oxygen and water, blocking further corrosion. If scratched, it re-forms spontaneously in the presence of oxygen.

**The Four Families:**

**1. Austenitic Stainless Steel (300 series — non-magnetic, FCC)**

- Composition: **16 – 26 % Cr, 6 – 22 % Ni**, low C; examples AISI 304 (18/8), 316 (Mo-added for marine).
- **Role of Nickel:** Stabilizes the **austenite (FCC) phase** at room temperature, providing excellent ductility, formability, weldability, and **cryogenic toughness** (no ductile-to-brittle transition).
- *Applications:* Kitchen sinks, surgical implants, food-processing equipment, chemical tanks, brewery vessels, exhaust manifolds.

**2. Ferritic Stainless Steel (400 series — magnetic, BCC)**

- Composition: **10.5 – 30 % Cr, very low Ni**; example AISI 430 (16 – 18 % Cr).
- **Role of Chromium (dominant):** Provides corrosion resistance without Ni, lowering cost. Lacks the FCC-stabilizing Ni, so it remains BCC (ferritic).
- *Applications:* Automotive exhaust systems, washing-machine drums, architectural cladding, indoor decorative panels.

**3. Martensitic Stainless Steel (400 series — magnetic, BCT)**

- Composition: **11.5 – 18 % Cr, 0.1 – 1.2 % C, low Ni**; examples AISI 410, 420, 440C.
- **Role of Carbon + Cr:** Cr provides corrosion resistance; the higher carbon enables **martensitic transformation** by quenching, producing extreme hardness (up to 60 HRC for 440C).
- *Applications:* Cutlery, surgical scalpels, turbine blades, pump shafts, valve components, ball bearings (440C).

**4. Duplex Stainless Steel (mixed FCC + BCC — partially magnetic)**

- Composition: **18 – 28 % Cr, 4 – 8 % Ni, sometimes Mo**; example AISI 2205.
- **Combined Cr + Ni:** Roughly equal proportions of ferrite and austenite phases. Higher strength (~2× austenitic) and superior resistance to **chloride stress-corrosion cracking**.
- *Applications:* Marine propeller shafts, oil-and-gas pipelines, desalination plants, chemical reactor vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Role of Chromium (10.5 %+):** Forms the Cr₂O₃ passive film. Higher Cr → better corrosion resistance (e.g., 316 with Mo for marine chloride environments).
> **Role of Nickel (typically 8 – 12 %):** Stabilizes FCC austenite, improves ductility, toughness, weldability, and cryogenic properties. Ni-free grades are cheaper but more brittle.

---

**(b) Discuss the engineering applications of aluminium, copper, and their alloys with suitable examples for each. [7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**A. Aluminium (ρ = 2700 kg/m³) and Its Alloys**

| Alloy | Composition | Typical Applications |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1xxx (pure Al) | ≥ 99 % Al | Cooking foil, electrical busbars, chemical tank linings |
| 2xxx (Al-Cu) | Cu 4 % | Aircraft structures (2024), truck body panels |
| 3xxx (Al-Mn) | Mn 1 % | Beverage cans (3004), roofing sheets, heat-exchanger fins |
| 5xxx (Al-Mg) | Mg 4 – 5 % | Marine hulls, pressure vessels, cryogenic tanks (5083) |
| 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si) | Mg + Si | Window/door extrusions, automotive panels, bicycle frames (6061, 6063) |
| 7xxx (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) | Zn 5 – 6 % | Aircraft wing skins, military bridges (7075-T6) |

**Special highlights:**

- **Duralumin (Al-4Cu-0.5Mg-0.5Mn):** First age-hardenable Al alloy; historically used in Zeppelins and Junkers aircraft.
- **Cast Al-Si alloys (e.g., A356, A380):** Engine blocks, cylinder heads, gearbox housings, motorcycle crankcases — chosen for excellent castability, low density, and good thermal conductivity.

---

**B. Copper (ρ = 8960 kg/m³) and Its Alloys**

Copper is the **second-best electrical conductor** (after silver) — the IACS (International Annealed Copper Standard) scale sets **100 % IACS = pure Cu**.

| Alloy | Composition | Typical Applications |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Pure Copper (C11000)** | 99.9 % Cu | Electrical cables, busbars, transformer windings, plumbing |
| **Brass — Cartridge brass** | 70 Cu – 30 Zn | Ammunition casings, lamp holders, radiator cores, musical instruments |
| **Brass — Muntz metal** | 60 Cu – 40 Zn | Marine condenser tubes, condenser plates, architectural trim |
| **Bronze — Gun metal** | 88 Cu – 10 Sn – 2 Zn | Bearings, bushes, gears for low-load machinery |
| **Bronze — Phosphor bronze** | 95 Cu – 5 Sn + 0.5 P | Springs, electrical contacts, Bourdon tubes |
| **Aluminium bronze** | 90 Cu – 10 Al | Marine propellers, pump impellers, non-sparking tools |
| **Cupronickel** | 70 Cu – 30 Ni | Coinage, marine condenser tubes, desalination plants |
| **Beryllium copper** | 98 Cu – 2 Be | Non-sparking tools for oil rigs, springs, moulds for plastics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is cupronickel used for coinage?** Because Ni hardens the alloy (improves wear resistance on coins), resists tarnishing, and has a distinctive silvery appearance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on this topic (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> 1. **Do NOT write vague statements** like *"Cast iron is used in machines"* — always specify **which type** (grey, white, malleable, ductile, CGI) and **why** that microstructure supports that application.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse HSS and carbide.** HSS retains hardness at red heat (~600 °C); cemented carbides at ~900 °C. Marks lost if you interchange them.
> 3. **For stainless steel questions,** *always* mention the **10.5 % Cr threshold** and the **Cr₂O₃ passive film** mechanism — failing to do so typically costs 2 marks.
> 4. **In numericals on Carbon Equivalent**, students frequently forget the **divisors (6, 5, 15)**. Memorise them.
> 5. **For Al-alloy questions,** name the **series (1xxx – 7xxx)** and the **principal alloying element** — listing just "aluminium alloy" without identification fetches partial credit only.
> 6. **Hand-write legibly.** KTU digital valuation has tight OCR — illegible numbers in CE or density calculations invite zero marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3 — Iron & Steel, Non-Ferrous):**

- ⭐ **Ferrous = Iron-based; Non-ferrous = everything else (Al, Cu, Zn, Mg, Ti, Pb, Sn, Ni).**
- ⭐ **Pig Iron 3.5 – 4.5 % C**; **Wrought Iron < 0.1 % C**; **Cast Iron 2 – 4 % C**; **Steel 0.02 – 2 % C**.
- ⭐ **Cast Iron types:** Grey (flake), White (cementite), Malleable (tempered rosettes), Ductile (nodular, Mg-treated), CGI (vermicular).
- ⭐ **Grey CI** = machine beds; **White CI** = crusher liners; **Ductile CI** = crankshafts.
- ⭐ **Plain Carbon Steel categories:** Low (0.05 – 0.25 % C), Medium (0.25 – 0.6 % C), High (0.6 – 1.0 % C), Ultra-High (1.0 – 2.0 % C).
- ⭐ **Low Carbon Steel** = structural I-sections, auto body; **Medium Carbon** = gears, axles, crankshafts; **High Carbon** = springs, chisels, blades.
- ⭐ **Alloy Steel** = deliberate addition of Cr, Ni, Mo, V, W, Co beyond 5 %.
- ⭐ **Stainless Steel** = minimum **10.5 % Cr** → passive Cr₂O₃ film; **Ni stabilises austenite**.
- ⭐ **Four families:** Austenitic (304), Ferritic (430), Martensitic (410), Duplex (2205).
- ⭐ **HSS (M2)** = 0.85 C, 6 W, 5 Mo, 4 Cr, 2 V → retains hardness at red heat.
- ⭐ **Carbon Equivalent (IIW):** $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$; CE > 0.45 → preheat before welding.
- ⭐ **Hardness–Tensile:** $\sigma_{uts} \approx 3.45 \times BHN$ (MPa) for steels.
- ⭐ **Density of alloy:** $\rho_{alloy} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_A}{\rho_A} + \dfrac{w_B}{\rho_B}}$ (rule of mixtures).
- ⭐ **Al alloys:** 2xxx = aircraft (2024); 6xxx = extrusions (6061, 6063); 7xxx = aerospace (7075).
- ⭐ **Cu alloys:** Brass = Cu-Zn; Bronze = Cu-Sn; Al-bronze = Cu-Al (propellers); Cupronickel = Cu-Ni (coinage); Be-Cu = non-sparking tools.
- ⭐ **Solder eutectic:** 61.9 % Sn – 38.1 % Pb at 183 °C.
- ⭐ **Specific strength winner:** Ti > 7075 Al > Mg > structural steel — explains aerospace material choices.
- ⭐ **For high-temperature creep → Cr-Mo steel; for cryogenic → 304L austenitic SS; for biocompatibility → Ti-6Al-4V.**
- ⭐ **Selection logic:** always balance **strength + temperature + corrosion + machinability + cost + mass** — there is **no single best material**, only the **best material for the application**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Nonferrous Metals - Extraction of Ore
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples of Nonferrous Metals - Extraction of Ore

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonferrous Metals:** Metallic elements and alloys that **do not contain iron (Fe) as a principal constituent** or contain it only in insignificant amounts. They are extracted from their naturally occurring mineral compounds called **ores**, which are rocks or sediments containing economically viable concentrations of metal-bearing minerals.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET205 - Module 3)**, the extraction of ore refers to the complete sequence of unit operations and unit processes employed to liberate and isolate a nonferrous metal from its parent mineralogical matrix. This encompasses the tripartite metallurgical discipline:

- **Pyrometallurgy** (Heat-driven extraction)
- **Hydrometallurgy** (Aqueous solution-based extraction)
- **Electrometallurgy** (Electrolysis-driven extraction)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology:**
> - **Ore:** A naturally occurring solid material from which a metal can be profitably extracted.
> - **Gangue:** The commercially worthless material that surrounds or is closely mixed with the ore mineral.
> - **Mineral:** A naturally occurring inorganic substance with a definite chemical composition.
> - **Beneficiation:** Pre-treatment of ore to improve its quality (grade) before the main extraction step.

### 1.2 Examples of Major Nonferrous Metals

| Nonferrous Metal | Principal Ore Mineral | Chemical Formula | Primary Extraction Route |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aluminum (Al)** | Bauxite | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Bayer Process + Hall-Héroult Process |
| **Copper (Cu)** | Chalcopyrite | $\text{CuFeS}_2$ | Pyrometallurgy (Smelting) |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | Sphalerite | $\text{ZnS}$ | Roasting + Electrolysis |
| **Lead (Pb)** | Galena | $\text{PbS}$ | Roast + Reduction |
| **Tin (Sn)** | Cassiterite | $\text{SnO}_2$ | Smelting + Refining |
| **Nickel (Ni)** | Pentlandite | $(\text{Fe,Ni})_9\text{S}_8$ | Pyrometallurgy / Mond Process |
| **Magnesium (Mg)** | Dolomite / Carnallite | $\text{CaMg(CO}_3)_2$ / $\text{KCl}\cdot\text{MgCl}_2\cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Electrolysis of fused chlorides |
| **Titanium (Ti)** | Ilmenite / Rutile | $\text{FeTiO}_3$ / $\text{TiO}_2$ | Kroll Process |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Cleaning a Stained Shirt"**
> 
> Imagine your favourite white shirt has been smeared with chocolate (the metal-bearing ore) and dust (the gangue). To get the shirt clean, you would:
> 1. **Shake off the dust** → This is **ore dressing/beneficiation** (crushing, grinding, magnetic separation, froth flotation).
> 2. **Pre-treat the chocolate stain with a solvent** → This is **roasting or leaching** (chemical pre-treatment of ore).
> 3. **Wash it out with hot water and detergent** → This is the **main extraction** (smelting, electrowinning, precipitation).
> 4. **Final rinse and iron** → This is **refining** (removing final traces of impurities to get pure metal).
> 
> Just as you cannot recover chocolate with a single step, you cannot extract a nonferrous metal without going through a carefully engineered **flow sheet of operations**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$
- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP):** $T = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 1\ \text{atm}$
- **Atomic Mass Unit:** $1\ \text{u} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$
- **Crushing Work Index (typical):** $W_i = 10$–$15\ \text{kWh/ton}$ for nonferrous ores.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Flow Sheet of Ore Extraction

Every nonferrous metal extraction — whether for copper, aluminum, or zinc — follows a structured flow sheet consisting of five logical stages. The *Why* and *How* of each step is as follows:

#### **Stage 1: Mining & Primary Crushing**
- **Why:** The ore exists as a heterogeneous mixture in nature, so it must first be excavated and reduced in size to liberate the valuable mineral grains.
- **How:** Open-cast (surface) or underground mining → jaw crushers → gyratory crushers → cone crushers.
- **Output:** Lump ore of size $\approx 10$–$20\ \text{cm}$.

#### **Stage 2: Comminution (Grinding)**
- **Why:** Liberation of mineral grains from gangue requires a very high surface-area-to-volume ratio.
- **How:** Ball mills, rod mills, SAG (Semi-Autogenous Grinding) mills reduce ore to $\le 200\ \text{mesh}$ ($74\ \mu\text{m}$).
- **Energy Equation (Bond's Law):**

$$W = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$

where $W$ = work input (kWh/ton), $W_i$ = work index, $P$ = product size (μm), $F$ = feed size (μm).

#### **Stage 3: Concentration / Beneficiation**
- **Why:** Most ores contain only 0.5%–5% of the desired metal. Direct smelting of such lean ore is uneconomical.
- **How:** Exploits the physical/chemical contrast between mineral and gangue.
  - **Froth Flotation:** Selective attachment of hydrophobic mineral particles to air bubbles.
  - **Magnetic Separation:** For magnetically susceptible minerals (e.g., ilmenite).
  - **Gravity Separation:** Using shaking tables, spirals, jigs (for dense minerals like cassiterite, galena).
  - **Leaching (Hydrometallurgical):** Selective dissolution in acids/alkalis (e.g., bauxite in NaOH).

#### **Stage 4: Extraction (Reduction / Smelting / Electrolysis)**
- **Why:** Convert the concentrated mineral into a crude metallic form.
- **How:**
  - **Pyrometallurgical:** Roasting $\rightarrow$ Smelting $\rightarrow$ Converting $\rightarrow$ Fire refining.
  - **Hydrometallurgical:** Leaching $\rightarrow$ Purification (SX) $\rightarrow$ Electrowinning (EW).
  - **Electrometallurgical:** Hall-Héroult (for Al), fused-salt electrolysis (for Mg).

#### **Stage 5: Refining**
- **Why:** Crude metal contains 1%–5% impurities which degrade mechanical/electrical properties.
- **How:** Electrolytic refining (e.g., Cu, Zn), zone refining (e.g., Si, Ge), distillation (e.g., Zn), or the Mond process (for Ni).

---

### 2.2 Pyrometallurgical Extraction — Deep Dive

Pyrometallurgy is the dominant route for **Cu, Pb, Zn, Sn, Ni**. The four key sub-operations are:

| Sub-Operation | Purpose | Typical Conditions | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calcination** | Decompose carbonates, hydrates | $T = 500$–$900°\text{C}$, oxidizing | $\text{ZnCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{CO}_2$ |
| **Roasting** | Convert sulfides to oxides, remove S/As/Sb | $T = 600$–$1000°\text{C}$, controlled O₂ | $2\text{ZnS} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{ZnO} + 2\text{SO}_2$ |
| **Smelting** | Reduce oxide to metal using carbon/fluxes | $T = 1200$–$1400°\text{C}$, coke bed | $\text{ZnO} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Zn} + \text{CO}$ |
| **Refining** | Remove residual impurities | Pyrometallurgical/electrolytic | Fire refining of blister Cu |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roasting Reactions (ZnS example):**
> - **Partial Roasting:** $\text{ZnS} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Zn} + \text{SO}_2$ (less common)
> - **Dead Roasting:** $2\text{ZnS} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{ZnO} + 2\text{SO}_2$ (preferred for hydrometallurgy)
> - **Sulfation Roasting:** $\text{ZnS} + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4$ (selective leaching)

---

### 2.3 Hydrometallurgical Extraction — Deep Dive

Hydrometallurgy is preferred for **low-grade and disseminated ores**, and is environmentally cleaner (avoids SO₂ emissions). The three core stages are:

1. **Leaching** — Selective dissolution of target metal using aqueous reagents.
   - **Acid leaching (Cu from oxides):** $\text{CuO} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{CuSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - **Alkali leaching (Al from bauxite — Bayer Process):** $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{NaAlO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
2. **Purification / Solvent Extraction (SX)** — Liquid-liquid extraction using organic reagents like LIX 64N (for Cu) or D2EHPA (for Zn).
3. **Electrowinning (EW)** — Recovery of metal from purified solution by electrolysis.

---

### 2.4 Electrometallurgical Extraction — Deep Dive

#### **A. Hall-Héroult Process (Aluminum)**
- **Cell voltage:** $V = 4.0$–$4.5\ \text{V}$
- **Current efficiency:** $\eta = 90$–$95\%$
- **Specific energy consumption:** $\approx 13$–$15\ \text{kWh/kg of Al}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall Cathodic Reaction (Hall-Héroult):**
> 
> $$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3\ (\text{dissolved in cryolite}) + \frac{3}{2}\,\text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{Al}\ (l) + \frac{3}{2}\,\text{CO}_2\ (g)$$

#### **B. Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Master Equation)**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F}$$

where $m$ = mass of metal deposited (g), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $\eta$ = current efficiency, $n$ = valency number, $F$ = Faraday's constant.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = 10\,W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ | Bond's comminution energy | kWh/ton |
| 2 | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of electrolysis | grams |
| 3 | $\%\,\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of metal in concentrate}}{\text{Mass of metal in feed}} \times 100$ | Metallurgical recovery | % |
| 4 | $\%\,\text{Grade} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of metal}}{\text{Total mass of ore}} \times 100$ | Ore grade | % |
| 5 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m}$ | Electrolytic energy intensity | kWh/kg |
| 6 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT\ln K$ | Thermodynamic feasibility of reduction | J/mol |
| 7 | $\text{Ellingham line: } \Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ | Predicts metal-oxide stability | kJ/mol |
| 8 | $\text{Stoichiometric air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2\ \text{required}}{\text{O}_2\ \text{in air}}\ ( = \times 4.35)$ | Combustion air demand | Nm³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used daily in **mineral processing plants** (e.g., Hindalco, Vedanta, NALCO, Sterlite), **foundries**, and **electrolytic refineries** to size crushers, calculate reagent doses, and design electrolytic cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bond's Crushing Energy Calculation

**Problem:** A nonferrous ore (chalcopyrite, $\text{CuFeS}_2$) is fed to a ball mill at $F = 5000\ \mu\text{m}$ and must be reduced to $P = 75\ \mu\text{m}$. The Work Index of the ore is $W_i = 12.5\ \text{kWh/ton}$. Calculate the energy required per ton of ore.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**

$$F = 5000\ \mu\text{m}, \quad P = 75\ \mu\text{m}, \quad W_i = 12.5\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Bond's Equation:**

$$W = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Inverse Square Roots:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{75}} = \frac{1}{8.6603} = 0.11547\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5000}} = \frac{1}{70.7107} = 0.01414\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Difference:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} = 0.11547 - 0.01414 = 0.10133\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 10 and $W_i$:**

$$W = 10 \times 12.5 \times 0.10133$$

$$W = 125 \times 0.10133 = 12.666\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Result:** Approximately **12.67 kWh of energy** is required to comminute one ton of chalcopyrite ore from 5 mm to 75 μm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This value dictates the size of the motor driving the ball mill. For a 100 ton/hour plant, the mill motor must be sized for at least $100 \times 12.67 = 1267\ \text{kW}$ (with a 1.2 safety factor → ~1500 kW).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Faraday's Law — Aluminum Production

**Problem:** A Hall-Héroult cell operates at $I = 150{,}000\ \text{A}$ for $t = 24\ \text{hours}$. The current efficiency is $\eta = 92\%$. Calculate the mass of aluminum produced in one day.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data:**

$$I = 150{,}000\ \text{A}, \quad t = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400\ \text{s}, \quad \eta = 0.92, \quad M_{Al} = 26.98\ \text{g/mol}, \quad n = 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Faraday's Law:**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$m = \frac{26.98 \times 150{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \times 0.92}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Numerator:**

$$26.98 \times 150{,}000 = 4{,}047{,}000$$

$$4{,}047{,}000 \times 86{,}400 = 3.4966 \times 10^{11}$$

$$3.4966 \times 10^{11} \times 0.92 = 3.2169 \times 10^{11}\ \text{g·A·s/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate Denominator:**

$$3 \times 96{,}485 = 289{,}455\ \text{C/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$m = \frac{3.2169 \times 10^{11}}{2.89455 \times 10^5} = 1.1114 \times 10^6\ \text{g}$$

**Result:** Approximately **1111.4 kg (~1.11 ton) of aluminum** is produced per cell per day.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Mass Balance in a Copper Smelter

**Problem:** A concentrator receives 1000 tons of chalcopyrite ore per day, with a head grade of 1.2% Cu. The concentrate grade is 28% Cu with 92% metallurgical recovery. Calculate: (a) Mass of concentrate produced, (b) Mass of Cu in concentrate.

**Step 1 — Calculate Cu in Feed (Recovery Definition):**

$$\%\,\text{Recovery} = \frac{\text{Cu in concentrate}}{\text{Cu in feed}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Cu in feed} = 1000 \times 0.012 = 12\ \text{tons Cu/day}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Recovery:**

$$\text{Cu in concentrate} = \frac{92}{100} \times 12 = 11.04\ \text{tons Cu/day}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Mass of Concentrate (from Grade):**

$$\%\,\text{Grade of concentrate} = \frac{\text{Cu in concentrate}}{\text{Mass of concentrate}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Mass of concentrate} = \frac{11.04}{0.28} = 39.43\ \text{tons/day}$$

**Result:** The plant produces **39.43 tons of concentrate** containing **11.04 tons of Cu** per day. The tailings contain 12 − 11.04 = 0.96 tons of Cu/day.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Extraction Route

| Ore Property | Recommended Route | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Sulfide ore, high grade (Cu > 1%)** | Pyrometallurgy | Energy-efficient; mature technology |
| **Sulfide ore, low grade (Cu < 0.5%)** | Hydrometallurgy (SX-EW) | Avoids slag losses, handles low grade |
| **Oxide ore, low grade** | Hydrometallurgy | Direct acid/alkali leaching |
| **Oxide ore, high grade (bauxite)** | Bayer + Hall-Héroult | Industry standard for Al |
| **Reactive metal (Na, Mg, Al)** | Electrolysis of fused salt | Carbothermic reduction is infeasible |
| **Noble metal (Au, Pt)** | Cyanidation / Aqua regia | Selective complexation |
| **Titanium** | Kroll Process (Mg reduction of TiCl₄) | Avoids carbide/oxide contamination |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Generic Flow Sheet for Nonferrous Ore Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ore Mine Open Cast or Underground] --> B[Primary Crushing Jaw or Gyratory Crusher]
    B --> C[Secondary Crushing Cone Crusher]
    C --> D[Grinding Ball Mill or SAG Mill]
    D --> E{Concentration Stage}
    E -->|Sulfide Ore| F1[Froth Flotation]
    E -->|Magnetic Mineral| F2[Magnetic Separation]
    E -->|Oxide Ore| F3[Leaching Acid or Alkali]
    F1 --> G[Concentrate 20 to 40 percent Metal]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> H[Purified Leach Liquor]
    G --> I{Extraction Route}
    H --> I
    I -->|Pyrometallurgy| J1[Roasting and Smelting]
    I -->|Hydrometallurgy| J2[Solvent Extraction and Electrowinning]
    I -->|Electrometallurgy| J3[Electrolysis of Fused Salt]
    J1 --> K[Crude Metal Blister Copper or Pig Lead]
    J2 --> L[Pure Metal Cathode Deposit]
    J3 --> L
    K --> M[Electrolytic Refining 99.9 percent Pure]
    L --> N[Final Product Shape Ingot Billet or Wire Bar]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Pyrometallurgical Flow Sheet — Copper Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chalcopyrite Concentrate CuFeS2] --> B[Roasting Partial]
    B --> C[Smelting in Reverb or Flash Furnace]
    C --> D[Matte 45 to 70 percent Cu]
    D --> E[Converting Pierce Smith Converter]
    E --> F[Blister Copper 98 to 99 percent Cu]
    F --> G[Fire Refining]
    G --> H[Anode Copper 99.5 percent Cu]
    H --> I[Electrolytic Refining]
    I --> J[Cathode Copper 99.99 percent Cu]
    I --> K[Anode Slime Contains Au Ag Pt Se Te]
```

### 4.3 Bayer Process + Hall-Héroult Flow Sheet — Aluminum Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bauxite Ore Al2O3 2H2O] --> B[Crushing and Grinding]
    B --> C[Digestion with NaOH at 200 degree C]
    C --> D[Sodium Aluminate Solution NaAlO2]
    D --> E[Filtration to Remove Red Mud Fe2O3 TiO2 SiO2]
    E --> F[Precipitation Seeding with Al OH 3 Crystals]
    F --> G[Alumina Hydrate Al OH 3]
    G --> H[Calcination at 1100 degree C]
    H --> I[Pure Alumina Al2O3]
    I --> J[Hall Heroult Cell with Cryolite Na3AlF6 Flux]
    J --> K[Liquid Aluminum Tapped at 960 degree C]
    J --> L[CO2 Gas Evolved at Carbon Anode]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Logic for Extraction Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is Ore Sulfide or Oxide} -->|Sulfide| Q2{Grade Above 1 percent}
    Q1 -->|Oxide| Q3{Is Metal Reactive Al Mg Ti}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Pyrometallurgy Smelt and Convert]
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Hydrometallurgy SX EW]
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Electrometallurgy Fused Salt Electrolysis]
    Q3 -->|No| P4[Hydrometallurgy Acid or Alkali Leaching]
    P1 --> R[Refining Step]
    P2 --> R
    P3 --> R
    P4 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Note:** The diagrams above are **Mermaid block-level functional flowcharts** rather than physical sketches (per the KTU schematic protocol). They map the decision logic and process interactions of a typical nonferrous metal extraction plant in a pedagogically clear, KTU-evaluator-friendly format.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "ore" and "gangue" with reference to nonferrous metal extraction. Give one example of each from the extraction of copper.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Ore (1 mark):** A naturally occurring solid material from which a metal can be profitably extracted. Example: Chalcopyrite ($\text{CuFeS}_2$) is the principal copper ore.
- **Gangue (1 mark):** The commercially worthless rocky/siliceous material that surrounds the valuable mineral in the ore body. Example: In chalcopyrite, the host rock (granite, quartz, pyrite) constitutes the gangue.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** The ore contains the target metal compound in economically extractable concentration, whereas gangue is the valueless portion discarded during concentration steps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three differences between pyrometallurgy and hydrometallurgy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Pyrometallurgy | Hydrometallurgy |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Medium | High-temperature furnace operations | Aqueous solution leaching |
| 2. Typical route | Roasting, smelting, converting | Leaching, SX, EW |
| 3. Suitability | High-grade sulfide ores | Low-grade and oxide ores |
| 4. Energy | High thermal energy | Low thermal, high electrical |
| 5. Effluent | SO₂ emissions (acid rain) | Liquid effluents, easier to control |

(Any three well-explained points carry full 3 marks.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) With neat flow diagrams, describe the extraction of copper from chalcopyrite ore by pyrometallurgical route. List the major chemical reactions involved at each stage. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concentration (1 mark):** Chalcopyrite ore (0.5–2% Cu) is crushed, ground, and subjected to **froth flotation** to produce a concentrate with 25–30% Cu. Gangue (silica, alumina) is rejected as tailings.

**Step 2 — Roasting (Partial) (1 mark):** The concentrate is partially roasted at $\approx 850°\text{C}$ to remove moisture, volatile impurities, and a portion of sulfur:
$$2\text{CuFeS}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_2\text{S} \cdot \text{FeS}\ (\text{matte formation}) + \text{SO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Smelting (2 marks):** Roasted calcine + flux (silica) + coke is smelted in a **reverberatory or flash furnace** at $1200$–$1300°\text{C}$:
- Iron sulfide is oxidized to FeO, which combines with $\text{SiO}_2$ flux to form **fayalite slag** ($\text{2FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2$).
- Copper sulfide forms a **matte** ($\text{Cu}_2\text{S} \cdot \text{FeS}$) which settles below the slag by gravity.

**Step 4 — Converting (2 marks):** The matte is charged into a **Peirce-Smith converter** where air is blown through:
$$\text{Cu}_2\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_2\text{O} + \text{SO}_2$$
$$2\text{Cu}_2\text{O} + \text{Cu}_2\text{S} \rightarrow 6\text{Cu} + \text{SO}_2$$
The product is **blister copper (98–99% Cu)**, so called because of the blistered surface formed by escaping SO₂.

**Step 5 — Fire Refining & Casting (1 mark):** Blister copper is melted in a refining furnace, oxidized to remove residual sulfur, poled with green logs to reduce residual $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, and cast into **anode shapes** (99.5% Cu).

> *(Neat flow diagram showing mining → concentration → roasting → smelting → converting → fire refining → anode casting is mandatory for full marks.)*

---

**(b) Explain the Hall-Héroult process for extraction of aluminum from purified alumina. State the role of cryolite and the overall cell reaction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Source (1 mark):** Purified alumina ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) is obtained from bauxite by the **Bayer process** (digestion in NaOH, precipitation, calcination).

**Step 2 — Cell Setup (2 marks):** A rectangular steel shell lined with carbon serves as the **cathode**. Carbon anodes (pre-baked or Söderberg type) are suspended from above. The cell is filled with a molten bath of **cryolite ($\text{Na}_3\text{AlF}_6$)** at $\approx 960°\text{C}$.

**Step 3 — Role of Cryolite (1 mark):** Cryolite acts as a **flux/solvent** to dissolve alumina (which has a melting point of $2050°\text{C}$ on its own). Pure cryolite melts at $\approx 1000°\text{C}$, and the cryolite–alumina mixture melts at $\approx 960°\text{C}$, drastically reducing energy consumption. It also improves electrolyte conductivity.

**Step 4 — Electrode Reactions (2 marks):**
- **At Cathode (reduction):** $\text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Al}\ (l)$
- **At Anode (oxidation):** $\text{C} + 2\text{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\ (g) + 4e^-$

**Step 5 — Overall Cell Reaction (1 mark):**
$$2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{C} \rightarrow 4\text{Al} + 3\text{CO}_2$$

The molten aluminum (density $\approx 2.3\ \text{g/cm}^3$) settles at the bottom of the cell and is **siphoned off periodically**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> - **Do NOT confuse Bayer Process (ore purification) with Hall-Héroult (metal extraction).** These are TWO separate stages and must be clearly demarcated.
> - Many students write only "electrolysis of alumina" without specifying the role of cryolite — this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> - Forgetting to mention the **anode consumption** (carbon is consumed; must be replenished) costs 1 mark.
> - Omitting the **flow diagram** in copper extraction questions leads to a **1–2 mark deduction**, as KTU 2024 evaluators explicitly award marks for "neat flow diagrams."

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Describe the Bayer process for purification of bauxite. List the chemical reactions and state the conditions of digestion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Purpose (1 mark):** Bauxite contains 30–60% $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ along with impurities like $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ (red mud), $\text{SiO}_2$, and $\text{TiO}_2$. The Bayer process selectively dissolves $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ in NaOH, separating it from insoluble impurities.

**Step 2 — Digestion (2 marks):** Crushed bauxite is digested with concentrated NaOH solution at $200$–$250°\text{C}$ and $30$–$40\ \text{atm}$ pressure in an autoclave:
$$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\text{NaAlO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 3 — Filtration (1 mark):** The sodium aluminate solution is filtered to remove **red mud** (iron oxide, titanium oxide, silica).

**Step 4 — Precipitation (Seeding) (2 marks):** The clear sodium aluminate solution is cooled and seeded with freshly precipitated $\text{Al(OH)}_3$ crystals. Alumina hydrate precipitates out:
$$\text{NaAlO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al(OH)}_3\downarrow + \text{NaOH}$$

**Step 5 — Calcination (1 mark):** The hydrate is washed and calcined at $1100$–$1200°\text{C}$ to obtain pure anhydrous alumina:
$$2\text{Al(OH)}_3 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

---

**(b) With a neat flow sheet, explain the extraction of zinc from zinc blende (sphalerite) ore by roasting followed by electrolysis. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Concentration (1 mark):** Sphalerite ($\text{ZnS}$) ore is crushed, ground, and concentrated by **froth flotation** to obtain a concentrate of $\approx 50$–$55\%$ Zn.

**Step 2 — Roasting (Dead Roast) (2 marks):** The concentrate is roasted in a fluidized-bed roaster at $900°\text{C}$ with excess air:
$$2\text{ZnS} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{ZnO} + 2\text{SO}_2\uparrow$$
The SO₂ is captured and converted to $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ (by-product credit).

**Step 3 — Leaching & Purification (1 mark):** The roasted ZnO is leached with dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$:
$$\text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
The leach liquor is purified by **cementation** (using Zn dust) to precipitate Cu, Cd, and other noble impurities.

**Step 4 — Electrolysis (Electrowinning) (2 marks):** Purified $\text{ZnSO}_4$ solution is electrolyzed using **Pb–Ag alloy anodes** and **Al cathodes** at a cell voltage of $3.3$–$3.5\ \text{V}$:
- **Cathode:** $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}\ (99.99\%\ \text{pure})$
- **Anode:** $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$

**Step 5 — Melting & Casting (1 mark):** Zinc is stripped from cathodes, melted in an induction furnace, and cast into ingots/shapes.

> *(Mandatory: flow sheet showing ore → flotation → roasting → leaching → purification → electrowinning → casting.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B, Alternate):**
> - **Spelling of "Bayer" (not "Byer" or "Bear")** is a 0.5-mark deduction if misspelled.
> - Failure to mention **digestion conditions** ($T = 200$–$250°\text{C}$, pressure) costs 1 mark in the Bayer process question.
> - In zinc extraction, students often forget that **roasting is exothermic and self-sustaining** in a fluidized bed — this is a 1-mark observation.
> - Many students skip the **SO₂ capture → H₂SO₄ by-product** step, which is an explicit KTU 2024 syllabus point (environmental aspect) — 1 mark deduction.
> - In any 14-mark question, **a missing flow diagram costs 1–2 marks** consistently across all KTU valuation centres.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- ⭐ **Nonferrous metals** = metals that are **not iron-based** (Al, Cu, Zn, Pb, Sn, Ni, Mg, Ti, etc.).
- ⭐ **Extraction flow sheet = Mining → Comminution → Concentration → Extraction → Refining** (memorize this 5-stage sequence).
- ⭐ **Concentration techniques:** Froth flotation (sulfide), magnetic separation, gravity separation, leaching.
- ⭐ **Copper** → Pyrometallurgy: Roasting → Smelting → Converting (Peirce-Smith) → Fire refining → Electrolytic refining. Final purity = **99.99%**.
- ⭐ **Aluminum** → Bayer process (purify bauxite) + Hall-Héroult process (electrolysis in cryolite bath).
- ⭐ **Zinc** → Roasting (ZnS → ZnO) → Leaching in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ → Purification by cementation → Electrowinning.
- ⭐ **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \eta}{n \cdot F}$ — must memorize.
- ⭐ **Bond's Law:** $W = 10 W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ — must memorize.
- ⭐ **Pyrometallurgy = high temperature**; **Hydrometallurgy = aqueous leaching + SX + EW**; **Electrometallurgy = electrolysis of fused salts**.
- ⭐ **Reactive metals (Al, Mg, Na)** cannot be reduced by carbon → require **electrolytic route** because of very negative Ellingham line positions.
- ⭐ **Cryolite ($\text{Na}_3\text{AlF}_6$)** reduces melting point of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ from $2050°\text{C}$ to $\approx 960°\text{C}$ — essential energy-saving role.
- ⭐ **Anode in Hall-Héroult = consumable carbon** (oxidized to CO₂).
- ⭐ **Sphalerite = ZnS**; **Chalcopyrite = $\text{CuFeS}_2$**; **Bauxite = $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$**; **Galena = PbS**; **Cassiterite = $\text{SnO}_2$** — memorize the formulae.
- ⭐ **Reverberatory furnace / Flash furnace** = smelting; **Peirce-Smith converter** = converting of Cu matte; **Fluidized bed roaster** = modern ZnS roasting.
- ⭐ **By-product recovery:** SO₂ → H₂SO₄; Anode slime → Au, Ag, Pt, Se, Te — economically significant in Cu extraction.
- ⭐ **In Part B answers, ALWAYS include a neat flow diagram** — KTU 2024 evaluators award 1–2 marks for this consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smelting and Refining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smelting and Refining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smelting** is a high-temperature pyrometallurgical extraction process in which a metal is obtained from its ore by chemical reduction of the metal compound (oxide, sulfide, carbonate, etc.) using a suitable reducing agent (coke, carbon, hydrogen, or another metal) at temperatures typically above the melting point of the metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refining** is the downstream purification process applied to the crude metal obtained from smelting, aimed at removing residual impurities (gangue, dissolved gases, other metals) to enhance the purity, mechanical properties, and chemical homogeneity of the final metal.

In simple, KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, smelting is the **"extraction and reduction stage"**, while refining is the **"purification and finishing stage"** of extractive metallurgy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Coffee Brewing):**
> Think of smelting like extracting espresso from coffee grounds. The grounds (ore) contain the essence (metal) locked inside. You apply heat and pressure (reducing agent + high temperature) to force the valuable content out into a usable form (molten metal). However, the first pour (crude metal) is bitter and contains unwanted oils and solids. **Refining** is the filtration and quality step, similar to running the espresso through a fine paper filter (slag removal, electrolytic separation) to obtain a clean, premium product (pure metal).

- **Ore** = raw, rocky material mined from the earth (e.g., Hematite $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$, Chalcopyrite $\text{CuFeS}_2$).
- **Flux** = the "cleaning detergent" added to bind with impurities and form slag.
- **Reducing agent** = the "key" that unlocks the metal from its chemical bond.
- **Slag** = the floating impurity layer skimmed off the top.
- **Matte** = a molten intermediate sulfide mixture (used in copper extraction).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Constants

- **Typical smelting temperature for iron:** $1150\ \text{°C}$ to $1600\ \text{°C}$ inside a blast furnace hearth.
- **Typical smelting temperature for copper:** $1150\ \text{°C}$ to $1300\ \text{°C}$ in a reverberatory furnace.
- **Refining of aluminum (Hoopes cell) voltage:** $\approx 5$ to $7\ \text{V}$.
- **Refining of copper electrolyte:** $0.2$ to $0.3\ \text{V}$ at current density of $200$ to $300\ \text{A/m}^2$.
- **Slag density (typical):** $2.5$ to $3.5\ \text{g/cm}^3$ (lighter than molten metal, so it floats).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Smelting and Refining is a **high-weightage** topic in Module 3 because it bridges primary metal extraction with downstream manufacturing. Examiners frequently test: (i) process flow, (ii) chemical reactions, and (iii) the difference between pyrometallurgical, hydrometallurgical, and electrometallurgical routes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Smelting Process — Stepwise Logic

The smelting process is not a single reaction but a carefully sequenced series of physical and chemical events:

1. **Drying and Preheating:** Moisture is driven off from the charge (ore + coke + flux). Endothermic at this stage.
2. **Calcination:** Carbonates and hydroxides decompose to oxides, releasing $\text{CO}_2$ or $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

$$\text{CaCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow$$

3. **Roasting (for sulfide ores):** Sulfide is partially oxidized to oxide, with sulfur dioxide released.

$$2\text{CuFeS}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cu}_2\text{S} \cdot 2\text{FeS} + \text{SO}_2 \uparrow$$

4. **Reduction (the heart of smelting):** The metal oxide is reduced by carbon (coke) or carbon monoxide at high temperature.

$$\text{FeO} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}$$

$$\text{FeO} + \text{CO} \rightarrow \text{Fe} + \text{CO}_2$$

5. **Slag Formation:** Flux (limestone $\text{CaCO}_3$ → $\text{CaO}$) combines with gangue (silica $\text{SiO}_2$, alumina $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) to form fusible slag.

$$\text{CaO} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaSiO}_3\ (\text{slag})$$

6. **Tapping:** Molten metal (heavier) settles at the bottom; molten slag (lighter) floats. Both are tapped separately.

---

## 2.2 Types of Smelting Furnaces (KTU High-Yield)

| Furnace Type | Fuel/Energy | Typical Application | Atmosphere |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Blast Furnace** | Coke (carbon) | Iron (Fe) from $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ | Reducing |
| **Reverberatory Furnace** | Oil / Gas / Pulverized coal | Copper matte from $\text{Cu}_2\text{S} \cdot \text{FeS}$ | Oxidizing/Neutral |
| **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** | Electric arcs (graphite electrodes) | Steelmaking from scrap / pig iron | Neutral/Reducing |
| **Induction Furnace** | Electromagnetic induction | Specialty steels, non-ferrous alloys | Neutral |
| **Cupola Furnace** | Coke | Cast iron (re-melting) | Reducing |
| **Converter (Bessemer / BOF)** | Air / Oxygen blast | Steel from pig iron, Copper matte to blister | Oxidizing |
| **Flash Smelting Furnace** | Oxygen-enriched air | Copper, Nickel concentrates | Oxidizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** KTU examiners commonly ask: *"Differentiate between a blast furnace and a reverberatory furnace"* or *"Why is the blast furnace considered a counter-current reactor?"* The answer hinges on the fact that **hot gases rise upward** while **solid charge descends**, maximizing heat and mass transfer efficiency.

---

## 2.3 The Refining Process — Stepwise Logic

Crude metal from smelting is impure. Refining removes:

- **Dissolved gases** ($\text{H}_2$, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{S}$, $\text{O}_2$ as $\text{SO}_2$)
- **Non-metallic inclusions** (slag particles, oxides)
- **Other metallic impurities** (e.g., Fe, Zn, As, Sb in crude copper)

### 2.3.1 Pyrometallurgical (Fire) Refining

Uses oxidation and reduction cycles at high temperature.

- **Oxidizing stage:** Impurities are oxidized and removed as slag or volatile oxides. Example: In copper fire refining, $\text{Fe}$, $\text{S}$, $\text{Zn}$ are oxidized first because they have a higher affinity for oxygen than copper.

$$2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{FeO} \cdot \text{SiO}_2\ (\text{slag})$$

- **Poling stage:** Green wooden poles are inserted into molten copper. They release hydrocarbons that reduce $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ back to $\text{Cu}$:

$$\text{Cu}_2\text{O} + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{Cu} + \text{CO} \uparrow$$

### 2.3.2 Electrorefining (KTU Favourite)

The impure metal is cast as the **anode**, a thin sheet of pure metal is the **cathode**, and an aqueous salt solution of the metal is the **electrolyte**. When current is passed, pure metal dissolves from the anode and deposits on the cathode; impurities either fall as **anode slime** (noble metals like Au, Ag, Pt) or remain dissolved.

- **Cathode reaction:** $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ (pure metal deposits)
- **Anode reaction:** $\text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$ (impure metal dissolves)

### 2.3.3 Zone Refining

A molten zone is slowly moved along an ingot. Impurities preferentially remain in the molten zone and are swept to one end, which is then cut off. Used for **semiconductor-grade** silicon and germanium.

### 2.3.4 Vacuum Refining and Distillation

Used for metals with high vapor pressure (Zn, Mg, Hg). The metal is vaporized and condensed elsewhere, leaving non-volatile impurities behind.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all key formulas, energy relationships, and boundary conditions you will need for KTU 2024 Scheme end-semester questions on Smelting and Refining.

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mass of metal from ore** | $M_{\text{metal}} = M_{\text{ore}} \times \dfrac{c \times R}{100}$ | Theoretical metal yield from an ore of grade $c\,\%$ and recovery $R\,\%$ | kg or tonnes |
| **Recovery / Yield** | $R = \dfrac{M_{\text{actual}}}{M_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Efficiency of extraction | dimensionless % |
| **Slag basicity ratio** | $B = \dfrac{\%\text{CaO} + \%\text{MgO}}{\%\text{SiO}_2 + \%\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}$ | Used to classify slag as acid or basic | dimensionless |
| **Energy required (theoretical)** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat to raise charge temperature | J or kJ |
| **Latent heat of fusion** | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ | Heat to melt metal at its melting point | J or kJ |
| **Faraday's Law (Electrorefining)** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass deposited at cathode: $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass, $n$ = electrons exchanged, $F = 96485\ \text{C/mol}$ | kg |
| **Current efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical deposit | dimensionless % |
| **Heat of reaction (Hess's Law)** | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = \Sigma \Delta H_f(\text{products}) - \Sigma \Delta H_f(\text{reactants})$ | Enthalpy change of smelting reduction | kJ/mol or kJ/kg |
| **Blast furnace stoichiometry (Fe)** | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$ | Reduction of hematite by carbon monoxide | — |
| **Coke rate (ironmaking)** | $C_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{\text{kg coke}}{\text{tonne hot metal}}$ | Specific coke consumption | kg/tHM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam tip:** Whenever a slag is mentioned, **always state the basicity ratio**. Acidic slag ($B < 1$) is used when gangue is basic (e.g., FeO, MnO); basic slag ($B > 1$) is used when gangue is acidic (e.g., $\text{SiO}_2$). Examiners award marks for this distinction.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Iron and Steel Industry:** Smelting in blast furnaces feeds integrated steel plants producing $>1.8$ billion tonnes/year globally.
- **Non-Ferrous Metallurgy:** Copper, zinc, lead, and nickel recovery is critical for electronics, batteries, and renewable energy hardware.
- **Refining in Electronics:** Electrorefined copper ($99.99\,\%$ pure) is used in high-conductivity wires, PCBs, and chip manufacturing.
- **Zone Refining:** Enables semiconductor-grade silicon ($>99.9999999\,\%$) used in microprocessors and solar cells.
- **Recycling Linkage:** Secondary smelting and refining of scrap (urban mining) is increasingly important in the circular economy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Mass of Iron from Hematite Ore

**Problem Statement:** A hematite ore contains $64\,\%$ $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ and $20\,\%$ $\text{SiO}_2$, with the remainder being moisture and loss-on-ignition. Calculate the **theoretical mass of iron** obtainable from $1000\ \text{kg}$ of ore, assuming complete reduction.

### Step 1 — Identify the Reducing Reaction

The smelting reaction in a blast furnace is:

$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$$

This tells us **1 mole of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ produces 2 moles of Fe**.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Molar Masses

- Molar mass of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$: $2 \times 55.85 + 3 \times 16.00 = 159.7\ \text{g/mol}$
- Molar mass of Fe: $55.85\ \text{g/mol}$

### Step 3 — Compute the Mass of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ in the Ore

From $1000\ \text{kg}$ of ore at $64\,\%$ purity:

$$m_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 1000\ \text{kg} \times 0.64 = 640\ \text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Stoichiometric Ratio

$$\text{If } 159.7\ \text{kg Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2 \times 55.85 = 111.7\ \text{kg Fe}$$

$$\text{Then } 640\ \text{kg Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow \frac{111.7}{159.7} \times 640\ \text{kg Fe}$$

$$m_{\text{Fe}} = \frac{111.7 \times 640}{159.7} = 447.65\ \text{kg}$$

### Step 5 — Express the Result

$$\boxed{m_{\text{Fe (theoretical)}} \approx 447.65\ \text{kg from 1000 kg of ore}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, the **actual yield** is $80$ to $95\,\%$ of this theoretical value due to slag entrainment, incomplete reduction, and tapping losses. The recovery ratio $R$ is therefore a key performance metric for any smelter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Required to Smelt 1 Tonne of Cast Iron

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the total heat energy required to smelt $1000\ \text{kg}$ of cast iron starting from room temperature ($25\ \text{°C}$). Assume: specific heat of iron $c_p = 0.45\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$; melting point $T_m = 1150\ \text{°C}$; latent heat of fusion $L_f = 247\ \text{kJ/kg}$.

### Step 1 — Sensible Heat to Reach Melting Point

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta T = 1150 - 25 = 1125\ \text{°C (or K)}$$

$$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 1000 \times 0.45 \times 1125 = 506{,}250\ \text{kJ}$$

### Step 2 — Latent Heat of Fusion

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f = 1000 \times 247 = 247{,}000\ \text{kJ}$$

### Step 3 — Total Theoretical Heat

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} = 506{,}250 + 247{,}000 = 753{,}250\ \text{kJ}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Furnace Efficiency

For a typical blast furnace, thermal efficiency $\eta_{\text{th}} \approx 50\,\%$ (rest lost as flue gases, wall losses, slag sensible heat). Actual energy required:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\eta_{\text{th}}} = \frac{753{,}250}{0.50} = 1{,}506{,}500\ \text{kJ} \approx 1.51\ \text{GJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{actual}} \approx 1.51 \times 10^6\ \text{kJ per tonne of cast iron}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A modern integrated steel plant consumes roughly $20$ to $25\ \text{GJ/tonne}$ of crude steel when accounting for coke production, sintering, pelletizing, and BOF steelmaking. Our calculation of $1.51\ \text{GJ}$ only covers the melting step itself. Always specify your system boundary in the exam.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Faraday's Law Applied to Copper Electrorefining

**Problem Statement:** An electrorefining cell operates at a current of $5000\ \text{A}$ for $24\ \text{hours}$. Calculate the mass of pure copper deposited at the cathode. Molar mass of Cu = $63.55\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$.

### Step 1 — State Faraday's Law

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$t = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400\ \text{s}$$

$$m = \frac{5000 \times 86{,}400 \times 63.55}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{2.745 \times 10^{10}}{192{,}970} = 142{,}257\ \text{g}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to kg

$$m \approx 142.26\ \text{kg of pure copper per cell per day}$$

### Step 4 — Realistic Adjustments

With current efficiency $\eta = 92\,\%$:

$$m_{\text{actual}} = 142.26 \times 0.92 \approx 130.88\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{actual}} \approx 130.88\ \text{kg Cu per cell per day}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Anode slime recovery:** In the same $24\ \text{h}$, noble metals (Au, Ag, Se, Te) settle as anode slime at the bottom of the cell. This is the primary economic justification for electrorefining — a single cell recovers **both** the pure cathode metal **and** valuable byproducts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Smelting Furnaces

| Parameter | Blast Furnace | Reverberatory Furnace | Electric Arc Furnace | Converter (BOF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Charge** | Iron ore + coke + flux | Roasted matte / ore | Scrap + DRI | Molten pig iron + scrap |
| **Capacity** | Very high (up to $5000\ \text{t/day}$) | Medium ($100$ to $500\ \text{t/day}$) | High ($100$ to $400\ \text{t/heat}$) | High ($300\ \text{t/heat}$) |
| **Fuel/Energy** | Coke | Oil / gas / coal | Electricity (arc) | Oxygen blow |
| **Atmosphere** | Reducing | Neutral/Oxidizing | Neutral | Oxidizing |
| **Product** | Pig iron | Copper matte / blister | Liquid steel | Liquid steel |
| **Refractory** | Carbon + fireclay | Silica / Magnesia / Chromite | Dolomite / Magnesia | Dolomite / Magnesia |
| **Slag basicity** | $B \approx 1.0$ to $1.3$ | $B \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$ (acidic for Cu) | $B \approx 2.0$ to $3.5$ (basic) | $B \approx 3.0$ to $4.0$ (basic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU Part B questions often include comparison-type sub-parts (e.g., *"Compare blast furnace and electric arc furnace in terms of capacity, fuel, and product"*). This matrix is a ready-made answer template.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Iron Smelting Flow

The diagram below maps the complete functional flow of iron ore from raw input to refined steel product, including all major smelting and refining stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawOre[Raw Hematite Ore Fe2O3] --> crushScreen[Crushing and Screening]
    crushScreen --> sintering[Sintering Plant]
    sintering --> chargePrep[Blast Furnace Charge Preparation]
    cokeOven[Coke Oven Coal to Coke] --> chargePrep
    chargePrep --> blastFurnace[Blast Furnace Smelting 1500 C]
    blastFurnace --> pigIron[Pig Iron and Slag Tapping]
    pigIron --> bosConverter[Basic Oxygen Furnace BOF]
    scrap[Steel Scrap] --> bosConverter
    bosConverter --> liquidSteel[Liquid Crude Steel]
    liquidSteel --> ladleRefine[Ladle Furnace Refining]
    ladleRefine --> degasser[Vacuum Degassing RH or VD]
    degasser --> continuousCast[Continuous Casting]
    continuousCast --> hotRoll[Hot Rolling Mill]
    hotRoll --> finishedSteel[Finished Steel Product]
    blastFurnace --> slagGran[Slag Granulation and Cement Use]
    bosConverter --> slagDump[BOF Slag Aggregate Use]
    cokeOven --> coGas[CO Gas Recovery]
    blastFurnace --> topGas[Blast Furnace Top Gas BF Gas]
    coGas --> powerPlant[Power Plant Energy Recovery]
    topGas --> powerPlant
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Copper Smelting and Refining

The diagram below maps the multi-stage extraction of copper from chalcopyrite concentrate to refined cathode copper, with electrorefining and byproduct recovery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    conc[Chalcopyrite Concentrate CuFeS2] --> dryer[Drying and Grinding]
    dryer --> flashSmelt[Flash Smelting Furnace]
    flashSmelt --> matte1[Copper Matte Cu2S FeS 60 percent Cu]
    matte1 --> convBess[Bessemer Converter]
    convBess --> blisterCu[Blister Copper 98 to 99 percent Cu]
    blisterCu --> fireRefine[Fire Refining Furnace]
    fireRefine --> anodeCast[Anode Casting 99 percent Cu]
    anodeCast --> electroCell[Electrorefining Cells]
    electroCell --> cathodeCu[Cathode Copper 99.99 percent Cu]
    electroCell --> anodeSlime[Anode Slime Au Ag Se Te]
    anodeSlime --> preciousMet[Precious Metal Recovery Plant]
    flashSmelt --> slag1[Flash Smelting Slag FeO SiO2]
    slag1 --> slagClean[Slag Cleaning Electric Furnace]
    slagClean --> slagDump1[Clean Slag to Construction]
    convBess --> slag2[Converter Slag Recycle to Flash]
    slag2 --> flashSmelt
    fireRefine --> so2Off[SO2 Off Gas to Sulfuric Acid Plant]
    flashSmelt --> so2Off
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Refining Method Selection Logic

This block diagram provides a decision-tree view of how the appropriate refining method is selected based on the metal's properties and the desired purity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    crudeMetal[Crude Metal from Smelting] --> decide{Target Purity and Metal Type}
    decide -->|Bulk Ferrous Fe and Steel| fireRef1[Fire Refining plus Ladle Metallurgy]
    decide -->|Bulk Non Ferrous Cu Pb Zn| fireRef2[Fire Refining]
    decide -->|High Purity 99.99 percent Cu Al| electroRef[Electrorefining or Electrorefining]
    decide -->|Semiconductor Si Ge| zoneRef[Zone Refining]
    decide -->|Volatile Metals Zn Mg Hg| vacuumRef[Vacuum Distillation]
    decide -->|Precious Metals Au Ag Pt| chemRef[Chemical Refining Aqua Regia]
    fireRef1 --> degass[Vacuum Degassing]
    fireRef2 --> polish[Poling Stage]
    electroRef --> slimeRec[Anode Slime Recovery]
    zoneRef --> multPass[Multiple Passes 5 to 30]
    vacuumRef --> condense[Condensation Collection]
    chemRef --> precip[Selective Precipitation]
```
