# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between a Shaper and a Planer with respect to the moving element and typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving Element** | The **ram (tool)** reciprocates linearly; the workpiece remains stationary on the table. | The **workpiece (table)** reciprocates linearly; the tool remains stationary on the tool head. |
| **Typical Application** | Used for machining small to medium workpieces such as slots, keyways, and small flat surfaces. | Used for machining large workpieces such as machine tool beds, columns, and large flat plates. |
| **Multi-tool Capability** | Single-tool operation. | Multi-tool simultaneous cutting (higher productivity). |

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for each correct contrasting point, 1 mark for application. Total 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the function of a clapper box in a shaper? Why is it essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **clapper box** is a hinged tool holder mounted on the ram of the shaper. Its functions are:

1. **Tool Lifting on Return Stroke:** During the return (idle) stroke, the clapper box pivots upward, lifting the cutting tool clear of the freshly machined surface. **(1 mark)**

2. **Prevention of Rubbing and Tool Wear:** Without the clapper box, the tool would rub against the workpiece during return, causing rapid tool wear, surface scratching, and heat generation. **(1 mark)**

3. **Improved Surface Finish:** By keeping the tool engaged only during the cutting stroke, the clapper box ensures clean chip removal and superior surface integrity. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the Quick-Return Ratio (QRR) of a crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism used in a shaper. (7 marks)**

**(b) A shaper is used to machine a flat surface on a mild steel workpiece of width 150 mm. The stroke length is set at 220 mm, the quick-return ratio is 1.75, and the cutting speed is 18 m/min. If the feed is 0.8 mm/stroke and depth of cut is 2.5 mm, calculate: (i) the number of strokes per minute, (ii) the material removal rate in cm³/min, and (iii) the time required to complete the machining. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the mechanism.
The crank-and-slotted-lever quick-return mechanism converts uniform rotary motion of the crank into variable reciprocating motion of the ram (tool). The cutting stroke is slower (more time), and the return stroke is faster (less time).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the crank angles.
- Cutting stroke corresponds to crank rotation of $(\pi + 2\beta)$ radians.
- Return stroke corresponds to crank rotation of $(\pi - 2\beta)$ radians.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Express the stroke times.
With uniform angular velocity $\omega$:

$$T_c = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\omega} \quad \text{and} \quad T_r = \frac{\pi - 2\beta}{\omega}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Form the QRR definition.
$$QRR = \frac{T_c}{T_r}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Substitute and simplify.
$$QRR = \frac{(\pi + 2\beta)/\omega}{(\pi - 2\beta)/\omega} = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}$$

**Final Answer:** 
$$\boxed{QRR = \frac{\pi + 2\beta}{\pi - 2\beta}}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Workpiece width $W = 150$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 220$ mm
- $QRR = 1.75$
- $V_c = 18$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke
- Depth of cut $d = 2.5$ mm

**(i) Number of strokes per minute (2 marks):**

$$V_c = \frac{2 L N}{1000} \implies N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{2L}$$

$$N = \frac{18 \times 1000}{2 \times 220} = \frac{18000}{440} = 40.91 \approx 41 \text{ strokes/min}$$

**(ii) MRR in cm³/min (3 marks):**

$$MRR = 500 \times V_c \times f \times d \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$MRR = 500 \times 18 \times 0.8 \times 2.5 = 18000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 18 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iii) Time required to complete machining (2 marks):**

The volume to be removed per unit pass:
$$V_{\text{per pass}} = W \times f \times d = 150 \times 0.8 \times 2.5 = 300 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ per stroke (per unit width stripped)}$$

Wait — correction: the total volume to be removed from the full workpiece (assuming we are reducing the entire width to depth $d$):

$$V_{\text{total}} = W \times L_{\text{work}} \times d$$

But in this question, the workpiece width is the width being machined, and the stroke length is 220 mm, so the area to be machined per pass is $W \times L = 150 \times 220 = 33000$ mm², and we are removing a depth of $d = 2.5$ mm.

Total volume to remove:
$$V_{\text{total}} = 150 \times 220 \times 2.5 = 82500 \text{ mm}^3$$

Time:
$$T = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{MRR} = \frac{82500}{18000} = 4.58 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 4.58 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]

**Q: (a) Explain the principle of surface grinding with a neat sketch. List the various types of surface grinding operations. (7 marks)**

**(b) During a surface grinding operation, the grinding wheel has a diameter of 250 mm and rotates at 1500 rpm. The workpiece is fed longitudinally at 5 m/min, the depth of cut is 0.02 mm, and the width of grinding is 30 mm. Calculate the material removal rate (MRR) in mm³/min and the specific energy consumption if the tangential cutting force is 25 N. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle of Surface Grinding (4 marks):**

Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process in which a **rotating grinding wheel** (bonded abrasive) removes material from a flat workpiece surface. The workpiece is mounted on a **reciprocating table** (or rotary table for creep-feed grinding) and passes beneath the wheel. A **coolant** is flooded over the grinding zone to:
- Reduce heat generation
- Flush away chips
- Improve surface finish
- Extend wheel life

The grinding wheel rotates at high peripheral speed (typically **20–35 m/s**), and the material is removed by the **micro-cutting action** of thousands of abrasive grits on the wheel periphery. Each grit acts as a miniature single-point cutting tool.

**Types of Surface Grinding (3 marks):**
1. **Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table Grinder** — wheel axis horizontal, table reciprocates.
2. **Vertical Spindle Reciprocating Table Grinder** — wheel axis vertical, table reciprocates.
3. **Horizontal Spindle Rotary Table Grinder** — wheel axis horizontal, table rotates.
4. **Vertical Spindle Rotary Table Grinder** — wheel axis vertical, table rotates (used for creep-feed).
5. **Creep-Feed Grinding** — very low feed, very high depth of cut (single pass).

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Wheel diameter $D = 250$ mm
- Wheel speed $N_w = 1500$ rpm
- Workpiece feed $v_w = 5$ m/min $= 5000$ mm/min
- Depth of cut $a = 0.02$ mm
- Width of grinding $b = 30$ mm
- Tangential cutting force $F_t = 25$ N

**(i) Material Removal Rate (4 marks):**

$$MRR = v_w \times a \times b$$

$$MRR = 5000 \times 0.02 \times 30$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 3000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(ii) Specific Energy (3 marks):**

First, compute the wheel peripheral speed:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi \times D \times N_w}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1500}{1000} = 1178.1 \text{ m/min}$$

Power consumed:
$$P = \frac{F_t \times V_s}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{25 \times 1178.1}{60000} = 0.491 \text{ kW}$$

Specific energy:
$$U = \frac{P \times 60}{MRR} = \frac{0.491 \times 60}{3000} \times 1000 = 9.82 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

(Or directly: $U = F_t \times v_w / MRR$ if we consider the cutting force component...)

$$\boxed{U \approx 9.82 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a typical specific energy value for grinding mild steel (typical range: **10–50 J/mm³**), confirming the calculation is physically reasonable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The single most common mistake in shaping/grinding numericals is forgetting to convert **mm³/min to cm³/min** (divide by 1000) or **m/min to mm/s** (multiply by 1000/60). Always write down the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Quick-Return Ratio Direction:** Some students compute $T_r / T_c$ instead of $T_c / T_r$. By **KTU convention**, QRR = Cutting Stroke Time / Return Stroke Time, which is **always greater than 1**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $V_c$ with $v_w$:** In grinding, $V_c$ (or $V_s$) refers to wheel peripheral speed, while $v_w$ refers to workpiece feed velocity. Mixing these up leads to dimensionally wrong answers.
> 
> 4. **Stroke Length vs. Workpiece Width:** The stroke length $L$ is set longer than the workpiece width $W$ to allow tool entry and exit. A common formula is $L = (1.3 \text{ to } 1.5) \times W$. Forgetting this clearance causes incomplete machining.
> 
> 5. **Missing Clapper Box Explanation:** In "Explain the working of a shaper" questions, examiners often allocate **1–2 marks specifically for the clapper box**. Skipping it costs easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Shaping and Finishing (Module 3):**

### A. Shaping Machine Fundamentals
- [ ] Shaper is a **reciprocating-type machine tool** using **single-point cutting tool**.
- [ ] Three motions: **Primary (cutting stroke), Secondary (cross feed), Tertiary (depth of cut)**.
- [ ] **Crank-and-slotted-lever** is the most common quick-return mechanism.
- [ ] **Quick-Return Ratio** $QRR = (\pi + 2\beta) / (\pi - 2\beta)$ — always greater than 1.
- [ ] **Clapper box** lifts the tool on return stroke — prevents rubbing and tool wear.
- [ ] **Cutting speed** $V_c = 2LN/1000$ m/min (where $L$ is stroke length in mm, $N$ is strokes/min).
- [ ] **MRR** $= 500 \times V_c \times f \times d$ mm³/min.

### B. Planer vs. Shaper vs. Slotter
- [ ] **Planer**: Workpiece reciprocates; multi-tool; for large workpieces.
- [ ] **Shaper**: Tool reciprocates; single-tool; for small to medium workpieces.
- [ ] **Slotter**: Vertical ram; for internal keyways, slots, and contours.

### C. Finishing Processes
- [ ] **Grinding**: Bonded abrasive wheel; multi-point cutting; high precision; uses coolant.
- [ ] **Honing**: Bonded stones with oscillating motion; for cylindrical bores; improves roundness.
- [ ] **Lapping**: Loose abrasive in carrier fluid; ultra-high precision; for sealing surfaces.
- [ ] **Superfinishing**: Fine stones with low pressure oscillation; lowest $R_a$ achievable mechanically.
- [ ] **Polishing**: Uses soft wheels and fine abrasives; mirror finish; no dimensional change.
- [ ] **Buffing**: Similar to polishing; uses softer wheels; for decorative finish.

### D. Key Numerical Formulas
- [ ] $QRR = (\pi + 2\beta)/(\pi - 2\beta)$ (derivable).
- [ ] $V_c = 2LN/1000$ m/min.
- [ ] $MRR = 500 V_c f d$ mm³/min (shaping).
- [ ] $MRR = v_w \times a \times b$ mm³/min (grinding).
- [ ] $G = V_w / V_s$ (grinding ratio).
- [ ] $R_a = f^2 / 8R$ (theoretical surface roughness).
- [ ] $U = P / MRR$ (specific energy).

### E. Standard Surface Roughness Values
- [ ] Shaping: $R_a$ = **1.6 to 6.3 μm**.
- [ ] Grinding: $R_a$ = **0.1 to 1.6 μm**.
- [ ] Honing: $R_a$ = **0.1 to 0.8 μm**.
- [ ] Lapping: $R_a$ = **0.05 to 0.4 μm**.
- [ ] Superfinishing: $R_a$ = **0.025 to 0.2 μm**.
- [ ] Polishing: $R_a$ = **0.0125 to 0.1 μm**.

### F. Examiner's Pet Topics (Year-on-Year High-Weightage)
- [ ] QRR derivation (asked almost every semester).
- [ ] Shaper vs. Planer vs. Slotter comparison.
- [ ] Clapper box function.
- [ ] MRR and cutting speed numericals.
- [ ] Surface grinding sketch and MRR calculation.
- [ ] Honing vs. Lapping distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Alloying ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Alloying** is the process of combining two or more elements, where at least one of them is a metal, to produce a new metallic material (an **alloy**) possessing properties superior to those of its constituent elements. The resulting alloy exhibits a unique combination of **mechanical**, **physical**, **chemical**, and **metallurgical** properties that are not achievable by any single pure metal. In the context of the **Fe–C system** (the backbone of modern manufacturing), alloying refers to the deliberate addition of controlled quantities of **alloying elements** such as Manganese (Mn), Chromium (Cr), Nickel (Ni), Molybdenum (Mo), Vanadium (V), Tungsten (W), Silicon (Si), Copper (Cu), Phosphorus (P), and Sulphur (S) into molten or solid-state iron to engineer specific engineering performance characteristics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > An *alloy* is a homogeneous or heterogeneous mixture of two or more elements, with at least one being a metal, possessing metallic properties. The mixture can be either **substitutional** (atoms replace host lattice atoms) or **interstitial** (small atoms occupy the interstitial voids of the host lattice). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe for a Superhero Cake" 🍰 Imagine you are baking a **plain flour cake** (pure metal — say, pure iron). It is **soft, bland, and structurally weak** — just like pure iron, which is too soft for any real engineering load. Now, you begin adding **ingredients (alloying elements)** to enhance specific properties: | Ingredient (Alloy Element) | Purpose (Property Enhanced) | |---|---| | 🍫 Cocoa powder (Carbon) | Increases **hardness** and **strength** | | 🥛 Milk (Manganese) | Improves **toughness** and **deoxidizes** during casting | | 🧈 Butter (Chromium) | Provides **corrosion resistance** and **wear resistance** | | 🍯 Honey (Nickel) | Adds **toughness** and **ductility** at low temperatures | | 🌰 Nuts (Molybdenum) | Boosts **high-temperature strength** and **creep resistance** | Just like a baker carefully measures each ingredient to bake a cake with the *exact* desired texture, a metallurgist precisely controls alloying composition to forge a metal with the *exact* desired mechanical behavior. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Alloying is essentially *materials engineering at the atomic level*. Each added element modifies the parent metal's crystal lattice, electron cloud, grain structure, or phase composition to deliver properties tailored for a specific engineering application (e.g., aircraft turbine blades, marine propellers, automotive crankshafts, surgical implants). ### 1.3 Why Do We Need Alloying? — Five Critical Engineering Reasons 1. **Strengthening Mechanism:** Pure metals have poor mechanical strength. Alloying introduces lattice strain, grain refinement, and precipitation hardening. 2. **Corrosion & Oxidation Resistance:** Elements like **Cr, Ni, Al, Si** form protective oxide layers (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel). 3. **Improved Hardenability:** Elements like **Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn** slow the transformation of austenite, enabling deeper hardening during heat treatment. 4. **High-Temperature Performance:** **Mo, W, V, Co** retain strength at elevated temperatures (crucial for gas turbines, boilers). 5. **Special Physical Properties:** **Si** increases magnetic permeability; **Mn** modifies electrical resistivity; **Ni** lowers the thermal expansion coefficient (Invar alloy: 36% Ni in Fe). > [!TIP] > **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:** > - **Hardenability** is measured in **Jominy Distance (mm)** — measured as the distance from the quenched end where hardness equals **50 HRC** (or a designated Rockwell C value). > - **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is a dimensionless number used to predict weldability of steels. > - **wt%** and **at%** are the standard composition units. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Alloying Elements (KTU High-Yield Knowledge) Alloying elements in steel can be broadly classified into **three categories** based on their effect on the **Fe–C (Iron–Carbon) phase diagram**: #### Category A — Austenite Stabilizers (γ-Formers) These elements **lower the critical temperature** and **expand the austenite (γ-FCC) phase field**. - **Nickel (Ni)**, **Manganese (Mn)**, **Cobalt (Co)**, **Copper (Cu)** > [!NOTE] > With sufficient Ni (>8%) or Mn (>12%), the austenite phase becomes **stable at room temperature**, producing **austenitic stainless steels** (e.g., 18-8 stainless: 18% Cr, 8% Ni). #### Category B — Ferrite Stabilizers (α-Formers) These elements **raise the critical temperature** and **expand the ferrite (α-BCC) phase field**, often forming a closed γ-loop. - **Chromium (Cr)**, **Silicon (Si)**, **Molybdenum (Mo)**, **Vanadium (V)**, **Tungsten (W)**, **Titanium (Ti)**, **Aluminum (Al)**, **Phosphorus (P)** #### Category C — Carbide Formers Elements with strong affinity for carbon; they form **hard, stable carbides** dispersed in the matrix. | Strength of Carbide Formation | Elements | |---|---| | **Weak** | Mn, Fe | | **Moderate** | Cr, W, Mo | | **Strong** | V, Ti, Nb, Zr | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strong carbide formers (V, Ti, Nb)** form ultra-hard, thermally stable carbides like **VC, TiC, NbC**. These are used in **high-speed steels (HSS)** like M2, M42, T1 grades for cutting tools. --- ### 2.2 Role of Individual Alloying Elements — The "Element Cards" 📋 > [!NOTE] > This is the **most frequently asked section** in KTU Module 3 examinations. Memorize at least 8 of these. | Element | Symbol | Primary Function | Typical wt% Range | KTU Application Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Carbon** | C | Primary hardening agent; increases strength & hardness | 0.05 – 2.0 | Plain carbon steels (AISI 1020, 1045) | | **Manganese** | Mn | Deoxidizer; increases hardenability; sulfide former (MnS) | 0.3 – 1.5 | Structural steels, rails | | **Silicon** | Si | Deoxidizer; increases strength; improves magnetic properties | 0.2 – 2.5 | Electrical steels (transformer cores) | | **Chromium** | Cr | Corrosion resistance (Cr₂O₃ film); increases hardenability; carbide former | 0.5 – 30 | Stainless steels (304, 410), ball bearings (52100) | | **Nickel** | Ni | Toughness, ductility, low-temp impact strength; austenite stabilizer | 0.5 – 35 | Austenitic SS (304), Invar alloy, maraging steels | | **Molybdenum** | Mo | High-temp strength, creep resistance; reduces temper brittleness | 0.2 – 5 | Boiler steels, HSS, armor plates | | **Vanadium** | V | Strong carbide former; grain refiner; secondary hardening | 0.1 – 2 | HSS (M2), springs, crankshafts | | **Tungsten** | W | Extreme hot hardness; strong carbide | 1 – 18 | HSS (T1), die steels, filaments | | **Phosphorus** | P | Strengthens ferrite; improves machinability | 0.02 – 0.1 | Free-cutting steels (AISI 12L14) | | **Sulphur** | S | Improves machinability (forms MnS inclusions) | 0.02 – 0.35 | Free-machining steels | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — "Board-Exam Survival Kit" 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **direct high-yield items** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the structure and boundary conditions. | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Boundary / Engineering Note | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula)** | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | All compositions in **wt%** | CE ≤ 0.55 → easily weldable; CE > 0.65 → requires preheating | | 2 | **Pellini's Cracking Susceptibility Index** | $P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{15} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Co}{5} + \dfrac{B}{0.5}$ | wt% | Used for low-carbon HSLA steels | | 3 | **Austenite / Ferrite Phase Fraction (Lever Rule)** | $W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{C_{\gamma} - C_{0}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}}$ | $W_{\alpha}$ = mass fraction; $C_{0}$ = alloy C content; $C_{\gamma}$, $C_{\alpha}$ = C% in γ and α at given T | Applied at any tie-line in Fe–C diagram | | 4 | **Rule of Mixtures (Substitutional Alloy — Linear Property)** | $P_{alloy} = P_{A} \cdot X_{A} + P_{B} \cdot X_{B}$ | $P$ = property; $X$ = atom fraction | Only valid for **ideal solutions** with no atomic interaction | | 5 | **Solid Solution Strengthening (Labusch Model)** | $\Delta \tau \propto G \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot c^{1/2}$ | $G$ = shear modulus; $\varepsilon$ = misfit strain; $c$ = solute concentration | Quantitative basis of **solid-solution hardening** | | 6 | **Austenite-to-Martensite Start Temperature (Andrews Empirical)** | $M_{s} \left(^{\circ}C\right) = 512 - 453C - 16.9Ni - 30Mn - 9.5Mo + 217(C)^{2} - 71.5(C \cdot Mn)$ | Compositions in wt% | Predicts quench-induced martensite formation | | 7 | **Austenite Grain Size Growth (Beck Equation)** | $D^{2} - D_{0}^{2} = K \cdot t^{n} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | $D$ = grain diameter; $t$ = time; $Q$ = activation energy; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Valid for isothermal grain growth | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Memorizing Carbon Equivalent Coefficients:** > *"**M**an has **6** sides, **C**hromium-**M**o-**V** has **5**, and **N**ickel-**C**opper has **15** — remember M6, C5, N15."* ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** formula is the **most critical equation** a production engineer learns in Module 3 because it directly determines: - **Welding procedure selection** — whether pre-heating, post-weld heat treatment (PWHT), or low-hydrogen electrodes are mandatory. - **Heat-affected zone (HAZ) cracking risk** in fabricated structures like pressure vessels, bridges, and ship hulls. - **Cold-cracking susceptibility** during arc welding. The **Ms-temperature formula** is vital in designing **quench-hardened components** (gears, shafts, dies) and in selecting **austempering / martempering** heat-treatment cycles. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Phase Fraction in the Fe–C Eutectoid System (Lever Rule) Consider a hypoeutectoid steel containing **0.4 wt% C** cooled slowly to **just above the eutectoid temperature (727 °C)**. At this temperature, the alloy consists of two phases: - **Ferrite (α)** with carbon solubility = **0.022 wt% C** - **Austenite (γ)** with carbon solubility = **0.77 wt% C** (eutectoid composition) We wish to find the **mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite** and **austenite** at this temperature. #### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction: **Step 1 — Identify the tie-line endpoints.** The horizontal tie-line at 727 °C connects the two single-phase regions (α and γ). The carbon content in each phase at equilibrium is read directly from the phase diagram: - $C_{\alpha} = 0.022 \text{ wt\%}$ - $C_{\gamma} = 0.77 \text{ wt\%}$ - Overall alloy composition: $C_{0} = 0.40 \text{ wt\%}$ **Step 2 — Apply mass conservation.** The total carbon in the alloy equals the weighted sum of carbon in each phase: $$ m_{total} \cdot C_{0} = m_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\alpha} + m_{\gamma} \cdot C_{\gamma} $$ **Step 3 — Introduce the mass fraction constraint.** Let $W_{\alpha}$ and $W_{\gamma}$ be the mass fractions of ferrite and austenite, respectively, such that: $$ W_{\alpha} + W_{\gamma} = 1 $$ Substituting $W_{\gamma} = 1 - W_{\alpha}$ into the conservation equation: $$ C_{0} = W_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\alpha} + (1 - W_{\alpha}) \cdot C_{\gamma} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange algebraically.** $$ C_{0} = W_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\alpha} + C_{\gamma} - W_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\gamma} $$ $$ W_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\alpha} - W_{\alpha} \cdot C_{\gamma} = C_{0} - C_{\gamma} $$ $$ W_{\alpha} \cdot (C_{\alpha} - C_{\gamma}) = C_{0} - C_{\gamma} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the ferrite fraction.** $$ W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{C_{\gamma} - C_{0}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for the austenite fraction (by symmetry).** $$ W_{\gamma} = \dfrac{C_{0} - C_{\alpha}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}} $$ **Step 7 — Plug in numerical values.** $$ W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{0.77 - 0.40}{0.77 - 0.022} $$ $$ W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{0.37}{0.748} $$ $$ W_{\alpha} = 0.4946 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \approx 49.5\% $$ $$ W_{\gamma} = 1 - 0.4946 = 0.5054 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \approx 50.5\% $$ **Step 8 — Interpretation.** Just above the eutectoid temperature, **49.5% of the microstructure is proeutectoid ferrite** and **50.5% is austenite** (which itself will transform to pearlite on further cooling through 727 °C). > [!NOTE] > **Geometric "Lever" Analogy:** Place a fulcrum at the alloy composition ($C_0$). The lever arms are inversely proportional to the mass fractions. The mass fraction of any phase is proportional to the *opposite* segment of the tie-line. This is why it is called the **lever rule** — it is mathematically identical to a mechanical lever in rotational equilibrium. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Problem: Carbon Equivalent Calculation **Problem (KTU-Style):** A low-alloy steel has the following composition: **C = 0.18%, Mn = 1.30%, Cr = 0.50%, Mo = 0.25%, V = 0.05%, Ni = 0.30%, Cu = 0.25%** (all in wt%). Determine: - (a) The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** using the IIW formula. - (b) The **weldability assessment** based on CE. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Recall the IIW formula.** $$ CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.** $$ CE = 0.18 + \dfrac{1.30}{6} + \dfrac{0.50 + 0.25 + 0.05}{5} + \dfrac{0.30 + 0.25}{15} $$ **Step 3 — Compute each term individually.** - Carbon term: $0.18$ - Manganese term: $\dfrac{1.30}{6} = 0.2167$ - Carbide-former term: $\dfrac{0.80}{5} = 0.1600$ - Austenite-former term: $\dfrac{0.55}{15} = 0.0367$ **Step 4 — Sum the terms.** $$ CE = 0.1800 + 0.2167 + 0.1600 + 0.0367 $$ $$ CE = 0.5934 $$ **Step 5 — Weldability verdict.** Since **CE = 0.5934 > 0.55**, this steel lies in the **"preheating recommended"** zone. The standard practice is: - Pre-heat to **150 – 200 °C** - Use **low-hydrogen electrodes** (e.g., E7018) - Consider **PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment)** to relieve residual stresses. > [!TIP] > **Critical Threshold Table for KTU:** | CE Value | Weldability Verdict | Required Action | |---|---|---| | CE ≤ 0.40 | Excellent | No preheating needed | | 0.40 < CE ≤ 0.55 | Good | Low preheat (75 – 100 °C) | | 0.55 < CE ≤ 0.65 | Fair | Mandatory preheat (150 – 200 °C) | | CE > 0.65 | Poor | Special procedures + PWHT | --- ### 3.3 Numerical Problem: Ms Temperature Prediction **Problem:** A gear steel has composition **C = 0.45%, Mn = 0.80%, Ni = 1.50%, Mo = 0.30%**. Predict the **martensite start temperature (Ms)** using the Andrews empirical equation. **Step 1 — Recall the formula.** $$ M_{s} \left(^{\circ}C\right) = 512 - 453C - 16.9Ni - 30Mn - 9.5Mo + 217(C)^{2} - 71.5(C \cdot Mn) $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ M_{s} = 512 - 453(0.45) - 16.9(1.5) - 30(0.80) - 9.5(0.30) + 217(0.45)^{2} - 71.5(0.45)(0.80) $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate each term.** - $453 \times 0.45 = 203.85$ - $16.9 \times 1.5 = 25.35$ - $30 \times 0.80 = 24.00$ - $9.5 \times 0.30 = 2.85$ - $217 \times (0.45)^{2} = 217 \times 0.2025 = 43.9425$ - $71.5 \times 0.45 \times 0.80 = 71.5 \times 0.36 = 25.74$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the main equation.** $$ M_{s} = 512 - 203.85 - 25.35 - 24.00 - 2.85 + 43.9425 - 25.74 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the final result.** $$ M_{s} = 512 - 203.85 = 308.15 $$ $$ M_{s} = 308.15 - 25.35 = 282.80 $$ $$ M_{s} = 282.80 - 24.00 = 258.80 $$ $$ M_{s} = 258.80 - 2.85 = 255.95 $$ $$ M_{s} = 255.95 + 43.9425 = 299.8925 $$ $$ M_{s} = 299.8925 - 25.74 = 274.15 \;^{\circ}C $$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** With $M_{s} \approx 274 \;^{\circ}C$, the steel will start forming martensite at this temperature during rapid quenching. This means the steel can be **fully hardened** in oil or polymer quenchants (water quenching risks cracking due to high thermal stresses). --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Alloy Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ALLOYING IN METALLURGY] --> B[BASE METAL] A --> C[ALLOYING ELEMENTS] A --> D[STRUCTURAL OUTCOME] B --> B1[Iron - Fe] B --> B2[Aluminum - Al] B --> B3[Copper - Cu] B --> B4[Titanium - Ti] B --> B5[Magnesium - Mg] C --> E[AUSTENITE STABILIZERS] C --> F[FERRITE STABILIZERS] C --> G[CARBIDE FORMERS] E --> E1[Nickel - Ni] E --> E2[Manganese - Mn] E --> E3[Cobalt - Co] E --> E4[Copper - Cu] F --> F1[Chromium - Cr] F --> F2[Silicon - Si] F --> F3[Molybdenum - Mo] F --> F4[Vanadium - V] F --> F5[Tungsten - W] F --> F6[Aluminum - Al] G --> G1[Weak - Mn Fe] G --> G2[Moderate - Cr W Mo] G --> G3[Strong - V Ti Nb Zr] D --> H[FCC PHASES] D --> I[BCC PHASES] D --> J[INTERMETALLICS] D --> K[CARBIDES] H --> H1[Austenitic SS - 304 316] I --> I1[Ferritic SS - 430] J --> J1[NiAl Cu3Au Ni3Al] K --> K1[Fe3C M7C3 M23C6 VC TiC] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the root node **ALLOYING IN METALLURGY**, which branches into **BASE METALS**, **ALLOYING ELEMENTS** (categorized by their phase-stabilizing role), and the resulting **STRUCTURAL OUTCOMES**. This map is exactly the kind of hierarchical classification examiners love to test. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Effect of Alloying Elements on Fe–C Phase Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Pure Fe-C Diagram
Eutectoid at 727 C
0.77 percent C] --> B1[Add Ni Mn Cu - Austenite Stabilizers] A1 --> B2[Add Cr Si Mo V - Ferrite Stabilizers] A1 --> B3[Add Cr V Ti Mo - Carbide Formers] B1 --> C1[Gamma region EXPANDS
Austenite stable at RT
Austenitic Stainless Steel] B2 --> C2[Alpha region EXPANDS
Gamma loop may close
Ferritic Stainless Steel] B3 --> C3[Fe3C REPLACED by MC M7C3
Hard carbides
High Speed Tool Steels] C1 --> D1[304 - 18Cr 8Ni
Non magnetic formable] C2 --> D2[430 - 17Cr
Magnetic corrosion resistant] C3 --> D3[M2 HSS - 6W 5Mo 4Cr 2V
Hot hardness to 600 C] ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Step-by-Step Alloying Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[STEP 1 - SELECT BASE METAL] --> P2[STEP 2 - DEFINE TARGET PROPERTIES] P2 --> P3[STEP 3 - CHOOSE ALLOYING ELEMENTS] P3 --> P4[STEP 4 - CALCULATE COMPOSITION using CE Ms Formulas] P4 --> P5[STEP 5 - MELTING in EAF or Induction Furnace] P5 --> P6[STEP 6 - DEOXIDIZATION with Si Mn Al] P6 --> P7[STEP 7 - SECONDARY REFINING LF or VD] P7 --> P8[STEP 8 - CASTING Continuous or Ingot] P8 --> P9[STEP 9 - HOT WORKING Rolling or Forging] P9 --> P10[STEP 10 - HEAT TREATMENT Quench Temper Anneal] P10 --> P11[STEP 11 - TESTING Tensile Impact Hardness] P11 --> P12[STEP 12 - FINAL COMPONENT] ``` > [!TIP] > **Production Engineering Insight:** Notice that alloying is **NOT just about composition** — Steps 5–10 (refining, casting, hot working, heat treatment) collectively determine the *final* microstructure and properties. A perfect chemistry with poor processing produces a defective alloy. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define the term "alloy". Distinguish between **substitutional** and **interstitial** solid solutions with one engineering example each.* **Model Answer (Examiner-Approved Structure):** **Definition (1 Mark):** An **alloy** is a homogeneous or heterogeneous mixture of two or more elements, with at least one being a metal, exhibiting metallic properties. Example: Steel (Fe + C), Brass (Cu + Zn). **Distinction Table (2 Marks):** | Parameter | Substitutional Solid Solution | Interstitial Solid Solution | |---|---|---| | Atomic size difference | < 15% (Hume-Rothery rule) | Very small atoms (< 41% of host radius) | | Position of solute | Replaces host atoms on lattice sites | Occupies interstitial voids | | Maximum solubility | Often unlimited | Always limited | | Examples | Cu–Ni, Cu–Zn (Brass), Au–Cu | Fe–C (austenite), Fe–N, Ti–O | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *List any **three roles of Manganese (Mn)** as an alloying element in steel. What is the typical range of Mn content in plain carbon steels?* **Model Answer:** Mn serves **three critical roles** in steelmaking: 1. **Deoxidizer (0.3 – 0.5 wt%):** Manganese reacts with dissolved oxygen in molten steel to form MnO, which floats off as slag. This prevents **gas porosity** and **FeO inclusions**. $$ \text{Mn} + \text{FeO} \rightarrow \text{MnO} + \text{Fe} $$ 2. **Sulfide Former:** Mn combines with harmful **sulphur** to form **MnS inclusions**, which are softer and more machinable than FeS. FeS causes **hot shortness** (cracking during hot rolling). $$ \text{Mn} + \text{FeS} \rightarrow \text{MnS} + \text{Fe} $$ 3. **Hardenability Booster (0.5 – 1.5 wt%):** Mn delays the austenite-to-pearlite transformation, allowing deeper hardening during quenching. **Typical Mn range in plain carbon steel: 0.30 – 0.80 wt%** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of alloying elements** in steel based on their effect on the **Fe–C phase diagram**. Discuss **austenite stabilizers, ferrite stabilizers, and carbide formers** with suitable examples and their influence on microstructure. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Alloying elements in steel are classified into three main categories based on how they shift the boundaries of the γ (austenite) region in the Fe–C phase diagram: **1. Austenite Stabilizers (γ-Formers) — 2.5 Marks** These elements **expand the austenite (γ-FCC) field** and **lower the eutectoid temperature**. They are characterized by an FCC crystal structure in their elemental form and similar atomic radii to γ-iron. - **Nickel (Ni):** Most widely used austenite stabilizer. At 8% Ni, the austenite phase is stable even at room temperature, giving rise to **austenitic stainless steels (e.g., AISI 304: 18Cr–8Ni)**. Ni also imparts **toughness at cryogenic temperatures**, making it indispensable for **LNG tanks** and **Arctic pipelines**. - **Manganese (Mn):** Strong austenite stabilizer. At >12% Mn, Hadfield steel (12% Mn) becomes fully austenitic and exhibits extraordinary **work-hardening** under impact — used in railway frogs, rock crushers, and armor plate. - **Cobalt and Copper** are also austenite formers, used in special-purpose alloys. **2. Ferrite Stabilizers (α-Formers) — 2.5 Marks** These elements **contract the γ-region**, often forming a closed γ-loop, and **promote BCC ferrite** even at elevated temperatures. - **Chromium (Cr):** Most industrially important. >12% Cr produces a passive **Cr₂O₃** film on the surface, conferring **stainless** properties (AISI 430 is ferritic SS with 17% Cr). Cr also shifts the eutectoid composition toward **lower carbon** values. - **Silicon (Si):** Used in electrical steels (3% Si) for transformer laminations due to its high magnetic permeability and low hysteresis loss. - **Molybdenum, Vanadium, Tungsten, Titanium, Aluminum** also stabilize ferrite. **Mo (0.5%)** dramatically improves **creep resistance** in boiler and turbine steels. **3. Carbide Formers — 2 Marks** Elements with strong carbon affinity form hard, refractory carbides dispersed in the ferrite matrix. The order of carbide-forming tendency is: $$ \text{Ti, Nb, V} > \text{Mo, W, Cr} > \text{Mn, Fe} $$ - **Strong carbide formers** (V, Ti, Nb) form **MC-type carbides (VC, TiC, NbC)** that are extremely hard and thermally stable, providing **secondary hardening** during tempering of high-speed steels. - **Moderate formers** (Cr, Mo, W) form **M₂₃C₆, M₆C, M₇C₃ carbides**, which contribute to wear resistance in tool and die steels. - These carbides obstruct dislocation motion and grain growth, thereby enhancing **hot hardness** and **wear resistance**. > [!NOTE] > **Closing Statement (Bonus Mark):** The synergistic combination of these element classes — e.g., 18% Cr (ferrite stabilizer) + 8% Ni (austenite stabilizer) — produces the balanced **austenitic-ferritic (duplex) microstructures** used in marine and chemical-plant applications. --- **(b)** A low-alloy steel for a pressure vessel has the composition **C = 0.22%, Mn = 1.40%, Cr = 0.60%, Mo = 0.30%, V = 0.10%, Ni = 0.40%, Cu = 0.20%**. Calculate the **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** and **Pellini Index ($P_{cm}$)**. Comment on the weldability and suggest a suitable welding procedure. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula).** $$ CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ Substituting: $$ CE = 0.22 + \dfrac{1.40}{6} + \dfrac{0.60 + 0.30 + 0.10}{5} + \dfrac{0.40 + 0.20}{15} $$ $$ CE = 0.22 + 0.2333 + 0.2000 + 0.0400 $$ $$ \boxed{CE = 0.6933} $$ **Step 2 — Pellini Index.** $$ P_{cm} = C + \dfrac{Si}{30} + \dfrac{Mn + Cu}{20} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{15} + \dfrac{Ni}{60} + \dfrac{Co}{5} + \dfrac{B}{0.5} $$ Since Si, Co, B are not specified (≈ 0): $$ P_{cm} = 0.22 + \dfrac{1.40 + 0.20}{20} + \dfrac{0.60 + 0.30 + 0.10}{15} + \dfrac{0.40}{60} $$ $$ P_{cm} = 0.22 + 0.0800 + 0.0667 + 0.0067 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{cm} = 0.3734} $$ **Step 3 — Weldability Verdict.** - $CE = 0.6933 > 0.65$ → **Poor weldability** by IIW standard. - $P_{cm} = 0.3734 > 0.35$ → **High hydrogen-cracking susceptibility** by Pellini standard. **Recommended Welding Procedure (1 Mark):** 1. **Pre-heat to 175 – 200 °C** and maintain interpass temperature. 2. Use **low-hydrogen basic-coated electrodes** (e.g., AWS E7018, E8018-B2 for low-alloy service). 3. Apply **Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)** at **600 – 650 °C for 1 hour per inch of thickness** to relieve residual stresses and temper the HAZ. 4. Control **hydrogen input** strictly (electrodes baked at 350 °C for 1 hour before use). 5. Use **stringer beads** (not wide weaving) to minimize HAZ width. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **role of Chromium, Nickel, and Molybdenum** as alloying elements in steel. State the composition and key applications of **any two grades of stainless steel**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Role of Chromium (Cr) — 1.5 Marks** - Cr is the **most important alloying element** for corrosion resistance. At >10.5% Cr, a **passive Cr₂O₃ film** forms on the surface, which self-repairs in oxygen-rich environments. - Cr is a **strong ferrite stabilizer** and **moderate carbide former**, forming **Cr₂₃C₆** carbides. - Cr increases **hardenability** and **high-temperature oxidation resistance**. - Application: Ball-bearing steel AISI 52100 (1.5% Cr), die steels (12% Cr). **Role of Nickel (Ni) — 1.5 Marks** - Ni is an **austenite stabilizer** that imparts **toughness, ductility**, and **impact resistance**, especially at **low temperatures** (down to –196 °C). - Ni improves **corrosion resistance** in acidic and chloride environments when combined with Cr. - Ni refines grain size and improves **fatigue strength**. - Application: Invar alloy (36% Ni) — near-zero thermal expansion; used in precision instruments, clocks, and bimetal thermostats. **Role of Molybdenum (Mo) — 1.5 Marks** - Mo imparts **high-temperature strength** and **creep resistance** due to its stable carbides (Mo₂C, Mo₆C). - Mo **counteracts temper brittleness** caused by P and Sn in alloy steels. - Mo improves **pitting corrosion resistance** in chloride-containing environments (seawater). - Application: Boiler tubes (1% Mo), HSS M2 (5% Mo), pressure vessels. **Stainless Steel Grades — 2.5 Marks** **Grade 1 — AISI 304 (Austenitic SS)** - Composition: **18% Cr + 8% Ni + 0.08% C max** (commonly called "18-8 stainless") - Structure: Fully austenitic (FCC), **non-magnetic** - Properties: Excellent corrosion resistance, good formability, weldability - Applications: Kitchen sinks, food-processing equipment, chemical tanks, architectural panels **Grade 2 — AISI 410 (Martensitic SS)** - Composition: **12% Cr + 0.15% C max** - Structure: Martensitic (BCT) after quenching — **magnetic and hardenable** - Properties: Moderate corrosion resistance, high hardness (up to 40 HRC), good wear resistance - Applications: Cutlery, surgical instruments, valve parts, steam turbine blades --- **(b)** Describe the **heat treatment cycles** for the following alloys and explain how alloying modifies the **TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram**: (i) **Plain carbon steel AISI 1040** (ii) **High-speed steel AISI M2** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) AISI 1040 (0.40% C plain carbon steel) — 3.5 Marks** **Heat Treatment Cycle:** - **Annealing:** Heat to **830 – 860 °C**, hold 1 hour, furnace cool. Produces soft pearlite + ferrite (~ 85 HRB). - **Normalizing:** Heat to **870 – 900 °C**, hold, **air cool**. Refines grain, gives uniform fine pearlite. - **Hardening:** Austenitize at **840 – 870 °C**, quench in **water or brine**. Achieves **~ 55 HRC** but with risk of cracking. - **Tempering:** Reheat to **400 – 600 °C**, hold, air cool. Trades hardness for toughness (final ~ 30–45 HRC depending on temper temperature). **Effect on TTT Diagram:** For plain carbon steel, the **TTT nose** is at ~550 °C with an incubation time of ~1 second. The transformation to pearlite is **rapid**, requiring very fast quench rates. This makes plain carbon steels prone to **distortion and quench cracking**, with limited **hardenability** (~5–10 mm section). **(ii) AISI M2 (High-Speed Steel — 0.85% C, 6% W, 5% Mo, 4% Cr, 2% V) — 3.5 Marks** **Heat Treatment Cycle:** - **Pre-heat (Two-stage):** 600 °C → 850 °C to minimize thermal shock. - **Austenitization:** Heat to **1210 – 1240 °C** (very high — alloying elements push this up). - **Quenching:** Oil or salt bath at **550 – 600 °C**, then air cool to room temperature. - **Tempering:** Triple temper at **540 – 570 °C for 2 hours each** to achieve **secondary hardening** via precipitation of fine **VC, Mo₂C, W₂C carbides**. - Final hardness: **62 – 65 HRC**, with **red hardness up to 600 °C**. **Effect on TTT Diagram:** Alloying elements (Cr, Mo, V, W) **shift the TTT nose dramatically to the right** — incubation time increases from ~1 s (plain carbon) to **~100 – 1000 s** in M2. This means: - The austenite remains untransformed even during **air cooling or oil quenching**. - **Hardenability is enormous** — sections up to 100 mm can be through-hardened. - **Retained austenite** at room temperature can be 20–30%, requiring multiple tempering cycles for transformation to martensite. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in "Alloying" Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "hardenability" with "hardness":** Hardenability = *depth of hardening*; hardness = *resistance to indentation*. Many students write them interchangeably and lose 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the multipliers in CE:** Writing CE = C + Mn + Cr + … without the **/6, /5, /15** factors. The examiner's key explicitly tests the *coefficients*. > 3. **Wrong austenite vs. ferrite stabilizer categorization:** Copper is an austenite stabilizer (NOT ferrite). Molybdenum is a ferrite stabilizer (NOT austenite). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Skipping the engineering recommendation in CE problems:** A CE value without a weldability verdict is an **incomplete answer**. Always close with the practical recommendation. > 5. **Phase-fraction lever-rule errors:** Using *volume* fractions instead of *mass* fractions when densities differ significantly. KTU prefers **mass fractions** unless the question explicitly states otherwise. > 6. **Ignoring units:** CE is **dimensionless**; Ms is in **°C**; grain size is in **mm**. Examiners allot half-marks for unit consistency. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Alloying:** - ✅ **Alloy definition:** Mixture of ≥ 2 elements, at least one metal, exhibiting metallic properties. - ✅ **Two solution types:** Substitutional (size difference < 15%) and Interstitial (small atoms like C, N). - ✅ **Three alloy-classes by phase effect:** - Austenite stabilizers → **Ni, Mn, Co, Cu** - Ferrite stabilizers → **Cr, Si, Mo, V, W, Ti, Al** - Carbide formers (weak → strong): **Mn < Cr, Mo, W < V, Ti, Nb** - ✅ **Master formula:** $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ - ✅ **CE thresholds:** ≤ 0.40 excellent; 0.40–0.55 good; 0.55–0.65 preheat needed; > 0.65 difficult. - ✅ **Lever rule:** $W_{\alpha} = \dfrac{C_{\gamma} - C_{0}}{C_{\gamma} - C_{\alpha}}$ — applies at any two-phase tie-line. - ✅ **Stainless steel grades:** - **304** → 18Cr/8Ni → austenitic, non-magnetic, kitchen/chemical use - **430** → 17Cr → ferritic, magnetic, automotive trim - **410** → 12Cr → martensitic, hardenable, cutlery - ✅ **Alloying modifies TTT:** Shifts nose **right** → more time for diffusion → increased **hardenability**. - ✅ **Mn is mandatory:** Acts as **deoxidizer + desulfurizer** — every commercial steel contains Mn. - ✅ **Molybdenum's unique role:** Combats **temper embrittlement** caused by P and Sn. - ✅ **V, Ti, Nb = secondary hardening:** Used in HSS for **hot hardness** and **cutting performance**. > [!TIP] > **One-Sentence Revision Summary:** > *"Alloying is the **engineered manipulation of metal chemistry and microstructure** to deliver tailored mechanical, chemical, and physical properties, and the **Carbon Equivalent formula is its single most powerful quantitative tool** for production and welding engineers."* ---

topics.title: Application of Nonferrous Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application of Nonferrous Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What are Nonferrous Metals?

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nonferrous Metals** are metallic alloys or pure metals in which **iron (Fe) is not the principal constituent**. They are extracted from ores other than iron-bearing minerals and possess distinctive combinations of physical, chemical, and mechanical properties — such as low density, high corrosion resistance, superior thermal/electrical conductivity, and excellent formability — that make them indispensable in specialized engineering applications.

The primary classification in the KTU 2024 Production Engineering syllabus includes:

- **Light Metals:** Aluminum (Al), Magnesium (Mg), Titanium (Ti), Beryllium (Be)
- **Heavy Nonferrous Metals:** Copper (Cu), Nickel (Ni), Lead (Pb), Tin (Sn), Zinc (Zn)
- **Precious Metals:** Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Platinum (Pt)
- **Refractory Metals:** Tungsten (W), Molybdenum (Mo), Chromium (Cr), Vanadium (V), Tantalum (Ta), Niobium (Nb)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a **kitchen toolbox**:
> - **Steel (Ferrous)** is your **heavy-duty hammer** — strong, hard, but heavy and prone to rust when wet.
> - **Nonferrous metals** are your **specialty tools** — the lightweight aluminum ladle, the corrosion-resistant copper-bottom pan, the silvery titanium knife that never stains.
> 
> Just as you would not use a hammer to stir soup, you would not use steel where you need lightness, conductivity, or rust-proof behavior. **Nonferrous metals are the "specialty specialists" of the engineering world.**

### Standard Reference Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Iron (Fe) atomic number | **Z = 26** | Defines ferrous boundary |
| Earth's crust composition (Al) | **~8.1 %** | Most abundant metal |
| Copper conductivity (IACS) | **100 % IACS** | International reference standard |
| Aluminum density | **2.70 g/cm³** | One-third that of steel |

> [!NOTE]
> The single key discriminator: **If Fe is not the majority element by mass, the alloy is classified as nonferrous.**

---

## 1.2 Why Nonferrous Metals Matter in Manufacturing

The driving forces for choosing nonferrous metals in modern manufacturing are encapsulated in four engineering attributes:

1. **Low Specific Weight (ρ):** Critical for aerospace, automotive, and portable equipment — quantified by the **specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio, σ/ρ).
2. **Corrosion Resistance:** Surface oxide films (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃, TiO₂) act as self-healing passivation layers.
3. **Electrical & Thermal Conductivity:** Cu, Ag, Al dominate electrical engineering.
4. **Special Functional Properties:** Superconductivity (Nb), shape memory (Ni-Ti), low expansion (Invar).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 3 of PCPET205 emphasizes the **application-side mapping** of nonferrous metals — *which metal goes where, and why*. Memorization of the **application–property linkage** is a high-frequency exam topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Aluminum and its Alloys — The Lightweight Champion

### Operating Logic
1. **Density advantage:** Al has ρ ≈ 2.70 g/cm³ vs. steel's 7.85 g/cm³ → **weight reduction of ~65 %** for equivalent volume.
2. **Self-passivating oxide:** Exposure to air instantly forms a 2–3 nm Al₂O₃ layer → excellent atmospheric corrosion resistance.
3. **Work hardening & age hardening:** Alloys 2xxx (Al-Cu), 6xxx (Al-Mg-Si), 7xxx (Al-Zn) achieve UTS up to **~570 MPa** (alloy 7075-T6).

### Classification by Processing Method

| Series | Principal Alloying Element | Heat-Treatable? | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xxx** | Pure Al (≥ 99 %) | No | Chemical tanks, electrical busbars |
| **2xxx** | Cu | Yes | Aircraft structures (skin, ribs) |
| **3xxx** | Mn | No | Beverage cans, roofing sheets |
| **5xxx** | Mg | No | Marine hulls, pressure vessels |
| **6xxx** | Mg + Si | Yes | Architectural extrusions, auto frames |
| **7xxx** | Zn | Yes | Aircraft wing spars, high-stress parts |
| **8xxx** | Li / others | Yes | Aerospace lightweight structures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **4-digit designation system** is universally used in KTU board questions. Memorize the principal alloying element of each series.

### Real-World Utility
- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 fuselage is **~50 % Al alloys by weight**.
- **Automotive:** Engine blocks, wheel rims, cylinder heads.
- **Packaging:** Aluminum foil (as thin as **0.006 mm**), beverage cans.
- **Electrical:** Overhead transmission lines (replaced Cu in long spans due to weight).

---

## 2.2 Copper and its Alloys — The Conductive Workhorse

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Pure Cu has electrical conductivity of **5.96 × 10⁷ S/m** (100 % IACS) — second only to silver.
2. Alloying reduces conductivity but enhances strength, machinability, and corrosion resistance.
3. The two main alloy families are **Brass (Cu-Zn)** and **Bronze (Cu-Sn, Cu-Al)**.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Material Indices for Metal Selection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific Strength} \quad & S_s = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho} \quad [\text{N·m/kg}] \\[6pt]
\text{Specific Stiffness} \quad & E_s = \frac{E}{\rho} \quad [\text{N·m/kg}] \\[6pt]
\text{Conductivity Efficiency} \quad & \kappa_v = \frac{\kappa}{\rho} \quad [\text{S·m}^2/\text{kg}] \\[6pt]
\text{Strength-to-Cost Ratio} \quad & \eta = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{C \cdot \rho} \quad [\text{per Rupee·m}^3/\text{kg}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Use case:** When an aerospace engineer selects between Al-7075 and Ti-6Al-4V for a wing bracket, the **specific strength** index is the deciding metric.

### Master Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Metal / Alloy | ρ (g/cm³) | UTS (MPa) | Melting Point (°C) | Defining Property | Top 3 Applications |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Al** | 2.70 | 90 | 660 | Lightness + conductivity | Foil, busbars |
| **Al-7075-T6** | 2.81 | 572 | 477 | High specific strength | Aircraft wing spars |
| **Pure Cu** | 8.96 | 220 | 1085 | Highest practical conductivity | Wires, windings |
| **Cartridge Brass (Cu-30Zn)** | 8.53 | 380 | 915 | Cold formability | Ammunition, radiator cores |
| **Phosphor Bronze (Cu-8Sn)** | 8.80 | 450 | 1027 | Springiness + wear resistance | Springs, bearings, gears |
| **Pure Ti** | 4.51 | 240 | 1668 | Corrosion immunity | Chemical reactors, implants |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | 4.43 | 950 | 1660 | Best strength/biocompatibility ratio | Jet engine blades, bone plates |
| **Pure Mg** | 1.74 | 130 | 650 | Lightest structural metal | Die-cast housings |
| **Monel (Ni-30Cu)** | 8.80 | 550 | 1350 | Seawater resistance | Marine propellers, pump shafts |
| **Nichrome (Ni-20Cr)** | 8.40 | 690 | 1400 | High resistivity + oxidation | Heating elements, toasters |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | 7.14 | 100 | 420 | Sacrificial anode | Galvanizing steel, die castings |
| **Lead (Pb)** | 11.34 | 17 | 327 | Radiation shielding | X-ray aprons, lead-acid batteries |
| **Tin (Sn)** | 7.30 | 15 | 232 | Food-safe + low melt | Solder, tin plating, food cans |

---

## 2.3 Magnesium, Titanium, and Nickel — The Specialty Triad

### Magnesium (Mg) — The Lightest Structural Metal
- **Density 1.74 g/cm³** — 35 % lighter than Al, four times lighter than steel.
- Hexagonal close-packed (HCP) crystal structure → poor room-temperature ductility.
- Highly flammable in powder/chip form → requires special machining (no sparks, no water).

> **Real-world use:** Steering wheel cores, laptop housings (older generations), helicopter transmission housings, automotive seat frames.

### Titanium (Ti) — The Corrosion-Proof Performer
- Density 4.51 g/cm³ → midway between Al and Fe.
- Forms a stable TiO₂ passivation layer → virtually immune to salt water and chlorides.
- **Ti-6Al-4V** is the "workhorse" alloy — used in **~50 % of all titanium applications**.

> **Real-world use:** Jet engine compressor blades (operating at ~400 °C), submarine hulls, surgical implants, chemical plant reactors.

### Nickel (Ni) — The Heat & Corrosion Specialist
- Face-centered cubic (FCC) → excellent ductility.
- Base element for superalloys (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy) used in jet engine turbine blades operating above **1000 °C**.
- **Invar (Fe-36Ni)** has near-zero thermal expansion (α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /K) → used in precision instruments, clock pendulums, and LNG tank supports.

---

## 2.4 Other Critical Nonferrous Metals

### Lead (Pb) — The Shield
- Highest density among common engineering metals (ρ = 11.34 g/cm³).
- Toxic, but irreplaceable for **radiation shielding** (X-ray rooms, nuclear reactors).
- Used in **lead-acid batteries** (still ~85 % of all rechargeable automotive batteries globally).

### Tin (Sn) — The Joiner
- Low melting point (232 °C) → used in solders (Sn-Pb or lead-free Sn-Ag-Cu).
- Non-toxic and corrosion-resistant → used for **tin-plating steel food cans** ("tin can").

### Zinc (Zn) — The Sacrificial Protector
- **Galvanizing:** Dipping steel into molten Zn (~450 °C) creates a protective coating.
- **Sacrificial anode:** Zn corrodes preferentially in cathodic protection systems for ships, pipelines, and offshore platforms.
- **Die casting:** Zn alloys (e.g., Zamak-3) are cast at ~420 °C → low energy cost, excellent dimensional accuracy.

### Tungsten (W) — The Refractory Champion
- Highest melting point of all metals: **3422 °C**.
- Density 19.3 g/cm³ (same as gold → used as gold alloy substitute in counterweights).
- Used in **filaments of incandescent bulbs**, X-ray tube targets, rocket nozzle liners.

### Precious Metals (Au, Ag, Pt)
- **Au (Gold):** Corrosion-proof electrical contacts, dental crowns, monetary reserve.
- **Ag (Silver):** Highest electrical conductivity of all elements → used in high-frequency RF contacts, photographic film (AgBr), antibacterial coatings.
- **Pt (Platinum):** Catalyst in catalytic converters, laboratory crucibles, pacemakers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Weight Savings from Aluminum Substitution

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):** A structural bracket currently made of mild steel (ρ_steel = 7.85 g/cm³) is to be redesigned in Al-6061-T6 (ρ_Al = 2.70 g/cm³) without changing its volume. The original steel bracket has a mass of 12.5 kg. Determine the percentage mass reduction achieved.

### Step 1 — Define the governing relationship

Mass is the product of density and volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m = \rho \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the geometry (and hence volume V) is held constant by the design constraint, the mass ratio depends only on the density ratio.

### Step 2 — Express the mass of the steel bracket

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{steel}} = \rho_{\text{steel}} \cdot V
\end{aligned}
$$

Given m_steel = 12.5 kg, we back-calculate V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \frac{m_{\text{steel}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{12.5 \text{ kg}}{7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert units to keep the calculation clean

Express ρ_steel in kg/m³ (1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7.85 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}, \quad
\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V = \frac{12.5}{7850} = 1.5924 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the aluminum mass for the same volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Al}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \cdot V = 2700 \times 1.5924 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Al}} = 4.2994 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the percentage mass reduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{m_{\text{steel}} - m_{\text{Al}}}{m_{\text{steel}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{12.5 - 4.2994}{12.5} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Final result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{8.2006}{12.5} \times 100 \approx 65.6 \ \text{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Cross-check using the direct density ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} = \left( 1 - \frac{\rho_{\text{Al}}}{\rho_{\text{steel}}} \right) \times 100 = \left( 1 - \frac{2.70}{7.85} \right) \times 100 \approx 65.6 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods agree — confirming the result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A 65.6 % weight saving at equal volume. In an aircraft with 100 such brackets (each ~12.5 kg in steel), the total mass reduction is **~820 kg**, translating directly into fuel savings of thousands of liters per aircraft per year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Strength Comparison (Al vs. Ti vs. Steel)

> **Problem Statement:** Compare the specific strength of (a) mild steel (σ_UTS = 450 MPa, ρ = 7850 kg/m³), (b) Al-7075-T6 (σ_UTS = 572 MPa, ρ = 2810 kg/m³), and (c) Ti-6Al-4V (σ_UTS = 950 MPa, ρ = 4430 kg/m³). Which is best for a weight-critical tension member?

### Step 1 — Specific strength formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_s = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{\rho} \quad [\text{kN·m/kg}]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Mild steel

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{s,\text{steel}} = \frac{450 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}}{7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}} = 57,325 \text{ N·m/kg} = 57.3 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Al-7075-T6

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{s,\text{Al}} = \frac{572 \times 10^{6}}{2810} = 203,558 \text{ N·m/kg} = 203.6 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Ti-6Al-4V

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{s,\text{Ti}} = \frac{950 \times 10^{6}}{4430} = 214,447 \text{ N·m/kg} = 214.4 \text{ kN·m/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Ranking

| Rank | Material | Specific Strength (kN·m/kg) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ti-6Al-4V** | 214.4 | Best, but very expensive |
| 2 | **Al-7075-T6** | 203.6 | Best performance-to-cost ratio |
| 3 | Mild Steel | 57.3 | Heavy, but cheap |

> **Conclusion:** For a weight-critical member, **Al-7075-T6 is the industrial sweet-spot** — 3.5× the specific strength of mild steel at a fraction of Ti's cost.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Application-to-Metal Mapping

| Engineering Requirement | Best Nonferrous Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Aircraft wing spar | **Al-7075-T6 / Ti-6Al-4V** | Maximum specific strength |
| High-voltage transmission line | **Al-1350** | Low ρ × acceptable σ → cheaper than Cu per km |
| Marine propeller | **Ni-Al bronze / Monel** | Seawater + cavitation resistance |
| Surgical bone plate | **Ti-6Al-4V** | Biocompatible + osseointegration |
| Sacrificial anode for hull | **Zn / Mg** | More electronegative than Fe |
| Car engine block | **Al-319 / Al-356** | Lightweight + good thermal conductivity |
| Solder for electronics | **Sn-37Pb / Sn-3.0Ag-0.5Cu** | Low melting point, wets Cu |
| Heating element (toaster) | **Nichrome (Ni-20Cr)** | High ρ + oxidation resistance up to 1100 °C |
| X-ray room shielding | **Pb (≥ 2 mm thick)** | High ρ + high Z attenuates X-rays |
| Lunar lander structural frame | **Al-Li 8090** | Lowest ρ among structural alloys |
| Chemical reactor for HCl | **Hastelloy C-276** | Resists Cl⁻ attack |
| Incandescent bulb filament | **W (tungsten)** | Melting point 3422 °C |
| Rocket nozzle throat | **W-Re alloy / graphite (composite)** | Withstands >3000 °C gas flow |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
