# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Q1]**

> List any **three advantages** of nonferrous metals over ferrous metals, with one example application for each advantage.

### Model Answer (3 marks, structured pointwise)

1. **Lower density (lightness):** Example — Al-7075 used in aircraft wing spars, reducing structural mass by ~65 % compared to mild steel. *(1 mark)*
2. **Superior corrosion resistance:** Example — Pure titanium used in chemical reactor vessels handling hot concentrated chlorides, where mild steel would fail within hours. *(1 mark)*
3. **Higher electrical / thermal conductivity:** Example — Pure copper (100 % IACS) used in transformer windings, where its conductivity is ~60× higher than that of mild steel. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper Q2]**

> Distinguish between **brass** and **bronze** in terms of composition, properties, and **two applications** of each.

### Model Answer

| Parameter | Brass | Bronze |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Cu + Zn (zinc is principal) | Cu + Sn (tin is principal); also Al-bronze, Si-bronze |
| **Color** | Yellow | Reddish-brown |
| **Key property** | Excellent cold workability, good machinability | High wear resistance, superior corrosion resistance in seawater |
| **Applications (2)** | Cartridge cases, radiator cores, decorative fittings, plumbing fittings *(1 mark)* | Marine propellers, bearings, springs, statues, bushings *(1 mark)* |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Per the ESE pattern, Module 3 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (either A or B). The sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Answer

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the **classification, composition, properties, and applications of aluminum and its alloys** in detail. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **galvanizing process** for corrosion protection of steel. List **two advantages** and **two limitations** of galvanizing. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for (a) — Aluminum and its Alloys (7 marks)

**(1) Introduction (1 mark):**
Aluminum is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust (~8.1 % by mass) and the third most abundant element after oxygen and silicon. It is extracted primarily from the ore **bauxite** through the **Bayer process** (refining to Al₂O₃) followed by the **Hall-Héroult electrolytic process**.

**(2) Properties of Pure Aluminum (2 marks):**
- Density ρ = 2.70 g/cm³ (about one-third of steel)
- Melting point 660 °C
- Excellent electrical conductivity ~61 % IACS
- Highly ductile and malleable
- Forms a self-healing Al₂O₃ passive layer → corrosion resistance
- Non-magnetic and non-sparking

**(3) Classification of Aluminum Alloys (2 marks):**

Aluminum alloys are classified into two groups:
- **Wrought alloys** (worked into sheet, plate, extrusions) — designated by 4-digit system (1xxx to 8xxx).
- **Cast alloys** — designated by 3-digit system (1xx.x to 9xx.x), produced by sand, die, or investment casting.

Heat-treatable vs. non-heat-treatable distinction:
- **Heat-treatable:** 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx, some 8xxx — strengthened by solution heat treatment and aging (precipitation hardening).
- **Non-heat-treatable:** 1xxx, 3xxx, 5xxx — strengthened only by cold working (strain hardening).

**(4) Applications (2 marks):**

| Sector | Application | Alloy Example |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Wing skins, ribs, fuselage frames | 2024, 7075 |
| Automotive | Engine blocks, cylinder heads, wheels | 319, 356, 6061 |
| Packaging | Beverage cans, foil | 3004, 1100 |
| Architecture | Window frames, curtain walls | 6063 |
| Marine | Boat hulls, superstructures | 5083, 5086 |
| Electrical | Busbars, transmission lines | 1350 |

---

### Model Solution for (b) — Galvanizing (7 marks)

**(1) Definition (1 mark):**
> Galvanizing is the process of applying a protective **zinc (Zn) coating** to steel or iron to prevent rusting. The most common method is **hot-dip galvanizing**.

**(2) Hot-Dip Galvanizing Process — Steps (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Steel Surface Preparation]:::step
    B[Degreasing: Alkaline or Acid Bath]:::step
    C[Pickling: H2SO4 / HCl to remove Rust]:::step
    D[Fluxing: ZnCl2 + NH4Cl]:::step
    E[Dip in Molten Zn 450 deg C]:::step
    F[Withdrawal and Cooling]:::step
    G[Coated Steel Part]:::final
    
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    
    classDef step fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef final fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

1. **Surface preparation:** Degreasing in alkaline bath to remove oils, dirt.
2. **Pickling:** Acid bath (H₂SO₄ or HCl) to remove mill scale and rust.
3. **Rinsing and fluxing:** ZnCl₂/NH₄Cl flux promotes wetting of steel by molten zinc.
4. **Hot-dip immersion:** Steel is dipped in molten zinc at **445–465 °C** for a few minutes. A series of Zn-Fe intermetallic layers form (Gamma, Delta, Zeta) topped by a pure Zn Eta layer.
5. **Withdrawal and cooling:** Controlled withdrawal speed determines coating thickness (typically **50–150 μm**).

**(3) Two Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Sacrificial (cathodic) protection:** Zinc corrodes preferentially to steel, so even if the coating is scratched, the underlying steel remains protected.
- **Long service life:** Galvanized steel can last 20–50+ years in rural environments and 10–25 years in coastal/industrial environments.

**(4) Two Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Limited high-temperature use:** Above ~200 °C, the Zn-Fe layers peel off.
- **Not suitable for strongly acidic or alkaline environments:** Zinc dissolves rapidly in pH < 6 or pH > 12.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Choice B]**

> **(a)** Compare the properties and applications of **copper, brass, and bronze**. State **two typical applications** of each. *(7 marks)*
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **applications of titanium alloys** in aerospace and biomedical engineering. Why is Ti-6Al-4V the most widely used titanium alloy? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for (a) — Copper vs. Brass vs. Bronze (7 marks)

| Property | Pure Copper | Brass (Cu + Zn) | Bronze (Cu + Sn) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Composition | ≥ 99.3 % Cu | 55–95 % Cu, 5–45 % Zn | 78–95 % Cu, 5–22 % Sn |
| Color | Reddish | Yellow | Reddish-brown |
| Density (g/cm³) | 8.96 | 8.4–8.7 | 8.7–8.9 |
| Corrosion resistance | Good | Good | Excellent (especially in seawater) |
| Electrical conductivity | 100 % IACS | 25–40 % IACS | 15–20 % IACS |
| Machinability | Fair | Excellent (especially with Pb) | Good |
| Wear resistance | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent |

**Applications:**

- **Pure Copper (2 applications):** *(1 mark)*
  1. Electrical wires, cables, transformer windings.
  2. Plumbing tubes, roofing, heat exchangers.
- **Brass (2 applications):** *(1 mark)*
  1. Cartridge cases, ammunition shells.
  2. Decorative hardware, plumbing fittings, musical instruments.
- **Bronze (2 applications):** *(1 mark)*
  1. Marine propellers, ship fittings (corrosion resistance).
  2. Bearings, bushings, gears (wear resistance).

*Plus property explanation (4 marks distributed as above.)*

---

### Model Solution for (b) — Titanium Alloys (7 marks)

**(1) Why titanium in aerospace? (2 marks)**
- Density 4.51 g/cm³ is ~57 % that of steel → significant weight reduction.
- Retains strength at elevated temperatures (up to ~600 °C for Ti-6Al-4V).
- Excellent fatigue resistance.
- Corrosion resistant due to passive TiO₂ layer.
- Non-magnetic and non-toxic.

**Aerospace applications:** Engine compressor blades and discs, airframe skin, hydraulic tubing, landing-gear forgings, helicopter rotor hubs, spacecraft pressure vessels.

**(2) Why titanium in biomedical? (2 marks)**
- **Biocompatible:** Does not provoke immune rejection.
- **Osseointegration:** Bone cells grow directly onto titanium surfaces.
- **Non-magnetic:** Safe for MRI scans.
- **Modulus closer to bone** (~110 GPa vs. 200 GPa for steel) → reduced stress shielding.
- Corrosion-proof in body fluids (simulated by Ringer's solution testing).

**Biomedical applications:** Bone plates, hip and knee implants, dental implants, spinal fusion cages, surgical instruments, pacemaker housings.

**(3) Why Ti-6Al-4V dominates (3 marks):**
- **Composition:** 90 % Ti, 6 % Al (alpha stabilizer), 4 % V (beta stabilizer) → alpha-beta microstructure offering the best strength-ductility balance.
- **Mechanical strength:** UTS ~950 MPa after heat treatment, ~3× stronger than commercially pure Ti.
- **Forgeability and weldability:** Can be hot-worked, machined, and welded (with inert gas shielding) using conventional methods.
- **Heat-treatable:** Can be solution-treated and aged to vary properties (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V ELI for medical use has reduced interstitial content).
- **Mature supply chain:** Accounts for **~50 % of all titanium alloy usage globally** and is the cheapest of all titanium alloys per kg of strength delivered.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing brass and bronze:** Brass = Cu-Zn; Bronze = Cu-Sn. Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this swap.
> 2. **Writing "Aluminium is lighter than steel"** without specifying the density value or % weight saving → **no full mark**. Always quantify.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention corrosion mechanism** in galvanizing answers (sacrificial anode principle) → **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **In titanium questions:** Do **not** write "titanium does not corrode" — it does, but the TiO₂ layer is **self-healing**. State it correctly.
> 5. **For Al-alloy designations:** Writing "6061 is an Al-Zn alloy" is **wrong** — it is Al-Mg-Si. The first digit denotes the principal alloying element family.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist the night before your KTU ESE.**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Nonferrous metal** = metal/alloy in which **Fe is NOT the principal element**.
- [ ] **Al** = lightest structural metal after Mg → density 2.70 g/cm³.
- [ ] **Cu** = 100 % IACS benchmark → 2nd highest conductivity after Ag.
- [ ] **Ti-6Al-4V** = "workhorse" titanium alloy → ~50 % of all Ti usage.
- [ ] **Invar** (Fe-36Ni) = near-zero thermal expansion.
- [ ] **Nichrome** (Ni-20Cr) = heating element material.
- [ ] **Monel** (Ni-30Cu) = seawater resistance champion.
- [ ] **W (Tungsten)** = highest melting point of all metals (**3422 °C**).

### Aluminum Designation Series (HIGH FREQUENCY in exams)
- [ ] 1xxx = Pure Al
- [ ] 2xxx = Al-Cu
- [ ] 3xxx = Al-Mn
- [ ] 4xxx = Al-Si (casting + brazing)
- [ ] 5xxx = Al-Mg
- [ ] 6xxx = Al-Mg-Si
- [ ] 7xxx = Al-Zn (strongest)
- [ ] 8xxx = Al-Li / others

### Copper Alloy Distinctions
- [ ] **Brass** = Cu + **Zn**
- [ ] **Bronze** = Cu + **Sn** (or Al, Si, Be variants)
- [ ] **Cupronickel** = Cu + Ni (used in coinage, condensers)

### Application Snapshots (memorize one application per metal)
- [ ] Al-7075 → aircraft wing spars
- [ ] Al-6061 → architectural extrusions
- [ ] Cu → electrical wiring
- [ ] Brass → cartridge cases
- [ ] Bronze → marine propellers
- [ ] Ti-6Al-4V → bone implants
- [ ] Monel → marine pump shafts
- [ ] Nichrome → toaster heating coil
- [ ] Zn → galvanizing / sacrificial anode
- [ ] Pb → X-ray shielding
- [ ] Sn-Pb → solder
- [ ] W → bulb filament
- [ ] Pt → catalytic converter
- [ ] Mg → die-cast housings

### Key Numerical Constants to Memorize
- [ ] Density of steel = **7.85 g/cm³**
- [ ] Density of Al = **2.70 g/cm³**
- [ ] Density of Ti = **4.51 g/cm³**
- [ ] Density of Mg = **1.74 g/cm³**
- [ ] Density of Cu = **8.96 g/cm³**
- [ ] Density of Pb = **11.34 g/cm³**
- [ ] Melting point of W = **3422 °C**
- [ ] Melting point of Al = **660 °C**
- [ ] Melting point of Cu = **1085 °C**
- [ ] Melting point of Ti = **1668 °C**

### Key Formulae
- [ ] Mass: $m = \rho \cdot V$
- [ ] Specific strength: $S_s = \sigma_{UTS} / \rho$
- [ ] Specific stiffness: $E_s = E / \rho$
- [ ] % Weight saving: $\left( 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{new}}{\rho_{old}} \right) \times 100$

### Process Snapshot
- [ ] **Galvanizing steps:** Degrease → Pickle → Rinse → Flux → Dip in molten Zn (445–465 °C) → Cool
- [ ] **Galvanizing protection mechanism:** Sacrificial cathodic protection (Zn is more electronegative than Fe)

> **Final Exam Tip:** Map **property → application** and **application → property** — questions will be asked in both directions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wood Based Integrated Pulp and Paper Mill - Wood Chips Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Wood Based Integrated Pulp and Paper Mill - Wood Chips Production ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wood Based Integrated Pulp and Paper Mill**: A **vertically combined industrial complex** in which the entire process chain — from raw wood logs reception, debarking, chipping, pulp manufacturing, paper/paperboard forming, drying, finishing, and even by-product recovery (tall oil, turpentine, lignin) — is carried out **sequentially under one organizational and geographical boundary**, achieving material flow efficiency, energy integration, and waste minimization. **Wood Chips Production** is the **front-end preparatory stage** of this integrated mill, where cleaned, debarked wood logs are mechanically reduced into uniformly sized, screenable, and digestible wood particles — called **wood chips** — that are dimensionally optimized for downstream **digesters** in the pulping process. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):** > *"Wood chips are small, slender pieces of wood of standardized dimensions — typically **length 15–30 mm**, **thickness 3–7 mm**, and **width 15–25 mm** — produced by mechanical reduction of debarked logs to facilitate uniform penetration of cooking liquor in chemical pulping."* --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview Imagine you are preparing a **masala chai** (Indian spiced tea). Before boiling, you must: 1. **Wash** the raw spices (clean the wood of dirt and grit). 2. **Peel** the outer unwanted layer (debark the log). 3. **Crush and slice** the spices into small pieces (chipping) so that the boiling water can **extract the essence quickly and uniformly**. In an integrated pulp mill, **wood chips are the "crushed spices"** of the paper world. The "essence" being extracted is the **cellulose fiber**, and the "boiling water" is the **cooking liquor** (white liquor — NaOH + Na₂S mixture). The smaller and more uniform the chips, the faster and more uniform the extraction — analogous to brewing a richer, more consistent chai. --- ### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Chip Length** | **15–30 mm** | Controls penetration rate | | **Chip Thickness** | **3–7 mm** | Controls diffusion distance | | **Chip Width** | **15–25 mm** | Governs chip packing density | | **Acceptable Fines** | < **2 %** (w/w) | Excess fines cause digester channeling | | **Overthick Chips** | < **5 %** (w/w) | Cause uncooked centers (shives) | | **Bulk Density of Chips** | **180–250 kg/m³** (oven-dry basis) | Affects digester volumetric capacity | | **Moisture Content** | **40–55 %** (green wood) | Affects chipper energy & screening | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **standard industry benchmark** for chip quality is the **SCAN-CM 40:94 (Nordic standard)** and **TAPPI T 257 cm-12** for sampling and classification. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Wood Chip Production The wood-chip production subsystem of an integrated pulp mill is structured into **six logically sequential operations**. Each stage is critical — a defect at any stage propagates downstream and degrades the entire pulp quality. **Stage 1 — Log Receiving and Storage** - Roundwood (logs) arrive by truck, rail, or river floating. - Logs are **metered (scaled by volume)**, **sorted by species** (softwood vs. hardwood), and **stored in log decks**. - Storage periods: **3–6 months** to allow natural drying and seasoning. **Stage 2 — Debarking (Barking)** - **Definition**: The complete removal of **bark** (the outer corky layer containing suberin, tannins, and dirt) from logs. - **Why debark?** - Bark contains **lignin-rich, fiber-less tissue** that produces dark specks ("bark specks") in finished paper. - Bark consumes **extra cooking chemicals** without yielding useful fiber. - Bark grit damages chipper knives and digester linings. **Stage 3 — Chipping (Primary Size Reduction)** - Logs are fed into a **chipper** (disc, drum, or screw type) and reduced to chips. - Output: **Bulk chip mass** containing accept chips, oversized chips, and fines. **Stage 4 — Chip Screening and Classification** - Bulk chip mass is passed over **vibratory or gyratory screens** with calibrated apertures. - Classification outcome: - **Accept chips** → to storage silo - **Overs (oversized)** → re-chipped in a **re-chipper** - **Fines (sawdust, splinters)** → used as **boiler fuel** for energy recovery - **Pin chips and slivers** → sent to secondary fiber line or fuel **Stage 5 — Chip Storage and Reclaiming** - Accept chips stored in **chip silos** (typically 20,000–100,000 m³ capacity). - Storage provides: - **Buffer capacity** between chipper and digester. - **Natural moisture equalization**. - **Opportunity for chip washing** (removal of grit, sand). - Stored in large **chip piles** (outdoor) or **chip silos** (indoor) for buffer. **Stage 6 — Chip Washing and Metering to Digester** - Final washing to remove residual grit, sand, and dirt. - Metered by **belt conveyor with weigh scale** into digester charging system. --- ### 2.2 Types of Chippers — Comparative Analysis | Chipper Type | Operating Principle | Capacity | Best Suited For | |---|---|---|---| | **Disc Chipper** | Logs fed at 20–45° to a rotating disc carrying 4–16 knives | High (50–300 ODt/h) | Hardwood & softwood, large mills | | **Drum Chipper** | Logs fed parallel to rotating drum with knives on periphery | Medium–High | Uniform, straight logs | | **Screw Chipper** | Logs pressed against a rotating conical screw | Low–Medium | Small mills, residual wood | | **Hammer Mill** | Impact reduction by swinging hammers | Low | Fines production, not preferred | --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **frequently tested in KTU ESE** for numerical sub-parts. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_{chip} = \dfrac{m_{accept}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Chipper/Accept efficiency | % | | 2 | $BD = \dfrac{m_{chips}}{V_{container}}$ | Bulk density of chips | kg/m³ | | 3 | $MC(\%) = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Moisture content of wood | % | | 4 | $Q_{chipper} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot k \cdot \eta_v}{60}$ | Theoretical chipper throughput | ODt/h | | 5 | $P_{chip} = K \cdot \rho \cdot BD \cdot L_{log}$ | Power for chipping | kW | | 6 | $Y_{pulp} = 0.45 \times m_{chips} \times (1 - MC)$ | Pulp yield from chips (avg.) | kg | | 7 | $\eta_{screen} = \dfrac{m_{overs}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Screening efficiency (overs removal) | % | | 8 | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{E_{chipper}}{m_{chips}}$ | Specific energy consumption | kWh/ODt | | 9 | $\dot{V}_{chip} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{chip}}{BD}$ | Volumetric chip flow | m³/h | | 10 | $C_{rot} = 2\pi \cdot r_{disc} \cdot N$ | Chipper disc tip speed | m/s | **Where:** - $D$ = disc diameter (m), $N$ = disc rpm, $k$ = number of knives, $\eta_v$ = volumetric feed factor - $K$ = empirical chipping constant (~0.8–1.2), $\rho$ = wood density (kg/m³) - $L_{log}$ = log length (m), $C_{rot}$ = chipper knife tip speed (m/s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Never confuse **green volume basis** with **oven-dry (OD) basis**. Always convert moisture content first before any mass calculation. Most KTU numericals test this trap. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Chipper Throughput from First Principles **Given data (standard KTU problem format):** - Disc chipper diameter $D = 2.8$ m - Disc speed $N = 500$ rpm - Number of knives $k = 12$ - Feed factor $\eta_v = 0.65$ (volumetric fill efficiency) - Log average diameter $d_{log} = 0.25$ m - Log length $L_{log} = 4.0$ m - Bulk density of chips $BD = 220$ kg/m³ - Wood density (green) $\rho = 850$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Compute disc tip speed:** $$ C_{rot} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N $$ $$ C_{rot} = \pi \times 2.8 \times 500 = 4398.2 \;\; \text{m/min} $$ $$ C_{rot} = \dfrac{4398.2}{60} = 73.30 \;\; \text{m/s} $$ > *Conversion logic:* The disc tip speed must fall within the **empirical safe window of 60–90 m/s** to ensure clean cuts without splintering. Our value of **73.3 m/s** is optimum. **Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area of a single log:** $$ A_{log} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{log}^{\,2} $$ $$ A_{log} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.25)^{2} = 0.04909 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Compute volume of wood fed per knife cut (length $L_{log}$):** $$ V_{cut} = A_{log} \times L_{log} = 0.04909 \times 4.0 = 0.1963 \;\; \text{m}^{3/log} $$ **Step 4 — Number of cuts per minute:** $$ n_{cuts} = k \times N = 12 \times 500 = 6000 \;\; \text{cuts/min} $$ > *Conversion logic:* Each of the 12 knives on the disc strikes 500 times per minute → 6000 cutting events per minute. **Step 5 — Theoretical volumetric throughput (no voids):** $$ \dot{V}_{theo} = n_{cuts} \times V_{cut} = 6000 \times 0.1963 = 1178.1 \;\; \text{m}^{3/min} $$ **Step 6 — Apply volumetric feed factor to get actual throughput:** $$ \dot{V}_{actual} = \eta_v \times \dot{V}_{theo} = 0.65 \times 1178.1 = 765.8 \;\; \text{m}^{3/min} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to mass flow (green basis):** $$ \dot{m}_{green} = \dot{V}_{actual} \times \rho = 765.8 \times 850 = 6.51 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{kg/min} $$ **Step 8 — Convert to oven-dry tonnes per hour:** Assuming moisture content $MC = 50\%$: $$ \dot{m}_{OD} = \dot{m}_{green} \times (1 - MC) = 6.51 \times 10^{5} \times 0.5 = 3.255 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{kg/h} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{OD} = 325.5 \;\; \text{ODt/h} $$ > *Conversion logic:* A moisture content of 50% means half the green mass is water; only the remaining half is oven-dry wood substance. The chipper throughput is reported on an **OD basis** for KTU numericals. **Final Answer:** The disc chipper has a throughput of **325.5 ODt/h**, which is consistent with a **medium-to-large capacity mill**. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Chip Quality Parameters | Quality Parameter | Softwood (Pine/Spruce) | Hardwood (Eucalyptus) | KTU Test Reference | |---|---|---|---| | Fiber length | **2.5–4.5 mm** | **0.8–1.5 mm** | TAPPI T 233 | | Chip bulk density | **180–220 kg/m³** | **220–280 kg/m³** | SCAN-CM 46:92 | | Typical chip thickness | **3–5 mm** | **4–7 mm** | SCAN-CM 40:94 | | Bark content (target) | < **1.0 %** | < **0.5 %** | TAPPI T 264 | | Pulping yield (Kraft) | **45–52 %** | **48–55 %** | TAPPI T 210 | | Cooking chemical demand | Higher (more lignin) | Lower | — | | Paper strength | Higher tear strength | Higher burst & opacity | — | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Softwood chips produce **stronger, longer-fiber paper** (used for packaging, kraft bags), while hardwood chips produce **smoother, more opaque paper** (used for printing/writing). An integrated mill typically runs a **60:40 softwood-to-hardwood chip blend** to balance strength and printability. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wood Chip Production Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Log Yard
Receiving and Sorting] --> B[Debarker
Rotary Drum Type] B --> C[Metal Detection
and Stone Trap] C --> D[Disc Chipper
Primary Size Reduction] D --> E[Chip Silo
Buffer Storage] E --> F[Vibratory Screen
Three Fraction Classification] F --> G[Oversize Re-chipper
Secondary Reduction] F --> H[Accept Chips
To Digester] F --> I[Fines
To Boiler Fuel] H --> J[Chip Washer
Grit Removal] J --> K[Chip Metering Bin
Continuous Digester Feed] K --> L[Pulp Mill
Chemical Pulping Section] classDef process fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef reject fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,J,K,L process class H output class I reject ``` ### 4.2 Material Flow — Integrated Pulp and Paper Mill Overview ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph upstream["UPSTREAM — Wood Preparation"] W1[Log Receiving] --> W2[Debarking] --> W3[Chipping] --> W4[Chip Screening] end subgraph midstream["MIDSTREAM — Pulping and Bleaching"] P1[Digester
Kraft Cooking] --> P2[Brown Stock Washing] --> P3[Bleaching Sequence] end subgraph downstream["DOWNSTREAM — Paper Making"] D1[Stock Preparation] --> D2[Fourdrinier Machine
Wet End] --> D3[Pressing and Drying] --> D4[Calendering and Reeling] end subgraph byproduct["BY-PRODUCT RECOVERY"] B1[Tall Oil] --> B2[Turpentine] B3[Black Liquor] --> B4[Recovery Boiler
Energy and Chemical Recycle] end W4 --> P1 P3 --> D1 P1 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> P1 P1 --> B1 P1 --> B2 classDef up fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px classDef down fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px classDef byp fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px class W1,W2,W3,W4 up class P1,P2,P3 mid class D1,D2,D3,D4 down class B1,B2,B3,B4 byp ``` ### 4.3 Chip Classification — Three-Fraction Vibratory Screen Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Bulk Chip Mass from Chipper] --> S[Vibratory Screen
Aperture 7mm Top Deck
Aperture 3mm Bottom Deck] S --> O[Oversize Fraction
Greater than 30mm Length] S --> AC[Accept Fraction
15 to 30mm Standard Chips] S --> F[Fines Fraction
Less than 3mm Thickness] O --> RC[Re-chipper
Secondary Reduction] RC --> R AC --> CS[Chip Silo Storage] F --> BF[Boiler Fuel
Energy Recovery] classDef input fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#555,stroke-width:1px classDef screen fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px classDef reject fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px class R input class S screen class AC,CS output class O,F,RC,BF reject ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight from Diagrams:** > - The **closed loop** between Re-chipper and Bulk Chip Mass shows the **mass-recovery recycling** that maximizes chip yield. > - The **black liquor → recovery boiler → digester** loop demonstrates the **chemical and energy integration** that makes the mill "integrated" — a key KTU conceptual question. > - The **3-fraction screening** is the industry-standard classification used in **SCAN-CM 40:94** chip quality analysis. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the term "wood chip" as used in pulp manufacture. List any four quality parameters used to grade wood chips.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A **wood chip** is a small, slender piece of wood of standardized dimensions — typically **15–30 mm in length, 3–7 mm in thickness, and 15–25 mm in width** — produced by mechanical reduction of debarked logs to facilitate uniform penetration of cooking liquor during chemical pulping. **Four Quality Parameters:** 1. **Chip dimensions** (length, thickness, width uniformity). 2. **Bulk density** (should be 180–250 kg/m³ OD basis). 3. **Bark content** (should be < 1% to avoid dark specks). 4. **Fines and pin-chip fraction** (should be < 2% to avoid digester channeling and screening losses). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why is debarking of logs essential before chipping in an integrated pulp and paper mill? Give three reasons.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Debarking is essential because: 1. **Bark is fiber-less** — it contains no useful cellulose fiber and only wastes pulping chemicals. 2. **Bark produces dark specks** in finished paper, degrading optical quality. 3. **Bark carries grit, sand, and dirt** that damage the chipper knives and the digester lining, increasing maintenance costs. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **wood chip production subsystem** of an integrated pulp and paper mill. Describe the functions of any **five major unit operations** involved. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A disc chipper has the following specifications: - Disc diameter $D = 3.0$ m - Number of knives $k = 10$ - Disc speed $N = 450$ rpm - Average log diameter $d_{log} = 0.20$ m - Average log length $L_{log} = 5.0$ m - Volumetric feed factor $\eta_v = 0.60$ - Wood density (green) $\rho = 900$ kg/m³ - Moisture content $MC = 45\%$ Calculate the **chipper throughput in oven-dry tonnes per hour (ODt/h)**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Functions (7 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the block diagram. **Five Major Unit Operations and Functions:** 1. **Log Receiving and Storage Yard** — Accepts roundwood, sorts by species, and stores for seasoning. 2. **Debarker (Rotary Drum Type)** — Removes bark by tumbling logs against each other in a rotating drum; bark is collected and sent to boiler. 3. **Disc Chipper** — Mechanically reduces debarked logs to chips using knives mounted on a high-speed rotating disc. 4. **Vibratory Chip Screen** — Classifies chips into three fractions: accept, overs, and fines. 5. **Re-chipper** — Re-processes oversize chips to meet dimensional standards; fines go to boiler fuel. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Cross-sectional area of one log** $$ A_{log} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{log}^{\,2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.20)^{2} = 0.03142 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 2: Volume of one log** $$ V_{log} = A_{log} \times L_{log} = 0.03142 \times 5.0 = 0.1571 \;\; \text{m}^{3/log} $$ **Step 3: Number of cuts per minute** $$ n_{cuts} = k \times N = 10 \times 450 = 4500 \;\; \text{cuts/min} $$ **Step 4: Theoretical volumetric throughput** $$ \dot{V}_{theo} = n_{cuts} \times V_{log} = 4500 \times 0.1571 = 706.9 \;\; \text{m}^{3/min} $$ **Step 5: Actual volumetric throughput** $$ \dot{V}_{actual} = \eta_v \times \dot{V}_{theo} = 0.60 \times 706.9 = 424.1 \;\; \text{m}^{3/min} $$ **Step 6: Mass flow (green basis)** $$ \dot{m}_{green} = \dot{V}_{actual} \times \rho = 424.1 \times 900 = 3.817 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{kg/min} $$ **Step 7: Convert to oven-dry basis** $$ \dot{m}_{OD} = \dot{m}_{green} \times (1 - MC) = 3.817 \times 10^{5} \times (1 - 0.45) = 2.099 \times 10^{5} \;\; \text{kg/min} $$ **Step 8: Convert to OD tonnes per hour** $$ \dot{m}_{OD} = \dfrac{2.099 \times 10^{5} \times 60}{1000} = 12595 \;\; \text{kg/h} = 12.6 \;\; \text{ODt/h} $$ **Final Answer:** The chipper throughput = **12.6 ODt/h**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the **disc chipper** and **drum chipper** in detail. Compare their **principles of operation, capacity, chip quality, and typical applications** with a comparative table. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define **bulk density, moisture content, and chip yield** with respect to wood chips. A pulp mill receives **500 ODt/day** of wood chips at **50% moisture content (wet basis)**. Calculate: (i) The **green mass** of chips received per day. (ii) The **green volume** of chips if bulk density is **200 kg/m³** (green basis). (iii) The **theoretical pulp output** if the average Kraft yield is **48%**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Disc vs Drum Chipper (7 Marks):** **Disc Chipper:** - **Principle**: A heavy steel disc rotates at **500–720 rpm** on a horizontal shaft. **Radial knives (4 to 16 in number)** are mounted on the disc face. Logs are fed at an angle of **20–45°** to the disc plane and struck by the knives; the **spout (fixed blade)** acts as the anvil. - **Capacity**: High — typically **50–300 ODt/h**. - **Chip Quality**: Uniform, clean cuts with minimal splintering. - **Applications**: Large modern Kraft mills processing mixed hardwood and softwood. **Drum Chipper:** - **Principle**: A **horizontal rotating drum** (length 1.5–4.0 m, diameter 1.0–2.5 m) carries **3 to 8 knives** on its periphery. Logs are fed **parallel to the drum axis** through a spout. - **Capacity**: Medium — typically **20–100 ODt/h**. - **Chip Quality**: Slightly more variable; may produce more slivers in hardwoods. - **Applications**: Medium mills, straight uniform logs. **Comparative Table:** | Parameter | Disc Chipper | Drum Chipper | |---|---|---| | Capacity | 50–300 ODt/h | 20–100 ODt/h | | Knives | 4–16 (radial) | 3–8 (peripheral) | | Feed angle | 20–45° | 0° (parallel) | | Tip speed | 60–90 m/s | 30–50 m/s | | Chip uniformity | Excellent | Good | | Maintenance | Higher knife change cost | Lower | | Capital cost | Higher | Lower | | Best for | Hardwood/softwood mix | Softwood, straight logs | **(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - Oven-dry throughput $\dot{m}_{OD} = 500$ ODt/day - Moisture content $MC = 50\%$ (wet basis) - Bulk density $BD_{green} = 200$ kg/m³ - Kraft yield $\eta_K = 48\%$ **(i) Green mass per day:** $$ MC = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{OD}}{m_{wet}} $$ Rearranging: $$ m_{OD} = m_{wet} \times (1 - MC) $$ $$ m_{wet} = \dfrac{m_{OD}}{1 - MC} = \dfrac{500}{1 - 0.50} = 1000 \;\; \text{t/day (green basis)} $$ **Answer (i):** **1000 tonnes/day (green mass)** **(ii) Green volume per day:** $$ V_{green} = \dfrac{m_{wet}}{BD_{green}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1000}{200} = 5000 \;\; \text{m}^{3/day} $$ **Answer (ii):** **5000 m³/day** **(iii) Theoretical pulp output:** $$ m_{pulp} = m_{OD} \times \eta_K = 500 \times 0.48 = 240 \;\; \text{t/day} $$ **Answer (iii):** **240 tonnes/day of oven-dry Kraft pulp** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Moisture content basis confusion** — MC can be given as **wet basis** or **dry basis**. Always check. Formula changes: $MC_{wet} = (m_w - m_{OD})/m_w$ vs. $MC_{dry} = (m_w - m_{OD})/m_{OD}$. > 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Mixing kg, tonnes, m³, m³/min, kg/h in a single chain. **Always write units at every step** to avoid 0.5-mark cut per step. > 3. **Forgetting the feed factor $\eta_v$** — Without this, the answer will be **~67% too high** (since $\eta_v = 0.60$ in the worked example). > 4. **Forgetting to convert knife-cuts-per-minute to OD tonnes per hour** — Most KTU students stop at kg/min and lose the final 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing chipper tip speed** — Don't compute circumference using $2\pi r$ AND $N$ and forget to convert to per-second. > 6. **Omitting the "function" part** in unit operation questions — Marks are split equally between diagram and function descriptions. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Wood Chip Production in Integrated Pulp & Paper Mill:** - ✅ An **integrated mill** performs wood preparation → pulping → papermaking → finishing in one location, with closed-loop chemical and energy recovery. - ✅ **Wood chips** are standardized particles — **15–30 mm length, 3–7 mm thickness, 15–25 mm width** — produced by debarking and chipping logs. - ✅ The **six stages** are: log receiving → debarking → chipping → screening → storage → washing/metering. - ✅ **Debarking** removes bark (no fiber, dark specks, grit) using rotary drum or hydraulic debarkers. - ✅ **Disc chipper** is the most common — high capacity (50–300 ODt/h), uniform chips, tip speed 60–90 m/s. - ✅ **Drum chipper** uses peripheral knives on a rotating drum; lower capacity; best for softwood straight logs. - ✅ **Vibratory screens** classify chips into **accept, overs (→ re-chipper), and fines (→ boiler fuel)**. - ✅ **Bulk density** of chips is **180–250 kg/m³ (OD basis)** — critical for digester volumetric capacity. - ✅ **Moisture content** of green wood is **40–55%**; must be accounted for in all throughput calculations. - ✅ **Fines < 2%, overs < 5%, bark < 1%** are the industry quality targets (SCAN-CM 40:94). - ✅ **Softwood chips** → longer fiber → higher tear strength; **Hardwood chips** → shorter fiber → smoother, more opaque paper. - ✅ **Kraft yield** from chips is typically **45–55%** (i.e., ~50% of wood mass becomes usable pulp). - ✅ **Black liquor recovery loop** (digester → recovery boiler → digester) is the defining feature of an integrated Kraft mill. - ✅ **Key formula to memorize**: $\dot{m}_{OD} = \eta_v \times k \times N \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_{log}^{\,2} \times L_{log} \times \rho \times (1-MC)$. - ✅ **Standards to cite in answers**: SCAN-CM 40:94 (chip classification), TAPPI T 257 cm-12 (sampling), TAPPI T 264 (bark content).

topics.title: Batch Digester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Batch Digester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Batch Digester** is a large, pressurized, cylindrical reaction vessel (reactor) used in the pulp and paper manufacturing industry to chemically convert lignocellulosic raw materials (wood chips, bagasse, bamboo, or agricultural residues) into chemical pulp by cooking them with a suitable cooking liquor under controlled conditions of **temperature**, **pressure**, and **time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPET205 – Module 4):**
> A Batch Digester is studied as a classic example of a **batch-type chemical processing equipment** used in process manufacturing. The syllabus focus is on its constructional features, the stepwise batch operating cycle, and the critical process parameters (temperature, pressure, residence time, liquor-to-wood ratio, active alkali charge) that determine pulp quality and yield.

In the dominant **Kraft (Sulfate) Process**, the cooking liquor is known as **White Liquor** — an aqueous solution of **Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH)** and **Sodium Sulfide (Na₂S)** — and the digester is charged, heated, cooked, and discharged in a **discrete, non-continuous cycle**, hence the name *"batch"*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Capacity (Industry Benchmark):** Industrial batch digesters typically range from **50 m³ to 250 m³** in volume, with operating pressures of **800 kPa to 1100 kPa** (≈ 8–11 bar) and cooking temperatures between **165 °C and 175 °C**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" for Wood

Think of a batch digester as a **giant industrial pressure cooker** designed specifically to "boil" wood chips in a strong chemical soup until the natural glue (lignin) holding the wood fibers together dissolves away, leaving behind clean cellulose fibers (pulp) for making paper.

**Plain-English Walkthrough:**

1. **Imagine a massive stainless-steel pressure cooker** standing as tall as a 4-storey building, capable of holding 50–250 cubic meters of material.
2. **You fill it with wood chips** (just like rice grains fill a domestic cooker), then pour in a strong alkaline chemical soup (White Liquor) — analogous to adding water and salt.
3. **You lock the lid tightly** and start heating from the outside using steam. As temperature rises, the chemicals attack and dissolve the lignin in the wood.
4. **After a precise cooking time** (typically 90–150 minutes at peak temperature), the contents are forcefully blown out into a receiving tank.
5. The cooked mass is then washed, screened, and bleached — eventually becoming the paper used in notebooks, newspapers, and packaging.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Batch"?** Unlike a continuous digester where chips enter and pulp exits constantly, a batch digester processes wood in **discrete charges** — fill → cook → empty → repeat — much like a bakery baking one tray of bread at a time.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Batch Digesters

Batch digesters are primarily classified based on **heating method** and **vessel orientation**:

| Classification Basis | Type | Salient Feature |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel Orientation | **Vertical (Stationary) Digester** | Tall upright cylinder; most common in modern mills |
| Vessel Orientation | **Horizontal (Rotary) Digester** | Cylindrical vessel that rotates slowly; older design |
| Heating Mode | **Direct Steam Heating** | Steam injected directly into liquor |
| Heating Mode | **Indirect (Forced Circulation) Heating** | Liquor circulated through external heat exchangers |
| Process Type | **Kraft (Sulfate) Digester** | Uses white liquor (NaOH + Na₂S) |
| Process Type | **Soda Digester** | Uses only NaOH solution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Focus:** The **Vertical Stationary Batch Digester with Forced Circulation** is the most academically and industrially relevant configuration and forms the basis of most KTU board questions on this topic.

---

## 1.4 Industrial Significance

- **Pulp yield** from a batch digester typically ranges between **45 % and 55 %** (oven-dry pulp basis).
- The **H-Factor** (a combined time-temperature severity index) is the single most important control parameter in batch digester operation.
- Batch digesters, despite being older technology than continuous digesters, are still preferred for **specialty pulps**, **agricultural residues**, and **small-to-medium capacity mills** due to their operational flexibility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features of a Vertical Batch Digester

A modern vertical batch digester consists of the following major components:

1. **Digester Shell (Vessel)**
   - Fabricated from carbon steel clad with stainless steel (typically SS-304L or SS-316L) on the inner surface to resist corrosion from alkaline liquor.
   - Top is a **conical or domed cap**; bottom is a **conical discharge hopper**.
   - Designed as per **ASME Section VIII** for unfired pressure vessels.

2. **Chip Packing System (Top Charging)**
   - A **chip chute** at the top allows wood chips to be charged by gravity or low-pressure steam packing.
   - A **rotary feeder** or **vibratory screw feeder** ensures uniform chip distribution and seals the vessel.

3. **Liquor Injection and Circulation System**
   - White liquor and black liquor are introduced through **liquor rings** with multiple spray nozzles.
   - In **forced circulation** digesters, the liquor is pumped out from the middle/lower section, passed through a **screen (extractor)**, heated in an external **heat exchanger** using steam, and re-injected at the top — ensuring uniform temperature profile.

4. **Heating System**
   - **Direct Heating:** Steam injected directly via spargers — simple but dilutes the liquor.
   - **Indirect Heating:** Heat exchangers — preferred for **better temperature control** and **higher solids concentration**.

5. **Relief and Safety Devices**
   - **Pressure Relief Valves (PRV)** — set typically at **1100–1200 kPa**.
   - **Blow Valves** at the bottom for rapid discharge.
   - **Rupture Discs** as a tertiary safety measure.
   - **Level Indicators, Temperature Probes (RTDs), and Pressure Transmitters** distributed along the height.

6. **Discharge System**
   - A large **blow valve** at the bottom releases the cooked pulp into a **blow tank** under sudden depressurization — the explosive decompression helps fiber separation.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The digester is essentially a **vertical shell-and-tube heat exchanger + chemical reactor** combined into one. The steam side is the heat source; the wood chip + liquor mixture is the reacting medium.

---

## 2.2 The Batch Operating Cycle — Stepwise Logic

A complete batch digester cycle consists of **six sequential stages**:

### Step 1: Chip Charging (≈ 10–15 minutes)
- Wood chips are fed from the top via the chip chute.
- **Steam packing** at low pressure (≈ 100–200 kPa) is applied to compact chips uniformly, minimizing air pockets (air causes oxidation and lowers pulp quality).
- The vessel is filled to about **70–80 %** of its total volume.

### Step 2: Liquor Filling (≈ 10–20 minutes)
- **White liquor** (and a portion of recovered **black liquor** for heat economy) is pumped in.
- The **liquor-to-wood ratio (L/W)** is maintained between **3.5 : 1 and 4.5 : 1** (mass basis).
- Liquor fills from the bottom upward to displace trapped air and ensure complete chip saturation.

### Step 3: Impregnation (≈ 30–60 minutes)
- The digester is held at a moderate temperature (typically **80–115 °C**) and pressure (**500–700 kPa**).
- Purpose: Allow the cooking chemicals to **diffuse uniformly** into the chip structure and saturate the cell walls before active delignification begins.
- This is a **mass-transfer-controlled** step.

### Step 4: Heating to Cooking Temperature (≈ 30–45 minutes)
- Steam is introduced (directly or indirectly) to raise the temperature from ~115 °C to the cooking temperature of **165–175 °C**.
- Heating rate is controlled (typically **1–2 °C/min**) to avoid uneven cooking and "burns" in the wood.

### Step 5: Cooking at Temperature (≈ 60–120 minutes)
- The digester is held at peak temperature for a precisely calculated **cooking time**.
- Active **delignification** occurs: lignin dissolves into the liquor (forming black liquor), while cellulose fibers remain largely intact.
- This is the **kinetically controlled** stage, governed by the **H-Factor**.

### Step 6: Blowing (Discharge) (≈ 10–15 minutes)
- The blow valve at the bottom is opened.
- The high internal pressure rapidly ejects the cooked pulp into a **blow tank**.
- The **explosive decompression** mechanically defibrates the cooked chips, separating fibers.
- Pressure drops from ~1100 kPa to atmospheric in seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Cycle Time:** Typically **3 to 5 hours** per batch. A typical mill runs **8 to 12 batches per day** per digester.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable, derived from first principles, and form the **core numerical toolkit** for KTU ESE questions on this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pulp Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{m_{pulp,\,od}}{m_{wood,\,od}} \times 100$ | % (oven-dry basis) | $m_{od}$ = oven-dry mass |
| 2 | **H-Factor (Vroom's Model)** | $H = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t_{cook}} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right) dt$ | Dimensionless (hours) | $T$ in **Kelvin**; $t$ in hours |
| 3 | **Relative Reaction Rate (k)** | $k = \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right)$ | hr⁻¹ | Arrhenius-type |
| 4 | **Active Alkali (AA)** | $AA = NaOH + \tfrac{1}{2}\,Na_2S$ | expressed as % Na₂O on wood | Standard pulp-industry convention |
| 5 | **Effective Alkali (EA)** | $EA = NaOH + \tfrac{1}{2}\,Na_2S$ | expressed as % Na₂O on wood | Same as AA (in Kraft process) |
| 6 | **Sulfidity (S)** | $S = \dfrac{Na_2S}{NaOH + Na_2S} \times 100$ | % | Typical Kraft: 25–35 % |
| 7 | **Liquor-to-Wood Ratio (L/W)** | $L/W = \dfrac{m_{liquor}}{m_{wood,\,od}}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 3.5 : 1 to 4.5 : 1 |
| 8 | **Steam Consumption (Approx.)** | $Q_{steam} = m_{wood} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{losses}$ | kJ / batch | $c_p$ of wood ≈ 1.3 kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | **Digester Production Rate** | $\dot{m}_{pulp} = \dfrac{V_{digester} \cdot \rho_{chips} \cdot Y}{t_{cycle}}$ | kg pulp / hr | $t_{cycle}$ includes all 6 stages |
| 10 | **Batch Time per Cycle** | $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{imp} + t_{heat} + t_{cook} + t_{blow}$ | hours | Total of all six stages |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Calculations:**
> When substituting temperature into the H-factor equation, **always convert Celsius to Kelvin** using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. This is a common pitfall in board exams.

---

## 2.4 The H-Factor — Concept and Significance

The **H-Factor** (developed by **Kenttä**, simplified by **Vroom**) is a **combined time-temperature severity index** that quantifies the cumulative cooking effect. It expresses the idea that **cooking damage is a function of BOTH time and temperature**, not either alone.

**Physical Interpretation:**
- A higher cooking temperature **dramatically** accelerates delignification (because the reaction rate follows an Arrhenius relationship).
- A short time at high temperature can produce the same degree of cooking as a long time at low temperature — provided the integrated H-Factor is identical.

**Engineering Utility:**
- It enables operators to **control pulp quality (Kappa number)** consistently across batches.
- Allows scaling cooking profiles from lab digesters to industrial digesters.
- Forms the basis of modern **Distributed Control System (DCS)** recipes in pulp mills.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the H-Factor Equation (Vroom's Model)

The delignification reaction in a Kraft digester is modeled as a **pseudo-first-order reaction** following the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = reaction rate constant (hr⁻¹)
- $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 1: Apply Empirical Constants for Kraft Delignification

For the bulk delignification phase of the Kraft process, empirically fitted values are:

$$A = e^{43.2} \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{E_a}{R} = 16{,}113 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Arrhenius

Substituting the empirical constants into the general Arrhenius expression:

$$k = e^{43.2} \cdot e^{-16{,}113 / T}$$

Applying the rule of exponents $a^x \cdot b^x = (ab)^x$:

$$k = \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right)$$

This gives the **relative reaction rate** at temperature $T$.

### Step 3: Integrate Over Cooking Time

The cumulative cooking effect (H-Factor) is the time-integral of this rate:

$$H = \int_{0}^{t_{cook}} k(T) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_{cook}} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T(t)}\right) dt$$

### Step 4: Discrete Approximation for Numerical Use

In practice, with step-wise temperature data, this is evaluated numerically as:

$$H \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T_i}\right) \cdot \Delta t_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The H-Factor is **dimensionless in concept but typically reported in hours**. Always show the units of $T$ in **Kelvin** in your answer script.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — H-Factor Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A batch digester cooks wood chips for **2 hours** at a constant temperature of **170 °C**. Calculate the H-Factor.

### Step 1: Convert Temperature to Kelvin

$$T = 170 + 273.15 = 443.15 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Relative Reaction Rate

$$k = \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{443.15}\right)$$

Computing the exponent term:

$$\dfrac{16{,}113}{443.15} = 36.3628$$

$$43.2 - 36.3628 = 6.8372$$

$$k = e^{6.8372}$$

Computing the exponential:

$$k = 929.86 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

### Step 3: Compute the H-Factor (Constant Temperature Integration)

Since $T$ is constant, the integral simplifies to:

$$H = k \cdot t = 929.86 \times 2 = 1859.7$$

$$\boxed{H \approx 1860 \text{ hours}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Cooking Time Adjustment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Kraft digester normally cooks at **170 °C for 2 hours**, giving $H = 1860$ hr. If the operator wants to cook at a **higher temperature of 175 °C**, what cooking time is needed to deliver the **same H-Factor**?

### Step 1: Compute the Reaction Rate at 175 °C

$$T = 175 + 273.15 = 448.15 \text{ K}$$

$$\dfrac{16{,}113}{448.15} = 35.9531$$

$$43.2 - 35.9531 = 7.2469$$

$$k_{175} = e^{7.2469} = 1402.4 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

### Step 2: Equate H-Factors

$$H_{170} = H_{175}$$

$$k_{170} \cdot t_{170} = k_{175} \cdot t_{175}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $t_{175}$

$$t_{175} = \dfrac{k_{170} \cdot t_{170}}{k_{175}}$$

$$t_{175} = \dfrac{929.86 \times 2}{1402.4}$$

$$t_{175} = \dfrac{1859.7}{1402.4}$$

$$\boxed{t_{175} = 1.326 \text{ hours} \approx 79.6 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A mere **5 °C increase in temperature reduces cooking time by ~34 %**. This is why precise temperature control in a batch digester is economically critical — a 1 °C drift can shift pulp quality measurably.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pulp Yield and Production Rate

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A vertical batch digester of capacity **120 m³** is charged with wood chips at a bulk density of **220 kg/m³** (oven-dry basis). The cooking cycle produces a pulp yield of **48 %** (oven-dry basis). The total batch cycle time (fill + impregnation + heat + cook + blow) is **4.5 hours**. Calculate the **oven-dry pulp production rate** in **kg/hr**.

### Step 1: Mass of Wood Charged

$$m_{wood} = V_{digester} \times \rho_{chips} = 120 \times 220 = 26{,}400 \text{ kg (od basis)}$$

### Step 2: Mass of Pulp Produced per Batch

$$m_{pulp} = Y \times m_{wood} = 0.48 \times 26{,}400 = 12{,}672 \text{ kg (od basis)}$$

### Step 3: Production Rate

$$\dot{m}_{pulp} = \dfrac{m_{pulp}}{t_{cycle}} = \dfrac{12{,}672}{4.5}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{pulp} = 2816 \text{ kg/hr (oven-dry basis)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Context:** A single 120 m³ digester producing ~2800 kg/hr is typical for a mid-sized mill. Modern continuous digesters achieve **3–5 times** higher throughput per unit volume, which is why batch digesters are reserved for specialty grades.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Stage | Governing Phenomenon | Critical Parameter | Typical Range | Control Variable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chip Charging | Bulk solid handling | Chip packing density | 180–240 kg/m³ (od) | Steam packing pressure |
| Liquor Filling | Liquid displacement | L/W ratio | 3.5 : 1 to 4.5 : 1 | Liquor flow rate |
| Impregnation | Mass transfer (diffusion) | Temperature, time | 80–115 °C, 30–60 min | Liquor circulation rate |
| Heating | Heat transfer | Heating rate | 1–2 °C/min | Steam flow |
| Cooking | Chemical kinetics (Arrhenius) | H-Factor | 800–2000 hr | T-profile + time |
| Blowing | Rapid depressurization | Discharge pressure | ~1100 kPa → 0 | Blow valve opening time |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component-Level Block Diagram of a Vertical Batch Digester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wood Chip Silo] --> B[Chip Chute and Feeder]
    B --> C[Top Charging Port]
    C --> D[Digester Vessel Clad SS-304L]
    D --> E[White Liquor Inlet Ring with Spray Nozzles]
    D --> F[Black Liquor Recirculation Extractor Screens]
    F --> G[Recirculation Pump]
    G --> H[External Heat Exchanger Steam Heated]
    H --> I[Heated Liquor Return to Top]
    D --> J[Steam Sparrers for Direct Heating]
    D --> K[RTD Temperature Probes Multiple Levels]
    D --> L[Pressure Transmitter and PRV]
    D --> M[Bottom Blow Valve]
    M --> N[Blow Tank and Pulp Discharge]
    O[DCS Control System] --> K
    O --> L
    O --> G
    O --> M
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**
- Solid arrows show the **flow path of materials** (chips, liquor, steam, pulp).
- The **DCS block** on the right represents the Distributed Control System that monitors temperature, pressure, and controls valves in real time.
- The **recirculation loop** (F → G → H → I → D) is the heart of forced-circulation heating.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Batch Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Chip Charging 10 to 15 min] --> S2[Stage 2 Liquor Filling 10 to 20 min]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Impregnation 30 to 60 min]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Heating to Cook Temp 30 to 45 min]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Cooking at 165 to 175 C 60 to 120 min]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Blowing and Discharge 10 to 15 min]
    S6 --> S1
```

**Interpretation:**
- This is a **closed cyclic process** — after the blow stage, the digester is ready for the next batch.
- The **sum of all six stage times** = total batch cycle time (typically 3–5 hours).
- This cycle is **repeated continuously**, typically **8–12 times per day** per digester.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Material and Energy Streams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUTS
        W[Wood Chips Oven Dry]
        WL[White Liquor NaOH plus Na2S]
        S[Steam from Boiler]
        BL[Recovered Black Liquor]
    end

    subgraph BATCH_DIGESTER
        R1[Charging and Impregnation Zone 80 to 115 C]
        R2[Heating Zone 115 to 165 C]
        R3[Cooking Zone 165 to 175 C]
        R4[Discharge Zone Blow]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS
        P[Chemical Pulp Cellulose Fibers]
        BLQ[Spent Black Liquor containing Lignin]
        COND[Recovered Condensate from Heating]
    end

    W --> R1
    WL --> R1
    BL --> R1
    S --> R2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> P
    R4 --> BLQ
    R2 --> COND
    COND --> BL
```

**Interpretation:**
- The diagram shows **three input streams** (wood, white liquor, steam) entering the digester and **three output streams** (pulp, black liquor, condensate) leaving it.
- The **black liquor** is sent to the chemical recovery section (not covered in Module 4, but contextually important).
- The **condensate** from indirect heating is recycled — a key energy-economy feature.

---

## 4.4 Temperature–Time Profile During a Typical Batch Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[0 min: 25 C Ambient] --> P2[15 min: 100 C Steam Packing]
    P2 --> P3[45 min: 115 C Impregnation Hold]
    P3 --> P4[90 min: 170 C Heating Completed]
    P4 --> P5[180 min: 170 C Cooking End]
    P5 --> P6[195 min: Blow to Discharge]
```

**Key Reading:** The **flat top** between P4 and P5 represents the **isothermal cooking phase** — the bulk of the H-Factor is accumulated here.
