## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define a batch digester. List any four major components of a vertical batch digester.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A batch digester is a large, pressurized cylindrical vessel used in the pulp and paper industry to convert lignocellulosic raw materials (wood chips) into chemical pulp by cooking them with an alkaline cooking liquor (white liquor) under controlled temperature, pressure, and time, in discrete non-continuous batches.

**Four Major Components (½ Mark each):**
1. **Digester Shell** – carbon steel clad with stainless steel, designed as per ASME Section VIII.
2. **Chip Charging System** – top-mounted chip chute with steam packing arrangement.
3. **Liquor Circulation System** – extractor screens, recirculation pump, heat exchanger.
4. **Blow Valve and Discharge System** – bottom-mounted for rapid depressurization and pulp discharge.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]
**What is the H-Factor? State its significance in batch digester operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** The H-Factor is a combined time-temperature severity index used to quantify the cumulative cooking effect in a batch digester. It is defined mathematically as the time-integral of the relative reaction rate of delignification:

$$H = \int_{0}^{t_{cook}} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right) dt$$

where $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin and $t$ is time in hours.

**Significance (1.5 Marks):**
1. It enables **consistent control of pulp quality (Kappa number)** across batches by combining time and temperature into a single variable.
2. It allows **scaling of cooking profiles** from laboratory to industrial digesters.
3. It forms the **basis of DCS-based cooking recipes** in modern pulp mills.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions — Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **constructional features** of a **vertical batch digester** used in the Kraft pulp process. Clearly label all major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete batch operating cycle** of a digester in detail, stating the typical temperature, pressure, and duration at each stage. What is the role of **impregnation** in this cycle? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a): Constructional Features (7 Marks)

A vertical batch digester is essentially a tall, upright, pressurized cylindrical vessel. Its major constructional features are:

1. **Digester Shell (2 Marks):** The vessel is fabricated from **carbon steel** with an inner lining of **austenitic stainless steel (SS-304L or SS-316L)** to resist corrosion from hot alkaline cooking liquor. It is designed as a **Class I pressure vessel** in accordance with **ASME Section VIII** for unfired pressure vessels. The top is closed by a **conical or hemispherical cap**, and the bottom tapers into a **conical discharge hopper** to facilitate pulp flow during blowing.

2. **Chip Charging System (1.5 Marks):** Wood chips are introduced at the top through a **chip chute** equipped with a **rotary feeder** or vibratory screw feeder. **Low-pressure steam packing** (100–200 kPa) is used to compact the chip bed uniformly and to displace air, preventing oxidation reactions that would darken the pulp.

3. **Liquor Circulation and Heating System (2 Marks):** White liquor and recovered black liquor are introduced through a **liquor ring** with multiple spray nozzles near the top. In **forced circulation** design, the liquor is drawn from middle/lower extractor screens, pumped through an **external shell-and-tube heat exchanger** heated by steam, and re-injected at the top. This ensures **uniform temperature distribution** throughout the chip bed.

4. **Instrumentation and Safety Devices (1 Mark):** Multiple **RTD temperature probes**, **pressure transmitters**, and **level indicators** are distributed along the height. **Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs)**, **rupture discs**, and a **blow valve** at the bottom are provided for safe operation.

5. **Discharge System (0.5 Mark):** A large, quick-opening **blow valve** at the bottom cone discharges cooked pulp into a blow tank under explosive depressurization, which mechanically separates fibers.

*Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram for a complete schematic view.*

---

#### Model Solution for (b): Batch Operating Cycle (7 Marks)

A complete batch digester cycle consists of **six stages** (refer to the cycle diagram in Section 4.2):

| Stage | Duration | Temperature | Pressure | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Chip Charging | 10–15 min | Ambient (25 °C) | 100–200 kPa (steam packing) | Fill vessel uniformly |
| 2. Liquor Filling | 10–20 min | 25–80 °C | 200–400 kPa | Saturate chips with liquor |
| 3. Impregnation | 30–60 min | 80–115 °C | 500–700 kPa | **Diffuse chemicals into chips** |
| 4. Heating to Cook T | 30–45 min | 115–170 °C | 700–1100 kPa | Raise to cooking temperature |
| 5. Cooking | 60–120 min | 165–175 °C | 800–1100 kPa | **Bulk delignification** |
| 6. Blowing | 10–15 min | Drops to 25 °C | 1100 kPa → 0 kPa | Discharge pulp |

**Role of Impregnation (3 Marks):**
Impregnation is a **mass-transfer-controlled** stage where cooking chemicals (NaOH and Na₂S) **diffuse uniformly** into the porous wood chip structure under moderate temperature (80–115 °C) and pressure (500–700 kPa). Without proper impregnation, the chip interior would be **under-cooked** while the exterior would be **over-cooked**, leading to:
- **High rejects** (uncooked chip portions)
- **Unacceptably high Kappa number variation**
- **Poor pulp strength properties**

Impregnation therefore **ensures uniform chemical distribution** before active delignification begins, dramatically improving pulp quality consistency.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Derive the **H-Factor equation** for a batch digester starting from the **Arrhenius equation**. State clearly the empirical constants used and their physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A batch digester cooks wood chips at **170 °C for 2 hours**. The operator wishes to **reduce cooking time by 25 %** while maintaining the **same H-Factor**. Calculate the **new cooking temperature** required. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a): H-Factor Derivation (7 Marks)

*(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation. Summary below:)*

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks):** The Kraft delignification reaction in a batch digester follows **first-order kinetics** with the rate constant described by the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where $k$ = rate constant (hr⁻¹), $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), and $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** For the **bulk delignification phase** of the Kraft process, empirical fitting gives:
- $\ln A = 43.2$, so $A = e^{43.2}$
- $E_a / R = 16{,}113 \text{ K}$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Substituting these into the Arrhenius equation:

$$k = e^{43.2} \cdot e^{-16{,}113 / T} = \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right)$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** The cumulative cooking effect (H-Factor) is the **time-integral** of the relative reaction rate from the start to the end of cooking:

$$\boxed{H = \int_{0}^{t_{cook}} \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T}\right) dt}$$

**Physical Significance of Constants:**
- The constant **43.2** (in the exponent) reflects the pre-exponential factor — it sets the baseline rate at very high temperatures.
- The constant **16,113 K** represents $E_a / R$ — the activation energy normalized by the gas constant. Its large magnitude reflects the **strong temperature sensitivity** of the Kraft delignification reaction.

---

#### Model Solution for (b): Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Initial temperature $T_1 = 170$ °C = **443.15 K**
- Initial cooking time $t_1 = 2$ hours
- New cooking time: reduced by 25 %, so $t_2 = 2 \times 0.75 =$ **1.5 hours**
- Same H-Factor: $H_1 = H_2$

**Step 1: Compute the Reaction Rate at 170 °C (1.5 Marks)**

$$k_1 = \exp\!\left(43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{443.15}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{16{,}113}{443.15} = 36.3628$$

$$k_1 = \exp(43.2 - 36.3628) = e^{6.8372} = 929.86 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Original H-Factor (1 Mark)**

$$H = k_1 \cdot t_1 = 929.86 \times 2 = 1859.7 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Equate H-Factors and Solve for $k_2$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$k_1 \cdot t_1 = k_2 \cdot t_2$$

$$k_2 = \dfrac{H}{t_2} = \dfrac{1859.7}{1.5} = 1239.8 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the New Temperature $T_2$ (3 Marks)**

From $k_2 = \exp(43.2 - 16{,}113 / T_2)$:

$$\ln(k_2) = 43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T_2}$$

$$\ln(1239.8) = 7.1224$$

$$7.1224 = 43.2 - \dfrac{16{,}113}{T_2}$$

$$\dfrac{16{,}113}{T_2} = 43.2 - 7.1224 = 36.0776$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{16{,}113}{36.0776} = 446.6 \text{ K}$$

**Convert to Celsius:**

$$T_2 = 446.6 - 273.15 = 173.45 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 173.5 \text{ °C}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** To **save 25 % cooking time** (from 2 hr to 1.5 hr) at constant pulp quality, the cooking temperature must be raised by only **~3.5 °C** — illustrating the **exponential sensitivity** of the Kraft cooking process to temperature.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Batch Digester Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K in the H-Factor equation.** A student who substitutes 170 directly into $16{,}113/T$ will get an answer off by an order of magnitude and lose **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Active Alkali (AA) and Effective Alkali (EA).** In the Kraft process, they are numerically equal (both = NaOH + ½ Na₂S as % Na₂O), but the terms have different industrial meanings and examiners often test this.
> 3. **Writing the six stages of the batch cycle in the wrong order** (e.g., cooking before impregnation). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for incorrect sequencing.
> 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — the H-Factor is conventionally reported in **hours**; pulp yield in **%**; production rate in **kg/hr**. Missing units = **½ to 1 mark** lost.
> 5. **Sketching the digester without labels or arrows** — a labeled block diagram carries **full marks**, but an unlabeled sketch (even if artistically beautiful) gets only partial credit.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Batch Digester (PCPET205 – Module 4):**

- ✅ A **batch digester** is a pressurized, cylindrical, non-continuous reactor for producing chemical pulp from lignocellulosic biomass.
- ✅ The **Kraft (Sulfate) process** uses **White Liquor (NaOH + Na₂S)** — this is the most commonly tested process.
- ✅ The vessel is typically made of **carbon steel clad with stainless steel** (SS-304L / SS-316L) to resist alkaline corrosion.
- ✅ **Six-stage batch cycle:** Charging → Liquor Filling → Impregnation → Heating → Cooking → Blowing.
- ✅ **Impregnation** is mass-transfer controlled; **cooking** is kinetically controlled.
- ✅ **Operating pressure:** 800–1100 kPa; **cooking temperature:** 165–175 °C; **L/W ratio:** 3.5:1 to 4.5:1.
- ✅ **H-Factor** combines time and temperature: $H = \int \exp(43.2 - 16{,}113/T)\, dt$, with $T$ in **Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Pulp yield** in Kraft process: 45–55 % (oven-dry basis).
- ✅ **Sulfidity:** typically **25–35 %** in modern Kraft mills.
- ✅ **Active Alkali** = NaOH + ½ Na₂S (expressed as % Na₂O on oven-dry wood).
- ✅ The H-Factor is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature — a 5 °C rise cuts cooking time by ~30 %.
- ✅ **Blowing** (sudden depressurization) serves two purposes: (1) rapid discharge, and (2) mechanical defibrating of cooked chips.
- ✅ Batch digesters are **less efficient** than continuous digesters but offer **greater operational flexibility** for specialty pulps and small-capacity mills.
- ✅ A **block diagram of the digester** showing chip inlet, liquor inlet, heating loop, control system, and blow outlet is a **must-draw** for KTU 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulp Washing Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pulp Washing Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pulp Washing** is a critical mass-transfer unit operation in the pulp and paper manufacturing process, performed immediately after the **digestion (cooking) stage**, in which dissolved solids (lignin, hemicellulose, and spent cooking chemicals collectively known as *black liquor*) are separated from the cellulose fiber suspension (pulp) using a **displacement or dilution-thickening mechanism**.

A **Pulp Washing Filter** is therefore the engineered equipment (most commonly a *Rotary Vacuum Filter*, *Pressure Diffuser*, or *Horizontal Belt Filter*) that physically implements this separation, recovering both clean pulp for downstream bleaching and concentrated black liquor for the chemical recovery boiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Pulp washing is a *solid-liquid separation* operation, not a true chemical reaction. The objective is to **maximize recovery of dissolved cooking liquor** while **minimizing fresh water consumption** and **energy used in subsequent evaporation** of the dilute black liquor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a sponge that has been soaked in a thick, dark coffee solution (the cooked pulp + black liquor). Imagine you want to clean the sponge without wasting the coffee:

- **Step 1:** You place the sponge on a perforated tray (the filter medium).
- **Step 2:** You pour clean water from the top (washing liquor).
- **Step 3:** Gravity and/or suction (vacuum) pulls the water through, *displacing* the coffee downward and out — *without diluting* it much.

The **displaced** coffee is *concentrated* (good for reuse), and the **sponge** is left *clean* (good for paper-making). The efficiency of this "sponge-cleaning" defines the **Washing Efficiency** of the filter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:** A modern Indian pulp mill producing **500 tonnes/day** of pulp can recover over **99% of the cooking chemicals** through an efficient multi-stage washing train, saving crores of rupees annually in NaOH and Na₂S makeup costs.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

The following metrics are universally accepted in pulp and paper engineering practice:

- **Displacement Ratio (DR)** — dimensionless, ideally approaching **1.0** for perfect plug-flow displacement.
- **Washing Efficiency (η or E)** — dimensionless, expressed as a percentage (**0–100 %**).
- **Norden Efficiency Factor (NEF)** — dimensionless, used to compare filter performance.
- **Dilution Factor (DF)** — typically reported in the range of **1.5 to 3.0** (kg of dilution liquor per kg of oven-dry pulp).
- **Wash Liquor-to-Pulp Ratio** — expressed as **m³ liquor / tonne oven-dry pulp (ODP)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physical Principle

Pulp washing operates on two complementary mass-transfer principles:

1. **Displacement Washing (Advection-Dominated):** Fresh wash liquor physically pushes the existing black liquor out of the fiber mat. This is fast and produces *concentrated* liquor.
2. **Diffusion Washing (Dispersion-Dominated):** The remaining liquor trapped *inside* the cell walls (lumen) and fiber pores diffuses out by concentration gradient. This is slow and produces *dilute* liquor.

A high-performance filter **maximizes displacement and minimizes diffusion losses**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Multi-Stage Counter-Current Washing Train

- **Step 1 — Thickening Zone:** Pulp from the digester enters the filter and is *dewatered* by vacuum/pressure, raising consistency from ~10% to ~25–35%.
- **Step 2 — First Wash Zone:** Wash liquor (shower #1) is sprayed on the pulp mat. The filtrate is **strongest in black liquor** (sent to evaporation).
- **Step 3 — Second Wash Zone:** A weaker wash liquor is applied. The filtrate becomes the *wash liquor for the previous stage* — this is **counter-current flow**.
- **Step 4 — Final Wash Zone:** Cleanest water is applied last. The filtrate from this stage becomes the wash for the *previous* zone, cascading backward.
- **Step 5 — Discharge:** Washed pulp is scraped off the drum/belt at ~25–30% consistency and sent to the bleach plant.

### 2.3 Types of Pulp Washing Filters (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| **Filter Type** | **Driving Force** | **Typical Consistency (In → Out)** | **Best For** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotary Vacuum Filter (RVF) | Vacuum (−50 to −80 kPa) | 1–2 % → 25–30 % | Hardwood & softwood kraft mills |
| Pressure Diffuser (PD) | Internal pressurized liquor | 8–12 % → 30–35 % | High-DR, low-effluent mills |
| Horizontal Belt Filter (HBF) | Vacuum + gravity | 2–3 % → 30–35 % | High-capacity, low-energy mills |
| Drum Washer (DD - Decker/Dorr) | Vacuum, submergence | 0.8–1.5 % → 12–18 % | Pre-washing / de-inking |
| Twin-Roll Press (TRP) | Mechanical squeeze | 3–5 % → 35–45 % | Post-wash dewatering / energy saving |
| Screw Press (SP) | Mechanical compression | 5–10 % → 40–50 % | Rejects thickening, dewatering |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **only** formulae a KTU 2024 board examiner expects for this topic. Master them and you secure the full 14 marks.

| **#** | **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Range** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Displacement Ratio | $DR = \dfrac{V_{wash}}{V_{liquor\,in\,mat}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le DR \le 1$ | Fraction of original liquor replaced by wash |
| 2 | Washing Efficiency (1-stage) | $E = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in} - C_{wash}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le E \le 1$ | Approach to ideal displacement |
| 3 | Norden Efficiency Factor | $NEF = E \cdot (n - 1)$ | dimensionless | Compares multi-stage washers |
| 4 | Dilution Factor | $DF = \dfrac{M_{diluted\,liquor}}{M_{oven\,dry\,pulp}}$ | kg/kg or m³/t ODP, typical 1.5–3.0 | Water added per tonne pulp |
| 5 | Solute Recovery (Black Liquor) | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out} \cdot V_{out}}{C_{in} \cdot V_{in}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ | Fraction of chemical recovered |
| 6 | Mass Balance on Solute | $L_p C_p = L_w C_w$ | kg of solute | Solute in = Solute out (steady state) |
| 7 | Plug-Flow Displacement Curve | $C/C_0 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x - vt}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | concentration profile | Ideal axial mixing model |
| 8 | Cake Consistency on Filter | $C_{cake} = \dfrac{M_{pulp}}{M_{pulp} + M_{liquor}} \times 100$ | % | Solid fraction in discharged mat |
| 9 | Filter Throughput (mass) | $\dot{m} = A \cdot v \cdot \rho_{cake}$ | tonnes/h | Production rate of filter |
| 10 | Vacuum Pump Power | $P_v = \dot{Q} \cdot \Delta P / \eta_v$ | kW | Energy to drive vacuum |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Sector** | **Use of Pulp Washing Filter Knowledge** |
|---|---|
| Kraft Paper Mills (e.g., ITC Bhadrachalam) | Sizing of multi-stage RVF battery to recover > 98 % NaOH |
| Bleached Pulp Production | Minimizing COD load entering bleach plant (BOD reduction) |
| Effluent Treatment Plants | Designing equalization tanks based on DR/DF output |
| Energy Auditing | Reducing evaporation load in recovery boiler = steam saving |
| Process Control Engineering | Tuning shower flows and vacuum levels via DCS feedback |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Stage Washing Efficiency Equation

We start from a **macroscopic solute mass balance** on a single filter stage. Let:
- $L_0$ = mass (or volume) of liquor carried in by the pulp feed
- $C_0$ = concentration of solute (e.g., total dissolved solids) in this incoming liquor
- $L_w$ = mass of wash liquor sprayed on the mat
- $C_w$ = concentration of solute in the wash liquor
- $L_1$ = mass of liquor leaving in the washed pulp
- $C_1$ = concentration of solute in the outgoing liquor

**Step 1 — Solute Mass Balance (Input):**

Solute entering the stage comes from two sources: the liquor carried in with the pulp, and the wash liquor itself.

$$S_{in} = L_0 \cdot C_0 + L_w \cdot C_w$$

**Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance (Output):**

Solute leaving the stage exits with the discharged (washed) pulp.

$$S_{out} = L_1 \cdot C_1$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Assumption:**

For a continuously operating filter, the mat moves at a steady rate, so accumulation is zero.

$$S_{in} = S_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Rearrange:**

$$L_0 \cdot C_0 + L_w \cdot C_w = L_1 \cdot C_1$$

**Step 5 — Define "Ideal" Displacement:**

In a *perfectly ideal* displacement washer, $L_1 = L_0 + L_w$ (liquor volume is conserved). The outgoing solute would all be the wash liquor concentration: $C_{1,ideal} = C_w$.

**Step 6 — Form the Efficiency Ratio:**

The washing efficiency $E$ is defined as the *fractional approach* of the actual outlet concentration toward the ideal outlet concentration.

$$E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w}$$

**Step 7 — Final Generalized Form:**

Therefore, the universal single-stage washing efficiency expression, with $C_w$ being the wash-liquor concentration entering, is:

$$E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w}$$

This derivation shows that $E = 0$ when the filter does *no* washing ($C_1 = C_0$), and $E = 1$ when the filter performs *perfect* displacement ($C_1 = C_w$).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Norden Efficiency Factor (NEF)

The Norden Factor allows a *fair* comparison between washers of different stages. The derivation proceeds by examining two extreme cases:

**Step 1 — Reference: Single-Stage Washer (n = 1):**

For one stage, the maximum achievable efficiency is by definition $E_{1} = E$.

**Step 2 — Reference: n-Stage Counter-Current Washer:**

In an ideal counter-current $n$-stage battery, the overall efficiency is given by:

$$E_{overall} = 1 - (1 - E)^n$$

**Step 3 — Define Norden Factor as "Effective Stages":**

Norden defined a *figure of merit* equal to the number of "perfect" displacement stages that would give the same overall efficiency:

$$NEF = E_{overall} \cdot (n - 1) \cdot \dfrac{1}{E_{ideal}}$$

For $E_{ideal} \approx 1$, this simplifies to the **classic Norden expression**:

$$NEF = E_{overall} \cdot (n - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Illustration:**

Suppose a 3-stage RVF has $E = 0.65$ per stage. Then:

$$E_{overall} = 1 - (1 - 0.65)^3 = 1 - (0.35)^3 = 1 - 0.0429 = 0.957$$

$$NEF = 0.957 \cdot (3 - 1) = 1.914 \approx 1.9$$

This means the 3-stage RVF performs equivalent to **1.9 ideal displacement stages** — a key metric for KTU numerical problems.

### 3.3 Derivation of Displacement Ratio from Volume Balance

**Step 1 — Volumetric Hold-up in Mat:**

The liquor volume retained in the pulp mat after the *thickening* (dewatering) zone is:

$$V_{hold} = \dfrac{m_{pulp} \cdot (1 - c_{thick})}{c_{thick} \cdot \rho_{liquor}}$$

where $c_{thick}$ is the mass fraction of liquor in the thickened mat, and $\rho_{liquor}$ is the liquor density.

**Step 2 — Wash Liquor Volume Applied:**

The volume of wash liquor sprayed per unit time on the mat is $V_{wash}$.

**Step 3 — Displacement Ratio Definition:**

By definition:

$$DR = \dfrac{V_{wash}}{V_{hold}}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting Cases:**

- If $DR = 0$: No wash applied → no displacement.
- If $DR = 1$: Wash volume exactly equals hold-up → all liquor replaced.
- If $DR > 1$: Excess wash applied → diminishing returns, but $C_1 \to C_w$.

**Step 5 — Relation to Efficiency (for $C_w \approx 0$, i.e., clean water wash):**

For the common industrial case where wash liquor is essentially clean water, the simplification $C_w = 0$ yields:

$$E = 1 - \dfrac{C_1}{C_0}$$

Combining with the volume ratio:

$$C_1 = C_0 \cdot e^{-DR} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E = 1 - e^{-DR}$$

This exponential relationship is the **single most-asked derivation in KTU Module 4 viva voce and 14-mark problems**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Filter Selection Decision Table

| **Decision Criterion** | **RVF** | **Pressure Diffuser** | **HBF** | **TRP** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capital Cost | Medium | High | High | Low |
| Operating Cost (Energy) | Medium | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| Black Liquor Concentration (g/L) | 130–170 | 150–180 | 140–175 | 180+ |
| Washing Efficiency (%) | 92–96 | 96–98 | 95–98 | 60–75 (single stage) |
| Dilution Factor (m³/t) | 2.0–3.0 | 1.5–2.0 | 1.5–2.5 | 0.5–1.0 |
| Suitability for Hardwood | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Fair |
| Maintenance Complexity | High | Medium | High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Pulp Washing Train in a Kraft Mill

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional process flow** of a typical 3-stage counter-current pulp washing battery as found in a kraft paper mill. It maps how the *pulp stream* (solid) and the *wash liquor stream* (liquid) move in **opposite directions**, which is the defining feature of counter-current operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    digesterA[Cooked Pulp from Digester] --> stage1A[Stage 1: Rotary Vacuum Filter]
    stage1A --> stage2A[Stage 2: Rotary Vacuum Filter]
    stage2A --> stage3A[Stage 3: Rotary Vacuum Filter]
    stage3A --> bleachA[Bleach Plant]

    stage3A -- Stage 3 Filtrate: Weak Liquor --> stage2A
    stage2A -- Stage 2 Filtrate: Medium Liquor --> stage1A
    stage1A -- Stage 1 Filtrate: Strong Black Liquor --> evapA[Evaporation Plant]

    waterA[Fresh Wash Water] --> stage3A
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **pulp** (orange path, left to right) becomes progressively cleaner.
- The **wash liquor** (blue path, right to left) becomes progressively more concentrated.
- The **strong black liquor** exits Stage 1 and is sent to evaporation/chemical recovery.
- The **fresh water** enters Stage 3 — the *cleanest* stage — ensuring the discharged pulp has the lowest residual chemical content.

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single Rotary Vacuum Filter (RVF)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **internal functional zones** of one RVF drum, mapping the rotation cycle through which the pulp mat passes. This is a typical KTU diagram question (5–8 marks).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DRUM1[Rotary Vacuum Drum Cycle]
        direction LR
        zoneA1[Zone A: Submergence and Pickup] --> zoneB1[Zone B: Thickening under Vacuum]
        zoneB1 --> zoneC1[Zone C: First Wash Shower]
        zoneC1 --> zoneD1[Zone D: Second Wash Shower]
        zoneD1 --> zoneE1[Zone E: Final Wash and Drainage]
        zoneE1 --> zoneF1[Zone F: Cake Discharge by Scraper]
    end

    subgraph PIPES1[Vacuum and Filtrate Piping]
        vacuum1[Vacuum Pump: Negative Pressure Source] --> zoneB1
        vacuum1 --> zoneC1
        vacuum1 --> zoneD1
        vacuum1 --> zoneE1
        tank1[Separated Filtrate Receivers] --> recirc1[Recirculated to Wash Train]
    end
```

**Engineering significance of each zone:**

| **Zone** | **Function** | **Mechanism** |
|---|---|---|
| A | Mat formation | Submerged in pulp suspension; vacuum picks up fibers on drum surface |
| B | Thickening | Liquor drawn inward; consistency rises from 1% to ~25% |
| C | Displacement wash #1 | Shower spray; strong black liquor filtered out |
| D | Displacement wash #2 | Counter-current wash; medium-strength liquor |
| E | Final wash | Weakest wash liquor; lowest residual solute |
| F | Discharge | Scraper blade lifts cake; mat falls to next stage |

### 4.3 Process Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Consistency Sensor on Cake] --> controllerA[PID Controller]
    flowA[Wash Flow Transmitter] --> controllerA
    vacuumA[Vacuum Level Sensor] --> controllerA
    controllerA --> valveA[Shower Flow Control Valve]
    controllerA --> vacvalveA[Vacuum Modulating Valve]
    controllerA --> speedA[Drum Speed VFD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above feedback architecture is what a KTU 2024 examiner expects when the question is *"With a block diagram, explain the process control scheme of a Rotary Vacuum Filter used in pulp washing."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Washing Efficiency" as applied to pulp washing filters. Write the mathematical expression and state its limiting values.*

**Model Answer:**

Washing Efficiency is the dimensionless parameter that quantifies how closely an actual pulp washing filter approaches the ideal of perfect displacement of black liquor by fresh wash liquor.

$$E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w}$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = concentration of solute in liquor entering the filter
- $C_1$ = concentration of solute in liquor leaving with the washed pulp
- $C_w$ = concentration of solute in the wash liquor applied

**Limiting values:**
- $E = 0$ → No washing occurs (filter acts only as a thickener).
- $E = 1$ → Perfect displacement (filter achieves ideal washing).

A typical industrial RVF operates at $E = 0.92$ to $0.96$.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the "Dilution Factor" in pulp washing? Why is it important to keep it as low as possible?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Dilution Factor (DF) is the ratio of the mass of total diluted liquor produced per unit mass of oven-dry pulp, given by:

$$DF = \dfrac{M_{diluted\,liquor}}{M_{ODP}}$$

**Units:** kg of dilution per kg of oven-dry pulp (or equivalently m³/tonne ODP, assuming liquor density ≈ 1000 kg/m³).

**Why minimization is critical:**
- A high DF means the black liquor leaving the filter is dilute.
- This dilute liquor must be **evaporated in the recovery boiler** to ~65–80% solids before combustion.
- Evaporation is the **largest steam consumer** in a kraft mill.
- Therefore, **lowering DF from 3.0 → 1.5 nearly halves the evaporation energy cost.**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to convert consistency (%) into liquor mass fraction (decimal) in mass-balance problems. *Always lose 2 marks here.*
> - **Pitfall 2:** Confusing **Norden Efficiency Factor** with simple overall efficiency. Remember $NEF = E_{overall} \cdot (n - 1)$.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Stating units where dimensionless quantities are expected (DR, E, NEF). *Always 0.5 mark penalty per error.*
> - **Pitfall 4:** Drawing a "rotating drum" schematic but failing to label the **vacuum zones, wash showers, and discharge scraper**. *Minimum 2 marks reserved for labelled diagram.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Rotary Vacuum Filter (RVF)** used for pulp washing. Discuss the role of the vacuum in each functional zone of the drum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-stage counter-current rotary vacuum filter battery washes unbleached kraft pulp. The incoming pulp consistency is 1.2% and the cake consistency after each stage is 28%. The wash liquor applied is clean water. The black liquor concentration entering the first stage is 180 g/L and the final discharged pulp carries liquor at 1.5 g/L. Calculate:
   (i) The single-stage washing efficiency $E$.
   (ii) The overall washing efficiency $E_{overall}$.
   (iii) The Norden Efficiency Factor (NEF). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of RVF — 7 marks**

A Rotary Vacuum Filter consists of a horizontal hollow drum, the surface of which is divided into compartments covered with a filter medium (e.g., stainless steel or polypropylene mesh). The drum rotates partially submerged in a vat of pulp suspension. The hollow interior of each compartment is connected via a **valve assembly (valve yoke)** to either:
- A vacuum source (for filtration zones)
- A source of low-pressure air (for the discharge zone)

The peripheral surface of the drum passes through the following functional zones:
- **Submergence zone (0–15% of cycle):** The drum is partly immersed; vacuum draws the fiber mat onto the surface.
- **Thickening zone (15–35%):** Continued vacuum dewatering raises consistency from 1% to ~25%.
- **Wash zone 1 (35–55%):** Wash liquor from shower #1 is applied; strong black liquor is drawn through.
- **Wash zone 2 (55–75%):** A second weaker wash is applied; medium liquor is recovered.
- **Final wash zone (75–90%):** Cleanest wash applied; final wash filtrate is recycled backward as shower #2 for the previous stage.
- **Discharge zone (90–100%):** Vacuum cut-off, low-pressure air blown inward, scraper blade removes the cake.

The vacuum (typically −50 to −80 kPa gauge) provides the **driving force for both thickening and washing**. The filtrates from each zone are collected separately in a **sealed filtrate receiver**, which separates liquids and gases; the liquid is sent to appropriate points in the counter-current cascade, and the gas (air + water vapor) is exhausted via the vacuum pump.

**Key advantage:** Continuous operation, high throughput (up to 1000 t/d per machine), well-suited to brown stock washing.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Inlet consistency = 1.2 % → liquor mass fraction = 0.988 (per kg pulp, liquor = 98.8 kg)
- Cake consistency = 28 % → liquor mass fraction in discharged pulp = 0.72
- Inlet liquor concentration $C_0$ = 180 g/L
- Outlet liquor concentration $C_1$ = 1.5 g/L
- Wash liquor $C_w$ = 0 g/L (clean water)

**(i) Single-stage efficiency:**

$$E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w} = \dfrac{180 - 1.5}{180 - 0} = \dfrac{178.5}{180} = 0.9917$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 0.992 \text{ or } 99.17\,\%}$$

**Marks breakdown:** formula (1) + substitution (1) + answer with units (1) = 3/3 for part (i)

**(ii) Overall 3-stage efficiency:**

For 3 stages in series, the overall efficiency is:

$$E_{overall} = 1 - (1 - E)^n = 1 - (1 - 0.9917)^3 = 1 - (0.0083)^3$$

$$(0.0083)^3 = 5.715 \times 10^{-7} \approx 5.7 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$E_{overall} = 1 - 5.7 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999994$$

$$\boxed{E_{overall} \approx 0.999999 \text{ or } 99.9999\,\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The discharged pulp has essentially no residual black liquor, ideal for downstream bleaching. This justifies why 3-stage counter-current RVF is the industry standard for high-quality pulp washing.

**(iii) Norden Efficiency Factor:**

$$NEF = E_{overall} \cdot (n - 1) = 0.9999994 \cdot (3 - 1)$$

$$\boxed{NEF \approx 2.0}$$

**Interpretation:** The 3-stage RVF performs equivalent to **2.0 ideal displacement stages**, confirming the design is highly optimized (NEF approaching the theoretical maximum of $n-1 = 2$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice for Internal Selection)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Displacement Washing** and **Diffusion Washing** mechanisms. Sketch the concentration profile in the fiber mat during a typical wash cycle and explain how it leads to the concept of the **Displacement Ratio**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure diffuser is used in a single-stage configuration to wash pulp. The following data are recorded:
- Pulp flow rate = 50 tonnes ODP/day
- Inlet black liquor concentration $C_0$ = 200 g/L
- Outlet liquor concentration $C_1$ = 8 g/L
- Wash liquor concentration $C_w$ = 2 g/L
- Wash liquor flow rate = 75 m³/h

Calculate:
   (i) The single-stage washing efficiency.
   (ii) The total mass of solute recovered per day in kg.
   (iii) The dilution factor if the discharged pulp consistency is 32 % and wash liquor density is 1010 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Displacement vs. Diffusion Washing — 7 marks**

**Displacement Washing** is the dominant mechanism in the early part of the wash cycle, when the wash liquor *physically pushes* the original black liquor out of the void spaces between fibers. It is an *advection-driven* process. The filtrate produced is *concentrated* (close to inlet concentration $C_0$), and the rate of solute removal is **fast**.

**Diffusion Washing** dominates in the later part of the cycle, when most of the bulk liquor has been displaced. The remaining solute is held *within* the fiber cell walls (lumen) and the *internal pores* of the fiber. The driving force is the **concentration gradient** between the fiber interior and the surrounding wash liquor. Fick's second law governs this process:

$$\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

It is a *slow* process and produces a *dilute* filtrate.

**Concentration Profile Sketch (verbal description for answer):**

A typical plot of filtrate concentration $C$ vs. cumulative wash volume $V_{wash}$ shows:
- An initial *flat plateau* at $C \approx C_0$ (displacement-dominated region).
- A *sharp break-through* point at $V_{wash} \approx V_{hold}$ (the displacement front).
- A *long tail* decaying exponentially toward $C = C_w$ (diffusion-dominated region).

The **Displacement Ratio** is defined as:

$$DR = \dfrac{V_{wash}}{V_{hold}}$$

A high DR (approaching or exceeding 1) implies that the bulk liquor has been physically pushed out, and the filter is operating in the *efficient* displacement regime. A low DR (<<1) means insufficient wash has been applied, and most of the solute remains in the mat.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $C_0$ = 200 g/L, $C_1$ = 8 g/L, $C_w$ = 2 g/L
- Pulp flow = 50 t ODP/day
- Wash liquor flow = 75 m³/h = 1800 m³/day
- Discharged cake consistency = 32 %
- Wash liquor density = 1010 kg/m³

**(i) Single-stage efficiency:**

$$E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w} = \dfrac{200 - 8}{200 - 2} = \dfrac{192}{198}$$

$$\boxed{E = 0.9697 \text{ or } 96.97\,\%}$$

**(ii) Mass of solute recovered per day:**

The solute leaving *with* the washed pulp = $C_1 \times$ (liquor flow in discharged pulp).

First, find the liquor flow leaving with the washed pulp. Per tonne ODP, the discharged cake is:

- At 32% consistency, 1 tonne (1000 kg) of ODP is accompanied by liquor = $\dfrac{1000 \times (1 - 0.32)}{0.32} = \dfrac{680}{0.32} = 2125$ kg liquor
- Convert to volume: $V_{out} = 2125 / 1010 = 2.104$ m³ per tonne ODP
- Total daily liquor in discharged pulp: $V_{out,total} = 2.104 \times 50 = 105.2$ m³/day

Solute leaving in discharged pulp:

$$M_{out} = 8 \text{ g/L} \times 105.2 \text{ m}^3 = 8 \times 1052 \text{ L} \times 1 \text{ g/L} = 841{,}600 \text{ g/day} = 841.6 \text{ kg/day}$$

Solute entering the filter = solute in incoming pulp liquor + solute in wash liquor.

Inlet liquor per tonne ODP (assume consistency ~10%):

- At 10 % consistency, 1000 kg ODP carries 9000 kg liquor = 9.0 m³ (assuming ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³)
- Solute in inlet liquor: $200 \text{ g/L} \times 9000 \text{ L} = 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ g/day per tonne ODP}$
- For 50 t ODP/day: $M_{in,pulp} = 1{,}800{,}000 \times 50 = 90{,}000{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 90{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$
- Solute in wash liquor: $2 \text{ g/L} \times 1800 \text{ m}^3 = 2 \times 1{,}800{,}000 \text{ g} = 3{,}600{,}000 \text{ g/day} = 3600 \text{ kg/day}$

Total solute in: $M_{in,total} = 90{,}000 + 3{,}600 = 93{,}600 \text{ kg/day}$

Solute recovered (sent to evaporator) = $M_{in,total} - M_{out}$

$$M_{recovered} = 93{,}600 - 841.6 = 92{,}758.4 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{M_{recovered} \approx 92{,}758 \text{ kg/day} = 92.76 \text{ tonnes/day}}$$

**Interpretation:** A single-stage pressure diffuser can recover ~92.76 tonnes of dissolved solids per day, demonstrating why it is the preferred choice for high-capacity modern mills.

**(iii) Dilution Factor:**

Total dilution = (liquor in discharged pulp) + (liquor in wash) − (liquor in feed pulp)

$$M_{dilution} = 105.2 \text{ m}^3 + 1800 \text{ m}^3 - 450 \text{ m}^3 = 1455.2 \text{ m}^3$$

(Note: feed liquor volume at 10% consistency = 9 m³/t × 50 t = 450 m³)

$$DF = \dfrac{M_{dilution}}{M_{ODP}} = \dfrac{1455.2 \text{ m}^3}{50 \text{ t ODP}}$$

$$\boxed{DF = 29.10 \text{ m}^3/\text{t ODP}}$$

**Engineering comment:** This is high compared to the target DF of 1.5–3.0. The high value indicates the assumed wash liquor flow (75 m³/h) is excessive; the actual process would use counter-current stages to reduce DF to 2.0–2.5 m³/t ODP.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- **Pulp washing** is a *solid–liquid separation* unit operation, **not** a chemical reaction.
- The black liquor contains **lignin, hemicellulose, and spent cooking chemicals** (NaOH, Na₂S in kraft process).
- **Counter-current** multi-stage washing is the industrial norm — minimizes fresh water use and maximizes black liquor concentration.
- **Washing efficiency** $E = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0 - C_w}$ — range is **0 to 1**.
- **Displacement ratio** $DR = V_{wash} / V_{hold}$ — for clean water wash, $E = 1 - e^{-DR}$.
- **Norden Efficiency Factor** $NEF = E_{overall} \cdot (n - 1)$ — maximum value is $(n - 1)$ for ideal displacement.
- **Dilution Factor** should be minimized (target: **1.5–3.0 m³/t ODP**) to reduce evaporation energy.
- **Rotary Vacuum Filter** = most common; uses **vacuum** as driving force.
- **Pressure Diffuser** = highest DR, lowest DF, highest capital cost.
- **Horizontal Belt Filter** = open structure, easy maintenance.
- **Twin-Roll Press** = used for *post-wash dewatering* (not primary washing).
- **Solute mass balance** in steady state: $L_0 C_0 + L_w C_w = L_1 C_1$.
- **Typical industry values:** $E_{single} \approx 0.92$–$0.96$; $E_{overall,3stage} \approx 0.99$+; $NEF \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- **Key design variables:** drum speed, vacuum level, wash shower flow rate, submergence depth, cake thickness.
- **Why counter-current?** Because it minimizes the *driving concentration difference* between the dirty liquor and the wash water *at the most contaminated end* — this is what gives high efficiency at low water consumption.
- **For 14-mark questions:** always include a **labelled diagram** (≥2 marks reserved) and **state the units** of every calculated quantity.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Pulp Washing Filters**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bleaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bleaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Bleaching** is a chemical wet-processing treatment applied to textile fibers, yarns, or fabrics to remove naturally occurring coloured impurities (chromophores), residual seed fragments, motes, and processing aids, in order to produce a uniformly **white, clean, and absorbent** substrate that is suitable for subsequent dyeing, printing, or finishing operations. It is a critical preparatory step in the textile manufacturing sequence, executed primarily through **oxidative** or **reductive** destruction of the coloured bodies present in the substrate.

In the strict KTU 2024 manufacturing terminology, bleaching is classified under the broader umbrella of **Pre-Treatment Processes (P-series)** in textile manufacturing, sitting between **Desizing (P-1)**, **Scouring (P-2)** and **Mercerization (P-3)** in the front-end finishing sequence, and immediately preceding **Dyeing** and **Printing**.

### 1.2 The Sequential Wet-Processing Flow

A typical cotton wet-processing line follows the sequence:

$$\text{Grey Fabric} \rightarrow \text{Desizing} \rightarrow \text{Scouring} \rightarrow \text{Mercerization} \rightarrow \textbf{Bleaching} \rightarrow \text{Dyeing/Printing} \rightarrow \text{Finishing}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE] **Think of it like this...**
> Imagine you spill turmeric water (a natural chromophore) on a white cotton shirt. Plain water will not remove the yellow stain. But if you treat the stained region with a mild oxidizing agent like hydrogen peroxide or dilute sodium hypochlorite, the yellow chromophore is chemically **destroyed** (broken into colourless, water-soluble fragments) and washed away — leaving the cloth visibly whiter. That, in essence, is the fundamental mechanism of **oxidative bleaching** applied at an industrial scale.

The coloured impurities in natural fibres (lignin, flavones, tannins in cotton; melanin granules in wool; sericin-yellowing compounds in silk) absorb visible light because they contain extended conjugated π-electron systems. Bleaching agents disrupt these conjugated systems by either:
- **Adding oxygen** across the double bonds (oxidation), or
- **Reducing the chromophore** to a leuco form (reduction).

Both pathways make the molecule **colourless** to the human eye.

### 1.4 Key Process Metrics (Standard Industrial Targets)

| Metric | Typical Target Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CIE Whiteness Index (WI)** | **70 – 80** (good bleach) | Dimensionless |
| **Berger Whiteness** | **> 90** for full bleach | Dimensionless |
| **Residual H₂O₂** | **< 50 ppm** before dyeing | mg/L |
| **pH of bleach bath** | **10.5 – 11.0** (H₂O₂ system) | pH units |
| **Temperature** | **80 – 95 °C** (H₂O₂ pad-steam) | °C |
| **Whiteness (Hunter Lab, L*)** | **> 92** | Dimensionless |

### 1.5 Classification of Bleaching — A Quick Map

> [!IMPORTANT] **Two Master Categories**
> 1. **Oxidative Bleaching** — uses oxidizing agents; most common in industry.
>    - Examples: H₂O₂, NaOCl, NaClO₂, KMnO₄, peroxy-acids.
> 2. **Reductive Bleaching** — uses reducing agents; milder, used for delicate fibres and whitening of dyed textiles.
>    - Examples: Na₂S₂O₄ (sodium hydrosulphite), NaHSO₃, SO₂ gas, thiourea dioxide.

A third emerging category is **Enzymatic Bleaching** using laccase/peroxidase enzymes on lignin-rich substrates (e.g., jute, linen).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Colour Removal — Why Bleaching Works

Natural fibres contain **chromophores** — molecular sub-units that absorb specific wavelengths in the visible spectrum (380 nm – 750 nm). The intensity of perceived colour depends on:

$$\varepsilon = \text{Molar extinction coefficient} \quad (\text{L} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{cm}^{-1})$$

Bleaching permanently alters (or destroys) these chromophores. The Beer-Lambert relationship governs how colour intensity is quantified:

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless),
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient,
- $c$ = concentration of chromophore (mol/L),
- $l$ = path length (cm).

The objective of bleaching is to **drive A → 0** by chemically reacting with the chromophore, which effectively reduces $c$ to negligible values.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Oxidative Bleaching (H₂O₂ System)

Hydrogen peroxide in alkaline conditions dissociates to form the **perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻)**, which is the active bleaching species:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{OH}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{HOO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The perhydroxyl anion then attacks the conjugated double bonds of chromophores:

$$\text{R}{-}\text{CH}{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{R'} + \text{HOO}^- \rightarrow \text{R}{-}\text{CH}(\text{OOH}){-}\text{CH}(\text{OH}){-}\text{R'}$$

The hydroperoxide intermediate is unstable and decomposes, breaking the conjugation permanently.

> [!NOTE] **Stabilization is Critical**
> H₂O₂ decomposition must be **controlled**, otherwise it simply liberates O₂ gas (wasted reagent) and may damage the cellulose. Industrial bleach baths therefore use **sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃)** as a stabilizer and **MgSO₄** as a co-stabilizer.

The unwanted decomposition reaction is:

$$2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Transition Metals}} 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2\uparrow$$

This is catalysed by trace Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Mn²⁺ ions — hence the use of **chelating agents (sequestrants)** like EDTA or DTPA in the bleach bath.

### 2.3 Mechanism of Reductive Bleaching (Na₂S₂O₄ System)

Sodium hydrosulphite in water dissociates to release the **sulphoxylate anion (SO₂⁻)**, which donates electrons to the chromophore:

$$\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaHSO}_3 + 2[\text{H}]$$

The liberated hydrogen reduces the chromophore to a colourless **leuco** form:

$$\text{R}{=}\text{R'} \;(\text{coloured}) + 2[\text{H}] \rightarrow \text{RH}{-}\text{R'H} \;(\text{leuco, colourless})$$

> [!WARNING] **Reductive Bleaching is NOT Permanent**
> Leuco forms are often re-oxidised on exposure to atmospheric oxygen, leading to **yellowing with time**. Reductive bleaching is therefore reserved for delicate fibres (wool, silk) and as a corrective "striping" treatment for dyed goods, not for the primary whitening of cotton.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cheat Sheet for Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | Beer–Lambert Law for absorbance | $A$ dimensionless |
| 2 | $T = 10^{-A}$ | Transmittance from absorbance | $T$ dimensionless (0–1) |
| 3 | $\%T = 100 \cdot 10^{-A}$ | Percentage transmittance | % |
| 4 | $\text{WI} = 4B - 3L \;(\text{Berger})$ | Berger Whiteness Index | $B, L$ = tristimulus values |
| 5 | $\text{WI}_{\text{CIE}} = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$ | CIE Whiteness (D65/10°) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | Definition of pH | For bath control |
| 7 | $[\text{HOO}^-] = K_b \cdot \dfrac{[\text{H}_2\text{O}_2]}{[\text{OH}^-]}$ | Perhydroxyl equilibrium | $K_b \approx 1.78 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{reacted}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$ | Process efficiency / yield | % |
| 9 | $\text{M:L} = \dfrac{\text{Mass of fabric (kg)}}{\text{Volume of liquor (L)}}$ | Material-to-Liquor ratio | Typically 1:8 to 1:15 |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m_0} \times 100\%$ | Weight loss due to chemical damage | % (should be < 5%) |
| 11 | $\text{CUP} = \dfrac{\text{Cellulose DP}_{\text{initial}}}{\text{Cellulose DP}_{\text{final}}}$ | Copper Number / DP damage index | Dimensionless |

> **Critical note on notation:** In every formula above, the symbol $\vert$ (vertical bar) used in formal logic and absolute value has been **avoided** to preserve markdown table integrity. In printed answer sheets, students may freely use $\vert x \vert$ notation.

### 2.5 Process Variants and Their Selection Logic

| Fibre Type | Preferred Bleach | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cotton** | H₂O₂ (alkaline) | Permanent white, no yellowing |
| **Wool** | Reductive (Na₂S₂O₄) or H₂O₂ (mild, acidic) | Avoids fibre damage |
| **Silk** | H₂O₂ (neutral pH) | Preserves sericin lustre |
| **Polyester** | NaClO₂ (acidic) | Effective on synthetic chromophores |
| **Jute / Flax** | Combined H₂O₂ + NaClO₂ | Lignin is stubborn |
| **Nylon** | NaClO₂ (acidic) | Avoids yellowing of polyamide |
| **Acrylic** | Reductive (Na₂S₂O₄) | Sensitive to alkali |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Control in Industry:** The whiteness index is monitored continuously using online **spectrophotometers** in continuous bleaching ranges, ensuring batch-to-batch consistency for downstream dyeing.
- **Effluent Engineering:** Residual H₂O₂ in bleach liquor must be destroyed (using catalase or sodium sulphite) before discharge, since it has a BOD impact and damages aquatic life.
- **Energy & Water Footprint:** Modern **Cold Bleaching** (using peracetic acid at 30 – 40 °C) reduces energy consumption by up to 60 % compared to conventional H₂O₂ pad-steam.
- **Sustainability:** Enzymatic bleaching (laccase + mediator) is an emerging green alternative for lignin-rich bast fibres.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Fibre Response

| Variable | Effect on Whiteness | Effect on Fibre Damage | Typical Control Window |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature ↑** | Increases | Increases sharply | 80 – 95 °C (cotton) |
| **pH ↑** (alkaline) | Increases for H₂O₂ | Increases (cellulose hydrolysis) | 10.5 – 11.0 |
| **H₂O₂ Concentration ↑** | Increases, then plateaus | Increases linearly | 30 – 50 g/L |
| **Time ↑** | Increases, then plateaus | Increases | 30 – 60 min |
| **Stabilizer ↑** | Decreases slightly | Protects fibre | 5 – 10 g/L silicate |
| **M:L Ratio ↑** (more liquor) | Better uniformity | Lower chemical concentration | 1:8 to 1:15 |

### 3.2 Derivation of Bleach Bath Kinetics (First-Order Model)

The rate of disappearance of coloured impurities in a bleach bath is empirically observed to follow **first-order kinetics** with respect to the chromophore concentration:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$

where:
- $C$ = concentration of chromophore at time $t$,
- $k$ = pseudo-first-order rate constant (depends on T, pH, [H₂O₂]).

**Step 1: Separate variables.**

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 2: Integrate both sides** with limits $C_0$ at $t = 0$ and $C_t$ at $t = t$.

$$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\ln C_t - \ln C_0 = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to the standard exponential decay form.**

$$\ln \left( \frac{C_t}{C_0} \right) = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate.**

$$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 6: Convert to % retention.**

$$\% \text{Chromophore remaining} = \frac{C_t}{C_0} \times 100 = e^{-k \cdot t} \times 100$$

**Step 7: Express the half-life.**

At $C_t = \dfrac{C_0}{2}$:

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$$

**Step 8: Temperature dependence (Arrhenius form).**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R \cdot T)}$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor,
- $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol),
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K),
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style

> [!NOTE] **Problem Statement (typical 7-mark analytical question)**
> A cotton fabric is bleached in a continuous pad-steam range. The chromophore concentration reduces from **100 units** to **10 units** over a steaming time of **40 minutes**. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate:
> 1. The rate constant $k$.
> 2. The time required to reduce the chromophore to **1 unit** (i.e., 99 % bleach).
> 3. The half-life of the bleaching reaction.
> 4. The activation energy if doubling the temperature from **353 K to 363 K** triples the rate constant.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Finding k from first-order equation.**

We have $C_0 = 100$, $C_t = 10$, $t = 40$ min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_t &= C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t} \\
10 &= 100 \cdot e^{-k \cdot 40} \\
\frac{10}{100} &= e^{-40k} \\
0.10 &= e^{-40k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(0.10) &= -40 \cdot k \\
-2.3026 &= -40 \cdot k \\
k &= \frac{2.3026}{40} = 0.0576 \; \text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $k = 0.0576 \; \text{min}^{-1}$

**Part (b): Time to reach C = 1 unit (99 % bleach).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 100 \cdot e^{-0.0576 \cdot t} \\
0.01 &= e^{-0.0576 \cdot t} \\
\ln(0.01) &= -0.0576 \cdot t \\
-4.6052 &= -0.0576 \cdot t \\
t &= \frac{4.6052}{0.0576} = 79.95 \;\text{min} \approx 80 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $t \approx 80$ minutes to reach 99 % bleach.

**Part (c): Half-life.**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.0576} = 12.03 \;\text{min}
$$

> **Result:** $t_{1/2} \approx 12$ minutes.

**Part (d): Activation energy from temperature dependence.**

The Arrhenius equation in two-temperature form is:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)
$$

Given: $k_2 = 3 k_1$, $T_1 = 353$ K, $T_2 = 363$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(3) &= \frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{353} - \frac{1}{363} \right) \\
1.0986 &= \frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \frac{363 - 353}{353 \times 363} \right) \\
1.0986 &= \frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \frac{10}{128139} \right) \\
1.0986 &= \frac{E_a}{8.314} \times 7.804 \times 10^{-5} \\
1.0986 &= E_a \times 9.387 \times 10^{-6} \\
E_a &= \frac{1.0986}{9.387 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.17 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J/mol} \\
E_a &\approx 117 \;\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $E_a \approx 117 \; \text{kJ/mol}$ — typical of diffusion-controlled bleaching reactions.

### 3.4 Process Material Balance (for Numerical Practice)

For a 1000 kg batch of cotton fabric at M:L = 1:10, using 40 g/L H₂O₂:

$$
V_{\text{liquor}} = 1000 \times 10 = 10\,000 \;\text{L}
$$

$$
m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}_2} = 40 \;\text{g/L} \times 10\,000 \;\text{L} = 400\,000 \;\text{g} = 400 \;\text{kg}
$$

Stoichiometric H₂O₂ consumed (assuming 30 % utilisation efficiency):

$$
m_{\text{consumed}} = 0.30 \times 400 = 120 \;\text{kg}
$$

This matches the empirical rule-of-thumb that 1 kg of 100 % H₂O₂ is required per 10 kg of grey cotton for an effective bleach.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Bleaching Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inlet: Grey Fabric Roll] --> B[Pre-washing Unit]
    B --> C[Desizing Section]
    C --> D[Scouring Section]
    D --> E[Water Rinse Tank]
    E --> F[Bleach Saturator Padder]
    F --> G[Steam Chamber 95C]
    G --> H[Hot Wash 80C]
    H --> I[Neutralisation Section]
    I --> J[Drying Stenter]
    J --> K[Outlet: Bleached Fabric]
    
    F --> L[Chemical Mixing Tank]
    L --> F
    
    subgraph chem["Chemical Preparation Module"]
        L1[H2O2 Storage] --> L
        L2[NaOH Dosing] --> L
        L3[Stabiliser] --> L
        L4[Wetting Agent] --> L
    end
```

### 4.2 Process Decision Tree — Bleach Agent Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Fibre Type?}
    Q1 -->|Natural Cellulosic| Q2{Cotton or Jute?}
    Q1 -->|Protein Fibre| Q3{Wool or Silk?}
    Q1 -->|Synthetic| Q4{Polyester or Nylon?}
    
    Q2 -->|Cotton| N1[H2O2 Alkaline Pad-Steam]
    Q2 -->|Jute or Flax| N2[Combined H2O2 + NaClO2]
    
    Q3 -->|Wool| N3[Reductive Na2S2O4 at 60C]
    Q3 -->|Silk| N4[H2O2 Neutral pH Cold]
    
    Q4 -->|Polyester| N5[NaClO2 Acidic pH 4]
    Q4 -->|Nylon| N6[NaClO2 Acidic pH 4 Mild]
    
    N1 --> R[Quality Check: CIE Whiteness]
    N2 --> R
    N3 --> R
    N4 --> R
    N5 --> R
    N6 --> R
    R -->|WI 80| S1[Approved: Forward to Dyeing]
    R -->|WI 70| S2[Rework: Re-bleach]
    R -->|WI 60| S3[Reject Batch]
```

### 4.3 Equipment Schematic — Continuous Bleaching Range (Pad-Steam)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Unwinder] --> A2[Guide Rollers]
    A2 --> A3[Saturator Padder 100 percent pick-up]
    A3 --> A4[Steam Chamber 30 to 60 min dwell]
    A4 --> A5[Hot Wash Box 80C]
    A5 --> A6[Hot Wash Box 60C]
    A6 --> A7[Cold Wash Box]
    A7 --> A8[Drying Cylinders]
    A8 --> A9[Stenter Frame]
    A9 --> A10[Plaiter Winder]
    
    subgraph control["Process Control System"]
        C1[Online Whiteness Sensor] -.-> A3
        C2[pH Probe] -.-> A3
        C3[Temperature Logger] -.-> A4
    end
```

### 4.4 Reaction Mechanism Schematic (H₂O₂ Bleaching)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[H2O2 + OH-] --> P2[HOO- Perhydroxyl Ion]
    P2 --> P3[Attack on Conjugated Chromophore]
    P3 --> P4[Hydroperoxide Intermediate]
    P4 --> P5[Cleavage of Conjugation]
    P5 --> P6[Colourless Fragments + Wash Off]
    
    P1 -.->|Unwanted Pathway| W1[Transition Metal Catalysis]
    W1 --> W2[O2 + H2O Wasted]
    W1 -.->|Blocked by| W3[Sodium Silicate Stabiliser]
```

### 4.5 Inputs, Outputs & Control Loops — Function Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I1[Grey Fabric] -->|Input| SYS
    I2[Chemicals H2O2 NaOH Stabiliser] -->|Input| SYS
    I3[Steam 95C] -->|Input| SYS
    I4[Water 80C Rinse] -->|Input| SYS
    
    SYS[Bleaching Plant System] --> O1[Bleached White Fabric]
    SYS --> O2[Effluent to ETP]
    SYS --> O3[Exhaust Air]
    SYS --> O4[Heat Recovery Stream]
    
    SYS -.->|Feedback| F1[Whiteness Sensor WI]
    SYS -.->|Feedback| F2[pH Transmitter]
    SYS -.->|Feedback| F3[Temperature TC]
    SYS -.->|Feedback| F4[Tension Controller]
    
    F1 -.-> SYS
    F2 -.-> SYS
    F3 -.-> SYS
    F4 -.-> SYS
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Note for Students**
> In KTU examination, schematic/flow-diagram questions typically test whether a student can **identify inputs, processes, outputs, and feedback loops**. The above block diagrams are the maximum-acceptable level of detail for a 7-mark sketch question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
>
> **Q1. Differentiate between oxidative and reductive bleaching. Give one example of each.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Feature | Oxidative Bleaching | Reductive Bleaching |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Mechanism | Adds oxygen / removes electrons | Adds electrons / reduces chromophore |
> | Permanence | Permanent white | Temporary (yellows back) |
> | Common Agent | H₂O₂, NaOCl, NaClO₂ | Na₂S₂O₄, NaHSO₃ |
> | Typical Fibres | Cotton, polyester | Wool, silk, dyed goods |
> | Bath pH | Alkaline (10.5 – 11) | Neutral to mildly acidic |
>
> **Example:** H₂O₂ on cotton (oxidative); Na₂S₂O₄ on wool (reductive). **[3 marks]**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
>
> **Q2. Why is sodium silicate added to the H₂O₂ bleach bath? What happens if it is omitted?**
>
> **Model Answer:** Sodium silicate acts as a **stabiliser** for H₂O₂ by forming a complex with transition metal ions (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Mn²⁺) that otherwise catalyse the wasteful decomposition of H₂O₂ into O₂ and H₂O. If omitted, H₂O₂ decomposes rapidly, leading to (a) loss of bleaching power, (b) excessive gas evolution causing fabric pinholes, and (c) uncontrolled pH drop. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
>
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
>
> **(a)** Explain the mechanism of hydrogen peroxide bleaching of cotton with relevant chemical equations. Discuss the role of stabiliser, wetting agent, and pH in the bleach bath. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 500 kg batch of cotton fabric is bleached in a 1:10 M:L bath using 35 g/L of 100 % H₂O₂. Calculate: (i) the total volume of liquor, (ii) the total H₂O₂ required, and (iii) the theoretical oxygen liberated if 40 % of the H₂O₂ decomposes wastefully. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanism & Bath Chemistry [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark] — Dissociation in Alkali:** H₂O₂ in the presence of NaOH dissociates to form the perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻), the active bleaching species.

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{OH}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{HOO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 [1.5 marks] — Attack on Chromophore:** HOO⁻ attacks the conjugated double bond of the natural chromophore in cotton (e.g., flavone derivatives), forming a hydroperoxide intermediate:

$$\text{R}{-}\text{CH}{=}\text{CH}{-}\text{R'} + \text{HOO}^- \rightarrow \text{R}{-}\text{CH(OOH)}{-}\text{CH(OH)}{-}\text{R'}$$

**Step 3 [1.5 marks] — Decomposition to Colourless Products:** The unstable hydroperoxide cleaves, breaking the chromophore conjugation permanently:

$$\text{R}{-}\text{CH(OOH)}{-}\text{CH(OH)}{-}\text{R'} \rightarrow \text{R}{-}\text{COOH} + \text{R'}{-}\text{CHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark] — Role of Stabiliser:** Sodium silicate (Na₂SiO₃) sequesters Fe³⁺/Cu²⁺/Mn²⁺ trace metals that catalyse the wasteful decomposition $2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$. Without stabiliser, bleaching efficiency collapses and the fabric is damaged by localised pitting.

**Step 5 [1 mark] — Role of Wetting Agent:** A non-ionic surfactant (e.g., nonylphenol ethoxylate) reduces surface tension of the aqueous bath, ensuring uniform penetration of H₂O₂ into the fibre capillaries.

**Step 6 [1 mark] — Role of pH:** pH = 10.5 – 11.0 is optimal: high enough to maximise [HOO⁻] for fast bleaching, but not so high as to hydrolyse cellulose (which occurs above pH 12 with strength loss).

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [2 marks] — Volume of Liquor:**

$$
V = M \times (\text{M:L ratio}) = 500 \;\text{kg} \times 10 = 5000 \;\text{L}
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Total H₂O₂ Required:**

$$
m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}_2} = 35 \;\text{g/L} \times 5000 \;\text{L} = 175\,000 \;\text{g} = 175 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 [3 marks] — Wasted H₂O₂ and O₂ Liberated:**

$$
m_{\text{wasted}} = 0.40 \times 175 = 70 \;\text{kg} \;\text{H}_2\text{O}_2
$$

Molecular mass of H₂O₂ = 34 g/mol. Moles wasted:

$$
n = \frac{70\,000}{34} = 2058.8 \;\text{mol}
$$

From the decomposition reaction, 2 mol H₂O₂ → 1 mol O₂. Therefore:

$$
n_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{2058.8}{2} = 1029.4 \;\text{mol}
$$

At STP, 1 mol O₂ = 22.4 L. Volume of O₂:

$$
V_{\text{O}_2} = 1029.4 \times 22.4 = 23\,059 \;\text{L} \approx 23.06 \;\text{m}^3
$$

> **Final Answer (b):** (i) 5000 L, (ii) 175 kg, (iii) ≈ 23.06 m³ of O₂ at STP.

---

> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
>
> **(a)** Compare batchwise (exhaust) bleaching and continuous (pad-steam) bleaching of cotton. Discuss the merits, demerits, and typical process parameters of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write short notes on: (i) Peracetic acid bleaching, (ii) Enzymatic bleaching, (iii) Optical whitening, and (iv) the role of sequestering agents in H₂O₂ baths. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Batch vs Continuous Bleaching [7 marks]:**

| Parameter | Batchwise (Exhaust / Winch) | Continuous (Pad-Steam) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fabric form** | Loose, rope form | Open-width, flat |
| **M:L ratio** | 1:8 to 1:15 (high) | Pick-up 90 – 110 % (low) |
| **Time** | 1 – 4 hours | 30 – 60 min (steam dwell) |
| **Temperature** | 80 – 95 °C | 95 – 102 °C (saturated steam) |
| **Chemical concentration** | 30 – 50 g/L H₂O₂ | 80 – 120 g/L H₂O₂ (conc.) |
| **Production rate** | Low (kg/batch) | Very high (50 – 100 m/min) |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High |
| **Labour** | High | Low |
| **Merits** | Flexibility, small lots | Uniformity, scale, low water |
| **Demerits** | Crease marks, longer cycle | High capital, less flexible |

**Step 1 [2 marks] — Batch Bleaching Mechanism:** Fabric is loaded into a winch or jet dyeing machine, H₂O₂ + NaOH + stabiliser liquor is added, temperature ramped to 85 °C, held for 60 – 90 min, then washed off. The fabric is in rope form throughout, which can cause **rope-mark creases** — a key disadvantage.

**Step 2 [2 marks] — Continuous Pad-Steam Mechanism:** Fabric is padded through concentrated bleach liquor (pick-up 100 %), then enters a **steam chamber** at 100 – 102 °C for 30 – 60 min dwell. The hot fabric then passes through wash boxes to remove residual chemicals.

**Step 3 [1.5 marks] — Merits:** Continuous systems offer superior **uniformity**, **lower water consumption** (closed loop), and are suited to large production runs typical of integrated mills.

**Step 4 [1.5 marks] — Demerits:** Continuous systems cannot economically handle **small lot sizes** or **delicate fabrics**; they require high capital investment and skilled maintenance.

**Part (b) — Short Notes [7 marks]:**

**(i) Peracetic Acid (PAA) Bleaching [1.5 marks]:**
Peracetic acid (CH₃COOOH) is a powerful oxidising bleach effective at low temperatures (30 – 60 °C). It decomposes to acetic acid and oxygen, leaving no harmful residue. Used as a **cold-bleach** alternative, saving up to 60 % energy. Particularly effective on synthetic fibres and as a one-bath bleach-dye process.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2$$

**(ii) Enzymatic Bleaching [1.5 marks]:**
Uses **laccase** (a copper-containing oxidase enzyme, EC 1.10.3.2) with a **mediator** (e.g., hydroxybenzotriazole, HBT) to oxidise lignin in bast fibres like jute, flax, and hemp. Operates at pH 4.5 – 5.5 and 50 – 70 °C. Environmentally benign, low water/energy footprint, but slower than chemical bleaching.

**(iii) Optical Whitening [1.5 marks]:**
Also called **fluorescent brightening**. Uses organic compounds (stilbene derivatives, coumarins) that absorb UV light (~350 nm) and re-emit visible blue light (~440 nm). This **blue fluorescence** counteracts the natural yellow tint of the fabric, making it appear "whiter than white". Applied in the final rinse.

**(iv) Role of Sequestering Agents [2.5 marks]:**
Sequestrants like **EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid)** and **DTPA (diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid)** chelate trace transition metal ions (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Mn²⁺) present in process water and on the fibre. These metal ions, if uncomplexed, catalyse the runaway decomposition of H₂O₂ into O₂ gas (wasted reagent) and free radicals (which cause cellulose chain scission and tensile-strength loss). Typical dosage: 0.5 – 2 g/L.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes)**
> 1. **Confusing bleaching with scouring** — Scouring removes *oily/waxy* impurities; bleaching removes *coloured* chromophores. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of stabiliser** — Many students write "H₂O₂ bleaches" and stop there. Full credit requires explaining **why** H₂O₂ does not simply decompose, and what role Na₂SiO₃, MgSO₄, and EDTA play.
> 3. **Wrong pH range** — H₂O₂ bleach bath pH is **10.5 – 11.0**, not 7 (neutral) and not 14 (strongly alkaline). Outside the window, either bleaching is poor or fabric is damaged.
> 4. **Unit mistakes in numericals** — g/L × L = g (not kg). Convert to kg only at the final step. Marks deducted for sloppy unit handling.
> 5. **Neglecting first-order kinetics in numerical** — For time-based bleaching questions, you **must** show the natural-log step: $\ln(C_t/C_0) = -kt$. Skipping this is a -2 mark penalty.
> 6. **Ignoring weight-loss control** — Any bleaching process that degrades cellulose to the extent that fabric weight loss exceeds 5 % is considered **over-bleached** and is rejected in industrial quality control.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Bleaching = chemical destruction of natural chromophores** in fibres to produce a uniformly white, absorbent substrate.
- Two main categories: **Oxidative** (H₂O₂, NaOCl, NaClO₂, PAA) and **Reductive** (Na₂S₂O₄, NaHSO₃).
- **Hydrogen peroxide is the industry workhorse** for cotton bleaching because it gives a permanent white without yellowing.
- The active species in alkaline H₂O₂ is the **perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻)**.
- Critical bath additives: **NaOH** (pH adjuster), **Na₂SiO₃** (stabiliser), **EDTA/DTPA** (sequestrant), **wetting agent** (surfactant).
- Optimal bleach bath: **pH 10.5 – 11.0, T 80 – 95 °C, 30 – 60 min, 30 – 50 g/L H₂O₂**.
- **First-order kinetics** govern chromophore disappearance: $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$.
- **Whiteness is quantified** by CIE Whiteness Index (preferred) or Berger Whiteness; target WI > 80 for full bleach.
- **Reductive bleaching is non-permanent** (leuco form re-oxidises) — used only for delicate fibres and as corrective treatment.
- **Cellulose damage control:** weight loss must stay < 5 %, and the Degree of Polymerisation (DP) must not fall below 70 % of its original value.
- **Modern sustainable methods:** Peracetic acid (cold bleach), enzymatic bleaching (laccase), and combined scouring-bleaching in one bath.
- **Residual H₂O₂ must be quenched** (using catalase enzyme or Na₂SO₃) before dyeing, otherwise it will oxidise the dye chromophore and produce faulty shades.
- **Continuous pad-steam** is the standard industrial method; **batch (winch/jet)** is used for small lots and delicate fabrics.
- **Exhausted bleach liquor** is treated in an Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) — high pH and residual H₂O₂ are neutralised/destroyed before discharge.

> **End of Module 4 — Bleaching Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
