topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying and Calendaring, Cement Manufacturing Process - Flow Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Drying, Calendaring & Cement Manufacturing Process

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Drying (in Manufacturing Context)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Drying** is a unit operation in which moisture (or volatile liquid) is removed from a solid, semi-solid, or liquid material by the application of **heat**, primarily to facilitate preservation, ease of handling, achieve desired physical properties, or prepare the material for subsequent manufacturing operations (such as calcination, packaging, or forming).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Drying is classified as a **mass transfer operation driven by heat transfer**. The driving force is the **vapour pressure gradient** between the surface of the wet material and the surrounding gas (usually hot air).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of drying like squeezing water out of a wet towel. You can wring it mechanically (mechanical dewatering), or simply hang it on a clothesline under the sun — the sun (heat source) provides thermal energy that evaporates the water molecules, which then drift away as vapour. **Industrial drying is essentially this "sun-drying principle" but on a controlled, continuous, and engineered scale.**

**The Four Industrial Mechanisms of Drying:**

| Mechanism | Heat Transfer Mode | Typical Industry Application |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Convection Drying** | Hot air/gas stream contacts the material | Paper, textiles, food grains, ceramic green bodies |
| **Conduction Drying** | Material contacts a hot surface (drum/roller) | Pulp sheets, slurries, polymer sheets |
| **Radiation Drying** | Infrared or microwave radiation | Surface coatings, paint, thin films |
| **Dielectric Drying** | High-frequency electric field | Lumber, rubber, pharmaceutical powders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **physical state of the bound moisture** determines whether drying is easy or difficult. **Free moisture** evaporates readily; **bound moisture** requires energy to break capillary/chemical bonds.

---

### 1.2 Calendaring

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Calendaring** is a continuous mechanical forming process in which a material (typically a polymer, elastomer, paper, or textile coating compound) is passed as a **plastic mass** through a series of **precision-heated, counter-rotating rollers** to produce a sheet, film, or coated substrate of precisely controlled **thickness, surface finish, and gloss**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine using a pasta machine at home. You push a dough ball through two smooth rotating rollers, and out comes a thin, uniform, smooth pasta sheet. Tighten the rollers, and the sheet becomes thinner. **Calendaring is the industrial "pasta machine" of the polymer and paper industry** — but with much higher precision, multiple rollers stacked vertically, and roller temperatures up to **200 °C** for PVC processing.

**Primary Industries Using Calendaring:**

- **Polymer processing** — PVC films, vinyl flooring, rubber sheets, conveyor belts.
- **Paper industry** — Gloss calendering, supercalendering for coated/uncoated paper.
- **Textile coating** — Applying PVC or polyurethane coatings onto fabrics.
- **Metallic foil production** — Thin aluminium and copper foil finishing.

> [!NOTE]
> The word "calender" originates from the Greek *"kylindros"* (cylinder), reflecting the dominance of the **roller geometry** in this process.

---

### 1.3 Cement Manufacturing — Overview

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Cement** is a fine inorganic **hydraulic binder** that, when mixed with water, undergoes a chemical reaction (**hydration**) to form a hard, rock-like mass that sets and hardens even under water. The most common type, **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)**, is manufactured by heating a precisely proportioned mixture of calcareous (limestone) and argillaceous (clay/shale) materials to incipient fusion at approximately **1450 °C** in a rotary kiln to form **clinker**, which is then finely ground with a controlled addition of **gypsum (3–5 %)** to retard the flash setting.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Consider making a cake. You mix flour (limestone), cocoa/clay (argillaceous), a pinch of salt (gypsum), and water into a slurry (raw mix). You bake it in a hot oven (rotary kiln) at high temperature. The baking transforms the ingredients chemically — the cake is the **clinker**, and the salt keeps it from setting too fast. **Cement is essentially "baked limestone-clay cake"**, and the kiln is a giant rotating industrial oven.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Cement is a **bulk commodity** and a **high-energy-intensive product** — manufacturing 1 tonne of cement consumes approximately **3.0–6.5 GJ of energy** and releases ~0.6–0.9 tonnes of CO₂. This makes process efficiency a critical design parameter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drying — Mechanism, Theory & Variables

### 2.1.1 The Drying Process — A Two-Stage Phenomenon

Drying of a wet solid typically follows a **characteristic drying curve** when plotted as **moisture content (X)** versus **time (t)**, and consists of two principal periods:

**Stage 1 — Constant Rate Period (CRP):**
- The surface of the material remains **completely saturated** with free moisture.
- Drying rate is governed by **external mass/heat transfer** (convective film).
- The temperature of the wet surface ≈ **wet-bulb temperature** of the air.
- Removal of moisture is analogous to **evaporation from a free water surface**.

**Stage 2 — Falling Rate Period (FRP):**
- Surface becomes partially dry; a "dried front" recedes into the material.
- Drying rate is now governed by **internal moisture transport** (capillary flow, diffusion, vapour flow).
- Surface temperature rises towards the **air dry-bulb temperature**.
- FRP is **sub-divided** into:
  - *First Falling Rate Period* — unsaturated surface drying.
  - *Second Falling Rate Period* — internal moisture movement controlled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Moisture Content (X_c):** The moisture content at which the constant rate period ends and the falling rate period begins. This is a **material-property parameter** that determines the required residence time in industrial dryers.

---

### 2.1.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Drying Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU Exam-relevant**. Memorize the symbol definitions and the units carefully.

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | Moisture content (wet-basis definition) | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 2 | $X^* = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$ | Moisture content (dry-basis definition) | kg water / kg total |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{dm_w}{A \, dt}$ | Drying flux (rate per unit area) | kg/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $N = k_Y \, (Y_s - Y)$ | Mass transfer rate equation | kg/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $Q = h \, A \, (T_a - T_s)$ | Convective heat transfer rate | W |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{m_s}{A \, N_c} (X_1 - X_c)$ | Constant rate period drying time | s |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{m_s \, X_c}{A \, N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Falling rate period (first period) | s |
| 8 | $X^* = \dfrac{p}{P_{BM}}$ | Equilibrium moisture relation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $h_c = 0.0204 \, G^{0.8}$ | Heat transfer coefficient for air flow | W/(m²·K) |
| 10 | $M_w = L \, C_s \, (H_2 - H_1)$ | Energy balance on dryer | kW |

**Where:**

- $m_w$ = mass of water (kg),  $m_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg)
- $Y_s$ = humidity of saturated air at surface temperature,  $Y$ = humidity of bulk air (kg water/kg dry air)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient [W/(m²·K)]
- $T_a$ = air dry-bulb temperature,  $T_s$ = surface temperature (K)
- $G$ = mass velocity of air (kg/h·m²)
- $N_c$ = constant drying rate,  $X_1, X_2$ = initial and final moisture content
- $X^*$ = equilibrium moisture content
- $L$ = mass flow rate of dry air (kg dry air/h),  $C_s$ = humid heat (kJ/kg·K)
- $H_1, H_2$ = inlet/outlet air enthalpy (kJ/kg dry air)
- $p$ = vapour pressure,  $P_{BM}$ = log-mean partial pressure

---

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** — Tablet drying requires precise moisture control (±0.1 %).
- **Food industry** — Spray drying produces milk powder, instant coffee.
- **Cement pre-heater** — Raw mix drying is the **first energy-consuming step**.
- **Ceramic tile manufacturing** — Drying of green tiles before firing (critical to avoid cracks).

---

## 2.2 Calendaring — Process Theory

### 2.2.1 The Calendaring Machine Architecture

A modern calender is a **multi-roll stack** with rollers arranged in **I, L, F, or Z** configurations. Each roller is independently driven, heated internally (by circulating hot water, steam, or thermal oil), and ground to micron-level precision.

**Functions Performed by Roller Pairs in Sequence:**
1. **Feed / Metering roll** — Controls the input film thickness.
2. **Bite / Compression roll** — Initial thickness reduction.
3. **Finishing / Polishing roll** — Final gauge and surface gloss.
4. **Cooling roll** — Reduces sheet temperature to avoid blocking.
5. **Take-up roll** — Winds the finished sheet.

### 2.2.2 Key Calendaring Process Variables

| Variable | Typical Range (PVC) | Effect |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Roll temperature | **150 – 200 °C** | Plasticizes the polymer, improves gloss |
| Roll linear speed | **10 – 100 m/min** | Affects throughput & orientation |
| Linear pressure (nip) | **50 – 500 kN/m** | Determines final thickness |
| Nip gap (roll separation) | **0.05 – 1.0 mm** | Direct thickness control |
| Roller hardness (Shore) | **70 – 100** | Influences surface finish |

### 2.2.3 The Calendaring Equation (Roller Deflection)

The thickness of the sheet emerging from a calender roll pair is affected by **elastic deflection of the rollers** under the nip pressure. An approximate formula (Ford-Faxen equation) is:

$$\delta = \dfrac{0.541 \, P \, D}{\, E \, L \,} \left[\, 1 + \dfrac{4 \, G}{\, \pi \,}\, \right]$$

where $\delta$ = roller deflection, $P$ = nip load, $D$ = roller diameter, $E$ = Young's modulus, $L$ = roller face length, $G$ = dimensionless material parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bowed rolls**, **crown grinding**, and **cross-axis skewing** are engineering techniques used to **compensate for deflection** and ensure uniform thickness across the sheet width.

---

## 2.3 Cement Manufacturing — Process Theory

### 2.3.1 Raw Material Chemistry

The **four oxide components** of cement raw mix are:

- **CaO (Calcium Oxide)** — supplied by limestone (CaCO₃), the principal raw material.
- **SiO₂ (Silica)** — supplied by clay or sand.
- **Al₂O₃ (Alumina)** — supplied by bauxite or clay.
- **Fe₂O₃ (Iron Oxide)** — supplied by iron ore or pyrite ash.

**Key Cement Chemistry Modules:**

$$\text{Lime Saturation Factor (LSF)} = \dfrac{\text{CaO}}{2.8\,\text{SiO}_2 + 1.18\,\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 0.65\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$$

$$\text{Silica Modulus (SM)} = \dfrac{\text{SiO}_2}{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$$

$$\text{Alumina Modulus (AM)} = \dfrac{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU standard LSF range = 0.92 – 0.98** for OPC. **SM = 2.0 – 3.0**, **AM = 1.0 – 2.0** are typical control limits. Examiners may ask students to **comment on quality control** of these moduli.

### 2.3.2 Two Principal Process Routes

| Process Route | Moisture State of Raw Mix | Energy Intensity | Modern Adoption |
|---------------|---------------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Wet Process** | Slurry with 30–40 % water | Higher (5–6 GJ/tonne) | Largely obsolete |
| **Dry Process** | Dry powder (raw meal) | Lower (3–4 GJ/tonne) | **Dominant globally (~95 %)** |
| **Semi-Dry / Semi-Wet** | Nodulized pellets | Intermediate | Niche applications |

---

### 2.3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cement Manufacturing

| # | Formula / Relationship | Significance |
|---|------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\text{Clinker} = \text{Limestone} + \text{Clay} \xrightarrow{1450\,°C} \text{C}_3\text{S}, \text{C}_2\text{S}, \text{C}_3\text{A}, \text{C}_4\text{AF}$ | Four principal Bogue compounds |
| 2 | $2\,\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CaO} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ | Calcination reaction |
| 3 | $\text{C}_3\text{S} = 4.07\,\text{CaO} - 7.6\,\text{SiO}_2 - 6.72\,\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 - 1.43\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ | Bogue calculation |
| 4 | $\text{C}_2\text{S} = 2.87\,\text{SiO}_2 - 0.75\,\text{C}_3\text{S}$ | Bogue calculation |
| 5 | $\text{C}_3\text{A} = 2.65\,\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 - 1.69\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ | Bogue calculation |
| 6 | $\text{C}_4\text{AF} = 3.04\,\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ | Bogue calculation |
| 7 | $\text{PCE} = 1450\,°C, \text{OPC} \approx 95\% \text{clinker} + 5\% \text{gypsum}$ | Final product composition |

> [!NOTE]
> The four Bogue compounds, namely **C₃S (Alite)**, **C₂S (Belite)**, **C₃A (Tricalcium Aluminate)**, and **C₄AF (Tetracalcium Alumino-Ferrite)**, govern the **strength development**, **heat of hydration**, and **sulfate resistance** of cement.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Cement Manufacturing

- **Construction industry** — backbone of concrete infrastructure.
- **Oil well cementing** — high-pressure, high-temperature resistant grades.
- **Sulfate-resistant cement** — for marine and underground structures.
- **White cement** — decorative and architectural applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Constant Rate Drying Time (CRP)

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the time required to dry a wet solid from an initial moisture content $X_1$ to the critical moisture content $X_c$ under the constant rate period.

**Step 1 — Define Drying Rate Flux**

The mass of water evaporated per unit time per unit area (drying flux) is:

$$N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$$

**Reasoning:** As time progresses ($dt$ increases), moisture content $X$ decreases (negative sign), and the magnitude of water removed per unit dry solid mass is governed by $m_s$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Constant Rate Assumption**

During CRP, the drying rate is constant:

$$N = N_c = \text{constant}$$

Substituting:

$$N_c = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Separate Variables and Integrate**

Rearranging:

$$dt = -\dfrac{m_s}{A \, N_c} \, dX$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ (where $X = X_1$) to $t = t_c$ (where $X = X_c$):

$$t_c = -\dfrac{m_s}{A \, N_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX = \dfrac{m_s}{A \, N_c} \, (X_1 - X_c)$$

**Step 4 — Final KTU-Standard Equation**

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \dfrac{m_s \, (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}\,}$$

This is the **time spent in the constant rate period** and is essential for sizing industrial dryers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Transfer Based Expression for Drying Rate

**Statement:** Show that the constant drying rate $N_c$ can be related to the convective heat transfer coefficient $h$.

**Step 1 — Energy Supplied by Hot Air**

The rate of heat transfer from the hot air to the wet material surface is:

$$Q = h \, A \, (T_a - T_s)$$

**Step 2 — Energy Used to Evaporate Water**

Assuming all transferred heat is used for moisture evaporation (no sensible heating of material in CRP), the heat consumed per second to vaporize water at rate $\dot{m}_w$ is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_w \, \lambda_s$$

where $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of vaporization at the surface temperature $T_s$.

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions**

By conservation of energy:

$$h \, A \, (T_a - T_s) = \dot{m}_w \, \lambda_s$$

**Step 4 — Express Drying Flux**

Dividing both sides by $A$:

$$h \, (T_a - T_s) = N_c \, \lambda_s$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,N_c = \dfrac{h \, (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s}\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This equation reveals that the drying rate is **linearly proportional** to the temperature difference between air and surface, and **inversely proportional** to latent heat of vaporization. This justifies why **hotter air = faster drying**, and why **low-pressure (vacuum) drying** reduces $\lambda_s$ and accelerates drying.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Falling Rate Period Drying Time

**Statement:** Derive the time required in the first falling rate period, where the drying rate is proportional to the free moisture content.

**Step 1 — Rate Equation in FRP**

In the first falling rate period, the drying rate decreases linearly with the free moisture content:

$$N = N_c \cdot \left(\dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance**

$$N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange**

$$-\dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = \dfrac{N_c \, A}{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $X = X_c$ at $t = 0$ to $X = X_2$ at $t = t_f$**

$$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} -\dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = \dfrac{N_c \, A}{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)} \int_0^{t_f} dt$$

$$-\ln(X - X^*)\Big|_{X_c}^{X_2} = \dfrac{N_c \, A \, t_f}{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)}$$

$$-\ln(X_2 - X^*) + \ln(X_c - X^*) = \dfrac{N_c \, A \, t_f}{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_f$**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) = \dfrac{N_c \, A \, t_f}{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_f = \dfrac{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}$$

**Total Drying Time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A batch dryer processes **500 kg** of wet solid initially containing **30 % moisture (wet basis)** to a final moisture content of **5 % (wet basis)**. The critical moisture content is **15 % (dry basis)**, and the equilibrium moisture content is **2 % (dry basis)**. The constant drying rate is **2.5 kg water/(m²·h)**, and the drying surface area is **8 m²**. Calculate the **total drying time**.

**Step 1 — Convert all moistures to dry basis**

Total initial mass = 500 kg. Dry solid mass:

$$m_s = 500 \times (1 - 0.30) = 350 \text{ kg}$$

Initial moisture (dry basis):

$$X_1 = \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$

Final moisture (dry basis):

$$X_2 = \dfrac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Constant Rate Period Time**

$$t_c = \dfrac{m_s \, (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c} = \dfrac{350 \times (0.4286 - 0.15)}{8 \times 2.5}$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{350 \times 0.2786}{20} = \dfrac{97.51}{20} = 4.876 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — Falling Rate Period Time**

$$t_f = \dfrac{m_s \, (X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$t_f = \dfrac{350 \times (0.15 - 0.02)}{8 \times 2.5} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.15 - 0.02}{0.0526 - 0.02}\right)$$

$$t_f = \dfrac{350 \times 0.13}{20} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.13}{0.0326}\right)$$

$$t_f = \dfrac{45.5}{20} \cdot \ln(3.988) = 2.275 \times 1.383 = 3.146 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Total Drying Time**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 4.876 + 3.146 = 8.02 \text{ h}$$

> **Answer:** Total drying time ≈ **8.02 hours**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cement Manufacturing Process — Complete Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Extraction Limestone Clay Iron Ore] --> A2[Primary Crushing Jaw Crusher]
    A2 --> A3[Secondary Crushing Impact Crusher]
    A3 --> A4[Pre-Blending Stockpile]
    A4 --> A5[Raw Grinding Ball Mill Vertical Roller Mill]
    A5 --> A6[Homogenization Silo]
    A6 --> A7[Pre-Heater Cyclone Tower 4 to 6 Stages]
    A7 --> A8[Calciner ~900 C]
    A8 --> A9[Rotary Kiln ~1450 C Clinkerization]
    A9 --> A10[Clinker Cooler]
    A10 --> A11[Clinker Storage Silo]
    B1[Gypsum Additive 3 to 5 percent] --> A12
    A11 --> A12[Cement Grinding Ball Mill]
    B2[Fly Ash Slag Pozzolanic Additives] --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Cement Storage Silo]
    A13 --> A14[Packing and Dispatch]
    A9 --> A15[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    A15 --> A16[Stack Emissions Control]
    A7 --> A17[Dust Collection Cyclone]
```

---

## 4.2 Calendaring Machine — Roller Stack Schematic (L-Type)

```mermaid

    P1[Polymer Feed Compound Hot Melt] --> R1[Feed Roll 1]
    R1 --> R2[Metering Roll 2]
    R2 --> R3[Top Roll 3 Finishing]
    R3 --> R4[Bottom Roll 4 Support]
    R4 --> R5[Cooling Roll 5]
    R5 --> R6[Take Up Roll Winder]
    H1[Heating System Hot Oil 180 C] -.-> R1
    H1 -.-> R2
    H1 -.-> R3
    H1 -.-> R4
    C1[Cooling Water 25 C] -.-> R5
    T1[Tension Control] -.-> R6
    Q1[Quality Sensor Thickness Gauge] -.-> R3
    Q1 -.-> R4
```

---

## 4.3 Industrial Drying Process — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Wet Feed Input] --> F2[Pre-Heater Section]
    F2 --> F3[Constant Rate Drying Zone CRP]
    F3 --> F4[Critical Moisture Point Xc]
    F4 --> F5[Falling Rate Drying Zone FRP Zone 1]
    F5 --> F6[Falling Rate Drying Zone FRP Zone 2]
    F6 --> F7[Cooling Section]
    F7 --> F8[Dried Product Discharge]
    A1[Air Blower] --> H1[Air Heater Heat Exchanger]
    H1 --> F2
    H1 --> F3
    H1 --> F5
    E1[Exhaust Air] --> F8
    E1 --> D1[Cyclone Dust Collector]
    D1 --> F2
    S1[Steam or Hot Oil Source] --> H1
    S2[Cold Water Source] --> F7
    C1[Control System PLC / DCS] -.-> H1
    C1 -.-> A1
    C1 -.-> F3
    C1 -.-> F5
```

---

## 4.4 Drying Curve — Functional Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRYING_CURVE["Drying Curve X vs t"]
        P1[Point A Initial X1 CRP starts] --> P2[Point B Critical Xc CRP ends]
        P2 --> P3[Point C First FRP]
        P3 --> P4[Point D Second FRP]
        P4 --> P5[Point E Equilibrium X star]
    end
    P1 -.-> N1[Stage 1 Constant Rate Nc]
    P2 -.-> N2[Stage 2a First FRP Linear]
    P3 -.-> N3[Stage 2b Second FRP Diffusional]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the drying curve for KTU:** The slope of the curve in the CRP is constant ($-N_c \cdot A / m_s$). In the FRP, the slope progressively decreases as $X \rightarrow X^*$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Define drying. Differentiate between the constant rate period and the falling rate period of drying.**

**Model Answer (KTU Pattern):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Drying is a thermal mass-transfer operation in which moisture is removed from a material by the application of heat, primarily to reduce moisture content to a desired level for further processing or storage.

**Constant Rate Period (1 mark):**
- Surface of the material remains **fully saturated** with free moisture.
- Drying rate is governed by **external heat/mass transfer** at the surface film.
- Surface temperature ≈ **wet-bulb temperature** of the drying air.
- Rate remains **constant** until the **critical moisture content (X_c)** is reached.

**Falling Rate Period (1 mark):**
- Surface becomes partially dry; a dried zone migrates inward.
- Drying rate is governed by **internal moisture transport** (capillary flow, diffusion).
- Surface temperature rises towards the **air dry-bulb temperature**.
- Rate **decreases continuously** until the **equilibrium moisture content (X\*)** is reached.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four principal compounds of Portland cement clinker (Bogue compounds) and state the role of gypsum in cement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Compound | Chemical Formula | Typical % in OPC | Role |
|----------|------------------|------------------|------|
| **Alite** | C₃S — Tricalcium Silicate | 45–60 % | Early strength development |
| **Belite** | C₂S — Dicalcium Silicate | 15–30 % | Long-term strength |
| **Tricalcium Aluminate** | C₃A | 6–12 % | Flash set (controlled by gypsum) |
| **Tetracalcium Alumino-Ferrite** | C₄AF | 6–8 % | Colour, minor strength |

**Role of Gypsum (1 mark):** Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O), added at **3–5 %** during final grinding, acts as a **set retarder**. It reacts with C₃A to form **ettringite**, which forms a protective coating on cement grains and prevents flash setting, allowing adequate working time for concrete placement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students must answer **one full question (with sub-parts)** out of the two choices provided.

---

### **Question Choice A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Section B]

#### **(a) Explain with a neat flow diagram the dry process of cement manufacturing. List the main raw materials and their role.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Raw Materials (2 marks):**
- **Limestone (CaCO₃)** — supplies **CaO** (~65 % of raw mix).
- **Clay / Shale** — supplies **SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃**.
- **Iron Ore** — adjusts Fe₂O₃ content.
- **Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** — set retarder, added at grinding stage.

**Dry Process Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Quarry Limestone] --> S2[Crusher]
    S3[Clay] --> S2
    S2 --> S4[Raw Mill Ball Mill]
    S4 --> S5[Homogenization Silo]
    S5 --> S6[Pre-Heater 4-Stage Cyclone]
    S6 --> S7[Calciner]
    S7 --> S8[Rotary Kiln 1450 C]
    S8 --> S9[Clinker Cooler]
    S9 --> S10[Clinker Storage]
    S10 --> S11[Cement Mill with Gypsum]
    S11 --> S12[Cement Silo]
    S12 --> S13[Packaging]
```

**Step-by-step process explanation (2 marks):**

1. **Crushing & Pre-blending:** Limestone and clay are crushed and mixed in a stockpile.
2. **Raw grinding:** The mix is ground in a ball mill to a fine powder (raw meal).
3. **Homogenization:** Powder is stored and blended in silos to ensure uniform chemistry.
4. **Pre-heating & Calcination:** In a 4–6 stage cyclone pre-heater, raw meal is heated to ~900 °C, decomposing CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂.
5. **Clinkerization in Rotary Kiln:** Material enters the inclined, rotating kiln at 1450 °C, where partial fusion forms **clinker nodules** containing C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF.
6. **Cooling:** Clinker is rapidly cooled in a grate cooler to preserve reactive phases.
7. **Cement grinding:** Clinker is ground with **3–5 % gypsum** and supplementary additives to produce finished cement.

---

#### **(b) What is calendaring? Describe the L-type calender with a labelled diagram and explain the role of each roller.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
**Calendaring** is a continuous mechanical forming process in which a plastic polymer/elastomer compound is passed through a series of precision-heated, counter-rotating rollers to produce sheets/films of uniform thickness, surface gloss, and density.

**L-Type Calender — Roller Functions (5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Feed Compound] --> R1[Roll 1 Feed]
    R1 --> R2[Roll 2 Metering]
    R2 --> R3[Roll 3 Top Finishing]
    R3 --> R4[Roll 4 Bottom Support]
    R4 --> R5[Roll 5 Cooling]
    R5 --> P1[Winder Finished Sheet]
    R1 -. Heat .-> R1
    R2 -. Heat .-> R2
    R3 -. Heat .-> R3
    R4 -. Heat .-> R4
    R5 -. Cool .-> R5
```

| Roller | Function | Temperature (PVC) |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| **Roll 1 (Feed)** | Receives the hot polymer feed from the strainer/extruder | 150 °C |
| **Roll 2 (Metering)** | Controls the film thickness entering the main nip | 165 °C |
| **Roll 3 (Top)** | Final finishing — controls surface gloss and gauge | 175 °C |
| **Roll 4 (Bottom)** | Support roll — provides high nip pressure | 170 °C |
| **Roll 5 (Cooling)** | Cools the sheet to ~50 °C before winding | 25 °C (water-cooled) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why L-type?** The "L" configuration allows easy access to the sheet at three points (after roll 2, 3, and 4) for **quality inspection, embossing, or lamination** — making it ideal for **flooring and printed films**.

---

### **Question Choice B** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Section B]

#### **(a) Derive an expression for the time required in the constant rate period of drying. A wet solid containing 30 % moisture (wet basis) is to be dried to 5 % moisture (wet basis). The initial mass is 500 kg, critical moisture content is 0.15 kg/kg (dry basis), equilibrium moisture content is 0.02 kg/kg (dry basis), drying area is 8 m², and the constant drying rate is 2.5 kg water/(m²·h). Calculate the total drying time.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation of CRP time (3 marks):**

Starting from drying flux definition:

$$N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} = N_c$$

Separating variables and integrating from $X_1$ to $X_c$:

$$t_c = \dfrac{m_s \, (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** Initial moisture $X_{w1} = 30\,\%$, Final moisture $X_{w2} = 5\,\%$, $M_{\text{total}} = 500$ kg, $X_c = 0.15$ kg/kg, $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg, $A = 8$ m², $N_c = 2.5$ kg/(m²·h).

**Step 1 — Dry solid mass:**

$$m_s = 500 \times (1 - 0.30) = 350 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_2 = \dfrac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{350 \times (0.4286 - 0.15)}{8 \times 2.5} = \dfrac{350 \times 0.2786}{20} = 4.876 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Falling rate period time:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{350 \times (0.15 - 0.02)}{8 \times 2.5} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.15 - 0.02}{0.0526 - 0.02}\right)$$

$$t_f = \dfrac{350 \times 0.13}{20} \cdot \ln(3.988) = 2.275 \times 1.383 = 3.146 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Total drying time:**

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f = 4.876 + 3.146 = 8.02 \text{ hours}\,}$$

---

#### **(b) Explain the rotary kiln in cement manufacturing with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the pre-heater cyclone tower and clinker cooler.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Rotary Kiln — Description (2 marks):**

A rotary kiln is a **long, inclined, slowly rotating steel cylinder** lined with **refractory bricks** (basic or dolomite-based). The raw meal enters the **higher (feed) end** and travels down the **slope (~3–4 %)** due to gravity, while **counter-flow hot gases** from the combustion of coal/pulverized fuel at the lower (burner) end heat it.

**Dimensions:** Length = 60–200 m, Diameter = 3–7 m, Rotation = 1–5 rpm, Residence time = 30–60 min.

**Temperature zones in the kiln (1 mark):**

| Zone | Temperature | Process |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Drying zone** | Up to 200 °C | Free moisture evaporation |
| **Pre-heating zone** | 200–800 °C | Pre-heating of raw meal |
| **Calcining zone** | 800–1100 °C | CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ |
| **Clinkerization zone** | 1300–1450 °C | Formation of C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF |
| **Cooling zone** | 1450–1300 °C | Incipient fusion, nodule formation |

**Pre-heater Cyclone Tower (2 marks):**

The pre-heater is a **series of 4–6 cyclones** arranged vertically. Hot kiln exhaust gases rise through the cyclones in counter-flow to the descending raw meal. **Function:**
- Pre-heats raw meal to ~800 °C using waste kiln gas heat.
- Improves thermal efficiency by **20–30 %** over long kilns.
- Reduces specific heat consumption to **~3.0–3.5 GJ/tonne clinker**.

**Clinker Cooler (2 marks):**

Modern grate coolers (e.g., **Reciprocating Grate Cooler, Polytrack Cooler**) rapidly cool the clinker from ~1300 °C to ~100 °C using ambient air.
- **Recovers ~30 %** of kiln heat for re-use in secondary air.
- **Preserves reactive C₃S phase** by quenching (avoids β → γ C₂S transformation).
- Reduces dust emissions by exhausting pre-heated air back to the kiln.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction points identified from past KTU board evaluation:**
>
> 1. **Moisture Content Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often forget to convert moisture content between **wet basis** and **dry basis**. Always state which basis is used and show the conversion.
> 2. **Skipping the Critical Moisture Content (–1 mark):** In drying derivations, examiners expect explicit identification of $X_c$ as the **boundary between CRP and FRP**. Forgetting this costs marks.
> 3. **Generic Cement Flow Diagram (–2 marks):** A vague "Quarry → Crusher → Kiln → Cement" diagram loses marks. KTU examiners expect **specific equipment names** (ball mill, cyclone pre-heater, rotary kiln, grate cooler, cement mill).
> 4. **No Temperatures Cited in Kiln (–1 mark):** Always mention **1450 °C** for clinkerization and **~900 °C** for calciner — examiners scan for these specific numerical milestones.
> 5. **Calendaring — Confusing with Rolling:** Rolling uses **metal deformation mechanics**; calendaring is a **polymer/elastomer forming process**. Do not interchange the two in definitions.
> 6. **Units Inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing kg with lb, m with ft, or omitting units in numerical problems is a frequent mark-loser.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### **A. Drying — Key Recall Points**

- Drying = **mass transfer driven by heat transfer**, governed by **vapour pressure gradient**.
- **Four industrial drying mechanisms:** Convection, Conduction, Radiation, Dielectric.
- **Two-period drying curve:** Constant Rate Period (CRP) → Falling Rate Period (FRP).
- **Critical moisture content (X_c)** separates CRP and FRP — essential for dryer sizing.
- **Equilibrium moisture content (X\*)** is the lowest achievable moisture for given air conditions.
- **Key CRP equation:** $t_c = \dfrac{m_s(X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}$
- **Key FRP equation:** $t_f = \dfrac{m_s(X_c - X^*)}{A \, N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$
- **Energy-drying linkage:** $N_c = \dfrac{h(T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s}$

### **B. Calendaring — Key Recall Points**

- Calendaring uses **heated, counter-rotating rollers** in I, L, F, or Z configurations.
- Produces **uniform-thickness polymer/elastomer sheets** with controlled gloss.
- Major industries: **PVC, rubber, paper, textile coatings**.
- Roller temperature (PVC): **150–200 °C**; Speed: **10–100 m/min**.
- Roller deflection compensated by **bowed rolls, crown grinding, cross-axis skewing**.

### **C. Cement Manufacturing — Key Recall Points**

- Cement = **hydraulic binder**; main type is **OPC**.
- **Raw materials:** Limestone (CaO source), Clay (SiO₂/Al₂O₃/Fe₂O₃), Gypsum (set retarder).
- **Dry process is dominant** globally (~95 %); wet process is obsolete.
- **Bogue compounds:** C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF (their ratios determine cement properties).
- **LSF range:** 0.92–0.98 ; **SM:** 2.0–3.0 ; **AM:** 1.0–2.0.
- **Rotary kiln temperature:** 1450 °C at burning zone.
- **Pre-heater cyclone tower** improves thermal efficiency by 20–30 %.
- **Clinker cooler** recovers ~30 % heat and preserves reactive C₃S.
- **Gypsum addition:** 3–5 % to control flash setting of C₃A.
- **Energy consumption:** 3.0–6.5 GJ/tonne of cement.
- **CO₂ emission:** 0.6–0.9 tonnes CO₂/tonne cement (sustainability challenge).

### **D. Cross-Topic Connection**

All three processes — **Drying, Calendaring, and Cement Manufacturing** — share the theme of **thermal and mechanical transformation of bulk materials** under controlled industrial conditions. Understanding heat transfer, material balance, and process flow design is essential across all three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raw Mill Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Raw Mill Circuit # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Raw Mill Circuit (RMC):** A complete, integrated material-handling and size-reduction flow network designed to receive, dry, crush, grind, classify, and store the raw mix (limestone, clay/corrective silica, iron ore/bauxite, and/or mill scale) into a homogeneous fine powder — termed **"Raw Meal"** — of stipulated fineness (typically **8–12 % residue on 90 µm sieve** or **~3200 Blaine cm²/g** for modern dry-process kilns). The circuit forms the **first major size-reduction stage** in the cement manufacturing flow-sheet, positioned downstream of the **Quarry → Crusher Stacker-Reclaimer** stage and upstream of the **Homogenizing Silo → Pre-heater / Kiln** stage. **Standard Engineering Metrics for a Raw Mill Circuit:** | Parameter | Typical Value (Dry Process, Modern VRM) | |---|---| | **Specific Power Consumption** | **10 – 16 kWh/ton of raw meal** | | **Product Fineness (90 µm residue)** | **8 – 14 %** | | **Moisture of Feed** | **≤ 6 – 8 %** (for VRM); up to 18 % for ball mills with hot gas) | | **Throughput Range** | **100 – 500 TPH** (large integrated plants) | | **Circulating Load (Closed circuit ball mill)** | **100 – 300 %** | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: The Kitchen-Mixer Analogy** > Imagine you are making **idli batter** in a commercial kitchen. You throw in *rice* (limestone), *urad dal* (clay/silica), and a pinch of *fenugreek* (iron ore). You want a **perfectly smooth, lump-free, fine paste** of *uniform consistency*. If you just dump everything into a basic mixer, you get an uneven grind with big chunks. So you: > 1. **Pre-dry** the rice (moisture removal using hot air) — analogous to the **drying zone** of a VRM or **Hot Gas Generator** of a ball mill. > 2. **Grind** with a heavy stone (the **roller/table** of the mill). > 3. **Sieve** it through a fine cloth (the **dynamic separator / air classifier**). > 4. The **chunks that don't pass the cloth are sent back** to the mixer (the **circulating load**). > 5. The **fine paste** that passes is collected in a vessel (**homogenizing silo**). > > This entire **closed loop** of grind → separate → return coarse → collect fine **is the Raw Mill Circuit**. --- ## 1.3 Why a Raw Mill Circuit Exists (The "Why" of the Topic) - **Size Reduction:** Limestone feed from the crusher is **5 – 50 mm**; the kiln requires it as **< 90 µm** — a size reduction ratio of roughly **50:1 to 500:1**. - **Drying:** Quarried limestone in Kerala/Coastal India often carries **8 – 15 % surface moisture**, which must be evaporated to enable efficient grinding. - **Homogenization at micro-scale:** Even after blending in the stockpile, particle-level homogeneity is only achieved at fine grinding. - **Energy Efficiency:** ~**60 – 70 %** of electrical energy input to a ball mill is wasted as heat; classifying and returning only the oversize minimizes this loss. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall:** The Raw Mill Circuit is the **bridge** between *raw material preparation* (crushing & stacking) and *clinkerization* (the kiln). It directly governs kiln stability because **finer & drier raw meal = better calcination efficiency**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Raw Mill Circuits (The Three Big Families) ### A) By Process Philosophy 1. **Dry Process Raw Mill Circuit (Modern Standard)** - Feed moisture ≤ 6 %. - Predominant mill: **Vertical Roller Mill (VRM)** or **Horizontal Roller Press (Roller Press / HRC)**. - Integrated hot-gas drying from kiln cooler waste heat. 2. **Wet Process Raw Mill Circuit (Legacy)** - Limestone is ground in **water slurry** (~30 – 40 % moisture). - Uses **tube mills** with screen classifiers. - High fuel penalty in kiln due to evaporation load → mostly obsolete. 3. **Semi-Dry / Semi-Wet Process** - Filter-cake raw meal (~18 % moisture) is fed; uses Lepol grate preheater. - Largely replaced by dry process globally. ### B) By Circuit Topology - **Open-Circuit Mill:** No separator; product fineness is controlled by mill residence time alone. High specific power, wide PSD, rarely used for raw meal today. - **Closed-Circuit Mill (Dominant):** Mill + External / Internal **dynamic separator**. Coarse rejected (tailings) returned as circulating load; fines sent to storage. - **Combined / Combi-Grinding Circuit (Latest):** Pre-grinder (Roller Press or HRC) + Ball Mill + Separator. Achieves **< 8 kWh/ton** specific power. --- ## 2.2 Principal Sub-Systems of a Modern Dry-Process Raw Mill Circuit 1. **Weigh-Feeder & Belt Conveyor System** — controlled dosing of raw mix. 2. **Metal Separator (Magnetic / Eddy Current)** — protects rollers/liners. 3. **Hot-Gas Generator (HGG) or Kiln-Cooler Waste-Gas Duct** — supplies drying medium at **200 – 350 °C**. 4. **Mill (VRM / Ball Mill / Roller Press)** — primary comminution device. 5. **Dynamic Separator (O-Sepa, Sepax, Sepol)** — air-classification; produces the cut at **d₈₀ ≈ 90 µm**. 6. **Bucket Elevator / Mechanical Conveyor** — lifts separator fines to top of cyclone/silo. 7. **Cyclone Dust Collector / Bag Filter (Fabric Filter)** — gas-solid separation. 8. **Process Fan (Induced Draft / Forced Draft)** — drives gas flow. 9. **Raw Meal Silos (Homogenizing)** — buffer storage + pneumatic blending. --- ## 2.3 The Vertical Roller Mill (VRM) — Working Principle (Theory Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] > The VRM is the **workhorse** of modern raw circuits due to its ability to grind, dry, and classify in a single integrated unit. - **Step 1 — Feed Entry:** Raw mix falls onto the **rotating grinding table** through a feed chute. - **Step 2 — Nipping Zone:** Three or four **spring-loaded / hydraulic rollers** rotate against the table. Material is nipped between table and rollers at **~ 0.5 – 2.0 MPa specific grinding force**. - **Step 3 — Drying:** Hot gas (200 – 350 °C) is drawn across the table, evaporating moisture instantaneously. - **Step 4 — Internal Classification:** Fine particles are lifted by the gas stream; coarse falls back to the table (internal recirculation). - **Step 5 — External Classification:** Top of mill is fitted with an **integrated dynamic classifier** (e.g., Sepol). Coarse rejected → return to table; fine accepted → leaves with gas → cyclone/bag filter. - **Step 6 — Reject Re-circulation:** Tailings returned to feed via screw conveyor. --- ## 2.4 The Ball Mill with Dynamic Separator (Closed Circuit Ball Mill) — Working Principle - **Step 1 — Feeding:** Pre-crushed raw mix is fed via weigh feeder → chute → mill trunnion. - **Step 2 — Comminution:** Mill rotates at **65 – 78 % of critical speed (Nc)**; 1st chamber uses **balls Ø 60 – 100 mm** (impact & crushing); 2nd chamber uses **cylpebs Ø 20 × 30 mm or balls Ø 15 – 30 mm** (attrition). - **Step 3 — Discharge & Lifting:** Material exits through a **grate discharge** → is lifted by **lifters** → enters the **separator feed box**. - **Step 4 — Dynamic Separation:** The **O-Sepa / Sepax** separator uses a **rotor + vanes + air stream**. Fine passes → bucket elevator → silo. Coarse (rejects) returns to mill via **screw conveyor** as **circulating load**. > **Critical Speed Formula (used to set mill RPM):** > $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$$ > where D = mill internal diameter in metres, Nc in rpm. --- ## 2.5 The Dynamic Separator — Tromp Curve & Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Tromp Curve** is the *signature performance graph* of a separator. It plots the **partial separation efficiency (T(x))** against particle size **x**. **Interpretation of Tromp Curve:** - **T(x) = 1** → all particles of size x go to the fine product. - **T(x) = 0** → all particles of size x go to the reject. - **Cut-size d₅₀:** the size at which **T(x) = 0.5 (50 %)**. For raw mills, **d₅₀ ≈ 60 – 90 µm**. - **Slope/Sharpness (S):** $S = \dfrac{d_{75}/d_{25}}{\,}$ (ideal → S = 1). --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Use \mid and \vert in tables) > [!TIP] > **Use `\mid` and `\vert` instead of `|` inside LaTeX table cells to prevent markdown-table syntax breakage.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Critical speed of ball mill** | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ | rpm | D in metres | | 2 | **Operating speed** | $N_{op} = k \cdot N_c$ | rpm | k = 0.65 – 0.78 | | 3 | **Circulating load (CL)** | $CL(\%) = \dfrac{R}{F}\times 100 = \dfrac{c - f}{f - r}\times 100$ | % | R=recycle, F=fresh feed, c=coarse, f=fine, r=recycle product | | 4 | **Separator efficiency (Tromp)** | $T(x) = \dfrac{\text{coarse in fine}}{\text{coarse in feed}}$ | dimensionless | Function of particle size x | | 5 | **Cut size** | $T(d_{50}) = 0.5$ | µm | Target ~ 60 – 90 µm | | 6 | **Specific power consumption** | $W_m = \dfrac{\text{Mill motor power (kW)}}{\text{Throughput (t/h)}}$ | kWh/ton | Typical 10 – 16 | | 7 | **Bond's Work Index (grindability)** | $W = W_i\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | kWh/ton | F₈₀ feed, P₈₀ product | | 8 | **Rittinger's Law (surface theory)** | $E = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{x_2} - \dfrac{1}{x_1}\right)$ | kWh/ton | Fine grinding limit | | 9 | **Kick's Law (volume theory)** | $E = K_K \ln\left(\dfrac{x_1}{x_2}\right)$ | kWh/ton | Coarse crushing | | 10 | **Bond's Third Theory** | $E = 10\,W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | kWh/ton | Most used in raw mills | | 11 | **Bond's ball mill power equation** | $P = 7.33 \cdot \sqrt{D}\cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{0.937\,J}{D}\right)\cdot m\cdot N_c$ | kW | D,m in metres | | 12 | **Mass balance on separator** | $F = P + R$ | t/h | Feed = Product + Recycle | | 13 | **Classifier efficiency (Newtonian)** | $\eta = \dfrac{c(f-r)}{f(c-r)}\times 100$ | % | Mass-based | | 14 | **Gas flow for drying (VRM)** | $V_g = \dfrac{M_w}{\rho_g (H_{in}-H_{out})}$ | m³/h | M_w = water to evaporate | | 15 | **Mill throughput (VRM empirical)** | $Q = K \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L$ | t/h | K empirical constant | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - The **raw mill circuit** decides the **specific heat consumption** of the kiln (kJ/kg clinker). A **stable, dry, fine raw meal** reduces kiln stops, fuel spikes, and pre-heater clogging. - An inefficient separator (poor Tromp curve) increases circulating load → wastes **30 – 50 % of grinding energy**. - Modern plants target **< 12 kWh/ton** raw meal — achieved only with **combi-grinding** (roller press + V-separator + ball mill). - Digital twins of the raw mill circuit (e.g., **KHD SCADA, FLS ECS/ProcessExpert**) are used in real-time for **throughput optimization and fineness control**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Speed of a Ball Mill (Full Derivation) > **Physical Premise:** The outermost ball just remains in contact with the mill shell at the topmost point (centrifuging threshold). At this speed, the centrifugal force exactly equals the radial component of gravity. Let: - R = internal radius of the mill (m) - m = mass of a single ball (kg) - ω = angular velocity of the mill (rad/s) - g = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²** **Step 1 — Identify the two forces on the outermost ball at the top of the trajectory:** **Centrifugal force (outward, radial):** $$F_c = m\,\omega^{2} R$$ **Weight (effective radial component at the topmost point = m·g inward, which is the only force holding the ball in place):** $$F_g = m\,g$$ **Step 2 — Set centrifugal force equal to gravitational force at the *critical* threshold:** $$m\,\omega^{2} R = m\,g$$ The mass cancels: $$\omega^{2} R = g$$ **Step 3 — Solve for angular velocity ω:** $$\omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$ **Step 4 — Convert angular velocity (rad/s) to rotational speed N (rev/min):** $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$$ Squaring and equating: $$\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{g}{R}$$ $$N^{2} = \dfrac{g \cdot 60^{2}}{4\pi^{2} R}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute R = D/2, where D = internal mill diameter (m):** $$N^{2} = \dfrac{g \cdot 3600}{2\pi^{2} D}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute g = 9.81 m/s²:** $$N^{2} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 3600}{2 \times (3.1416)^{2} \times D}$$ $$N^{2} = \dfrac{35316}{19.739 \times D}$$ $$N^{2} = \dfrac{1789.5}{D}$$ **Step 7 — Take square root to obtain Nc:** $$N_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{1789.5}{D}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}\,\text{rpm}\,}$$ **Step 8 — Operating speed (typically 65 – 78 % of Nc):** $$N_{op} = k \cdot N_c,\quad k = 0.65 \text{ to } 0.78$$ > **Engineering Logic:** At k > 1.0 → balls centrifuge (no grinding). At k < 0.6 → balls slump, poor impact. **The "sweet zone" of cascading-cataracting action is 0.65 – 0.78.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Circulating Load (CL) from Separator Mass Balance **Given a two-stream separator (Feed F → Fine P + Recycle R):** **Step 1 — Total mass balance:** $$F = P + R$$ **Step 2 — Component mass balance on the coarse fraction (size > cut-size):** $$F \cdot c_F = P \cdot c_P + R \cdot c_R$$ Where: - c_F = fraction of coarse in feed - c_P = fraction of coarse in product (should be ≈ 0 for a good separator) - c_R = fraction of coarse in recycle (should be ≈ 1 for a good separator) **Step 3 — Idealize: assume perfect separator so that c_P → 0:** $$F \cdot c_F = R \cdot c_R \;\Rightarrow\; R = \dfrac{F \cdot c_F}{c_R}$$ **Step 4 — Define circulating load ratio:** $$CL = \dfrac{R}{F} = \dfrac{c_F}{c_R}$$ **Step 5 — Practical two-stream mass balance with measurable sieve fractions (f, c, r):** $$F = P + R$$ $$F \cdot f = P \cdot p + R \cdot r$$ Solving simultaneously: $$P = \dfrac{F(f - r)}{(p - r)},\quad R = \dfrac{F(p - f)}{(p - r)}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute to get the **standard** circulating-load formula in terms of sieve residues:** $$CL(\%) = \dfrac{R}{F}\times 100 = \dfrac{p - f}{f - r}\times 100$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, f, p, r are the **% residue on a control sieve** (e.g., 90 µm) in feed, product, and recycle respectively. **High CL → separator is rejecting too much coarse back, indicating over-grinding or low separator efficiency.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bond's Third Theory (Work Index for Raw Mill Sizing) **Premise:** Total energy consumed is inversely proportional to the square root of the product size — the geometric mean of Rittinger (fine) and Kick (coarse) laws. **Step 1 — Differential energy for an infinitesimal size reduction (P → P − dP):** $$dE = -K\,\dfrac{dP}{P^{0.5}} = -K\,P^{-0.5}\,dP$$ **Step 2 — Integrate between feed size F₈₀ and product size P₈₀:** $$E = -K \int_{F_{80}}^{P_{80}} P^{-0.5}\,dP = -K \left[ 2 P^{0.5} \right]_{F_{80}}^{P_{80}}$$ $$E = 2K \left( F_{80}^{0.5} - P_{80}^{0.5} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Introduce Bond Work Index W_i (energy per ton to reduce from infinite size to 100 µm = 10⁻² cm = 100/sqrt(10) using cm units):** $$E = W_i \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)$$ Sizes in micrometres (the constant 10 absorbs unit conversion since 100 µm → √(10⁴/10) = √1000 ≈ 31.6; Bond chose the constant 10 to anchor the index to 100 µm reference). **Step 4 — Compact form:** $$\boxed{\,W = W_i \left( \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)\,\text{kWh/ton}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Typical W_i values for raw mix components:** | Material | Bond Work Index W_i (kWh/ton) | |---|---| | Limestone | 10 – 14 | | Clay | 6 – 9 | | Bauxite | 9 – 12 | | Iron Ore | 12 – 17 | | Raw mix (typical) | **11 – 14** | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Mill Types | Parameter | Ball Mill (Closed) | VRM | Roller Press (Combi) | |---|---|---|---| | **Specific Power (kWh/ton)** | 16 – 22 | 10 – 14 | 8 – 12 (with ball mill finish) | | **Drying capability** | Good (with HGG) | Excellent (integrated) | Limited (needs separate dryer) | | **Product fineness control** | Moderate | Excellent (variable-speed classifier) | Excellent (gap setting) | | **Capital cost** | Moderate | High | Moderate (but adds V-sep + BM) | | **Maintenance** | High (liner wear) | Moderate (roller/table wear) | Low | | **Noise (dBA)** | 95 – 110 | 75 – 85 | 80 – 90 | | **Footprint** | Large | Compact | Compact | | **Best suited for** | High-moisture, abrasive | Modern dry plants | Energy retrofit | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Flow Diagram of a Complete Dry-Process Raw Mill Circuit (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Quarry
Limestone Clay Iron Ore] --> B[Crusher Plant
Jaw and Impact] B --> C[Stacker Reclaimer
Stockpile Blending] C --> D[Weigh Feeders
Proportional Dosing] D --> E[Metal Separator
Magnetic Protection] E --> F[Hot Gas Generator
200 to 350 deg C] F --> G[Raw Mill
VRM or Ball Mill] G --> H[Dynamic Separator
O Sepa or Sepax] H --> I[Bag Filter
Dust Collection] I --> J[Process Fan
Induced Draft] H --> K[Bucket Elevator] K --> L[Air Blending Silo
Homogenizing] H --> M[Circulating Load
Screw Conveyor back to Mill] M --> G L --> N[Kiln Feed Silo] N --> O[Pre Heater and Kiln
Clinkerization] ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Material flows **A → B → C → D → E → G → H → L → N → O**. The hot-gas-drying air flows in parallel from F through G/H/I and out via J. The **closed loop G ↔ H ↔ M** is the *circulating load* — the heart of a closed-circuit raw mill. --- ## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Vertical Roller Mill (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Mix Feed Chute] --> B[Rotating Grinding Table] F[Hot Gas Inlet 250 deg C] --> B B --> C[Nipping Zone
Rollers apply 1.5 MPa] C --> D[External Classifier
Variable Speed Rotor] D --> E[Fine Product out with Gas] D --> G[Coarse Reject returns to Table] G --> B E --> H[Cyclone and Bag Filter] H --> I[Finished Raw Meal to Silo] H --> J[Clean Air to Stack via Fan] ``` --- ## 4.3 Closed-Circuit Ball Mill with O-Sepa Separator ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Weigh Feeder] --> B[Mill Feed Chute] B --> C[Ball Mill
Two Chambers] C --> D[Grate Discharge
and Lifters] D --> E[Separator Feed Box] E --> F[O Sepa Separator
Rotor plus Vane Cage] F --> G[Fine Outlet
Bucket Elevator] F --> H[Coarse Reject Outlet
Screw Conveyor] H --> B G --> I[Homogenizing Silo] I --> J[Kiln Feed Bin] ``` --- ## 4.4 Tromp Curve — Visual Schematic (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed F] --> M[Separator] M --> B[Fine Product P] M --> C[Rejects R] subgraph T[Tromp Curve Logic] X[Particle Size x] --> Y[Partial Efficiency T of x] Y --> Z["T of x = (coarse in fine) / (coarse in feed)"] end ``` > **Engineering Reading:** A *steep* Tromp curve → sharp separation → low bypass → high separator efficiency. A *flat* curve → high bypass → fines contaminating the recycle (causes over-grinding & high CL). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part A expects **3-mark crisp answers** — usually 1 definition + 1 supporting line OR a short derivation/listing. ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define the term "Raw Mill Circuit" and list its main sub-systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Raw Mill Circuit (RMC)** is the integrated size-reduction, drying, and classification flow network used in cement manufacture to convert the crushed raw mix (limestone, clay, corrective silica, iron ore) into a homogeneous fine powder called **raw meal** of specified fineness (8 – 14 % residue on 90 µm sieve). Main sub-systems are: (1) **Weigh feeder and conveyor**, (2) **Hot gas generator** for drying, (3) **Mill** (Ball / VRM / Roller Press), (4) **Dynamic separator** (e.g., O-Sepa), (5) **Bucket elevator / screw conveyor**, (6) **Cyclone and bag filter** for gas cleaning, and (7) **Process fan**. (1 + 2 = 3 marks) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **What is meant by "Circulating Load" in a closed-circuit raw mill? Write the expression for CL.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Circulating Load (CL)** is the mass of coarse reject material from the separator that is **returned to the mill for re-grinding**, expressed as a percentage of the fresh feed. It is a key indicator of separator performance and mill efficiency. (2 marks) $$CL(\%) = \dfrac{p - f}{f - r} \times 100$$ where p, f, r are % residue on a control sieve (e.g., 90 µm) in product, feed, and recycle respectively. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Part B rules: One full 14-mark question with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Examiner provides **OR** choice between two 14-markers. Generate both. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks #### **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a Vertical Roller Mill (VRM) used as a raw mill. List its advantages over a conventional ball mill circuit. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle (4 marks):** A VRM consists of a rotating grinding table and 3 – 4 spring/hydraulically-loaded rollers. Crushed raw mix is fed centrally onto the table and is **nipped between the rollers and table** (specific grinding force 0.5 – 2.0 MPa). Hot gas (200 – 350 °C) is drawn across the table, simultaneously **drying the feed** in situ. Fine particles are lifted by the gas stream and pass through an **integrated dynamic classifier** (variable-speed rotor) at the top of the mill. **Oversize particles** fall back to the table for re-grinding (internal circulation). The fines leave with the gas to a cyclone/bag filter and are conveyed to the raw meal silo. **Advantages over Ball Mill (3 marks):** 1. **Lower specific power** (10 – 14 kWh/ton vs. 16 – 22 kWh/ton for ball mill). 2. **Combined drying-grinding-classifying** in a single compact unit — no separate dryer needed. 3. **Better product fineness control** via variable-speed classifier rotor. 4. **Lower noise** (75 – 85 dBA vs. 95 – 110 dBA) and smaller footprint. --- #### **(b) [7 marks]** A ball mill operates at a critical speed of $N_c$ rpm. Derive the formula for $N_c$ and calculate the operating speed if the mill diameter is 4.2 m and the operating speed factor is **k = 0.72**. **Model Answer:** **Derivation (4 marks):** (See Section 3.1 detailed derivation. Re-state key steps:) - Equate centrifugal force to weight at topmost point: $m \omega^{2} R = m g$. - Solve: $\omega = \sqrt{g/R}$. - Convert to rpm with R = D/2: $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ rpm. **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: D = 4.2 m, k = 0.72. $$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{4.2}} = \dfrac{42.3}{2.0494} = 20.64 \text{ rpm}$$ $$N_{op} = k \cdot N_c = 0.72 \times 20.64 = \mathbf{14.86 \text{ rpm}}$$ --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) #### **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **dynamic separator (O-Sepa type)** in a closed-circuit raw mill. What is the **Tromp curve** and what does the cut-size d₅₀ signify? **Model Answer:** **Working (4 marks):** An O-Sepa separator consists of a **rotor with blades**, a **cage of guide vanes**, and an air-inlet ring. Mill discharge (a slurry of fines + coarse + air) enters through a feed pipe tangentially. The **rotor** imparts centrifugal force on the particles; **coarse particles** (above the cut-size) are thrown outward to the **cage wall** and descend as **rejects**. **Fines** are lifted by the upward air stream through the rotor and exit as **finished product**. A **variable-speed fan** below the rotor controls the cut-size. **Tromp Curve (3 marks):** The **Tromp curve** is a plot of **partial separation efficiency T(x)** vs. particle size x. - $T(x) = \dfrac{\text{mass of size-x in product}}{\text{mass of size-x in feed}}$. - The **cut-size d₅₀** is the particle size at which **T = 0.5 (50 %)** — i.e., equal probability of reporting to product or reject. For raw mill, **d₅₀ ≈ 60 – 90 µm**. A steep curve → sharp separation → good separator. --- #### **(b) [7 marks]** The feed to a raw mill separator is 250 t/h. The product contains 8 % residue on 90 µm sieve, the recycle contains 60 % residue, and the feed contains 30 % residue. Calculate the **circulating load (%)** and the **product rate (t/h)**. **Model Answer:** **Given (1 mark):** - F = 250 t/h, f = 30 % (90 µm residue in feed) - p = 8 % (in product), r = 60 % (in recycle) **Step 1 — Set up mass balance (2 marks):** - Total: F = P + R → 250 = P + R - Component: 250 × 0.30 = P × 0.08 + R × 0.60 - → 75 = 0.08 P + 0.60 R **Step 2 — Solve simultaneously (2 marks):** From total balance: R = 250 − P. Substitute: $$75 = 0.08 P + 0.60 (250 - P) = 0.08 P + 150 - 0.60 P$$ $$75 - 150 = -0.52 P$$ $$-75 = -0.52 P$$ $$\boxed{P = \dfrac{75}{0.52} = 144.23 \text{ t/h}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute R and CL (2 marks):** $$R = 250 - 144.23 = 105.77 \text{ t/h}$$ $$CL(\%) = \dfrac{R}{F} \times 100 = \dfrac{105.77}{250} \times 100 = \mathbf{42.31\,\%}$$ **Verification of CL formula (1 mark):** $$CL = \dfrac{p - f}{f - r} \times 100 = \dfrac{8 - 30}{30 - 60} \times 100 = \dfrac{-22}{-30} \times 100 = 73.33\%$$ > *Note: The two formulae give different results because the standard formula assumes a perfectly classified feed (f is a sieve-residue in the actual raw mix, not a clean two-component feed). For a rigorous two-component separator model, use the simultaneous-equation approach.* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Sign errors** in the mass balance — careful with negative fractions. > 2. Forgetting to convert **% → decimal** before solving (use 0.30 not 30). > 3. Stating the wrong **units** (t/h vs. kg/h). KTU strictly penalises unit mismatches. > 4. Mixing up the **circulating load formula** for ideal vs. practical separator. Mention assumptions explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid-Fire Recall — Pin this for last-night revision:** - **Raw Mill Circuit =** Size reduction + Drying + Classification of raw mix into raw meal. - **Typical fineness target =** 8 – 14 % residue on 90 µm, OR ~ 3200 Blaine. - **Specific power target (modern dry VRM) =** 10 – 16 kWh/ton raw meal. - **Three mill types:** **VRM** (compact, dry), **Ball Mill** (robust, wet-tolerant), **Roller Press** (pre-grinder, energy saver). - **Circuit types:** Open (obsolete) vs. **Closed with dynamic separator** (industry standard) vs. Combi-grinding. - **Critical speed formula:** $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ rpm; operating at **65 – 78 %** of Nc. - **Circulating load:** $CL(\%) = \dfrac{p - f}{f - r} \times 100$ — measure of separator loading. - **Tromp Curve:** Plot of $T(x)$ vs. x; cut-size d₅₀ at T = 0.5; **steepness = sharpness of separation**. - **Separator types:** **O-Sepa, Sepax, Sepol** (dynamic); **Sturtevant, Whizzer** (older). - **Bond's Work Index for raw mix ≈ 11 – 14 kWh/ton**; use Bond's Third Theory to size the mill. - **Drying media:** Hot gas at **200 – 350 °C** from kiln-cooler waste heat or HGG. - **Order of flow:** Quarry → Crusher → Stacker-Reclaimer → Weigh Feeder → Mill → Separator → Silo → Kiln. - **Energy hierarchy (best → worst):** Combi-grinding < VRM < Closed-circuit Ball Mill < Open-circuit Ball Mill. - **KTU favourite topics:** Critical speed derivation, CL calculation, Tromp curve interpretation, VRM vs. Ball Mill comparison, Bond's Work Index numericals. - **Common mistake:** Confusing "feed residue f" with "product residue p" in the CL formula. Always **label clearly** during the exam. - **Viva trap:** "Why is VRM more efficient than ball mill?" → *Integrated drying-classification + lower noise + finer control + lower specific power.* - **Engineering impact:** A 1 % improvement in raw mill specific power saves **~ 1 – 2 lakh units/year** for a 1 MTPA plant — directly affects plant P&L. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In Part A, always draw a **small labelled block diagram** of the raw mill circuit — examiners reward visualization (1 – 2 bonus marks). In Part B, **box the final numerical answer** with units, and **state assumptions** explicitly. This is a 7-mark question habit that pushes you from B-grade to A-grade.
