topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cement Beneficiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cement Beneficiation** is the systematic sequence of physical, chemical, and mechanical treatment operations applied to the raw materials of cement (primarily limestone, clay/marl, sand, and iron ore) in order to **upgrade their quality, ensure chemical homogeneity, and achieve a controlled chemical composition** before they are fed into the pyro-processing (kiln) stage.

In strict KTU 2024 scheme terms, cement beneficiation is the **upstream engineering activity** that converts a heterogeneous, low-grade, run-of-mine (ROM) mineral mixture into a **homogenized, fine-grained raw mix (raw meal)** that satisfies three classical quality moduli — the **Lime Saturation Factor (LSF)**, **Silica Modulus (SM)**, and **Alumina Modulus (AM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Beneficiation is *not* a single unit operation — it is a chain of interlocked unit operations: **Extraction → Primary Crushing → Secondary/Tertiary Crushing → Pre-blending → Raw Material Storage (Homogenization) → Grinding (Raw Milling) → Separation (Cyclones/Filters) → Dosing/Corrective Additives**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a dish for a hundred guests, but the vegetables you bought from the market vary in size, ripeness, and taste. Before cooking, you would:
1. **Sort** the good pieces from the bad ones (→ *crushing & screening*),
2. **Wash** the dirt off (→ *washing & scrubbing*),
3. **Chop and mix** them uniformly in a big bowl (→ *blending & homogenization*),
4. **Grind** to a fine paste if the recipe demands it (→ *raw milling*),
5. **Adjust salt** if the mix is too bland (→ *corrective additive dosing*).

Cement beneficiation is the **industrial-scale "kitchen-prep"** that ensures the kiln receives a perfectly uniform, chemically balanced "recipe" of raw mix, every minute, every hour, for 365 days a year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric:** A modern 10,000 TPD (tonnes per day) cement plant typically processes **~14,000–15,000 TPD of raw materials** through beneficiation before they reach the kiln. Even a 1% deviation in raw mix chemistry can alter the final clinker quality dramatically.

---

## 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics (The "Three Moduli")

For any raw mix, the chemist must satisfy three numerical constraints. These are the **heart of cement beneficiation control**:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (OPC) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lime Saturation Factor | **LSF** | **0.92 – 0.98** | Controls free lime (CaO) vs. combined lime |
| Silica Modulus | **SM** | **2.0 – 3.0** | Controls silicate vs. aluminate phases |
| Alumina Modulus | **AM** | **1.0 – 2.0** | Controls tricalcium aluminate (C₃A) content |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard reference oxide percentages assumed for pure raw mix calculation are: **CaO = 65%**, **SiO₂ = 21%**, **Al₂O₃ = 6%**, **Fe₂O₃ = 3%** (with LSF=1, SM=2.5, AM=1.0). These are the **KTU board-exam defaults**.

---

## 1.4 Why Beneficiation? — The Engineering Motivation

1. **Run-of-mine variability**: Limestone CaO content varies from **38% to 54%** across a single quarry face.
2. **Kiln tolerance is low**: A rotary kiln has only a **~15-second residence time** for raw mix → chemistry must be precise.
3. **Energy economics**: Homogenized feed reduces specific heat consumption (Kcal/kg of clinker) by **3–8%**.
4. **Product quality**: Proper LSF/SM/AM directly controls 28-day compressive strength of cement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Flow of Cement Beneficiation

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to **list, sequence, and explain** the following operations:

### Stage A — **Extraction & Quarrying**
- Drilling → Blasting → Loading (Shovel/Hydraulic Excavator) → Hauling (Dumper/Tipper).
- Selective mining based on **core sampling** and **chemical analysis** of benches.

### Stage B — **Primary Crushing**
- Equipment: **Jaw Crusher** or **Gyratory Crusher**.
- Reduces ROM size from **1,500 mm → 100–150 mm**.
- Reduction Ratio: **6 : 1 to 8 : 1**.

### Stage C — **Secondary & Tertiary Crushing**
- Equipment: **Cone Crusher (Standard & Short Head)**, **Hammer Mill**, **Impact Crusher**.
- Output size: **20–25 mm**, often **<10 mm** in dry process plants.

### Stage D — **Pre-blending (Pre-homogenization)**
- Equipment: **Circular/Rectangular Pre-blending Stockpile** (Chevron / Cone-Shell stacking).
- Goal: Reduce chemistry variation by a factor of **~10×** (Raw CaO std. dev. drops from **±5% to ±0.5%**).
- The **Stacker-Reclaimer** builds longitudinal layers (Chevron method) and reclaims vertically across all layers.

### Stage E — **Raw Material Grinding (Raw Milling)**
- Two technologies:
  - **Dry Process**: Vertical Roller Mill (VRM) or Ball Mill with High Efficiency Separator (HES).
  - **Wet Process**: Tube Mill with slurry tanks (rare in modern plants).
- Output: **Raw Meal** with **75–90% passing 90 microns** (170 mesh).
- VRM specific power consumption: **10–18 kWh/tonne**; Ball Mill: **18–25 kWh/tonne**.

### Stage F — **Classification & Separation**
- Equipment: **Cyclone Separators**, **Bag Filters** (Electrostatic Precipitators are avoided in raw mills due to explosion risk).
- **High Efficiency Separator (HES)** uses centrifugal + gravitational force to control top size and rejects coarse particles back to mill.

### Stage G — **Corrective Additive Dosing**
- Based on continuous **X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF) / Prompt Gamma Neutron Activation Analysis (PGNAA)** analyzer output, corrective materials are dosed:
  - **High-CaO deficiency** → Add high-limestone component.
  - **High-SiO₂** → Add high-limestone or iron ore.
  - **High-Al₂O₃** → Add sand (silica correction) or iron ore (Fe₂O₃ correction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Without precise dosing, the resulting clinker would contain unwanted free lime (CaO), reducing strength and causing "unsoundness" (volume expansion and cracking) in the final concrete.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for the Part B 14-mark derivation questions.

### (A) The Three Moduli

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $\text{LSF} = \dfrac{\text{CaO}}{2.8 \cdot \text{SiO}_2 + 1.18 \cdot \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 0.65 \cdot \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$ | **Lime Saturation Factor** — measures how much lime is "saturated" by the acidic oxides |
| $\text{SM} = \dfrac{\text{SiO}_2}{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$ | **Silica Modulus** — measures silicate to aluminate phase ratio |
| $\text{AM} = \dfrac{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3}{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}$ | **Alumina Modulus** — controls C₃A content in clinker |

> **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE:** Always write absolute moduli values as `\vert \text{LSF} \vert` if needed; never use pipe `|` inside markdown tables.

### (B) Raw Mix Proportioning — Basis: 100 kg Clinker

In cement chemistry, the standard calculation is anchored to **100 kg of clinker produced**, with the four main oxides targeted as:

$$
\text{CaO} = 65 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{SiO}_2 = 21 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 = 6 \text{ kg}, \quad \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 = 3 \text{ kg}
$$

The raw material composition is then solved using a **two-component system (Limestone + Clay)** algebraic material balance:

Let:
- $L$ = kg of limestone, $C$ = kg of clay
- $l_i$, $c_i$ = weight % of oxide $i$ in limestone and clay respectively

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CaO balance:} \quad & L \cdot l_{\text{CaO}} + C \cdot c_{\text{CaO}} = 65 \\
\text{SiO}_2 \text{ balance:} \quad & L \cdot l_{\text{SiO}_2} + C \cdot c_{\text{SiO}_2} = 21 \\
\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ balance:} \quad & L \cdot l_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} + C \cdot c_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 6 \\
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ balance:} \quad & L \cdot l_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} + C \cdot c_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### (C) Blaine's Equation (For Modulus Recalculation After Dosing)

When a corrective material is added in proportion $x$ (mass fraction), the new LSF, SM, AM are recomputed as the **weighted average** of the original mix and the corrective:

$$
\text{LSF}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{(1-x) \cdot \text{LSF}_{\text{orig}} \cdot \text{Acidicity}_{\text{orig}} + x \cdot \text{LSF}_{\text{corr}} \cdot \text{Acidicity}_{\text{corr}}}{(1-x) \cdot \text{Acidicity}_{\text{orig}} + x \cdot \text{Acidicity}_{\text{corr}}}
$$

where $\text{Acidicity} = 2.8 \cdot \text{SiO}_2 + 1.18 \cdot \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 0.65 \cdot \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ is the denominator of the LSF.

### (D) Real-world Utility

- **Process engineers** in cement plants use LSF/SM/AM for **every shift** to decide on corrective dosing.
- **Quality control labs** cross-check with **XRF analysis** every 1–2 hours.
- **Plant designers** use the moduli to size raw material homogenization silos — higher variability → larger silo volume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the LSF Equation (Origin and Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks (Part A, 3 marks):** *"Why are the constants 2.8, 1.18, and 0.65 in the LSF formula?"* This is a high-value conceptual derivation.

The LSF is derived from the **four key mineralogical reactions** in clinker formation, where one mole of each major oxide produces a specific mass of clinker mineral:

| Clinker Mineral | Chemical Formula | Mass of CaO combined per unit acid oxide |
|---|---|---|
| Tricalcium Silicate (C₃S / Alite) | $3\text{CaO}\cdot\text{SiO}_2$ | $\dfrac{3 \times 56}{60} = 2.80$ kg CaO per kg SiO₂ |
| Dicalcium Silicate (C₂S / Belite) | $2\text{CaO}\cdot\text{SiO}_2$ | (Subsumed in C₃S) |
| Tricalcium Aluminate (C₃A) | $3\text{CaO}\cdot\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | $\dfrac{3 \times 56}{102} = 1.647$ kg CaO per kg Al₂O₃ ≈ 1.18 (industrial practice uses this) |
| Tetracalcium Aluminoferrite (C₄AF) | $4\text{CaO}\cdot\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3\cdot\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ | $\dfrac{4 \times 56}{160} = 1.40$ kg CaO per kg (Al₂O₃+Fe₂O₃) — simplified to 0.65 for Fe₂O₃ alone in the LSF form |

**Step 1 — Molecular Weights (Standard)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CaO} &= 40 + 16 = 56 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{SiO}_2 &= 28 + 32 = 60 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 &= 2(27) + 3(16) = 102 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 &= 2(56) + 3(16) = 160 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric CaO Demand**

For complete combination to clinker minerals, the mass of CaO required per kg of each acid oxide:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For SiO}_2 \to \text{C}_3\text{S}: \quad & \frac{3 \times 56}{60} = \frac{168}{60} = 2.80 \text{ kg CaO / kg SiO}_2 \\
\text{For Al}_2\text{O}_3 \to \text{C}_3\text{A}: \quad & \frac{3 \times 56}{102} = \frac{168}{102} \approx 1.647 \text{ kg CaO / kg Al}_2\text{O}_3 \\
\text{For Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \to \text{C}_4\text{AF}: \quad & \frac{4 \times 56}{160} = \frac{224}{160} = 1.40 \text{ kg CaO / kg Fe}_2\text{O}_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Industrial Simplification**

Industry uses a **simplified LSF denominator** (1.18 for Al₂O₃ and 0.65 for Fe₂O₃) which accounts for the fact that not all CaO needs to fully react (allowing a small fraction of uncombined/free lime that is desired for sound cement). Hence:

$$
\boxed{\text{LSF} = \dfrac{\% \text{CaO}}{2.8 \cdot \% \text{SiO}_2 + 1.18 \cdot \% \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 0.65 \cdot \% \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}}
$$

> **Why LSF < 1 always?** The industrial value is kept at **0.92 – 0.98** so that a controlled amount (~1–2%) of free lime remains, which helps in early strength gain.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-mark pattern)

> **Problem (Modeled on KTU University Exam Pattern):**
> A cement raw mix analysis gives:
> - CaO = 42.5%, SiO₂ = 13.0%, Al₂O₃ = 3.5%, Fe₂O₃ = 2.5%
> 
> Calculate **(a)** LSF, **(b)** SM, **(c)** AM, and comment on the quality of the raw mix.

**Solution — Part (a): LSF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSF} &= \frac{42.5}{2.8 \times 13.0 + 1.18 \times 3.5 + 0.65 \times 2.5} \\
&= \frac{42.5}{36.40 + 4.13 + 1.625} \\
&= \frac{42.5}{42.155} \\
&= 1.0082
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** LSF > 1.0 → **Excess free lime is expected** → Reduce limestone or add acidic corrective (clay/sand).

**Solution — Part (b): SM**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SM} &= \frac{13.0}{3.5 + 2.5} = \frac{13.0}{6.0} = 2.167
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** SM = 2.17 lies in the **acceptable range (2.0–3.0)** for OPC.

**Solution — Part (c): AM**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AM} &= \frac{3.5}{2.5} = 1.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** AM = 1.40 is in the **good range (1.0–2.0)** → C₃A formation will be moderate; cement will be **sulphate-resistant to a fair degree**.

**Overall Verdict:** Mix is **acceptable in moduli (SM, AM)** but **LSF must be corrected** by adding a high-silica or high-alumina corrective material.

---

## 3.3 Raw Mix Proportioning (Two-Material Algebraic Solution)

**Worked Example — Bonus for KTU 14-Mark Depth**

> Two raw materials are available: **Limestone L** (CaO = 53%, SiO₂ = 1.2%, Al₂O₃ = 0.5%, Fe₂O₃ = 0.3%) and **Clay C** (CaO = 0.5%, SiO₂ = 58%, Al₂O₃ = 22%, Fe₂O₃ = 8%). Target clinker oxides (per 100 kg clinker): CaO = 65, SiO₂ = 21, Al₂O₃ = 6, Fe₂O₃ = 3. Determine L and C using a **two-equation simultaneous system** based on CaO and SiO₂.

**Step 1 — Set up equations (CaO and SiO₂ balances)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.53 L + 0.005 C &= 65 \quad \text{...(CaO balance)} \\
0.012 L + 0.58 C &= 21 \quad \text{...(SiO}_2 \text{ balance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for L from the first equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.53 L &= 65 - 0.005 C \\
L &= \frac{65 - 0.005 C}{0.53} = 122.642 - 0.00943 C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the second equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.012 (122.642 - 0.00943 C) + 0.58 C &= 21 \\
1.4717 - 0.0001132 C + 0.58 C &= 21 \\
0.5798868 C &= 19.5283 \\
C &= 33.68 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Find L**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 122.642 - 0.00943 \times 33.68 = 122.642 - 0.3176 = 122.32 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify using Al₂O₃ and Fe₂O₃ (should be within ±5%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ achieved} &= 0.005 \times 122.32 + 0.22 \times 33.68 = 0.612 + 7.41 = 8.02 \text{ kg} \quad (\text{Target } 6) \\
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \text{ achieved} &= 0.003 \times 122.32 + 0.08 \times 33.68 = 0.367 + 2.694 = 3.06 \text{ kg} \quad (\text{Target } 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Decision:** Al₂O₃ is **+33% above target** → need a small amount of silica or iron-ore correction to bring it down. A third material (sand/iron-ore) is introduced in industrial practice — KTU may ask for this 3-component system as a follow-up sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cement Beneficiation

Below is a **Mermaid block-flow diagram** mapping every interlocked unit operation in cement beneficiation, from quarry to kiln feed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quarry ROM Material] --> B[Drilling and Blasting]
    B --> C[Loading by Shovel or Excavator]
    C --> D[Hauling by Dumper]
    D --> E[Primary Crusher Jaw or Gyratory]
    E --> F[Secondary Crusher Cone Standard]
    F --> G[Tertiary Crusher Cone Short Head]
    G --> H[Pre-blending Stockpile Chevron Method]
    H --> I[Stacker Reclaimer Homogenization]
    I --> J[Raw Material Silo Storage]
    J --> K[Vertical Roller Mill or Ball Mill]
    K --> L[High Efficiency Separator HES]
    L --> M[Bag Filter Dust Collection]
    M --> N[Raw Meal Silos]
    N --> O[XRF Online Analyzer Feedback Loop]
    O --> P[Corrective Additive Dosing Limestone Clay Sand Iron Ore]
    P --> N
    N --> Q[Homogenized Raw Meal to Kiln]

    classDef stage1 fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage2 fill:#3a5f8f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage3 fill:#5f8fbf,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage4 fill:#8fbfdf,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef stage5 fill:#bfdfef,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef stage6 fill:#dfefff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366

    class A,B,C,D stage1
    class E,F,G stage2
    class H,I,J stage3
    class K,L,M stage4
    class N,O,P stage5
    class Q stage6
```

---

## 4.2 Process Hierarchy & Material Size Reduction Map

The following **Mermaid graph** illustrates the **size reduction chain** with the unit operation at each stage and the corresponding **reduction ratio**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeROM[ROM Limestone 1500 mm] -->|RR 6 to 8| nodeP[Primary Crusher 100 to 150 mm]
    nodeP -->|RR 4 to 5| nodeS[Secondary Crusher 25 to 40 mm]
    nodeS -->|RR 3 to 4| nodeT[Tertiary Crusher 6 to 10 mm]
    nodeT -->|RR 50 to 100 via VRM| nodeRM[Raw Meal 75 to 90 micron]

    classDef romStyle fill:#660000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef primaryStyle fill:#993300,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef secondaryStyle fill:#cc6600,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef tertiaryStyle fill:#cc9900,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef mealStyle fill:#003366,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

    class nodeROM romStyle
    class nodeP primaryStyle
    class nodeS secondaryStyle
    class nodeT tertiaryStyle
    class nodeRM mealStyle
```

---

## 4.3 Process-Functional Architecture (Quality Control Feedback Loop)

This **Mermaid flowchart** represents the **closed-loop control** nature of modern cement beneficiation, where continuous XRF analysis drives corrective dosing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Raw Meal Stream] --> anl[Online XRF Analyzer]
    anl -->|Calculate LSF, SM, AM| ctrl[Plant DCS Controller]
    ctrl -->|Modulate Dosing Setpoints| dos1[Dosing Bin Limestone]
    ctrl -->|Modulate Dosing Setpoints| dos2[Dosing Bin Clay]
    ctrl -->|Modulate Dosing Setpoints| dos3[Dosing Bin Sand]
    ctrl -->|Modulate Dosing Setpoints| dos4[Dosing Bin Iron Ore]
    dos1 --> mixer[Dynamic Homogenizing Silo]
    dos2 --> mixer
    dos3 --> mixer
    dos4 --> mixer
    mixer --> kiln[Output to Kiln Feed]
    anl -.->|Feedback Signal| ctrl

    classDef sensorStyle fill:#002b5c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef controlStyle fill:#0066cc,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef doseStyle fill:#3399ff,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputStyle fill:#66cc33,stroke:#003300,color:#003300

    class inp1,anl sensorStyle
    class ctrl controlStyle
    class dos1,dos2,dos3,dos4 doseStyle
    class mixer,outputStyle outputStyle
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Lime Saturation Factor (LSF). What is the typical range for Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) raw mix?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Lime Saturation Factor (LSF) is the ratio of the percentage of lime (CaO) actually present in the raw mix to the percentage of lime theoretically required to combine with the acidic oxides (SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃) to form the four principal clinker minerals — C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, and C₄AF.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$
\text{LSF} = \frac{\% \text{CaO}}{2.8 \cdot \% \text{SiO}_2 + 1.18 \cdot \% \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 0.65 \cdot \% \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}
$$

**Typical range for OPC:** **0.92 to 0.98**

> **Why below 1.0?** A slight deficit ensures a controlled small amount of free lime (~1–2%), which improves early strength without causing unsoundness.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the role of a pre-blending stockpile in cement raw material preparation? Mention the stacking method commonly used.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of pre-blending stockpile:**
> 1. **Reduces chemical variability** of the raw mix coming from the quarry/crushers.
> 2. Provides an **intermediate buffer storage** of 3–7 days' plant consumption.
> 3. Allows the **stack-reclaimer** system to mix successive layers, achieving a homogenized blend.

> **Common stacking method:** **Chevron (Windrow) Method** — the stacker moves back and forth depositing material in overlapping longitudinal layers, and the reclaimer cuts a vertical slice across all layers, ensuring uniform blending.

> **Result:** Raw mix variation in CaO is reduced from ±5% to ±0.5% (a ten-fold improvement in homogeneity).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student is expected to answer **either** the entire OR-group.

---

### **Question A. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various unit operations involved in cement raw material beneficiation from quarrying to raw meal preparation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The chemical analysis of a cement raw mix is given below. Calculate the **LSF, SM, and AM**. Comment on whether the mix is suitable for OPC. **(7 marks)**

| Oxide | Percentage |
|---|---|
| CaO | 43.0% |
| SiO₂ | 14.0% |
| Al₂O₃ | 3.2% |
| Fe₂O₃ | 2.0% |

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The block diagram showing quarrying → primary crushing → secondary crushing → tertiary crushing → pre-blending → raw milling → raw meal storage is as shown in **Section 4.1** above. The student should reproduce the seven-stage flow with the following key points:

1. **Quarrying**: Drilling, blasting, loading by shovel, and hauling by dumper.
2. **Primary crushing**: Jaw crusher reduces 1500 mm to 100–150 mm.
3. **Secondary/Tertiary crushing**: Cone crusher reduces to 20–25 mm or <10 mm.
4. **Pre-blending**: Chevron stockpile with stacker-reclaimer.
5. **Raw milling**: VRM or ball mill with HES.
6. **Raw meal storage**: Homogenizing silos with continuous XRF sampling.
7. **Corrective dosing**: Limestone, clay, sand, iron-ore controlled by DCS based on LSF, SM, AM feedback.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget the feedback control loop** (online XRF + corrective dosing). A mere linear list of equipment without the closed-loop control aspect will lose 1–2 marks. Always end the block diagram with the **feedback arrow** from XRF analyzer to dosing bins.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the LSF denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.8 \times 14.0 &= 39.20 \\
1.18 \times 3.2 &= 3.776 \\
0.65 \times 2.0 &= 1.30 \\
\text{Sum} &= 39.20 + 3.776 + 1.30 = 44.276
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute LSF:**

$$
\text{LSF} = \frac{43.0}{44.276} = 0.971
$$

> **LSF = 0.97 → Acceptable (within 0.92–0.98 range)**

**Step 3 — Compute SM:**

$$
\text{SM} = \frac{14.0}{3.2 + 2.0} = \frac{14.0}{5.2} = 2.692
$$

> **SM = 2.69 → Acceptable (within 2.0–3.0 range)**

**Step 4 — Compute AM:**

$$
\text{AM} = \frac{3.2}{2.0} = 1.60
$$

> **AM = 1.60 → Acceptable (within 1.0–2.0 range)**

**Final Verdict:** All three moduli are within the OPC range. The mix is **suitable for OPC manufacture** without corrective dosing.

---

### **Question B. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Two raw materials are available: **Limestone** (CaO = 52%, SiO₂ = 1.5%, Al₂O₃ = 0.5%, Fe₂O₃ = 0.4%) and **Clay** (CaO = 1.0%, SiO₂ = 60%, Al₂O₃ = 20%, Fe₂O₃ = 7%). Using a target composition of CaO = 65%, SiO₂ = 21%, Al₂O₃ = 6%, Fe₂O₃ = 3% (per 100 kg clinker basis), determine the proportions of limestone and clay. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the significance of the **Silica Modulus (SM)** and **Alumina Modulus (AM)** in raw mix design? What happens if **SM is too low** or **AM is too high**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Let $L$ = kg limestone, $C$ = kg clay. Use **two-equation system** on CaO and SiO₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.52 L + 0.01 C &= 65 \quad \text{...(1)} \\
0.015 L + 0.60 C &= 21 \quad \text{...(2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**From (1):**

$$
L = \frac{65 - 0.01 C}{0.52} = 125.0 - 0.01923 C
$$

**Substitute into (2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.015 (125.0 - 0.01923 C) + 0.60 C &= 21 \\
1.875 - 0.0002885 C + 0.60 C &= 21 \\
0.5997115 C &= 19.125 \\
C &= 31.89 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Then:**

$$
L = 125.0 - 0.01923 \times 31.89 = 125.0 - 0.613 = 124.39 \text{ kg}
$$

**Total raw mix:** $L + C = 156.28 \text{ kg}$ of raw feed → produces **100 kg of clinker** (loss of ~56 kg as CO₂ from calcination of limestone).

**Verification with Al₂O₃ and Fe₂O₃:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 &= 0.005 \times 124.39 + 0.20 \times 31.89 = 0.622 + 6.378 = 7.0 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 &= 0.004 \times 124.39 + 0.07 \times 31.89 = 0.498 + 2.232 = 2.73 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both are within 5–10% of the target, indicating a feasible mix with minor corrective adjustment possible.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Significance of SM:**

- SM controls the **silicate-to-aluminate ratio** in clinker.
- **High SM (>3.0)** → High C₃S, low C₃A → high strength, low heat of hydration, **low early strength**.
- **Low SM (<2.0)** → Low C₃S, high C₃A → **high early strength** but **lower ultimate strength** and **higher heat of hydration** (unsuitable for mass concrete dams).

**Significance of AM:**

- AM controls the **C₃A-to-C₄AF ratio** in clinker.
- **High AM (>2.0)** → High C₃A → **low sulphate resistance** (concrete deteriorates in sulphate-rich environments).
- **Low AM (<1.0)** → High C₄AF → poor early strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe the moduli as "ratios" without stating the **concrete engineering consequences**. Always link SM and AM to **specific applications** (mass concrete, sulphate resistance, etc.) to secure full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ **Cement Beneficiation = Quarrying + Crushing + Pre-blending + Raw Milling + Homogenization + Corrective Dosing.** All six must be listed in order.

- ✅ **Three Moduli are the heart of cement chemistry control:**
  - **LSF** = 0.92 to 0.98 (always < 1.0)
  - **SM** = 2.0 to 3.0
  - **AM** = 1.0 to 2.0

- ✅ **LSF Formula constants derivation:** 2.8 = (3 × 56)/60 from C₃S; 1.18 from industrial simplification of C₃A; 0.65 from C₄AF simplification.

- ✅ **Pre-blending Chevron method** reduces CaO variation by **~10×**.

- ✅ **Crushing sequence:** Primary (Jaw, RR 6–8) → Secondary (Cone, RR 4–5) → Tertiary (Short-Head Cone, RR 3–4) → VRM (RR 50–100).

- ✅ **Vertical Roller Mill (VRM)** is preferred over Ball Mill in modern dry process plants: lower power, higher drying capacity.

- ✅ **Standard target raw mix (per 100 kg clinker):** CaO = 65, SiO₂ = 21, Al₂O₃ = 6, Fe₂O₃ = 3.

- ✅ **Corrective materials:** Limestone (raise CaO), Clay/Sand (raise SiO₂, Al₂O₃), Iron Ore (raise Fe₂O₃, lower AM).

- ✅ **Online analyzers** (XRF or PGNAA) drive the **closed-loop control** of dosing — this is mandatory in any modern plant diagram.

- ✅ **Key difference between dry and wet process:** Wet uses ~30–40% water in slurry form (more dust-free, better homogenization but higher fuel cost); Dry is the modern standard.

- ✅ **Industrial acceptance range for LSF is intentionally below 1.0** to allow ~1–2% free lime for early strength, while preventing unsoundness.

- ✅ **Common numerical trap:** When asked to "find new LSF after dosing," students forget to use the **weighted-average form** of LSF (Blaine's equation), not simple arithmetic average.

- ✅ **Keyword-rich answer phrases** examiners look for: *"run-of-mine," "reduction ratio," "homogenizing silo," "corrective dosing," "LSF, SM, AM," "closed-loop control."* Use them naturally in answers for higher impression marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Method and Equipment Used in Portland Cement Manufacture, Petroleum Refining Process - Flow Diagram Illustrating Refining of Crude Oil Well to Saleable Product
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET205 - FUNDAMENTALS OF MANUFACTURING SYSTEMS AND  PROCESSES (PRODUCTION ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Method and Equipment Used in Portland Cement Manufacture, Petroleum Refining Process - Flow Diagram Illustrating Refining of Crude Oil Well to Saleable Product

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Portland Cement — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Portland Cement** is a hydraulic binder produced by intimately grinding together a carefully proportioned mixture of **calcareous** (lime-bearing, primarily limestone CaCO₃) and **argillaceous** (clay-bearing, primarily Al₂O₃·2SiO₂·2H₂O) raw materials, burning this mixture at a temperature of approximately **1450 °C** in a rotary kiln (a long, slightly inclined, rotating steel cylinder) to produce **clinker**, and then grinding the resulting clinker with a small amount of gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O, typically **3–5 %** by mass) to a fine powder. The setting and hardening of this binder is a chemical reaction with water, not a physical drying process.

The product owes its name to the resemblance of the hardened cement to the natural limestone quarried on the Isle of Portland, England. The manufacturing of cement is one of the most **energy-intensive** large-scale chemical processes, consuming roughly **3.4 GJ to 6.3 GJ** of thermal energy per tonne of cement produced.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Recipe"

Think of Portland cement as baking a very particular kind of cake. In a kitchen:
- **Limestone** (CaCO₃) is the *flour* — the main bulk.
- **Clay** (silica + alumina + iron oxide) is the *eggs* — the binding chemistry.
- **Heat** in the kiln is the *oven*.
- **Gypsum** added at the end is the *pinch of salt* that controls the rate of setting (retards flash set).

Just as no cake is edible until it has been baked at the right temperature for the right duration, no cement works until the raw mix has been *clinkered* at **~1450 °C** and then ground to a fine powder with high **surface area** (typically **300–350 m²/kg** by Blaine's test).

## 1.2 Petroleum Refining — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroleum Refining** is the industrial chemical-engineering process of separating, converting, and purifying the complex, multi-component crude oil (a naturally occurring dark-coloured, viscous liquid composed predominantly of hydrocarbons with carbon numbers typically between **C₁ to C₄₀+**) into a spectrum of useful saleable products such as **LPG, Naphtha, Gasoline (Petrol), Kerosene, Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF), Diesel, Lubricating Oils, Bitumen, and Petroleum Coke**, by exploiting the differences in their **boiling points** (fractional distillation) and by applying **cracking**, **reforming**, **alkylation**, and **treating** operations.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Salad Dressing Separator"

Imagine a freshly shaken bottle of salad dressing. Crude oil is exactly that — a thick, black, complex mixture where hundreds of different molecules are jumbled together. When left to *settle*, lighter and heavier components naturally separate. **Fractional distillation** does the same thing deliberately: it heats the crude oil, sends the vapours up a tall column, and **each component "picks its floor"** — the temperature at which it condenses back to liquid — just like a person finding the right altitude on a mountain where the climate matches their preference. Heavy, oily molecules stay at the bottom; light gases escape from the top.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight**
> - A typical modern refinery processes **80,000 to 600,000 barrels of crude oil per day** (1 barrel = **159 litres**).
> - Crude oil API gravity ranges from **10° (heavy) to 50° (light)**; API° is defined as $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{Specific Gravity}} - 131.5$.

---

## 1.3 Importance in the KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus

These two processes are studied together in Module 4 because they are the textbook examples of **batch-to-continuous process industries**:
- They highlight the role of **process flow sheeting**, **material balance**, and **energy integration** in non-mechanical manufacturing.
- They illustrate large-scale *chemical/thermal* conversion of *natural raw materials* into engineered products.
- They reinforce the CO mapping to **CO4** of the PCPET205 course (understanding process industries and material flow).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Principal Routes for Portland Cement Manufacture

The first major engineering decision in cement manufacture is whether to use the **Wet Process** or the **Dry Process** (and its modern variant, the **Semi-Dry / Pre-heater / Pre-calciner Process**).

### 2.1.1 Wet Process (Older, now obsolete in most new plants)

- Raw materials are ground with water in a **ball mill** to form a slurry containing **32–40 % water**.
- The slurry is fed to the rotary kiln.
- **Disadvantage:** High fuel consumption (≈ **6.3 GJ/tonne** of clinker) because the kiln must first evaporate the slurry water before clinkering.
- **Fuel cost** can be **50 % of the manufacturing cost** of cement.

### 2.1.2 Dry Process (Modern Industry Standard)

- Raw materials are dried and ground to a fine powder.
- Modern plants use a **Suspension Pre-heater (SP)** tower of **4–6 cyclones** + a **Pre-calciner** (a separate furnace chamber that calcines ~ **90 %** of the limestone before the main kiln).
- **Fuel consumption** drops to **3.4 – 4.5 GJ/tonne**.

### 2.1.3 Comparison Table

| Parameter | Wet Process | Dry Process |
|---|---|---|
| Raw mix form | Slurry (32-40 % water) | Dry powder |
| Pre-heater | None | 4-6 stage cyclone pre-heater |
| Pre-calciner | None | Yes (modern) |
| Fuel consumption (GJ/tonne) | 5.9 – 6.3 | 3.4 – 4.5 |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Product quality | Less uniform | More uniform |
| Current usage | < 5 % globally | > 95 % globally |

## 2.2 Detailed Manufacturing Steps and Equipment

The cement manufacturing process is broken into the following **four distinct engineering operations**:

### Step 1 — **Extraction and Crushing of Raw Materials**

- **Limestone** is extracted from quarries by **drilling and blasting** or by **surface miners**.
- **Clay / Shale** is extracted from adjacent quarries.
- The run-of-mine (ROM) limestone is reduced from boulder size (1 m) to **80–100 mm** in **Jaw Crushers** (primary) and **Hammer / Impact Crushers** (secondary).

### Step 2 — **Raw Material Grinding and Mixing**

- The crushed limestone and clay are proportioned according to the desired **modulus values** (see §2.4) and fed to:
  - **Ball Mill** (a rotating horizontal cylinder partially filled with steel balls).
  - **Vertical Roller Mill (VRM)** — modern, energy-efficient alternative.
  - **Roll Press / High Pressure Grinding Rolls (HPGR)** — pre-grinder.
- Output: **Raw Meal** (dry process) or **Raw Slurry** (wet process).
- The fineness of raw meal is monitored by **residue on 90 µm sieve ≤ 10 %**.

### Step 3 — **Clinkering in the Rotary Kiln**

- The rotary kiln is a **steel cylinder**, **3–8 m diameter**, **50–230 m long**, lined with **refractory bricks** (typically **magnesia-spinel** or **dolomite-based** in the burning zone).
- It is inclined at **3–4°** to the horizontal and rotates at **0.5–5 rpm**.
- As the raw meal moves from the cold end to the hot end, it passes through the following thermal zones:

| Zone | Temperature (°C) | Process / Equipment Role |
|---|---|---|
| Drying zone | 20 – 200 | Free moisture evaporates |
| Pre-heating zone | 200 – 600 | Clay dehydroxylation |
| Calcining zone | 600 – 900 | CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ |
| Burning / Sintering zone | 900 – 1450 | Clinker mineral formation (liquid phase) |
| Cooling zone | 1450 – 1000 | Clinker nodules solidify |

- The **clinker** exits as dark grey nodules (3–25 mm).
- It is cooled in a **Grate Cooler** or **Rotary Cooler**.

### Step 4 — **Cement Grinding (Finish Grinding)**

- Cooled clinker is mixed with **3–5 % gypsum** (and optionally **fly ash, slag, pozzolana** for blended cements) and ground in a **Cement Ball Mill** or **VRM** to produce the final cement.
- **Cement fineness** is measured by:
  - Blaine's air permeability test: **specific surface area ≥ 225 m²/kg** (IS 269 for OPC 43 grade) and **≥ 225 m²/kg** (OPC 53 grade).
  - Residue on **45 µm sieve ≤ 10 %**.

## 2.3 The Bogue's Compound — The Four Mineral Phases of Clinker

The four principal mineral phases in Portland cement clinker, identified by **R. H. Bogue**, are:

| Compound | Chemical Formula | Abbreviation | Typical % | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tricalcium Silicate | 3CaO·SiO₂ | C₃S | 30 – 55 | Early strength (7 days) |
| Dicalcium Silicate | 2CaO·SiO₂ | C₂S | 15 – 45 | Late strength (> 28 days) |
| Tricalcium Aluminate | 3CaO·Al₂O₃ | C₃A | 5 – 12 | Flash set (controlled by gypsum) |
| Tetracalcium Aluminoferrite | 4CaO·Al₂O₃·Fe₂O₃ | C₄AF | 5 – 12 | Flux, lowers clinkering temp |

## 2.4 Bogue's Equations (High-Yield for KTU)

Given the **oxide composition** of the clinker (CaO, SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃ — denoted C, S, A, F by weight %), Bogue's empirical equations predict the mineral composition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3S &= 4.071\,C \;-\; 7.602\,S \;-\; 6.718\,A \;-\; 1.430\,F \\
\%\,C_2S &= 8.602\,S \;+\; 5.068\,A \;+\; 1.079\,F \;-\; 3.071\,C \\
\%\,C_3A &= 2.650\,A \;-\; 1.692\,F \\
\%\,C_4AF &= 3.043\,F
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Bogue's equations assume the clinker is in **equilibrium** at room temperature. In practice, the actual mineral content can differ from these calculated values by ± **2–5 %**.

### 2.4.1 The Three Moduli of Cement Manufacture

| Modulus | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Lime Saturation Factor (LSF)** | $\text{LSF} = \dfrac{C}{2.8\,S + 1.18\,A + 0.65\,F}$ | 0.92 – 0.98 |
| **Silica Modulus (SM)** | $\text{SM} = \dfrac{S}{A + F}$ | 2.0 – 3.0 |
| **Alumina Modulus (AM)** | $\text{AM} = \dfrac{A}{F}$ | 1.0 – 4.0 |

- **LSF < 0.92:** under-burnt, low strength.
- **LSF > 1.0:** free lime, unsound cement.
- **Low SM:** high C₃A, high early strength, low liquid phase.
- **Low AM:** low liquid-phase viscosity, easy to burn.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Cement

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bogue — C₃S | $4.071C - 7.602S - 6.718A - 1.430F$ | % | Clinker mineral content |
| 2 | Bogue — C₂S | $8.602S + 5.068A + 1.079F - 3.071C$ | % | Clinker mineral content |
| 3 | Bogue — C₃A | $2.650A - 1.692F$ | % | Clinker mineral content |
| 4 | Bogue — C₄AF | $3.043F$ | % | Clinker mineral content |
| 5 | Lime Saturation Factor | $C / (2.8S + 1.18A + 0.65F)$ | — | Kiln operation control |
| 6 | Silica Modulus | $S / (A + F)$ | — | Strength control |
| 7 | Alumina Modulus | $A / F$ | — | Liquid phase control |
| 8 | Raw mix LSF (raw meal) | $\dfrac{100\,C}{2.8\,S + 1.18\,A + 0.65\,F}$ | — | Quality assurance |
| 9 | Clinker LHV (typical) | ~ 750 – 850 kcal/kg | kcal/kg | Energy balance |
| 10 | Specific surface area (Blaine) | $S = \dfrac{K}{\rho}\,\dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon^3}}{(1-\varepsilon)^2}\sqrt{t}$ | m²/kg | Cement fineness |

## 2.6 Petroleum Refining — Deep Theoretical Analysis

Crude oil is a mixture of **paraffins (alkanes)**, **naphthenes (cycloalkanes)**, **aromatics**, and **resins/asphaltenes**. Refining is the sequence of **separation** (based on boiling point) and **conversion** (changing molecular size/shape) processes.

### 2.6.1 Overall Refining Flow

The complete modern refinery is organised as:
1. **Crude Desalting** — electrostatic coalescer, removes salts and water.
2. **Atmospheric Distillation** — primary fractionator (a tall bubble-cap / sieve-tray column).
3. **Vacuum Distillation** — separates heavy residues without cracking them.
4. **Conversion Units** — cracking (FCC, hydrocracker), coking, visbreaking.
5. **Reforming / Upgrading** — catalytic reformer, alkylation, isomerization, Merox.
6. **Treating** — hydrodesulphurization (HDS), amine treating (sour gas removal), sweetening.
7. **Blending** — finished-product tank farm.

### 2.6.2 Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC) — Product Fractions

| Draw Tray (Top → Bottom) | Typical Boiling Range (°C) | Fraction | Major Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overhead | < 30 | Dry Gas (C₁–C₂) | Refinery fuel |
| Overhead | 30 – 70 | LPG (C₃–C₄) | Domestic / auto fuel |
| Side stream 1 | 70 – 100 | Light Naphtha | Gasoline pool |
| Side stream 2 | 100 – 150 | Heavy Naphtha | Gasoline + petrochemical feed |
| Side stream 3 | 150 – 250 | Kerosene | Jet fuel (ATF) |
| Side stream 4 | 250 – 350 | Diesel (Gas Oil) | Highway diesel |
| Side stream 5 | 350 – 370 | Atmospheric Gas Oil (AGO) | FCC feed |
| Bottoms | > 370 | Reduced Crude (RCO) | Vacuum column feed |

### 2.6.3 Key Conversion Processes

| Process | Feed | Catalyst / Method | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO) | Zeolite Y catalyst, 480–540 °C | Gasoline, LPG, propylene |
| **Catalytic Reforming** | Heavy Naphtha | Pt / Cl / Al₂O₃, 480–520 °C | High-octane gasoline, aromatics, H₂ |
| **Hydrocracking** | VGO, coker gas oil | Ni-Mo / SiO₂-Al₂O₃, high H₂ pressure | Jet fuel, diesel |
| **Alkylation** | C₃ / C₄ olefins + isobutane | H₂SO₄ or HF, 0–10 °C | High-octane alkylate (iso-octane) |
| **Isomerization** | C₅–C₆ n-paraffins | Pt / chlorided alumina | Iso-paraffins (high RON) |
| **Delayed Coking** | Vacuum residue | Thermal, ~ 500 °C | Petroleum coke, coker gas oil |
| **Visbreaking** | Vacuum residue | Thermal, mild (~ 470 °C) | Reduced viscosity fuel oil |

### 2.6.4 Refinery Octane Quality Concepts (KTU Favourite)

- **Research Octane Number (RON)** — engine test at 600 rpm, mild conditions.
- **Motor Octane Number (MON)** — engine test at 900 rpm, severe conditions.
- **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** $= \dfrac{(\text{RON} + \text{MON})}{2}$ — number shown on US petrol pumps.

For catalytic reforming, the **dehydrogenation** of **methylcyclohexane (MCH)** to **toluene (TOL)** is the most important model reaction for aromatic production:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_7\text{H}_{14} \;\; & \longrightarrow \;\; \text{C}_7\text{H}_8 \; + \; 3\,\text{H}_2 \\
\text{methylcyclohexane} \quad & \quad \text{toluene} \quad \text{hydrogen}
\end{aligned}
$$

This reaction produces **molecular hydrogen** — a critical by-product used in **hydrodesulphurization (HDS)** units.

### 2.6.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Petroleum

| # | Concept | Equation / Relation | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | API Gravity | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | Crude oil classification |
| 2 | Specific Gravity (SG) | $SG = \rho_{\text{oil}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ at 15.56 °C | Density measure |
| 3 | AKI (Pump Octane) | $(\text{RON} + \text{MON}) / 2$ | Petrol quality labelling |
| 4 | ASTM D86 — T50 (°C) | Distillation mid-boiling point | Volatility index |
| 5 | Watson (UOP) K-factor | $K = \dfrac{\sqrt[3]{T_B}}{SG}$ | Paraffinic vs aromatic character |
| 6 | Cetane Number (diesel) | CN = % n-cetane × 1 + % heptamethylnonane × 0 | Diesel ignition quality |
| 7 | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) | psi or kPa | Volatility safety |
| 8 | Yield of FCC gasoline | $Y_{gasoline} = 0.55\,V_{feed}$ (typical) | Material balance |
| 9 | Material balance | $\Sigma \text{Input} = \Sigma \text{Output} + \Sigma \text{Accumulation}$ | Mass conservation |
| 10 | Energy balance | $\Sigma H_{in} + Q_{in} = \Sigma H_{out} + Q_{out} + W_{net}$ | Refinery design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:**
> - API gravity is a *direct definition-based* numerical problem.
> - Bogue's equations require *oxide composition* substitution and algebraic simplification.
> - Watson's K-factor is a *single-line* calculation; the examiner tests whether you can convert °R (Rankine) from °F.
> - RON/MON/AKI problems test *conceptual* understanding, not heavy algebra.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Process | Industry Use | Career Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Portland cement | Civil engineering — concrete, mortar, grout, pre-cast | Structural / civil engineer |
| Petroleum refining | Transportation, petrochemicals, lubricants, asphalt | Chemical / petroleum engineer |
| Bogue's analysis | Cement plant quality control (lab chemists) | Quality engineer |
| API gravity | Crude oil trade pricing, blending | Petroleum trader / refinery engineer |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bogue's Composition Calculation

> **Question:** A Portland cement clinker has the following oxide analysis by weight: CaO = 67.0 %, SiO₂ = 22.0 %, Al₂O₃ = 6.0 %, Fe₂O₃ = 3.0 %, and other minor oxides = 2.0 %. Calculate the four Bogue compounds (C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF) and the LSF.

### Step 1 — Normalize the Major Oxides (to sum to 100 % of active oxides)

The 2 % of "minor oxides" is ignored in Bogue's formula (it represents MgO, K₂O, Na₂O, SO₃, etc.). We **renormalize** CaO, SiO₂, Al₂O₃, Fe₂O₃ to 100 %:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{67.0}{98.0} \times 100 = 68.367\,\% \\
S_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{22.0}{98.0} \times 100 = 22.449\,\% \\
A_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{6.0}{98.0} \times 100 = 6.122\,\% \\
F_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{3.0}{98.0} \times 100 = 3.061\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into Bogue's Equations

**For C₃S:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3S &= 4.071\,C - 7.602\,S - 6.718\,A - 1.430\,F \\
&= 4.071 \times 68.367 \;-\; 7.602 \times 22.449 \;-\; 6.718 \times 6.122 \;-\; 1.430 \times 3.061 \\
&= 278.27 - 170.65 - 41.13 - 4.38 \\
&= 62.11\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For C₂S:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_2S &= 8.602\,S + 5.068\,A + 1.079\,F - 3.071\,C \\
&= 8.602 \times 22.449 \;+\; 5.068 \times 6.122 \;+\; 1.079 \times 3.061 \;-\; 3.071 \times 68.367 \\
&= 193.10 + 31.03 + 3.30 - 209.95 \\
&= 17.48\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For C₃A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3A &= 2.650\,A - 1.692\,F \\
&= 2.650 \times 6.122 \;-\; 1.692 \times 3.061 \\
&= 16.22 - 5.18 \\
&= 11.04\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**For C₄AF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_4AF &= 3.043\,F \\
&= 3.043 \times 3.061 \\
&= 9.31\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify the Total

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3S + \%\,C_2S + \%\,C_3A + \%\,C_4AF &= 62.11 + 17.48 + 11.04 + 9.31 \\
&= 99.94 \;\approx\; 100\,\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Lime Saturation Factor (LSF)

Using the **original** (not normalized) oxide percentages is acceptable; the most common textbook convention uses the **normalized** values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSF} &= \frac{C}{2.8\,S + 1.18\,A + 0.65\,F} \\
&= \frac{68.367}{2.8 \times 22.449 + 1.18 \times 6.122 + 0.65 \times 3.061} \\
&= \frac{68.367}{62.86 + 7.22 + 1.99} \\
&= \frac{68.367}{72.07} \\
&= 0.949
\end{aligned}
$$

This **LSF = 0.95** lies in the ideal range of **0.92 – 0.98**, indicating a well-burnt clinker of good quality. ✔

### Step 5 — Silica Modulus and Alumina Modulus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SM} &= \frac{S}{A + F} = \frac{22.449}{6.122 + 3.061} = \frac{22.449}{9.183} = 2.45 \\
\text{AM} &= \frac{A}{F} = \frac{6.122}{3.061} = 2.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Both moduli lie in the recommended range — the clinker is **siliceous** (good strength) with **moderate liquid-phase fluidity**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — API Gravity & Specific Gravity Interconversion

> **Question:** A crude oil has a density of 920 kg/m³ at 15.56 °C. Calculate (a) its specific gravity, and (b) its API gravity. State whether the oil is "light" or "heavy".

### Step 1 — Compute the Specific Gravity (SG)

Density of water at 15.56 °C is **999 kg/m³** (we may take it as **1000 kg/m³** for simplicity in KTU problems).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SG} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.920
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the API Gravity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} &= \frac{141.5}{\text{SG}} - 131.5 \\
&= \frac{141.5}{0.920} - 131.5 \\
&= 153.80 - 131.5 \\
&= 22.30^{\circ}\,\text{API}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Classification

Crude oils are categorised as:

| API Gravity | Classification |
|---|---|
| API < 22° | **Heavy crude** |
| 22° ≤ API ≤ 31° | **Medium crude** |
| API > 31° | **Light crude** |

Since the calculated API is **22.3°**, this crude lies right at the boundary — it is on the **lighter side of medium crude** and is preferred by refiners because it yields more gasoline and diesel per barrel.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Petroleum Refinery Material Balance on ADC

> **Question:** An Atmospheric Distillation Column processes **100 000 kg/h** of desalted crude. The yield pattern (by mass) is:
> - LPG = 2 %
> - Naphtha = 18 %
> - Kerosene = 14 %
> - Diesel = 22 %
> - AGO = 12 %
> - Reduced Crude (Bottoms) = 30 %
> - **Losses (unaccounted) = 2 %**
> Verify the material balance and compute the mass flow of each product.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance Identity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma\,\text{Input} \;=\; \Sigma\,\text{Output} \;+\; \Sigma\,\text{Losses}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Check Closure (Sum of All Yields)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma\,\text{Yield} &= 2 + 18 + 14 + 22 + 12 + 30 + 2 \\
&= 100\,\% \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The material balance is internally consistent (closure satisfied).

### Step 3 — Compute Individual Product Mass Flow Rates

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{LPG}} &= 0.02 \times 100{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{Naphtha}} &= 0.18 \times 100{,}000 = 18{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{Kerosene}} &= 0.14 \times 100{,}000 = 14{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m_{\text{Diesel}}} &= 0.22 \times 100{,}000 = 22{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m_{\text{AGO}}} &= 0.12 \times 100{,}000 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m_{\text{RCO}}} &= 0.30 \times 100{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m_{\text{Losses}}} &= 0.02 \times 100{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma\,\text{Output + Losses} &= 2{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 14{,}000 + 22{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 2{,}000 \\
&= 100{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \;=\; \text{Input} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cement Kiln Clinker Production from Raw Mix

> **Question:** A cement plant feeds **150 tonnes/h** of raw meal (dry) containing **44.5 % CaO** to a rotary kiln with a pre-calciner. The loss-on-ignition (LOI = CO₂ driven off) is **35.5 %** of the raw meal. Calculate the **clinker output** in tonnes/h and the **clinker:raw meal ratio** (commonly called the **"clinker factor"**).

### Step 1 — Clinker Mass Output

The CO₂ is driven off in the calcining zone, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{clinker}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{raw meal}} \times (1 - \text{LOI}) \\
&= 150 \times (1 - 0.355) \\
&= 150 \times 0.645 \\
&= 96.75 \text{ tonnes/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Clinker Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Clinker Factor} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{clinker}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{raw meal}}} = \frac{96.75}{150} = 0.645
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches industry-typical values of **0.60 – 0.65** for dry-process kilns with pre-calciner.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Cement vs Petroleum — Side-by-Side)

| Parameter | Portland Cement | Petroleum Refining |
|---|---|---|
| Feed | Limestone + Clay | Crude oil |
| Process type | Continuous, mostly dry | Continuous, all fluid |
| Main reactor | Rotary Kiln | Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation + FCC |
| Operating T (°C) | 1450 (kiln) / 200–500 (mill) | 350–540 (ADC, FCC) / 700 (FCC regenerator) |
| Operating P (bar) | Near-atmospheric | 1–30 (ADC) / 1–200 (hydrocracker) |
| Primary chemical step | Calcination + Clinkering | Fractionation + Cracking |
| Product form | Powder | Liquid / gas / solid |
| Major energy use | Coal (thermal) | Crude + natural gas |
| By-product | CO₂, kiln dust | Petroleum coke, sulphur, slurry |
| Key engineering challenge | CO₂ capture, alternative fuels | Sulphur control, octane/cetane balance |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read before writing**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** oxide composition to 100 % before applying Bogue's equations → loses 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing **% by mass of oxide** vs **% by mass of compound** in Bogue.
> 3. Using API formula in the wrong direction (use $141.5/\text{SG} - 131.5$, not the reverse).
> 4. Mixing up **distillation (physical separation)** with **cracking (chemical conversion)** in the refinery flow.
> 5. Failing to draw a **flow diagram** in 14-mark questions — diagrams carry 2–3 marks.
> 6. Mentioning **wet process** advantages (e.g., better raw mix homogeneity) without comparing it to dry process — examiner wants a comparison.
> 7. Writing **"petroleum"** when the question asks for **"petrochemical"** — they are different industries.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Portland Cement. List the four Bogue compounds and their typical functions in hardened cement paste.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Portland cement is a hydraulic binder obtained by burning an intimate mixture of calcareous (limestone) and argillaceous (clay) materials at a temperature of about 1450 °C in a rotary kiln to form **clinker**, and then grinding the clinker with about 3–5 % gypsum to a fine powder. The setting and hardening occur by chemical reaction with water (hydration).

The four Bogue compounds are:
1. **C₃S (Tricalcium silicate, 3CaO·SiO₂)** — 30–55 % — responsible for **early strength** (1–7 days).
2. **C₂S (Dicalcium silicate, 2CaO·SiO₂)** — 15–45 % — responsible for **later-age strength** (> 28 days).
3. **C₃A (Tricalcium aluminate, 3CaO·Al₂O₃)** — 5–12 % — causes **flash set**; gypsum is added to retard.
4. **C₄AF (Tetracalcium aluminoferrite, 4CaO·Al₂O₃·Fe₂O₃)** — 5–12 % — acts as a **flux** and lowers the clinkering temperature.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is meant by "API gravity" of crude oil? A crude oil has SG = 0.88. Calculate its API gravity and state whether it is light, medium, or heavy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

API gravity is an **inverse measure of density** of crude oil relative to water, expressed in degrees API. It was developed by the American Petroleum Institute to characterise crude oils. The formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} &= \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5 \\
&= \frac{141.5}{0.88} - 131.5 \\
&= 160.80 - 131.5 \\
&= 29.3^{\circ}\,\text{API}
\end{aligned}
$$

Classification: API = 29.3° lies in the range **22°–31°**, so the crude is a **medium crude** (bordering on light). It will yield a good proportion of gasoline and middle distillates.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **dry process** of Portland cement manufacture. List the major equipment used at each stage. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

#### **Model Solution**

The dry process of cement manufacture consists of the following sequential stages (each with its main equipment):

1. **Quarrying and Crushing**
   - Limestone is quarried by drilling and blasting.
   - Clay/shale is extracted separately.
   - Equipment: **Jaw Crusher** (primary) → **Hammer/Impact Crusher** (secondary) → size reduced to ~ 80 mm.

2. **Pre-blending and Storage**
   - Crushed raw materials are stacked in longitudinal piles by a **Stacker** for homogenisation.
   - Stored in **silos** to balance composition variation.

3. **Raw Grinding**
   - Dry raw mix is ground in a **Ball Mill** (tumbling cylinder with steel balls) or modern **Vertical Roller Mill (VRM)**.
   - Hot gases from the kiln are used to dry the material simultaneously.
   - Product: **Raw Meal** with residue on 90 µm sieve ≤ 10 %.

4. **Homogenisation**
   - Raw meal is pneumatically blended in a **blending silo** (typically two-compartment, airswept).

5. **Pre-heating**
   - The raw meal is suspended in the hot kiln exhaust gases in a **4–6 stage cyclone pre-heater tower**.
   - This pre-heats the meal to ~ 800 °C using waste heat, **saving 30 % fuel**.

6. **Pre-calcining**
   - A separate **Calciner Vessel** burns 50–60 % of the total fuel, achieving **~90 % calcination** of CaCO₃.
   - Inlet temperature ~ 850 °C; outlet ~ 1100 °C.

7. **Clinkering in Rotary Kiln**
   - A **rotary kiln** — steel cylinder, 3–8 m diameter, 50–230 m long, inclined 3–4°, lined with **refractory bricks** (magnesia–spinel in the burning zone), rotating at 0.5–5 rpm.
   - Maximum temperature: **1450 °C** at the burning zone.
   - Material residence time: 30–45 min.
   - Output: **Clinker** nodules (3–25 mm).

8. **Cooling**
   - Clinker is cooled in a **Grate Cooler** (modern, 70–80 % heat recovery) or **Rotary Cooler** (older).
   - Cooled clinker: ~ 100 °C.

9. **Cement Grinding (Finish Mill)**
   - Clinker + **3–5 % gypsum** (and supplementary cementitious materials like fly ash, slag) is ground in a **Cement Ball Mill** or **VRM**.
   - Fineness: Blaine specific surface area ≥ 225 m²/kg (OPC 43) and ≥ 225 m²/kg (OPC 53); residue on 45 µm ≤ 10 %.

10. **Storage and Packing**
    - Finished cement is stored in **silos** and dispatched via **bag packers** or **bulk tankers**.

**Flow Diagram (must be drawn on the answer sheet — see §4.1 in these notes for the schematic version).**

#### **Mark Split (Examiner's View)**

| Sub-part | Marks | Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| Flow diagram with stages | 3 | All 10 stages labelled, arrows correct |
| Equipment names (crusher, mill, pre-heater, calciner, kiln, cooler) | 2 | 1 mark for correct identification, 1 mark for correct sequence |
| Temperature / process parameters | 1 | 1450 °C, 800 °C pre-heat, etc. |
| Clarity of explanation | 1 | Sub-steps explained in sentences |
| **Total for (a)** | **7** | |

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Bogue's equations** used to estimate the mineral composition of a cement clinker. The oxide composition of a clinker is: CaO = 66.5 %, SiO₂ = 21.0 %, Al₂O₃ = 5.5 %, Fe₂O₃ = 3.5 % (and the balance 3.5 % is minor oxides). Calculate **C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF** and the **Lime Saturation Factor (LSF)**. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

#### **Model Solution**

**Theory (3 marks):**

Bogue's equations are empirical formulas that compute the four major mineral phases (C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF) of a Portland cement clinker from the **weight percentages of the four major oxides**: **C** = CaO, **S** = SiO₂, **A** = Al₂O₃, **F** = Fe₂O₃. The formulas assume thermodynamic equilibrium and complete crystallisation of the named compounds at ambient temperature.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3S &= 4.071\,C - 7.602\,S - 6.718\,A - 1.430\,F \\
\%\,C_2S &= 8.602\,S + 5.068\,A + 1.079\,F - 3.071\,C \\
\%\,C_3A &= 2.650\,A - 1.692\,F \\
\%\,C_4AF &= 3.043\,F
\end{aligned}
$$

The LSF is defined as the ratio of actual lime to the lime required to combine with the acidic oxides to form the principal clinker minerals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSF} = \frac{C}{2.8\,S + 1.18\,A + 0.65\,F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Normalize major oxides to sum to 100 %:**

Sum of C + S + A + F = 66.5 + 21.0 + 5.5 + 3.5 = 96.5 %

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{66.5}{96.5} \times 100 = 68.91\,\% \\
S_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{21.0}{96.5} \times 100 = 21.76\,\% \\
A_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{5.5}{96.5} \times 100 = 5.70\,\% \\
F_{\text{norm}} &= \frac{3.5}{96.5} \times 100 = 3.63\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute C₃S:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3S &= 4.071 \times 68.91 - 7.602 \times 21.76 - 6.718 \times 5.70 - 1.430 \times 3.63 \\
&= 280.53 - 165.42 - 38.29 - 5.19 \\
&= 71.63\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute C₂S:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_2S &= 8.602 \times 21.76 + 5.068 \times 5.70 + 1.079 \times 3.63 - 3.071 \times 68.91 \\
&= 187.18 + 28.89 + 3.92 - 211.62 \\
&= 8.37\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute C₃A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_3A &= 2.650 \times 5.70 - 1.692 \times 3.63 \\
&= 15.11 - 6.14 \\
&= 8.96\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute C₄AF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,C_4AF &= 3.043 \times 3.63 = 11.05\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify closure:**

$$
71.63 + 8.37 + 8.96 + 11.05 = 100.01 \approx 100\,\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Compute LSF (using normalized values):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSF} &= \frac{68.91}{2.8 \times 21.76 + 1.18 \times 5.70 + 0.65 \times 3.63} \\
&= \frac{68.91}{60.93 + 6.73 + 2.36} \\
&= \frac{68.91}{70.02} \\
&= 0.984
\end{aligned}
$$

LSF = 0.98 lies at the **upper edge** of the recommended range (0.92–0.98). The clinker is **well-burnt with high early strength (high C₃S = 71.6 %)**. ✔

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **block flow diagram**, describe the **petroleum refining process from crude oil well to saleable product**. Explain the role of the **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)**, **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)**, and **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** in a modern refinery. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

#### **Model Solution**

**Sequence of Operations (must be drawn — see §4.3 schematic):**

1. **Wellhead and Three-Phase Separator** — Crude oil emerging from the well contains dissolved gas, water, and salt. A **three-phase separator** at the wellhead splits the stream into oil, gas, and water.
2. **Stabilisation** — Light ends (C₁–C₄) are removed in a **stabilisation column** to give a stable crude.
3. **Desalting** — Salts (mostly NaCl, MgCl₂) are removed in an **electrostatic desalter** to prevent downstream corrosion.
4. **Atmospheric Distillation Column (ADC)** — The desalted crude is heated in a **fired heater** to **350–400 °C** and flashed into the ADC. Vapours rise, and trays (sieve / valve / bubble-cap) condense fractions at progressively lower temperatures as they ascend.

| Draw Position | Product | Boiling Range (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| Overhead | Off-gas + LPG | < 70 |
| Side 1 | Light Naphtha | 70 – 100 |
| Side 2 | Heavy Naphtha | 100 – 150 |
| Side 3 | Kerosene (ATF) | 150 – 250 |
| Side 4 | Diesel (LGO) | 250 – 350 |
| Side 5 | AGO | 350 – 370 |
| Bottoms | Reduced Crude (RCO) | > 370 |

5. **Vacuum Distillation Unit (VDU)** — RCO is distilled at **50–100 mmHg** absolute pressure to avoid cracking. Produces:
   - **Vacuum Gas Oil (VGO)** — feed to FCC.
   - **Vacuum Residue** — feed to coker / bitumen / visbreaker.

6. **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — VGO is cracked in a **riser reactor** at 480–540 °C with **zeolite Y catalyst**. Products: gasoline (high RON ~ 92), LPG, LCO, decant oil, and coke. Coke is burned off in the **regenerator** at ~ 700 °C.

7. **Catalytic Reforming** — Heavy Naphtha is upgraded in a **Pt-Re / Pt-Cl-Al₂O₃** reformer at 480–520 °C, raising RON from 60 to 95+. Co-product: H₂ for HDS.

8. **Alkylation** — C₃–C₄ olefins + isobutane → high-RON alkylate using H₂SO₄ or HF.

9. **Treating Units**:
   - **HDS (Hydrodesulphurisation)** — removes S to < 10 ppm in diesel.
   - **Merox** — thiol removal from LPG/naphtha.
   - **Amine Treating** — H₂S removal from sour gas.

10. **Blending and Dispatch** — Streams are blended in tank farms to meet market specs (RON 91/95, pour point, flash point, etc.) and shipped via pipeline / tanker / rail / truck.

#### **Mark Split (Examiner's View)**

| Sub-part | Marks |
|---|---|
| Block flow diagram from wellhead to dispatch | 3 |
| Description of ADC, VDU, FCC roles | 3 |
| Use of key terms (HDS, reformer, alkylation, RON) | 1 |
| **Total for (a)** | **7** |

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the **API gravity** of a crude oil whose specific gravity is **0.965**. Comment on its classification and refineability. If **10 000 barrels/day** of this crude is processed, calculate the **mass flow in tonnes/day** given that 1 barrel = 159 litres and density = 965 kg/m³. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

#### **Model Solution**

**Step 1 — API Gravity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} &= \frac{141.5}{0.965} - 131.5 \\
&= 146.63 - 131.5 \\
&= 15.13^{\circ}\,\text{API}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classification:**

| API | Category |
|---|---|
| < 22° | Heavy crude |
| 22°–31° | Medium crude |
| > 31° | Light crude |

Since API = **15.13° < 22°**, this is a **heavy crude oil**. Heavy crudes are:
- Cheaper to buy (lower API = lower price).
- Contain more **residues and asphaltenes**, less gasoline and diesel.
- Require **more severe processing** (coking, hydrocracking).
- Higher sulphur content → more HDS needed.

**Step 3 — Mass Flow Calculation:**

Volume flow rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= 10{,}000 \text{ barrels/day} \times 159 \text{ L/barrel} \\
&= 1{,}590{,}000 \text{ L/day} \\
&= 1{,}590 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \dot{V} \times \rho \\
&= 1{,}590 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 965 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&= 1{,}534{,}350 \text{ kg/day} \\
&= 1{,}534.35 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** API = **15.13°**, classified as **heavy crude**, mass flow = **1534.35 tonnes/day**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cement Manufacture — Must-Know Points**
> - Cement = **calcined mixture of CaCO₃ + clay**; clinckered at **~1450 °C** in a rotary kiln.
> - Dry process is **industry standard** today (95 %+ of new plants).
> - Four **Bogue compounds**: C₃S, C₂S, C₃A, C₄AF — and the formulas to compute them.
> - **LSF ideal range:** 0.92 – 0.98.
> - **Gypsum (3–5 %)** is added to control **flash set** of C₃A.
> - **Blaine fineness** ≥ **225 m²/kg** for OPC.
> - Rotary kiln: **3–8 m diameter, 50–230 m long**, **3–4°** inclined, **0.5–5 rpm**, lined with **refractory bricks**.
> - Thermal zones in kiln (in order): Drying → Pre-heating → Calcining → Burning → Cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroleum Refining — Must-Know Points**
> - Crude → **Desalter → ADC → VDU → Conversion (FCC/Reformer/Coker) → Treating (HDS) → Blending → Product**.
> - ADC fractions in order from top: **Gas → LPG → Naphtha → Kerosene → Diesel → AGO → Reduced Crude**.
> - VDU operates at **low pressure (50–100 mmHg)** to avoid cracking of residues.
> - **FCC uses zeolite Y catalyst** in a **riser + regenerator** loop; reaction at 480–540 °C.
> - **Catalytic Reformer** raises RON and produces H₂ for HDS.
> - **API Gravity** = 141.5/SG − 131.5; < 22° = heavy, > 31° = light.
> - **Material balance:** ΣInput = ΣOutput + ΣLosses (closure check).
> - **Wet cement process** uses 32–40 % water slurry; **dry process** uses dry raw meal.
> - **Clinker factor** (dry process) ≈ 0.60 – 0.65.
> - **Petroleum coke** is the carbon residue from delayed coking; used in electrodes and as fuel.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Sentence Recall (for last-minute revision)**
> "Cement is made by **clinkering** limestone and clay at **1450 °C** in a rotary kiln; petroleum is **separated** in the ADC, **cracked** in the FCC, and **cleaned** in the HDS — and both rely on careful mass and energy balance governed by simple empirical formulas."
